utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: Who’s there?

utterances.speaker: FRANCISCO
utterances.text: Nay, answer me. Stand and unfold yourself.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: Long live the King!

utterances.speaker: FRANCISCO
utterances.text: Barnardo.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: He.

beat_states.desire_state: to identify the figure in the dark and safely complete the changing of the guard
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty as a loyal sentinel while managing his fear of the supernatural threat on the platform
beat_states.obstacle: total darkness and the inability to identify who approaches; heightened anxiety from previous ghost sightings

affect_state.rationale: Barnardo is anxious and on edge, arriving at a post where a ghost has been seen. His challenge out of turn reveals high arousal and low certainty about what he might encounter. Relief comes only when Francisco identifies him.

social_state.rationale: Barnardo is slightly lower status in this moment — he has broken protocol by challenging first, and Francisco corrects him. Once identified, there is collegial warmth between fellow soldiers.

known_facts: He is arriving to relieve Francisco on the night watch
A ghost resembling the dead King has appeared on previous nights
It is bitter cold and dark on the platform at Elsinore
hidden_secrets: He has arranged for Horatio and Marcellus to come verify the ghost sighting
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo knows what he has seen on prior nights and is primed to expect the supernatural. His breaking of protocol suggests his knowledge of the ghost is actively distorting his behavior.

beat_states.defense_state: preemptive aggression — calling out first to seize control of the encounter
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is the one arriving to relieve Francisco, yet he challenges first as though he is the one on guard — his fear overrides protocol
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Barnardo's challenge could simply be a dramaturgical device to establish tension, with less psychological weight — he may just be nervous about the cold and dark rather than specifically about the ghost

beat_states.desire_state: to maintain authority and proper military protocol while identifying the approaching figure
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to survive his watch and be safely relieved — to restore order and normalcy in a time of national unease
beat_states.obstacle: an unknown figure in the darkness who has broken protocol by challenging him first

affect_state.rationale: Francisco is startled by the breach of protocol but quickly reasserts himself. He is fatigued and uneasy from his long watch in the cold. His command 'Stand and unfold yourself' restores some sense of control, but the underlying dread persists.

social_state.rationale: As the sentinel currently on post, Francisco holds procedural authority. His correction of Barnardo asserts higher status. Warmth is minimal until identification is confirmed, then slightly collegial.

known_facts: He is the sentinel currently on duty at the platform
It is midnight and his watch is nearly over
Something has made his watch deeply unsettling — he is 'sick at heart'
hidden_secrets: The depth of his psychological distress during the watch
epistemic_state.rationale: Francisco knows the protocols and his post. He may or may not have seen the ghost himself, but something has deeply disturbed him during this watch, which he has not yet articulated.

beat_states.defense_state: reasserting rank and procedure — correcting the breach to regain control of the situation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is described as 'sick at heart' moments later, suggesting his authoritative correction masks a deep weariness and dread
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Francisco's sharpness could be purely procedural military discipline rather than fear-driven — a seasoned soldier simply doing his job correctly

utterances.speaker: FRANCISCO
utterances.text: You come most carefully upon your hour.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: ’Tis now struck twelve. Get thee to bed, Francisco.

utterances.speaker: FRANCISCO
utterances.text: For this relief much thanks. ’Tis bitter cold, / And I am sick at heart.

beat_states.desire_state: to take over the watch efficiently and reassure his fellow soldier
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to confirm the reality of the Ghost he has previously seen and find collective witness to the threat
beat_states.obstacle: Francisco's unexpected confession of malaise, which mirrors and threatens to amplify Barnardo's own suppressed anxiety about what he has seen on prior watches

affect_state.rationale: Barnardo maintains a surface of competence and control — he knows the time, he gives orders — but his slightly elevated arousal betrays anticipation. He arrived early and nervously challenged first (Beat 1), suggesting he is not as composed as he performs.

social_state.rationale: As the arriving reliever, Barnardo assumes gentle authority — telling Francisco to go to bed, taking charge. His warmth is genuine collegial care for a fellow soldier suffering in the cold.

known_facts: It is midnight — time for the changing of the watch
He has previously seen something alarming on the battlements
He has arranged for Horatio and Marcellus to come as witnesses
hidden_secrets: He has seen the Ghost on prior nights and expects it may appear again tonight
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo possesses crucial knowledge he does not share with Francisco — the Ghost sightings. His brisk practicality may be a deliberate choice not to burden Francisco, or not to speak of it until his chosen witnesses arrive.

beat_states.defense_state: deflecting — keeping the exchange briskly practical ('Get thee to bed') to avoid confronting shared dread
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: projects calm authority and caretaking but is himself anxious about what the night may bring, having arranged for Horatio and Marcellus to join him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Barnardo's briskness could indicate genuine unconcern rather than suppressed anxiety, though his nervous challenge in Beat 1 and his pre-arranged witness plan undermine a purely calm reading.

beat_states.desire_state: to be relieved of his watch and escape the cold, dark battlements
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to survive and maintain sanity in an atmosphere of creeping dread
beat_states.obstacle: a deep unease that transcends the physical cold — something is wrong at Elsinore

affect_state.rationale: Francisco is exhausted, cold, and haunted by something he cannot name. His 'sick at heart' is an unguarded moment of vulnerability that breaks through his professional composure. Low arousal because he is depleted rather than agitated.

social_state.rationale: As the outgoing sentry being relieved, Francisco is in a slightly subordinate position — grateful, deferential. His warmth is genuine; Barnardo's arrival is welcome comfort in the dark.

known_facts: Barnardo has arrived punctually at midnight
It is bitterly cold
He has stood watch alone in the dark
hidden_secrets: The source of his 'sickness at heart' — possibly he has seen or sensed the Ghost, or feels the political dread of Denmark's military preparations
epistemic_state.rationale: Francisco knows something is deeply wrong but may not be able to articulate it. His 'sick at heart' suggests a disturbance beyond the cold — possibly a prior sighting of the Ghost or ambient dread — that he does not fully explain.

beat_states.defense_state: minimizing — attributing his existential dread to physical discomfort ('bitter cold') before the involuntary confession 'sick at heart'
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: tries to perform soldierly normalcy and gratitude but cannot suppress an admission of profound spiritual malaise
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: 'Sick at heart' could be purely physical exhaustion and cold rather than supernatural dread, though Shakespeare's placement of it as the play's emotional keynote suggests something deeper.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: Have you had quiet guard?

utterances.speaker: FRANCISCO
utterances.text: Not a mouse stirring.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: Well, good night. / If you do meet Horatio and Marcellus, / The rivals of my watch, bid them make haste.

beat_states.desire_state: To confirm whether the ghost appeared again and to ensure Horatio and Marcellus arrive quickly as witnesses/reinforcements
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To validate and share the terrifying supernatural experience so he is not alone with it
beat_states.obstacle: Francisco seems unaware of anything unusual, and Horatio and Marcellus haven't arrived yet

affect_state.rationale: Barnardo is nervous about the ghost's potential reappearance. His question about quiet guard is genuinely anxious — he needs to know if the apparition came. His rush to summon Horatio and Marcellus betrays unease beneath a composed exterior.

social_state.rationale: As the relieving guard, Barnardo now holds slight authority in the exchange. He's collegial but somewhat perfunctory — his mind is elsewhere, focused on the coming watch and his companions.

known_facts: He has seen an apparition on a previous watch
Horatio and Marcellus are supposed to join his watch tonight
He needs witnesses to confirm what he has seen
hidden_secrets: He is deeply frightened by the ghost and has arranged for Horatio specifically to verify the sighting
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo knows about the ghost but hasn't yet shared this with Francisco. His question about quiet guard is a covert intelligence-gathering move — testing whether the ghost appeared during Francisco's shift.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through casual pleasantries — hiding his anxiety about the ghost behind routine guard-change protocol
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He asks a seemingly routine question ('quiet guard?') but his urgency about Horatio and Marcellus reveals a deeper anxiety he's masking
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Barnardo's question could be purely routine military protocol with no subtext about the ghost, though his urgency about Horatio and Marcellus suggests otherwise.

beat_states.desire_state: To end his watch and leave this cold, unsettling post as quickly as possible
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To survive his duty and return to safety and warmth
beat_states.obstacle: He must complete the formal handoff before he can leave

affect_state.rationale: Francisco is fatigued and eager to leave. His terse answer suggests a man who wants to close this chapter. The famous 'sick at heart' feeling lingers beneath his matter-of-fact report.

social_state.rationale: Francisco is the departing guard, deferring to Barnardo's instructions about Horatio and Marcellus. Minimal warmth — professional but not engaged.

known_facts: Nothing visibly unusual happened during his watch
He has been cold and unsettled ('sick at heart')
His watch is over
hidden_secrets: His sickness at heart may stem from a deeper unease he cannot articulate — a foreboding about Denmark's state
false_beliefs: That nothing significant is wrong — that the quiet of his watch means all is well
epistemic_state.rationale: Francisco reports factual quiet but may be unaware of the apparition that has appeared on previous nights. His unease is intuitive rather than informed.

beat_states.defense_state: Minimization — 'Not a mouse stirring' compresses a long, anxious watch into terse reassurance, shutting down further inquiry
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His clipped, minimal answer ('Not a mouse stirring') may mask that the watch was more psychologically oppressive than he lets on — he earlier admitted to being 'sick at heart'
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Francisco's brevity could simply be exhaustion and cold rather than psychological avoidance — he may genuinely have experienced an uneventful watch.

utterances.speaker: FRANCISCO
utterances.text: I think I hear them.— Stand ho! Who is there?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Friends to this ground.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: And liegemen to the Dane.

utterances.speaker: FRANCISCO
utterances.text: Give you good night.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: O farewell, honest soldier. Who hath relieved you?

utterances.speaker: FRANCISCO
utterances.text: Barnardo hath my place. Give you good night.

beat_states.desire_state: To safely hand off his watch and leave this unsettling post as quickly as possible
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To survive his duty and escape the dread of the night watch
beat_states.obstacle: Unknown figures approaching in the dark — potential threat before he can leave

affect_state.rationale: Initial spike of alertness at hearing footsteps, quickly resolved into relief once identities are confirmed. His clipped, efficient farewells betray eagerness to leave.

social_state.rationale: A common soldier addressing his social superiors (Horatio is a scholar, Marcellus an officer). Polite but minimal warmth — he wants out, not conversation.

known_facts: Barnardo has relieved him
His shift is over
The night has been quiet in terms of earthly threats
hidden_secrets: He is 'sick at heart' — something about this watch deeply disturbs him beyond normal duty
epistemic_state.rationale: Francisco may or may not have seen the Ghost himself, but his earlier admission of being 'sick at heart' and his eagerness to leave suggest awareness that something is deeply wrong on this platform.

beat_states.defense_state: Procedural formality as shield — hiding behind military protocol to manage fear
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Relieved to be leaving yet eager to discharge duty properly; his haste to depart suggests something unspoken about the night
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Francisco's brevity could simply be exhaustion and cold rather than psychological dread, but his earlier 'sick at heart' line weights toward unease.

beat_states.desire_state: To gain safe passage onto the platform and begin his investigation of the reported Ghost
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To use reason and direct observation to determine the truth about the supernatural apparition
beat_states.obstacle: Must first identify himself to the armed, nervous sentinel in the dark

affect_state.rationale: Measured and composed. His brief, declarative answer ('Friends to this ground') projects calm authority but he is arriving to test an extraordinary claim, so underlying arousal is present.

social_state.rationale: As a scholar and friend of the prince, Horatio holds higher social rank than the sentinels. His warmth is neutral — he's task-oriented, not here for fellowship.

known_facts: Marcellus and Barnardo have reported seeing a ghost resembling the dead King
He has been invited to witness it himself
He is skeptical of the claim
hidden_secrets: His skepticism — he likely regards this as 'fantasy' but has agreed to come
false_beliefs: The Ghost is probably a product of the soldiers' overwrought imaginations
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio arrives as the designated rational witness. He knows the report but disbelieves it. His false belief will be shattered when the Ghost appears.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual composure — presenting himself as allied and unthreatening
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: A rationalist skeptic voluntarily coming to a haunted watch post; his calm self-identification masks the curiosity and possible anxiety driving him here
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may already be more anxious than he lets on — his willingness to come suggests the report unsettled him more than pure skepticism would warrant.

beat_states.desire_state: To arrive at the platform, confirm the watch is set, and prepare for the Ghost's potential reappearance with Horatio as witness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To validate his terrifying experience by having a credible witness confirm the Ghost's reality
beat_states.obstacle: Must navigate the challenge protocol and transition the watch before he can get to his real purpose

affect_state.rationale: Outwardly warm and correct ('O farewell, honest soldier'), but elevated arousal — he has seen the Ghost twice and expects it again. His politeness to Francisco is genuine but performative, masking anticipation and fear.

social_state.rationale: An officer, slightly above Francisco in rank. Notably warmer than Horatio — 'honest soldier' is affectionate and acknowledging. He has camaraderie with these men who share the terrifying watch.

known_facts: He has seen the Ghost on two previous nights
He has brought Horatio specifically to witness it
Barnardo has already relieved Francisco
hidden_secrets: The full depth of his fear about what the Ghost portends for Denmark
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus is the orchestrator of this night's plan. He knows what he's seen and has arranged for Horatio's presence as intellectual validation. His question about who relieved Francisco is also tactical — confirming Barnardo is in place for the vigil.

beat_states.defense_state: Military formality and social grace — using protocol and courtesy to maintain composure despite inner dread
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Performing normal military courtesy while burning with the extraordinary knowledge that a dead king's ghost walks this platform
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus's warmth to Francisco could also be an attempt to extract information — probing whether Francisco saw anything unusual on his watch.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Holla, Barnardo.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: Say, what, is Horatio there?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: A piece of him.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: Welcome, Horatio.— Welcome, good Marcellus.

beat_states.desire_state: to confirm that Horatio has actually come so the Ghost can be witnessed by a scholar
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to validate the terrifying supernatural event and understand what it means for Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: uncertainty whether Horatio will take the apparition seriously or even show up

affect_state.rationale: Barnardo is genuinely relieved and pleased that Horatio has come. His eagerness to confirm Horatio's presence reveals how much he needs this validation. He's warmer here than in previous beats — the arrival of reinforcements eases his isolation.

social_state.rationale: Barnardo warmly welcomes both men; he defers slightly to Horatio's scholarly status while maintaining the host-like authority of being the one already on watch.

known_facts: The Ghost appeared the previous night
Horatio is a scholar who might be able to address the Ghost
Marcellus was supposed to bring Horatio
hidden_secrets: The depth of his fear about the apparition
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo has been waiting specifically for this reinforcement. His quick question about Horatio reveals this was the arrangement.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Relief at having allies mixed with anxiety about what they may witness tonight

beat_states.desire_state: to fulfill his obligation of coming but signal his skepticism about what he's been told
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to apply reason and scholarship to understand the truth of Denmark's situation
beat_states.obstacle: he doesn't believe in the Ghost and is here reluctantly, humoring his friends

affect_state.rationale: 'A piece of him' is wry, self-deprecating, and slightly cold — the remark of a man who is intellectually assured but physically uncomfortable (it's freezing) and emotionally withholding. Low arousal, high control: the skeptic maintaining his composure.

social_state.rationale: Horatio occupies higher social/intellectual status as the university scholar. His witty, minimal reply signals he's not fully investing warmth in the situation — he's here as a favor, maintaining ironic distance.

known_facts: Marcellus and Barnardo claim to have seen a Ghost
He has been asked to come witness it
false_beliefs: The Ghost is likely a product of imagination or fantasy — 'tis but our fantasy'
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is the rational skeptic. He will shortly express explicit disbelief. His 'piece of him' suggests he's only partly present — his mind hasn't committed to taking this seriously.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization through dry wit — 'A piece of him' distances himself from full commitment to this errand
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has come despite his disbelief, suggesting curiosity wars with his rationalist pride
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The line 'A piece of him' could also reflect genuine physical misery from the cold, a self-deprecating joke about being half-frozen rather than intellectual detachment.

beat_states.desire_state: to announce himself and his companion to the sentinels on watch
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to confirm the supernatural phenomenon he has witnessed and secure credible validation
beat_states.obstacle: the darkness and tension of the watch make identification necessary before approach

affect_state.rationale: Marcellus is purposeful and slightly energized — he's arriving with Horatio, the scholar he's recruited. He feels some anticipation about what the night may bring.

social_state.rationale: A fellow soldier greeting his comrade with easy familiarity; mid-status, warm but efficient.

known_facts: He has seen the Ghost on previous nights
He has brought Horatio to witness it
Barnardo is on watch tonight
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus knows the Ghost has appeared and has taken the initiative to bring a credible witness.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: What, has this thing appeared again tonight?

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: I have seen nothing.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Horatio says ’tis but our fantasy / And will not let belief take hold of him / Touching this dreaded sight twice seen of us. / Therefore I have entreated him along / With us to watch the minutes of this night, / That, if again this apparition come, / He may approve our eyes and speak to it.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Tush, tush, ’twill not appear.

beat_states.desire_state: to have his experience of the ghost validated by a credible witness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to understand and respond to the ominous supernatural disturbance threatening Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio's skepticism and the ghost's failure to appear so far tonight

affect_state.rationale: Anxious and on edge from previous sightings, but holding back; feels exposed to ridicule if the ghost doesn't reappear; his terse answer masks deeper fear and need for validation

social_state.rationale: Lower status than scholar Horatio; deferential in keeping his answer brief and factual rather than arguing

known_facts: He has personally seen the apparition on previous nights
Tonight it has not yet appeared
Horatio is skeptical
hidden_secrets: The depth of his fear about what the ghost means
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo knows what he saw but is uncertain whether it will reappear to prove him right

beat_states.defense_state: restraint — giving a minimal factual report to avoid seeming credulous before the skeptic
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he desperately wants Horatio to believe him but responds with cautious understatement ('I have seen nothing') rather than pressing his case
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Barnardo's brevity could indicate he is letting Marcellus take the lead as the more persuasive advocate

beat_states.desire_state: to debunk the ghost sighting and prove it is mere imagination
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain rational control over reality through intellectual authority
beat_states.obstacle: his friends' sincere belief and their insistence he witness the apparition himself

affect_state.rationale: Projects confident dismissiveness with his 'Tush, tush' but the very fact he showed up on a cold midnight watch betrays some underlying unease; he performs certainty more than he fully possesses it

social_state.rationale: As a scholar, he holds intellectual authority over the soldiers; his tone is slightly condescending ('Tush, tush') but he did come as a favor, showing some collegial warmth

known_facts: Barnardo and Marcellus claim to have seen a ghost twice
He has been specifically entreated to come as a credible witness
He is a scholar and therefore expected to be able to address a spirit
hidden_secrets: His willingness to come suggests more curiosity or anxiety than he lets on
false_beliefs: The apparition will not appear; it is mere fantasy
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio positions himself as the empiricist who must see to believe, but his false belief that nothing will appear is about to be shattered

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — using rational skepticism to ward off the possibility of something terrifying and inexplicable
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he came to the watch voluntarily (suggesting curiosity or concern) while openly dismissing the possibility of seeing anything
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may be performing skepticism partly to calm the frightened soldiers, acting as an emotional anchor rather than purely expressing disbelief

beat_states.desire_state: to convince Horatio to take the ghost sighting seriously and to have his own sanity confirmed
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to ensure the supernatural threat to Denmark is properly recognized and addressed by those with authority
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio's entrenched skepticism and intellectual superiority

affect_state.rationale: Frustrated that Horatio dismisses them, anxious about the apparition, but energized by his plan to use Horatio as validator; the word 'dreaded' reveals genuine fear beneath his organized speech

social_state.rationale: Mid-status soldier deferring to Horatio's intellectual authority while maintaining warm collegial bonds with both men; he frames his entreaty as collaborative

known_facts: He has seen the apparition twice
Horatio is a scholar who can potentially speak to the ghost
Horatio does not yet believe them
Barnardo also witnessed the ghost
hidden_secrets: His deeper fear that the ghost portends something terrible for Denmark
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus is epistemically secure about what he saw but strategically aware that his testimony alone is insufficient — hence bringing Horatio

beat_states.defense_state: appeal to authority — framing Horatio's presence as the solution, elevating him to judge so his eventual confirmation will be irrefutable
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he calls the sight 'dreaded' revealing genuine terror while simultaneously trying to sound measured and strategic in his plan to use Horatio as a witness
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus may also be seeking Horatio's scholarly ability to communicate with the ghost, not just validation — his goal is practical as much as epistemic

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: Sit down awhile, / And let us once again assail your ears, / That are so fortified against our story, / What we have two nights seen.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Well, sit we down, / And let us hear Barnardo speak of this.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: Last night of all, / When yond same star that’s westward from the pole / Had made his course t’ illume that part of heaven / Where now it burns, Marcellus and myself, / The bell then beating one—

beat_states.desire_state: To convince Horatio of the ghost's reality by drawing him into the narrative with vivid, precise detail
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To have the supernatural event validated by a credible witness so it can be reported and acted upon
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio's skepticism — his ears are 'fortified' against the story

affect_state.rationale: Barnardo is invested and urgent — he knows what he saw and is building toward the climactic moment. His arousal rises as he reconstructs the scene. He feels some vulnerability because his credibility is on the line before a scholar.

social_state.rationale: Barnardo is lower status — a common soldier trying to persuade a university-educated man. He compensates with hospitality ('sit down awhile') and careful rhetorical framing, showing warmth and deference.

known_facts: He and Marcellus have seen the apparition on two consecutive nights
The exact time and celestial positioning when the ghost appeared
Horatio is skeptical and considers their story a fantasy
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo is an honest eyewitness marshaling precise observational data — the star's position, the bell striking one — to build an irrefutable case.

beat_states.defense_state: Anchoring his account in concrete, verifiable astronomical detail to preempt accusations of fantasy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Needs to appear calm and authoritative to be believed, but is reliving an experience that terrified him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Barnardo may also be using the storytelling as a kind of ritual to manage his own fear — narrating the event gives him control over something that overwhelmed him.

beat_states.desire_state: To hear the account firsthand and evaluate it rationally before committing to belief
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve as a trustworthy rational witness who can later advise Hamlet with accurate information
beat_states.obstacle: His own intellectual pride and skepticism war against the sincerity of two fellow Danes he cannot easily dismiss

affect_state.rationale: Horatio projects calm control but his certainty is shaken — he wouldn't be here at midnight on a freezing battlement if he weren't at least half-persuaded. Low arousal on the surface masks growing unease.

social_state.rationale: Horatio holds higher social/intellectual status as the scholar whose validation the soldiers seek. His 'Well, sit we down' is a gracious but somewhat condescending granting of audience.

known_facts: Marcellus and Barnardo claim to have seen a ghost on two nights
He was specifically brought here to witness and verify
He considers himself a man of reason and philosophy
false_beliefs: The apparition is likely a product of the soldiers' fear and fantasy
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio's false belief — that this is mere imagination — is about to be shattered. He has agreed to listen but has not yet surrendered his rationalist framework.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — maintaining the posture of a detached auditor who will judge the evidence on its merits
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He agrees to sit and listen ('Well, sit we down') which is already a concession, yet he frames it as a rational inquiry rather than admitting curiosity or anxiety
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's willingness to come and sit may indicate he is more open to the supernatural than he lets on — his skepticism could be partly performative, maintaining face before common soldiers.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Peace, break thee off! Look where it comes again.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: In the same figure like the King that’s dead.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Thou art a scholar. Speak to it, Horatio.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: Looks he not like the King? Mark it, Horatio.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Most like. It harrows me with fear and wonder.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: It would be spoke to.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Speak to it, Horatio.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: What art thou that usurp’st this time of night, / Together with that fair and warlike form / In which the majesty of buried Denmark / Did sometimes march? By heaven, I charge thee, speak.

beat_states.desire_state: To have Horatio witness and confirm the Ghost's reality, vindicating his earlier testimony
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be believed and to understand the supernatural threat facing Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio was skeptical and dismissive; the Ghost may vanish before it can be fully verified

affect_state.rationale: High arousal from the Ghost's sudden appearance. Fear is present but partially offset by vindication — he was telling the truth. He deflects to Horatio, showing he feels out of his depth but relieved to have a witness.

social_state.rationale: His status rises from the earlier beat where he was the unbelieved storyteller — now he's the proven witness. He is collegial, eager to include Horatio, warm in his urgency.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared before in this same form
The Ghost resembles the dead King
The Ghost must be spoken to by someone of learning
Horatio is now seeing what he and Marcellus described
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo's knowledge is experiential — he has seen this before and recognizes the pattern. He knows the protocol that a scholar should address the spirit.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflecting his own fear by focusing on identification and proof — turning terror into evidence-gathering
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Triumphant that the Ghost appeared to prove him right, yet terrified by its actual presence

beat_states.desire_state: To confront and identify the apparition — to master his terror through rational challenge
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To apply reason and learning to protect the state and understand what threatens Denmark's order
beat_states.obstacle: His entire rational worldview has just been shattered; he is experiencing genuine fear and awe; the Ghost may not answer or may overwhelm him

affect_state.rationale: Deeply negative valence — 'harrows me with fear and wonder.' Maximum arousal as he faces the impossible. His certainty has been destroyed. He grasps for control through the formal speech act of charging the ghost. Extremely vulnerable — his entire identity as a rational man is cracking.

social_state.rationale: Despite his inner collapse, his social status rises — everyone defers to him as the one who must speak. His warmth is low; he is not connecting interpersonally but performing a charged ritual duty. His address to the Ghost is imperious, attempting authority.

known_facts: He now sees the Ghost with his own eyes
The Ghost bears the exact likeness of the dead King
He had previously dismissed the reports as fantasy
The Ghost has not yet spoken
hidden_secrets: The depth of his fear — he admits it aloud but his proud challenge attempts to mask how profoundly shaken he is
false_beliefs: That the Ghost may be an impersonation or usurpation of the King's form rather than the King's actual spirit
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio's epistemic state has undergone a radical shift. His use of 'usurp'st' reveals he's still trying to frame this within categories he can manage — perhaps it's not really the King but something wearing his form. His worldview is in crisis.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — he frames the encounter in legalistic and theological terms ('usurp'st,' 'I charge thee') to impose rational structure on the terrifying
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He came to debunk the ghost story as 'fantasy' and now must speak to what he called 'this thing' — his skepticism has collapsed but his pride and duty compel him to confront what he cannot explain
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's 'usurp'st' could also be read not as doubt about the Ghost's identity but as a political accusation — the Ghost, like Claudius, is an usurper of what rightfully belongs to another realm, making this a displaced expression of political anxiety rather than epistemological doubt.

beat_states.desire_state: To get Horatio to speak to the Ghost immediately before it disappears
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To find authoritative help in understanding and managing the supernatural threat on their watch
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio is stunned and may freeze; the Ghost could vanish at any moment; Marcellus himself lacks the scholarly authority to address it

affect_state.rationale: Extremely high arousal — the Ghost is here NOW. Negative valence from fear, but some excitement at vindication. He feels limited control; he can only push Horatio to act. Vulnerable because helpless before a supernatural entity.

social_state.rationale: Marcellus takes charge of the situation practically — interrupting Barnardo's story, directing Horatio — but defers intellectually and spiritually to the scholar. His warmth is functional, focused on the task.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared on previous nights
It resembles the dead King of Denmark
A scholar is needed to speak to a ghost
Horatio is a scholar capable of addressing it
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus operates on folk knowledge about ghosts requiring scholarly address. His understanding is practical, not philosophical.

beat_states.defense_state: Delegating the dangerous task to Horatio — using his role as organizer to avoid direct confrontation with the supernatural
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He brought Horatio to witness and speak, but his urgency reveals his own deep fear — he is both the man of action orchestrating the encounter and someone desperate to hide behind Horatio's learning

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: It is offended.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: See, it stalks away.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Stay! speak! speak! I charge thee, speak!

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: ’Tis gone and will not answer.

beat_states.desire_state: to confirm and narrate what is happening so the group stays united in their perception
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to have his testimony validated and to protect the watch
beat_states.obstacle: the Ghost is leaving before they can learn anything

affect_state.rationale: Barnardo's line is observational and relatively calm — he's been through this before. There's quiet frustration but also some satisfaction that Horatio has now witnessed the apparition.

social_state.rationale: Barnardo occupies a supporting role, narrating rather than leading. His status is slightly elevated because his earlier claims are now proven true.

known_facts: The Ghost appeared before and has now appeared again
Horatio has now witnessed it firsthand
The Ghost departs without speaking
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo's knowledge is experiential and grounded in repeated observation. He knows this pattern.

beat_states.defense_state: retreating into the role of neutral observer to avoid direct confrontation with the supernatural
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: vindicated that the Ghost appeared (proving he wasn't lying) but frustrated it reveals nothing

beat_states.desire_state: to compel the Ghost to speak and reveal its purpose before it vanishes
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to use reason and courage to protect Denmark and his friend Hamlet from unknown dangers
beat_states.obstacle: the Ghost refuses to respond and disappears despite his commands

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is in crisis — the triple repetition of 'speak' reveals desperation and escalating panic beneath his commanding tone. His intellectual framework is collapsing. High arousal, low control, deep vulnerability masked by imperatives.

social_state.rationale: He assumes maximum authority in the moment, trying to dominate the Ghost through the act of charging it to speak. Warmth is minimal — this is confrontation, not connection.

known_facts: The Ghost is real — he has seen it himself
It resembles the dead King Hamlet
It will not respond to commands
His prior skepticism was wrong
hidden_secrets: The depth of his fear and philosophical disorientation
false_beliefs: That a scholar's authority to 'charge' a spirit might compel it to speak
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio's entire epistemology has shifted in seconds. He now knows the supernatural is real but has no framework to process this. His attempt to command it reveals a false belief in the power of scholarly authority over spirits.

beat_states.defense_state: asserting rational authority ('I charge thee') to maintain composure against terror
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: his skeptical worldview has just been shattered yet he instinctively reaches for authority and control through language
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's commanding tone could also be read as a performance of bravery for Marcellus and Barnardo to maintain his credibility after having doubted them

beat_states.desire_state: to make sense of the Ghost's departure and warn the others of its mood
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to understand and neutralize the supernatural threat to Denmark's safety
beat_states.obstacle: the Ghost has vanished without communicating, leaving them with no answers

affect_state.rationale: Marcellus reads the Ghost's emotional state ('offended') which shows his attentiveness but also his anxiety about having provoked it. His final line acknowledging it's gone carries resignation and deflation.

social_state.rationale: As the experienced witness, he frames the encounter for the group, maintaining a supportive, collegial role while deferring the big confrontation to Horatio.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared again as before
The Ghost seemed offended by Horatio's challenge
It departed without speaking
false_beliefs: The Ghost's departure was caused by offense rather than some other constraint
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus interprets the Ghost's behavior through a human emotional lens — attributing offense — which may or may not be accurate.

beat_states.defense_state: deferring interpretive authority — offering observations rather than confronting the Ghost directly
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: wants to engage the Ghost but is relieved it's gone
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus may be subtly rebuking Horatio for his aggressive approach, implying a gentler method was needed

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: How now, Horatio, you tremble and look pale. / Is not this something more than fantasy? / What think you on ’t?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Before my God, I might not this believe / Without the sensible and true avouch / Of mine own eyes.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Is it not like the King?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: As thou art to thyself. / Such was the very armor he had on / When he the ambitious Norway combated. / So frowned he once when, in an angry parle, / He smote the sledded Polacks on the ice. / ’Tis strange.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Thus twice before, and jump at this dead hour, / With martial stalk hath he gone by our watch.

beat_states.desire_state: to force Horatio to acknowledge the ghost is real and validate what he and Marcellus have been witnessing
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be taken seriously and have the supernatural threat recognized by an authority figure
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio's prior skepticism — Barnardo needs to press while Horatio is shaken

affect_state.rationale: Barnardo seizes the moment of Horatio's visible fear to press his advantage; he's excited to be vindicated but still frightened by what they've all witnessed

social_state.rationale: Barnardo momentarily rises in status — the common soldier has seen what the scholar dismissed, and he presses this with a mix of camaraderie and pointed questioning

known_facts: The ghost has now appeared in front of Horatio
Horatio previously dismissed their account as fantasy
Horatio is visibly shaken — trembling and pale
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo has witnessed the ghost multiple times and now has empirical proof of the apparition through Horatio's own reaction

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: triumphant vindication mixed with genuine fear — he's pleased to be proven right but terrified of what being right means

beat_states.desire_state: to process and make rational sense of an experience that shatters his worldview
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to understand the truth and act as a reliable intellectual witness — his identity as a scholar and rational man is at stake
beat_states.obstacle: his own empiricism and philosophical framework cannot account for what he has just seen with his own eyes

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is deeply shaken ('tremble and look pale'), invoking God in his confession; his intellectual armor is cracked, and the shift from 'fantasy' to 'true avouch of mine own eyes' registers as a seismic personal crisis

social_state.rationale: Horatio's status has dropped — he was the authoritative skeptic and now must humble himself before the soldiers who were right; his warmth increases as shared terror creates solidarity

known_facts: He has seen the ghost with his own eyes
The ghost bears exact resemblance to the dead King Hamlet
The ghost wore the same armor King Hamlet wore fighting Norway
The ghost's expression matched King Hamlet's frown when he fought the Polacks
The ghost departed when addressed
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio's epistemic state has undergone a revolution — his prior false belief that the ghost was fantasy is now corrected, and he is rapidly building a detailed evidentiary case from memory and observation

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — he immediately channels his shock into detailed forensic analysis of the armor, the historical parallels, cataloguing observable facts
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: his rational mind resists belief in the supernatural, yet his own senses — which he trusts above all — have confirmed it; he is forced to surrender his skepticism
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may also be performing composure — his shift to detailed historical analysis could be a desperate attempt to regain control rather than genuine calm analysis

beat_states.desire_state: to confirm the ghost's identity as King Hamlet and establish what should be done about it
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to protect Denmark and his watch from a supernatural threat by enlisting someone with the knowledge and connections to act
beat_states.obstacle: he lacks the education and status to interpret the ghost's meaning himself

affect_state.rationale: Marcellus is less shaken than Horatio because he's seen this before, but the confirmation and Horatio's fear deepen his own unease; he is methodical, feeding Horatio information

social_state.rationale: Marcellus operates as a reliable witness and collaborator; he defers to Horatio's interpretive authority but supplies crucial testimony, maintaining a warm cooperative dynamic

known_facts: The ghost has appeared three times total, including tonight
It appears at the same dead hour each time
It walks with a martial stalk
It resembles the dead King Hamlet
Horatio now confirms the resemblance
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus has the most accumulated observational data about the ghost's pattern and behavior; he provides the crucial detail about timing and repetition

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: relief that Horatio now believes them, but growing dread as the pattern of the ghost's repeated appearances suggests something urgent and unresolved

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: In what particular thought to work I know not, / But in the gross and scope of mine opinion / This bodes some strange eruption to our state.

beat_states.desire_state: to regain intellectual composure by framing the ghost's appearance within a rational, political interpretation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to understand and make sense of the world through reason and scholarly authority
beat_states.obstacle: his own shaken state — he cannot identify a specific explanation, only a vague foreboding; the ghost defies his rational framework

affect_state.rationale: Horatio has just been deeply shaken by the ghost (trembling, pale). He's trying to recover but openly admits confusion. The negative valence reflects dread; arousal is elevated from the supernatural encounter; certainty is low as he confesses he doesn't know what to think; control is diminished — he's been humbled. His vulnerability is high because the man who came to debunk is now conceding something terrible is afoot.

social_state.rationale: His status has dropped from his earlier position of skeptical authority — Barnardo just called out his trembling. He partially reclaims status by offering an interpretation, but he's been humbled. Warmth is moderate; he's now genuinely collaborating with the watchmen rather than condescending.

known_facts: He has now seen the ghost twice with his own eyes
The ghost resembles the dead King Hamlet
The ghost departed when commanded to speak
Denmark is in a state of military preparation
hidden_secrets: The depth of his fear and disorientation is greater than he lets on
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio's admission 'I know not' is remarkably honest for a scholar. He possesses the facts but lacks a framework to interpret them. His pivot to 'strange eruption to our state' reveals he's reaching toward political causation — connecting the ghost to Denmark's unrest — but this is intuition, not knowledge.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — converting raw fear into political analysis and abstraction ('strange eruption to our state')
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he admits he cannot think clearly ('I know not') while simultaneously offering a sweeping interpretive conclusion — his need to appear authoritative wars with genuine bewilderment
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may be deliberately understating his understanding to avoid alarming the soldiers further, or to buy time before committing to a specific theory he's already forming about old Hamlet and Fortinbras

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Good now, sit down, and tell me, he that knows, / Why this same strict and most observant watch / So nightly toils the subject of the land, / And why such daily cast of brazen cannon / And foreign mart for implements of war, / Why such impress of shipwrights, whose sore task / Does not divide the Sunday from the week. / What might be toward that this sweaty haste / Doth make the night joint laborer with the day? / Who is ’t that can inform me?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: That can I. / At least the whisper goes so: our last king, / Whose image even but now appeared to us, / Was, as you know, by Fortinbras of Norway, / Thereto pricked on by a most emulate pride, / Dared to the combat; in which our valiant Hamlet / ( For so this side of our known world esteemed him) / Did slay this Fortinbras, who by a sealed compact, / Well ratified by law and heraldry, / Did forfeit, with his life, all those his lands / Which he stood seized of, to the conqueror. / Against the which a moiety competent / Was gagèd by our king, which had returned / To the inheritance of Fortinbras / Had he been vanquisher, as, by the same comart / And carriage of the article designed, / His fell to Hamlet. Now, sir, young Fortinbras, / Of unimprovèd mettle hot and full, / Hath in the skirts of Norway here and there / Sharked up a list of lawless resolutes / For food and diet to some enterprise / That hath a stomach in ’t; which is no other / ( As it doth well appear unto our state) / But to recover of us, by strong hand / And terms compulsatory, those foresaid lands / So by his father lost. And this, I take it, / Is the main motive of our preparations, / The source of this our watch, and the chief head / Of this posthaste and rummage in the land.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: I think it be no other but e’en so. / Well may it sort that this portentous figure / Comes armèd through our watch so like the king / That was and is the question of these wars.

beat_states.desire_state: to validate Horatio's account and connect it to the Ghost's armored appearance, reinforcing the emerging theory
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to have his prior experience of the Ghost acknowledged and understood within a meaningful framework
beat_states.obstacle: he is not the primary knower here — his role is secondary to Horatio's narrative authority

affect_state.rationale: Relieved that a coherent explanation is emerging, though still unsettled. His arousal has come down from the ghost sighting. He gains some certainty from Horatio's narrative but remains aware of the ominous implications.

social_state.rationale: He defers to Horatio's greater knowledge but contributes meaningfully by drawing the connection between the Ghost's armor and the Fortinbras conflict. His warmth is collegial.

known_facts: The ghost resembles the late King Hamlet
The ghost appeared in armor
Horatio's account of the Fortinbras conflict explains the military preparations
The ghost's armored appearance may relate to the threatened war
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo synthesizes what he's observed (the armed ghost) with what Horatio has explained (the Fortinbras threat), making an interpretive leap that the ghost's appearance is connected to the coming war.

beat_states.defense_state: alignment — he reduces his own anxiety by attaching himself to Horatio's authoritative explanation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he eagerly endorses a rational political explanation while simultaneously calling the figure 'portentous,' implicitly acknowledging it may signify something beyond politics
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Barnardo's eagerness to confirm the political reading may be motivated more by a need for reassurance than by genuine conviction

beat_states.desire_state: to demonstrate his understanding, impose rational order on the night's events, and connect the Ghost to the political crisis with young Fortinbras
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to use reason and knowledge to master fear and protect those around him through understanding
beat_states.obstacle: the Ghost's appearance defies his rationalist framework — he must work to integrate the supernatural into a political explanation

affect_state.rationale: He regains composure by narrating history — the act of explaining restores his sense of control. But beneath the authority, the ghost's image haunts him. His certainty is moderate because he qualifies his account ('the whisper goes so,' 'I take it').

social_state.rationale: He rises to the role of expert and narrator, commanding the scene. His warmth is moderate — he is generous with information but primarily performing competence.

known_facts: Old King Hamlet killed Fortinbras of Norway in single combat
By sealed compact, Fortinbras forfeited his lands to Hamlet
Young Fortinbras has gathered lawless soldiers to reclaim those lands
This threat is the reason for Denmark's military preparations and nightly watches
The ghost appeared in the armor of the late king
false_beliefs: He may overestimate how fully the political explanation accounts for the Ghost's appearance — the Ghost may have a more personal, familial purpose
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio has court-adjacent knowledge ('the whisper goes so') and scholarly ability to synthesize it. He hedges his account but presents it with authority. His framework is political, which may blind him to the Ghost's true purpose.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — converting supernatural terror into a detailed historical-political lecture
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: moments ago he was trembling and confessing the ghost bodes 'some strange eruption to our state'; now he speaks with professorial authority, as if knowledge could banish dread
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may be performing confidence he doesn't fully feel, using the lecture as a way to manage his own shaken nerves after the ghost encounter

beat_states.desire_state: to understand the reason behind the military buildup and nightly watches — to make sense of the larger context that might explain the Ghost's appearance
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to protect Denmark by understanding the threats it faces
beat_states.obstacle: he lacks the political knowledge and education to piece the situation together himself

affect_state.rationale: Anxious and unsettled by the ghost, he channels his unease into urgent questioning. He feels uninformed and dependent on others for answers, which increases his vulnerability.

social_state.rationale: He openly defers to whoever 'knows,' acknowledging his lower status in terms of knowledge. His warmth comes from communal address — 'sit down, and tell me' — creating solidarity.

known_facts: Denmark is under strict nightly watch
Cannon are being cast and shipwrights are working without rest
Foreign arms are being purchased
The ghost of the late king has appeared in armor
epistemic_state.rationale: He observes the material preparations around him but cannot connect them to a cause. He senses something momentous is approaching but needs Horatio to supply the narrative.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection from supernatural fear into practical, political concerns — grounding anxiety in the material world
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he has just witnessed a ghost but pivots to asking about geopolitics, as if rational explanation could contain his dread

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: A mote it is to trouble the mind’s eye. / In the most high and palmy state of Rome, / A little ere the mightiest Julius fell, / The graves stood tenantless, and the sheeted dead / Did squeak and gibber in the Roman streets; / As stars with trains of fire and dews of blood, / Disasters in the sun; and the moist star, / Upon whose influence Neptune’s empire stands, / Was sick almost to doomsday with eclipse. / And even the like precurse of feared events, / As harbingers preceding still the fates / And prologue to the omen coming on, / Have heaven and Earth together demonstrated / Unto our climatures and countrymen. / But soft, behold! Lo, where it comes again! / I’ll cross it though it blast me.— Stay, illusion! / If thou hast any sound or use of voice, / Speak to me. / If there be any good thing to be done / That may to thee do ease and grace to me, / Speak to me. / If thou art privy to thy country’s fate, / Which happily foreknowing may avoid, / O, speak! / Or if thou hast uphoarded in thy life / Extorted treasure in the womb of earth, / For which, they say, you spirits oft walk in death, / Speak of it. / Stay and speak!— Stop it, Marcellus.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Shall I strike it with my partisan?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Do, if it will not stand.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: ’Tis here.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: ’Tis here.

beat_states.desire_state: To track and locate the Ghost so it can be confronted
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To have the supernatural threat he has witnessed validated and resolved by those with more authority
beat_states.obstacle: The Ghost is moving and evasive; Barnardo has no special authority or means to stop it

affect_state.rationale: His terse two-word exclamation shows high alertness and adrenaline. He's more certain than before (this confirms his earlier sightings) but still lacks any control over the situation. His role is purely observational.

social_state.rationale: Barnardo occupies the lowest status in this trio — he reports, he doesn't command or act. His contribution is purely informational, supporting Horatio's leadership in this crisis.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared again, confirming the pattern
The Ghost moves and changes location
Horatio's verbal conjurations have not succeeded in making it speak
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo has the most experiential knowledge of the Ghost's behavior patterns but the least theoretical framework to interpret them. His brief utterance reflects raw empirical observation.

beat_states.defense_state: Vigilant watchfulness — retreating to the familiar role of sentinel and lookout rather than engaging directly
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has seen this Ghost before yet remains a passive reporter rather than an active agent, suggesting his repeated exposure has not diminished his fear
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Barnardo's ''Tis here' could express not just alertness but a kind of vindicated excitement — proof that he wasn't imagining things.

beat_states.desire_state: To compel the Ghost to speak and reveal its purpose before it vanishes again
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To use reason and knowledge to protect Denmark from impending catastrophe
beat_states.obstacle: The Ghost refuses to speak and is disappearing; Horatio's rational framework is inadequate to control a supernatural entity

affect_state.rationale: Horatio escalates from learned exposition to frantic pleading — 'Speak to me' repeated three times shows mounting desperation. 'I'll cross it though it blast me' reveals he knows he's risking his life, yet his need to understand overrides self-preservation. The rapid shift from scholarly lecture to desperate commands shows a man losing control.

social_state.rationale: He reasserts scholarly authority through the Roman history speech, positioning himself as the one who 'knows' (answering Marcellus's request), but his frantic commands to Marcellus at the end ('Stop it, Marcellus') show he's now coordinating the group as a leader in crisis, not merely a detached observer.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared twice and resembles the dead King
Denmark is preparing for war against Fortinbras
Historical precedent: supernatural portents preceded Julius Caesar's assassination
The Ghost refuses to speak and keeps vanishing
Traditional reasons spirits walk: unfinished deeds, foreknowledge of doom, hidden treasure
false_beliefs: That the correct ritual formula or appeal might force the Ghost to speak to him specifically
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is applying every framework he has — classical learning, folk belief about spirits, Christian conjuration — throwing everything at the wall to see what sticks. His epistemology is in crisis; he's no longer sure what kind of knowledge applies.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — framing the Ghost through classical historical precedent (Rome, Julius Caesar) to maintain scholarly authority over an experience that terrifies him
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: The scholar who demanded empirical proof now desperately invokes ritual conjuration formulas, revealing his rationalism has completely collapsed under pressure
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's long Roman speech could be read as stalling — buying time to compose himself before confronting the Ghost directly, rather than genuinely trying to explain the situation to Marcellus.

beat_states.desire_state: To physically stop the Ghost from escaping so Horatio can get answers
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty as a soldier-sentinel protecting Denmark from unknown threats
beat_states.obstacle: Uncertainty about whether physical force can affect a supernatural being

affect_state.rationale: High arousal and readiness to act. His brief question ('Shall I strike it?') shows a man channeling fear into decisive action. The uncertainty is in the phrasing — he asks permission rather than acting, suggesting doubt about whether this is the right move.

social_state.rationale: Marcellus defers to Horatio's authority (asking rather than acting), positioning himself as the faithful subordinate ready to execute orders. His willingness to physically confront the Ghost shows loyalty to the group.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared multiple times on the watch
Horatio's scholarly attempts to make it speak have failed
He has a partisan (weapon) available
false_beliefs: That a physical weapon might be able to stop or detain a ghost
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus operates from a soldier's pragmatic epistemology: if words fail, try force. He doesn't know the rules of supernatural engagement.

beat_states.defense_state: Action orientation — converting fear into the readiness for physical combat, his comfort zone as a soldier
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He offers to strike a ghost with a weapon, revealing both his courage and his helpless inadequacy — a soldier whose only tool is violence facing something violence cannot touch
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus's offer to strike could be read as a test — probing whether Horatio will sanction violence against something that looks like their king.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: ’Tis gone. / We do it wrong, being so majestical, / To offer it the show of violence, / For it is as the air, invulnerable, / And our vain blows malicious mockery.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO
utterances.text: It was about to speak when the cock crew.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: And then it started like a guilty thing / Upon a fearful summons. I have heard / The cock, that is the trumpet to the morn, / Doth with his lofty and shrill-sounding throat / Awake the god of day, and at his warning, / Whether in sea or fire, in earth or air, / Th’ extravagant and erring spirit hies / To his confine, and of the truth herein / This present object made probation.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: It faded on the crowing of the cock. / Some say that ever ’gainst that season comes / Wherein our Savior’s birth is celebrated, / This bird of dawning singeth all night long; / And then, they say, no spirit dare stir abroad, / The nights are wholesome; then no planets strike, / No fairy takes, nor witch hath power to charm, / So hallowed and so gracious is that time.

beat_states.desire_state: to contribute the crucial detail that the ghost was about to speak before being interrupted
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be taken seriously as a witness and have the ghost's significance acknowledged
beat_states.obstacle: the ghost is gone and his observation is only a tantalizing near-miss — they almost learned something

affect_state.rationale: He is certain about what he observed — the ghost's mouth opened — but frustrated and slightly haunted by the near-communication. Arousal is moderate, coming down from the encounter.

social_state.rationale: A brief, factual interjection — he defers to Horatio's interpretive authority but asserts his value as an eyewitness.

known_facts: The ghost appeared to be about to speak
The cock's crow interrupted it
This is the second night the ghost has appeared
epistemic_state.rationale: Barnardo offers a concrete observation without interpretation, staying in the domain of what he directly witnessed.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: frustration at the lost opportunity versus relief that the terrifying encounter is over

beat_states.desire_state: to rationally interpret and categorize the ghost's behavior within a framework of natural and supernatural law
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to use his scholarly intellect to protect his friends and serve the state by understanding this portent
beat_states.obstacle: the phenomenon defies his rational worldview; he must reconcile what he has seen with what he can explain

affect_state.rationale: He has moved from shock to active analysis. The rhetorical flourish of his language ('trumpet to the morn,' 'god of day') suggests he is regaining composure through eloquence, but the underlying unease persists. He notes the ghost 'started like a guilty thing' — projecting guilt onto it gives him interpretive control.

social_state.rationale: Assumes the role of learned interpreter, explaining the ghost's behavior to the soldiers. His authority is now fully established — he is the one making meaning.

known_facts: The ghost fled at the cock's crow
It appeared to react with guilt or fear
The cock traditionally signals dawn and disperses spirits
The ghost bore the likeness of dead King Hamlet
It was about to speak
hidden_secrets: His deep private fear that this portent signals genuine catastrophe for Denmark
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio synthesizes direct observation with classical learning. He now fully accepts the ghost's reality and is building a theory of its nature — a 'guilty thing' confined by cosmic law. His knowledge is growing but incomplete: he doesn't know what the ghost wanted to say.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — converting raw terror into scholarly analysis of cockcrow lore and spirit behavior
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he came as a skeptic and is now fully engaged in supernatural theorizing, adopting the very framework he once dismissed
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's description of the ghost as 'a guilty thing' may be unconscious projection — his own guilt at having dismissed his friends' earlier reports.

beat_states.desire_state: to make sense of the ghost's disappearance and find spiritual reassurance against the supernatural threat
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to protect Denmark and himself from unknown dangers by understanding the omen
beat_states.obstacle: the ghost has vanished without delivering its message, leaving the mystery unresolved

affect_state.rationale: The initial fear is subsiding into a meditative, almost devotional tone. He moves from regret about the violence toward the ghost to a comforting folk-Christian cosmology. Still deeply unsettled but actively seeking solace.

social_state.rationale: Speaks as a peer sharing folk wisdom and religious comfort with his companions; creates communal warmth through shared belief.

known_facts: The ghost vanished when the cock crowed
They attempted to strike it and it was invulnerable
It appeared in the form of the dead king
false_beliefs: That Christian holy seasons completely ward off all spirits — a folk belief he clings to for comfort
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus draws on popular superstition and Christian tradition to explain the ghost's departure, filling his epistemic gap with faith.

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization through religious faith — invoking sacred time and Christian protection to contain his fear
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: torn between awe/reverence for the ghost ('so majestical') and the need to domesticate it within a safe Christian framework
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus's religious speech could be a deliberate performance of piety to manage the group's collective terror rather than genuine belief.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: So have I heard and do in part believe it. / But look, the morn in russet mantle clad / Walks o’er the dew of yon high eastward hill. / Break we our watch up, and by my advice / Let us impart what we have seen tonight / Unto young Hamlet; for, upon my life, / This spirit, dumb to us, will speak to him. / Do you consent we shall acquaint him with it / As needful in our loves, fitting our duty?

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Let’s do ’t, I pray, and I this morning know / Where we shall find him most convenient.

beat_states.desire_state: To take decisive, rational action by bringing Hamlet into the situation as the one person who can unlock the ghost's purpose
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve Hamlet faithfully and to make sense of the world through reason and duty
beat_states.obstacle: The ghost has refused to speak to them; Horatio cannot resolve this mystery alone and must rely on others' consent and cooperation

affect_state.rationale: Relief at dawn breaking and the ghost departing mixes with lingering unease; he reasserts control by proposing a plan, but the night has shaken his rationalist worldview more than he lets on

social_state.rationale: He assumes natural leadership as the scholar among soldiers, but frames the proposal democratically ('Do you consent') and appeals to shared bonds of love and duty toward Hamlet

known_facts: The ghost appeared twice and resembles the dead King Hamlet
The ghost refused to speak to any of them
Denmark is arming for potential war with Norway
Dawn has arrived and the ghost has vanished
Young Hamlet is nearby and accessible
hidden_secrets: His rational worldview has been more deeply destabilized than he is willing to openly acknowledge
false_beliefs: He assumes the ghost will necessarily speak to young Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio synthesizes all observed data and makes an inference — the ghost's resemblance to the dead king suggests it has business with the son — but this is an educated guess, not certainty

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — framing supernatural terror as a practical problem requiring a logical next step
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Says he only 'in part' believes the superstitious lore Marcellus has cited, yet stakes his life ('upon my life') on the conviction the ghost will speak to Hamlet — his skepticism is crumbling faster than he admits
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may also be motivated by a desire to warn Hamlet of potential danger rather than simply solve the mystery — protective love rather than intellectual curiosity driving the proposal

beat_states.desire_state: To act immediately on Horatio's plan and bring Hamlet to the ghost as quickly as possible
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty as a loyal soldier-subject and protect the state of Denmark from supernatural and political threats
beat_states.obstacle: None in this moment — Horatio's proposal aligns perfectly with what Marcellus wants; the only obstacle is the logistical one of finding Hamlet

affect_state.rationale: Relieved to have a clear course of action after a terrifying night; energized by dawn and purpose; defers to Horatio's plan which gives him a concrete role

social_state.rationale: Positions himself as the practical implementer — he knows where to find Hamlet, contributing useful local knowledge while deferring to Horatio's intellectual authority

known_facts: He has seen the ghost on multiple nights
He knows where Hamlet can be found this morning
The ghost vanished at the cock's crow
Horatio, the skeptic, now believes
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus's knowledge is practical and observational; he has been the one who originally brought Horatio to witness the ghost, and now his local knowledge of Hamlet's whereabouts becomes the key asset

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His eagerness to act suggests he may be more frightened than he shows — urgency masks residual fear from the night's encounters
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus's quick agreement and knowledge of Hamlet's location could suggest he had already been thinking of going to Hamlet before Horatio proposed it — the plan may have been his unspoken wish all along

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Though yet of Hamlet our dear brother’s death / The memory be green, and that it us befitted / To bear our hearts in grief, and our whole kingdom / To be contracted in one brow of woe, / Yet so far hath discretion fought with nature / That we with wisest sorrow think on him / Together with remembrance of ourselves. / Therefore our sometime sister, now our queen, / Th’ imperial jointress to this warlike state, / Have we ( as ’twere with a defeated joy, / With an auspicious and a dropping eye, / With mirth in funeral and with dirge in marriage, / In equal scale weighing delight and dole) / Taken to wife. Nor have we herein barred / Your better wisdoms, which have freely gone / With this affair along. For all, our thanks. / Now follows that you know. Young Fortinbras, / Holding a weak supposal of our worth / Or thinking by our late dear brother’s death / Our state to be disjoint and out of frame, / Colleaguèd with this dream of his advantage, / He hath not failed to pester us with message / Importing the surrender of those lands / Lost by his father, with all bonds of law, / To our most valiant brother— so much for him. / Now for ourself and for this time of meeting. / Thus much the business is: we have here writ / To Norway, uncle of young Fortinbras, / Who, impotent and bedrid, scarcely hears / Of this his nephew’s purpose, to suppress / His further gait herein, in that the levies, / The lists, and full proportions are all made / Out of his subject; and we here dispatch / You, good Cornelius, and you, Voltemand, / For bearers of this greeting to old Norway, / Giving to you no further personal power / To business with the King more than the scope / Of these dilated articles allow. / Farewell, and let your haste commend your duty.

utterances.speaker: CORNELIUS/ VOLTEMAND
utterances.text: In that and all things will we show our duty.

beat_states.desire_state: To demonstrate loyalty and competence, receive their commission, and depart swiftly on the king's business
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the state faithfully and advance their standing at court
beat_states.obstacle: No significant obstacle — they are given clear instructions and accept them readily

affect_state.rationale: They are functionaries responding to royal command — mildly positive at being entrusted with an important diplomatic mission, calm and dutiful, certain of their role, but with little personal control over events. Low vulnerability as the stakes for them personally are minimal.

social_state.rationale: They are subordinate courtiers performing deference to the king. Their warmth is formal and institutional rather than personal — they pledge duty, not affection.

known_facts: Young Fortinbras is making demands on Danish territory
They are to deliver a letter to old Norway
Their authority is strictly limited to the scope of the written articles
The new king has married the former queen
epistemic_state.rationale: They know only what has been publicly disclosed. They are instruments of policy with no hidden knowledge or private agenda revealed in the text.

beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: They may harbor private opinions about Claudius's legitimacy or the hasty marriage, but nothing in the text supports this — their line is pure obedience.

beat_states.desire_state: To establish unquestioned legitimacy as king, consolidate power, and project composed, rational authority over the court
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure and maintain the throne he has seized by marrying Gertrude and eliminating any challenge to his rule
beat_states.obstacle: The recent death of his brother (which he caused) creates moral suspicion; the hasty marriage raises eyebrows; Young Fortinbras threatens the state; Hamlet's grief is a silent accusation

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is riding high on his successful seizure of power and feels largely in command of the room, but there is underlying anxiety about legitimacy and the Fortinbras threat. He carefully modulates his tone to appear balanced — neither too happy nor too sad.
