social_state.rationale: He occupies the highest status position in the room and performs a measured warmth — thanking the court, acknowledging collective wisdom — but the warmth is strategic and performative rather than genuine. He uses the royal 'we' throughout to assert institutional authority.

known_facts: He killed King Hamlet
He has married Gertrude to secure the throne
Young Fortinbras is making territorial demands
Old Norway is bedridden and may not know of Fortinbras's plans
The court has consented to the marriage
Cornelius and Voltemand are reliable envoys
hidden_secrets: He murdered his brother to take the throne
His grief for his brother is entirely performed
false_beliefs: He believes he has successfully convinced the court that his actions are wise and legitimate
He believes the diplomatic letter to Norway will resolve the Fortinbras threat
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius operates with deep knowledge of his own crime but must construct a public reality that conceals it. He is a masterful political performer who believes his rhetorical control equals actual control of the situation.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — framing every morally questionable act (hasty marriage, minimal mourning) as wise statecraft and collective decision-making
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Must project grief for the brother he murdered while celebrating his own ascension; must appear humble while asserting absolute authority
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: One could read Claudius as genuinely conflicted about his brother's death — experiencing real sorrow alongside ambition — rather than purely performing grief. Some productions play him as a capable ruler who happens to have committed a terrible act, rather than a pure Machiavel.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: We doubt it nothing. Heartily farewell. / And now, Laertes, what’s the news with you? / You told us of some suit. What is ’t, Laertes? / You cannot speak of reason to the Dane / And lose your voice. What wouldst thou beg, Laertes, / That shall not be my offer, not thy asking? / The head is not more native to the heart, / The hand more instrumental to the mouth, / Than is the throne of Denmark to thy father. / What wouldst thou have, Laertes?

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: My dread lord, / Your leave and favor to return to France, / From whence though willingly I came to Denmark / To show my duty in your coronation, / Yet now I must confess, that duty done, / My thoughts and wishes bend again toward France / And bow them to your gracious leave and pardon.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Have you your father’s leave? What says Polonius?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Hath, my lord, wrung from me my slow leave / By laborsome petition, and at last / Upon his will I sealed my hard consent. / I do beseech you give him leave to go.

beat_states.desire_state: To publicly demonstrate his generosity and magnanimity by granting Laertes' request easily
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To legitimize his reign and consolidate power by appearing a benevolent, confident sovereign
beat_states.obstacle: No real obstacle here — this is an easy win; the challenge is making it look maximally generous

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in his element — holding court, dispensing favors, performing kingship. He feels confident, expansive, and firmly in command of this interaction.

social_state.rationale: Maximum status as the enthroned king publicly offering before being asked. Warmth is high but calculated — he's binding Polonius' family to him through conspicuous favor.

known_facts: Laertes wants to return to France
Polonius is a key political ally whose loyalty must be cultivated
Laertes came specifically for the coronation
Polonius has given reluctant consent
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet to seize the throne
His generosity toward Laertes is partly designed to contrast with and set up his denial of Hamlet's wish to leave
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is fully informed about this minor court matter and uses it strategically. He already knows the answer before he asks Polonius.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing largesse to mask the illegitimacy of his succession — every small favor is political theater
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He lavishes warmth on Laertes and Polonius while preparing to deny Hamlet a far simpler request, revealing that his generosity is strategic rather than genuine
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may genuinely like Laertes and Polonius, and his warmth may be partially sincere rather than entirely calculated

beat_states.desire_state: To obtain the King's permission to return to France as quickly and smoothly as possible
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live freely and pursue his own life away from the suffocating obligations and dangers of the Danish court
beat_states.obstacle: He must perform sufficient deference and gratitude to a new king he may not fully trust, without appearing disloyal for wanting to leave

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is a young man petitioning power — he's hopeful but subordinate, carefully managing his words. Some anxiety about whether the request will be granted easily.

social_state.rationale: Low status as a petitioner before the king, but elevated by his father's position. Warmth is courteous and deferential but not intimate.

known_facts: His father has reluctantly given consent
He needs the King's formal permission to leave
He came to Denmark specifically for the coronation
hidden_secrets: His eagerness to leave may reflect discomfort with the new political order or simply youthful desire for Parisian freedom
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is straightforward in this beat — his situation is simple and his knowledge limited to his own desires and his father's consent.

beat_states.defense_state: Framing his desire to leave as the natural completion of duty rather than an escape — 'that duty done, my thoughts bend again toward France'
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He frames his departure as loyalty fulfilled, but leaving so quickly after a coronation could signal ambivalence about the new regime
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may be more politically savvy than he appears, using exaggerated deference to mask deeper reservations about Claudius' legitimacy

beat_states.desire_state: To publicly demonstrate both his paternal authority and his loyal service to the King simultaneously
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain and increase his political influence by being indispensable to the crown
beat_states.obstacle: He must balance appearing as a good father (who reluctantly lets his son go) with appearing as a loyal subject (who defers to the King's judgment)

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is comfortable in his role as elder statesman. Mild positive affect — he's on stage doing what he does best: performing wisdom and importance. Low vulnerability because the stakes are low.

social_state.rationale: High status as chief counselor, directly addressed by the King. Warmth is moderate — he's formal and self-important rather than emotionally effusive.

known_facts: He has given Laertes permission to go
The King is in a generous, favor-granting mood
His political position depends on remaining useful to Claudius
hidden_secrets: The degree to which his 'slow leave' was genuinely reluctant vs. theatrical self-aggrandizement
false_beliefs: He believes his elaborate performance of reluctant consent makes him appear wise and measured rather than simply long-winded
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius is a man who confuses verbosity with wisdom. He knows the political landscape well but overestimates the impression his rhetorical flourishes make.

beat_states.defense_state: Exaggerating his reluctance to grant leave — 'slow leave,' 'laborsome petition,' 'hard consent' — to appear both a careful father and a man who takes duty seriously
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He claims his consent was wrung from him with difficulty, but he's actively beseeching the King to grant it — suggesting his reluctance was more performance than reality
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may genuinely be grieved to let Laertes go and his elaborate language reflects real paternal anxiety rather than pure performance

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Take thy fair hour, Laertes. Time be thine, / And thy best graces spend it at thy will.— / But now, my cousin Hamlet and my son—

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: A little more than kin and less than kind.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: How is it that the clouds still hang on you?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Not so, my lord; I am too much in the sun.

beat_states.desire_state: To express his contempt and grief without openly breaking decorum — to resist Claudius's claim on him while maintaining deniability
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To honor his dead father's memory and resist the corruption he perceives in the new order
beat_states.obstacle: He is publicly constrained by his inferior position as subject to the King; he cannot openly accuse or attack

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in deep grief and bitter anger. His puns show intellectual sharpness and arousal, but he feels powerless in his situation. The vulnerability is high — his father is dead, his mother remarried, and he is isolated in this court.

social_state.rationale: Though a prince, he is subordinate to the new King and marginalized. His warmth toward Claudius is openly hostile, barely disguised by wit. He is cold and distancing.

known_facts: His father is dead
His mother married his uncle with indecent speed
Claudius has taken the throne that might have been his
The court expects him to accept the new order
hidden_secrets: The depth of his revulsion toward the marriage
His possible suspicion that something is wrong about his father's death
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has not yet encountered the Ghost, so he does not know of the murder. His bitterness stems from the marriage and succession. His asides reveal a mind already working in subtext and concealment.

beat_states.defense_state: Weaponized wordplay and punning as a form of passive aggression — using double meanings ('kin/kind', 'sun/son') to express hostility while maintaining plausible deniability
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is forced to participate in a public court scene that legitimizes the man he despises, while his grief demands authentic expression — he is both performing compliance and subverting it simultaneously
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's puns could be read as more melancholic and self-lacerating than aggressive — 'too much in the sun' as complaint about unwanted attention rather than attack

beat_states.desire_state: To publicly integrate Hamlet into his new family order and neutralize Hamlet's visible mourning as a political embarrassment
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To legitimize his reign and secure unchallenged power over Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's conspicuous grief and hostility, which publicly undermines Claudius's narrative of smooth succession

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is confident in his public role but slightly unsettled by Hamlet's refusal to perform compliance. His question about 'clouds' is probing — he senses resistance but tries to stay magnanimous and controlled.

social_state.rationale: He holds supreme public status as King and addresses Hamlet from a position of authority. His warmth is performed — the terms of endearment are strategic rather than genuine.

known_facts: Hamlet is visibly mourning and wearing black in court
Hamlet has not accepted Claudius as father or king
The court is watching this exchange
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet to seize the throne
He married Gertrude partly to consolidate power
false_beliefs: He may believe he can manage Hamlet through public shows of paternal affection
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius knows Hamlet is hostile but likely underestimates the depth of Hamlet's suspicion. He is unaware that Hamlet's bitter wordplay ('less than kind') may carry deeper meaning about his nature/unnaturalness.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing paternal warmth to mask political calculation — claiming Hamlet as 'my son' to assert dominance through familial language
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Calling Hamlet 'cousin' and 'son' simultaneously reveals the awkwardness of his usurped position — he must claim intimacy with the man whose father he replaced and whose throne he took
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may genuinely feel some affection for Hamlet as his nephew and wish to reconcile, not purely out of political calculation but also guilt

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Good Hamlet, cast thy nighted color off, / And let thine eye look like a friend on Denmark. / Do not forever with thy vailèd lids / Seek for thy noble father in the dust. / Thou know’st ’tis common; all that lives must die, / Passing through nature to eternity.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ay, madam, it is common.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: If it be, / Why seems it so particular with thee?

beat_states.desire_state: To express his contempt for the court's shallow grief without fully breaking decorum
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To honor his father's memory and expose the moral corruption surrounding him
beat_states.obstacle: He is constrained by his position as prince, by his mother's direct address, and by the public setting of the court

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in deep grief and bitter anger. His one-line reply is compressed fury — 'common' aimed at Gertrude's behavior as much as at mortality. He barely contains himself. The control is low because the irony is close to open hostility.

social_state.rationale: Despite being a prince, Hamlet has been marginalized — Claudius was just given attention to Laertes, and now his mother speaks to him as if managing a problem child. His warmth toward her is near-frozen, laced with resentment.

known_facts: His father is dead
His mother remarried with indecent speed
Claudius now occupies his father's throne and bed
The court has moved on from mourning
hidden_secrets: The depth of his disgust at his mother's sexuality and remarriage
His sense that something is fundamentally rotten in the state
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has not yet encountered the Ghost; his knowledge is based on observation and moral intuition. He suspects corruption but has no evidence of murder.

beat_states.defense_state: irony — using the word 'common' as a weapon, turning his mother's platitude back against her with contemptuous double meaning
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is deeply wounded by his mother yet must respond to her as a dutiful son; his brevity masks volcanic emotion
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'Ay, madam, it is common' could be read as exhausted resignation rather than pointed irony — a man too depleted to argue rather than one deliberately weaponizing language.

beat_states.desire_state: To coax Hamlet out of his conspicuous mourning and reintegrate him into the court's new order
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve her new marriage, her son's loyalty, and the stability of her world
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's visible grief and resentment, which implicitly accuses her of insufficient mourning and betrayal

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude begins with gentle maternal authority but is destabilized by Hamlet's curt, cutting reply. Her follow-up question ('Why seems it so particular with thee?') reveals irritation but also genuine confusion — she senses his hostility but doesn't fully understand it. She is more exposed than she wants to be.

social_state.rationale: She speaks as Queen and mother, invoking both roles to claim authority, but her warmth is genuine even if strategically deployed. The public setting compels her to manage appearances.

known_facts: Hamlet is wearing black and visibly mourning beyond what the court considers appropriate
His father is dead and she has remarried quickly
Death is a universal human experience
hidden_secrets: The degree to which guilt or unease about her rapid remarriage may motivate her desire for Hamlet to stop mourning
false_beliefs: That Hamlet's grief is primarily about the natural fact of death rather than about her remarriage and possible betrayal
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude seems genuinely not to grasp the full depth of Hamlet's anger. She reads his mourning as excessive attachment to a universal fact, not as moral accusation.

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — universalizing death as 'common' to normalize her own quick recovery and remarriage
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She appeals to maternal warmth while simultaneously minimizing the very loss that should bind mother and son together
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may fully understand Hamlet's accusation and is deliberately deflecting in public, performing innocence — her 'Why seems it so particular' could be a conscious challenge rather than genuine confusion.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: “ Seems,” madam? Nay, it is. I know not “ seems.” / ’Tis not alone my inky cloak, good mother, / Nor customary suits of solemn black, / Nor windy suspiration of forced breath, / No, nor the fruitful river in the eye, / Nor the dejected havior of the visage, / Together with all forms, moods, shapes of grief, / That can denote me truly. These indeed “ seem,” / For they are actions that a man might play; / But I have that within which passes show, / These but the trappings and the suits of woe.

beat_states.desire_state: To assert the authenticity of his grief and reject his mother's implication that he is performing mourning
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To honor his father's memory and maintain his moral integrity in a world he sees as corrupt and false
beat_states.obstacle: His mother and the court treat his grief as excessive display, reducing his genuine suffering to mere appearance

affect_state.rationale: Deeply grieving but channeling pain into controlled, precise verbal assertion. High certainty about the reality of his inner state. The arousal is restrained but present — he seizes on his mother's word 'seems' with pointed intensity. Vulnerability is significant: he is exposing the depth of his feeling even as he claims it cannot be shown.

social_state.rationale: Publicly subordinate as son and subject, but claiming moral authority through the superiority of genuine feeling over courtly pretense. Warmth toward his mother is cool — he addresses her as 'madam' and 'good mother' with formal distance, subtly rebuking her for remarrying and for trivializing his mourning.

known_facts: His father is dead
His mother has remarried quickly to his uncle
The court expects him to move past his grief
His mother used the word 'seems' to characterize his mourning
hidden_secrets: The depth of his disgust at his mother's remarriage
His possible suicidal ideation (revealed in the next soliloquy)
His sense that something is fundamentally rotten in Denmark
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has not yet encountered the Ghost, so he does not know of the murder. His knowledge is limited to the observable facts of hasty remarriage and his own unshakeable grief. He has no false beliefs at this point — his suspicions about the wrongness of events are largely correct.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — he transforms raw emotion into a philosophical distinction between seeming and being, controlling his pain through rhetoric
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He claims his grief 'passes show' yet delivers one of the most eloquent and theatrical speeches in the play — his very denial of performance is itself a performance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: An alternative reading is that Hamlet is not merely correcting his mother but actively shaming her — using the seem/be distinction to accuse the entire court (and especially Gertrude and Claudius) of being performers and hypocrites, making this as much an attack as a defense.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: ’Tis sweet and commendable in your nature, Hamlet, / To give these mourning duties to your father. / But you must know your father lost a father, / That father lost, lost his, and the survivor bound / In filial obligation for some term / To do obsequious sorrow. But to persever / In obstinate condolement is a course / Of impious stubbornness. ’Tis unmanly grief. / It shows a will most incorrect to heaven, / A heart unfortified, a mind impatient, / An understanding simple and unschooled. / For what we know must be and is as common / As any the most vulgar thing to sense, / Why should we in our peevish opposition / Take it to heart? Fie, ’tis a fault to heaven, / A fault against the dead, a fault to nature, / To reason most absurd, whose common theme / Is death of fathers, and who still hath cried, / From the first corse till he that died today, / “ This must be so.” We pray you, throw to earth / This unprevailing woe and think of us / As of a father; for let the world take note, / You are the most immediate to our throne, / And with no less nobility of love / Than that which dearest father bears his son / Do I impart toward you. For your intent / In going back to school in Wittenberg, / It is most retrograde to our desire, / And we beseech you, bend you to remain / Here in the cheer and comfort of our eye, / Our chiefest courtier, cousin, and our son.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Let not thy mother lose her prayers, Hamlet. / I pray thee, stay with us. Go not to Wittenberg.

beat_states.desire_state: To make Hamlet stop mourning publicly, submit to Claudius's authority, and remain at Elsinore under his watch rather than return to Wittenberg
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne, his marriage, and his legitimacy by neutralizing Hamlet as a political and moral threat
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's conspicuous, defiant grief publicly delegitimizes Claudius's reign and reminds the court that something is rotten in Denmark

affect_state.rationale: Claudius speaks from a position of performative command, but the length and insistence of his speech betrays underlying anxiety about Hamlet's resistance. He escalates from gentle praise to insults ('unmanly,' 'simple and unschooled') to bribes ('most immediate to our throne'), suggesting he feels less in control than he projects.

social_state.rationale: Performs the highest status in the room as king and surrogate father, but the warmth is calculated and instrumental — each affectionate gesture ('dearest father bears his son') is embedded in a command structure. The warmth is weaponized.

known_facts: He murdered Old Hamlet
Hamlet's grief is a public rebuke to his reign
Hamlet wishes to return to Wittenberg, which would remove him from surveillance
Hamlet's claim to the throne is strong and must be managed
hidden_secrets: He is the murderer of the father Hamlet mourns
His 'fatherly love' is a political performance to contain a rival
false_beliefs: He believes his rhetorical skill is sufficient to bring Hamlet into compliance
He may believe Hamlet's grief is merely excessive mourning rather than suspicion
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius operates with a massive epistemic asymmetry — he knows the truth of the murder but must perform as though death was natural. He likely does not yet know about the Ghost or any suspicion Hamlet may harbor beyond grief.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — transforms his political need to control Hamlet into philosophical wisdom about the naturalness of death, disguising self-interest as paternal concern
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims fatherly love for Hamlet while systematically dismantling Hamlet's right to grieve the very father Claudius murdered; offers the throne's proximity as a bribe while fearing Hamlet's claim to it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A softer reading: Claudius may genuinely feel some affection for Hamlet and some guilt, making this speech partly sincere advice mixed with political calculation — the contradiction between real feeling and real crime.

beat_states.desire_state: To keep Hamlet close, to have him stay at Elsinore, and to end his visible suffering which pains her
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain her family bonds and emotional security by holding together her new marriage and her relationship with her son
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's grief and alienation — he has already publicly rebuked her with 'I know not seems' — and his desire to flee to Wittenberg

affect_state.rationale: Her two brief lines after Claudius's long speech are emotionally exposed and direct — 'Let not thy mother lose her prayers.' She drops all rhetorical armor and speaks as a mother, not a queen. The brevity reveals how much is at stake for her personally, and how little power she feels she has over Hamlet's decision.

social_state.rationale: She speaks with the authority of a queen and mother but subordinates her status to emotional appeal. Her warmth is genuine and unguarded in contrast to Claudius's calculated performance.

known_facts: Hamlet is in deep grief over his father's death
Hamlet is hostile to her remarriage
Hamlet wants to return to Wittenberg
hidden_secrets: The depth of her own possible guilt or unease about the speed of her remarriage
false_beliefs: She likely believes Old Hamlet died of natural causes
She may believe that time and proximity will heal Hamlet's grief
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude appears genuinely unaware of the murder. Her emotional logic is simpler and more direct than Claudius's — she wants her son near her and believes maternal love can fix what is broken.

beat_states.defense_state: Denial — she refuses to engage with the depth of Hamlet's accusation and reduces the situation to a simple maternal request
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She genuinely loves Hamlet and wants him near, but her remarriage is the very source of his anguish; her plea for closeness is also an unconscious request that he accept the situation that torments him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may be more politically aware than she appears — her brief appeal after Claudius's speech could be a calculated tag-team strategy, using maternal emotion to seal what his rhetoric began. Some productions play her as more complicit.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I shall in all my best obey you, madam.

beat_states.desire_state: To end this public confrontation while preserving his integrity and signaling his allegiance to his mother over Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To honor his father's memory and resist the corrupt new order while maintaining his own moral truth
beat_states.obstacle: He has been publicly cornered by both Claudius and Gertrude to stay in Elsinore and cease mourning; outright refusal would be dangerous and socially untenable

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is suppressing intense grief and rage beneath a surface of cold, minimal compliance. His arousal is deliberately tamped down — he's choosing strategic restraint. He feels trapped but finds a sliver of control in his pointed omission of Claudius.

social_state.rationale: He has just been publicly lectured and diminished by the King. His warmth is reserved only for his mother — a trace of genuine feeling — but even that is complicated by betrayal. He concedes status publicly while covertly asserting moral superiority through his selective address.

known_facts: His father died recently and suspiciously quickly
His mother married his uncle with unseemly haste
Claudius has publicly tried to reframe his grief as abnormal and unmanly
Both Claudius and Gertrude want him to stay in Elsinore rather than return to Wittenberg
hidden_secrets: He harbors deep contempt and possible suspicion toward Claudius
He is already contemplating the meaninglessness of life and possibly self-destruction
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet does not yet know of the Ghost or the murder, but his instincts about the wrongness of the situation are acute. His deliberate exclusion of Claudius from his response suggests he already positions Claudius as illegitimate.

beat_states.defense_state: Passive aggression — technically complying while pointedly addressing only his mother, excluding Claudius from acknowledgment
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He appears to submit and obey, but the deliberate choice of 'madam' over any address to the King is an act of quiet rebellion disguised as filial duty
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A simpler reading: Hamlet genuinely capitulates to his mother's plea out of love, and the exclusion of Claudius is less calculated and more instinctive — he simply cannot bring himself to acknowledge the usurper as father or king.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Why, ’tis a loving and a fair reply. / Be as ourself in Denmark.— Madam, come. / This gentle and unforced accord of Hamlet / Sits smiling to my heart, in grace whereof / No jocund health that Denmark drinks today / But the great cannon to the clouds shall tell, / And the King’s rouse the heaven shall bruit again, / Respeaking earthly thunder. Come away.

beat_states.desire_state: To publicly claim Hamlet's grudging compliance as a wholehearted victory and reassert dominance over the court narrative
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne, his marriage, and his legitimacy by neutralizing Hamlet as a threat
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's reply was pointedly directed to his mother, not to Claudius, and was at best a minimal, passive concession — not the warm submission Claudius needs

affect_state.rationale: Outwardly triumphant and expansive — ordering cannons, toasting — but beneath the performance there is anxiety about whether anyone sees through his spin. He overcompensates with grandiosity (cannon fire, heaven respeaking thunder) precisely because the accord is neither gentle nor unforced. The hyperbolic celebration betrays insecurity.

social_state.rationale: He asserts maximum royal status — 'Be as ourself in Denmark' equates his person with the state. The warmth is performed rather than genuine; the invitation to Hamlet is a public display of magnanimity designed to bind Hamlet politically rather than to express real affection.

known_facts: Hamlet addressed his compliance to Gertrude, not to him
Hamlet's grief and hostility are real and directed at him
The court is watching how he handles Hamlet's resistance
He murdered Old Hamlet and married Gertrude
hidden_secrets: He murdered his brother to gain the throne and Gertrude
He recognizes Hamlet's reply was a veiled act of defiance
false_beliefs: That this public performance of celebration will effectively contain Hamlet's dissent
That offering Hamlet the status of heir ('Be as ourself') will satisfy or co-opt him
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is a sophisticated political operator who reads Hamlet's snub accurately but chooses to publicly misrepresent it. His false beliefs concern strategy — he underestimates how deep Hamlet's alienation runs and overestimates the power of spectacle and patronage to neutralize it.

beat_states.defense_state: Reframing — seizing Hamlet's lukewarm compliance and performing it as a loving, unforced accord to the entire court, drowning any ambiguity in celebratory noise
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He knows Hamlet's reply was a deliberate snub directed only at Gertrude, yet he must publicly perform delight and satisfaction as though he received genuine filial loyalty
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A more generous reading: Claudius genuinely feels relief that Hamlet has stayed, and his celebration is partly authentic — he wants familial harmony both for political and personal reasons, and the cannon-fire is an expression of real (if performative) joy.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O, that this too, too sullied flesh would melt, / Thaw, and resolve itself into a dew, / Or that the Everlasting had not fixed / His canon ’gainst self-slaughter! O God, God, / How weary, stale, flat, and unprofitable / Seem to me all the uses of this world! / Fie on ’t, ah fie! ’Tis an unweeded garden / That grows to seed. Things rank and gross in nature / Possess it merely. That it should come to this: / But two months dead— nay, not so much, not two. / So excellent a king, that was to this / Hyperion to a satyr; so loving to my mother / That he might not beteem the winds of heaven / Visit her face too roughly. Heaven and Earth, / Must I remember? Why, she would hang on him / As if increase of appetite had grown / By what it fed on. And yet, within a month / ( Let me not think on ’t; frailty, thy name is woman!), / A little month, or ere those shoes were old / With which she followed my poor father’s body, / Like Niobe, all tears— why she, even she / ( O God, a beast that wants discourse of reason / Would have mourned longer!), married with my uncle, / My father’s brother, but no more like my father / Than I to Hercules. Within a month, / Ere yet the salt of most unrighteous tears / Had left the flushing in her gallèd eyes, / She married. O, most wicked speed, to post / With such dexterity to incestuous sheets! / It is not, nor it cannot come to good. / But break, my heart, for I must hold my tongue.

beat_states.desire_state: To release the unbearable anguish of his father's death and mother's betrayal — to find some outlet for grief he has been commanded to suppress
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To honor his father's memory and restore moral order to a world that has become corrupt and meaningless
beat_states.obstacle: He is forbidden to act — constrained by social duty ('I must hold my tongue'), by religious prohibition against suicide, by filial obedience to his mother, and by the political reality of Claudius's power

affect_state.rationale: Suicidal ideation, disgust at the world and his own flesh, oscillating between despairing numbness and surges of outraged grief. He is certain about the moral wrongness of what has happened but utterly powerless to change it. The soliloquy builds in intensity — each new detail of his mother's betrayal re-wounds him. The final line reveals total emotional suppression under enormous pressure.

social_state.rationale: Alone on stage, he reveals how diminished he feels — comparing himself unfavorably to Hercules, feeling impotent against Claudius's regime. His warmth toward his dead father is high but toward the living world is bitter and misanthropic; his contempt for his mother and uncle is scalding, yet threaded with heartbreak.

known_facts: His father died approximately two months ago
His mother married his uncle Claudius within a month of the funeral
His father was a far superior man to Claudius
His mother appeared deeply devoted to his father while he lived
Suicide is a mortal sin according to Christian doctrine
He is expected to publicly accept the situation and remain silent
hidden_secrets: He harbors suicidal thoughts
He views the marriage as incestuous
He holds deep contempt for both Claudius and Gertrude
His obedience to Claudius and Gertrude is entirely performative
false_beliefs: He may overidealize his father's perfection and his parents' marriage
He attributes his mother's remarriage entirely to female frailty rather than considering political necessity or manipulation
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows the facts of the timeline and the moral implications as he sees them, but he does not yet know HOW his father died. His judgment of Gertrude is absolute and gendered ('frailty, thy name is woman'), possibly distorted by the intensity of his grief and sense of betrayal. He has no knowledge yet of murder — only of what he perceives as obscene haste and moral corruption.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — channeling raw suicidal despair into rhetorical argument, classical allusion, and moral categorization; also self-silencing as a containment strategy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He desperately wants to speak/act/die but forces himself into silence and compliance; he idolizes his father as Hyperion while being unable to emulate his strength; he condemns his mother's frailty while being paralyzed by his own
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings emphasize that Hamlet's disgust is less about moral outrage and more about a narcissistic wound — his mother's rapid remarriage shatters his sense of his own family's specialness and his place in the world. The suicidal ideation could also be read as performative self-dramatization rather than genuine intent, though the weight of the language argues against this.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Hail to your Lordship.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I am glad to see you well. / Horatio— or I do forget myself!

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: The same, my lord, and your poor servant ever.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Sir, my good friend. I’ll change that name with you. / And what make you from Wittenberg, Horatio?— / Marcellus?

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: My good lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I am very glad to see you. To Barnardo. Good even, sir.— / But what, in faith, make you from Wittenberg?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: A truant disposition, good my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I would not hear your enemy say so, / Nor shall you do my ear that violence / To make it truster of your own report / Against yourself. I know you are no truant. / But what is your affair in Elsinore? / We’ll teach you to drink deep ere you depart.

beat_states.desire_state: To reconnect with a trusted friend and find genuine human warmth after the suffocating court performance
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To find truth and authentic connection in a world of corruption and false appearances
beat_states.obstacle: His overwhelming grief and isolation threaten to consume even this moment of joy; he also senses something unusual about their presence and needs to understand why they've come

affect_state.rationale: Genuine pleasure at seeing Horatio lifts him from suicidal despair, but underneath he remains fragile; his rapid questioning about their reason for being in Elsinore shows uncertainty and a need to understand

social_state.rationale: As prince he has high status but deliberately levels it — insisting Horatio is his 'good friend' not 'servant,' greeting Marcellus and Barnardo with equal warmth. He is actively democratic and generous, which itself is a marker of his authentic character versus the court's hierarchy

known_facts: Horatio is a fellow student at Wittenberg and a man of intellectual integrity
Horatio is not typically a truant — something specific has brought him to Elsinore
His father's funeral has recently occurred and his mother has remarried
hidden_secrets: He was just contemplating suicide moments ago
He harbors deep suspicion and disgust toward Claudius and Gertrude
false_beliefs: He does not yet know about the Ghost's appearance
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet senses something is off about Horatio's presence but hasn't yet been told. His pressing questions ('what make you from Wittenberg?') show his sharp mind detecting that Horatio's answer is evasive

beat_states.defense_state: Deflecting his own pain by channeling energy into generous warmth toward Horatio; using wit and affection to mask his raw emotional state from the soliloquy moments before
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Moments ago he wished for death and dissolution; now he is suddenly animated, warm, and socially generous — the speed of the shift reveals how desperate he is for authentic connection
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's excessive warmth could also be read as performative — testing whether Horatio is truly loyal before revealing anything of his inner state

beat_states.desire_state: To inform Hamlet about the Ghost but needing to find the right moment; also to reconnect respectfully with his friend
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve Hamlet faithfully through truth-telling and loyal companionship
beat_states.obstacle: The gravity of his news (the Ghost) makes him hesitant to blurt it out; Hamlet's rapid questioning puts him on the spot about his reasons for being in Elsinore, forcing him to deflect temporarily

affect_state.rationale: Pleased to see Hamlet but carrying the weight of disturbing news; nervous about how to deliver it; slightly destabilized by Hamlet's direct questioning about his reasons for being in Elsinore

social_state.rationale: Positions himself deferentially ('your poor servant ever') despite Hamlet's insistence on equality; his warmth is genuine but constrained by social convention and the anxiety of his mission

known_facts: He has seen the Ghost of Hamlet's father on the battlements
He came to Elsinore specifically because of the Ghost
Hamlet's father recently died and Gertrude remarried quickly
hidden_secrets: The true reason he is in Elsinore — the Ghost sighting
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is withholding critical information, waiting for the right moment to disclose what he's witnessed; his 'truant disposition' answer is a conscious lie

beat_states.defense_state: Self-deprecation ('truant disposition') as a stalling mechanism to avoid revealing the Ghost too abruptly
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He came specifically because of the Ghost — an urgent supernatural matter — yet presents himself as casually visiting, creating a tension between his true purpose and his stated reason
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's deflection could be read as genuine modesty rather than strategic delay, with the Ghost reveal being opportunistic rather than planned

beat_states.desire_state: To support Horatio in delivering the news of the Ghost to Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty as a loyal soldier by ensuring the prince knows about the supernatural threat on the battlements
beat_states.obstacle: His lower social status means he must follow Horatio's lead on when and how to reveal the information; he cannot take initiative in this conversation

affect_state.rationale: Anxious about the Ghost revelation but taking comfort in Hamlet's warm reception; feels relatively powerless in directing the conversation

social_state.rationale: As a soldier and not a fellow student, he occupies a lower social tier; his single line 'My good lord' is respectful and warm but appropriately subordinate

known_facts: He has witnessed the Ghost on multiple occasions
Horatio also saw the Ghost and confirmed it
They have come to tell Hamlet about the Ghost
hidden_secrets: The Ghost sighting and its details
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus shares Horatio's knowledge of the Ghost but is letting Horatio handle the revelation given Horatio's closer relationship with Hamlet

beat_states.defense_state: Maintaining formal courtesy as a way to stay present without overstepping
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He carries urgent, terrifying news but must perform routine social pleasantries
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus may be more anxious than shown — the Ghost terrified him — and his brevity could reflect genuine fear about what happens next

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: My lord, I came to see your father’s funeral.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I prithee, do not mock me, fellow student. / I think it was to see my mother’s wedding.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Indeed, my lord, it followed hard upon.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Thrift, thrift, Horatio. The funeral baked meats / Did coldly furnish forth the marriage tables. / Would I had met my dearest foe in heaven / Or ever I had seen that day, Horatio! / My father— methinks I see my father.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Where, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: In my mind’s eye, Horatio.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: I saw him once. He was a goodly king.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: He was a man. Take him for all in all, / I shall not look upon his like again.

beat_states.desire_state: To share his grief and outrage over his mother's hasty remarriage with a trusted friend, and to honor his father's memory
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To restore moral order and authenticity in a world he perceives as corrupt and false
beat_states.obstacle: The overwhelming pain of loss compounded by betrayal; social isolation at court where no one shares his grief; the impossibility of bringing his father back

affect_state.rationale: Deep grief and bitterness dominate, but Horatio's presence opens a valve — Hamlet is more emotionally exposed here than in the court scene. The sardonic wit shows high cognitive arousal. He is certain of the wrongness of events but feels powerless to change them. The final lines about his father reveal raw, undefended tenderness.

social_state.rationale: With Horatio, Hamlet drops the hostile prince persona and engages as an equal — 'fellow student.' Genuine warmth emerges; Horatio is the one person he trusts enough to be bitter and vulnerable with simultaneously.

known_facts: His father is dead
His mother married Claudius with indecent haste
Horatio is a trusted fellow student from Wittenberg
The funeral and wedding were scandalously close together
His father was an exceptional man and king
hidden_secrets: The depth of his suicidal ideation (from the preceding soliloquy)
His suspicion or intuition that something is fundamentally wrong beyond the remarriage
false_beliefs: He does not yet know his father was murdered
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows the observable facts of the hasty marriage but not the hidden crime. His grief is based on what he sees as moral obscenity, not yet on knowledge of murder. His comment 'methinks I see my father' is an unconscious preparation for the ghost revelation to come.

beat_states.defense_state: Bitter wit and sardonic humor ('Thrift, thrift, Horatio') used to manage unbearable emotional pain — intellectualizing grief through irony
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Desperate need to connect with someone about his pain versus an instinct to deflect with cutting humor; claims to see his father then retreats to 'in my mind's eye,' oscillating between magical thinking and rational control
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'methinks I see my father' could be read as a deliberate test of Horatio — probing whether Horatio has something to tell him, rather than a purely spontaneous expression of grief

beat_states.desire_state: To carefully and honestly report what he knows while gauging Hamlet's emotional state before revealing the ghost sighting
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve and protect Hamlet through loyal truth-telling
beat_states.obstacle: The delicacy of Hamlet's emotional state; uncertainty about how to introduce the shocking news of the ghost; not wanting to seem mad or to further destabilize his friend

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is genuinely sympathetic (negative valence from shared grief) but internally charged with the secret of the ghost (moderate arousal). He maintains composure but is uncertain about when and how to deliver his news. When Hamlet says 'methinks I see my father,' Horatio's startled 'Where, my lord?' reveals a spike in arousal — the uncanny echo of what he actually witnessed.

social_state.rationale: Horatio is deferential ('my lord') but genuinely warm — their relationship transcends court hierarchy. He positions himself as a respectful subordinate who is also an authentic friend.

known_facts: He has seen the ghost of King Hamlet on the battlements
The funeral and wedding were suspiciously close together
Hamlet is in profound grief
King Hamlet was a 'goodly king'
hidden_secrets: He has witnessed the ghost and has come specifically to tell Hamlet about it, not merely for the funeral
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio's stated reason for coming ('to see your father's funeral') is a partial truth — he has also come to report the ghost. His epistemic advantage over Hamlet is significant and creates dramatic tension in every line of this exchange, especially at 'methinks I see my father.'

beat_states.defense_state: Diplomatic restraint — offering measured, careful responses that validate Hamlet's feelings without escalating ('Indeed, my lord, it followed hard upon')
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He possesses urgent, extraordinary news about the ghost but must suppress it while performing the role of ordinary sympathetic visitor; his calm exterior conceals intense anticipation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may genuinely have come for the funeral and only learned about the ghost upon arriving, making his initial statement fully honest rather than strategically incomplete

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: My lord, I think I saw him yesternight.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Saw who?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: My lord, the King your father.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: The King my father?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Season your admiration for a while / With an attent ear, till I may deliver / Upon the witness of these gentlemen / This marvel to you.

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what Horatio means — desperate to confirm the impossible possibility that his father has been seen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To honor his father's memory and restore moral order; this news could transform his paralysis into purpose
beat_states.obstacle: The claim is so extraordinary it defies reason; Hamlet's grief makes him both desperate to believe and terrified of being deceived

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is stunned, thrown off balance. His echoing questions ('Saw who?' / 'The King my father?') reveal a man whose grief is suddenly destabilized by shock. Arousal spikes enormously; certainty is very low; he is profoundly exposed.

social_state.rationale: Though a prince, Hamlet's repeating questions place him in a subordinate epistemic position — Horatio has knowledge Hamlet needs. His warmth toward Horatio remains, as this is the one person he trusts.

known_facts: His father is dead and recently buried
Horatio is a trustworthy scholar not given to fantasy
Horatio claims to have seen the King his father last night
hidden_secrets: His suicidal ideation from his soliloquy moments ago
His deep suspicion and disgust toward Claudius and Gertrude
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of radical epistemic disruption — everything he thought he knew about death and his father's finality is being challenged. He has no information yet about the ghost's appearance or nature.

beat_states.defense_state: Disbelief as protection — repeating Horatio's words back as questions to guard against false hope
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Desperately wants his father back yet cannot rationally accept the possibility of a ghost; the man who just wished for death is suddenly electrified by life
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's echoing could be read as suspicion that Horatio is mocking him or has been sent to test him, though this is unlikely given their established trust.

beat_states.desire_state: To deliver the extraordinary news of the ghost credibly and prepare Hamlet to hear it without dismissing it
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve and protect Hamlet through loyal friendship and honest counsel
beat_states.obstacle: The news is so incredible it risks being dismissed as madness; Hamlet's volatile emotional state makes careful delivery essential

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is nervous about delivering this news but bolstered by having witnesses. He maintains measured composure but is internally anxious about how Hamlet will respond. He saw the ghost himself so has reasonable certainty, but the subject matter is unsettling.

social_state.rationale: Horatio is deferential ('My lord') but steps into a position of epistemic authority — he has knowledge Hamlet desperately needs. His warmth is high; every word is chosen with care for Hamlet's wellbeing.

known_facts: He saw a figure resembling King Hamlet on the battlements last night
Marcellus and Barnardo also witnessed the apparition
The ghost appeared in the King's armor
Hamlet is in deep grief
hidden_secrets: The full terrifying details of the ghost's appearance and behavior
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio possesses critical eyewitness knowledge and strategically withholds details until he has secured Hamlet's full attention and rational engagement.

beat_states.defense_state: Preemptive rhetorical framing — asking Hamlet to 'season his admiration' to control the reception of his claim
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: A rational scholar who must advocate for an irrational supernatural experience; he needs Hamlet to believe something he himself can barely believe
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's careful rhetoric could be read as his own lingering doubt about what he saw — he may be seeking Hamlet's authority to validate his own experience.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: For God’s love, let me hear!

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Two nights together had these gentlemen, / Marcellus and Barnardo, on their watch, / In the dead waste and middle of the night, / Been thus encountered: a figure like your father, / Armed at point exactly, cap-à-pie, / Appears before them and with solemn march / Goes slow and stately by them. Thrice he walked / By their oppressed and fear-surprisèd eyes / Within his truncheon’s length, whilst they, distilled / Almost to jelly with the act of fear, / Stand dumb and speak not to him. This to me / In dreadful secrecy impart they did, / And I with them the third night kept the watch, / Where, as they had delivered, both in time, / Form of the thing ( each word made true and good), / The apparition comes. I knew your father; / These hands are not more like.

beat_states.desire_state: to hear every detail of the ghost sighting immediately, without delay
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to uncover the truth about his father's death and honor his memory
beat_states.obstacle: he is dependent on Horatio's account and cannot verify it himself yet

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is electrified by the news. His single urgent line ('For God's love, let me hear!') reveals a man seized by desperate hope and agitation. His grief has been given a possible outlet. He surrenders control entirely to Horatio's narrative, making himself deeply vulnerable.

social_state.rationale: Though a prince commanding information, Hamlet's urgency strips away formality; he is openly pleading with a trusted friend, elevating Horatio's importance while showing genuine emotional warmth and need.

known_facts: His father is dead
Horatio claims to have seen his father's ghost
Horatio is a trustworthy, rational scholar
hidden_secrets: His deep suspicion and disgust regarding Claudius and Gertrude's marriage
His recent contemplation of suicide
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is transitioning from incredulity to belief. He has not yet heard the full account but Horatio's credibility makes him inclined to accept it.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: desperate hunger for supernatural confirmation of his father's presence vs. rational fear of what it might mean
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's urgency could partly stem from a fear that the ghost signals something terrible about the manner of his father's death, not just longing to see him again.

beat_states.desire_state: to deliver a precise, credible account of the ghost to Hamlet so he will believe it and act
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve and protect Hamlet through loyal truth-telling
beat_states.obstacle: the extraordinary nature of the claim risks disbelief; he must convey something that defies rational explanation while maintaining his scholarly credibility

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is still shaken by what he witnessed but has composed himself into a careful witness. He speaks with controlled gravity, but the memory of the apparition still agitates him. His certainty is high because he saw it himself and it matched the soldiers' account exactly.

social_state.rationale: Horatio positions himself as a faithful servant-scholar delivering testimony to his prince. His warmth is evident in the care he takes — he knows this concerns Hamlet's dead father and handles the account with solemnity and compassion.

known_facts: He personally witnessed the ghost on the third night
Marcellus and Barnardo saw it twice before
The figure was armed head to toe and resembled King Hamlet exactly
The ghost walked solemnly and did not speak
The soldiers were terrified into silence
hidden_secrets: He may suspect the ghost's appearance portends political danger for Denmark
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is an eyewitness with corroborating testimony. He is careful to present what he knows as fact ('each word made true and good') and stakes his personal credibility ('These hands are not more like').

beat_states.defense_state: methodical precision — he builds the account with corroborating witnesses, specific details, and personal verification to shield against any accusation of credulity
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: a rational scholar compelled to vouch for a supernatural event that contradicts his worldview
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may be slightly calibrating his delivery to provoke Hamlet into wanting to see the ghost himself, subtly leading him toward the watch.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: But where was this?

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: My lord, upon the platform where we watch.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Did you not speak to it?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: My lord, I did, / But answer made it none. Yet once methought / It lifted up its head and did address / Itself to motion, like as it would speak; / But even then the morning cock crew loud, / And at the sound it shrunk in haste away / And vanished from our sight.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: ’Tis very strange.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: As I do live, my honored lord, ’tis true. / And we did think it writ down in our duty / To let you know of it.

beat_states.desire_state: to extract every concrete detail about the ghost's appearance and behavior
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to uncover the truth about his father's death and fulfill his duty to his father's memory
beat_states.obstacle: the event is secondhand, already past, and the ghost vanished before communicating its purpose

affect_state.rationale: Intensely agitated beneath a controlled surface. The ghost raises desperate hope mixed with dread. He doesn't yet know what to make of it, so certainty is low. He maintains procedural control through questioning but is emotionally exposed.

social_state.rationale: Operates as prince receiving a report from loyal subjects; his rapid-fire questions assert authority. Warmth is moderate — he trusts Horatio and is grateful but too consumed by the information to express it.

known_facts: A figure resembling his father appeared on the platform where the watch stands
Horatio spoke to it but received no verbal answer
The figure made a motion as if to speak but vanished at the cock's crow
Three witnesses confirm the sighting
hidden_secrets: He has been contemplating suicide
He suspects foul play in his father's death
His grief is far deeper than anyone around him knows
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is rapidly assembling facts. He withholds his own darkest thoughts and suspicions, using the interrogation to build a picture before revealing anything of himself.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectual control — channeling overwhelming emotion into methodical questioning
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: desperate to believe this is his father's spirit yet aware that credulity could be dangerous; his terse 'Tis very strange' suppresses volcanic feeling under a mask of rational composure
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's brevity ('Tis very strange') could indicate skepticism rather than suppressed intensity — he may be testing whether Horatio will oversell the story

beat_states.desire_state: to faithfully and completely convey what he witnessed so Hamlet can judge for himself
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve Hamlet with intellectual honesty and loyal friendship
beat_states.obstacle: the experience defies his own rationalist worldview; he must report something he still struggles to fully credit

affect_state.rationale: He has processed the initial shock and now delivers testimony with measured conviction. Arousal is elevated but contained by scholarly discipline. He stakes his honor on the truth, which increases his certainty even as the content unsettles him.

social_state.rationale: Deferential to Hamlet ('my honored lord') while asserting the gravity of his report. Warmth is genuine — he frames the disclosure as an act of duty born from care.

known_facts: He personally saw and addressed the apparition
The figure resembled the late King Hamlet in armor
It seemed about to speak but fled at the cock's crow
Marcellus and Bernardo also witnessed it
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is an open book here — his entire purpose is transparent disclosure. He has no hidden agenda.

beat_states.defense_state: anchoring credibility in precise factual detail to manage his own discomfort with the supernatural
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: a scholar who has just seen something that overturns his philosophy, swearing on his life to its truth

beat_states.desire_state: to corroborate Horatio's account and demonstrate his reliability to Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty as a loyal soldier and gain the prince's trust and approval
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio is the primary spokesman; Marcellus has limited space to contribute

affect_state.rationale: Steady and dutiful. He is certain of what he saw and where. Low control because the conversation is driven by Hamlet and Horatio. Minimal vulnerability — he is a soldier doing his job.

social_state.rationale: Lowest status in the group — a soldier addressing his prince. Respectful and formal ('My lord'). Warmth is professional rather than personal.

known_facts: The ghost appeared on the platform where the watch is kept
He witnessed the apparition on multiple occasions
Horatio was brought as a credible witness to verify the sighting
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus contributes the concrete spatial detail. He has no hidden knowledge or agenda beyond dutiful reporting.

beat_states.defense_state: deferring to higher-status speakers while asserting his one piece of specific knowledge
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus may also be seeking validation from Hamlet that they were right to come forward, given the extraordinary nature of the claim

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Indeed, sirs, but this troubles me. / Hold you the watch tonight?

utterances.speaker: ALL
utterances.text: We do, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Armed, say you?

utterances.speaker: ALL
utterances.text: Armed, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: From top to toe?

utterances.speaker: ALL
utterances.text: My lord, from head to foot.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Then saw you not his face?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: O, yes, my lord, he wore his beaver up.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What, looked he frowningly?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: A countenance more in sorrow than in anger.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Pale or red?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Nay, very pale.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: And fixed his eyes upon you?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Most constantly.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I would I had been there.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: It would have much amazed you.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Very like. Stayed it long?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: While one with moderate haste might tell a hundred.

utterances.speaker: BARNARDO/ MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Longer, longer.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Not when I saw ’t.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: His beard was grizzled, no?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: It was as I have seen it in his life, / A sable silvered.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I will watch tonight. / Perchance ’twill walk again.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: I warrant it will.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: If it assume my noble father’s person, / I’ll speak to it, though hell itself should gape / And bid me hold my peace. I pray you all, / If you have hitherto concealed this sight, / Let it be tenable in your silence still; / And whatsomever else shall hap tonight, / Give it an understanding but no tongue. / I will requite your loves. So fare you well. / Upon the platform, ’twixt eleven and twelve, / I’ll visit you.

utterances.speaker: ALL
utterances.text: Our duty to your Honor.

beat_states.desire_state: To demonstrate loyalty to Hamlet and pledge obedience to his command for secrecy
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill their duty as loyal subjects and companions to Prince Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: The burden of keeping a terrifying supernatural secret while serving in a court they may not fully trust

affect_state.rationale: Relief at having transferred the burden of knowledge to Hamlet, combined with residual unease about the ghost and the secrecy demanded of them. They are now bound to silence and subordinate to Hamlet's plan.

social_state.rationale: They formally subordinate themselves with 'Our duty to your Honor,' reinforcing the hierarchical relationship while expressing genuine loyalty and respect.

known_facts: They have witnessed the ghost of King Hamlet
Prince Hamlet will come to the platform tonight
They are sworn to secrecy about the apparition
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus, Bernardo, and Horatio collectively share the same witnessed events. They have no additional hidden information beyond what they've reported.

beat_states.defense_state: Submission to authority as a way of managing their own fear and uncertainty
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: They address Hamlet with formal 'duty' language while having just shared an intensely intimate and frightening supernatural experience with him — the formality masks the emotional bond forged by shared terror
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Their formal response could mask private doubts about whether confronting the ghost is wise, or anxiety about the political implications of what they've witnessed at Elsinore.

beat_states.desire_state: To take control of the situation by confronting the ghost himself and ensuring absolute secrecy
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To discover the truth about his father's death and restore moral order
beat_states.obstacle: The supernatural uncertainty of whether the ghost is truly his father or a demonic deception; the danger of others learning about the apparition

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is galvanized — this is the first time since his father's death that he has a concrete course of action. His arousal is high with a mixture of dread and desperate hope. He asserts control through commanding secrecy and setting the rendezvous, but underneath lies the terrifying possibility of confronting his dead father's spirit.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet fully assumes his princely authority here, issuing directives and binding the men to silence. His warmth is moderate — 'I will requite your loves' acknowledges their loyalty with genuine gratitude, but the tone is predominantly commanding and strategic.

known_facts: A ghost resembling his father has appeared on the battlements multiple times
It appeared armed head to foot and looked sorrowful
It appeared between eleven and twelve at night
Horatio, Marcellus, and Bernardo have all witnessed it
hidden_secrets: His deep suspicion that something is rotten in Denmark — possibly foul play in his father's death
His suicidal ideation and disgust at his mother's remarriage
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet now possesses critical new information about the ghost. His insistence on secrecy suggests he already intuits that this apparition may reveal something politically dangerous. He does not yet know what the ghost wants but suspects it bears a message.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — converting overwhelming grief and fear into strategic planning and decisive action
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Declares fearless resolve to speak to the ghost even if hell gapes open, yet simultaneously reveals deep anxiety by insisting on total secrecy — suggesting he fears what this encounter might reveal or unleash
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's commanding tone could be read as performative bravado masking genuine terror — his 'though hell itself should gape' line may be less resolve than self-persuasion, talking himself into courage he doesn't yet feel.

beat_states.desire_state: To support Hamlet and confirm that meeting the ghost is the right course of action
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To faithfully serve and protect his friend Hamlet through loyalty and honest counsel
beat_states.obstacle: He has delivered the disturbing news and now must cede control of the situation to Hamlet, whose emotional state he cannot fully gauge

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is relatively steady — his brief 'I warrant it will' is measured and confident in its factual claim. He is subordinate to Hamlet's authority here and cedes the initiative, but feels the gravity of the situation.

social_state.rationale: Horatio defers to Hamlet's princely authority and plan-making. His warmth is expressed through loyal compliance and his earlier decision to bring this news directly to Hamlet.

known_facts: The ghost appeared three times
It closely resembles the late King Hamlet
It vanished at the cock's crow
Hamlet intends to confront it tonight
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is the most reliable witness and has no hidden agenda. He has shared everything he knows transparently.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His scholarly rationalism has been shattered by witnessing the ghost, yet he now confidently warrants its return — he has crossed from skeptic to believer faster than his intellect can process
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's terse 'I warrant it will' could contain suppressed anxiety — he may fear what will happen when Hamlet confronts the ghost but chooses not to voice his concern.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Your loves, as mine to you. Farewell. / My father’s spirit— in arms! All is not well. / I doubt some foul play. Would the night were come! / Till then, sit still, my soul. Foul deeds will rise, / Though all the earth o’erwhelm them, to men’s eyes.

beat_states.desire_state: to steel himself for the coming night's encounter and contain the explosive suspicion now igniting inside him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to uncover the truth about his father's death and fulfill his duty to his father's memory
beat_states.obstacle: time itself — he must wait for nightfall; also the enormity and uncertainty of what the ghost's appearance implies

affect_state.rationale: The revelation of the ghost in armor floods Hamlet with dread and dark excitement. He suspects foul play but cannot yet confirm it. He attempts to discipline himself ('sit still, my soul') but the high arousal and negative valence betray how shaken he is. His control is low — he's barely containing himself.

social_state.rationale: He dismisses Horatio and the guards with genuine warmth ('Your loves, as mine to you') but immediately turns inward. He is performing princely composure — maintaining status — while his inner world is in freefall. The brief warmth toward his friends is authentic but quickly swallowed by the soliloquy's private anguish.

known_facts: A ghost resembling his father has appeared on the battlements
The ghost appeared armed head to foot in the king's armor
The ghost appeared with a sorrowful expression
His mother married his uncle with suspicious haste
He has arranged to watch tonight on the platform
hidden_secrets: He suspects his father was murdered ('foul play')
He is connecting Claudius's rise to the throne with potential crime
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is leaping from the data of the ghost's armed, sorrowful appearance to an intuitive hypothesis of murder. He does not yet have confirmation, but his suspicion is crystallizing rapidly. The phrase 'all is not well' marks the moment suspicion hardens into near-conviction.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — converting raw grief and terror into a philosophical maxim ('foul deeds will rise') to manage overwhelming emotion
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he is simultaneously desperate for the night to come and terrified of what he will discover; he commands his soul to 'sit still' while every fiber of him is in revolt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: An alternative reading is that Hamlet's 'foul play' suspicion is still unfocused — not yet directed specifically at Claudius but at the general wrongness of the political situation. His couplet could be a general moral pronouncement rather than a personal vow.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: My necessaries are embarked. Farewell. / And, sister, as the winds give benefit / And convey is assistant, do not sleep, / But let me hear from you.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Do you doubt that?

beat_states.desire_state: to secure Ophelia's promise to write to him and to establish a protective connection before departing
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to protect his family's honor and his sister's virtue, maintaining his role as guardian
beat_states.obstacle: he is leaving for France and will lose direct oversight of Ophelia and her relationship with Hamlet

affect_state.rationale: Mildly positive in the warmth of sibling farewell, but underlying anxiety about leaving Ophelia unprotected; moderate arousal from the imminent departure; uncertain about what will happen in his absence; diminished control as he's about to lose proximity

social_state.rationale: Occupies the elder brother/protector role, asserting gentle authority; genuine warmth toward his sister tempered by a paternalistic stance

known_facts: He is departing for France imminently
Ophelia has a relationship or connection with Prince Hamlet
His belongings are already aboard the ship
hidden_secrets: He harbors deep concern about Hamlet's intentions toward Ophelia that he has not yet fully voiced
epistemic_state.rationale: The request for letters is a prelude to the larger warning he is about to deliver; he knows more about court dynamics and Hamlet's attentions than he has yet revealed in this beat

beat_states.defense_state: displacement — channeling his deeper anxiety about Ophelia and Hamlet into a mundane request for letters
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he frames a controlling impulse as casual brotherly affection
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The opening lines could be purely transactional — a genuine simple farewell with no hidden anxiety — but the scene's trajectory strongly suggests this is a setup for his warning about Hamlet

beat_states.desire_state: to affirm her loyalty and closeness to her brother while asserting that his doubt is unnecessary
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be recognized as trustworthy and capable of managing her own life and affections
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes' request implicitly doubts her reliability and foreshadows further paternalistic control

affect_state.rationale: Warm and relatively at ease in this moment; low arousal as this is familiar sibling territory; fairly certain of her own faithfulness; moderate control — she can respond freely here but senses she may be about to be lectured

social_state.rationale: Lower status as the younger sister being instructed, but high warmth — genuine affection in the playful retort

known_facts: Laertes is leaving for France
She has a connection with Hamlet
Her brother cares about her well-being
hidden_secrets: The depth of her feelings for Hamlet and possibly the extent of their private interactions
false_beliefs: She may believe Hamlet's affections are sincere and lasting
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia is aware of the family dynamic but may not yet anticipate the full weight of the warning Laertes is about to deliver; she likely underestimates the political dimension of Hamlet's courtship

beat_states.defense_state: deflection through light teasing — turning his earnest request into something slightly absurd
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she pushes back with confidence here, but her deeper pattern is compliance and obedience to male authority figures
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Her 'Do you doubt that?' could carry a note of genuine hurt rather than playful challenge, suggesting she already feels surveilled and mistrusted

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: For Hamlet, and the trifling of his favor, / Hold it a fashion and a toy in blood, / A violet in the youth of primy nature, / Forward, not permanent, sweet, not lasting, / The perfume and suppliance of a minute, / No more.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: No more but so?

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Think it no more. / For nature, crescent, does not grow alone / In thews and bulk, but, as this temple waxes, / The inward service of the mind and soul / Grows wide withal. Perhaps he loves you now, / And now no soil nor cautel doth besmirch / The virtue of his will; but you must fear, / His greatness weighed, his will is not his own, / For he himself is subject to his birth. / He may not, as unvalued persons do, / Carve for himself, for on his choice depends / The safety and the health of this whole state. / And therefore must his choice be circumscribed / Unto the voice and yielding of that body / Whereof he is the head.
