Then, if he says he loves you, / It fits your wisdom so far to believe it / As he in his particular act and place / May give his saying deed, which is no further / Than the main voice of Denmark goes withal. / Then weigh what loss your honor may sustain / If with too credent ear you list his songs / Or lose your heart or your chaste treasure open / To his unmastered importunity. / Fear it, Ophelia; fear it, my dear sister, / And keep you in the rear of your affection, / Out of the shot and danger of desire. / The chariest maid is prodigal enough / If she unmask her beauty to the moon. / Virtue itself ’scapes not calumnious strokes. / The canker galls the infants of the spring / Too oft before their buttons be disclosed, / And, in the morn and liquid dew of youth, / Contagious blastments are most imminent. / Be wary, then; best safety lies in fear. / Youth to itself rebels, though none else near.

beat_states.desire_state: To convince Ophelia to guard herself against Hamlet's advances and protect her chastity and honor
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect the family honor and maintain his role as guardian/patriarch in his father's stead
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's emotional attachment to Hamlet and her possible naivety about the political realities of Hamlet's position

affect_state.rationale: He speaks with mounting rhetorical urgency and passion — the sheer length and escalating imagery (from violets to cankers, perfume to contagious blastments) reveals genuine anxiety beneath his confident posture. He is frightened for her but performs authority.

social_state.rationale: Assumes the higher-status role of elder brother/protector dispensing wisdom, but the repeated 'dear sister' and tender natural imagery reveal genuine warmth and affection underneath the patriarchal lecturing

known_facts: Hamlet has been showing favor/attention to Ophelia
Hamlet is a prince whose marriage choices are constrained by state affairs
He is about to depart for France and will not be present to watch over Ophelia
A woman's reputation is fragile and easily damaged in this court
hidden_secrets: His own anxieties about losing influence over his sister while abroad
Possible awareness of his own double standard regarding sexual conduct
false_beliefs: That Hamlet's feelings are necessarily shallow or purely driven by youthful appetite
That Ophelia is too naive to judge the situation for herself
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes makes political arguments that are sound regarding Hamlet's constrained position, but assumes the worst about Hamlet's sincerity and underestimates Ophelia's capacity for judgment

beat_states.defense_state: Projection — channeling his own anxieties about leaving home and losing control over his sister's virtue into an elaborate rhetorical performance of worldly wisdom
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to respect Hamlet ('Perhaps he loves you now') while systematically reducing Hamlet's affection to mere appetite and political impossibility; his protective love for Ophelia borders on controlling possession
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may be partly motivated by jealousy or possessiveness over Ophelia rather than pure brotherly concern — his obsessive focus on her sexual purity could signal displaced anxieties about masculinity and family honor

beat_states.desire_state: To understand whether her brother is right and to defend the validity of her feelings for Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be loved authentically and to navigate between her own desires and the demands of her family
beat_states.obstacle: The weight of her brother's authority and argument, combined with her genuine uncertainty about Hamlet's true intentions

affect_state.rationale: Her single line carries a wounded quality — 'No more but so?' reveals hurt at having her relationship reduced to a 'trifle.' She is deeply exposed: her feelings are being publicly dissected, and she has little power to counter her brother's authority

social_state.rationale: Subordinate in the sibling dynamic — she is the younger sister being lectured. Her warmth is present but strained; she listens dutifully but her question carries a quiet sting of resistance

known_facts: Hamlet has expressed love/favor toward her
Her brother disapproves of the relationship
Hamlet is a prince with political obligations
hidden_secrets: The depth of her emotional and possibly physical involvement with Hamlet
Her private belief that Hamlet's love may be genuine
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia has direct experience of Hamlet's attentions but lacks the political worldliness her brother claims. Her question suggests she holds knowledge of the relationship that contradicts his dismissal.

beat_states.defense_state: Quiet deflection through brevity — her single pointed question ('No more but so?') challenges his dismissiveness without openly rebelling
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She wants to believe Hamlet's love is real but cannot entirely dismiss her brother's warnings; her brevity masks the depth of her emotional investment
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia's brevity could indicate she has already internalized doubt about Hamlet and is seeking confirmation rather than resisting — her 'No more but so?' might be genuine despair rather than challenge

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: I shall the effect of this good lesson keep / As watchman to my heart. But, good my brother, / Do not, as some ungracious pastors do, / Show me the steep and thorny way to heaven, / Whiles, like a puffed and reckless libertine, / Himself the primrose path of dalliance treads / And recks not his own rede.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: O, fear me not. / I stay too long. But here my father comes. / A double blessing is a double grace. / Occasion smiles upon a second leave.

beat_states.desire_state: to deflect Ophelia's counterattack gracefully and exit with his authority intact
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his role as protective older brother and uphold family honor before departing
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's retort has landed — she has exposed his hypocrisy — and he needs to save face while also genuinely needing to leave

affect_state.rationale: Slightly stung but buoyant — the arrival of Polonius rescues him from having to respond substantively. He is genuinely pleased to see his father, which elevates his mood. The quick pivot suggests slight discomfort he's covering with warmth.

social_state.rationale: He reasserts gentle elder-brother status by being magnanimous rather than defensive. His warmth toward both Ophelia and his arriving father is genuine — he is a young man full of feeling at the moment of departure.

known_facts: Ophelia's accusation of hypocrisy is at least partially true
He is about to depart for France
Polonius is approaching
hidden_secrets: His own libertine behavior in Paris, which Ophelia has intuited
false_beliefs: He may believe his advice to Ophelia is purely protective rather than partly controlling
epistemic_state.rationale: He is self-aware enough to not deny Ophelia's charge but not self-aware enough to see how his protectiveness mirrors patriarchal control.

beat_states.defense_state: breezy deflection — 'O, fear me not' brushes off her challenge without engaging its substance; pivoting to Polonius's arrival redirects attention
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He knows Ophelia's charge has merit (he likely does tread the 'primrose path') but cannot acknowledge it without undermining the advice he just gave
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: His 'O, fear me not' could be entirely sincere — he may genuinely believe he will be virtuous in Paris, making Ophelia's charge feel unwarranted rather than accurate.

beat_states.desire_state: to assert her own dignity and deflect Laertes' patronizing advice back onto him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be recognized as a whole person with her own moral agency, not merely a vessel to be guarded
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes holds social authority as older brother and male; she cannot openly reject his counsel

affect_state.rationale: She is mildly amused and emboldened by her own cleverness, but still operating under the constraint of filial obedience. There is real feeling for Hamlet beneath her compliance, creating underlying vulnerability.

social_state.rationale: She remains subordinate as younger sister but the warmth is genuine — this is sibling teasing, affectionate even as it stings. She pushes back from a lower-status position with humor rather than rebellion.

known_facts: Laertes has warned her about Hamlet's intentions
Laertes is a young man in Paris likely engaging in his own dalliances
She has genuine feelings for Hamlet
hidden_secrets: The depth of her attachment to Hamlet may be greater than she lets on
epistemic_state.rationale: She knows her brother well enough to call out his hypocrisy. She understands the double standard being applied but cannot fully name it.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection through wit — turning moral scrutiny back on the advisor
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she accepts the lesson outwardly ('I shall the effect of this good lesson keep') while simultaneously undermining his authority to give it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: She may be entirely sincere in accepting the lesson and only gently teasing — the challenge may be lighter than it reads, more playful than resistant.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Yet here, Laertes? Aboard, aboard, for shame! / The wind sits in the shoulder of your sail, / And you are stayed for. There, my blessing with thee. / And these few precepts in thy memory / Look thou character. Give thy thoughts no tongue, / Nor any unproportioned thought his act. / Be thou familiar, but by no means vulgar. / Those friends thou hast, and their adoption tried, / Grapple them unto thy soul with hoops of steel, / But do not dull thy palm with entertainment / Of each new-hatched, unfledged courage. Beware / Of entrance to a quarrel, but, being in, / Bear ’t that th’ opposèd may beware of thee. / Give every man thy ear, but few thy voice. / Take each man’s censure, but reserve thy judgment. / Costly thy habit as thy purse can buy, / But not expressed in fancy ( rich, not gaudy), / For the apparel oft proclaims the man, / And they in France of the best rank and station / Are of a most select and generous chief in that. / Neither a borrower nor a lender be, / For loan oft loses both itself and friend, / And borrowing dulls the edge of husbandry. / This above all: to thine own self be true, / And it must follow, as the night the day, / Thou canst not then be false to any man. / Farewell. My blessing season this in thee.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Most humbly do I take my leave, my lord.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: The time invests you. Go, your servants tend.

beat_states.desire_state: To depart with his father's blessing and fulfill the filial duty of respectful leave-taking
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To honor his family name while pursuing his own independence and worldly experience
beat_states.obstacle: His father's lengthy sermon delays departure and demands patient deference when Laertes is eager to go — and has just been advising Ophelia himself

affect_state.rationale: Mildly positive from receiving a blessing, but restrained. Low control — he is subject to his father's monologue and cannot interrupt. Moderate arousal from the anticipation of departure. Some vulnerability in being publicly positioned as the child receiving instruction.

social_state.rationale: Deliberately lowered status ('Most humbly') in deference to his father. Warmth is genuine but contained — he respects and loves Polonius but the dynamic is hierarchical, not intimate.

known_facts: His ship is waiting and servants attend him
He has his father's blessing
He has warned Ophelia about Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He may privately regard some of his father's advice as obvious or excessive
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is in a straightforward epistemic position — he knows the practical situation and his family dynamics. His inner life in this beat is largely reactive.

beat_states.defense_state: Compliance — performing filial humility ('Most humbly do I take my leave') to expedite the interaction and avoid conflict
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has just spent the prior beats lecturing Ophelia with his own worldly advice, mirroring exactly the paternal authority he now must patiently endure — he is both his father's critic and his father's copy
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may be more genuinely moved by his father's blessing than his terse reply suggests — 'Most humbly' could reflect real emotion compressed by the urgency of departure rather than mere politeness.

beat_states.desire_state: To imprint his worldly wisdom onto Laertes before departure, securing his son's conduct as a reflection of the family's honor
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain control over his children and his family's reputation at court
beat_states.obstacle: Time is running out — the ship is waiting and Laertes must leave immediately, compressing Polonius's need to instruct into a rushed moment

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is in his element — performing the role of wise patriarch. Positive valence from the authority position, high certainty in his own counsel, high sense of control. But underneath the rehearsed maxims is a father sending a son away, with a flicker of vulnerability he won't acknowledge.

social_state.rationale: He occupies the dominant patriarchal position, dispensing wisdom from above. Warmth is moderate — there is genuine affection in the blessing ('My blessing season this in thee') but it is filtered through performative authority and self-regard rather than emotional intimacy.

known_facts: Laertes is departing for France imminently
The ship and servants are waiting
French court culture demands a certain presentation
Laertes is young and potentially susceptible to bad company and excess
hidden_secrets: He will later send Reynaldo to spy on Laertes in France, suggesting he does not fully trust his own advice to hold
false_beliefs: He believes his string of maxims constitutes genuine wisdom rather than conventional platitudes
He believes he can control Laertes's behavior through verbal instruction alone
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius operates with high confidence in his own sagacity but his later actions (spying) reveal an unconscious awareness that words are insufficient to govern his son.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — converting genuine parental anxiety about losing control of his son into a polished catalog of maxims, keeping emotion at arm's length
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He scolds Laertes for delay ('Aboard, aboard, for shame!') yet is the one who prolongs the departure with his lengthy speech — his need to control overrides his stated urgency
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Polonius is genuinely moved by the departure and the maxims, though clichéd, represent his authentic best effort at love — the performance IS his love language, not a substitute for it.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Farewell, Ophelia, and remember well / What I have said to you.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: ’Tis in my memory locked, / And you yourself shall keep the key of it.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Farewell.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure Ophelia's promise to heed his warning about Hamlet before he departs
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect his family's honor and his sister's virtue
beat_states.obstacle: He is running out of time — the ship is waiting and Polonius has already scolded him for lingering

affect_state.rationale: The farewell is tender but tinged with worry; he is losing direct influence over Ophelia the moment he boards the ship, and his repeated 'remember well' reveals anxiety that his words may not hold

social_state.rationale: He speaks as protective elder brother, asserting gentle authority, but the warmth of sibling love dominates this final exchange

known_facts: Hamlet has been courting Ophelia
He has warned her at length about Hamlet's intentions
Their father has just reinforced similar warnings
The ship is waiting and he must leave immediately
hidden_secrets: His own potential hypocrisy regarding libertine behavior abroad, as Ophelia hinted at
false_beliefs: That his warning alone can shield Ophelia from Hamlet's influence
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes believes his counsel has been sufficient but cannot know whether Ophelia will truly follow it once he is gone

beat_states.defense_state: Compression of anxiety into a final authoritative directive
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants to linger and ensure Ophelia's safety, but must leave and surrender control over her choices to her and their father
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The repetition of 'farewell' and the command to remember could also be read as Laertes performing brotherly duty more than feeling genuine concern — fulfilling a role before departure

beat_states.desire_state: To reassure Laertes and end the conversation gracefully without revealing the depth of her feelings for Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To navigate between obedience to her family and her authentic desire for Hamlet's love
beat_states.obstacle: She has been counseled by both brother and father to distrust Hamlet, creating internal pressure against her own heart

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is emotionally contained but uncertain; she is being told how to feel by everyone around her, and her compliance masks confusion and suppressed longing

social_state.rationale: She occupies the subordinate position — younger sister, obedient daughter — but responds with genuine affection and grace

known_facts: Laertes and Polonius both warn her against Hamlet
Hamlet has made tenders of affection to her
Laertes is departing for France
hidden_secrets: The true depth of her feelings for Hamlet
Her potential doubt about whether her brother's and father's counsel is correct
false_beliefs: That obedience to family will resolve her internal conflict
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia knows the facts of everyone's positions but is trapped between external authority and private feeling; her epistemic world is shaped by others' interpretations rather than her own judgment

beat_states.defense_state: Compliance as a deflection — by locking the lesson in memory she closes the conversation without revealing her true feelings
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She promises to remember and obey, but the locked-memory metaphor subtly externalizes responsibility — 'you shall keep the key' places the burden of enforcement on Laertes, not on herself
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia's response could be read as entirely sincere and dutiful — she genuinely believes she should listen to Laertes and has no internal resistance, making her later tragedy one of pure obedience rather than suppressed desire

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: What is ’t, Ophelia, he hath said to you?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: So please you, something touching the Lord Hamlet.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Marry, well bethought. / ’Tis told me he hath very oft of late / Given private time to you, and you yourself / Have of your audience been most free and bounteous. / If it be so ( as so ’tis put on me, / And that in way of caution), I must tell you / You do not understand yourself so clearly / As it behooves my daughter and your honor. / What is between you? Give me up the truth.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: He hath, my lord, of late made many tenders / Of his affection to me.

beat_states.desire_state: to answer her father truthfully without betraying Hamlet or incurring her father's wrath
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be a good and obedient daughter while preserving the authentic love she feels from Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: her father's intimidating interrogation and the impossible position of loyalty to Hamlet versus obedience to Polonius

affect_state.rationale: Caught between two men's demands on her loyalty, she is anxious, uncertain of how much to reveal, and acutely aware she has no power in this exchange. Her careful phrasing reveals high self-monitoring under pressure.

social_state.rationale: Deeply subordinate — addresses him as 'my lord,' uses 'so please you,' performs deference at every turn. Yet there is a residual warmth in her willingness to be honest about Hamlet's affection.

known_facts: Hamlet has repeatedly expressed affection to her
Both Laertes and now Polonius are suspicious of Hamlet's intentions
She has been meeting with Hamlet privately
hidden_secrets: The depth of her own emotional attachment to Hamlet
Possibly the specific content and intimacy of their private meetings
false_beliefs: That Hamlet's 'tenders of affection' are straightforwardly sincere and will lead to honorable courtship
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia knows the facts of the courtship firsthand but lacks the political awareness to see how others interpret it; she believes in Hamlet's sincerity where her father and brother do not.

beat_states.defense_state: minimization — uses careful, formal language ('tenders of his affection') to present Hamlet's courtship as respectful and dignified rather than illicit
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: wants to defend Hamlet's sincerity and her own feelings, yet her conditioning toward obedience makes her frame the truth in terms that already cede authority to her father
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may be more strategically aware than she appears — her careful word choice ('tenders') could be a deliberate attempt to frame the relationship in elevated, honorable terms to protect both herself and Hamlet.

beat_states.desire_state: to extract the full truth about Ophelia's relationship with Hamlet and assert parental control over her behavior
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his family's position at court by managing his children as extensions of his political influence
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's reticence and the possibility she has already compromised her honor without his knowledge

affect_state.rationale: Agitated by the threat to family honor and his reputation at court, but feels firmly in command of the paternal interrogation. Some underlying anxiety about what he doesn't yet know.

social_state.rationale: Wielding full patriarchal authority; his tone is admonishing and cold — 'you do not understand yourself so clearly' — rather than warm or protective. He speaks as a judge, not a comforter.

known_facts: Hamlet has been spending private time with Ophelia
Others at court have reported this to him
Laertes just whispered something to Ophelia about Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He may intend to use Ophelia's relationship with Hamlet as political currency depending on the outcome
false_beliefs: That Ophelia lacks the intelligence to understand her own situation without his guidance
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius has secondhand intelligence but needs direct confirmation; his demand 'Give me up the truth' reveals he knows enough to be alarmed but not enough to be certain of the details.

beat_states.defense_state: preemptive authority — frames the conversation as a matter of honor and duty before she can defend herself
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: claims to be receiving information cautiously ('put on me, in way of caution') while already having decided Ophelia has behaved improperly
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may be genuinely frightened for Ophelia's safety and honor rather than purely controlling — his sharpness could mask real parental fear about a prince's intentions toward his daughter.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Affection, puh! You speak like a green girl / Unsifted in such perilous circumstance. / Do you believe his “ tenders,” as you call them?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: I do not know, my lord, what I should think.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Marry, I will teach you. Think yourself a baby / That you have ta’en these tenders for true pay, / Which are not sterling. Tender yourself more dearly, / Or ( not to crack the wind of the poor phrase, / Running it thus) you’ll tender me a fool.

beat_states.desire_state: To find a way to reconcile her own feelings for Hamlet with the overwhelming pressure from her father and brother
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be a dutiful daughter while preserving some authentic selfhood and emotional truth
beat_states.obstacle: Her father's domineering authority and rhetorical aggression leave no space for her own perspective

affect_state.rationale: Deeply negative valence from being shamed and infantilized. Moderate arousal — she is distressed but suppressing it. Extremely low certainty and control as her father systematically dismantles her capacity to trust her own judgment.

social_state.rationale: Reduced to the status of a child ('green girl,' 'baby'). She offers mild warmth through compliance and deference but receives none in return.

known_facts: Hamlet has expressed love to her
Both her brother and father warn her against believing Hamlet
She is expected to obey her father absolutely
hidden_secrets: She likely believes in the sincerity of Hamlet's affection but cannot voice this
Her private emotional life with Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia is being epistemically colonized — her own knowledge of Hamlet's behavior is being overwritten by her father's interpretation. Her 'I do not know' may reflect genuine epistemic collapse under pressure.

beat_states.defense_state: Strategic self-erasure — 'I do not know what I should think' hands the power to him, deflecting his anger by becoming a blank slate
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She likely does believe Hamlet's tenders are genuine but cannot say so; her statement of not knowing what to think may be both honest confusion and self-protective submission
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may genuinely be uncertain and relieved to have her father tell her what to think, having been destabilized by Laertes' earlier warning

beat_states.desire_state: To crush Ophelia's belief in Hamlet's affections and assert total control over her romantic life
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain and advance his family's position at court by controlling every variable, especially his daughter's sexuality
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's naïve attachment to Hamlet and her hesitancy to fully submit to his interpretation

affect_state.rationale: Agitated and dismissive, fully certain he is right. High arousal from indignation at her naivety. Feels firmly in control of the conversation and uses rhetorical flourishes (the extended 'tender' wordplay) as demonstrations of dominance.

social_state.rationale: Exercising maximum patriarchal authority. Warmth is actively negative — calling her a 'green girl' and 'baby' is deliberately belittling. He is instructing, not nurturing.

known_facts: Hamlet has made 'tenders' of affection to Ophelia
Laertes also warned Ophelia about Hamlet
He holds absolute authority over Ophelia's choices
Hamlet is a prince and therefore his romantic promises may be politically strategic
hidden_secrets: His primary concern is his own reputation and political exposure, not Ophelia's emotional wellbeing
false_beliefs: That Hamlet's affections are entirely counterfeit and calculated
That his worldly cynicism constitutes wisdom rather than projection
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius operates from courtier's cynicism — he assumes all expressions of love from a prince are tactical. He may be wrong about Hamlet's sincerity, but his court experience makes him confident.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual superiority — using wordplay and rhetorical dominance to maintain distance from any emotional vulnerability about his daughter
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to protect Ophelia's honor but is primarily protecting his own reputation and political standing; his 'tender me a fool' reveals his true fear is being made to look foolish, not her wellbeing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius could be genuinely terrified for Ophelia's future if she is seduced and abandoned by a prince, and his harshness masks real paternal fear

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: My lord, he hath importuned me with love / In honorable fashion—

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Ay, “ fashion” you may call it. Go to, go to!

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: And hath given countenance to his speech, my lord, / With almost all the holy vows of heaven.

beat_states.desire_state: to defend Hamlet's sincerity and legitimize her experience of his courtship
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be allowed to love and be loved authentically
beat_states.obstacle: her father's dismissiveness and superior authority over her

affect_state.rationale: She is emotionally exposed, pleading her case from a position of weakness. Each interruption by Polonius destabilizes her further, but she still pushes back with what evidence she has — the holy vows. Arousal is moderate as she summons courage to speak against her father's dismissals.

social_state.rationale: Deeply subordinate to her father — she addresses him as 'my lord' and must beg for credibility. Her warmth reflects her earnest, non-combative nature even in disagreement.

known_facts: Hamlet has courted her with declarations of love
Hamlet has sworn holy vows to her
Her father and brother both distrust Hamlet's intentions
She is expected to obey her father
hidden_secrets: The depth of her own feelings for Hamlet, which she has not fully articulated
false_beliefs: Hamlet's vows are reliable proof of his genuine and lasting love
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia has direct experience of Hamlet's courtship but lacks the worldly cynicism to question whether vows can be strategically deployed. She takes sacred language at face value.

beat_states.defense_state: appeal to honor — framing Hamlet's advances as 'honorable' and religiously sanctioned to counter her father's cynicism
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she genuinely believes in Hamlet's sincerity yet each time her father mocks her, her certainty wavers — she is caught between her own lived experience and her father's worldly authority
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may already harbor private doubts about Hamlet's sincerity but defends him out of emotional need rather than genuine conviction

beat_states.desire_state: to demolish Ophelia's naive faith in Hamlet's courtship and reassert parental control
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to manage his family's reputation and proximity to power by controlling his children's behavior
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's persistent belief in Hamlet's sincerity, which resists his cynical framing

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is irritated but firmly in command. His certainty is high — he has already decided Hamlet's intentions are dishonorable. His 'Go to, go to!' shows impatience and dismissive authority. Very low vulnerability; he is the patriarch exercising power.

social_state.rationale: He holds absolute authority in this dynamic as father and lord of the household. His warmth is negative — he is condescending and derisive, not nurturing. The mocking repetition ('Go to, go to!') signals contempt for her naivety.

known_facts: Hamlet has been courting Ophelia
Laertes also warned Ophelia about Hamlet
Hamlet is a prince whose marriage prospects are not his own to determine
Young men make false promises to seduce women
hidden_secrets: He may intend to use Ophelia's relationship with Hamlet as political currency with the King
false_beliefs: He is certain Hamlet's vows are entirely calculated and insincere
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius operates from courtly experience and a transactional worldview. His certainty about Hamlet's duplicity is based on generalized cynicism rather than specific evidence, making it potentially a false belief — Hamlet's feelings may be or have been genuine.

beat_states.defense_state: cynical deflection — seizing on Ophelia's own word ('fashion') and weaponizing it to undermine her argument before she can finish
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he positions himself as protecting Ophelia's honor, yet his interruptions reveal a need for dominance and intellectual superiority that has little to do with her wellbeing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may partly fear that Hamlet IS sincere, which would be politically dangerous — a genuine attachment between a prince and his daughter could expose the family to court intrigue and royal displeasure

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Ay, springes to catch woodcocks. I do know, / When the blood burns, how prodigal the soul / Lends the tongue vows. These blazes, daughter, / Giving more light than heat, extinct in both / Even in their promise as it is a-making, / You must not take for fire. From this time / Be something scanter of your maiden presence. / Set your entreatments at a higher rate / Than a command to parle. For Lord Hamlet, / Believe so much in him that he is young, / And with a larger tether may he walk / Than may be given you. In few, Ophelia, / Do not believe his vows, for they are brokers, / Not of that dye which their investments show, / But mere implorators of unholy suits, / Breathing like sanctified and pious bawds / The better to beguile. This is for all: / I would not, in plain terms, from this time forth / Have you so slander any moment leisure / As to give words or talk with the Lord Hamlet. / Look to ’t, I charge you. Come your ways.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: I shall obey, my lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To end the painful confrontation and retreat from her father's overwhelming authority
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be loved and to navigate between the men who control her life — her father and Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Her father's absolute prohibition directly contradicts her feelings for Hamlet; she has no power to resist or negotiate

affect_state.rationale: The brevity of 'I shall obey, my lord' after Polonius's torrent signals emotional shutdown. She is deeply hurt and confused — her certainty about Hamlet's love has been systematically dismantled by both Laertes and Polonius. She has zero agency. The flatness of her response registers profound helplessness.

social_state.rationale: Completely subordinated. Her status is at its lowest — she is a daughter receiving orders. The slight warmth reflects residual filial loyalty and the habit of dutiful affection, but it is drained of genuine connection.

known_facts: Hamlet has declared love to her in honorable fashion
Both her brother and father insist Hamlet's vows are false
She is forbidden from speaking with Hamlet
hidden_secrets: She likely still believes in Hamlet's sincerity despite her compliance
The depth of her emotional attachment to Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia is caught between her direct experience of Hamlet's tenderness and the authoritative claims of her family. She has not been given any actual evidence that Hamlet is insincere — only assertions. Her epistemic state is genuinely destabilized.

beat_states.defense_state: Suppression — she compresses all her feelings, doubts, grief, and potential resistance into five monosyllabic words of compliance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She obeys outwardly while her inner world — her love for Hamlet, her belief in his sincerity which she just defended — remains unresolved and now forcibly silenced
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may already be internalizing her father's view and beginning to doubt Hamlet, making her obedience less conflicted and more genuinely persuaded — the seed of the doubt that will make her complicit in the nunnery scene

beat_states.desire_state: To absolutely forbid Ophelia from any further contact with Hamlet and ensure her total compliance
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain control over his household and protect his family's political standing at court
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's naïve belief in Hamlet's sincerity and her emotional attachment to the prince

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is agitated beneath his rhetorical flourishes — the escalating imagery (springes, brokers, bawds) reveals genuine alarm — but he feels completely in command of the situation and utterly certain of his worldview. His final 'I charge you' is a naked exercise of patriarchal authority.

social_state.rationale: Maximum patriarchal dominance; he speaks not as a nurturing father but as an authority issuing edicts. The warmth is slightly negative — his language is dismissive ('springes to catch woodcocks' mocks her credulity) and his final command brooks no discussion.

known_facts: Hamlet has pursued Ophelia with vows of love
Hamlet is a prince with greater social freedom than Ophelia
Young men's passions are often fleeting
Ophelia's sexual reputation is a family political asset
hidden_secrets: His primary concern is political positioning rather than Ophelia's emotional well-being
false_beliefs: Hamlet's affections are purely predatory and insincere
He fully understands the situation and has complete wisdom about it
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius operates from a cynical, reductive framework about male desire that may be partially correct in general but potentially wrong about Hamlet specifically. His certainty blinds him to nuance.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — transforms a potentially vulnerable situation (his daughter's seduction) into a series of worldly maxims and metaphors to maintain emotional distance and authority
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to be protecting Ophelia's honor and well-being, but is primarily motivated by political self-preservation and treating her as a commodity whose value must be managed
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may genuinely fear for Ophelia's ruin and is acting from real paternal love buried under courtier habits — his verbosity masking anxiety rather than mere pomposity

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: The air bites shrewdly; it is very cold.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: It is a nipping and an eager air.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What hour now?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: I think it lacks of twelve.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: No, it is struck.

beat_states.desire_state: to endure the wait and confirm the Ghost's appearance at the appointed hour
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to discover the truth about his father's death and fulfill his duty to his father's memory
beat_states.obstacle: the uncertainty of whether the Ghost will appear, the cold and darkness amplifying his anxiety

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is anxious and exposed on the cold battlements, waiting for something supernatural. His small talk betrays nervous energy. He is not in control of the situation — he is at the mercy of whether or not the Ghost chooses to appear.

social_state.rationale: As prince, he holds inherent authority, but his manner here is companionable and equal with Horatio and Marcellus — he needs their support and solidarity in this vigil.

known_facts: Horatio and Marcellus have reported seeing a Ghost resembling his father
They have arranged to meet on the platform at midnight
His father is dead and his mother has remarried Claudius
hidden_secrets: The depth of his suspicion and grief about his father's death
His revulsion toward Claudius and Gertrude's marriage
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has been told about the Ghost but has not yet seen it himself. He comes with hope and dread but no confirmed knowledge of foul play yet.

beat_states.defense_state: deflect — masking his dread and anticipation with small talk about the weather and time
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: outwardly casual and conversational while inwardly consumed with existential dread about encountering his dead father's spirit
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's comment about the biting air could be read as a more loaded, metaphorical statement about the corruption in Denmark itself — the cold as moral atmosphere.

beat_states.desire_state: to support Hamlet through the vigil and provide reliable, rational companionship
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve and protect Hamlet through loyal friendship and honest counsel
beat_states.obstacle: the supernatural nature of what he has witnessed challenges his scholarly rationalism

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is uneasy but composed. He echoes Hamlet's observation about the cold to match his friend's register, maintaining calm solidarity. His uncertainty about the time ('I think it lacks of twelve') shows subtle nervousness beneath his steady exterior.

social_state.rationale: Horatio is deferential to the prince but their bond is warm and genuine. He mirrors Hamlet's language, showing attunement and loyalty.

known_facts: He has personally witnessed the Ghost on a previous night
The Ghost resembled the late King Hamlet
He reported the sighting to Hamlet and arranged this vigil
false_beliefs: He slightly misjudges the hour, thinking it is not yet midnight
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is an honest and transparent character. His slight error about the time reveals he is more unsettled than he lets on.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualize — keeping responses measured and factual to manage his own unease
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: a rational scholar who has already seen a ghost and must reconcile that experience with his worldview

beat_states.desire_state: to establish the facts and confirm the moment of potential supernatural encounter
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty as a loyal soldier and witness to extraordinary events
beat_states.obstacle: his subordinate rank means he must assert information carefully in the presence of the prince

affect_state.rationale: Marcellus is on edge but certain about the time — his correction of Horatio shows alertness and nervous attentiveness. He knows from experience that midnight is the critical hour.

social_state.rationale: As a soldier in the presence of the prince and a scholar, Marcellus is lower in status, but his direct correction of Horatio shows he is confident in his role as experienced watchman on this platform.

known_facts: He has seen the Ghost on multiple occasions
The Ghost appears around midnight
The clock has struck twelve
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus is the most experienced witness of the Ghost's appearances and is attuned to the timing. His correction is based on direct sensory knowledge.

beat_states.defense_state: ground — anchoring himself in concrete fact (the clock has struck) to manage fear
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: a dutiful soldier who has seen something that terrifies him but must remain at his post
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus's correction could be read as urgency — alerting the group that the Ghost's hour has arrived and they must be ready.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Indeed, I heard it not. It then draws near the season / Wherein the spirit held his wont to walk. / What does this mean, my lord?
utterances.stage_direction: A flourish of trumpets and two pieces goes off.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: The King doth wake tonight and takes his rouse, / Keeps wassail, and the swagg’ring upspring reels; / And, as he drains his draughts of Rhenish down, / The kettledrum and trumpet thus bray out / The triumph of his pledge.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Is it a custom?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ay, marry, is ’t, / But, to my mind, though I am native here / And to the manner born, it is a custom / More honored in the breach than the observance. / This heavy-headed revel east and west / Makes us traduced and taxed of other nations. / They clepe us drunkards and with swinish phrase / Soil our addition. And, indeed, it takes / From our achievements, though performed at height, / The pith and marrow of our attribute. / So oft it chances in particular men / That for some vicious mole of nature in them, / As in their birth ( wherein they are not guilty, / Since nature cannot choose his origin), / By the o’ergrowth of some complexion / ( Oft breaking down the pales and forts of reason), / Or by some habit that too much o’erleavens / The form of plausive manners— that these men, / Carrying, I say, the stamp of one defect, / Being nature’s livery or fortune’s star, / His virtues else, be they as pure as grace, / As infinite as man may undergo, / Shall in the general censure take corruption / From that particular fault. The dram of evil / Doth all the noble substance of a doubt / To his own scandal.

beat_states.desire_state: To express his contempt for Claudius's debauchery and morally distance himself from the corrupt Danish court while waiting for the Ghost
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To restore moral order and honor his father's memory against the corruption Claudius represents
beat_states.obstacle: He is trapped as a native Dane, implicated in the very culture he despises; he cannot yet act against Claudius

affect_state.rationale: Deep disgust and moral indignation at Claudius's revelry; the long philosophical digression reveals rising agitation beneath intellectual composure; the speech spirals away from its own syntax, suggesting he is losing control of his thoughts even as he tries to master them

social_state.rationale: Adopts the role of moral authority and teacher with Horatio, lecturing on Danish customs; warm enough to confide but primarily performing intellectual superiority and princely judgment

known_facts: Claudius is drinking and reveling tonight
Danish drinking customs damage the nation's reputation abroad
The Ghost has appeared before and may appear again tonight
Claudius has married his mother and taken his father's throne
hidden_secrets: His philosophical generalization about 'one defect' corrupting a whole nature is really about Claudius specifically
He suspects deeper corruption in Claudius beyond mere drunkenness
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows the surface facts but not yet the truth about his father's murder; his philosophical speech unconsciously anticipates the revelation — as if he senses that Claudius's 'one defect' is far worse than drinking

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — transforms personal disgust at Claudius into a philosophical meditation on how a single flaw corrupts an entire nature
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to be speaking generally about Danish customs and 'particular men,' but is transparently channeling his specific rage at Claudius; the philosophical detachment masks volcanic personal grief
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The speech could be read as Hamlet's self-reflection — worrying that his own 'vicious mole of nature' (melancholy, indecision) will corrupt his virtues, making this a displaced self-diagnosis rather than solely an attack on Claudius

beat_states.desire_state: To understand the sounds from the castle and keep Hamlet focused on the imminent appearance of the Ghost
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be a faithful friend and rational witness who helps Hamlet navigate dangerous truths
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has launched into an extended philosophical digression that moves far from the practical matter at hand; Horatio cannot redirect without being rude to his prince

affect_state.rationale: Mildly anxious about the Ghost's imminent arrival and the cold; uncertain about what is happening at the castle; quietly absorbing Hamlet's intensity without fully understanding its personal stakes

social_state.rationale: Defers to Hamlet as prince and friend; his brief questions ('What does this mean?' 'Is it a custom?') are those of a respectful listener and loyal companion, not a challenger

known_facts: The Ghost appeared on previous nights
They are waiting for the Ghost to appear again
Trumpets and cannon are sounding from the castle
Hamlet is deeply unhappy about the current state of Denmark
false_beliefs: Hamlet's philosophical speech is a calm, general observation about Danish culture rather than a barely contained personal attack on Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is a scholar and outsider to the Danish court; he takes Hamlet's speech more or less at face value as cultural commentary, though he likely senses the personal edge

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Wants to steer conversation back to the Ghost but must defer to Hamlet's emotional need to vent about Claudius
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may fully understand that Hamlet is attacking Claudius specifically and is playing along by asking innocent questions to let Hamlet vent safely

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Look, my lord, it comes.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Angels and ministers of grace, defend us! / Be thou a spirit of health or goblin damned, / Bring with thee airs from heaven or blasts from hell, / Be thy intents wicked or charitable, / Thou com’st in such a questionable shape / That I will speak to thee. I’ll call thee “ Hamlet,” / “ King,” “ Father,” “ Royal Dane.” O, answer me! / Let me not burst in ignorance, but tell / Why thy canonized bones, hearsèd in death, / Have burst their cerements; why the sepulcher, / Wherein we saw thee quietly interred, / Hath oped his ponderous and marble jaws / To cast thee up again. What may this mean / That thou, dead corse, again in complete steel, / Revisits thus the glimpses of the moon, / Making night hideous, and we fools of nature / So horridly to shake our disposition / With thoughts beyond the reaches of our souls? / Say, why is this? Wherefore? What should we do?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: It beckons you to go away with it / As if it some impartment did desire / To you alone.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Look with what courteous action / It waves you to a more removèd ground. / But do not go with it.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: No, by no means.

beat_states.desire_state: To compel the Ghost to reveal its purpose and identity — to know why his dead father has returned
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To discover the truth about his father's death and fulfill his duty as son
beat_states.obstacle: The Ghost is silent and mysterious; Hamlet cannot know if it is benign or demonic; the encounter overwhelms rational comprehension

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is overwhelmed with awe, terror, grief, and desperate need. His long rhetorical barrage is a bid for control in a situation where he has almost none. The cascade of questions reveals profound uncertainty. His emotional arousal is near maximum.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet asserts princely authority by naming the Ghost with royal titles and commanding it to speak. Beneath the authority is filial love and grief — warmth directed at the memory of his father.

known_facts: A figure resembling his dead father has appeared
His father was buried in the sepulcher
The Ghost wears full armor ('complete steel')
The Ghost is beckoning him
hidden_secrets: His deep suspicion and melancholy about the circumstances of his father's death
His desperate hope that the Ghost will validate his unease about Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet does not yet know if this is truly his father's spirit or a demon. He suspends judgment but cannot suppress hope that it carries revelation.

beat_states.defense_state: Ritualistic invocation of divine protection ('Angels and ministers of grace') as a shield against possible damnation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Terrified yet compulsively drawn forward; acknowledges the Ghost may be a 'goblin damned' yet insists on engaging it as 'Father'
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's torrent of words could be read as a stalling tactic — he is terrified and speaking to delay having to actually follow or engage the Ghost physically

beat_states.desire_state: To protect Hamlet from potential supernatural danger while accurately reporting what he observes
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve and safeguard his friend through loyal reason and witness
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is clearly drawn to the Ghost and may not heed warnings; Horatio cannot control supernatural forces

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is frightened and alarmed. His terse, urgent lines ('No, by no means') betray high anxiety beneath his characteristic restraint. He feels powerless to control the situation.

social_state.rationale: Horatio speaks as a devoted subordinate and friend — lower status but high warmth, risking overstepping by contradicting Hamlet's apparent intention to follow.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared before to the guards
It now beckons Hamlet specifically
It seems to want a private audience with Hamlet alone
false_beliefs: The Ghost is likely dangerous and should not be followed
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio interprets the Ghost's beckoning as potentially malevolent. His scholarly caution leads him to assume the worst, which may or may not be accurate.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalist caution — maintaining scholarly distance to counterbalance the terrifying unknown
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He originally sought Hamlet out to report the Ghost, effectively inviting this encounter, yet now urgently forbids Hamlet from following it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's prohibition may also stem from fear of losing his only true friend rather than purely rational assessment of danger

beat_states.desire_state: To prevent Hamlet from following the Ghost into isolation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty as a loyal soldier by protecting the prince
beat_states.obstacle: The Ghost's beckoning is compelling, and Hamlet outranks him — Marcellus may not be able to physically or socially restrain the prince

affect_state.rationale: Marcellus is frightened and feels the weight of responsibility. Having seen the Ghost before, he knows its power to unsettle. His direct plea reveals genuine fear for Hamlet's safety.

social_state.rationale: As a common soldier addressing the prince, Marcellus is significantly lower in status. His warmth is expressed through protective concern, though more formal and dutiful than Horatio's intimate friendship.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared on multiple nights
It now gestures Hamlet toward a more remote location
Its manner appears courteous but its intentions are unknown
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus is a practical observer. He notes the Ghost's behavior accurately but lacks framework to interpret its ultimate purpose.

beat_states.defense_state: Appealing to observable evidence ('Look with what courteous action') to ground the warning in shared perception rather than mere opinion
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He observes the Ghost's 'courteous action' — acknowledging its seeming benevolence — while simultaneously insisting Hamlet must not follow it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus's warning may partly reflect superstitious soldier's dread rather than calculated assessment — his fear of the supernatural overriding any possibility the Ghost could be benign

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: It will not speak. Then I will follow it.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Do not, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Why, what should be the fear? / I do not set my life at a pin’s fee. / And for my soul, what can it do to that, / Being a thing immortal as itself? / It waves me forth again. I’ll follow it.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: What if it tempt you toward the flood, my lord? / Or to the dreadful summit of the cliff / That beetles o’er his base into the sea, / And there assume some other horrible form / Which might deprive your sovereignty of reason / And draw you into madness? Think of it. / The very place puts toys of desperation, / Without more motive, into every brain / That looks so many fathoms to the sea / And hears it roar beneath.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: It waves me still.— Go on, I’ll follow thee.

beat_states.desire_state: To follow the Ghost and discover what it wants to communicate — to make contact with his dead father at any cost
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To discover the truth about his father's death and fulfill his duty to his father's memory
beat_states.obstacle: The Ghost will not speak in the open; Horatio physically and verbally tries to restrain him; the unknown danger of following a supernatural being

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is galvanized by the Ghost's beckoning — high arousal and determination override fear. His dismissal of his own life's value ('not at a pin's fee') reveals deep underlying depression and vulnerability, but in the moment he feels empowered by his recklessness. The certainty is in his resolve to follow, not in what awaits.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet asserts princely authority — he will not be told what to do by a subject, however beloved. His warmth toward Horatio is momentarily suppressed by his single-minded fixation on the Ghost. He brushes Horatio aside almost impatiently.

known_facts: A Ghost resembling his father has appeared and is beckoning him
The Ghost will not speak in the presence of others
His father died recently under circumstances Hamlet finds suspicious
His mother has married his uncle with indecent haste
hidden_secrets: His suicidal ideation — 'I do not set my life at a pin's fee' is more literal than Horatio may realize
He suspects foul play in his father's death
false_beliefs: That his soul is necessarily safe from supernatural corruption
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows the Ghost beckons him specifically and privately. His claim about his soul's immortality protecting it may be theologically naive — Horatio's warning about madness addresses exactly the danger Hamlet dismisses.

beat_states.defense_state: Devaluation of his own life — by claiming his life is worthless, he neutralizes any argument based on self-preservation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims total indifference to his own life and safety while simultaneously pursuing the Ghost with desperate purposefulness — a man who doesn't care about living yet is driven to act
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be unconsciously seeking self-destruction — following the Ghost toward potential death is a socially acceptable form of the suicidal wish expressed in 'too too solid flesh.' The pursuit of truth may be secondary to the death drive.

beat_states.desire_state: To physically and rhetorically prevent Hamlet from following the Ghost into unknown danger
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect and serve his friend and prince — to be the rational anchor Hamlet needs
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's reckless determination and his rank as prince make him impossible to physically restrain or command

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is genuinely terrified — not just of the Ghost but of losing Hamlet. His escalating imagery (flood, cliff, madness) betrays mounting panic. He has lost control of the situation; his arguments grow more desperate and elaborate as each one fails. Deep uncertainty about what the Ghost is or intends.

social_state.rationale: Horatio is subordinate and knows it — his 'Do not, my lord' is a plea, not a command. Yet his warmth and genuine concern for Hamlet override social propriety; he pushes the boundaries of what a subject may say to a prince.

known_facts: The Ghost appeared and resembles King Hamlet
The Ghost is beckoning Hamlet away from the group
The Ghost refuses to speak publicly
The battlements overlook dangerous cliffs and the sea
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is the most epistemically honest character — he openly acknowledges what he doesn't know and constructs worst-case scenarios based on genuine possibilities. His knowledge of the physical terrain (the cliff, the sea) is practical and grounded.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — he channels his fear into elaborate rational arguments about cliffs, madness, and the mechanics of supernatural deception
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is a scholar who studied this Ghost with intellectual curiosity just hours ago, now desperately trying to prevent any further contact with it

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: You shall not go, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Hold off your hands.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Be ruled. You shall not go.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: My fate cries out / And makes each petty arture in this body / As hardy as the Nemean lion’s nerve. / Still am I called. Unhand me, gentlemen. / By heaven, I’ll make a ghost of him that lets me! / I say, away!— Go on. I’ll follow thee.

beat_states.desire_state: To break free from his friends' physical restraint and follow the Ghost at any cost
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To discover the truth about his father's death and fulfill his destiny
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio and Marcellus physically holding him back, preventing him from following the Ghost

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of ecstatic, almost manic determination. His body feels electrified ('each petty arture'). He experiences a sense of destiny-driven certainty that overrides fear and social bonds. The threat to kill anyone who stops him shows extreme arousal. Vulnerability is present but suppressed — he is running toward something terrifying.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet pulls full royal rank — 'gentlemen' distances them, the death threat asserts absolute hierarchical dominance. He abandons all warmth and reciprocity with his friends to assert sovereign will.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared and beckons him
The Ghost resembles his dead father
His friends are genuinely trying to protect him
Following the Ghost could be dangerous
hidden_secrets: His suspicion that something was wrong with his father's death
The depth of his despair and suicidal ideation that makes him indifferent to the danger
false_beliefs: That fate is calling him specifically and that this is an unambiguous summons he must obey
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet interprets the Ghost's beckoning as fatalistic destiny rather than possible demonic temptation. His certainty about the call of fate may be a false belief driven by grief and desire for paternal contact.

beat_states.defense_state: Grandiose identification with fate and mythic imagery to override rational fear and others' concerns
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Desperate need for human connection and counsel versus violent willingness to harm his closest friends to pursue the supernatural
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's violence and grandiosity could be read as a suicidal impulse disguised as heroic destiny — he doesn't care if he dies, so following the Ghost is not courage but despair.

beat_states.desire_state: To physically prevent Hamlet from following the Ghost into potential danger or madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect Hamlet through loyal friendship, reason, and care
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's overwhelming physical force, royal authority, and death threat

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is frightened both of the supernatural event and of losing Hamlet. His attempt to command ('You shall not go') shows desperate urgency. He is losing control of the situation rapidly as Hamlet turns violent.

social_state.rationale: Horatio momentarily oversteps his station by commanding the prince ('Be ruled'), driven by genuine love and concern. His warmth is high — this is an act of profound caring — but his status is being forcibly reasserted downward by Hamlet's royal threat.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared on multiple nights
The Ghost is beckoning Hamlet away from safety
Spirits may lead men to madness or death
Hamlet is in an extreme emotional state
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is the most epistemically grounded character present. He knows the danger is real and his assessment of the risk is arguably more accurate than Hamlet's fatalistic interpretation.

beat_states.defense_state: Appeals to rational rule ('Be ruled') as a defense against the terrifying irrationality of the moment
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Deep loyalty demands he protect Hamlet, but Hamlet's direct command and threat of violence demand he release him — duty to friend versus obedience to prince

beat_states.desire_state: To physically stop Hamlet from following the Ghost into danger
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty as a loyal soldier and protector of the prince
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's royal command and physical resistance, plus the death threat

affect_state.rationale: Marcellus is frightened and out of his depth. He speaks first and briefly — 'You shall not go, my lord' — showing urgency but also deference ('my lord'). He is quickly overwhelmed by Hamlet's ferocity.

social_state.rationale: As a soldier addressing a prince, Marcellus is lower status but acts protectively. His 'my lord' maintains deference even while physically restraining royalty. Less intimate than Horatio but genuinely concerned.

known_facts: He has witnessed the Ghost on prior nights
The Ghost beckons Hamlet away
Something is rotten in the state of Denmark (his later line suggests growing awareness)
Following the Ghost could be lethal
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus has practical knowledge of the Ghost's appearances and a soldier's instinct for danger, but lacks Horatio's scholarly framework for understanding the supernatural.

beat_states.defense_state: Assertion of duty and loyalty as justification for physically restraining the prince
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Soldier's duty to obey his prince conflicts directly with his protective instinct — Hamlet orders him to let go, but letting go may mean Hamlet's death
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Marcellus may also be motivated partly by self-preservation — if the prince dies on his watch, he bears responsibility.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: He waxes desperate with imagination.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Let’s follow. ’Tis not fit thus to obey him.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Have after. To what issue will this come?

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Something is rotten in the state of Denmark.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Heaven will direct it.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Nay, let’s follow him.

beat_states.desire_state: to follow and protect Hamlet from whatever danger the ghost may lead him into
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to safeguard Hamlet through loyal friendship and rational counsel
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has broken free and disappeared into the darkness with the ghost; Horatio has lost physical control of the situation

affect_state.rationale: Deeply anxious for Hamlet's safety, feeling powerless after failing to restrain him, uncertain about the ghost's intentions, and exposed in his inability to protect his friend

social_state.rationale: As Hamlet's closest companion he holds moderate social standing among the group; his warmth is high — every line is motivated by care for Hamlet's wellbeing

known_facts: The ghost has appeared and beckoned Hamlet away
Hamlet has broken free from their restraint and followed it
Hamlet was in a state of desperate agitation
The ghost resembles the dead king
false_beliefs: That heaven will simply resolve whatever is unfolding without further human intervention
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is well-informed about the ghost's appearances but has no knowledge of what the ghost intends to reveal; his invocation of heaven may be wishful thinking more than genuine belief

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — framing the crisis as a philosophical question ('To what issue will this come?') and deferring to divine providence ('Heaven will direct it')
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His rational, scholarly mind tells him to trust heaven's direction, yet his body is compelled to chase after Hamlet — faith and action pulling against each other
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's 'Heaven will direct it' could be read not as genuine faith but as a desperate attempt to reassure himself and Marcellus — a man grasping at any comfort when reason fails him

beat_states.desire_state: to pursue Hamlet immediately and restore order to a situation that has spiraled out of control
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty as a loyal soldier by protecting the prince and the integrity of the state
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio's momentary hesitation and tendency toward philosophical reflection rather than decisive action

affect_state.rationale: High alarm and agitation; he senses deep wrongness not just in this moment but in all of Denmark; feels the situation slipping but is more action-oriented than Horatio, giving him slightly more sense of agency

social_state.rationale: A soldier subordinate to both Hamlet and Horatio in rank, yet in this moment he takes the lead, pushing Horatio to act; his warmth reflects genuine concern for the prince

known_facts: The ghost has appeared on multiple nights
Hamlet has followed the ghost against their wishes
Denmark is in political turmoil with military preparations underway
The ghost appeared in the dead king's armor
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus perceives a connection between the supernatural visitation and the political corruption of Denmark — his famous line synthesizes an intuition that goes beyond what he can prove but is essentially correct

beat_states.defense_state: displacement — channeling personal fear into a grand political diagnosis ('Something is rotten in the state of Denmark')
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He insists they should not 'obey' Hamlet's command to stay, revealing a soldier's conflict between duty to follow orders and duty to protect his prince
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: 'Something is rotten in the state of Denmark' could be read as Marcellus already suspecting foul play in King Hamlet's death, making the line less a general observation and more a pointed accusation hovering beneath the surface

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Whither wilt thou lead me? Speak. I’ll go no further.

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: Mark me.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I will.

beat_states.desire_state: to command Hamlet's full, undivided attention before delivering the revelation of his murder
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to compel Hamlet to avenge his murder and restore moral order
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's fear and resistance; the limited time before dawn forces the Ghost to return to purgatory

affect_state.rationale: The Ghost speaks with the authority of the dead who know the truth. 'Mark me' is a terse, absolute command — no pleading, no explanation yet. There is suffering underneath (purgatorial torment) but the dominant mode is controlled urgency.

social_state.rationale: The Ghost occupies supreme paternal and royal authority. In this beat he withholds warmth strategically — 'Mark me' is not tender, it is imperious. The warmth will come later to bind Hamlet emotionally.

known_facts: He was murdered by his brother Claudius
The method was poison poured in his ear
His wife has married his murderer
He suffers in purgatory for sins unconfessed
hidden_secrets: The full horror of the murder he is about to reveal
The nature of his purgatorial suffering
epistemic_state.rationale: The Ghost possesses complete knowledge of the crime and the afterlife. He is the bearer of truth that will shatter Hamlet's world. He strategically withholds this information to build to maximum impact.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: the Ghost must weaponize his son's love and grief while knowing the burden of revenge will destroy Hamlet's peace
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Ghost's terseness ('Mark me') could be read as a spirit struggling against temporal or metaphysical constraints rather than pure tactical authority — urgency born of limited time rather than dominance.

beat_states.desire_state: to assert some measure of control over this terrifying encounter by demanding the Ghost speak before he goes further
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to discover the truth about his father's death and find purpose in his paralyzed grief
beat_states.obstacle: overwhelming fear and uncertainty about whether this spirit is benign or malevolent

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of high dread and awe. He has followed a ghost into darkness and now plants his feet, trying to reclaim agency. His quick pivot to 'I will' shows he is ultimately submissive to the Ghost's authority, revealing how little control he truly has.

social_state.rationale: Despite his initial demand, Hamlet quickly defers. He is a son before his father's spirit — subordinate, but with filial warmth and reverence underneath the fear.

known_facts: A ghost resembling his father has appeared on the battlements
The ghost has beckoned him away from his companions
His father died recently and his mother remarried with suspicious speed
Horatio and the guards warned him not to follow
hidden_secrets: He already suspects foul play in his father's death
He harbors deep disgust toward his mother and Claudius
false_beliefs: He may still believe his father died of a serpent's bite
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is on the threshold of revelation. He knows something is deeply wrong in Denmark but does not yet have confirmation. He has followed the Ghost against all rational advice, driven by emotional need.

beat_states.defense_state: establishing boundaries to manage terror — setting conditions on his compliance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he is simultaneously drawn forward by desperate need to hear his father and pulling back in primal fear of the supernatural
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'I'll go no further' could be read less as fear and more as a tactical demand — a negotiation move to force the Ghost to reveal its purpose before Hamlet commits further.

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: My hour is almost come / When I to sulf’rous and tormenting flames / Must render up myself.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Alas, poor ghost!

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: Pity me not, but lend thy serious hearing / To what I shall unfold.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Speak. I am bound to hear.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure Hamlet's full, undivided attention before revealing the truth of his murder
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To compel Hamlet to avenge his murder and restore justice to Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: Limited time before he must return to purgatorial suffering; Hamlet's pity threatens to derail the conversation into sympathy rather than action

affect_state.rationale: The Ghost is in torment but strategically composed. He reveals his suffering not as a plea but as a framing device — establishing the stakes and his authority. High certainty about what he must communicate, moderate control despite the ticking clock of his allotted time.

social_state.rationale: Maintains patriarchal and supernatural authority over Hamlet. The warmth is minimal — he deliberately cuts off Hamlet's sympathetic response ('Pity me not') to maintain a transactional, mission-focused dynamic rather than a tender father-son reunion.

known_facts: He was murdered
He suffers in purgatorial flames
His time to communicate is limited
Hamlet is willing to listen
hidden_secrets: The identity and method of his murderer
The full nature of Claudius's crimes
The depth of Gertrude's involvement or betrayal
epistemic_state.rationale: The Ghost possesses the complete truth of his murder and holds it back strategically, preparing the ground before the revelation. He correctly reads Hamlet's willingness.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflects Hamlet's compassion by redirecting focus away from his own suffering toward the task at hand
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Evokes his own suffering to establish urgency and gravitas, then immediately rejects the pity that suffering provokes
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Ghost's mention of torment could be read as a manipulative bid for sympathy designed to deepen Hamlet's emotional obligation, rather than a genuine confession of suffering.

beat_states.desire_state: To hear the Ghost's revelation and understand the truth of his father's fate
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To discover the truth about the corruption in Denmark and find a course of action that restores meaning to his shattered world
beat_states.obstacle: Overwhelming emotional shock and grief at witnessing his father's spectral suffering; uncertainty about what he is about to hear

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is emotionally overwhelmed — confronting his dead father's suffering triggers deep grief and horror. High arousal from the supernatural encounter. Low certainty because he doesn't yet know what will be revealed. Low control as he yields entirely to the Ghost's authority. Extremely vulnerable — stripped of defenses, open, and obedient.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet assumes a subordinate, filial position — both as son to father and as mortal before a supernatural being. His warmth is high: his first impulse is tender compassion for his father's pain, and 'I am bound to hear' expresses dutiful love.

known_facts: His father's ghost has appeared and led him apart
The Ghost suffers in some form of afterlife torment
The Ghost has something crucial to reveal
He has sworn to follow and listen
hidden_secrets: His own suicidal despair and disgust at Claudius and Gertrude's marriage
false_beliefs: Possibly still believes his father died of natural causes (snakebite)
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is on the threshold of revelation. He knows something is deeply wrong but does not yet possess the specific knowledge of murder. His 'I am bound to hear' signals readiness to receive what he suspects will be devastating.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His instinct is compassion ('Alas, poor ghost!') but he is immediately corrected and must suppress empathy in order to become a receptive instrument for information — feeling deeply while being told not to feel
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: 'I am bound to hear' could carry a double meaning — both dutiful willingness and a sense of fatalistic entrapment, suggesting Hamlet already senses this knowledge will become an unbearable burden.

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: So art thou to revenge, when thou shalt hear.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What?
