beat_states.desire_state: To hook Hamlet's commitment to revenge before revealing the full truth
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To compel Hamlet to avenge his murder and restore moral order
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has not yet heard the crime and may not be ready to accept the burden

affect_state.rationale: The Ghost is purposeful and commanding, strategically deploying the word 'revenge' before Hamlet knows what he must avenge — a rhetorically dominant move. His suffering is real but controlled here; he is the one holding information and therefore power.

social_state.rationale: The Ghost speaks from a position of supernatural authority and parental command. Warmth is suppressed in favor of urgency and manipulation — this is not a tender moment but a recruitment.

known_facts: He was murdered by Claudius
Hamlet does not yet know how he died
His time on earth is limited
Hamlet has pledged to follow and listen
hidden_secrets: The full details of the murder by poison
Claudius seduced Gertrude
The enormity of what he is about to demand of his son
epistemic_state.rationale: The Ghost possesses complete knowledge of the crime and is strategically rationing it to maximize Hamlet's emotional commitment before full disclosure.

beat_states.defense_state: withholding — releasing information in calculated increments to maximize emotional impact
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Torn between paternal love for Hamlet and the need to weaponize him as an instrument of vengeance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Ghost's 'So art thou' could be read as a genuine equating of their shared suffering rather than manipulation — a father seeing his own torment mirrored in his son's future.

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what the Ghost is demanding of him — what requires revenge
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To discover the truth about his father's death and find moral clarity in a corrupted world
beat_states.obstacle: Complete ignorance of the crime; the Ghost is withholding the crucial information

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet's single-word 'What?' is a gasp of shocked openness — maximally aroused, stripped of rhetorical defense. He is entirely at the mercy of what the Ghost will say next. The word 'revenge' has destabilized him; he didn't expect it.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet is subordinate — a son before a father, a mortal before a spirit. His warmth reflects filial devotion and willingness to serve, though it is tinged with fear.

known_facts: His father's ghost has appeared to him
The Ghost suffers in purgatorial flames
The Ghost has just introduced the concept of revenge
Something terrible must have happened to warrant this visitation
hidden_secrets: His deep suspicion that something was wrong about his father's death
His hatred of Claudius and the marriage to Gertrude
false_beliefs: His father died of natural causes (a serpent's bite)
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is on the threshold of revelation. He suspects corruption but does not yet know the specific truth. His 'What?' marks the exact pivot point from suspicion to knowledge.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His eagerness to hear competes with a nascent dread of what he is about to learn — he senses the word 'revenge' carries an irreversible weight
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'What?' could be read not as shock but as impatient exasperation — he has been led far and told little, and he is pressing the Ghost to stop being cryptic and speak plainly.

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: I am thy father’s spirit, / Doomed for a certain term to walk the night / And for the day confined to fast in fires / Till the foul crimes done in my days of nature / Are burnt and purged away. But that I am forbid / To tell the secrets of my prison house, / I could a tale unfold whose lightest word / Would harrow up thy soul, freeze thy young blood, / Make thy two eyes, like stars, start from their spheres, / Thy knotted and combinèd locks to part, / And each particular hair to stand an end, / Like quills upon the fearful porpentine. / But this eternal blazon must not be / To ears of flesh and blood. List, list, O list! / If thou didst ever thy dear father love—

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O God!

beat_states.desire_state: To seize Hamlet's full emotional commitment before revealing the murder, ensuring he will act on the revelation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To compel Hamlet to avenge his murder and restore justice
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet may not fully believe or may not be emotionally prepared to receive and act upon the terrible truth; divine restrictions limit what the Ghost can reveal about purgatory

affect_state.rationale: The Ghost is suffering but rhetorically masterful — deploying escalating horror with controlled precision. High certainty about his mission, high control over the rhetorical strategy, but underlying anguish about his damnation and the foul crime done to him.

social_state.rationale: Commands supreme paternal and supernatural authority. The 'If thou didst ever thy dear father love' line weaponizes parental love — warmth exists but is instrumentalized to bind Hamlet to obligation.

known_facts: He was murdered
He is condemned to purgatorial suffering
He knows the identity of his killer
He knows the full horror of what awaits in the afterlife
Hamlet loved his father
hidden_secrets: The specific identity and method of his murderer (not yet revealed)
The full secrets of purgatory he is forbidden to share
epistemic_state.rationale: The Ghost possesses complete knowledge of the crime and the afterlife but strategically withholds it, building rhetorical pressure before the key revelation.

beat_states.defense_state: Withholding the worst details of purgatorial suffering to maintain authority and mystique rather than appearing pitiable
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to be forbidden from telling the secrets of the prison house yet deploys vivid, terrifying imagery of what he COULD describe — manipulating through the very prohibition he cites
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Ghost may be a demonic entity manipulating Hamlet through emotional exploitation rather than a genuine paternal spirit — the elaborate rhetorical setup could indicate calculated seduction rather than genuine fatherly anguish.

beat_states.desire_state: To receive the truth about his father while emotionally surviving the terror of the encounter
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To understand the corruption in Denmark and find his purpose in confronting it
beat_states.obstacle: The sheer emotional and metaphysical terror of confronting his dead father's suffering spirit; the overwhelming guilt-trigger of 'If thou didst ever thy dear father love'

affect_state.rationale: 'O God!' is a cry of pure overwhelmed anguish — the Ghost's emotional manipulation has cracked Hamlet open completely. Extremely high arousal, near-total loss of control. The invocation of filial love pierces directly into Hamlet's deepest grief and guilt about his father.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet is rendered completely subordinate — a child before a father, a mortal before a spirit. His warmth is raw, unguarded love and grief pouring out involuntarily.

known_facts: This is his father's spirit
His father suffers in purgatorial fires
The Ghost has something terrible to reveal
There were 'foul crimes' connected to his father's death
hidden_secrets: His own suicidal ideation from the 'too too solid flesh' soliloquy
His deep suspicion that something was wrong about the death
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is being prepared for revelation but does not yet know the specific crime. The Ghost's rhetoric confirms his worst suspicions that something deeply wrong occurred.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Desperately wants to hear the truth yet is being psychologically shattered by the preparation for it; his rational intellect is being overwhelmed by primal filial grief and supernatural dread
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'O God!' could contain a flash of doubt about whether this spirit is trustworthy — an instinctive prayer for protection as much as an exclamation of grief.

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: Revenge his foul and most unnatural murder.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Murder?

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: Murder most foul, as in the best it is, / But this most foul, strange, and unnatural.

beat_states.desire_state: To ignite Hamlet's outrage and commitment to revenge by naming the crime explicitly
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To compel Hamlet to avenge his murder and restore justice to Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's shock and potential disbelief; the limited time the Ghost has before dawn

affect_state.rationale: The Ghost speaks with absolute certainty and controlled fury. He knows exactly what happened and is deliberately building the revelation in stages. His affect is deeply negative but purposeful — he is weaponizing his suffering to galvanize Hamlet.

social_state.rationale: The Ghost commands from a position of supernatural and paternal authority. Warmth is minimal here — this is a father demanding action, not offering comfort. The rhetorical escalation ('most foul, strange, and unnatural') asserts dominance over the narrative.

known_facts: He was murdered
The murder was committed by someone close (unnatural)
The full details he is about to reveal
hidden_secrets: The identity of the murderer (Claudius) — being withheld for maximum impact
epistemic_state.rationale: The Ghost possesses complete knowledge of his own death and is parceling it out strategically. He names the act before the agent, building dramatic and emotional pressure on Hamlet.

beat_states.defense_state: Channeling anguish into righteous authority to maintain control of the revelation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Must strategically escalate horror while remaining coherent enough to direct Hamlet's response
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Ghost's triple emphasis on 'foul, strange, and unnatural' could suggest he is as much processing his own trauma as strategizing — the repetition may betray lingering personal anguish rather than pure tactical control.

beat_states.desire_state: To comprehend and absorb the staggering revelation — desperate for more information
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To understand the truth of his world and act rightly in response to it
beat_states.obstacle: The sheer psychic shock of hearing his father was murdered; the collapse of his understanding of reality

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of extreme shock. His single-word response ('Murder?') reveals a mind blown open — high arousal, very low control, maximum vulnerability. He is being acted upon rather than acting. The certainty is low because the word 'murder' restructures everything he thought he knew.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet is entirely subordinate in this exchange — a son receiving devastating news from a father-spirit. His status is diminished by shock. The warmth reflects his profound emotional connection and grief for his father.

known_facts: His father's ghost has appeared to him
His father is suffering in purgatory
His father was murdered
hidden_secrets: His own prior suspicions about the state of Denmark and Claudius
false_beliefs: Possibly still holding a residual assumption that his father died of natural causes — now being destroyed
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is in the moment of epistemic rupture. The word 'murder' has just been spoken, and his entire understanding of his father's death is collapsing. He does not yet know who did it or how.

beat_states.defense_state: Echoic repetition ('Murder?') as a reflexive attempt to process overwhelming information
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He suspected something was deeply wrong in Denmark but hearing 'murder' from his father's ghost shatters the boundary between suspicion and unbearable reality
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'Murder?' could be read not as pure shock but as a kind of grim confirmation — he may have already sensed foul play and is now hearing his darkest suspicion validated, which would shift the affect toward bitter vindication rather than pure horror.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Haste me to know ’t, that I, with wings as swift / As meditation or the thoughts of love, / May sweep to my revenge.

beat_states.desire_state: to learn the full details of the murder immediately so he can act
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty to his father and restore moral order
beat_states.obstacle: he does not yet know the identity of the murderer or the circumstances of the crime

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is surging with adrenaline and filial fury, but he does not yet possess the knowledge he needs; his certainty is moderate because he believes the Ghost but hasn't heard the specifics. The simile of 'wings as swift' suggests he is trying to master the situation through will, but the underlying affect is grief-stricken and destabilized.

social_state.rationale: He is subordinate to the Ghost — a supplicant son begging for information from a spectral father. His warmth is directed toward his father's memory and the love that motivates his pledge.

known_facts: His father was murdered
The murder was 'foul and most unnatural'
The Ghost demands revenge
The Ghost claims to be his father's spirit
hidden_secrets: He is speaking with a ghost on the battlements, unknown to the court
false_beliefs: He assumes he will be able to act as swiftly as he now promises
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has the essential fact of murder confirmed but lacks the crucial detail of the murderer's identity. His false belief in his own swiftness is the dramatic seed of the delay that will define the entire play.

beat_states.defense_state: grandiose identification — inflating himself into a swift instrument of vengeance to ward off the horror and helplessness of what he's hearing
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: his language compares revenge to 'meditation' and 'thoughts of love' — the most contemplative and tender images possible — revealing an unconscious resistance to violence even as he pledges himself to it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's extreme eagerness could be read as a performative display for the Ghost — a son desperately trying to prove himself worthy of his father's love and trust, rather than a genuine readiness to kill.

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: I find thee apt; / And duller shouldst thou be than the fat weed / That roots itself in ease on Lethe wharf, / Wouldst thou not stir in this. Now, Hamlet, hear. / ’Tis given out that, sleeping in my orchard, / A serpent stung me. So the whole ear of Denmark / Is by a forgèd process of my death / Rankly abused. But know, thou noble youth, / The serpent that did sting thy father’s life / Now wears his crown.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O, my prophetic soul! My uncle!

beat_states.desire_state: To reveal Claudius as the murderer and ignite Hamlet's resolve for revenge
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To have his murder avenged and the corruption in Denmark purged
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet may not believe the truth or may not be emotionally prepared to act against his own uncle

affect_state.rationale: The Ghost is in command of this disclosure, strategically building from the false narrative ('a serpent stung me') to the shattering reveal. His certainty is absolute — he knows exactly what happened. Controlled fury beneath a deliberate rhetorical structure.

social_state.rationale: Maintains patriarchal and monarchical authority ('thou noble youth'), but warmth is tempered by the instrumental nature of the address — he is recruiting an avenger as much as speaking to a son.

known_facts: Claudius murdered him
The public believes a serpent killed him in the orchard
Denmark has been deceived by a 'forged process'
Claudius now wears the crown
hidden_secrets: The full method of the murder (poison in the ear — not yet revealed)
The extent of Gertrude's involvement or complicity
epistemic_state.rationale: The Ghost possesses complete knowledge of his own murder and is parceling it out strategically. He withholds the specific details of the poisoning for the next beat to maximize dramatic and emotional impact.

beat_states.defense_state: Controlling the pacing of revelation — building methodically from the public lie to the devastating truth
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Must manipulate his own son's emotions while claiming paternal love; uses rhetorical seduction even as he demands righteous action
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Ghost could be a demonic entity manipulating Hamlet's existing suspicions rather than revealing genuine truth — this is the play's own epistemological question.

beat_states.desire_state: To confirm the terrible suspicion he has already harbored about Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To discover the truth about the rottenness in Denmark and determine the right course of action
beat_states.obstacle: The shattering emotional weight of having his worst intuition confirmed — his uncle is a murderer and usurper

affect_state.rationale: The exclamatory outburst reveals a man overwhelmed — high arousal, deeply negative valence. Yet there is a strange certainty, even satisfaction, in having his intuition validated. Control is low; he is reacting involuntarily. Extremely vulnerable — his entire world order is confirmed as corrupt.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet is subordinate to the Ghost's authority and revelation. His brief cry is that of a receiving subject, not an agent. Some warmth in the implicit trust of his own 'prophetic soul' and alignment with his father.

known_facts: His father was murdered
Claudius is the murderer
The public story of a serpent bite is a lie
His prior suspicions about Claudius were correct
hidden_secrets: He has harbored suspicions about Claudius before this encounter
epistemic_state.rationale: 'My prophetic soul' reveals that Hamlet already intuited something was deeply wrong with Claudius — the Ghost's revelation crystallizes pre-existing suspicion into certainty. He now knows the who but not yet the how.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — 'O, my prophetic soul!' frames the horror as the fulfillment of prophecy, converting trauma into a narrative of vindicated intuition
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Relief at confirmed suspicion collides with horror at the truth; he wanted to know yet the knowing is devastating
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'prophetic soul' could indicate he is projecting his pre-existing hatred of Claudius onto the Ghost's words, too eagerly accepting a narrative that confirms his bias — raising the question of whether he is a critical thinker or a willing believer.

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: Ay, that incestuous, that adulterate beast, / With witchcraft of his wits, with traitorous gifts— / O wicked wit and gifts, that have the power / So to seduce!— won to his shameful lust / The will of my most seeming-virtuous queen. / O Hamlet, what a falling off was there! / From me, whose love was of that dignity / That it went hand in hand even with the vow / I made to her in marriage, and to decline / Upon a wretch whose natural gifts were poor / To those of mine. / But virtue, as it never will be moved, / Though lewdness court it in a shape of heaven, / So, lust, though to a radiant angel linked, / Will sate itself in a celestial bed / And prey on garbage. / But soft, methinks I scent the morning air. / Brief let me be. Sleeping within my orchard, / My custom always of the afternoon, / Upon my secure hour thy uncle stole, / With juice of cursèd hebona in a vial / And in the porches of my ears did pour / The leprous distilment, whose effect / Holds such an enmity with blood of man / That swift as quicksilver it courses through / The natural gates and alleys of the body, / And with a sudden vigor it doth posset / And curd, like eager droppings into milk, / The thin and wholesome blood. So did it mine, / And a most instant tetter barked about, / Most lazar-like, with vile and loathsome crust / All my smooth body. / Thus was I, sleeping, by a brother’s hand / Of life, of crown, of queen at once dispatched, / Cut off, even in the blossoms of my sin, / Unhouseled, disappointed, unaneled, / No reck’ning made, but sent to my account / With all my imperfections on my head. / O horrible, O horrible, most horrible! / If thou hast nature in thee, bear it not. / Let not the royal bed of Denmark be / A couch for luxury and damnèd incest. / But, howsomever thou pursues this act, / Taint not thy mind, nor let thy soul contrive / Against thy mother aught. Leave her to heaven / And to those thorns that in her bosom lodge / To prick and sting her. Fare thee well at once. / The glowworm shows the matin to be near / And ’gins to pale his uneffectual fire. / Adieu, adieu, adieu. Remember me.

beat_states.desire_state: To fully reveal the murder, condemn Claudius and Gertrude's betrayal, command Hamlet to revenge, and protect Gertrude from Hamlet's direct harm before time runs out
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure justice and remembrance through Hamlet's action, restoring the moral order violated by his murder
beat_states.obstacle: Time is running out as dawn approaches; he must compress an enormous emotional and narrative payload into a shrinking window; he also risks overwhelming Hamlet or corrupting him

affect_state.rationale: Intense rage at Claudius and bitter grief over Gertrude's betrayal drive extreme negative valence and high arousal. He is certain of the facts but losing control — time forces him to rush, and the memory of his own unshriven death surfaces raw vulnerability. The final 'O horrible, most horrible' is an involuntary eruption of anguish breaking through his commanding rhetoric.

social_state.rationale: He speaks from paternal and royal authority, commanding a son's obedience. Warmth flickers — 'O Hamlet, what a falling off' carries genuine intimate grief, and the protective injunction about Gertrude reveals residual love — but it is largely subordinated to the imperative of vengeance and moral judgment.

known_facts: Claudius murdered him by pouring poison in his ear while he slept
Gertrude was seduced by Claudius before the murder
He died without last rites — unhouseled, disappointed, unaneled
He is suffering in purgatorial fires for his sins
Dawn is approaching and he must depart
hidden_secrets: The full nature of his own 'imperfections' and sins he was sent to account for
Whether his account of Gertrude's willing seduction is entirely accurate or colored by jealous rage
false_beliefs: That Hamlet can both execute bloody revenge and keep his mind untainted
epistemic_state.rationale: The Ghost possesses complete eyewitness knowledge of his own murder and the circumstances of the affair. However, his characterization of Gertrude may be distorted by wounded pride. His instruction to Hamlet contains an internal logical impossibility he either cannot see or chooses to ignore.

beat_states.defense_state: Idealizing his own love and virtue while demonizing Claudius — splitting to maintain moral authority and avoid acknowledging any complicity in the marriage's failure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Commands revenge but then warns 'taint not thy mind'; demands Hamlet kill but preserve his soul — an impossible double bind. Also: confesses he died 'with all my imperfections on my head' yet presents himself as morally superior throughout.
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Ghost may be a demonic or purgatorial figure deliberately planting an impossible double bind to corrupt Hamlet — the contradictory instructions (revenge but stay pure, kill but don't taint your mind) could be read as a trap rather than genuine paternal concern.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O all you host of heaven! O Earth! What else? / And shall I couple hell? O fie! Hold, hold, my heart, / And you, my sinews, grow not instant old, / But bear me stiffly up. Remember thee? / Ay, thou poor ghost, whiles memory holds a seat / In this distracted globe. Remember thee? / Yea, from the table of my memory / I’ll wipe away all trivial, fond records, / All saws of books, all forms, all pressures past, / That youth and observation copied there, / And thy commandment all alone shall live / Within the book and volume of my brain, / Unmixed with baser matter. Yes, by heaven! / O most pernicious woman! / O villain, villain, smiling, damnèd villain! / My tables— meet it is I set it down / That one may smile and smile and be a villain. / At least I am sure it may be so in Denmark. / So, uncle, there you are. Now to my word. / It is “ adieu, adieu, remember me.” / I have sworn ’t.

beat_states.desire_state: To permanently inscribe the Ghost's revelation and command into his very being, sealing his vow of revenge
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty to his father by avenging his murder and restoring moral order
beat_states.obstacle: The overwhelming magnitude of the revelation threatens to shatter his mind and body; he must hold himself together physically and psychologically to carry out the task

affect_state.rationale: Extreme emotional flooding — horror, grief, rage — all simultaneous. He is near physical collapse ('Hold, hold, my heart'), yet forces himself toward certainty and resolve. The wild oscillation between cosmic invocation, bitter sarcasm ('At least I am sure it may be so in Denmark'), and solemn oath reveals someone barely holding the seams together. High arousal, low control despite desperate attempts to impose it.

social_state.rationale: He is alone and feels crushed under the burden placed on him — diminished by the enormity of the task. Warmth collapses into contempt for Gertrude ('pernicious woman') and Claudius ('smiling, damnèd villain'), with only tenderness remaining for the 'poor ghost' of his father.

known_facts: Claudius murdered King Hamlet by pouring poison in his ear
Claudius seduced Gertrude before the murder
Claudius is a smiling, deceptive villain
The Ghost commands him to remember and avenge
Gertrude is complicit at minimum in the hasty remarriage
hidden_secrets: He now knows the true cause of his father's death
He has sworn revenge against the sitting king
false_beliefs: That he can wipe his mind clean of all prior knowledge and devote it entirely to this single purpose
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has received the full revelation from the Ghost and believes it completely. His epistemic world has been shattered and rebuilt around this single terrible truth. The false belief is his overestimation of his own capacity for single-minded action — the very speech that vows focus demonstrates its impossibility.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — he channels overwhelming emotion into ritualistic note-taking and mnemonic pledging, converting psychic chaos into a structured oath
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He swears total, singular devotion to revenge yet his mind fractures in multiple directions — invoking heaven, earth, hell, cursing his mother, cursing Claudius, making sardonic observations about Danish villainy — revealing a mind already 'distracted' even as it vows absolute focus
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: This could be read as the onset of genuine madness rather than performative resolve — the fragmented syntax, cosmic invocations, and compulsive note-taking ('My tables') may indicate Hamlet is already psychologically breaking rather than steeling himself, making the 'antic disposition' less a chosen strategy and more an involuntary response to trauma.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: My lord, my lord!

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Lord Hamlet.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Heavens secure him!

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: So be it.

utterances.speaker: MARCELLUS
utterances.text: Illo, ho, ho, my lord!

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Hillo, ho, ho, boy! Come, bird, come!

beat_states.desire_state: to regain composure and control the situation before his friends reach him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father's murder while concealing the terrible knowledge he now carries
beat_states.obstacle: he is in a state of psychological upheaval from the Ghost's revelation and must now face his friends without revealing what he knows

affect_state.rationale: The Ghost's revelation has left him emotionally wrecked but hyper-aroused. His 'Hillo, ho, ho, boy!' is a desperate performance of normalcy/wildness. He knows the truth now but feels utterly unmoored in how to proceed. Deep vulnerability masked by manic energy.

social_state.rationale: He reasserts his princely status through playful command (calling them like hawks), but there is distance — he cannot be truly warm because he must now conceal everything. The falconer's call establishes him as master, them as birds answering his summons.

known_facts: Claudius murdered his father by pouring poison in his ear
Gertrude was seduced by Claudius before the murder
The Ghost has charged him with revenge
He must not harm his mother
Horatio and Marcellus are searching for him
hidden_secrets: Everything the Ghost revealed about the murder and Claudius's guilt
His newly sworn obligation to revenge
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet now possesses the central secret of the play. He knows his friends are loyal but recognizes he cannot share the full truth. His epistemic burden is enormous.

beat_states.defense_state: manic performance — adopting a wild, playful tone (falconer's call) to mask his shattered interior state
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: internally devastated and burdened with world-shattering knowledge, yet externally performing giddy, almost antic playfulness
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The manic playfulness could be genuine relief at hearing friendly human voices after the terrifying supernatural encounter, rather than a deliberate mask.

beat_states.desire_state: to find Hamlet and ensure he is safe and sane after the encounter with the Ghost
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to protect and support his friend Hamlet with rational loyalty
beat_states.obstacle: he cannot see Hamlet clearly; he has no idea what the Ghost said or what state Hamlet is in

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is genuinely frightened for Hamlet's safety and soul. His call 'Heavens secure him!' reveals both fear and helplessness. High arousal from searching in the dark after a supernatural event.

social_state.rationale: Deferential ('My lord, my lord!') but deeply caring. His warmth is genuine — this is his closest friend possibly in mortal or spiritual danger. He subordinates himself to Hamlet's rank but his concern transcends formality.

known_facts: A ghost resembling King Hamlet appeared
Hamlet followed the Ghost alone against their warnings
The Ghost beckoned Hamlet away privately
false_beliefs: The Ghost may have harmed or driven Hamlet mad
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio has no idea what was communicated. He fears the worst — that the Ghost was a demonic entity that may have damaged Hamlet's mind or soul.

beat_states.defense_state: invoking divine protection ('Heavens secure him!') to manage his own fear
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: a rationalist scholar who has now witnessed a ghost and must pray for supernatural protection

beat_states.desire_state: to locate Hamlet and confirm he is alive and himself
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty as a loyal soldier and subject to the Prince
beat_states.obstacle: darkness, distance, and uncertainty about what happened to Hamlet with the Ghost

affect_state.rationale: Fearful but more practically oriented than Horatio. His 'Illo, ho, ho' is a falconer's call — a functional, earthy method of locating someone. Less existential dread than Horatio, more active searching energy.

social_state.rationale: Clearly subordinate — addresses Hamlet by title. His warmth is genuine but more professionally dutiful than Horatio's intimate concern. He is a loyal guard doing his job with heart.

known_facts: He has seen the Ghost on multiple nights
Hamlet followed the Ghost alone
He and Horatio were unable to prevent Hamlet from going
epistemic_state.rationale: Marcellus knows the least of the three about what is really happening. He is the most in the dark about the content of the Ghost's message and Hamlet's inner state.

beat_states.desire_state: To process the overwhelming revelation while keeping the Ghost's secrets hidden from his friends
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder and restore moral order

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of extreme agitation following the Ghost's revelations. He now knows the truth about his father's murder but is barely holding himself together. His 'O, wonderful!' is charged with bitter irony and manic energy — he is overwhelmed.

social_state.rationale: He retains his princely status but is emotionally withdrawing from his friends, creating distance through cryptic responses rather than engaging warmly

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Desperate need to share the unbearable truth versus absolute obligation to keep it secret

His repeated questioning ('But you'll be secret?') reveals lingering doubt despite their oaths.

Horatio and Marcellus witnessed the Ghost but do not know what it said
He needs allies but cannot afford leaks

beat_states.defense_state: Withholding information as leverage — using secrecy as a control mechanism to manage his own overwhelming vulnerability

social_state.rationale: Horatio defers completely to Hamlet's authority, swearing by heaven. His warmth and devotion are evident in his willingness to submit to whatever Hamlet demands.

Hamlet is deeply disturbed by what he learned

beat_states.desire_state: To collectively swear secrecy and earn Hamlet's trust

The Ghost appeared and spoke privately to Hamlet
Something terrible has been revealed

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is a practical soldier confronting supernatural terror, trying to maintain composure and duty

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: There’s never a villain dwelling in all Denmark / But he’s an arrant knave.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: There needs no ghost, my lord, come from the grave / To tell us this.

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect Horatio's probing while concealing the Ghost's revelation about Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder while navigating the treacherous court
beat_states.obstacle: He cannot reveal what the Ghost told him, but his friends are pressing him for information

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is agitated and burdened by the Ghost's command. He knows exactly who the villain is but is barely containing himself, producing wild, ironic speech that borders on manic deflection. His control is slipping even as he tries to maintain it.

social_state.rationale: He holds the status of prince and possessor of secret knowledge, but his warmth toward Horatio is temporarily suppressed by the urgency of concealment and the strain of the encounter

known_facts: Claudius murdered his father
The Ghost has commanded revenge
He must not reveal this to Horatio and Marcellus

The Ghost's command to avenge the murder
The method of the murder
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet possesses the full truth but deliberately speaks in absurd generalities — 'there's never a villain in Denmark but he's an arrant knave' — a tautology designed to say nothing while his mind screams everything

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement through antic disposition — using absurd, obvious truisms to mask the specific, devastating truth
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He burns to name Claudius as villain but must hide the knowledge behind a banal generalization that says everything and nothing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be genuinely struggling to process the revelation and the banality is not strategic but symptomatic of psychological overwhelm

beat_states.desire_state: To get Hamlet to speak plainly about what the Ghost revealed
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect and support Hamlet through loyal friendship and rational counsel
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is evading with absurd generalizations rather than confiding in him

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Why, right, you are in the right. / And so, without more circumstance at all, / I hold it fit that we shake hands and part, / You, as your business and desire shall point you / ( For every man hath business and desire, / Such as it is), and for my own poor part, / I will go pray.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: These are but wild and whirling words, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I am sorry they offend you, heartily; / Yes, faith, heartily.

utterances.text: There’s no offense, my lord.

beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder while navigating a world of deception
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio's genuine concern and perceptiveness see through Hamlet's deflections, making clean disengagement difficult

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is internally shattered by the Ghost's revelation, highly agitated but trying to perform nonchalance. His 'wild and whirling words' betray his loss of control. The shift to mock-sincere apology ('Yes, faith, heartily') shows him oscillating between genuine feeling and performative irony.

social_state.rationale: He retains princely authority but deploys it erratically. His warmth toward Horatio is real but suppressed — the ironic, dismissive tone creates artificial distance from his dearest friend.

Horatio can see through his erratic behavior

beat_states.defense_state: Manic evasiveness — using rapid, disjointed speech and false casualness to prevent further questioning
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He desperately needs his closest friend's support but must isolate himself to protect the secret and begin his mission; his feigned lightness masks existential dread
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'I will go pray' could be entirely sincere rather than deflective — he may genuinely feel spiritually overwhelmed and in need of divine guidance after encountering a ghost and learning of fratricide.

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what has happened to Hamlet and to reach him through his erratic behavior
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be a loyal, stabilizing presence and truth-seeker for his friend and prince
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's deliberate obfuscation and manic deflection prevent genuine connection

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is worried and unsettled by Hamlet's behavior. His observation about 'wild and whirling words' is measured but betrays alarm. His quick 'There's no offense' retreats from confrontation, showing he's navigating between concern and deference.

social_state.rationale: Horatio is subordinate but speaks with the earned intimacy of a trusted friend. His directness ('wild and whirling words') is an act of care, and his immediate reassurance ('There's no offense') preserves relational warmth.

known_facts: The Ghost appeared and spoke privately with Hamlet
Hamlet's behavior has changed dramatically since the encounter
He has sworn an oath of secrecy
Hamlet is deliberately concealing what the Ghost said

beat_states.defense_state: Rational composure — naming what he sees plainly as a way to anchor the situation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants to press harder to help his friend but must respect the boundary Hamlet is setting as both friend and prince
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: 'There's no offense' could carry a subtle note of hurt or frustration rather than pure diplomatic smoothing — Horatio may feel genuinely stung by being shut out.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Yes, by Saint Patrick, but there is, Horatio, / And much offense, too. Touching this vision here, / It is an honest ghost— that let me tell you. / For your desire to know what is between us, / O’ermaster ’t as you may. And now, good friends, / As you are friends, scholars, and soldiers, / Give me one poor request.

utterances.text: What is ’t, my lord? We will.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Never make known what you have seen tonight.

beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder while maintaining strategic concealment of his knowledge

social_state.rationale: He reasserts his princely authority ('give me one poor request' is a command framed as humility) while simultaneously invoking bonds of friendship and fellowship — 'as you are friends, scholars, and soldiers' — a warm but calculated appeal to multiple loyalties

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His scholarly, skeptical nature craves explanation, but his devotion to Hamlet compels him to accept mystery without question
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's immediate compliance may mask deeper alarm about Hamlet's mental state — his quick 'We will' could be an attempt to calm rather than genuine passive acceptance

beat_states.obstacle: Horatio's rational hesitation and the surreal disruption of the Ghost's voice from below, which threatens to unravel Hamlet's authority over the moment

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is wired, frenetic — the laughter is a pressure valve for enormous psychological strain. He's trying to direct a situation that keeps being interrupted by supernatural forces he cannot control. The familiar tone masks deep disorientation.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as leader of this conspiracy, directing Horatio, but his warmth toward Horatio is genuine — he needs his friend's loyalty. His casual address of the Ghost paradoxically lowers the Ghost's status to reclaim his own.

known_facts: His father was murdered by Claudius
The Ghost demands an oath of secrecy
Horatio and others witnessed the Ghost
He must adopt an 'antic disposition'
hidden_secrets: The full content of the Ghost's revelation about Claudius
His emerging plan to feign madness
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet holds the privileged knowledge from the Ghost and is now managing its containment — he knows things Horatio does not and must keep it that way

beat_states.defense_state: Manic humor and familiar bravado ('Ha, ha, boy, sayst thou so? Art thou there, truepenny?') — deflecting the terror of the supernatural through wild, almost hysterical playfulness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He treats his father's ghost with jocular irreverence ('boy,' 'truepenny,' 'fellow in the cellarage') while simultaneously obeying the Ghost's command to secure the oath — a deep split between grief/reverence and the need to maintain control through antic posturing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's jocular tone toward the Ghost could indicate he is already beginning to doubt the Ghost's authenticity, testing it, or beginning the antic disposition immediately

beat_states.desire_state: To comply with Hamlet's request while processing the deeply unsettling supernatural event
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be a faithful friend and rational support to Hamlet in a world that has become irrational
beat_states.obstacle: His rationalist worldview is being shattered by the Ghost's voice from beneath the earth; he is being asked to swear to something he does not fully understand

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is deeply unsettled — the Ghost's voice from the cellarage is profoundly disturbing. His brief, compliant line suggests a man overwhelmed, ceding control to Hamlet, uncertain of everything except his loyalty.

social_state.rationale: He explicitly defers to Hamlet ('my lord'), accepting subordinate status. His warmth is expressed through willingness to follow — loyalty under extreme duress.

known_facts: A ghost appeared resembling the late King Hamlet
The Ghost spoke privately with Hamlet
Something deeply significant was communicated
The Ghost's voice now comes from beneath the ground
false_beliefs: He may assume the Ghost's message was less earth-shattering than it actually was
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio knows a great deal about the appearance but nothing about the content of the Ghost's revelation; he is operating on trust

beat_states.defense_state: Formal deference ('Propose the oath, my lord') — retreating into protocol and duty as a way to manage overwhelming strangeness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His philosophical rationalism wars with the undeniable evidence of the supernatural; he submits to an oath whose full implications he cannot grasp
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's 'Propose the oath, my lord' could be read as subtly testing whether Hamlet is in his right mind — offering compliance as a way to monitor Hamlet's mental state

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Hic et ubique? Then we’ll shift our ground. / Come hither, gentlemen, / And lay your hands again upon my sword. / Swear by my sword / Never to speak of this that you have heard.

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: Swear by his sword.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Well said, old mole. Canst work i’ th’ earth so fast?— / A worthy pioner! Once more remove, good friends.

beat_states.desire_state: To ensure the oath of secrecy is properly sworn, reinforcing Hamlet's command over his companions
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure revenge against Claudius by empowering and binding Hamlet to the task
beat_states.obstacle: The living may not take the oath seriously enough without supernatural enforcement

affect_state.rationale: The Ghost is resolute and purposeful. Its repeated interventions show high certainty and determination. It is not vulnerable — it operates from beyond death — but there is a restless urgency suggesting unresolved anguish driving its insistence.

social_state.rationale: The Ghost exerts supernatural authority, commanding from below the earth. There is no warmth here — this is pure enforcement. Its status is derived from its otherworldly nature and its identity as the former king.

known_facts: Claudius murdered him
Hamlet has been told the truth
The companions have not yet properly sworn
Secrecy is vital to the revenge plot
hidden_secrets: The full nature of purgatorial suffering
Whether it is truly a benevolent spirit or something else
epistemic_state.rationale: The Ghost operates with total knowledge of the crime and clear intentionality about what must happen next. Its epistemic position is one of certainty — it knows the truth and demands others be bound to protect it.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Reduced from the full pathos of a murdered king to a disembodied voice underground — majestic authority expressed through an almost comically insistent repetition
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Ghost's underground shifting and insistent repetition could suggest a demonic entity manipulating Hamlet rather than the genuine spirit of his father — a possibility the play itself leaves open

beat_states.desire_state: To bind Horatio and Marcellus to absolute secrecy through a sacred oath, reinforced by the Ghost's supernatural authority
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the Ghost's command of revenge while maintaining strategic control over who knows what
beat_states.obstacle: The Ghost keeps shifting position underground, disrupting the solemnity of the oath ceremony and unsettling everyone

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is electrically charged with nervous energy. His jocular tone ('old mole') barely conceals profound disorientation. He is trying to maintain authority but the Ghost's unpredictable underground movements undermine his sense of control. High arousal, low certainty about what is happening.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet asserts leadership over his friends, directing them physically and demanding oaths. His warmth is minimal — 'good friends' is perfunctory — because he is operating in command mode. Yet his playful address to the Ghost ('old mole') creates a strange democratization of the supernatural.

known_facts: The Ghost has revealed Claudius murdered King Hamlet
The Ghost is moving beneath the earth and can speak from below
Horatio and Marcellus have not yet sworn a satisfactory oath
Secrecy is essential to any future action
hidden_secrets: The full content of the Ghost's revelation about Claudius
His emerging plan to feign madness
The depth of his psychological disturbance
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet possesses critical knowledge that he must protect. His antic behavior here may already be a rehearsal for the 'antic disposition' he will shortly announce. He knows more than anyone else on stage about the murder.

beat_states.defense_state: Manic humor and irreverence ('old mole,' 'worthy pioner') used to mask terror and assert mastery over a situation that is deeply uncanny
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Deeply shaken by the supernatural reality of the Ghost moving beneath the earth, yet performing jovial familiarity with it as if it were a game — terror disguised as wit
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may genuinely be losing psychological coherence here rather than strategically performing — the manic wit could be incipient madness rather than calculated deflection

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: O day and night, but this is wondrous strange.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: And therefore as a stranger give it welcome. / There are more things in heaven and earth, Horatio, / Than are dreamt of in your philosophy. But come. / Here, as before, never, so help you mercy, / How strange or odd some’er I bear myself / ( As I perchance hereafter shall think meet / To put an antic disposition on) / That you, at such times seeing me, never shall, / With arms encumbered thus, or this headshake, / Or by pronouncing of some doubtful phrase, / As “ Well, well, we know,” or “ We could an if we would,” / Or “ If we list to speak,” or “ There be an if they might,” / Or such ambiguous giving-out, to note / That you know aught of me— this do swear, / So grace and mercy at your most need help you.

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: Swear.

beat_states.desire_state: To ensure the oath of secrecy is sealed absolutely
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To achieve vengeance against Claudius through Hamlet's action
beat_states.obstacle: The limitations of its spectral existence — it can only intervene with a single word from beneath the earth

affect_state.rationale: The ghost is singular in purpose — absolute certainty about what must happen. Its third repetition of 'Swear' carries the weight of supernatural compulsion. Arousal is elevated but contained in the economy of utterance.

social_state.rationale: The ghost commands from a position of absolute supernatural authority. In this iteration, the fatherly warmth has receded entirely; it functions as a binding, almost juridical force.

known_facts: The truth of its own murder by Claudius
Hamlet has accepted the charge of revenge
The oath has not yet been fully secured from all witnesses
hidden_secrets: The full nature of its purgatorial existence and what drives its urgency
epistemic_state.rationale: The ghost possesses perfect knowledge of the crime and acts with complete conviction. Its repeated interventions suggest awareness that the oath must be ironclad.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: The ghost operates as both a loving father concerned for his son and an implacable force demanding blood vengeance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The ghost's insistent repetition could be read as demonic compulsion rather than paternal protection — a reading that would align with Hamlet's own later doubts about the ghost's nature.

beat_states.desire_state: To bind Horatio and Marcellus to absolute secrecy about the ghost and about his future feigned madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder while navigating a court of deception
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio's rational skepticism and the enormity of what he's asking them to conceal

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet has seized control of the situation, channeling his shock into strategic planning. He's energized and purposeful but the negative valence reflects the terrible burden of knowledge. His certainty is moderate — he knows what he must do tactically but the larger mission remains daunting.

social_state.rationale: He has assumed full authority over his friends, issuing commands and demanding sacred oaths. The warmth is present but muted — he addresses Horatio with some intellectual camaraderie ('your philosophy') but the dominant register is imperious.

known_facts: His father was murdered by Claudius
The ghost has commanded revenge
He will need to disguise his intentions at court
Horatio and Marcellus are witnesses to the ghost
hidden_secrets: The specific content of the ghost's revelation about Claudius
His plan to adopt an antic disposition as strategic cover
The depth of his uncertainty about how to proceed with revenge
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet possesses explosive knowledge about the murder and is now actively managing information flow. He reveals the antic disposition plan to his friends precisely to prevent them from inadvertently exposing him, but withholds the ghost's specific accusations.

beat_states.defense_state: Preemptive concealment — announcing the antic disposition as a strategic mask before anyone can interpret his future behavior as genuine madness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is simultaneously planning calculated deception (the antic disposition) while being genuinely destabilized by the ghost's revelation; his controlled strategic thinking masks deep inner turmoil
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's announcement of the antic disposition could be read not as calculated strategy but as a genuine premonition that he is losing his grip on sanity — a preemptive excuse rather than a plan.

beat_states.desire_state: To make sense of what he has witnessed and to express his genuine bewilderment
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be a loyal, rational companion to Hamlet while maintaining intellectual integrity
beat_states.obstacle: The supernatural events completely defy his philosophical framework; he cannot process what he's experiencing

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is deeply unsettled. His single line is an eruption of overwhelm from a man who typically maintains composure. His certainty about reality has been shattered, and he has little control over the escalating situation Hamlet is orchestrating.

social_state.rationale: Horatio is in a subordinate position, being commanded by Hamlet. His warmth reflects genuine concern for and loyalty to his friend, even as he's being swept along.

known_facts: A ghost appeared and spoke to Hamlet privately
The ghost's voice has followed them across the platform
Hamlet is behaving erratically and intensely
false_beliefs: That his philosophy should be able to account for all phenomena
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio knows something extraordinary has occurred but lacks the crucial information about what the ghost actually said. Hamlet's rebuke about his philosophy stings precisely because it's true.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — framing the experience as 'wondrous strange' to contain it within rational language
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His empirical, scholarly mind is being forced to accept phenomena it cannot explain, creating genuine epistemic crisis
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's 'wondrous strange' could be a subtle attempt to slow Hamlet down, to introduce caution before Hamlet commits to whatever rash course he's planning.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Rest, rest, perturbèd spirit.— So, gentlemen, / With all my love I do commend me to you, / And what so poor a man as Hamlet is / May do t’ express his love and friending to you, / God willing, shall not lack. Let us go in together, / And still your fingers on your lips, I pray. / The time is out of joint. O cursèd spite / That ever I was born to set it right! / Nay, come, let’s go together.

beat_states.desire_state: To bind his friends to secrecy, dismiss the ghost, and steel himself for the burden of revenge
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty to his father by avenging his murder and restoring moral order
beat_states.obstacle: The crushing weight of the task ahead, his isolation, and the enormity of what he now knows versus what he can share

affect_state.rationale: The famous couplet ('The time is out of joint...') reveals deep anguish and reluctant acceptance. He knows what he must do (high certainty) but feels overwhelmed by it (low control). The tender farewell to friends and the bitter aside show a man caught between love and despair, the arousal tempered by exhaustion after the ghost encounter.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as humble ('so poor a man') but simultaneously commands the situation — giving orders, demanding silence, leading them offstage. His warmth is genuine; he reaches out to his friends with real affection precisely because he senses the loneliness ahead.

known_facts: Claudius murdered his father by poison in the ear
Gertrude may have been complicit or seduced before the murder
The ghost demands revenge but forbids harm to Gertrude
He must conceal all of this from everyone, including these loyal friends
He may adopt an 'antic disposition' to cover his actions
hidden_secrets: The full content of the ghost's revelation about Claudius's fratricide
His nascent plan to feign madness
The depth of his despair and sense of doom about the task
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet possesses devastating knowledge he cannot share. The 'rest, rest, perturbèd spirit' confirms he believes the ghost is his father's true spirit. He has no false beliefs at this moment — his epistemological crisis has temporarily resolved into grim certainty.

beat_states.defense_state: Self-deprecation ('so poor a man as Hamlet') masks the terrifying new knowledge and authority he carries; performative humility deflects from the radical shift in his inner world
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He expresses warm love and fellowship toward Horatio and Marcellus while simultaneously sealing himself into total isolation by withholding everything he knows; he accepts the mission to 'set it right' while cursing that he was born to do it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The 'cursèd spite' lines could be read not as reluctant acceptance but as a moment of grandiosity — Hamlet casting himself as the singular chosen agent of cosmic justice, feeding a messianic self-conception that will ultimately paralyze rather than motivate him.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Give him this money and these notes, Reynaldo.

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: I will, my lord.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: You shall do marvelous wisely, good Reynaldo, / Before you visit him, to make inquire / Of his behavior.

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: My lord, I did intend it.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Marry, well said, very well said. Look you, sir, / Inquire me first what Danskers are in Paris; / And how, and who, what means, and where they keep, / What company, at what expense; and finding / By this encompassment and drift of question / That they do know my son, come you more nearer / Than your particular demands will touch it. / Take you, as ’twere, some distant knowledge of him, / As thus: “ I know his father and his friends / And, in part, him.” Do you mark this, Reynaldo?

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: Ay, very well, my lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To ensure Reynaldo understands and executes an elaborate surveillance scheme on Laertes in Paris
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain control over his family and his position at court through intelligence and manipulation
beat_states.obstacle: Reynaldo may not grasp the subtlety of the indirect espionage method Polonius is devising; the distance to Paris makes direct control impossible

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is energized and self-satisfied in his scheming, feeling in command of the situation. He relishes his own cleverness in devising the indirect approach. Mild positive affect from exercising authority and intellect.

social_state.rationale: Polonius commands from a position of clear authority over his servant. His warmth is superficial — 'good Reynaldo,' 'very well said' — these are patronizing compliments designed to keep Reynaldo compliant, not genuine warmth.

known_facts: Laertes is in Paris
Reynaldo is a trusted servant who can be sent as an agent
There are Danish expatriates in Paris who may know Laertes
Indirect questioning is more effective than direct inquiry
hidden_secrets: He fundamentally distrusts his own son's behavior abroad
His need for control extends to surveillance of his own children
false_beliefs: He believes his scheme is brilliantly subtle and unique when it is an overwrought, convoluted plan
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius operates from a position of assumed superior knowledge about human nature and espionage, but his elaborate scheming reveals more about his own anxious need for control than about any real threat from Laertes.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — transforms parental anxiety into an elaborate procedural scheme, distancing himself from the emotional vulnerability of not trusting his own son
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presents himself as a loving, providing father (sending money and notes) while simultaneously orchestrating covert surveillance on his son, revealing deep distrust masked as care
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius could be read as genuinely worried about Laertes' safety and well-being in a foreign city, with the surveillance stemming from protective love rather than controlling anxiety

beat_states.desire_state: To receive clear instructions, demonstrate competence, and be dismissed to carry out his task
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position and favor by serving Polonius reliably
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius is overexplaining and micromanaging a task Reynaldo had already planned to do, requiring patience and performed deference

affect_state.rationale: Neutral to slightly suppressed affect. Low arousal — he is enduring a lecture. Low control because he cannot steer the conversation or hasten its conclusion. Some vulnerability as a subordinate who must perform attentiveness.

social_state.rationale: Clearly subordinate, addressing Polonius as 'my lord' repeatedly. His warmth is minimal — professional and dutiful rather than personally invested.

known_facts: He is being sent to Paris to deliver money and notes to Laertes
He is also expected to spy on Laertes' behavior
He had already planned to make inquiries independently
hidden_secrets: He may find Polonius's elaborate instructions unnecessary or tedious
epistemic_state.rationale: Reynaldo is competent and already ahead of Polonius in planning, but his subordinate position requires him to receive instructions as though they are new information.

beat_states.defense_state: compliance — suppresses any irritation or sense of redundancy by performing eager agreement
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He already intended to inquire about Laertes' behavior ('I did intend it') but must perform as though Polonius's elaborate instructions are revelatory and necessary
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Reynaldo could be genuinely impressed by Polonius's cunning and grateful for the detailed guidance, rather than merely tolerating it

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: “ And, in part, him, but,” you may say, “ not well. / But if ’t be he I mean, he’s very wild, / Addicted so and so.” And there put on him / What forgeries you please— marry, none so rank / As may dishonor him, take heed of that, / But, sir, such wanton, wild, and usual slips / As are companions noted and most known / To youth and liberty.

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: As gaming, my lord.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Ay, or drinking, fencing, swearing, / Quarreling, drabbing— you may go so far.

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: My lord, that would dishonor him.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Faith, no, as you may season it in the charge. / You must not put another scandal on him / That he is open to incontinency; / That’s not my meaning. But breathe his faults so quaintly / That they may seem the taints of liberty, / The flash and outbreak of a fiery mind, / A savageness in unreclaimèd blood, / Of general assault.

beat_states.desire_state: To instruct Reynaldo precisely how to spread calculated, carefully calibrated rumors about Laertes to smoke out his son's true behavior in Paris
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain control over his family and political position through surveillance and manipulation of information
beat_states.obstacle: Reynaldo's literal-mindedness and moral hesitation threaten to derail the subtlety of the scheme

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is in his element — scheming, lecturing, enjoying his own cleverness. High sense of control as the master manipulator instructing a subordinate. Mild pleasure in the intellectual exercise of calibrating exactly how much dishonor is acceptable.

social_state.rationale: Fully dominant over Reynaldo as employer and social superior. Warmth is minimal — this is transactional and instrumental, with Reynaldo treated as a tool rather than a confidant.

known_facts: Laertes is in Paris and beyond direct observation
Young men commonly engage in gambling, drinking, and other vices
Indirect inquiry through planted rumors can extract truth from acquaintances
hidden_secrets: His deep anxiety about losing control over his children's actions and reputation
The scheme reveals his fundamental distrust of Laertes despite public shows of paternal affection
false_beliefs: That this level of surveillance is normal and proportionate parental concern
That he can perfectly calibrate the damage of planted rumors
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius operates from a spy-master's epistemology — he assumes truth is always hidden and must be extracted through indirect, deceptive means. He believes he can control information flow with precision.

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — reframing espionage and slander as reasonable parental prudence
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to protect Laertes's honor while actively instructing someone to spread false rumors about him; loves his son yet treats him as an object to be managed
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may genuinely fear Laertes has fallen into serious vice and this elaborate scheme masks real parental terror rather than mere controlling behavior

beat_states.desire_state: To understand Polonius's instructions clearly and fulfill his duty without crossing ethical lines that trouble him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve his master competently while preserving his own moral comfort and standing
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius's instructions seem to require dishonoring Laertes, which conflicts with Reynaldo's sense of propriety and loyalty

affect_state.rationale: Mildly uncomfortable and uncertain — trying to follow convoluted logic while sensing the scheme is morally dubious. Low control as a subordinate who must ultimately comply. His objections ('that would dishonor him') show genuine unease.

social_state.rationale: Clearly subordinate, receiving orders. His objection shows a modest warmth or loyalty toward Laertes — or at minimum a servant's instinct to protect the family honor he serves.

known_facts: He is being sent to Paris to spy on Laertes
He must spread specific types of rumors to extract information
Polonius wants to know Laertes's true behavior abroad
hidden_secrets: His private opinion of Polonius's scheme and whether he finds it excessive or foolish
epistemic_state.rationale: Reynaldo is a practical man receiving instructions. He grasps the mechanics but pushes back on the ethical implications, suggesting he thinks more clearly about reputational damage than Polonius admits to.

beat_states.defense_state: moral objection as boundary-setting — raises concerns to limit how far he must go
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Serves a master he must obey but whose schemes make him uncomfortable; complicit in manipulation while voicing ethical reservations
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Reynaldo's objections may be merely performative — seeking explicit permission to cover himself rather than expressing genuine moral concern

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: But, my good lord—

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Wherefore should you do this?

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: Ay, my lord, I would know that.

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain control of the conversation and preemptively address Reynaldo's objection before he can fully voice it
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain absolute control over information and people in order to secure his family's position and his own power at court
beat_states.obstacle: Reynaldo's interruption threatens to derail his carefully constructed scheme; he may sense Reynaldo doubts the plan

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is energized and self-satisfied, enjoying the performance of his own cunning. He feels in command of the exchange, anticipating and co-opting Reynaldo's question before it can become a challenge.

social_state.rationale: He asserts dominance by speaking Reynaldo's question for him, treating him as a tool rather than a collaborator. Minimal warmth — this is a master instructing a subordinate.

known_facts: Laertes is in Paris and needs surveillance
The indirect method of spreading mild slanders will draw out true information about Laertes' behavior
Reynaldo is a loyal servant who will follow orders
hidden_secrets: His deep anxiety about Laertes' conduct abroad and what it could mean for the family's reputation
false_beliefs: He believes his convoluted instructions are brilliantly clear
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius is confident in his espionage methodology but blind to how confusing and circuitous his delivery is. He mistakes Reynaldo's confusion for a question about purpose rather than comprehension.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — turning Reynaldo's objection into a rhetorical question he can answer on his own terms
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is so eager to demonstrate his cleverness that he keeps interrupting the very servant he needs to instruct clearly
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may have momentarily lost his train of thought and is using the rhetorical question to buy himself time to remember where he was going

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what Polonius actually wants him to do — to get clarity on confusing and morally questionable instructions
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To faithfully serve his master while protecting his own sense of propriety and competence
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius keeps interrupting and speaking over him, preventing him from voicing his genuine confusion and objection

affect_state.rationale: Reynaldo is confused and slightly frustrated. He lacks certainty about the plan and feels little control over the conversation. His vulnerability comes from being unable to challenge his master directly.

social_state.rationale: Clearly subordinate, deferential. His 'Ay, my lord' signals compliance and respect even as he asserts a small need for understanding. Warmth is neutral-low — professional rather than personal.

known_facts: He is being sent to Paris to spy on Laertes
He is to spread mild slanders about Laertes to draw out information
Polonius has given him money and notes to deliver
hidden_secrets: He likely finds the scheme distasteful or foolish but cannot say so
epistemic_state.rationale: Reynaldo grasps the basic outline but genuinely doesn't understand the logic of why spreading false rumors would yield truthful information. His 'I would know that' is sincere.

beat_states.defense_state: Deference — couching his confusion as polite agreement rather than outright challenge
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants to object to the scheme but his subordinate position forces him to frame his objection as mere curiosity
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Reynaldo may be subtly pushing back, using feigned confusion as a way to force Polonius to justify an ethically dubious plan

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Marry, sir, here’s my drift, / And I believe it is a fetch of wit. / You, laying these slight sullies on my son, / As ’twere a thing a little soiled i’ th’ working, / Mark you, your party in converse, him you would sound, / Having ever seen in the prenominate crimes / The youth you breathe of guilty, be assured / He closes with you in this consequence: / “ Good sir,” or so, or “ friend,” or “ gentleman,” / According to the phrase or the addition / Of man and country—

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: Very good, my lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To make Reynaldo fully grasp and admire the cunning logic of his indirect espionage scheme
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain absolute control over his family and their reputations through surveillance and manipulation
beat_states.obstacle: Reynaldo's slowness to understand the scheme and Polonius's own tendency to lose himself in elaborate explanation

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is enjoying himself — relishing the unfolding of his own cleverness. He feels in command of the conversation and the scheme, with elevated energy as he builds toward his point. Low vulnerability because he's performing mastery, though his need to explain at such length hints at underlying need for validation.

social_state.rationale: Polonius occupies clear authority as master instructing servant. His warmth is minimal — Reynaldo is an instrument, not a companion. The occasional 'sir' and conversational framing is performative rather than genuinely warm.

known_facts: Laertes is in Paris and beyond direct observation
Indirect methods of gathering intelligence through planted rumors can reveal truth
Social convention dictates how strangers will respond to insinuations about a mutual acquaintance
hidden_secrets: His deep anxiety about Laertes's behavior abroad, masked as casual prudence
His fundamental distrust of his own children's autonomy
false_beliefs: He believes this scheme is a brilliant 'fetch of wit' rather than an overcomplicated and potentially damaging approach
He assumes he can predict and control the social responses of strangers in Paris
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius operates from a worldview where human behavior is mechanistic and predictable, and where his manipulations are more sophisticated than they actually are. His knowledge of courtly social mechanics is real but his confidence in predicting outcomes is inflated.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — wrapping a morally dubious surveillance scheme in the language of wit and artful reasoning
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He believes himself brilliantly strategic, yet his explanation is convoluted and self-admiring, suggesting insecurity about whether his cleverness is recognized
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may be stalling and improvising the scheme as he speaks, with less premeditation than he claims — his verbosity could indicate he's constructing the plan in real time rather than revealing a pre-formed strategy.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: At “ closes in the consequence”— ay, marry— / He closes thus: “ I know the gentleman. / I saw him yesterday,” or “ th’ other day” / ( Or then, or then, with such or such), “ and as you say, / There was he gaming, there o’ertook in ’s rouse, / There falling out at tennis”; or perchance / “ I saw him enter such a house of sale”— / Videlicet, a brothel— or so forth. See you now / Your bait of falsehood take this carp of truth; / And thus do we of wisdom and of reach, / With windlasses and with assays of bias, / By indirections find directions out. / So by my former lecture and advice / Shall you my son. You have me, have you not?

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: My lord, I have.

beat_states.desire_state: To demonstrate and confirm that Reynaldo fully grasps the elaborate espionage scheme for spying on Laertes
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain total control over his family and their reputations through surveillance and manipulation

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: God be wi’ you. Fare you well.

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: Good my lord.

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO
utterances.text: I shall, my lord.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS

utterances.speaker: REYNALDO

beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain control over his family and protect his political position through surveillance and information

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: My lord, as I was sewing in my closet, / Lord Hamlet, with his doublet all unbraced, / No hat upon his head, his stockings fouled, / Ungartered, and down-gyvèd to his ankle, / Pale as his shirt, his knees knocking each other, / And with a look so piteous in purport / As if he had been loosèd out of hell / To speak of horrors— he comes before me.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Mad for thy love?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: My lord, I do not know, / But truly I do fear it.

beat_states.desire_state: to unburden herself of a terrifying encounter and seek her father's guidance and protection
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be a dutiful daughter while navigating her genuine feelings for Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: she is frightened and confused by Hamlet's behavior, unsure whether his madness is real or connected to her rejection of him

social_state.rationale: She is supplicant to her father, deferring completely ('My lord'), but her warmth is subdued — she is too frightened for tenderness, though genuine concern for Hamlet bleeds through.

He believes he can manage this situation to his advantage at court

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — immediately converting his daughter's distress into a political puzzle he can solve
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he caused this situation by ordering Ophelia to reject Hamlet, yet he leaps to self-congratulatory diagnosis rather than guilt
