utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: What said he?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: He took me by the wrist and held me hard. / Then goes he to the length of all his arm, / And, with his other hand thus o’er his brow, / He falls to such perusal of my face / As he would draw it. Long stayed he so. / At last, a little shaking of mine arm, / And thrice his head thus waving up and down, / He raised a sigh so piteous and profound / As it did seem to shatter all his bulk / And end his being. That done, he lets me go, / And, with his head over his shoulder turned, / He seemed to find his way without his eyes, / For out o’ doors he went without their helps / And to the last bended their light on me.

beat_states.desire_state: to faithfully convey the overwhelming experience of Hamlet's visit and to be believed and comforted by her father
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be a dutiful daughter and loyal beloved — to reconcile obedience to her father with her love for Hamlet

beat_states.obstacle: he must rely entirely on Ophelia's account and cannot verify it himself

social_state.rationale: He operates as father-interrogator, maintaining authority over Ophelia; warmth is minimal as he treats her account as intelligence rather than attending to her emotional distress.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Come, go with me. I will go seek the King. / This is the very ecstasy of love, / Whose violent property fordoes itself / And leads the will to desperate undertakings / As oft as any passions under heaven / That does afflict our natures. I am sorry. / What, have you given him any hard words of late?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: No, my good lord, but as you did command / I did repel his letters and denied / His access to me.

His primary motivation is political advancement, not concern for Hamlet or Ophelia

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — framing Hamlet's disturbing behavior as a clinical category ('ecstasy of love') to maintain control over the narrative
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He claims to be 'sorry' about Hamlet's suffering while simultaneously being excited by the political opportunity this represents
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may genuinely feel some guilt for having caused this situation by ordering Ophelia to reject Hamlet, and 'I am sorry' may carry more authentic remorse than a purely political reading suggests.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: That hath made him mad. / I am sorry that with better heed and judgment / I had not coted him. I feared he did but trifle / And meant to wrack thee. But beshrew my jealousy! / By heaven, it is as proper to our age / To cast beyond ourselves in our opinions / As it is common for the younger sort / To lack discretion. Come, go we to the King. / This must be known, which, being kept close, might move / More grief to hide than hate to utter love. / Come.

beat_states.desire_state: To rush to the King with his diagnosis of Hamlet's madness, positioning himself as the one who has cracked the mystery while simultaneously absolving himself of blame for having misjudged the situation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain and elevate his position as indispensable counselor to the crown through superior insight into court affairs
beat_states.obstacle: His own prior misjudgment — he ordered Ophelia to reject Hamlet, potentially causing the prince's madness, which could reflect badly on him

affect_state.rationale: Energized by the urgency of bringing news to the King. His certainty is high — he's fully committed to the love-madness theory. But there's a flicker of genuine regret and vulnerability in admitting he was wrong, quickly papered over by self-serving rationalization. The rhyming couplet at the end signals his sense of mastery and closure.

social_state.rationale: He momentarily lowers himself by admitting error to Ophelia ('I am sorry'), showing unusual warmth, but quickly reasserts authority by commandeering the next move — going to the King. He speaks to Ophelia as someone whose role is simply to follow him ('Come, go we').

known_facts: Hamlet appeared before Ophelia in a state of apparent madness
He previously ordered Ophelia to reject Hamlet's advances
Hamlet's behavior fits the pattern of love-madness as Polonius understands it
This information must be reported to the King immediately
hidden_secrets: His deeper fear that he may have directly caused harm to the prince through his meddling, which could have serious political consequences for him
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is caused purely by unrequited love for Ophelia
Reporting this to the King will redound to his credit rather than his blame
Keeping this secret would cause more harm than revealing it
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius has constructed an airtight narrative — love rejected equals madness — and is now fully invested in it. He cannot see Hamlet's possible strategic performance of madness. His final couplet ('being kept close, might move / More grief to hide than hate to utter love') is self-serving reasoning dressed as wisdom: he frames disclosure as duty when it is really self-promotion.

beat_states.defense_state: Self-exculpation through philosophical generalization — reframing his error as a universal failing of old age ('proper to our age / To cast beyond ourselves') to diffuse personal responsibility
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is simultaneously apologizing for his misjudgment and rushing to exploit the very situation his misjudgment may have caused, turning his failure into political currency
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A more generous reading: Polonius is genuinely stricken by guilt that his over-cautious parenting has harmed a prince, and his rush to the King is a sincere attempt to remedy the situation — the philosophical generalization about old age reflecting real self-awareness rather than mere deflection.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Welcome, dear Rosencrantz and Guildenstern. / Moreover that we much did long to see you, / The need we have to use you did provoke / Our hasty sending. Something have you heard / Of Hamlet’s transformation, so call it, / Sith nor th’ exterior nor the inward man / Resembles that it was. What it should be, / More than his father’s death, that thus hath put him / So much from th’ understanding of himself / I cannot dream of. I entreat you both / That, being of so young days brought up with him / And sith so neighbored to his youth and havior, / That you vouchsafe your rest here in our court / Some little time, so by your companies / To draw him on to pleasures, and to gather / So much as from occasion you may glean, / Whether aught to us unknown afflicts him thus / That, opened, lies within our remedy.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Good gentlemen, he hath much talked of you, / And sure I am two men there is not living / To whom he more adheres. If it will please you / To show us so much gentry and goodwill / As to expend your time with us awhile / For the supply and profit of our hope, / Your visitation shall receive such thanks / As fits a king’s remembrance.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Both your Majesties / Might, by the sovereign power you have of us, / Put your dread pleasures more into command / Than to entreaty.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: But we both obey, / And here give up ourselves in the full bent / To lay our service freely at your feet, / To be commanded.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Thanks, Rosencrantz and gentle Guildenstern.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Thanks, Guildenstern and gentle Rosencrantz. / And I beseech you instantly to visit / My too much changèd son.— Go, some of you, / And bring these gentlemen where Hamlet is.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Heavens make our presence and our practices / Pleasant and helpful to him!

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Ay, amen!

beat_states.desire_state: To appear dutiful, loyal, and genuinely concerned for Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To advance his standing at court by serving the King's wishes
beat_states.obstacle: The tension between serving the crown and genuine friendship with Hamlet

affect_state.rationale: He's eager to please and somewhat nervous in the presence of royalty. His pious language may mask uncertainty about what they're being asked to do.

social_state.rationale: He is a courtier subordinate to the King and Queen, performing deference and warmth. His status is entirely dependent on royal favor.

known_facts: The King and Queen are worried about Hamlet
They've been summoned to court for this specific purpose
They are childhood friends of Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He may suspect the assignment is more surveillance than therapy
false_beliefs: He may believe he can genuinely help Hamlet while also serving the King's intelligence needs
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern knows what he's been asked to do but may not fully grasp the political stakes or how thoroughly he's being used as an instrument.

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — framing the spying mission as a benevolent act ('pleasant and helpful')
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He invokes heaven's blessing on an enterprise that is fundamentally deceptive — asking God to sanctify surveillance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern may be entirely sincere in his wish to help Hamlet, with no awareness yet of the ethical compromise involved

beat_states.desire_state: To dispatch Rosencrantz and Guildenstern efficiently to spy on Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne and neutralize any threat Hamlet poses to his power
beat_states.obstacle: He cannot appear manipulative; he must maintain the facade of benevolent concern

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is satisfied that his plan is in motion. He's measured and controlled, performing gratitude while maintaining strategic command of the situation.

social_state.rationale: He performs warmth and graciousness from a position of absolute authority, but the warmth is calculated — these are tools, not friends.

known_facts: Hamlet's behavior has changed dramatically
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are childhood friends of Hamlet
They have agreed to the mission
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
His primary motive is intelligence-gathering to assess the threat Hamlet poses
false_beliefs: He may believe this scheme will be sufficient to uncover Hamlet's intentions
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius knows the surface facts about Hamlet's transformation but not its true cause — which is ironic since he is the cause.

beat_states.defense_state: projection — framing his surveillance operation as paternal concern
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He frames espionage as care, thanking instruments of manipulation as if they were honored guests

beat_states.desire_state: To get Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to Hamlet immediately so her son might be helped
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To restore her son to health and maintain the harmony of her family and marriage
beat_states.obstacle: She doesn't understand what's wrong with Hamlet and feels urgency and helplessness

affect_state.rationale: Her urgency ('instantly to visit') reveals maternal anxiety. The 'too much changèd son' phrase carries grief and bewilderment. Her 'Ay, amen!' is earnest, even desperate.

social_state.rationale: She mirrors Claudius's thanks but adds genuine maternal warmth and urgency. She speaks from queenly authority but her emotional need is transparent.

known_facts: Hamlet has undergone a dramatic personality change
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern knew Hamlet in youth
She married Claudius shortly after King Hamlet's death
hidden_secrets: She may suppress awareness that her hasty marriage contributed to Hamlet's distress
false_beliefs: She likely believes this intervention will help Hamlet
She may not suspect Claudius of murder
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude operates from partial knowledge and maternal instinct, unable or unwilling to examine the deeper causes of Hamlet's suffering.

beat_states.defense_state: denial — she attributes Hamlet's transformation to an unspecified malady rather than confronting its possible roots in her marriage
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She genuinely loves Hamlet and wants to help him, yet she is complicit in the scheme to spy on him through his friends
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: She may be more politically astute than she appears, performing maternal concern to maintain alliance with Claudius's surveillance agenda

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Th’ ambassadors from Norway, my good lord, / Are joyfully returned.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Thou still hast been the father of good news.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Have I, my lord? I assure my good liege / I hold my duty as I hold my soul, / Both to my God and to my gracious king, / And I do think, or else this brain of mine / Hunts not the trail of policy so sure / As it hath used to do, that I have found / The very cause of Hamlet’s lunacy.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: O, speak of that! That do I long to hear.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Give first admittance to th’ ambassadors. / My news shall be the fruit to that great feast.

beat_states.desire_state: To immediately learn the cause of Hamlet's madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne and neutralize any threat Hamlet poses to his reign
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius is withholding the key information, teasing it out slowly and insisting on protocol

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is hopeful at the prospect of an explanation for Hamlet's lunacy but anxious about what it might reveal. His eagerness ('O, speak of that!') betrays a loss of composed control. He's vulnerable because Hamlet's madness could expose his crime.

social_state.rationale: Maintains royal authority but extends unusual warmth and flattery to Polonius ('father of good news') as a strategic move to encourage disclosure.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving strangely/mad
The Norway ambassadors have returned with news
Polonius claims to know the cause of Hamlet's lunacy
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He fears Hamlet may know or suspect the truth
false_beliefs: Polonius may genuinely have found the cause of Hamlet's behavior
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is desperate for any explanation for Hamlet's madness that is NOT the truth — that Hamlet suspects the murder. He seizes on Polonius's claim with intense hope precisely because a benign explanation would relieve his guilt-driven paranoia.

beat_states.defense_state: flattering Polonius to extract information faster
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Wants to appear composed and kingly but is desperate and anxious about Hamlet's behavior
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius's flattery of Polonius could be entirely performative court politeness rather than genuine urgency, but the exclamatory 'O, speak of that!' suggests real emotional need.

beat_states.desire_state: To build suspense and maximize his own importance before revealing his discovery about the cause of Hamlet's madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain and elevate his position as the King's most indispensable counselor
beat_states.obstacle: The King wants the information immediately, which would undercut Polonius's dramatic staging of the reveal

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is relishing this moment — he has two pieces of good news and is savoring the power of being the bearer. High certainty because he believes he has correctly diagnosed Hamlet. High control because he is managing the sequence of revelations. Low vulnerability because he feels secure in the King's favor.

social_state.rationale: Nominally deferential to the King but functionally controlling the interaction by dictating the order of business. Warmth is moderate — performing loyalty rather than genuine intimacy.

known_facts: The Norway ambassadors have returned successfully
Hamlet has written love letters to Ophelia
Ophelia has rejected Hamlet on his orders
Hamlet's behavior changed after Ophelia's rejection
hidden_secrets: He ordered Ophelia to cut off contact with Hamlet
He has read Hamlet's private letters to Ophelia
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is caused by lovesickness for Ophelia
His political instincts are as sharp as ever
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius has constructed a plausible but incorrect causal narrative: rejected love equals madness. He is confident in this reading, blind to deeper political and psychological realities, and unaware that his own meddling may have complicated matters.

beat_states.defense_state: Wrapping self-promotion in protestations of humble duty
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims humble duty to God and King while actively manipulating the moment for maximum personal glory
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius's delaying tactic ('Give first admittance to th' ambassadors') could be read as genuine courtly protocol rather than self-aggrandizement, but the 'fruit to that great feast' metaphor reveals he is consciously staging a climax.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Thyself do grace to them and bring them in. / He tells me, my dear Gertrude, he hath found / The head and source of all your son’s distemper.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: I doubt it is no other but the main— / His father’s death and our o’erhasty marriage.

beat_states.desire_state: To share the promising news that Polonius has found the cause of Hamlet's madness, redirecting attention away from any political or moral causes
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne and neutralize all threats to his power, including Hamlet's dangerous behavior
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude may not accept Polonius's explanation and may instead point to the true cause—their hasty marriage and the murder

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is cautiously optimistic, performing confidence in Polonius's diagnosis. He is eager to have a manageable explanation for Hamlet's behavior, but there's underlying anxiety about what the real cause might be.

social_state.rationale: He addresses Gertrude intimately ('my dear Gertrude') to maintain the appearance of a caring husband and co-ruler, asserting his role as the one who receives and manages intelligence.

known_facts: Polonius claims to have found the cause of Hamlet's madness
Hamlet has been behaving erratically
He himself murdered Old Hamlet
He has deployed Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to spy on Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He murdered his brother to take the throne and Gertrude
He fears Hamlet's madness may be connected to knowledge of the murder
false_beliefs: Polonius's theory about Hamlet's madness may be the full explanation
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius wants to believe Polonius's diagnosis because a lovesick Hamlet is far less dangerous than a Hamlet who suspects regicide. He doesn't yet know what Polonius's specific theory is.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection—seizing on Polonius's theory to avoid confronting the guilt of fratricide and usurpation as the real source of Hamlet's disturbance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He presents hope and relief about finding the 'source' of Hamlet's distemper, but deep down he likely fears the true source is his own crime

beat_states.desire_state: To speak the truth she already intuits—that Hamlet's suffering stems from his father's death and her rapid remarriage
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect her son and maintain her bond with him, while preserving her own position and marriage
beat_states.obstacle: Claudius and Polonius are constructing alternative narratives that let everyone off the hook, and she cannot fully confront her own guilt without destabilizing her marriage

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is in a state of quiet, clear-eyed grief and guilt. She is more certain than Claudius about the cause, less defended, and emotionally exposed by naming her own culpability in the 'o'erhasty marriage.'

social_state.rationale: She speaks with the authority of a mother's knowledge, gently but firmly countering Claudius's hopeful framing. The warmth is muted by the weight of what she's saying.

known_facts: Hamlet has been deeply disturbed since his father's death
She married Claudius very quickly after Old Hamlet's death
Hamlet has expressed disgust at the marriage
Polonius claims to have an explanation
hidden_secrets: The depth of her own guilt about the speed of the remarriage
Her awareness that she may have betrayed her son's trust and love
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude has no false beliefs here—she reads the situation with painful accuracy. She does not know about the murder but correctly identifies the two most visible wounds: death and remarriage.

beat_states.defense_state: Partial confession as deflection—she names the hasty marriage as a cause, which acknowledges guilt without going further into darker territory
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She simultaneously wants to hear a comforting alternative explanation and cannot stop herself from voicing the painful truth she already knows
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Gertrude is performing modesty or self-deprecation strategically to seem like a caring mother, rather than genuinely believing her own guilt—but the directness and specificity of 'o'erhasty' argues against this.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Well, we shall sift him. / Welcome, my good friends. / Say, Voltemand, what from our brother Norway?
utterances.stage_direction: Enter Ambassadors Voltemand and Cornelius with Polonius.

utterances.speaker: VOLTEMAND
utterances.text: Most fair return of greetings and desires. / Upon our first, he sent out to suppress / His nephew’s levies, which to him appeared / To be a preparation ’gainst the Polack, / But, better looked into, he truly found / It was against your Highness. Whereat, grieved / That so his sickness, age, and impotence / Was falsely borne in hand, sends out arrests / On Fortinbras, which he, in brief, obeys, / Receives rebuke from Norway, and, in fine, / Makes vow before his uncle never more / To give th’ assay of arms against your Majesty. / Whereon old Norway, overcome with joy, / Gives him three-score thousand crowns in annual fee / And his commission to employ those soldiers, / So levied as before, against the Polack, / With an entreaty, herein further shown, / That it might please you to give quiet pass / Through your dominions for this enterprise, / On such regards of safety and allowance / As therein are set down.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: It likes us well, / And, at our more considered time, we’ll read, / Answer, and think upon this business. / Meantime, we thank you for your well-took labor. / Go to your rest. At night we’ll feast together. / Most welcome home!

beat_states.desire_state: To efficiently dispatch the Norway diplomatic matter so he can focus on the Hamlet problem
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain secure, unchallenged control of the throne he usurped
beat_states.obstacle: Multiple concerns competing for his attention — Fortinbras threat, Hamlet's madness, his own guilt

affect_state.rationale: Genuinely pleased that the Norway situation resolved favorably without military action, which validates his diplomatic approach. Low arousal because this is routine state business and his real anxiety lies elsewhere. High control — he is performing kingship effectively.

social_state.rationale: At peak sovereign authority receiving ambassadors and dispensing judgments. Warmth is formulaic and diplomatic — 'Most welcome home!' is gracious but performative rather than deeply felt.

known_facts: Norway has suppressed Fortinbras's direct threat against Denmark
Fortinbras has vowed not to attack Denmark
Fortinbras's forces are redirected against Poland
Norway requests safe passage through Danish territory
Polonius claims to have found the cause of Hamlet's madness
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet to seize the throne
His real anxiety is about Hamlet, not Fortinbras
false_beliefs: The Fortinbras matter is fully resolved and no longer a threat
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius receives the diplomatic report at face value. He does not perceive that granting Fortinbras passage with an army could be dangerous. His mind is already on 'sifting' Hamlet — the Norway business is a box to check.

beat_states.defense_state: Compartmentalization — he tidily shelves the Norway matter to maintain the appearance of a king fully in control of all affairs
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Projects confident mastery of statecraft while internally preoccupied with the far more dangerous domestic threat of Hamlet's behavior
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may be more strategically cautious than he appears — his deferral to 'more considered time' could indicate genuine wariness about the safe-passage request rather than mere distraction.

beat_states.desire_state: To deliver his diplomatic report successfully and earn the King's approval
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the crown faithfully and maintain his position as a trusted courtier
beat_states.obstacle: The complexity of the message — he must present a diplomatically delicate situation (Norway's partial compliance with a new request attached) clearly and favorably

affect_state.rationale: Confident in his report and relieved the mission succeeded. Moderate positive valence from completing his duty. Low arousal — this is professional delivery. Control is moderate because he's a messenger subject to the King's judgment.

social_state.rationale: Clearly subordinate, performing the role of a competent instrument of the crown. Warmth is minimal — this is diplomatic business, not personal connection.

known_facts: Old Norway initially believed Fortinbras was preparing against Poland
Norway discovered Fortinbras was actually targeting Denmark
Norway arrested Fortinbras's preparations and received his vow of obedience
Norway rewarded Fortinbras with money and redirected his commission against Poland
Norway requests safe passage through Denmark for Fortinbras's forces
epistemic_state.rationale: Voltemand is a faithful reporter of facts as presented to him by the Norwegian court. He has no hidden agenda and transmits the information as received.

beat_states.defense_state: Formality — he shields himself behind precise diplomatic language and protocol

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: This business is well ended. / My liege, and madam, to expostulate / What majesty should be, what duty is, / Why day is day, night night, and time is time / Were nothing but to waste night, day, and time. / Therefore, since brevity is the soul of wit, / And tediousness the limbs and outward flourishes, / I will be brief. Your noble son is mad. / “ Mad” call I it, for, to define true madness, / What is ’t but to be nothing else but mad? / But let that go.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: More matter with less art.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Madam, I swear I use no art at all. / That he’s mad, ’tis true; ’tis true ’tis pity, / And pity ’tis ’tis true— a foolish figure, / But farewell it, for I will use no art. / Mad let us grant him then, and now remains / That we find out the cause of this effect, / Or, rather say, the cause of this defect, / For this effect defective comes by cause. / Thus it remains, and the remainder thus. / Perpend. / I have a daughter ( have while she is mine) / Who, in her duty and obedience, mark, / Hath given me this. Now gather and surmise. / To the celestial, and my soul’s idol, the most beautified Ophelia— / That’s an ill phrase, a vile phrase; “ beautified” is a vile phrase. But you shall hear. Thus:

beat_states.desire_state: To impress the King and Queen with his discovery of Hamlet's madness and its cause, establishing himself as indispensable
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure and elevate his position as the most trusted advisor to the crown
beat_states.obstacle: His own compulsive verbosity and self-consciousness about rhetoric undermine his attempt to appear decisive and brief; Gertrude's impatience

affect_state.rationale: He is excited and self-satisfied about his discovery, highly aroused by the opportunity to perform before royalty, fairly certain of his conclusion about Hamlet, but losing control of his own speech — his eagerness and nervous energy keep overriding his stated intention to be brief. Vulnerability is moderate: he is staking his reputation on this reading.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as a man of wisdom lecturing the monarchs, reaching above his station while technically deferring to them ('My liege, and madam'). Warmth is limited — this is a performance, not genuine connection, though he shows some paternal possessiveness about Ophelia.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving erratically/madly
Ophelia received a love letter from Hamlet
He instructed Ophelia to reject Hamlet's advances
He possesses Hamlet's letter as physical evidence
hidden_secrets: He ordered Ophelia to cut off contact with Hamlet, potentially contributing to Hamlet's distress
His motivation is partly self-serving career advancement, not purely concern for the state
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is straightforwardly caused by lovesickness for Ophelia
His rhetorical display is impressive and effective
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius has assembled a plausible but reductive causal narrative. He genuinely believes he has solved the mystery. He is unaware that Hamlet's disturbance has far deeper causes (the Ghost, Claudius's guilt). His self-awareness about his own verbosity is superficial — he recognizes it intellectually but cannot control it.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — wrapping a simple revelation in elaborate rhetorical display to mask anxiety about whether his intelligence will be valued
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He declares 'brevity is the soul of wit' while being extraordinarily long-winded; he claims to use 'no art at all' while deploying obvious rhetorical figures — he cannot stop performing even when he knows it's counterproductive
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius could be read as deliberately stalling and building suspense to maximize the dramatic impact of his revelation, rather than being genuinely unable to be brief — a calculated theatrical strategy rather than a character flaw.

beat_states.desire_state: To hear the actual substance of what Polonius knows about her son's condition — immediately and without ornamentation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect Hamlet and understand what is wrong with him, while maintaining the stability of her new marriage and household
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius's infuriating inability to get to the point; her own anxiety about Hamlet's state making her impatient

affect_state.rationale: Irritated and anxious — the negative valence comes from frustration and maternal worry. Arousal is elevated from impatience and concern. She is uncertain about what she's about to hear. She exerts control through her terse interjection but underneath is a mother frightened for her son.

social_state.rationale: She speaks from unquestioned royal authority — one line is sufficient to silence and redirect a senior courtier. Warmth is slightly negative: she is curt, dismissive, and in no mood for Polonius's self-indulgence.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving strangely/madly
Polonius claims to know the cause
She and Claudius previously speculated the cause may be the old King's death and their hasty marriage
hidden_secrets: She may privately suspect her remarriage has wounded Hamlet deeply
The depth of her guilt or denial about marrying Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude is in a state of not-knowing, waiting for Polonius's information. She has her own private theory about Hamlet's distress but has not articulated it fully. She has no knowledge of the Ghost or of Claudius's crime.

beat_states.defense_state: Directness as control — she wields royal authority and blunt speech to manage her anxiety about Hamlet
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She wants to know the truth about Hamlet's madness but may fear what that truth reveals about her own role in causing it (her hasty remarriage)
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's interruption could be read less as maternal anxiety and more as political impatience — a queen who simply cannot tolerate a courtier wasting the crown's time, with less emotional investment than the maternal reading suggests.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Came this from Hamlet to her?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Good madam, stay awhile. I will be faithful. / This, in obedience, hath my daughter shown me, / And more above, hath his solicitings, / As they fell out by time, by means, and place, / All given to mine ear.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: But how hath she received his love?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: What do you think of me?

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: As of a man faithful and honorable.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: I would fain prove so. But what might you think, / When I had seen this hot love on the wing / ( As I perceived it, I must tell you that, / Before my daughter told me), what might you, / Or my dear Majesty your queen here, think, / If I had played the desk or table-book / Or given my heart a winking, mute and dumb, / Or looked upon this love with idle sight? / What might you think? No, I went round to work, / And my young mistress thus I did bespeak: / “ Lord Hamlet is a prince, out of thy star. / This must not be.” And then I prescripts gave her, / That she should lock herself from his resort, / Admit no messengers, receive no tokens; / Which done, she took the fruits of my advice, / And he, repelled ( a short tale to make), / Fell into a sadness, then into a fast, / Thence to a watch, thence into a weakness, / Thence to a lightness, and, by this declension, / Into the madness wherein now he raves / And all we mourn for.

beat_states.desire_state: to determine the true cause of Hamlet's madness and whether it poses a threat to his throne
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his grip on power and neutralize any threat Hamlet represents
beat_states.obstacle: He depends on Polonius's testimony and cannot yet verify it independently; Polonius's verbosity delays his access to actionable intelligence

affect_state.rationale: Outwardly composed and kingly, but internally anxious — each piece of information is being filtered through his guilty conscience. The love-theory is appealing but he can't fully trust it yet

social_state.rationale: Maintains highest status as king; his warmth toward Polonius is instrumental — he validates Polonius to ensure continued loyalty and disclosure

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving erratically

utterances.text: It may be, very like.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS

utterances.text: Not that I know.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Take this from this, if this be otherwise. / If circumstances lead me, I will find / Where truth is hid, though it were hid, indeed, / Within the center.

utterances.speaker: KING

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: You know sometimes he walks four hours together / Here in the lobby.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: So he does indeed.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: At such a time I’ll loose my daughter to him. / To the King. Be you and I behind an arras then. / Mark the encounter. If he love her not, / And be not from his reason fall’n thereon, / Let me be no assistant for a state, / But keep a farm and carters.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN

social_state.rationale: As Queen she retains formal authority, but this line lowers her guard — 'poor wretch' is intimate, diminutive, maternal. She momentarily drops the political mask to show genuine warmth, though she remains in a public setting with Polonius and Claudius.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Away, I do beseech you both, away. / I’ll board him presently. O, give me leave. / How does my good Lord Hamlet?

utterances.text: Not I, my lord.

utterances.text: Honest, my lord?

utterances.text: That’s very true, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: For if the sun breed maggots in a dead dog, being a good kissing carrion— Have you a daughter?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: I have, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Let her not walk i’ th’ sun. Conception is a blessing, but, as your daughter may conceive, friend, look to ’t.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: How say you by that? Still harping on my daughter. Yet he knew me not at first; he said I was a fishmonger. He is far gone. And truly, in my youth, I suffered much extremity for love, very near this. I’ll speak to him again.— What do you read, my lord?

beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose corruption and root out the truth while protecting himself behind a mask of madness

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Words, words, words.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: What is the matter, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Between who?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: I mean the matter that you read, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Slanders, sir; for the satirical rogue says here that old men have gray beards, that their faces are wrinkled, their eyes purging thick amber and plum-tree gum, and that they have a plentiful lack of wit, together with most weak hams; all which, sir, though I most powerfully and potently believe, yet I hold it not honesty to have it thus set down; for yourself, sir, shall grow old as I am, if, like a crab, you could go backward.

beat_states.desire_state: To toy with Polonius, insult him to his face while maintaining the shield of madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose the corruption and hypocrisy of Elsinore while navigating his mission of revenge
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius keeps probing, trying to find method in the madness; Hamlet must sustain the antic disposition without revealing his true mind

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in command of this exchange. He takes sardonic pleasure in the wordplay ('Between who?'), the deliberate misunderstanding, and the escalating insult about old men's decrepitude aimed squarely at Polonius. He feels intellectually dominant and darkly amused.

social_state.rationale: As prince he holds inherent status, and his wit further elevates him over Polonius. But his warmth is actively hostile — the detailed catalogue of old-age infirmities is a sustained, barely-disguised attack on Polonius personally, ending with the crab/backward insult.

known_facts: Polonius is spying on him on behalf of Claudius
Polonius is Ophelia's father and has likely interfered in their relationship
His antic disposition is convincing Polonius he is mad
hidden_secrets: His madness is performed, not real
He knows or suspects Polonius's role in Ophelia's withdrawal
The Ghost's revelation about Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet reads Polonius accurately as a meddlesome tool of the court. He knows the old man is fishing for the cause of his 'madness' and deliberately feeds him absurdity laced with truths.

beat_states.defense_state: Feigned madness as a mask — deliberate wordplay and nonsense to deflect Polonius's interrogation while delivering pointed insults
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuinely enjoys the intellectual cruelty of humiliating Polonius, yet this sport is also a displacement of his rage at larger targets (Claudius, Gertrude, his own inaction)
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Hamlet's cruelty toward Polonius reflects genuine deteriorating mental stability rather than pure performance — that the antic disposition is bleeding into real bitterness and contempt.

beat_states.desire_state: To extract meaningful information from Hamlet's speech that confirms his theory of love-madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position as indispensable counselor to the King by proving his diagnosis of Hamlet's condition correct
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's answers are bewildering, slippery, and seem to oscillate between nonsense and pointed insult, making it impossible to pin down a coherent confession

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is off-balance. Each answer from Hamlet wrong-foots him (the 'Between who?' pun catches him flat). He senses the description of old men is pointed at him, creating discomfort, but he suppresses this awareness in favor of clinical observation. His control over the conversation is minimal.

social_state.rationale: Despite his court rank, Polonius is being reduced by Hamlet's wit. He maintains a surface of concerned, warm inquiry ('my lord') but is socially subordinated — he's the one being played with, not the one directing the exchange.

known_facts: Hamlet appears to be reading a book
Hamlet's responses contain apparent references to aging and decrepitude
The King and Queen have tasked him with discovering Hamlet's malady
hidden_secrets: He orchestrated Ophelia's rejection of Hamlet
He plans to stage an encounter between Ophelia and Hamlet for surveillance
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is caused by unrequited love for Ophelia
Hamlet's insults are products of derangement rather than deliberate mockery
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius filters all evidence through his pre-existing theory. The pointed description of foolish old men nearly breaks through his self-deception, but his confirmation bias and vanity keep his false belief intact.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalizing every insult as evidence of madness rather than recognizing deliberate mockery — 'though this be madness, yet there is method in't'
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Senses there is meaning and intelligence behind Hamlet's words but cannot reconcile this with his committed diagnosis of love-madness; his vanity prevents him from seeing he is the butt of a joke
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: It is possible Polonius is more aware of Hamlet's mockery than he lets on, and his persistence is partly political performance — he must return to Claudius with something, so he presses on despite recognizing he's being insulted.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Though this be madness, yet there is method in ’t.— Will you walk out of the air, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Into my grave?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Indeed, that’s out of the air. Aside. How pregnant sometimes his replies are! A happiness that often madness hits on, which reason and sanity could not so prosperously be delivered of. I will leave him and suddenly contrive the means of meeting between him and my daughter.— My lord, I will take my leave of you.

beat_states.desire_state: To repel Polonius and end the encounter while maintaining his antic disposition
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father by exposing the corruption around him while navigating his own existential crisis
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius keeps engaging him, probing and refusing to simply leave

affect_state.rationale: The grave reference carries real despair beneath its wit. He maintains control of the exchange but the suicidal undertone suggests genuine pain leaking through the performance.

social_state.rationale: As prince he holds inherent authority; his cold, cutting replies keep Polonius at a distance and assert dominance through contemptuous wordplay

known_facts: Polonius is spying on him for the King
Polonius is Ophelia's father and likely controlling her
His 'madness' is being scrutinized
hidden_secrets: His antic disposition is deliberately performed
He knows the ghost's accusation against Claudius
His grave comment may reflect genuine suicidal ideation
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet reads Polonius's agenda clearly and uses that knowledge to toy with him, knowing every word will be reported back

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through morbid wit — the 'antic disposition' mask that allows him to speak truth while appearing mad
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His quip about the grave reveals genuine death-wish ideation disguised as madman's humor — he cannot always distinguish performance from feeling
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The grave line could be purely performative rather than revealing genuine death-wish — a calculated shock tactic to accelerate Polonius's departure

beat_states.desire_state: To confirm his theory that Hamlet's madness stems from love for Ophelia and to arrange their meeting as proof
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his position at court by being the one who correctly diagnoses and resolves the Hamlet problem
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's responses are unsettlingly sharp and resist easy categorization as simple madness

affect_state.rationale: He is intrigued and somewhat impressed by Hamlet's 'pregnant replies,' slightly excited by what he sees as confirming evidence, but unsettled by the sharpness of the wit — his certainty wavers even as he narrates confidence in his aside

social_state.rationale: Despite being a senior counselor, he is consistently outmaneuvered in the exchange; his warmth is performative — solicitous concern masking surveillance

known_facts: Hamlet is behaving strangely
Hamlet's replies contain uncanny intelligence
He has the King and Queen's commission to investigate
hidden_secrets: He plans to use Ophelia as bait in a staged encounter
He is reporting everything to Claudius
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is caused by rejected love for Ophelia
He is cleverer than Hamlet and can manage the situation
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius acknowledges the method in Hamlet's madness but fundamentally misreads its source, locking himself into a theory that flatters his own importance

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalizing Hamlet's disturbing insights as lucky accidents of madness rather than confronting their meaning
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He recognizes the 'method in the madness' — the intelligence in Hamlet's replies — yet clings to his diagnosis of lovesickness because admitting otherwise would undermine his entire political strategy
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may have more genuine concern for Hamlet's wellbeing than typically played — his 'out of the air' suggestion could reflect real worry about the prince's health alongside political calculation

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: You cannot, sir, take from me anything that I will more willingly part withal— except my life, except my life, except my life.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Fare you well, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: These tedious old fools.

beat_states.desire_state: to dismiss Polonius while letting genuine despair leak through the mask of madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father and confront the corruption of the world, despite his own existential paralysis
beat_states.obstacle: his genuine death-wish and weariness keep breaking through his performed antic disposition

affect_state.rationale: The repetition of 'except my life' three times carries the weight of genuine suicidal despair — low energy but deep pain. The dismissive aside about 'tedious old fools' restores some contemptuous control, but the core affect is exhaustion and death-wish.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet maintains princely superiority, dismissing Polonius as a tedious old fool. Zero warmth — contempt for Polonius as a tool of the corrupt court.

known_facts: Polonius is spying on him for Claudius
Polonius is Ophelia's father who has restricted her access to Hamlet
He must maintain his antic disposition to avoid detection
hidden_secrets: His grief and suicidal ideation are real, not merely performed
He knows about the Ghost's revelation regarding Claudius
He suspects Polonius is an instrument of Claudius's surveillance
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet sees through Polonius entirely and uses the encounter to practice his madness, but the real feelings that surface are uncontrolled leakage rather than strategic performance.

beat_states.defense_state: antic disposition — using the guise of madness to express real suicidal ideation without being taken seriously
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs madness to control the situation, yet the thrice-repeated 'except my life' reveals an authentic, unperformed despair that exceeds the performance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The triple repetition could be entirely performed — a calculated escalation of the madness act to unsettle Polonius and make him report alarming behavior to Claudius, rather than genuine despair.

beat_states.desire_state: to extract himself from the conversation with useful intelligence to report to Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to secure his position at court by proving his theory that Hamlet's madness stems from love for Ophelia
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's responses are erratic and disturbing, making it impossible to confirm his theory cleanly

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is unsettled by Hamlet's repeated reference to death. He retreats politely, slightly shaken but still clinging to his interpretive framework. He has lost control of the exchange.

social_state.rationale: Polonius has been consistently outmaneuvered and mocked throughout the exchange. His farewell is courteous but reflects diminished status — he is the one withdrawing, not dismissing.

known_facts: Hamlet's behavior is erratic and potentially dangerous
There is 'method' in Hamlet's seeming madness
He has been tasked by Claudius to investigate Hamlet's condition
hidden_secrets: He engineered Ophelia's rejection of Hamlet
He intends to arrange an eavesdropping encounter between Hamlet and Ophelia
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is primarily caused by unrequited love for Ophelia
Hamlet's death-wish is merely a symptom of lovesickness
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius filters everything through his pet theory about Ophelia. He cannot perceive the genuine existential crisis or the political awareness behind Hamlet's 'madness.'

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — he frames Hamlet's disturbing death-wish as further evidence of lovesickness rather than confronting its genuine darkness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He recognizes 'method in the madness' yet cannot bring himself to acknowledge the real despair in Hamlet's words, because doing so would undermine his neat theory
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may be more disturbed by the death-wish than he shows, and his quick exit reflects genuine alarm rather than mere strategic withdrawal.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: You go to seek the Lord Hamlet. There he is.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: God save you, sir.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: My honored lord.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: My most dear lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To establish rapport with Hamlet through courtly deference and the appearance of genuine affection
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the King's commission and advance his own standing at court by unlocking Hamlet's secret distress
beat_states.obstacle: He doesn't yet know how Hamlet will respond or how to read his rumored madness

affect_state.rationale: Cautiously optimistic but uncertain; entering a charged situation with Hamlet's unpredictable behavior as an unknown variable

social_state.rationale: More formally deferential than Rosencrantz — 'my honored lord' signals respect for rank over intimacy, positioning himself lower in the hierarchy

known_facts: Claudius and Gertrude have sent for him to investigate Hamlet
Hamlet has undergone some alarming transformation
He and Rosencrantz were Hamlet's university companions
hidden_secrets: He has been commissioned to spy on Hamlet and report back to the King
false_beliefs: He may believe the mission is benign and in Hamlet's best interest
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern may partly rationalize the spying as helping a troubled friend, which eases his conscience but blinds him to the moral compromise

beat_states.defense_state: Concealing his true purpose behind formal courtesy and the conventions of friendship
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Addressing Hamlet as 'honored lord' — emphasizing hierarchical deference — while supposedly arriving as an equal friend
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern's more formal greeting may indicate he is slightly more self-conscious or morally uncomfortable about the deception than Rosencrantz

beat_states.desire_state: To hand off Hamlet to Rosencrantz and Guildenstern so they can extract information where he has failed
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position of power and influence by being the one who diagnoses and manages Hamlet's madness
beat_states.obstacle: He has been unable to get clear intelligence from Hamlet himself and needs others to succeed where he could not

affect_state.rationale: Relieved to exit the bewildering exchange with Hamlet, mildly flustered but performing authority as he introduces the newcomers to their task

social_state.rationale: Reasserts his role as court manager and information broker by directing Rosencrantz and Guildenstern toward Hamlet; functional warmth only

known_facts: Hamlet has been speaking in riddling, seemingly mad ways
The King and Queen have summoned Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to spy on Hamlet
His own direct interrogation of Hamlet yielded no clear results
hidden_secrets: He still believes Hamlet's madness stems from rejected love for Ophelia
He is aware he was outwitted and mocked by Hamlet in the preceding exchange
false_beliefs: Hamlet's antic behavior is primarily caused by love for Ophelia
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius clings to his love-madness theory despite mounting evidence of Hamlet's sharp wit; he redirects the investigation to save face

beat_states.defense_state: Reasserting competence by efficiently managing the handoff, positioning himself as the orchestrator
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He presents himself as in control of the situation while retreating from a conversation where Hamlet clearly dominated him

beat_states.desire_state: To greet Hamlet warmly and reestablish their old friendship as a basis for the mission
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To curry favor with the King and Queen by successfully discovering what ails Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Uncertainty about Hamlet's current mental state and how he will receive them

affect_state.rationale: Nervous energy as he approaches an unpredictable Hamlet; outwardly cheerful but inwardly anxious about whether his duplicity will be detected

social_state.rationale: Performing high warmth through 'my most dear lord' — deferential to Hamlet's princely rank while signaling intimate friendship

known_facts: The King and Queen have summoned him to investigate Hamlet's transformation
Hamlet was once a close friend from university
Hamlet has been behaving strangely
hidden_secrets: He is here on a spying mission for Claudius and Gertrude
false_beliefs: He may believe he can successfully conceal his true purpose from Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz knows his mission but underestimates Hamlet's penetrating intelligence

beat_states.defense_state: Hiding behind effusive warmth and the mask of old friendship to conceal the espionage mission
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Performing genuine affection while secretly serving as a spy for the King
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may have some genuine gladness to see Hamlet, making the warmth partially authentic rather than purely tactical

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: My excellent good friends! How dost thou, Guildenstern? Ah, Rosencrantz! Good lads, how do you both?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: As the indifferent children of the earth.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Happy in that we are not overhappy. / On Fortune’s cap, we are not the very button.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Nor the soles of her shoe?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Neither, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Then you live about her waist, or in the middle of her favors?

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Faith, her privates we.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: In the secret parts of Fortune? O, most true! She is a strumpet. What news?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: None, my lord, but that the world’s grown honest.

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain the pretense of a casual friendly visit while gauging Hamlet's mental state
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the King's commission and secure royal favor by helping diagnose Hamlet's affliction
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's rapid bawdy wit pulls him into increasingly compromising wordplay that makes him a follower rather than a leader

affect_state.rationale: Guildenstern is more verbally engaged than Rosencrantz, willing to play along with the bawdy Fortune conceit ('her privates we'). This suggests slightly more comfort but also less control—he's being drawn into Hamlet's game.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to the prince but performing the role of witty equal. His pun on 'privates' (both private citizens and genitals) shows him trying to match Hamlet's register and maintain the appearance of easy friendship.

known_facts: He was sent by Claudius and Gertrude
Hamlet has undergone some transformation
He and Rosencrantz are supposed to draw Hamlet out
hidden_secrets: His true purpose at Elsinore is surveillance
false_beliefs: He likely believes Hamlet is genuinely glad to see them without suspicion
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern shares the same mission knowledge as Rosencrantz. His willingness to engage in bawdy play suggests he believes the friendly facade is working.

beat_states.defense_state: Matching Hamlet's playful register to seem like an engaged friend rather than a spy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Enjoys the banter and the old friendship dynamic while knowing he's betraying that friendship
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern may be more genuinely enjoying the reunion than performing, making him the more conflicted of the pair

beat_states.desire_state: To assess whether Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are genuine friends or sent by the King, while maintaining his antic disposition
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To uncover the truth about his father's murder and the corruption surrounding him
beat_states.obstacle: He cannot yet determine their true purpose and must perform warmth while remaining vigilant

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized by the encounter and performs high spirits, but underneath he is uncertain of their motives. The bawdy wordplay about Fortune as a strumpet reveals his cynical worldview while also serving as a test of how they respond.

social_state.rationale: As prince, he holds inherent status. He performs warmth and camaraderie with effusive greetings, but the warmth is strategic—he's steering the conversation through escalating sexual innuendo to see how far they'll follow his lead.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are his old university friends
His uncle has recently become king
People at court are watching him closely
He has just been performing madness for Polonius
hidden_secrets: He suspects they were sent for by Claudius
His antic disposition is deliberate
He knows about his father's murder from the Ghost
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet already suspects their arrival is orchestrated but hasn't confirmed it yet. His line 'What news?' is a probe. Rosencrantz's answer that 'the world's grown honest' will deepen his suspicion.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing exaggerated friendliness and bawdy wit as a mask to probe their intentions
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuine pleasure at seeing old friends wars with deep suspicion about why they've appeared now
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may genuinely be happy to see them and the testing is secondary to real affection in this early moment

beat_states.desire_state: To appear as a casual, friendly visitor while concealing that he was summoned by the King and Queen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the King's wishes and advance his own standing at court by extracting information from Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's quick wit and unpredictability make it hard to control the conversation or steer it toward intelligence-gathering

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is somewhat anxious beneath his casual demeanor. His answers are deliberately unremarkable—'indifferent children of the earth,' 'the world's grown honest'—which suggests careful self-monitoring. The ironic claim about the world's honesty betrays nervousness.

social_state.rationale: As a courtier addressing the prince, he's in a subordinate position. He shows warmth but it's guarded and measured, playing the good-natured friend without revealing too much.

known_facts: He was summoned by Claudius and Gertrude to spy on Hamlet
Hamlet has been behaving strangely
He is an old school friend of Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He is here on the King's orders to discover the cause of Hamlet's transformation
false_beliefs: He may believe he can fool Hamlet about the reason for his visit
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz knows his mission but underestimates Hamlet's perceptiveness. His claim that 'the world's grown honest' is either deeply ironic or nervously improvised.

beat_states.defense_state: Hiding behind bland, modest answers to avoid revealing his true purpose
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Feels genuine affection as an old friend while carrying out a covert mission of surveillance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may be genuinely uncomfortable with his mission and the 'world's grown honest' line is unconscious self-indictment

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Then is doomsday near. But your news is not true. Let me question more in particular. What have you, my good friends, deserved at the hands of Fortune that she sends you to prison hither?

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Prison, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Denmark’s a prison.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Then is the world one.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: A goodly one, in which there are many confines, wards, and dungeons, Denmark being one o’ th’ worst.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: We think not so, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Why, then, ’tis none to you, for there is nothing either good or bad but thinking makes it so. To me, it is a prison.

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect Hamlet's provocative characterization of Denmark as a prison and keep the conversation light
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the King's commission by drawing out Hamlet's inner state while maintaining the appearance of friendship
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's sharp intelligence and unexpected framing make it difficult to steer the conversation

affect_state.rationale: Guildenstern is caught off guard by Hamlet's sudden pivot to dark, loaded language. His brief interjection reveals discomfort and uncertainty — he doesn't know how to handle this and feels the ground shifting under him.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to the Prince, and further diminished by Hamlet's rhetorical control of the exchange. Attempting a veneer of friendly concern.

known_facts: He and Rosencrantz were summoned by Claudius and Gertrude
They are supposed to discover the cause of Hamlet's transformation
Hamlet appears to be in a strange and melancholy state
hidden_secrets: Their visit is commissioned by the King and Queen
false_beliefs: They may believe Hamlet does not suspect their true purpose
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern knows his mission but underestimates Hamlet's perceptiveness. The 'prison' comment may not yet register as a trap.

beat_states.defense_state: Feigned surprise and innocence — 'Prison, my lord?' buys time and avoids engagement
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Must appear as a genuine friend while serving as an informant for the King
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern's surprise may be genuine — he may not yet be in full spy mode and is simply confused by Hamlet's rhetoric.

beat_states.desire_state: To test whether Rosencrantz and Guildenstern have been sent to spy on him, and to signal that he knows something is wrong
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose the truth behind the corruption in Denmark and avenge his father
beat_states.obstacle: He cannot yet be certain of their true loyalties or mission

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is strategically probing but also genuinely expressing his felt entrapment in Denmark. The philosophical tone gives him control, but the content ('Denmark's a prison') reveals real anguish. He is moderately aroused — alert, watchful, calculating — while experiencing genuine dysphoria.

social_state.rationale: He maintains princely authority and intellectual dominance, steering the conversation where he wants it. Warmth has cooled from the initial greeting — he is now in investigative mode, creating a trap with his 'prison' metaphor to see how they respond.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern have arrived at Elsinore unexpectedly
Claudius and Gertrude have been monitoring him
His father was murdered by Claudius
Denmark under Claudius feels like a prison to him
hidden_secrets: He suspects they were sent for by the King and Queen
He knows about the Ghost's revelation
His 'antic disposition' is partly strategic
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with strong suspicion but not yet confirmation that his friends are agents. He uses the prison metaphor as a litmus test — their reaction will tell him much about their allegiance.

beat_states.defense_state: Philosophical abstraction as a screen — using the metaphor of prison to probe without directly accusing
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuinely glad to see old friends yet deeply suspicious they've been recruited against him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be less strategic here and more genuinely expressing existential despair, with the testing of his friends being secondary to his need to articulate his suffocating reality.

beat_states.desire_state: To normalize the conversation and counter Hamlet's dark framing of Denmark
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To carry out the royal commission while preserving the friendship with Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's philosophical agility keeps redirecting and deepening the metaphor, making it impossible to dismiss

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is mildly anxious, trying to keep things easy and agreeable. He's losing control of the exchange as Hamlet insists on his metaphor. His attempts at philosophical rebuttal ('Then is the world one') are outmatched.

social_state.rationale: Deferential ('my lord') and attempting friendliness, but clearly outranked both socially and intellectually in this exchange.

known_facts: He and Guildenstern were summoned to Elsinore by the King and Queen
They are to discover what ails Hamlet
Hamlet seems to be speaking in dark, philosophical terms
hidden_secrets: Their true purpose at Elsinore as commissioned observers
false_beliefs: That their purpose remains hidden from Hamlet
That gentle disagreement can redirect Hamlet's mood
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is operating with the same commission as Guildenstern but seems slightly more willing to engage Hamlet's ideas, albeit superficially. He does not grasp that Hamlet is testing them.

beat_states.defense_state: Polite contradiction — 'We think not so, my lord' and 'Then is the world one' attempt to neutralize without confronting
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Trying to simultaneously gather intelligence and appear natural, while Hamlet's intensity makes both impossible
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may genuinely believe Denmark is fine and be expressing honest disagreement rather than strategic deflection.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Why, then, your ambition makes it one. ’Tis too narrow for your mind.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O God, I could be bounded in a nutshell and count myself a king of infinite space, were it not that I have bad dreams.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Which dreams, indeed, are ambition, for the very substance of the ambitious is merely the shadow of a dream.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: A dream itself is but a shadow.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Truly, and I hold ambition of so airy and light a quality that it is but a shadow’s shadow.

beat_states.desire_state: To reinforce the ambition diagnosis by reframing Hamlet's 'bad dreams' as evidence of frustrated political desire
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve Claudius's investigation and maintain his position at court by extracting useful intelligence from Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet reduces every assertion to nihilistic abstraction, dissolving the category of ambition before it can be pinned to him

affect_state.rationale: Guildenstern is moderately engaged, feeling somewhat clever for connecting dreams to ambition, but there's an undercurrent of unease as Hamlet's responses keep dissolving solid ground.

social_state.rationale: Like Rosencrantz, subordinate to Hamlet but performing equality through philosophical discourse. Warmth is calculated — enough to sustain the conversation but serving an ulterior purpose.

known_facts: Claudius and Gertrude commissioned this investigation
Hamlet claims to have bad dreams
Hamlet said Denmark is a prison
hidden_secrets: He is an agent of Claudius in this conversation
false_beliefs: Hamlet's 'bad dreams' are symptoms of thwarted ambition
The philosophical frame is a productive way to draw Hamlet out
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern latches onto 'bad dreams' as diagnostic evidence, but misreads Hamlet's existential despair as political frustration. He doesn't grasp that Hamlet is aware of and evading their agenda.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual assertion — uses philosophical language to maintain the appearance of parity with Hamlet
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Poses as a disinterested philosopher while actively constructing a narrative that serves Claudius's interests
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern may be slightly sharper than Rosencrantz and partially sense that Hamlet is toying with them, contributing to his eagerness to redirect the conversation

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's probing about his ambition while revealing just enough to test their loyalties
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To uncover the truth about his father's death and the corruption around him while protecting his inner world from surveillance
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are steering the conversation toward ambition — precisely the political motive Claudius wants diagnosed in him

affect_state.rationale: The mention of 'bad dreams' is a genuine slip of anguish beneath the controlled philosophical sparring. He's melancholic but intellectually engaged, maintaining surface control while hinting at deep disturbance.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet maintains princely intellectual dominance in the exchange, but warmth has cooled as he senses these friends are instruments of others. He engages but holds them at philosophical arm's length.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent for by the King and Queen
They are probing whether his discontent stems from thwarted ambition
Denmark feels like a prison to him
hidden_secrets: The Ghost's revelation about Claudius's murder of his father
His antic disposition is a deliberate strategy
His 'bad dreams' are connected to the burden of revenge and existential horror
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is fully aware they are fishing for a political diagnosis. He knows more than he reveals, using the 'bad dreams' confession as both genuine expression and bait to see how they respond.

beat_states.defense_state: Philosophical abstraction — transforms a political accusation into metaphysical wordplay, making his suffering appear as mere intellectual musing
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims he could be content in a nutshell yet admits to 'bad dreams' — simultaneously asserting mastery over his mind and confessing torment
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may genuinely be losing the ability to distinguish between performed and real despair — the 'bad dreams' may be an involuntary confession rather than a calculated lure

beat_states.desire_state: To diagnose Hamlet's malady as political ambition and extract a confession that his discontent stems from not being king
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill Claudius's charge by discovering the cause of Hamlet's transformation, securing royal favor
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet keeps redirecting the conversation into abstract philosophy, making it impossible to pin down a concrete political grievance

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is energized by the intellectual exchange and believes he's making progress by keeping 'ambition' central to the conversation, but Hamlet's agility keeps slipping past him.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to Hamlet in rank and intellect, Rosencrantz maintains a surface friendliness while performing the role of concerned companion. His warmth is instrumental.

known_facts: Claudius and Gertrude sent them to discover what ails Hamlet
Hamlet has declared Denmark a prison
Hamlet mentioned 'bad dreams'
hidden_secrets: He is reporting to Claudius and Gertrude
false_beliefs: Hamlet's discontent is likely rooted in thwarted political ambition
The philosophical banter is genuine casual exchange rather than Hamlet's evasion
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz interprets Hamlet's prison metaphor through the lens Claudius provided — ambition — and misreads the philosophical play as confirmation rather than deflection.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual mirroring — follows Hamlet into philosophical abstraction to appear a peer rather than an interrogator
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presents himself as a casual philosophical interlocutor while actively steering toward a predetermined conclusion about ambition
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may be genuinely enjoying the wordplay and partially forgetting his mission in the pleasure of intellectual companionship

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Then are our beggars bodies, and our monarchs and outstretched heroes the beggars’ shadows. Shall we to th’ court? For, by my fay, I cannot reason.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ/ GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: We’ll wait upon you.

beat_states.desire_state: To shut down the philosophical probing before Rosencrantz and Guildenstern can extract more about his inner state
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To navigate the corrupt world around him while pursuing the truth about his father's murder
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are persistently steering conversation toward his ambition/mental state, likely on orders

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in controlled performance mode — slightly agitated that the conversation is probing too close to his actual feelings about ambition and Denmark, but successfully maintaining intellectual dominance and redirecting

social_state.rationale: Maintains princely authority by controlling the flow of conversation and deciding when it ends; warmth has cooled as suspicion of their mission solidifies

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent for by the King and Queen
They are probing him about ambition specifically
Denmark feels like a prison to him
hidden_secrets: His antic disposition is deliberate
He suspects his father was murdered by Claudius
He is testing whether R&G are spies
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is increasingly certain these friends are instruments of Claudius; his logical game about beggars and monarchs is a smokescreen to avoid revealing his true preoccupations

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual obfuscation — uses a logical paradox to render the conversation absurd and then abruptly changes subject
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Feigns inability to reason ('I cannot reason') while demonstrating razor-sharp reasoning in the very act of saying it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may genuinely be exhausted by the philosophical sparring and recognizes he's close to revealing too much — the 'I cannot reason' is partially sincere fatigue

beat_states.desire_state: To stay close to Hamlet and continue observing his behavior for the King
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the King's commission by discovering the cause of Hamlet's transformation
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet keeps outmaneuvering them intellectually and redirecting the conversation before they can learn anything useful

affect_state.rationale: They've lost the thread of the conversation entirely — Hamlet's paradox about beggars and shadows left them nowhere to go; they feel outmatched and uncertain, simply agreeing to follow

social_state.rationale: Subordinate position reinforced by offering to 'wait upon' Hamlet; maintaining surface friendliness but the power dynamic is entirely in Hamlet's hands

known_facts: They were sent by Claudius and Gertrude to spy on Hamlet
Hamlet seems fixated on Denmark as a prison
Hamlet deflected the ambition line of questioning
hidden_secrets: Their true mission from the King and Queen
false_beliefs: They may still believe Hamlet hasn't fully grasped that they were sent for
epistemic_state.rationale: They have gathered almost nothing useful — Hamlet's philosophical evasions have left them with impressions but no clear diagnosis of his condition

beat_states.defense_state: Deferential compliance — masking surveillance as friendly companionship
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presenting themselves as willing servants/friends while actually functioning as spies; the phrase 'wait upon you' carries both courtly deference and the reality of monitoring
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: They may genuinely be relieved the uncomfortable philosophical sparring is ending and are happy to simply accompany Hamlet, still hoping a more natural opportunity to observe will arise

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
