utterances.text: No such matter. I will not sort you with the rest of my servants, for, to speak to you like an honest man, I am most dreadfully attended. But, in the beaten way of friendship, what make you at Elsinore?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: To visit you, my lord, no other occasion.

beat_states.desire_state: To force Rosencrantz to confess the true reason for his visit — that he was sent for by Claudius and Gertrude
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To uncover the truth behind every deception in Elsinore and fulfill his duty to his father's ghost
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz deflects with a pat, unconvincing answer rather than admitting the truth

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is fairly certain they were sent for and is strategically in control of the conversation. He drops the philosophical wordplay and shifts to a direct, warmer register — 'in the beaten way of friendship' — which is both genuine feeling and calculated pressure. Moderate arousal as he moves in for the key question.

social_state.rationale: He reasserts princely status ('my servants') while simultaneously offering genuine warmth and honesty as bait. The move from abstract debate to personal directness signals he's taking charge of the interaction's terms.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were summoned by Claudius and Gertrude
They have been deflecting his questions with philosophical games
He is being surveilled by the court
hidden_secrets: He is testing whether they will be honest with him or remain agents of Claudius
His antic disposition is partly performed
He knows about the ghost's revelation regarding his father's murder
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet already suspects (and likely knows) the truth. His direct question is a loyalty test, not a genuine inquiry. He gives them an opening to be honest.

beat_states.defense_state: Self-deprecation ('most dreadfully attended') masks his strategic interrogation as casual friendship talk
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuinely nostalgic for the old friendship while simultaneously weaponizing that intimacy to extract a confession
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be genuinely hoping they'll prove loyal friends and is more emotionally invested in their answer than his controlled demeanor suggests

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain the cover story that this is a casual friendly visit and avoid revealing the king's commission
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the king and queen's directive while preserving his relationship with Hamlet and securing royal favor
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's direct, emotionally sincere question puts enormous pressure on the lie — the appeal to friendship makes deception feel personally treacherous

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is uncomfortable and on the back foot. Hamlet's shift to sincerity ('like an honest man,' 'beaten way of friendship') makes the lie harder to deliver. His quick, short answer betrays nervousness — it's too simple, too clean, and he likely senses Hamlet isn't buying it.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to Hamlet in rank and in conversational control. His warmth is performative — trying to seem like a casual friend — but the power asymmetry is palpable. Hamlet is leading; Rosencrantz is following.

known_facts: He was sent by Claudius and Gertrude to spy on Hamlet
Hamlet is behaving strangely
Hamlet has just pointedly asked the real reason for their visit
hidden_secrets: The king and queen's commission to observe and report on Hamlet's behavior
false_beliefs: He may believe his lie is convincing enough to satisfy Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz knows the truth and is actively concealing it. His false belief is that the simple answer will suffice, when in fact Hamlet's tone signals he already knows.

beat_states.defense_state: Simplistic denial — offering the most uncomplicated, innocent-sounding answer possible to shut down further inquiry
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Wants to be a genuine friend to Hamlet but is actively betraying that friendship by lying to his face in this exact moment
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may be genuinely conflicted and on the verge of confessing, with this pat answer being a last attempt to avoid the discomfort of betrayal before caving

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Beggar that I am, I am even poor in thanks; but I thank you, and sure, dear friends, my thanks are too dear a halfpenny. Were you not sent for? Is it your own inclining? Is it a free visitation? Come, come, deal justly with me. Come, come; nay, speak.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: What should we say, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Anything but to th’ purpose. You were sent for, and there is a kind of confession in your looks which your modesties have not craft enough to color. I know the good king and queen have sent for you.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: To what end, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: That you must teach me. But let me conjure you by the rights of our fellowship, by the consonancy of our youth, by the obligation of our ever-preserved love, and by what more dear a better proposer can charge you withal: be even and direct with me whether you were sent for or no.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: What say you?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Nay, then, I have an eye of you.— If you love me, hold not off.

beat_states.desire_state: To find a way to respond to Hamlet without incriminating himself or disobeying the King
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the royal commission while preserving his relationship with Hamlet for mutual benefit
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's direct confrontation leaves no room for evasion; he lacks the rhetorical skill to outmaneuver Hamlet

affect_state.rationale: Guildenstern is disoriented by Hamlet's sudden shift from philosophical banter to pointed interrogation. His single line reveals someone who has lost footing and is genuinely unsure how to proceed. He feels exposed.

social_state.rationale: His status has dropped sharply — he is being questioned like a suspect. His warmth is neutral; he cannot deploy friendship without it sounding like a further deception under these conditions.

known_facts: He was sent by the King and Queen
Hamlet is pressing them for a confession
Their composure has failed to hide their purpose
hidden_secrets: The King and Queen's specific charge to spy on Hamlet
false_beliefs: Playing innocent might still work
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern knows everything Rosencrantz knows but is slightly more passive in his evasion. His 'What should we say' is both a genuine expression of being trapped and a tactical attempt to make Hamlet articulate the answer himself.

beat_states.defense_state: Feigning helpless confusion ('What should we say, my lord?') to buy time and shift responsibility
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presenting himself as open and uncertain while actively concealing the truth of his mission
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern's question could be a sincere appeal — he may genuinely not know what Hamlet wants to hear, caught between honesty and obedience

beat_states.desire_state: To force Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to confess they were sent by the King and Queen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To uncover the truth behind the corruption surrounding him and determine who can be trusted
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's evasiveness and loyalty to the King and Queen

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is highly alert and strategically aggressive. He already knows the answer but the act of forcing the confession carries real emotional stakes — the potential loss of these friendships. His certainty is high (he reads their faces), and he maintains control through escalating rhetorical pressure, but underneath there is genuine pain at their betrayal.

social_state.rationale: He oscillates between princely authority ('I have an eye of you') and intimate appeal ('by the rights of our fellowship'). He deploys warmth tactically but his dominant register is that of an interrogator who holds moral high ground.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent for by the King and Queen
Their faces betray guilt and discomfort
They are attempting to conceal their mission
hidden_secrets: His antic disposition is a deliberate strategy
He suspects the King of murdering his father
He is cataloguing who around him is a spy
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet reads their body language with precision and already possesses the conclusion before asking. His epistemic advantage is complete; the interrogation is performative, designed to extract a verbal admission.

beat_states.defense_state: Preemptive self-deprecation ('beggar that I am') to disarm and create moral pressure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Invoking genuine love and fellowship to manipulate a confession, simultaneously testing whether authentic friendship still exists while assuming it doesn't
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may genuinely hope they'll choose friendship over duty, making this less a trap and more a desperate plea for one honest ally

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect Hamlet's direct questioning without either lying outright or betraying the King's instructions
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the King and Queen's commission while maintaining Hamlet's trust for personal advancement
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's relentless, escalating pressure and his clear perception of their deception

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is increasingly uncomfortable and losing ground. His attempts to redirect ('To what end, my lord?' and 'What say you?') reveal someone scrambling. He feels caught between two authorities and doesn't know how to navigate it.

social_state.rationale: His status is collapsing under Hamlet's interrogation. He is subordinate both as a courtier and as someone being morally outmaneuvered. His warmth is muted — he cannot be warm without risking exposure.

known_facts: He was sent by the King and Queen to observe Hamlet
Hamlet suspects they were sent for
Hamlet can read their discomfort
hidden_secrets: The specific nature of the King and Queen's instructions
false_beliefs: They might still be able to conceal their mission
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz knows the truth Hamlet is demanding but believes (decreasingly) that evasion is still possible. His 'What say you?' to Guildenstern is a last-ditch attempt to share the burden of disclosure.

beat_states.defense_state: Redirecting questions back to Hamlet or to Guildenstern to avoid being the one to speak
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Wanting to preserve a friendship he is actively betraying by serving as an instrument of surveillance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz's 'To what end, my lord?' could be a genuine question — he may not have been given specific instructions beyond 'cheer Hamlet up'

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: My lord, we were sent for.

beat_states.desire_state: to relieve himself of the burden of deception by confessing they were summoned by the King and Queen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty to the crown while preserving his friendship with Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: admitting the truth means betraying the King and Queen's trust and revealing his own complicity in spying on Hamlet

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet's persistent, penetrating questioning has worn down Guildenstern's defenses. The confession carries shame and relief simultaneously. He feels cornered and exposed — Hamlet has made it impossible to maintain the pretense, so there's a capitulation here that is both uncomfortable and freeing.

social_state.rationale: By confessing, Guildenstern submits to Hamlet's superior position in this exchange. The act of truth-telling carries a residual warmth — it is a gesture toward the old friendship, an acknowledgment that Hamlet deserved honesty — but it is dampened by the awareness that the confession itself reveals how little agency Guildenstern has.

known_facts: He and Rosencrantz were sent for by King Claudius and Queen Gertrude
Their mission is to observe and report on Hamlet's behavior
Hamlet has already detected their true purpose through his questioning
Continuing to deny would be futile and further damage the relationship
hidden_secrets: The specific instructions given by Claudius and Gertrude about what to discover
The degree to which he has already reported back or plans to report back
false_beliefs: That this minimal confession may satisfy Hamlet and restore some trust
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern recognizes the game is up — Hamlet's relentless probing in the prior beats has made the lie unsustainable. But he likely underestimates how deeply Hamlet will hold this betrayal against him going forward.

beat_states.defense_state: minimizing — reduces the confession to its barest form to avoid elaborating on the full scope of the betrayal
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: wants to be honest with Hamlet but knows this honesty simultaneously exposes his role as an instrument of surveillance against his friend
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern may be confessing strategically rather than out of genuine surrender — calculating that partial honesty now might paradoxically serve the King's mission better by keeping Hamlet talking and trusting them somewhat.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I will tell you why; so shall my anticipation prevent your discovery, and your secrecy to the King and Queen molt no feather. I have of late, but wherefore I know not, lost all my mirth, forgone all custom of exercises, and, indeed, it goes so heavily with my disposition that this goodly frame, the Earth, seems to me a sterile promontory; this most excellent canopy, the air, look you, this brave o’erhanging firmament, this majestical roof, fretted with golden fire— why, it appeareth nothing to me but a foul and pestilent congregation of vapors. What a piece of work is a man, how noble in reason, how infinite in faculties, in form and moving how express and admirable; in action how like an angel, in apprehension how like a god: the beauty of the world, the paragon of animals— and yet, to me, what is this quintessence of dust? Man delights not me, no, nor women neither, though by your smiling you seem to say so.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: My lord, there was no such stuff in my thoughts.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Why did you laugh, then, when I said “ man delights not me”?

beat_states.desire_state: To preempt Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's report by controlling the narrative of his melancholy, giving them a version of truth that reveals nothing actionable
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father while navigating a court of surveillance and betrayal without exposing his true intentions
beat_states.obstacle: He must share enough to seem candid without revealing his knowledge of the murder or his feigned madness; his genuine despair threatens to bleed through the performance

affect_state.rationale: The speech oscillates between genuine existential despair and rhetorical mastery. He is in pain but channeling it strategically. High arousal from the emotional and intellectual energy of the speech. Control is relatively high because he chose to speak — he is the one shaping the narrative. Yet vulnerability is high because real feeling leaks through the performance.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet dominates the exchange through sheer rhetorical brilliance and his princely authority. The warmth he showed earlier has cooled — he's now testing Rosencrantz with the sharp 'Why did you laugh, then?' catching him in a reaction and reasserting intellectual superiority.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent for by the King and Queen
They will report back whatever he says
His melancholy has a real cause (his father's murder, his mother's marriage) that he must conceal
Rosencrantz smirked at 'man delights not me'
hidden_secrets: The Ghost's revelation about Claudius's murder of King Hamlet
His 'antic disposition' is at least partly a deliberate strategy
He is already planning to test Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is hyper-observant — catching Rosencrantz's smile and immediately interrogating it. He knows exactly why he's depressed but presents it as mysterious even to himself ('but wherefore I know not'). This deliberate false claim is strategic disinformation.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — transforms genuine anguish into a dazzling philosophical performance, controlling what his spies can report back
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: The 'antic disposition' demands he perform madness, yet the despair he describes is deeply authentic — he is simultaneously confessing and concealing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: It's possible Hamlet is not strategically preempting but genuinely unburdening himself in a moment of authentic confession, with the philosophical grandeur being a symptom of real depression rather than a calculated performance

beat_states.desire_state: To recover from an involuntary tell and prevent Hamlet from reading his true thoughts
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the King and Queen's commission while maintaining Hamlet's trust — serving two masters
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's intelligence and perceptiveness make it nearly impossible to hide anything; his own involuntary smile has betrayed a thought

affect_state.rationale: Caught off-guard by Hamlet's observation of his smile. He's uncomfortable, uncertain how to navigate, and losing control of the interaction. The quick denial suggests anxiety about being read.

social_state.rationale: Thoroughly subordinate in this exchange — Hamlet has the rhetorical power, the social rank, and the psychological upper hand. Rosencrantz's warmth is minimal; he's in survival mode.

known_facts: He and Guildenstern were sent by Claudius and Gertrude to spy on Hamlet
Hamlet knows they were sent for
Hamlet has just described profound melancholy
Players are arriving (likely knows this from earlier)
hidden_secrets: The specific instructions given by Claudius about what to discover
false_beliefs: That his smile went unnoticed or was insignificant
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is outmatched epistemically. He thought he could passively observe but Hamlet reads him like a text. His denial ('no such stuff in my thoughts') is likely a lie — the smile almost certainly carried a sexual innuendo reading.

beat_states.defense_state: Flat denial — immediately disavows the implication Hamlet reads into his smile
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is supposed to be drawing Hamlet out, but instead finds himself constantly on the defensive, exposed by Hamlet's superior wit
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz's smile may have been genuinely innocent — nervous laughter at the beauty and strangeness of Hamlet's speech rather than a bawdy reading — making his denial sincere

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: To think, my lord, if you delight not in man, what Lenten entertainment the players shall receive from you. We coted them on the way, and hither are they coming to offer you service.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: He that plays the king shall be welcome— his Majesty shall have tribute on me. The adventurous knight shall use his foil and target, the lover shall not sigh gratis, the humorous man shall end his part in peace, the clown shall make those laugh whose lungs are tickle o’ th’ sear, and the lady shall say her mind freely, or the blank verse shall halt for ’t. What players are they?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Even those you were wont to take such delight in, the tragedians of the city.

beat_states.desire_state: To seize on the arrival of the players as a potential instrument and distraction, while maintaining his performative wit
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder and expose the truth of Claudius's guilt
beat_states.obstacle: His melancholy and paralysis of will; the surveillance of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern

affect_state.rationale: A notable spike in energy and positive affect at the mention of players. The catalogue of stock theatrical roles is delivered with relish and wit, suggesting genuine pleasure — yet the line 'He that plays the king shall be welcome' carries a subversive, loaded edge pointing toward Claudius. He is energized but still guarded.

social_state.rationale: Reasserts princely generosity and cultural authority by promising patronage to the players. Warmer now than in the preceding existential monologue, engaging Rosencrantz with something closer to camaraderie through shared knowledge of theater.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent for by the King and Queen
A troupe of players is approaching Elsinore
He knows the tragedians of the city from before
hidden_secrets: He is already beginning to sense the players could be instrumentally useful
The line about 'he that plays the king' is a covert reference to Claudius's illegitimate kingship
He knows the Ghost's accusation against Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's mind is already working strategically beneath the witty surface. His immediate, almost reflexive comment about the king-player receiving 'tribute' betrays the political-theatrical scheme germinating in his mind.

beat_states.defense_state: Manic wit and theatrical cataloguing as deflection from his earlier existential despair
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Has just confessed he finds no delight in man, yet immediately brightens with genuine enthusiasm at the prospect of players — art offers what life cannot
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The enthusiasm could be read as purely genuine nostalgia and escapism with no strategic dimension yet — the Mousetrap idea may not crystallize until later

beat_states.desire_state: To redirect the conversation away from Hamlet's despair and test his disposition through lighter subject matter
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the King and Queen's commission by observing and reporting on Hamlet's mental state
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's unpredictable shifts between melancholy and manic wit make him hard to read

affect_state.rationale: Relieved to have a safer conversational topic after the tense confession scene. Moderately engaged but still cautious, reading Hamlet's reactions carefully. The logical pivot from 'you delight not in man' to 'what will the players get from you' shows calculated diagnostic thinking.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate position maintained. The warmth is functional — he brings good news of entertainment as a courtier-friend, but it serves an intelligence-gathering purpose.

known_facts: He and Guildenstern encountered the players on the road
The players are the tragedians of the city that Hamlet formerly enjoyed
Hamlet has just expressed deep melancholy and disillusionment with humanity
hidden_secrets: He is still operating under Claudius and Gertrude's instructions to diagnose Hamlet
false_beliefs: He may believe the players' arrival is a fortunate coincidence useful for cheering Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz uses the players as a diagnostic probe — if Hamlet can't enjoy even theater, his condition is dire. He doesn't yet suspect Hamlet will weaponize the players.

beat_states.defense_state: Adopting the role of casual bearer of pleasant news to avoid confrontation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presenting the players' arrival as innocent entertainment while it actually serves his surveillance mission — testing whether Hamlet can still take pleasure in anything
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz could be genuinely trying to cheer a friend rather than strategically probing, with the surveillance motive secondary in this moment

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: How chances it they travel? Their residence, both in reputation and profit, was better both ways.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: I think their inhibition comes by the means of the late innovation.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Do they hold the same estimation they did when I was in the city? Are they so followed?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: No, indeed are they not.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: How comes it? Do they grow rusty?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Nay, their endeavor keeps in the wonted pace. But there is, sir, an aerie of children, little eyases, that cry out on the top of question and are most tyrannically clapped for ’t. These are now the fashion and so berattle the common stages ( so they call them) that many wearing rapiers are afraid of goose quills and dare scarce come thither.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What, are they children? Who maintains ’em? How are they escoted? Will they pursue the quality no longer than they can sing? Will they not say afterwards, if they should grow themselves to common players ( as it is most like, if their means are no better), their writers do them wrong to make them exclaim against their own succession?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Faith, there has been much to-do on both sides, and the nation holds it no sin to tar them to controversy. There was for a while no money bid for argument unless the poet and the player went to cuffs in the question.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Is ’t possible?

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: O, there has been much throwing about of brains.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Do the boys carry it away?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Ay, that they do, my lord— Hercules and his load too.

beat_states.desire_state: To contribute meaningfully to the conversation and maintain his presence alongside Rosencrantz
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the royal commission while preserving his standing with both Hamlet and the court
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz dominates this exchange, leaving Guildenstern marginal; he struggles to find his moment

affect_state.rationale: Guildenstern is mildly positive but passive. His single interjection ('much throwing about of brains') is clever but reveals his peripheral position. Low control — he's following the conversational lead of others.

social_state.rationale: Guildenstern occupies the lowest status position in this beat — barely present, contributing one line. His warmth is neutral, offering an amusing quip to stay connected.

known_facts: The theatrical controversy has been intellectually heated
He and Rosencrantz are on a royal mission to observe Hamlet
hidden_secrets: His primary loyalty is to Claudius, not to Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern shares the same basic knowledge as Rosencrantz but contributes less detail, suggesting either less knowledge or less confidence in this arena.

beat_states.defense_state: Minimal — he inserts a single witty confirmation to stay relevant without overexposing himself
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Wants to be seen as Hamlet's intellectual equal but is reduced to a single supporting line in this beat
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern's quietness might indicate he is more carefully observing Hamlet's reactions than Rosencrantz is — functioning as the more watchful spy of the pair

beat_states.desire_state: To engage in a safe, intellectually stimulating topic that lets him assess the state of the world outside Elsinore while avoiding emotional exposure
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To find truth and meaning in a world of deception and corruption
beat_states.obstacle: His melancholy and suspicion of everyone's motives, including Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is temporarily lighter and more engaged — theater is one of the few subjects that genuinely interests him. His rapid-fire questioning shows intellectual arousal. He feels more in control here than in the preceding personal confession.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet reasserts his natural social dominance through the role of keen questioner. He's warmer here than in recent beats — the theatrical topic creates camaraderie and shared intellectual ground.

known_facts: A troupe of players is approaching Elsinore
The adult players have been displaced by child actors
The theatrical world mirrors political instability
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent for by the King and Queen
hidden_secrets: He is already formulating how to use the players for his own purposes
He suspects the world's corruption extends from court to culture
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is gathering intelligence about the players with genuine curiosity but also potential strategic intent. His question about whether the boys will 'exclaim against their own succession' reveals his awareness of how people are manipulated into undermining their own futures — a theme deeply personal to him.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection into theatrical gossip — channeling his intensity into a culturally safe topic to avoid further personal revelation after his 'quintessence of dust' speech
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuinely curious and animated about theater while simultaneously using the conversation as a shield against deeper interrogation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's engagement with the theater topic may be entirely genuine enthusiasm rather than strategic deflection — he may simply love theater and momentarily forget his troubles

beat_states.desire_state: To keep Hamlet engaged in pleasant, neutral conversation and fulfill his assignment to observe Hamlet's state of mind
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the King and Queen's directive while maintaining his friendship with Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: He must appear natural and friendly while secretly reporting back; also limited by his own intellectual mediocrity in keeping up with Hamlet

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is more at ease discussing theater than probing Hamlet's melancholy. He's animated enough to give detailed accounts of the boy actors. He follows Hamlet's lead and has modest control over the conversation's direction.

social_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is in a subordinate role, answering Hamlet's questions like a courtier reporting news. His warmth is moderate — he seems genuinely engaged in the gossip.

known_facts: The adult players lost their audience to child actors
The 'War of the Theatres' has been a major cultural event
The players are traveling because they can't sustain themselves in the city
He was sent by the King and Queen to observe Hamlet
hidden_secrets: His primary loyalty is to Claudius, not to Hamlet
false_beliefs: That this theatrical conversation is innocuous and not being strategically processed by Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz knows the surface facts of London theatrical life well but doesn't perceive how Hamlet might be connecting cultural corruption to political corruption, or how the players might become tools in Hamlet's plans.

beat_states.defense_state: Hiding behind the role of informative companion — offering cultural gossip as a way to seem useful and non-threatening
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Relieved to be on safe conversational ground after the tense confession scene, yet still performing friendship while serving as a spy
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may be genuinely enjoying the conversation with no strategic awareness at all — simply a young man chatting about entertainment trends

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: It is not very strange; for my uncle is King of Denmark, and those that would make mouths at him while my father lived give twenty, forty, fifty, a hundred ducats apiece for his picture in little. ’Sblood, there is something in this more than natural, if philosophy could find it out.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: There are the players.

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect Hamlet away from his dangerous political commentary about Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill Claudius's commission by drawing out the cause of Hamlet's transformation while maintaining the appearance of friendship
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's analogy has veered into dangerously direct political territory that Guildenstern cannot safely engage with

affect_state.rationale: Guildenstern is anxious — Hamlet's bitter remark about Claudius puts him in a compromising position. He seizes on the players' arrival as an escape hatch, suggesting relief-seeking behavior under stress.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to Hamlet in rank and intellect, unable to match his rhetorical force. His terse announcement is socially neutral — neither warm nor cold, purely functional.

known_facts: Claudius sent him to spy on Hamlet
Hamlet seems bitter about the current state of Denmark
The players are arriving
Hamlet has drawn a pointed analogy between fashion in theater and political allegiance
hidden_secrets: He and Rosencrantz were sent by Claudius to discover the cause of Hamlet's behavior
false_beliefs: Hamlet's bitterness may stem from melancholy rather than specific knowledge of wrongdoing
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern likely registers the dangerous political content of Hamlet's speech but may not fully grasp that Hamlet is hinting at murder. He knows enough to want to change the subject immediately.

beat_states.defense_state: Avoidance — abruptly changing the subject to the arriving players to escape a politically dangerous conversation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is supposed to probe Hamlet's mind but must retreat when Hamlet's revelations become too politically charged to safely acknowledge
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern's line may be a genuine, innocent observation about the players arriving rather than a deliberate deflection — simply noting a fact at a convenient moment

beat_states.desire_state: To expose the absurdity and corruption of a world that elevates Claudius, using the players' situation as a bitter analogy
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To find and enact justice for his father's murder while making sense of a morally inverted world
beat_states.obstacle: His disgust and bewilderment at human fickleness threaten to overwhelm his capacity for strategic action

affect_state.rationale: The analogy between the child actors supplanting adult players and Claudius supplanting his father triggers genuine bitterness. The oath 'Sblood marks a rupture in composure — arousal spikes as he nearly breaks cover on his real grievance. He sees the pattern clearly but feels powerless against it.

social_state.rationale: He speaks from a position of intellectual authority, delivering bitter social commentary, but warmth toward Rosencrantz and Guildenstern has cooled as he uses them as an audience for his veiled indictment rather than treating them as genuine friends

known_facts: Claudius is now King of Denmark
Courtiers who once scorned Claudius now pay for his miniature portraits
Public favor is fickle and follows power
The players have been displaced by a fashionable novelty, paralleling his father's displacement
hidden_secrets: He is drawing a direct parallel between the players' displacement and his father's murder/usurpation
He suspects or knows Claudius murdered his father
His 'antic disposition' is partly a mask
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is acutely aware of the political analogy he is drawing but conceals it as mere philosophical musing. His observation about something 'more than natural' may also hint at the supernatural knowledge the Ghost gave him.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — channeling personal rage about Claudius's usurpation into a seemingly philosophical observation about human nature
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He presents himself as a detached philosophical observer while the 'Sblood oath reveals visceral personal fury about his uncle's illegitimate rise
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be deliberately testing whether Rosencrantz and Guildenstern will report his seditious-sounding comments about Claudius back to the King, using this as a probe of their loyalty

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Gentlemen, you are welcome to Elsinore. Your hands, come then. Th’ appurtenance of welcome is fashion and ceremony. Let me comply with you in this garb, lest my extent to the players, which, I tell you, must show fairly outwards, should more appear like entertainment than yours. You are welcome. But my uncle-father and aunt-mother are deceived.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: In what, my dear lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I am but mad north-north-west. When the wind is southerly, I know a hawk from a handsaw.

beat_states.desire_state: To understand whether Hamlet is truly mad or playing a game, and to extract actionable intelligence for Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the king's commission and maintain favor at court while preserving the friendship with Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's language is bewilderingly layered — his welcome seems genuine but his references to 'uncle-father' and strategic madness make it impossible to pin down his mental state

affect_state.rationale: Guildenstern is unsettled and losing his footing. Hamlet's rapid shifts between gracious hospitality and cryptic warnings leave him uncertain. He senses he may be outmatched intellectually and that Hamlet sees through their mission.

social_state.rationale: Guildenstern is subordinate both as a subject to a prince and as someone being managed by a sharper mind. His 'my dear lord' maintains affectionate deference but he is clearly the one reacting, not leading.

known_facts: Claudius and Gertrude sent them to observe Hamlet
Hamlet has acknowledged knowing they were sent for
Players are arriving
hidden_secrets: The full extent of his and Rosencrantz's commission from Claudius
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness may be genuine and unpredictable
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern is caught between Hamlet's confession that he is only selectively mad and the possibility that such a claim is itself a symptom of madness. He cannot resolve the epistemological puzzle Hamlet has set.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing innocent curiosity to mask his surveillance role
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants to be Hamlet's friend but is functionally his enemy's agent; his simple question 'In what, my dear lord?' is both genuine concern and espionage
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern's 'In what, my dear lord?' could be genuinely affectionate concern rather than intelligence-gathering — he may still be partly operating as a real friend trying to understand

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain control of the social situation while signaling to Rosencrantz and Guildenstern that his madness is strategic, not genuine
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder while navigating a court of surveillance and deception
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are spies for Claudius, yet Hamlet still feels some residual affection and needs to manage what they report back

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and in command here — performing hospitality, strategically dropping hints, enjoying the intellectual game. His control is high as he orchestrates what they see and hear, though there's underlying tension about his uncle-father's deception.

social_state.rationale: He assumes princely authority in welcoming them, but the warmth is measured and performative — he explicitly frames his welcome as 'fashion and ceremony,' distancing genuine feeling from ritual. The warmth drops further with the cryptic warning at the end.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent for by Claudius and Gertrude
Players are arriving at Elsinore
Claudius and Gertrude believe his madness is genuine
His own madness is a deliberate performance, at least in part
hidden_secrets: The Ghost's revelation about Claudius murdering King Hamlet
His plan to use the players to test Claudius's guilt
The full extent of his strategic sanity
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating from a position of superior knowledge — he knows why they are here, he knows his madness is performed, and he is now telegraphing this selectively. The 'north-north-west' line is a precise disclosure: he can distinguish friend from foe when conditions allow.

beat_states.defense_state: Controlled disclosure — revealing just enough about his 'madness' to confuse and destabilize their mission while maintaining plausible deniability
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He genuinely welcomes old friends while knowing they are instruments of his enemies; he warns them he is not truly mad while cloaking the warning in riddling language that could itself seem mad
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'mad north-north-west' line could indicate genuine moments of instability — that his feigned madness sometimes bleeds into real disorientation, and this is a more honest confession than a strategic warning

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Well be with you, gentlemen.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Hark you, Guildenstern, and you too— at each ear a hearer! That great baby you see there is not yet out of his swaddling clouts.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Haply he is the second time come to them, for they say an old man is twice a child.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I will prophesy he comes to tell me of the players; mark it.— You say right, sir, a Monday morning, ’twas then indeed.

beat_states.desire_state: To bond with Rosencrantz and Guildenstern through shared mockery of Polonius while maintaining his antic disposition
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To navigate a corrupt court by controlling who knows what about his true thoughts and intentions
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius's arrival interrupts his private conversation; Rosencrantz and Guildenstern may still be spies

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in his element — quick-witted, commanding the social space. He accurately predicts Polonius's purpose, which gives him a sense of mastery. Genuine pleasure in ridicule, though underlying anxiety remains.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as the insider sharing a joke, pulling R&G into his orbit as audience for his contempt of Polonius. High status through intellectual superiority and princely entitlement.

known_facts: Polonius is a tedious old counselor who serves Claudius
The players are coming to Elsinore
Polonius is approaching to deliver news
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent for by Claudius
hidden_secrets: His antic disposition is a deliberate performance
He is already formulating plans around the players' arrival
He knows R&G were summoned to spy on him
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's 'prophecy' about Polonius demonstrates sharp situational awareness. He knows the court's mechanisms and uses that knowledge to perform omniscience.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through wit — uses humor about Polonius to consolidate alliance with R&G while concealing his real preoccupations
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He draws R&G close as confidants while suspecting they are Claudius's agents; he performs spontaneous wit while calculating every word
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's mockery of Polonius could be partly genuine affection turned bitter — Polonius is Ophelia's father, and Hamlet may be displacing anger about that relationship onto ridicule.

beat_states.desire_state: To deliver his news about the players' arrival and fulfill his role as useful intermediary to the prince
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position of influence at court by being indispensable to both the king and the prince
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet dismisses and ignores him, treating him as a figure of fun rather than a dignified counselor

affect_state.rationale: Polonius enters with courtly pleasantness but is immediately sidelined. His brief greeting suggests he is unaware of the mockery about to follow, maintaining his usual self-satisfied composure.

social_state.rationale: Despite his official high rank, Hamlet's treatment reduces his effective status. His greeting is polite but perfunctory — warmth is professional, not genuine.

known_facts: The players have arrived at Elsinore
He has been tasked by Claudius to observe Hamlet
He believes Hamlet is mad from love of Ophelia
hidden_secrets: He orchestrated the severing of Ophelia's contact with Hamlet
He is reporting Hamlet's behavior to Claudius
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness stems from unrequited love for Ophelia
He is successfully managing Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius remains locked in his false diagnosis of Hamlet's condition and doesn't perceive that Hamlet sees through him entirely.

beat_states.defense_state: Oblivious self-importance — he doesn't register (or chooses not to register) that he is being mocked
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He believes he is shrewdly managing Hamlet while Hamlet openly ridicules him to his face
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may partly sense the hostility behind Hamlet's wit but chooses to absorb it as part of managing a 'mad' prince.

beat_states.desire_state: To stay in Hamlet's good graces by playing along with his humor
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the king's commission by staying close to Hamlet and extracting information about his state of mind
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's wit moves fast and demands quick social alignment; he must laugh at Polonius without revealing his own loyalties to Claudius

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is navigating a treacherous middle ground. His quip is clever enough but reactive — he follows Hamlet's lead rather than initiating. Moderate anxiety about maintaining the performance of friendship.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to Hamlet in every way — intellectually, socially, politically. His warmth is partly genuine (old school friend) and partly strategic (maintaining access).

known_facts: Claudius and Gertrude sent him to observe Hamlet
Hamlet has already confronted him about being sent for
Polonius is a senior court figure
hidden_secrets: He is still operating under Claudius's commission despite Hamlet's confrontation
false_beliefs: He may believe Hamlet has accepted his and Guildenstern's explanation and trusts them again
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz knows his mission is compromised but continues to perform friendship. He may underestimate how much Hamlet sees through the performance.

beat_states.defense_state: Mirroring — he matches Hamlet's tone with a witty rejoinder about old men being twice children to signal alliance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs the role of Hamlet's friend and confidant while actually serving as Claudius's informant
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz's quip about old men being twice children could be genuinely spontaneous humor rather than calculated appeasement — he may still feel some authentic camaraderie with Hamlet.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: My lord, I have news to tell you.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: My lord, I have news to tell you: when Roscius was an actor in Rome—

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: The actors are come hither, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Buzz, buzz.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Upon my honor—

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Then came each actor on his ass.

beat_states.desire_state: To humiliate and dismiss Polonius, refusing to let him control the conversation or deliver his news with any dignity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Maintaining his antic disposition while asserting control over a court he despises, keeping his enemies off-balance
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius persists in trying to deliver his message despite being mocked

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is enjoying himself here — this is one of his performative highs. He has complete verbal dominance over Polonius and relishes each interruption. The antic disposition is a source of pleasure and power in this moment.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet wields his princely status as a weapon, treating Polonius as a fool unworthy of completing a sentence. Each interruption is a status assertion disguised as madness. Zero warmth — active contempt.

known_facts: The actors are already coming — Rosencrantz and Guildenstern told him moments ago
Polonius is a tedious old man who serves Claudius
Polonius is likely spying on him or managing him on Claudius's behalf
hidden_secrets: He already knows about the actors and is feigning surprise/ignorance
He is already formulating a plan to use the players against Claudius
His madness is performed, not real
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's epistemic advantage is the engine of his mockery — he knows everything Polonius is about to say, so each interruption is a demonstration of how unnecessary and foolish Polonius is.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through wordplay and absurdist interruption — refusing to let Polonius complete a single thought without being undercut
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He already knows the actors are coming (Rosencrantz and Guildenstern just told him) but must perform ignorance while simultaneously signaling he already knows, creating a layered mockery
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's mocking could also be read as genuine irritation at being managed by Polonius, with the wordplay serving as a pressure valve for real frustration rather than pure sport

beat_states.desire_state: To deliver his news about the actors' arrival and be received as a useful, important messenger to the prince
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position as indispensable counselor by being the conduit of information and influence at court
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet refuses to let him speak, interrupting and mocking him at every turn, stripping him of conversational agency

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is flustered and losing control of the exchange. Each interruption lowers his certainty about how to proceed. He tries to assert himself ('Upon my honor') but is instantly deflated again. His professional dignity is under assault.

social_state.rationale: Despite being a senior counselor, Polonius is being treated like a bumbling messenger. He tries to maintain minimal warmth and deference to the prince but is being publicly diminished.

known_facts: A troupe of actors has arrived at Elsinore
Hamlet has been behaving strangely/madly
He has been tasked with observing and managing Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He plans to use Ophelia as bait to test Hamlet's madness
He has reported to the King and Queen about Hamlet's condition
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is caused by love for Ophelia
He is the first to bring Hamlet the news about the actors
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius doesn't realize Hamlet already knows about the actors, making his news-bearing function redundant and his pompous delivery even more absurd. His core false belief about the source of Hamlet's behavior persists.

beat_states.defense_state: Appeals to his own honor ('Upon my honor') to try to reclaim authority and credibility after being repeatedly undercut
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He believes he is managing a madman and should therefore be patient, yet his pride keeps being wounded by Hamlet's dismissals
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may be more deliberately strategic than he appears — using the actors' arrival as a pretext to observe Hamlet's state more closely, in which case his persistence is diagnostic rather than merely officious

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: The best actors in the world, either for tragedy, comedy, history, pastoral, pastoral-comical, historical-pastoral, tragical-historical, tragical-comical-historical-pastoral, scene individable, or poem unlimited. Seneca cannot be too heavy, nor Plautus too light. For the law of writ and the liberty, these are the only men.

beat_states.desire_state: To impress Hamlet with the quality and versatility of the arriving players, establishing his own cultural authority and sophistication
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position as indispensable advisor and gatekeeper at court by demonstrating his knowledge and usefulness
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has been mocking and deflecting him; Polonius must prove his relevance and expertise despite Hamlet's contempt

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is in his element — performing expertise with genuine enthusiasm and high self-assurance. He feels no doubt about his proclamation, energized by the opportunity to hold forth. Low vulnerability because he is completely unaware of how ridiculous he sounds.

social_state.rationale: Attempting to elevate his status through cultural authority and connoisseurship before a prince who has been treating him as a fool. Moderate warmth as he is trying to serve Hamlet's entertainment but primarily serving his own ego.

known_facts: A troupe of actors has arrived at Elsinore
The actors are reputed to be the best in their profession
He knows the classical references — Seneca for tragedy, Plautus for comedy
He has been tasked with announcing the players to Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He and the King have arranged to use Ophelia to spy on Hamlet
He has shared Hamlet's love letters with the King
false_beliefs: He believes his elaborate genre taxonomy demonstrates sophisticated taste rather than pedantic absurdity
He believes Hamlet's madness stems from lovesickness for Ophelia
He believes he is impressing Hamlet with this speech
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius possesses genuine surface knowledge of theatrical convention but lacks self-awareness about how his compulsive over-categorization undermines his credibility. His false beliefs center on his overestimation of his own impression on others.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual overcompensation — hiding his insecurity behind an exhaustive, pedantic catalogue of genres to project mastery
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Believes he is demonstrating refined theatrical knowledge but is actually revealing pompous absurdity through his increasingly ridiculous compound genre categories
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may be deliberately performing excess to entertain Hamlet and lift his spirits — a more generous reading where the pedantry is intentional showmanship rather than unconscious pomposity

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O Jephthah, judge of Israel, what a treasure hadst thou!

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: What a treasure had he, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: One fair daughter, and no more, / The which he lovèd passing well. / Why,

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Still on my daughter.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Am I not i’ th’ right, old Jephthah?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: If you call me “ Jephthah,” my lord: I have a daughter that I love passing well.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Nay, that follows not.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: What follows then, my lord?

beat_states.desire_state: To torment Polonius by implying he is sacrificing Ophelia, while maintaining the cover of madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose corruption and hypocrisy in the court while navigating toward justice for his father
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius refuses to fully understand the barb, treating Hamlet's words as mere madness rather than accusation

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is enjoying the intellectual cat-and-mouse game, feeling in control of the exchange. He takes sardonic pleasure in Polonius's inability to grasp the full sting of the Jephthah comparison — that Jephthah sacrificed his daughter.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet wields his princely status and superior wit to dominate the exchange. His warmth is deeply negative — he holds Polonius in contempt as a manipulative courtier who pimps his own daughter.

known_facts: Polonius has a daughter, Ophelia, whom Hamlet loved
Polonius has been using Ophelia to spy on Hamlet
The story of Jephthah involves a father who sacrifices his daughter to fulfill a vow
The players are arriving at court
hidden_secrets: He is deliberately comparing Polonius to Jephthah to accuse him of sacrificing Ophelia for political gain
His madness is performed, not real
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows precisely what the Jephthah allusion means and is testing whether Polonius will catch the implication that he is destroying his own daughter. Polonius catches only the surface — 'he loves his daughter' — not the sacrificial subtext.

beat_states.defense_state: Antic disposition — using biblical allusion and song fragments to disguise a pointed accusation as lunacy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuinely angry at Polonius for using Ophelia as a political tool, yet must channel that anger through the mask of playful madness rather than direct confrontation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may also be processing genuine grief over the loss of Ophelia's love, and the Jephthah reference contains a buried lament for what Polonius's interference has cost them both.

beat_states.desire_state: To decode Hamlet's madness and confirm his theory that love for Ophelia is the cause
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position as chief counselor by proving his diagnosis of Hamlet's madness to the King
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's speech is cryptic and allusive, making it hard to extract clear diagnostic evidence; Polonius can only partially follow

affect_state.rationale: Polonius feels a flicker of satisfaction at hearing Ophelia mentioned again (confirming his thesis) but is destabilized by not understanding the full meaning. He is being led rather than leading, which makes him anxious beneath his composure.

social_state.rationale: Despite being a senior counselor, Polonius is subordinate in this exchange — Hamlet controls the topic, the allusions, and the rhythm. Polonius attempts mild warmth and accommodation ('If you call me Jephthah, my lord') to keep the conversation flowing.

known_facts: Hamlet keeps referencing his daughter Ophelia
Hamlet is quoting from a ballad about Jephthah
Jephthah was a biblical judge of Israel
hidden_secrets: He has been orchestrating Ophelia's interactions with Hamlet to spy for the King
false_beliefs: Hamlet's fixation on daughters proves lovesickness for Ophelia is the root cause of his madness
The Jephthah comparison is merely about a father who loved his daughter, not about sacrifice
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius grasps only the flattering half of the Jephthah allusion — a father who loved his daughter well — completely missing that Jephthah destroyed his daughter through his own ambition. This confirms bias blinds his analysis.

beat_states.defense_state: Selective hearing — he filters Hamlet's words to confirm his pre-existing theory ('Still on my daughter'), ignoring the threatening implications of the Jephthah story
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Pleased that Hamlet's words seem to confirm his theory about Ophelia, yet uneasy that he cannot fully control or understand the conversation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may have a dim, suppressed awareness that Hamlet is needling him more pointedly than mere madness would explain, suggested by his aside 'Still on my daughter' which could carry a note of wariness.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: As by lot, God wot / It came to pass, as most like it was— / Why, / and then, you know, / the first row of the pious chanson will show you more, for look where my abridgment comes. / You are welcome, masters; welcome all.— I am glad to see thee well.— Welcome, good friends.— O my old friend! Why, thy face is valanced since I saw thee last. Com’st thou to beard me in Denmark?— What, my young lady and mistress! By ’r Lady, your Ladyship is nearer to heaven than when I saw you last, by the altitude of a chopine. Pray God your voice, like a piece of uncurrent gold, be not cracked within the ring. Masters, you are all welcome. We’ll e’en to ’t like French falconers, fly at anything we see. We’ll have a speech straight. Come, give us a taste of your quality. Come, a passionate speech.

utterances.speaker: FIRST PLAYER
utterances.text: What speech, my good lord?

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what is being asked of him and to serve his patron well
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his troupe's livelihood and reputation through excellent performance
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's rapid-fire energy and abrupt demand for a speech without specifying which one leaves the player needing clarification

affect_state.rationale: Pleased to be welcomed warmly by a prince but slightly uncertain — the demand is sudden and unspecified. As a traveling player dependent on patronage, there is inherent vulnerability in the encounter.

social_state.rationale: Low status as a common player before a prince, but the warmth of Hamlet's greeting elevates him slightly. He responds with professional courtesy and openness.

known_facts: Hamlet is a prince and former patron who knows their work
He has been asked for a passionate speech on the spot
The troupe has arrived at the Danish court
false_beliefs: This is likely a straightforward entertainment request from a prince
epistemic_state.rationale: The First Player has no knowledge of Hamlet's deeper schemes or the political tensions at court. He takes the request at face value.

beat_states.defense_state: Professional deference — responds with respectful brevity to navigate an unpredictable royal patron
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The First Player may sense something unusual in Hamlet's intensity and be slightly wary, reading the 'antic disposition' as genuine instability

beat_states.desire_state: To seize control of the moment by redirecting attention from Polonius to the arriving players, and to immediately commission a passionate performance
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose the truth of his father's murder and hold a mirror up to the corruption at Elsinore
beat_states.obstacle: He must maintain his antic disposition while genuinely connecting with the players he needs for his plan; the court is watching

affect_state.rationale: This is one of the most energized and genuinely positive moments for Hamlet — he is animated, witty, warm, and in command. The players represent both nostalgia and a tool for action. His arousal is high as he rapidly shifts between teasing old friends, noting physical changes, and demanding a performance.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet assumes the role of generous patron and host — high status but exercised with real affection. He greets each player individually, jokes about the boy actor's growth, and shows genuine familiarity. He is expansive and commanding yet approachable.

known_facts: These are the best actors available and he has seen them perform before
The boy actor has grown significantly since last he saw the troupe
He can use a passionate speech to test both their quality and potentially to catch the King's conscience
Polonius was about to announce the players — Hamlet cuts him off by greeting them directly
hidden_secrets: He is already formulating the plan to use the players to test Claudius's guilt
His warmth, while genuine, is also strategic — he needs the players' willing cooperation
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with clarity of purpose here. He knows these players, knows their capabilities, and is already thinking ahead to how he can deploy them. The ghost's accusation drives his need for a 'passionate speech.'

beat_states.defense_state: Manic sociability and wit serve as a screen for his calculating intent — the 'mad prince' persona allows him to dismiss Polonius mid-conversation and pivot seamlessly
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuine warmth and delight at seeing the players coexists with ruthless instrumentalization of them for his revenge plot
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The manic energy could be read as genuinely spontaneous rather than strategic — Hamlet may not yet have fully formed the Mousetrap plan and is simply delighted to see old friends, with the idea crystallizing only later in his 'O what a rogue and peasant slave' soliloquy

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: The rugged Pyrrhus, like th’ Hyrcanian beast— / The rugged Pyrrhus, he whose sable arms, / Black as his purpose, did the night resemble / When he lay couchèd in th’ ominous horse, / Hath now this dread and black complexion smeared / With heraldry more dismal. Head to foot, / Now is he total gules, horridly tricked / With blood of fathers, mothers, daughters, sons, / Baked and impasted with the parching streets, / That lend a tyrannous and a damnèd light / To their lord’s murder. Roasted in wrath and fire, / And thus o’ersizèd with coagulate gore, / With eyes like carbuncles, the hellish Pyrrhus / Old grandsire Priam seeks. / I heard thee speak me a speech once, but it was never acted, or, if it was, not above once; for the play, I remember, pleased not the million: ’twas caviary to the general. But it was ( as I received it, and others whose judgments in such matters cried in the top of mine) an excellent play, well digested in the scenes, set down with as much modesty as cunning. I remember one said there were no sallets in the lines to make the matter savory, nor no matter in the phrase that might indict the author of affection, but called it an honest method, as wholesome as sweet and, by very much, more handsome than fine. One speech in ’t I chiefly loved. ’Twas Aeneas’ tale to Dido, and thereabout of it especially when he speaks of Priam’s slaughter. If it live in your memory, begin at this line— let me see, let me see: / ’tis not so; it begins with Pyrrhus: / So, proceed you.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: ’Fore God, my lord, well spoken, with good accent and good discretion.

utterances.speaker: FIRST PLAYER
utterances.text: Anon he finds him / Striking too short at Greeks. His antique sword, / Rebellious to his arm, lies where it falls, / Repugnant to command. Unequal matched, / Pyrrhus at Priam drives, in rage strikes wide; / But with the whiff and wind of his fell sword / Th’ unnervèd father falls. Then senseless Ilium, / Seeming to feel this blow, with flaming top / Stoops to his base, and with a hideous crash / Takes prisoner Pyrrhus’ ear. For lo, his sword, / Which was declining on the milky head / Of reverend Priam, seemed i’ th’ air to stick. / So as a painted tyrant Pyrrhus stood / And, like a neutral to his will and matter, / Did nothing. / But as we often see against some storm / A silence in the heavens, the rack stand still, / The bold winds speechless, and the orb below / As hush as death, anon the dreadful thunder / Doth rend the region; so, after Pyrrhus’ pause, / Arousèd vengeance sets him new a-work, / And never did the Cyclops’ hammers fall / On Mars’s armor, forged for proof eterne, / With less remorse than Pyrrhus’ bleeding sword / Now falls on Priam. / Out, out, thou strumpet Fortune! All you gods / In general synod take away her power, / Break all the spokes and fellies from her wheel, / And bowl the round nave down the hill of heaven / As low as to the fiends!

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: This is too long.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: It shall to the barber’s with your beard.— Prithee say on. He’s for a jig or a tale of bawdry, or he sleeps. Say on; come to Hecuba.

beat_states.desire_state: To dismiss Polonius's interruption and maintain control of the performance, using it as another opportunity to humiliate him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose the corruption around him while maintaining his antic disposition as protective cover
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius's impatience threatens to derail the recitation Hamlet is using to set up his larger plan with the players

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is enjoying his superiority over Polonius, energized by the verbal sparring. He feels in command of the room and relishes the insult about the beard. Low vulnerability because Polonius poses no real threat.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet asserts princely dominance, publicly ridiculing Polonius as a philistine who only likes jigs and bawdry. The contempt is barely disguised as jest.

known_facts: Polonius is a fool easily manipulated
The Player can recite the Hecuba speech which is emotionally relevant to Hamlet's situation
Polonius is likely spying on him for Claudius
hidden_secrets: He is steering toward the Hecuba speech deliberately because a grieving queen mirrors his own mother's expected grief — or lack thereof
He is already forming the plan to use the players to test Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows exactly what he wants from this recitation and sees through Polonius entirely. His request to 'come to Hecuba' reveals strategic intentionality beneath the clowning.

beat_states.defense_state: Antic disposition — uses wit and insult to deflect any challenge to his authority in the moment
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He genuinely wants to hear the speech about Hecuba (which connects to his own grief and his plan) but must channel that urgency through the mask of madcap humor
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's impatience with the long buildup may also reflect genuine restlessness — he needs to hear about Hecuba's grief to fuel his own resolve, and Polonius's interruption actually aligns with his own desire to skip ahead.

beat_states.desire_state: To end or shorten the tedious recitation and reassert some control over the social situation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position as trusted counselor by managing the prince and reporting to the king
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet immediately turns the complaint into another insult, leaving Polonius publicly diminished

affect_state.rationale: Genuinely bored and slightly irritated, but lacking the authority to do anything about it. The insult about his beard and the accusation of preferring jigs and bawdry stings. He is passive, unable to counter Hamlet's wit.

social_state.rationale: His attempt to assert taste backfires into public humiliation. He is being tolerated rather than respected, spoken about in the third person while present ('He's for a jig').

known_facts: Hamlet is behaving erratically
He is supposed to be observing Hamlet for signs of lovesick madness
The speech has gone on at considerable length
hidden_secrets: He has arranged for Ophelia to be used as bait to test Hamlet
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness stems from unrequited love for Ophelia
His own critical interruption is a reasonable social act rather than a blunder
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius remains oblivious to Hamlet's strategic use of the players and continues to read everything through his lovesickness theory. He does not realize he is being systematically degraded.

beat_states.defense_state: Retreat into aesthetic judgment — frames his boredom as legitimate critical opinion rather than admitting powerlessness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants to be seen as a cultured man of taste but his impatience reveals he is exactly the philistine Hamlet accuses him of being
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may be deliberately trying to seem like a simple audience member to avoid appearing threatening, though this reading is less supported by the text.

utterances.speaker: FIRST PLAYER
utterances.text: But who, ah woe, had seen the moblèd queen—

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: “ The moblèd queen”?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: That’s good. “ Moblèd queen” is good.

utterances.speaker: FIRST PLAYER
utterances.text: Run barefoot up and down, threat’ning the flames / With bisson rheum, a clout upon that head / Where late the diadem stood, and for a robe, / About her lank and all o’erteemèd loins / A blanket, in the alarm of fear caught up— / Who this had seen, with tongue in venom steeped, / ’Gainst Fortune’s state would treason have pronounced. / But if the gods themselves did see her then / When she saw Pyrrhus make malicious sport / In mincing with his sword her husband’s limbs, / The instant burst of clamor that she made / ( Unless things mortal move them not at all) / Would have made milch the burning eyes of heaven / And passion in the gods.

beat_states.desire_state: To absorb the image of Hecuba's grief as a reproach to his own inaction — to feel the full weight of a wife mourning a murdered husband/king
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder, but first to understand why he cannot act — the speech about a grieving queen mirrors Gertrude's failure to grieve
beat_states.obstacle: His own emotional paralysis — an actor can weep for Hecuba while he, with real cause, does nothing

affect_state.rationale: The image of a queen mourning her slaughtered husband is a direct mirror of what Gertrude should have done. Hamlet's interjection about 'moblèd' is a thin intellectual shield over rising anguish. His vulnerability is very high — this speech is building the pressure that will erupt in his 'rogue and peasant slave' soliloquy.

social_state.rationale: He is the prince directing the performance and genuinely appreciating the Player's art. His warmth toward the Player is real — a fellow lover of language and truth.

known_facts: His father was murdered by Claudius (per the Ghost)
His mother married the murderer with indecent speed
This speech about Hecuba's grief is a direct parallel to Gertrude's situation
hidden_secrets: He is using this performance to probe his own emotional capacity for revenge
He is comparing Gertrude unfavorably to Hecuba
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet chose this speech deliberately. Every detail — the queen's grief, the husband's dismemberment, the cosmic outrage — maps onto his own situation. He knows exactly what he's doing, even as the emotion threatens to overwhelm the strategy.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — he pauses on the word 'moblèd' as if savoring rhetoric, deflecting from the emotional devastation the content is causing him
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is simultaneously drawn into the speech's grief (identifying with Hecuba/his father) and distancing himself through literary appreciation of a single word — intellectualizing to survive the emotion
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: His comment on 'moblèd' could be purely genuine literary delight rather than a defense mechanism, though the dramatic context strongly suggests both operate simultaneously

beat_states.desire_state: To demonstrate his taste and critical judgment as an audience member, maintaining his role as court intellectual
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position of influence and appear wise and relevant in all situations
beat_states.obstacle: He has no genuine emotional engagement with the content — he can only respond to surface aesthetics

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is entirely comfortable and untouched by the speech's emotional content. His mild pleasure is aesthetic and social — he's pleased to have a critical opinion. Zero vulnerability because the content about a murdered king and grieving queen does not penetrate his consciousness at all.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as a co-connoisseur alongside Hamlet, borrowing Hamlet's approval of the word to echo it. His warmth is performative social lubrication.

known_facts: Hamlet enjoys this type of speech
He considers himself a man of cultural sophistication
He once acted Julius Caesar in university
hidden_secrets: He is secretly monitoring Hamlet's behavior for signs of madness to report to the King
false_beliefs: He believes Hamlet's interest in this speech is mere theatrical enthusiasm rather than deeply personal
He believes his own critical judgment is genuinely valued
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius is oblivious to the psychological subtext of why Hamlet requested this particular speech. He sees surfaces only — a trait that will prove fatal.

beat_states.defense_state: superficiality — reducing profound grief to a matter of pleasant diction
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He just complained the speech was too long, now praises a detail — his critical opinions are performative and inconsistent, driven by social positioning rather than genuine response
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius might be genuinely moved by the word choice and expressing authentic aesthetic pleasure, though his track record of superficial response and his prior complaint about length undercuts this

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Look whe’er he has not turned his color and has tears in ’s eyes. Prithee, no more.

beat_states.desire_state: To stop the recitation, which he finds tedious and emotionally excessive
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain control over court proceedings and demonstrate his superior judgment and authority
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet requested the speech and seems invested in it; the Player is performing with genuine emotional power that commands attention

affect_state.rationale: Mildly irritated and uncomfortable with the emotional display. His certainty is high — he knows he wants this stopped. But his control is limited because Hamlet is his social superior and the one who requested the performance. The tears unsettle him slightly, though he'd never admit it.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as the arbiter of taste and propriety, commenting on the Player's emotion as if from above, but his repeated requests to stop reveal he's not truly in command. Minimal warmth — his concern for the Player's tears is performative observation, not empathy.

known_facts: The Player is visibly weeping and has changed color
The speech concerns Hecuba's grief over Priam's murder
Hamlet specifically requested this speech
This recitation has gone on longer than Polonius finds acceptable
hidden_secrets: He is spying on Hamlet for Claudius and assessing his mental state
false_beliefs: He believes Hamlet's interest in theater is mere entertainment or madness, not recognizing its strategic psychological function
He believes Hamlet's lovesickness for Ophelia explains his behavior
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius observes surfaces accurately — he sees the tears, the changed color — but consistently misreads the deeper significance of events around him. He has no idea that Hamlet is testing the emotional power of performance as a tool to catch the King's conscience.
