beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — converting the Player's genuine emotional display into an observable curiosity, distancing himself from its power by commenting on it rather than feeling it
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He draws attention to the Player's tears as remarkable while simultaneously trying to shut down the very performance that produced them — he wants to seem perceptive while actually being impatient and uncomfortable
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may be genuinely disturbed by the emotional content — the theme of a king's murder and a queen's grief might touch something closer to the current political reality at Elsinore than he consciously recognizes, and his desire to stop the speech could be an unconscious avoidance of uncomfortable parallels.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: ’Tis well. I’ll have thee speak out the rest of this soon.— Good my lord, will you see the players well bestowed? Do you hear, let them be well used, for they are the abstract and brief chronicles of the time. After your death you were better have a bad epitaph than their ill report while you live.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: My lord, I will use them according to their desert.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: God’s bodykins, man, much better! Use every man after his desert and who shall ’scape whipping? Use them after your own honor and dignity. The less they deserve, the more merit is in your bounty. Take them in.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Come, sirs.

beat_states.desire_state: To ensure the players are well treated and housed so he can use them for his plan, while asserting moral authority over Polonius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose the truth of his father's murder and hold the guilty accountable
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius's shallow, self-important nature and his tendency to treat people according to rank and utility rather than generosity

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and purposeful after the Player's speech. He channels his emotion into decisive action — arranging for the players. His moral rebuke of Polonius gives him a sense of control and intellectual satisfaction, though beneath this lies the weight of what he's planning.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet commands from princely authority, issuing directives to Polonius. His warmth toward Polonius is minimal — the moral correction ('God's bodykins, man, much better!') is sharp and condescending, treating Polonius as morally inferior. His warmth is reserved implicitly for the players themselves.

known_facts: The players can perform a speech that mirrors his father's murder
Polonius will obey his instructions regarding the players
Players are powerful cultural instruments ('abstract and brief chronicles of the time')
He needs the players for his emerging plan to test Claudius
hidden_secrets: He is already formulating the plan to use the players to catch Claudius's conscience
The Player's speech about Priam and Hecuba has deeply moved and shamed him
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is strategically maneuvering — his instructions to Polonius serve both a genuine ethical principle and the practical need to keep the players close and well-disposed for his trap.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual superiority — channeling his inner turmoil into moral lecturing to maintain control of the situation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is performing calm, princely authority while internally roiling with the emotion stirred by the Player's speech and his forming plan against Claudius
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's moral lecturing could be read as genuine philosophical conviction about human dignity rather than primarily strategic positioning — the ethics of generosity as a deeply held belief independent of his plot.

beat_states.desire_state: To fulfill Hamlet's request efficiently and maintain his role as dutiful courtier, while preserving his own sense of propriety
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position of influence at court by managing and surveilling Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's moral rebuke challenges his transactional worldview and puts him on the back foot socially

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is mildly chastened and uncomfortable. His initial response ('according to their desert') was his natural instinct, and Hamlet's sharp correction leaves him with diminished authority. He retreats quickly into action ('Come, sirs') to end the uncomfortable exchange.

social_state.rationale: Polonius is subordinated here — lectured by the prince on moral philosophy. His status drops as Hamlet publicly corrects him. His warmth is neutral-to-cool; he's performing duty rather than genuine hospitality toward the players.

known_facts: Hamlet wants the players well housed and treated
Hamlet is emotionally engaged with the players and their art
He is still observing Hamlet for signs of lovesick madness
hidden_secrets: He is spying on Hamlet for the King
He has arranged with Claudius to use Ophelia to test Hamlet
false_beliefs: Hamlet's erratic behavior stems from love for Ophelia
Hamlet's interest in the players is merely entertainment or distraction
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius completely misses the strategic significance of Hamlet's engagement with the players. He sees an eccentric prince, not a man laying a trap.

beat_states.defense_state: Retreating into bureaucratic obedience — reduces the interaction to logistics to escape Hamlet's moral challenge
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He instinctively believes people should be treated 'according to their desert' (a meritocratic/hierarchical view) but cannot argue this with a prince who outranks him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius's 'according to their desert' could be a deliberate test of Hamlet's values rather than a sincere belief, though this seems unlikely given his character.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Follow him, friends. We’ll hear a play tomorrow. / Dost thou hear me, old friend? Can you play “ The Murder of Gonzago”?
utterances.stage_direction: As Polonius and Players exit, Hamlet speaks to the First Player.

utterances.speaker: FIRST PLAYER
utterances.text: Ay, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: We’ll ha ’t tomorrow night. You could, for a need, study a speech of some dozen or sixteen lines, which I would set down and insert in ’t, could you not?

utterances.speaker: FIRST PLAYER
utterances.text: Ay, my lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To comply with the prince's requests and secure the company's engagement at court
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain the troupe's livelihood and professional standing through noble patronage
beat_states.obstacle: The request to learn inserted lines on short notice is demanding, but the obstacle is minor — pleasing the prince is both duty and opportunity

affect_state.rationale: Mildly positive — he has a paying engagement at court. Low control because he is entirely at the prince's direction. Moderate uncertainty about what Hamlet is really after with these specific requests. Some vulnerability as a traveling player dependent on noble favor.

social_state.rationale: Clearly subordinate — answering with brief 'Ay, my lord' responses that acknowledge the status gap. Warmth is mild; he is responsive to Hamlet's friendly tone but maintains professional distance.

known_facts: The prince wants 'The Murder of Gonzago' performed tomorrow night
Hamlet wants to insert a dozen or sixteen lines of his own composition
The company has been given lodging and good treatment by Polonius
false_beliefs: This is likely just a prince's whimsical theatrical preference
epistemic_state.rationale: The First Player has no reason to suspect that this performance is a political trap. He takes the request at face value as a noble patron's artistic preference.

beat_states.defense_state: Professional deference — limiting responses to brief affirmations to avoid overstepping
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Possible curiosity about why Hamlet is so specific about this particular play and the inserted lines, but professional discretion keeps him from asking
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The First Player may sense something unusual in Hamlet's intensity and specificity but suppresses curiosity out of professional discretion

beat_states.desire_state: To secure the First Player's commitment to perform 'The Murder of Gonzago' with Hamlet's inserted lines that will trap Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To ascertain Claudius's guilt and fulfill the Ghost's command for revenge
beat_states.obstacle: He must appear casual and offhand about a request that is in fact the linchpin of his entire plan to expose Claudius

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet has seized on a concrete plan of action after the emotional turbulence of watching the Player's speech. He feels a surge of purpose and agency — control is high because he is orchestrating events. Arousal is elevated from the excitement of the forming plan. Certainty is moderate — he believes this stratagem can work but hasn't proven it yet.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet exercises princely authority — commanding a performance, dictating the play, inserting his own lines — but does so with affable warmth ('old friend,' 'dost thou hear me'), using familiar address to secure loyalty and discretion.

known_facts: The players can perform 'The Murder of Gonzago'
The play's plot mirrors the Ghost's account of King Hamlet's murder
He can insert lines into the performance to sharpen the parallel to Claudius's crime
Claudius will be in the audience
hidden_secrets: He intends to use the play as a mousetrap to test Claudius's guilt
The Ghost has told him Claudius murdered his father
His 'antic disposition' is a deliberate strategy
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with maximum strategic awareness. He knows exactly why he wants this play performed and what the inserted speech must accomplish, but he reveals none of this to the First Player.

beat_states.defense_state: Masking the urgency and strategic importance of the request behind a tone of friendly, casual patronage
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Burning with desperate purpose internally while projecting nonchalant, almost throwaway requests to a player

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Very well. Follow that lord— and look you mock him not. First Player exits. My good friends, I’ll leave you till night. You are welcome to Elsinore.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Good my lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To dismiss Rosencrantz and Guildenstern so he can be alone to think and plan
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: He needs solitude to process the Player's emotion, plan the mousetrap, and confront his own inaction regarding revenge
beat_states.obstacle: The continued presence of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern, whom he now distrusts as spies

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized by his emerging plan for the play-within-the-play but roiling underneath with self-recrimination. He maintains surface composure and directorial authority but is desperate for privacy. The Player's tears have catalyzed something in him.

social_state.rationale: He reasserts princely authority by giving orders about the First Player and dismissing his 'friends.' The warmth is thin and formulaic — 'You are welcome to Elsinore' is polite but distancing, not intimate.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent by Claudius and Gertrude to spy on him
The players can perform 'The Murder of Gonzago' tomorrow
A player can summon genuine emotion for a fiction while Hamlet cannot act on real grief
He can use the play to test Claudius's guilt
hidden_secrets: He is planning to use the play as a trap for Claudius
He knows Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are not truly his friends
The Ghost's revelation about Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is now operating with clear strategic intent. He conceals his plan from everyone and maintains the pretense of normalcy with R&G.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing courteous hospitality as a mask to conceal his urgency and turmoil
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He offers warm welcome ('You are welcome to Elsinore') to men he knows are sent to spy on him; his graciousness is strategic deception
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The warmth could be read as genuine residual affection for old schoolfellows, momentarily surfacing before he retreats into solitude and self-lacerating soliloquy

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain his position and fulfill his mission by staying in Hamlet's good graces
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the King and Queen by monitoring Hamlet and reporting back, while preserving the friendship facade
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is clearly dismissing them, and Rosencrantz senses he cannot penetrate Hamlet's real thoughts

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is passive and uncertain. His brief reply ('Good my lord') reveals someone with nothing to offer — he has gained no real intelligence and is being managed by Hamlet rather than managing him. Low arousal reflects compliance and resignation.

social_state.rationale: Completely subordinate. His three-word reply acknowledges Hamlet's authority and his own powerlessness in this exchange. Minimal warmth — perfunctory politeness.

known_facts: Hamlet has arranged a play for tomorrow
Hamlet seems interested in the players
Hamlet earlier caught them out as spies
hidden_secrets: He reports to Claudius and Gertrude on Hamlet's behavior
false_beliefs: Hamlet may still consider them genuine friends despite the earlier confrontation
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz has limited insight into Hamlet's true state. He likely does not grasp the strategic significance of the play arrangement.

beat_states.defense_state: Retreating into formulaic courtesy to avoid revealing his surveillance role
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs the role of grateful friend while serving as an agent of the crown against that friend
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz's brevity could indicate discomfort at being dismissed or awareness that Hamlet has seen through them entirely

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ay, so, good-bye to you. / Now I am alone. / O, what a rogue and peasant slave am I! / Is it not monstrous that this player here, / But in a fiction, in a dream of passion, / Could force his soul so to his own conceit / That from her working all his visage wanned, / Tears in his eyes, distraction in his aspect, / A broken voice, and his whole function suiting / With forms to his conceit— and all for nothing! / For Hecuba! / What’s Hecuba to him, or he to Hecuba, / That he should weep for her? What would he do / Had he the motive and the cue for passion / That I have? He would drown the stage with tears / And cleave the general ear with horrid speech, / Make mad the guilty and appall the free, / Confound the ignorant and amaze indeed / The very faculties of eyes and ears. Yet I, / A dull and muddy-mettled rascal, peak / Like John-a-dreams, unpregnant of my cause, / And can say nothing— no, not for a king / Upon whose property and most dear life / A damned defeat was made. Am I a coward? / Who calls me “ villain”? breaks my pate across? / Plucks off my beard and blows it in my face? / Tweaks me by the nose? gives me the lie i’ th’ throat / As deep as to the lungs? Who does me this? / Ha! ’Swounds, I should take it! For it cannot be / But I am pigeon-livered and lack gall / To make oppression bitter, or ere this / I should have fatted all the region kites / With this slave’s offal. Bloody, bawdy villain! / Remorseless, treacherous, lecherous, kindless villain! / O vengeance! / Why, what an ass am I! This is most brave, / That I, the son of a dear father murdered, / Prompted to my revenge by heaven and hell, / Must, like a whore, unpack my heart with words / And fall a-cursing like a very drab, / A stallion! Fie upon ’t! Foh! / About, my brains!— Hum, I have heard / That guilty creatures sitting at a play / Have, by the very cunning of the scene, / Been struck so to the soul that presently / They have proclaimed their malefactions; / For murder, though it have no tongue, will speak / With most miraculous organ. I’ll have these players / Play something like the murder of my father / Before mine uncle. I’ll observe his looks; / I’ll tent him to the quick. If he do blench, / I know my course. The spirit that I have seen / May be a devil, and the devil hath power / T’ assume a pleasing shape; yea, and perhaps, / Out of my weakness and my melancholy, / As he is very potent with such spirits, / Abuses me to damn me. I’ll have grounds / More relative than this. The play’s the thing / Wherein I’ll catch the conscience of the King.

beat_states.desire_state: To overcome his paralysis, find certainty about Claudius's guilt, and devise a concrete plan of action
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the Ghost's command and avenge his father's murder while being certain of its justice
beat_states.obstacle: His own inability to act, self-doubt about whether the Ghost is truthful or a devil exploiting his melancholy, and uncertainty about Claudius's guilt

affect_state.rationale: The soliloquy moves through extreme self-loathing and shame (low valence, high arousal, no control) toward a nascent plan that partially restores agency. The rising certainty at the end ('The play's the thing') provides a momentary surge of control, but the dominant emotional texture is volcanic self-contempt and anguish. He is deeply vulnerable — alone, admitting cowardice, admitting susceptibility to demonic manipulation.

social_state.rationale: Alone, he strips himself of all social status — calling himself rogue, peasant slave, coward, ass, whore, drab. He is brutal and cold toward himself. He occupies the lowest self-assigned status in the play. No warmth toward anyone — contempt for Claudius, contempt for himself.

known_facts: The Ghost has accused Claudius of murder
An actor can summon genuine-seeming emotion for a fiction
Guilty people at plays have been known to confess their crimes spontaneously
The players will perform tomorrow and can learn additional lines
He himself has failed to act despite having real cause
hidden_secrets: He plans to use the play to test Claudius's guilt
He suspects the Ghost may be a devil exploiting his melancholy
He recognizes his own psychological vulnerability and possible manipulation
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is epistemically sophisticated — he doubts even the Ghost, considers that his own melancholy makes him susceptible to demonic deception. He refuses to act on insufficient evidence. His plan to use the play represents a shift from emotional reaction to empirical testing. No clearly false beliefs — his doubt about the Ghost is actually epistemically responsible.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — transforms overwhelming emotional paralysis into a rational plan (the play-within-a-play), converting shame into strategic action
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He despises himself for expressing emotion in words rather than action, yet his greatest breakthrough comes precisely through words — devising the play. He shames himself as a coward while simultaneously demonstrating extraordinary intellectual courage and cunning.
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest the self-flagellation is itself a performance — that Hamlet enjoys and indulges in his own rhetoric of self-hatred as a further form of delay, and that the plan is yet another intellectual dodge rather than genuine movement toward action.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: And can you by no drift of conference / Get from him why he puts on this confusion, / Grating so harshly all his days of quiet / With turbulent and dangerous lunacy?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: He does confess he feels himself distracted, / But from what cause he will by no means speak.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Nor do we find him forward to be sounded, / But with a crafty madness keeps aloof / When we would bring him on to some confession / Of his true state.

beat_states.desire_state: To demonstrate greater perceptiveness than Rosencrantz by offering a sharper analysis of Hamlet's evasiveness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain royal favor and prove his value as an instrument of the King
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's 'crafty madness' has outmaneuvered their attempts at extraction

affect_state.rationale: Slightly more confident than Rosencrantz in his analytical offering, but still anxious about failing the King. His observation about 'crafty madness' shows he's genuinely unsettled by Hamlet's impenetrability.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to the King but subtly competing with Rosencrantz for credit. Slightly cooler and more analytical than Rosencrantz — performing competence rather than warmth.

known_facts: Hamlet is not genuinely open to being questioned
Hamlet's madness appears strategic — 'crafty'
Their direct approaches have been deflected
false_beliefs: May believe he and Rosencrantz can still eventually crack Hamlet's defenses
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern has slightly more insight than Rosencrantz — he perceives the deliberateness in Hamlet's evasion. But he doesn't understand the full stakes or that Hamlet has identified them as the King's agents.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualizes the failure — reframes it as Hamlet being cunningly evasive rather than their being outwitted
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Calling Hamlet's madness 'crafty' inadvertently acknowledges Hamlet may not be mad at all, which undermines the King's framing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern's phrase 'crafty madness' could be a subtle warning to the King that Hamlet is more dangerous than he appears, rather than mere reporting

beat_states.desire_state: To extract intelligence about what Hamlet knows or suspects about his father's murder
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne, his queen, and his life by neutralizing Hamlet's threat
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is evasive and Rosencrantz/Guildenstern have failed to penetrate his defenses

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is anxious and frustrated — his spies have yielded nothing. His use of 'dangerous lunacy' reveals high stakes fear beneath controlled kingly questioning. He doesn't know what Hamlet knows, which terrifies him.

social_state.rationale: Commands the room as king, directing his agents. The warmth is low — this is instrumental, not caring. He is using Rosencrantz and Guildenstern as tools.

known_facts: Hamlet is behaving erratically and possibly dangerously
He murdered King Hamlet and usurped the throne
He deployed Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to spy on Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He murdered his brother to gain the crown and Gertrude
His interrogation of R&G is driven by guilt-fueled paranoia, not paternal concern
false_beliefs: He may still believe Hamlet's disturbance could be mere madness rather than informed suspicion
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is probing to determine whether Hamlet's 'confusion' is genuine madness or a calculated response to knowledge of the murder. He cannot ask directly without exposing himself.

beat_states.defense_state: Frames Hamlet's behavior as 'lunacy' to deflect from the possibility that Hamlet has real cause for his disturbance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Publicly performs concern for Hamlet's wellbeing while privately desperate to determine if Hamlet knows the truth about the murder

beat_states.desire_state: To satisfy the King's demand for information while covering for his own failure to extract it
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain royal favor and advancement by serving the King's wishes
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet admitted distraction but refused to reveal its cause — Rosencrantz has nothing substantive to report

affect_state.rationale: Nervous reporting to a powerful king with insufficient results. Mildly ashamed of failure, anxious about the king's reaction, but trying to present the partial information (Hamlet confessed distraction) as something of value.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate position, reporting up to sovereign power. Mildly warm in his obsequiousness — eager to please, deferential.

known_facts: Hamlet admits to feeling distracted
Hamlet refuses to name the cause
The King wants to know why Hamlet is behaving erratically
false_beliefs: May believe Hamlet's old friendship with them is still genuine enough that their approach was reasonable
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz has surface-level observations but no real insight into Hamlet's mind. He likely doesn't grasp the depth of the political danger or Hamlet's awareness of their role as spies.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflects blame by emphasizing that Hamlet chose silence, implying the failure is Hamlet's stubbornness, not their incompetence
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Wants to appear useful and loyal but has genuinely failed at the task
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may be more cynically calculating than obsequious — strategically sharing just enough to stay in the game

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Did he receive you well?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Most like a gentleman.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: But with much forcing of his disposition.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Niggard of question, but of our demands / Most free in his reply.

beat_states.desire_state: to give an honest but diplomatically careful account of Hamlet's guarded behavior
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain favor with the King and Queen while navigating his conflicted loyalty to Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz is painting a rosier picture than the reality; Guildenstern feels compelled to add a corrective

affect_state.rationale: Guildenstern is slightly more uncomfortable than Rosencrantz. His interjection — 'but with much forcing of his disposition' — reveals he perceived Hamlet's strain and resistance, and he's uneasy about glossing over it.

social_state.rationale: Same subordinate position as Rosencrantz but slightly less ingratiating, more focused on accuracy than pleasing.

known_facts: Hamlet was polite but it cost him visible effort
Hamlet was not naturally forthcoming
Hamlet did not reveal the source of his disturbance
hidden_secrets: the degree to which Hamlet may have seen through their mission
false_beliefs: that the 'forcing of disposition' was merely Hamlet's melancholy rather than active distrust of them
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern perceives more of the truth than Rosencrantz — that Hamlet's courtesy was performed, not genuine — but he doesn't fully grasp that Hamlet has identified them as agents of the King.

beat_states.defense_state: hedging — offering the complication without fully contradicting his partner
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he wants to appear useful to the King but also feels the need to be truthful about Hamlet's resistance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern's correction could be read as subtle self-protection — establishing on record that Hamlet was difficult, so that if their mission fails, they can't be blamed for incompetence.

beat_states.desire_state: to find reassurance that Hamlet is not entirely lost to her, that he still shows signs of his former self
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to protect her son and reconcile the fractured family while maintaining her own position
beat_states.obstacle: she is dependent on intermediaries for information about her son's inner state

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is anxious and seeking comfort; her question is tender and maternal but tinged with worry. She has limited control over the situation and must rely on these courtiers.

social_state.rationale: She speaks as Queen with full authority but her question reveals genuine maternal warmth and concern for her son's welfare.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving strangely
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent to investigate
they have now met with Hamlet
hidden_secrets: her hasty marriage to Claudius and any guilt she feels about it
false_beliefs: that Rosencrantz and Guildenstern can be trusted to give her a full and honest account
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude knows something is wrong but doesn't know the depth of Hamlet's knowledge or pain. She takes the courtiers' reports at face value.

beat_states.defense_state: grasping at surface politeness as evidence of deeper wellness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she wants honest information about Hamlet but also wants to hear he is well
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's question could also be a strategic move to steer the conversation toward her son's emotional state before Claudius can redirect it toward political threat assessment.

beat_states.desire_state: to satisfy the royal couple's inquiry while presenting himself as competent and useful
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain favor with the King and Queen by successfully spying on Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet gave them very little real information; they must report something meaningful from almost nothing

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is mildly anxious about disappointing the monarchs. He puts a positive spin on the encounter — 'most like a gentleman,' 'most free in his reply' — to mask the emptiness of what they actually learned.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate courtier reporting to his sovereigns. He is polite and eager to please, performing deference.

known_facts: Hamlet received them politely but guardedly
Hamlet answered their questions but did not initiate conversation
Hamlet did not reveal the cause of his transformation
hidden_secrets: the degree to which they feel they failed in their mission
false_beliefs: that Hamlet's courtesy indicates some openness to them
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz knows the surface facts of the encounter but misreads or overvalues Hamlet's politeness, not recognizing that Hamlet has already seen through them.

beat_states.defense_state: deflecting failure by emphasizing Hamlet's outward courtesy and his willingness to reply
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he wants to appear successful in his mission but knows Hamlet revealed nothing of substance

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Did you assay him to any pastime?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Madam, it so fell out that certain players / We o’erraught on the way. Of these we told him, / And there did seem in him a kind of joy / To hear of it. They are here about the court, / And, as I think, they have already order / This night to play before him.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: ’Tis most true, / And he beseeched me to entreat your Majesties / To hear and see the matter.

beat_states.desire_state: to position himself as the indispensable insider who facilitated access to Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to cement his status as the King's most trusted and essential advisor
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz is getting the credit for reporting this news; Polonius must insert himself

affect_state.rationale: Eagerly seizing the moment to confirm and amplify Rosencrantz's report, pleased that Hamlet came to HIM with the request — this validates his proximity to power

social_state.rationale: Asserting his advisory status by jumping in to confirm and one-up Rosencrantz; warmth is performative — directed at pleasing the royals

known_facts: Hamlet asked him to invite the King and Queen to the play
The players will perform tonight
He still believes Hamlet's distress stems from rejected love for Ophelia
hidden_secrets: He is about to use Ophelia as bait in the eavesdropping scheme
false_beliefs: That he has correctly diagnosed Hamlet's condition as lovesickness
That Hamlet's enthusiasm for the play is simply recreational
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius is blind to Hamlet's strategic intelligence; he reads the play invitation as innocent entertainment, not recognizing it as a trap Hamlet is setting

beat_states.defense_state: self-aggrandizement — emphasizing his personal role as intermediary
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: presents himself as Hamlet's trusted confidant while simultaneously orchestrating his surveillance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may be slightly more calculating here — using the play invitation as a way to set up the eavesdropping scene he's about to propose, timing his interjection strategically

beat_states.desire_state: to find some wholesome activity or diversion that might cure Hamlet's melancholy
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to restore her son's wellbeing and maintain familial harmony
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet remains opaque and unreachable; she cannot penetrate his inner state

affect_state.rationale: Anxious mother grasping for any positive sign; her brief question reveals she's searching for hopeful news about her son's state

social_state.rationale: As Queen she commands the room, but her warmth is genuine maternal concern directed through her proxies Rosencrantz and Guildenstern

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving strangely
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent to investigate
They have failed to extract a clear cause
hidden_secrets: Her hasty remarriage may be contributing to Hamlet's distress
false_beliefs: That Hamlet's condition might be remedied through simple pastimes and diversions
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude is fishing for practical intelligence, hoping recreational engagement might signal a path to recovery

beat_states.defense_state: channeling maternal anxiety into practical problem-solving
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: genuinely wants to help Hamlet while participating in a surveillance operation against him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's question could be a performative show of maternal concern for the court's benefit rather than genuine worry

beat_states.desire_state: to deliver a useful report that satisfies the King and Queen and justifies his mission
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain royal favor and advance his position at court
beat_states.obstacle: He has largely failed to extract meaningful intelligence from Hamlet and must offer something of value

affect_state.rationale: Relieved to finally have something positive to report; the news of the players is his one tangible offering after an otherwise fruitless mission

social_state.rationale: Subordinate courtier reporting upward, trying to be agreeable and useful; warm enough to seem earnest but deferential

known_facts: Players arrived and Hamlet showed joy at the news
The players are already at court
They plan to perform tonight
Hamlet was evasive about the cause of his distress
hidden_secrets: He may sense Hamlet suspects their true purpose
false_beliefs: That the players' visit is simply a happy coincidence that cheered Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz reports the surface event accurately but has no insight into why Hamlet was so animated about the players — he misses that Hamlet has a strategic purpose for them

beat_states.defense_state: deflecting from his failure by highlighting the one positive discovery — the players
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: presenting himself as a loyal friend to Hamlet while actively reporting on him to his parents/rulers

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: With all my heart, and it doth much content me / To hear him so inclined. / Good gentlemen, give him a further edge / And drive his purpose into these delights.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: We shall, my lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To redirect Rosencrantz and Guildenstern toward keeping Hamlet distracted and occupied so the real trap (the Ophelia encounter) can be set
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To neutralize the threat Hamlet poses to his crown and life by discovering what Hamlet knows
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern have failed to extract the true cause of Hamlet's behavior; Claudius still lacks actionable intelligence

affect_state.rationale: Claudius performs satisfaction but his certainty is low — he still doesn't know what Hamlet is thinking. He maintains high control by pivoting smoothly to dismiss Rosencrantz and Guildenstern before the more critical eavesdropping scene.

social_state.rationale: Claudius commands from the throne with genial authority — 'Good gentlemen' is warm but firmly directive. He maintains clear hierarchical superiority while performing avuncular care.

known_facts: Hamlet has shown interest in the players and the planned performance
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern failed to discover the root of Hamlet's transformation
Hamlet is behaving strangely but possibly not entirely mad
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He is about to use Ophelia as bait to spy on Hamlet with Polonius
He suspects Hamlet may know or suspect the murder
false_beliefs: The players and entertainment may genuinely serve as a useful distraction for Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius knows his intelligence-gathering through Rosencrantz and Guildenstern has yielded little. He's pivoting to Plan B — the staged encounter — but publicly frames the play as a welcome diversion.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing contentment and warmth to mask his deeper anxiety and calculating intent — using the players as a smokescreen for his real surveillance plan
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Outwardly expressing delight at Hamlet's interest in the players while inwardly preparing a far more invasive scheme to spy on Hamlet with Ophelia
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may genuinely feel a moment of relief that Hamlet has found some engagement, briefly hoping the madness might be manageable without extreme measures

beat_states.desire_state: To fulfill the King's command and maintain his position of favor at court
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To advance socially by serving the King faithfully and being seen as useful
beat_states.obstacle: He has failed to extract meaningful intelligence from Hamlet and risks appearing incompetent

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz has delivered a largely empty report and is relieved to be given a simple, clear task. Low control — he is entirely at the King's disposal. Moderate vulnerability as a courtier who has not fully delivered.

social_state.rationale: Deeply subordinate, offering minimal words of compliance. The brevity of 'We shall, my lord' signals complete deference and awareness of his inferior position.

known_facts: Hamlet was evasive and did not reveal the cause of his disturbance
Hamlet showed interest in the players
The King wants Hamlet kept occupied
false_beliefs: He may believe his mission is primarily about helping Hamlet rather than surveilling him
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz has limited understanding of the deeper political stakes. He knows his surface assignment but not the full scope of Claudius's scheming.

beat_states.defense_state: Retreating into obedient formality to avoid scrutiny of his failure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Professing loyal service to the King while possibly sensing he is being used as a pawn in a game he doesn't fully understand
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may be more politically aware than he appears, understanding that compliance without curiosity is the safest posture in a dangerous court

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Sweet Gertrude, leave us too, / For we have closely sent for Hamlet hither, / That he, as ’twere by accident, may here / Affront Ophelia. / Her father and myself, lawful espials, / Will so bestow ourselves that, seeing unseen, / We may of their encounter frankly judge / And gather by him, as he is behaved, / If ’t be th’ affliction of his love or no / That thus he suffers for.

beat_states.desire_state: To remove Gertrude from the scene so he can execute his plan to spy on Hamlet's encounter with Ophelia and determine the cause of his madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his hold on the throne and neutralize the threat Hamlet poses to his power and secret
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude's presence complicates the espionage; he cannot conduct surveillance with her there, and he must frame the spying as legitimate and justified

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in command mode — he has a plan, he's executing it, and he feels relatively in control. Slight positive valence from the sense of agency. Moderate arousal from the stakes involved. Some underlying vulnerability because the outcome of this test could confirm his worst fears about Hamlet.

social_state.rationale: He exercises full royal authority, dismissing the Queen from the room with a gentle but firm command. The 'Sweet Gertrude' is performative warmth — enough to soften the dismissal but fundamentally an exercise of dominance. He positions himself as a concerned stepfather making clinical decisions.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving strangely and 'suffering'
Polonius believes Hamlet's madness stems from love for Ophelia
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern failed to discover the true cause
Hamlet has been summoned to this location
Ophelia will be placed as bait in the encounter
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet and usurped the throne
His deeper motive is assessing whether Hamlet is a political threat, not merely diagnosing lovesickness
He is already considering more drastic measures if Hamlet proves dangerous
false_beliefs: That this scheme will definitively reveal the cause of Hamlet's transformation
That he can fully control and manage the Hamlet situation through surveillance
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius operates with partial knowledge — he knows his own guilt but doesn't know what Hamlet knows. He's using Polonius's love theory as a diagnostic framework, but his real concern is whether Hamlet's behavior masks political awareness of the murder.

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — frames covert spying as lawful and reasonable diagnostic method, calling himself and Polonius 'lawful espials'
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Uses the language of sweetness and care ('Sweet Gertrude') while orchestrating a deceptive trap; claims to want to understand Hamlet's suffering while his deeper motive is to assess the threat Hamlet poses to him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may already suspect that love is NOT the cause and is going through this exercise partly to satisfy Polonius and partly to buy time while he formulates a contingency plan — the espionage is as much about managing Polonius as about diagnosing Hamlet.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: I shall obey you. / And for your part, Ophelia, I do wish / That your good beauties be the happy cause / Of Hamlet’s wildness. So shall I hope your virtues / Will bring him to his wonted way again, / To both your honors.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Madam, I wish it may.

beat_states.desire_state: to respond dutifully to the Queen's blessing while managing the enormous pressure being placed on her
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be a dutiful daughter and virtuous woman while preserving her connection to Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: she is being used as a tool by her father and the King, and now the Queen's hopes add further weight to an encounter she dreads

affect_state.rationale: She is anxious about the coming encounter, has no agency in the plan, and the Queen's kind words ironically increase her emotional exposure. Her brief reply betrays emotional suppression under high internal arousal.

social_state.rationale: Ophelia is markedly subordinate — addressed by a Queen, tasked by her father, instrumentalized by the King. Her warmth is genuine but restrained by the formality of her position.

known_facts: she has been instructed to encounter Hamlet while Claudius and Polonius hide and watch
she has returned Hamlet's letters and gifts as instructed
the Queen hopes she is the cause of Hamlet's madness
hidden_secrets: her own unresolved feelings for Hamlet
her awareness that this encounter is a staged trap
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia knows more about the deceptive nature of this encounter than her brief, innocent-sounding reply suggests. She conceals her complicity in the trap behind a wish.

beat_states.defense_state: constriction — her reply is minimal and formulaic, retreating into obedience to avoid revealing the turmoil beneath
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she wishes it may work (genuine longing for Hamlet's recovery) while knowing she is participating in a deception that may destroy any remaining trust between them
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia's 'I wish it may' could be read as entirely sincere and hopeful rather than anxious — a young woman genuinely praying love will heal the man she cares for.

beat_states.desire_state: to express genuine hope that Ophelia's love can cure Hamlet, while gracefully complying with Claudius's request to leave
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to preserve her son's wellbeing and maintain the stability of her marriage and household
beat_states.obstacle: she is being excluded from the scheme to observe Hamlet, losing control over how her son is handled

affect_state.rationale: She hopes but cannot be sure; being sent away diminishes her control. The gentleness toward Ophelia masks anxiety about Hamlet's state. Her compliance with 'I shall obey you' reveals subordination she may not fully embrace.

social_state.rationale: As Queen she maintains high status even while obeying, and she extends genuine maternal warmth to Ophelia — treating her almost as a potential daughter-in-law, conferring dignity on her.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving wildly
Claudius and Polonius plan to observe Hamlet's encounter with Ophelia
Ophelia and Hamlet had a romantic connection
hidden_secrets: her own possible guilt about her hasty remarriage and its effect on Hamlet
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness may be primarily caused by lovesickness for Ophelia
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude clings to the lovesickness explanation because it is the least threatening diagnosis — it avoids implicating her own actions or suggesting political danger.

beat_states.defense_state: displacement — channeling her anxiety about Hamlet onto a hopeful romantic narrative rather than confronting darker possibilities
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she obeys Claudius's dismissal ('I shall obey you') while simultaneously asserting maternal authority by addressing Ophelia directly, revealing tension between her submission to Claudius and her need to mother the situation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may be performing warmth toward Ophelia partly to manage appearances, aware that Polonius and Claudius are watching, rather than expressing fully sincere hope.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Ophelia, walk you here.— Gracious, so please you, / We will bestow ourselves. To Ophelia. Read on this book, / That show of such an exercise may color / Your loneliness.— We are oft to blame in this / ( ’Tis too much proved), that with devotion’s visage / And pious action we do sugar o’er / The devil himself.

beat_states.desire_state: To stage-manage the encounter between Hamlet and Ophelia so that the King can observe and confirm Polonius's theory about lovesickness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain and increase his position of power and influence at court by being the indispensable counselor who understands everything
beat_states.obstacle: The deception must appear natural; Ophelia must perform convincingly, and the artifice of the setup must not be detected by Hamlet

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is in his element as stage-director of this trap, feeling high control and fairly certain of his plan, but the aside about 'devotion's visage' betrays a flicker of guilt and unease that momentarily lowers valence and introduces slight vulnerability

social_state.rationale: He commands Ophelia with paternal authority and addresses the King deferentially but as a trusted co-conspirator; warmth toward Ophelia is minimal — she is an instrument of his plan

known_facts: Hamlet has been sending love tokens and letters to Ophelia
The King and he will hide to observe Hamlet's behavior with Ophelia
Ophelia must appear to be reading alone and pious to seem natural
The Queen hopes Ophelia's beauty is the cause of Hamlet's madness
hidden_secrets: He is using his own daughter as bait in a surveillance operation
He recognizes the moral dubiousness of this deception even as he enacts it
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is primarily caused by unrequited love for Ophelia
This scheme will definitively prove his theory and cement his standing
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius operates with a confident but incomplete picture — he is sure love explains Hamlet's behavior, blind to deeper political and existential currents, yet momentarily lucid about the hypocrisy of his own methods

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — he distances himself from the moral ugliness of using his daughter as bait by philosophizing about hypocrisy in general terms
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is actively 'sugaring o'er the devil' with pious action in the very moment he acknowledges that people do exactly this — a flash of self-awareness he cannot fully integrate
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The aside about 'devotion's visage' could be read as purely impersonal moralizing with no self-awareness at all — Polonius simply enjoying the sound of his own wisdom — rather than a genuine moment of conscience

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: O, ’tis too true! / How smart a lash that speech doth give my conscience. / The harlot’s cheek beautied with plast’ring art / Is not more ugly to the thing that helps it / Than is my deed to my most painted word. / O heavy burden!

beat_states.desire_state: to momentarily unburden himself of the crushing guilt over his fratricide
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his stolen crown and power while escaping the consequences of his crime
beat_states.obstacle: his own conscience, awakened by Polonius's casual mention of pious appearance hiding deception

affect_state.rationale: This is a rare crack in Claudius's composed exterior. The guilt is certain and overwhelming — he knows exactly what he's done. His control collapses momentarily. High arousal from the sudden sting of conscience. Deep negative valence from self-disgust.

social_state.rationale: In this aside, he drops from his usual high-status kingly performance. He is alone with himself, stripped of social pretense. No warmth — only self-revulsion and the cold recognition of his own hypocrisy.

known_facts: He murdered his brother King Hamlet
His public persona is a facade covering his crime
Polonius's words about 'devotion's visage' and 'pious action' sugaring over 'the devil himself' apply directly to him
He and Polonius are about to spy on Hamlet's encounter with Ophelia
hidden_secrets: He is the murderer of Old Hamlet
His marriage to Gertrude was built on fratricide
His 'painted word' conceals the ugliness of regicide
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is one of the most epistemically clear characters in the play — he knows exactly what he has done. This aside is remarkable precisely because it is a moment of total self-knowledge. No false beliefs operate here; the pain comes from seeing the truth too clearly.

beat_states.defense_state: displacement — comparing his guilt to a harlot's painted cheek externalizes his self-loathing into metaphor rather than naming the murder directly
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is the architect of the trap being set for Hamlet, performing kingly authority, yet is suddenly undone by an accidental trigger that exposes his inner torment — the manipulator momentarily becomes the most vulnerable person on stage
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: One could read this as a calculated performance — Claudius speaking aloud to test whether anyone is listening, gauging the room — but the aside convention and the later prayer scene (3.3) confirm this as genuine anguish.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: I hear him coming. Let’s withdraw, my lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To execute the spying plan flawlessly by getting himself and the King hidden before Hamlet arrives
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain and increase his political influence by proving his theory about Hamlet's madness and demonstrating his indispensability to the King
beat_states.obstacle: Time pressure — Hamlet is approaching and they must conceal themselves immediately or the entire scheme will be exposed

affect_state.rationale: Elevated arousal from the urgency of hearing Hamlet approach; high certainty that his plan will work; positive valence from the excitement of his scheme unfolding; feels in control as the stage-manager of this eavesdropping scenario

social_state.rationale: He addresses the King with deference ('my lord') but is effectively directing the King's movements, giving him a momentary operational authority; warmth is low — Ophelia is left alone as bait with no parting reassurance

known_facts: Hamlet is approaching — he can hear him
The trap is set with Ophelia positioned with her prayer book
He and the King must be hidden to observe the encounter
He believes Hamlet's madness stems from rejected love for Ophelia
hidden_secrets: He has orchestrated this entire encounter to prove his own theory and secure his position
He is using his own daughter as an instrument of surveillance
false_beliefs: He believes Hamlet's antic disposition is caused by lovesickness for Ophelia
He assumes Hamlet will not detect the surveillance
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius operates with high confidence in his diagnosis of Hamlet but is blind to Hamlet's intelligence and perceptiveness; he also seems oblivious to the significance of the King's guilt-ridden aside moments ago

beat_states.defense_state: compartmentalization — setting aside the King's unexpected guilty confession to focus on the task at hand
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is the architect of a scheme to spy on intimate emotional truth, yet he is fundamentally incapable of recognizing genuine emotional depth — he just witnessed the King's anguished confession and shows no reaction to it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may have registered the King's guilty confession and is deliberately ignoring it to protect himself politically — choosing not to know what he has just heard

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: To be or not to be— that is the question: / Whether ’tis nobler in the mind to suffer / The slings and arrows of outrageous fortune, / Or to take arms against a sea of troubles / And, by opposing, end them. To die, to sleep— / No more— and by a sleep to say we end / The heartache and the thousand natural shocks / That flesh is heir to— ’tis a consummation / Devoutly to be wished. To die, to sleep— / To sleep, perchance to dream. Ay, there’s the rub, / For in that sleep of death what dreams may come, / When we have shuffled off this mortal coil, / Must give us pause. There’s the respect / That makes calamity of so long life. / For who would bear the whips and scorns of time, / Th’ oppressor’s wrong, the proud man’s contumely, / The pangs of despised love, the law’s delay, / The insolence of office, and the spurns / That patient merit of th’ unworthy takes, / When he himself might his quietus make / With a bare bodkin? Who would fardels bear, / To grunt and sweat under a weary life, / But that the dread of something after death, / The undiscovered country from whose bourn / No traveler returns, puzzles the will / And makes us rather bear those ills we have / Than fly to others that we know not of? / Thus conscience does make cowards of us all, / And thus the native hue of resolution / Is sicklied o’er with the pale cast of thought, / And enterprises of great pitch and moment / With this regard their currents turn awry / And lose the name of action.— Soft you now, / The fair Ophelia.— Nymph, in thy orisons / Be all my sins remembered.

beat_states.desire_state: To reason through whether existence or self-annihilation is the rational choice — to find justification for action or release from unbearable suffering
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the ghost's command and set right a disordered world, but his paralysis over action/inaction connects directly to his inability to act against Claudius
beat_states.obstacle: His own intellect and moral consciousness — the dread of unknown consequences after death, and the fear that thought itself destroys the capacity to act

affect_state.rationale: Profoundly negative in feeling but controlled in expression — a measured, almost detached tone masks suicidal despair. Low arousal belies the gravity of the content; he is deeply uncertain about metaphysical reality and has lost sense of agency. The shift at 'Soft you now' shows a sudden recalibration as external reality intrudes.

social_state.rationale: Speaking alone (he believes), status is self-directed — he positions himself as both philosopher-judge and condemned patient of the world's injustices. The brief warmth toward Ophelia ('fair Ophelia,' 'Nymph') at the end is tinged with formality and distance, possibly performative given his suspicion of surveillance.

known_facts: Claudius killed his father
He has been commanded by the ghost to take revenge
He has failed to act despite knowing the truth
Ophelia has been positioned here — he may suspect a trap
Death is irreversible and its aftermath unknowable
hidden_secrets: He is contemplating suicide
He knows Claudius is a murderer
He may be aware or suspect that Claudius and Polonius are watching
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's epistemological crisis is the engine of the speech — the 'undiscovered country' represents the limit of knowledge itself. He knows the facts of his situation but cannot resolve them into action. His awareness of Ophelia's sudden convenient presence may feed his suspicion of being watched, coloring the shift in tone at the end.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — transforming personal anguish into abstract philosophical inquiry, universalizing his specific suffering into 'we' and 'us' to distance from it
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He craves the peace of death yet fears it; he condemns his own cowardice for not acting yet demonstrates the very paralysis he diagnoses; he generalizes about human weakness while speaking from deeply personal torment
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The entire soliloquy may be partially performative — if Hamlet suspects he is being observed (having already intuited Claudius and Polonius's scheming), the speech could be a deliberate display of melancholic distraction to reinforce the 'antic disposition' and mislead his watchers, rather than a purely genuine outpouring of existential crisis.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Good my lord, / How does your Honor for this many a day?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I humbly thank you, well.

beat_states.desire_state: to maintain emotional distance and assess whether this encounter is genuine or staged
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father while navigating a court full of deceit and surveillance
beat_states.obstacle: his residual feelings for Ophelia and suspicion that she is being used against him

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is emotionally raw from the soliloquy, still in a state of existential despair. His flat, polite response suggests deliberate emotional suppression. He is guarded but not yet aggressive.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet holds higher social rank and his terse reply subtly asserts distance. The courtly politeness is cool and withholding rather than warm — a marked contrast to a former lover.

known_facts: Polonius is a meddler who serves the king
Claudius is watching him closely
Ophelia was told to reject his advances previously
the court suspects his madness
hidden_secrets: he is feigning or exaggerating madness
he knows his uncle murdered his father
he has been contemplating suicide
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet likely suspects but may not yet be certain that this meeting is a setup. His guard is already up from the soliloquy's dark meditation and his general distrust of the court.

beat_states.defense_state: emotional withdrawal — his curt, formulaic reply ('I humbly thank you, well') keeps Ophelia at arm's length
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he has just contemplated suicide and the meaninglessness of existence, yet must now perform social normalcy; he may still love Ophelia but suspects she is a tool of his enemies
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may not yet suspect the trap at all and his coldness is simply the residue of his soliloquy — he is still half-absent, not yet fully engaged with Ophelia

beat_states.desire_state: to initiate contact with Hamlet and draw him into conversation as instructed
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be a dutiful daughter and obedient subject while preserving her love for Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's unpredictable behavior and her own conflicted feelings about being used as a spy

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is nervous and exposed — she knows she is being watched and used as bait. Her pleasant greeting conceals deep anxiety about the encounter and guilt about her role in the trap.

social_state.rationale: She adopts a deferential, gentle tone ('Good my lord,' 'your Honor'), positioning herself as subordinate. Her warmth is genuine but strained by the circumstances.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving strangely
her father and the king are hidden and watching
she has been instructed to return Hamlet's love tokens
Hamlet once courted her
hidden_secrets: she is part of a deliberate surveillance operation
false_beliefs: that Hamlet may still be approachable through gentle courtly address
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia knows the trap but not Hamlet's full state of mind. She may not realize how deeply suspicious and volatile he has become.

beat_states.defense_state: performing normalcy — using courtly pleasantries to mask the staged nature of the encounter
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she loves Hamlet yet is complicit in deceiving him; she must appear casual while her father and king watch from hiding
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may be genuinely hoping this encounter will reveal that Hamlet still loves her, using the staged meeting as an excuse to reconnect

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: My lord, I have remembrances of yours / That I have longèd long to redeliver. / I pray you now receive them.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: No, not I. I never gave you aught.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: My honored lord, you know right well you did, / And with them words of so sweet breath composed / As made the things more rich. Their perfume lost, / Take these again, for to the noble mind / Rich gifts wax poor when givers prove unkind. / There, my lord.

beat_states.desire_state: to sever the connection with Ophelia and deny any past intimacy
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to cut through the corruption and falseness of the world around him, including relationships that may be weaponized against him
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's physical presence and her insistence on returning tokens of genuine love, which threatens to reawaken feeling and expose his vulnerability

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet has just emerged from the existential abyss of 'To be or not to be.' Ophelia's approach triggers suspicion and buried pain. His flat denial is controlled on the surface but masks deep emotional turbulence and uncertainty about whether she is complicit in surveillance.

social_state.rationale: He asserts dominance through cold denial, refusing the social ritual of gift-return, treating her with deliberate coldness to maintain the upper hand and test her.

known_facts: He gave Ophelia gifts and love letters
Polonius is Ophelia's father and a schemer
The King and Polonius have been monitoring his behavior
He has been instructed to encounter Ophelia here
hidden_secrets: He suspects this encounter is staged to spy on him
His antic disposition is at least partly performed
He still harbors feelings for Ophelia
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet likely suspects or knows he is being watched (Polonius's 'loosing' of Ophelia). His denial of the gifts is strategic rather than delusional — he knows what he gave her but refuses to acknowledge it in what he perceives as a trap.

beat_states.defense_state: disavowal — flatly negating reality to create emotional distance and protect himself from manipulation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He did give her those gifts and likely still loves her, yet he denies ever having done so, caught between genuine feeling and suspicion that she is being used as a trap
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's denial could represent genuine dissociation — his identity has fractured to the point where the man who loved Ophelia feels like a different person, and the denial is sincere in psychological terms even if factually false.

beat_states.desire_state: to return Hamlet's remembrances and thereby achieve closure or provoke him into revealing his true feelings
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be a dutiful daughter and faithful lover — these two imperatives are now in direct, agonizing conflict
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's cold denial that he ever gave her anything, which invalidates her experience and leaves her emotionally stranded between obedience to her father and love for Hamlet

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is deeply hurt by Hamlet's denial. Her carefully prepared speech about returning the gifts dissolves into raw grief. The line 'their perfume lost' betrays the sensory intimacy of what she has lost. She is exposed, performing for hidden watchers while experiencing real devastation.

social_state.rationale: She begins with deference ('My lord,' 'My honored lord') but her reproach ('when givers prove unkind') is a rare assertion of dignity. She occupies a subordinate position but claims moral high ground.

known_facts: Hamlet gave her gifts and sweet words
Hamlet has been behaving strangely and unkindly
Her father and the King are hidden and watching
She was instructed to return the gifts as part of a test
hidden_secrets: She is complicit in the surveillance arranged by Polonius and Claudius
She may still love Hamlet despite performing rejection
false_beliefs: She may believe Hamlet's transformation is caused by lovesickness for her, as her father claimed
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia knows this is a staged encounter but her emotions are genuine. She is caught in an impossible epistemic position — tasked with extracting information from someone she loves while concealing the watchers behind the arras.

beat_states.defense_state: moralization — framing the return of gifts as a principled act ('rich gifts wax poor when givers prove unkind') to dignify what is actually a devastating emotional moment
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She says she has 'longed long to redeliver' the gifts, suggesting she wanted to hold onto them; she is performing a duty she doesn't fully want to perform, using moral language to cover heartbreak
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may be more willingly complicit than sympathetic readings suggest — her moral speech could be genuinely angry rather than heartbroken, a woman who has come to believe Hamlet used and discarded her.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ha, ha, are you honest?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: My lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Are you fair?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: What means your Lordship?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: That if you be honest and fair, your honesty should admit no discourse to your beauty.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Could beauty, my lord, have better commerce than with honesty?

beat_states.desire_state: To test Ophelia's truthfulness and expose the corruption he perceives in her and all women
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To purge the rot from Denmark and confront the falseness that surrounds him
beat_states.obstacle: He suspects Ophelia is being used as a tool by Claudius and Polonius; he may still have feelings for her that complicate his attack

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is agitated and bitter, wielding his intellect like a weapon. He controls the exchange but his rapid, aggressive questioning betrays inner turbulence. The 'Ha, ha' is mirthless — a barking, almost manic onset that signals he has shifted from contemplation to attack mode.

social_state.rationale: He dominates the exchange, firing questions without answering hers, asserting total conversational power. His warmth is aggressively withdrawn — he treats her as a philosophical specimen rather than a person he once loved.

known_facts: Ophelia has returned his gifts at someone's direction
He suspects Polonius and Claudius are watching this encounter
His mother remarried with indecent haste, corrupting his view of female constancy
Beauty can corrupt honesty more easily than honesty can transform beauty
hidden_secrets: He is performing madness at least partly for hidden observers
He still harbors feeling for Ophelia beneath the cruelty
He has just been contemplating suicide
false_beliefs: That Ophelia is willingly complicit in the plot against him rather than a coerced pawn
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet operates on multiple levels — testing Ophelia, performing for watchers, and processing his own misogynistic philosophical spiral. His question 'Are you honest?' is simultaneously about chastity, truthfulness, and whether she is spying.

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement — channeling his rage at his mother's betrayal and the world's corruption onto Ophelia through philosophical aggression
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is punishing the woman he loved for the sins of others, using cruelty as a shield against his own vulnerability and grief
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be genuinely asking — not just testing — hoping against hope that Ophelia will prove herself honest and break through his despair, making the cruelty a desperate plea for authentic connection.

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what Hamlet means and to maintain connection with him despite his bewildering hostility
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be a dutiful daughter while preserving her love for Hamlet and her own sense of worth
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's sudden aggression and cryptic philosophical attacks leave her unable to find footing; she is caught between her father's instructions and her genuine feelings

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is deeply confused and increasingly frightened. Her brief, questioning responses ('My lord?' 'What means your Lordship?') reveal she is scrambling to keep up. Her final response about beauty and honesty shows she is trying to engage intellectually but from a position of bewilderment.

social_state.rationale: She is entirely subordinate — using formal address ('my lord,' 'your Lordship'), asking permission to understand. She retains some warmth, still trying to engage him as a person she cares about, offering a gentle counter-argument about beauty and honesty.

known_facts: Her father and the king are hidden and watching
She was instructed to return Hamlet's gifts
Hamlet once professed deep love for her
Hamlet has been behaving strangely
hidden_secrets: She is complicit in the eavesdropping plot
She may still love Hamlet despite being told to reject him
false_beliefs: That Hamlet's madness is genuine and caused by love for her
That honesty and beauty are naturally complementary
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia does not fully grasp that Hamlet may know about the surveillance. She takes his philosophical attack at face value, trying to parse its meaning, not yet understanding it as an accusation of her complicity.

beat_states.defense_state: Appealing to reason and conventional wisdom — she tries to meet his philosophical provocations with logical, innocent responses
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is performing obedience to her father's scheme while genuinely wanting to reach Hamlet; she cannot reconcile the man she loved with this hostile stranger
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may understand more than she lets on — her question 'Could beauty have better commerce than with honesty?' could be a quiet, dignified defense of herself and their former love, not mere naïveté.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ay, truly, for the power of beauty will sooner transform honesty from what it is to a bawd than the force of honesty can translate beauty into his likeness. This was sometime a paradox, but now the time gives it proof. I did love you once.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Indeed, my lord, you made me believe so.

beat_states.desire_state: To philosophically justify his rejection of Ophelia while testing whether she is complicit in the corruption around him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose and root out the corruption in Denmark, even at the cost of his own relationships and happiness
beat_states.obstacle: His genuine past love for Ophelia conflicts with his conviction that beauty corrupts honesty, and his suspicion that she is being used against him

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet oscillates between bitter philosophical certainty about corruption and the vulnerability of admitting past love. The admission 'I did love you once' is a moment where control slips and genuine feeling surfaces through the intellectual defense.

social_state.rationale: He lectures from a position of intellectual and moral superiority, but the admission of past love introduces a flicker of warmth that undermines his dominant coldness

known_facts: He loved Ophelia in the past
The world around him is corrupt — 'the time gives it proof'
Beauty can be used as a tool of manipulation
Ophelia is returning his remembrances on someone else's instruction (he suspects)
hidden_secrets: He may suspect Claudius and Polonius are watching this encounter
His antic disposition is partly performance
He is still emotionally attached to Ophelia despite his philosophical stance
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's worldview has hardened since his father's murder and mother's remarriage — he now sees beauty as inherently corrupting rather than paradoxically so. His admission of love is factual but he frames it as past tense deliberately.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — converting personal pain into philosophical argument about beauty and honesty
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He admits 'I did love you once' — a sudden crack of sincerity breaking through his cynical philosophical armor, revealing the wound beneath the lecture
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be deliberately performing cruelty for the hidden observers, saying 'I did love you once' specifically to bait Ophelia into a response that will reveal whether she is acting as a spy

beat_states.desire_state: To hold onto the truth of Hamlet's love as something real, to get him to acknowledge what existed between them
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain her sense of self and worth through the validation of genuine love in a world where she is controlled by others
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is philosophically dismantling the very possibility of honest love, and she is caught between his cruelty and her father's instructions

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is deeply wounded. Hamlet's admission 'I did love you once' gives a brief flash of hope immediately followed by the past tense's sting. Her reply carries the weight of betrayal — 'you made me believe so' — implying he is responsible for her vulnerability.

social_state.rationale: She is in a subordinate position — a woman being lectured by a prince — but her response carries quiet dignity and residual warmth rooted in genuine feeling

known_facts: Hamlet courted her with words, letters, and gifts
Her father and the king are listening to this conversation
Hamlet is behaving strangely and cruelly
hidden_secrets: She is acting as a decoy in Polonius and Claudius's scheme
She still loves Hamlet
false_beliefs: She may believe Hamlet's madness is genuine rather than performed
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia knows the external facts of their relationship and the setup of this encounter, but cannot penetrate Hamlet's true state of mind. Her 'you made me believe so' reveals she is no longer certain his love was ever real.

beat_states.defense_state: Quiet assertion — she gently but firmly corrects the record, insisting on the reality of his courtship
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She obediently follows her father's plan to test Hamlet, yet her response reveals genuine hurt and a need for Hamlet to own his feelings rather than erase them
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia's reply could be read as more accusatory than vulnerable — a pointed indictment rather than a wounded plea — suggesting she is angrier than she appears

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: You should not have believed me, for virtue cannot so inoculate our old stock but we shall relish of it. I loved you not.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: I was the more deceived.

beat_states.desire_state: to sever Ophelia's attachment to him and destroy her hope
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to purge the corruption around him, even at the cost of destroying what he loves, as part of his larger war against the rotten state
beat_states.obstacle: his genuine past love for Ophelia and the pain of denying it; his awareness that she may be a pawn being used against him

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in pain but controlling its expression through deliberate coldness. His certainty is low because he is performing a lie he doesn't fully believe. High vulnerability because he is actively destroying something precious to him.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself above Ophelia by lecturing her about what she should and shouldn't have believed, wielding superior knowledge and moral authority. Warmth is aggressively negative — he is being deliberately cruel.

known_facts: He suspects or knows Claudius and Polonius are watching
Ophelia has been deployed as a tool to test him
He did love Ophelia in the past
Human nature is inherently corrupted ('our old stock')
hidden_secrets: He still loves Ophelia
His cruelty is strategic, not sincere
He is performing madness partly for the hidden audience
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with high awareness of the surveillance situation. His declaration 'I loved you not' is a tactical lie designed to protect himself and possibly to protect her by pushing her away from him.
