beat_states.defense_state: denial of love as self-protective cruelty — by claiming he never loved her, he preemptively destroys the bond before it can be weaponized against him
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He simultaneously tells her 'I loved you not' while having just referenced his love as real in the previous beat ('I did love you once' is implied in his discourse about beauty corrupting honesty). The contradiction reveals that his cruelty is performed, not felt.
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may genuinely be in a nihilistic state where he has convinced himself his love was never real — that all human feeling is corrupted by original sin ('our old stock'). In this reading, his self-loathing has genuinely destroyed his capacity to believe in his own love.

beat_states.desire_state: to hold onto the truth of Hamlet's love and her own worth in the face of his denial
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be loved and valued by Hamlet, which gives her life meaning and identity
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's direct, brutal denial of ever having loved her, which annihilates the foundation of her emotional world

affect_state.rationale: Devastated but contained. Low arousal because the shock is paralyzing rather than activating. Her certainty about reality is shattered. She has almost no control over this interaction. Maximum vulnerability — her heart is fully exposed.

social_state.rationale: She has been placed in a humiliatingly low position — told she was foolish to believe him. Her line carries a trace of warmth through its honesty and grief, but it also subtly redirects blame back to him: she was deceived, meaning he was the deceiver.

known_facts: Hamlet once gave her tokens of love
He is now denying that love
Her father and the King are watching from hiding
hidden_secrets: She is participating in a scheme to spy on Hamlet
She is being torn between obedience to her father and loyalty to Hamlet
false_beliefs: She may believe Hamlet's cruelty reflects his true feelings rather than a performance
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia is caught between what she experienced (his love) and what he now asserts (it was never real). She likely does not fully understand the strategic dimension of his cruelty.

beat_states.defense_state: quiet dignified withdrawal — her brief, restrained response conceals the magnitude of her devastation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She implies she was deceived, which means she still believes his past love was real (or appeared real) even as she accepts his current claim. She cannot reconcile the Hamlet who loved her with the one speaking now.
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia's 'I was the more deceived' could carry a note of bitter accusation rather than pure grief — she may be more angry than wounded, subtly indicting Hamlet as a liar and manipulator.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Get thee to a nunnery. Why wouldst thou be a breeder of sinners? I am myself indifferent honest, but yet I could accuse me of such things that it were better my mother had not borne me: I am very proud, revengeful, ambitious, with more offenses at my beck than I have thoughts to put them in, imagination to give them shape, or time to act them in. What should such fellows as I do crawling between earth and heaven? We are arrant knaves all; believe none of us. Go thy ways to a nunnery. Where’s your father?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: At home, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Let the doors be shut upon him that he may play the fool nowhere but in ’s own house. Farewell.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: O, help him, you sweet heavens!

beat_states.desire_state: To drive Ophelia away from him and from the corrupt world, while simultaneously testing whether she is complicit in the surveillance he suspects
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To purge the rot in Denmark and fulfill his duty to his father's ghost, even at the cost of destroying what he loves
beat_states.obstacle: His genuine feelings for Ophelia conflict with his need to reject her; his suspicion that Polonius is hidden nearby watching

affect_state.rationale: Escalating fury mixed with genuine self-disgust; the sudden pivot to 'Where's your father?' reveals his paranoid alertness — he is performing rage but also genuinely spiraling. His vulnerability leaks through the litany of self-accusation.

social_state.rationale: He dominates the exchange through verbal force and commands ('Get thee to a nunnery'), but his warmth is actively hostile — yet the hostility itself is a distortion of care. When Ophelia lies about her father, his contempt snaps into cold authority.

known_facts: He strongly suspects Polonius is hiding nearby and listening
Ophelia has been set up as bait in this encounter
He declared he did not love Ophelia in the previous beat
The ghost's revelation about Claudius and the moral corruption of the court
hidden_secrets: He knows about Claudius's murder of his father
His antic disposition is at least partly performed
He may still love Ophelia beneath the cruelty
epistemic_state.rationale: The trap question 'Where's your father?' is a deliberate test — when Ophelia answers 'At home,' Hamlet's suspicion is confirmed that she is lying and complicit. This triggers his immediate attack on Polonius ('Let the doors be shut upon him').

beat_states.defense_state: Self-flagellation as deflection — by cataloging his own sins he preempts vulnerability and transforms intimate pain into generalized misanthropy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He simultaneously warns Ophelia away from sinners (protecting her) and verbally brutalizes her (destroying her); his self-loathing and his cruelty are two faces of the same wound
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Hamlet catches a glimpse of Polonius hiding at this moment rather than already suspecting — the question 'Where's your father?' may be the moment of discovery rather than confirmation of prior suspicion

beat_states.desire_state: To endure Hamlet's onslaught and fulfill her father's instructions while desperately hoping to reach the Hamlet she once knew
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be a dutiful daughter and faithful lover — two allegiances now tearing her apart
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's escalating verbal violence makes connection impossible; her lie about her father's location traps her in complicity she didn't choose

affect_state.rationale: She is utterly overwhelmed — the man she loved is savaging himself and her, commanding her to a nunnery, and she has no agency in the situation. Her invocation of heaven is a cry of helplessness, not a tactic. She is breaking.

social_state.rationale: Completely subordinate — she is spoken at, commanded, tested. Yet her warmth persists in her genuine concern for his wellbeing, expressed in her plea to the heavens.

known_facts: Her father and the King are hidden and watching
She was instructed to return Hamlet's gifts and engage him
Hamlet has just denied loving her and told her to go to a nunnery
hidden_secrets: Polonius and Claudius are eavesdropping on this conversation
false_beliefs: She likely believes Hamlet's madness is genuine rather than performed
She may believe her lie about Polonius's location was harmless
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia is caught between two knowledge systems — what her father told her to do and what she is witnessing. She doesn't grasp that her lie about Polonius's location was a test she just failed, sealing the destruction of her relationship with Hamlet.

beat_states.defense_state: Obedient compliance — she retreats into the scripted lie her father gave her, defaulting to dutiful obedience as a shield against chaos
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She lies to protect her father's scheme while genuinely wanting to help Hamlet; her prayer to 'sweet heavens' reveals she believes his madness is real even as she participates in the apparatus designed to diagnose it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings give Ophelia more awareness — she may understand that Hamlet suspects the eavesdropping and that her lie has consequences, making her aside to heaven an expression of guilt as much as compassion

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: If thou dost marry, I’ll give thee this plague for thy dowry: be thou as chaste as ice, as pure as snow, thou shalt not escape calumny. Get thee to a nunnery, farewell. Or if thou wilt needs marry, marry a fool, for wise men know well enough what monsters you make of them. To a nunnery, go, and quickly too. Farewell.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Heavenly powers, restore him!

beat_states.desire_state: To drive Ophelia away permanently, severing the bond before it can be weaponized against him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To purge corruption from Denmark and fulfill his duty to his father's ghost, which requires destroying all compromised attachments
beat_states.obstacle: His genuine love for Ophelia pulls against his need to repudiate her; his awareness that they are likely being watched constrains authentic expression

affect_state.rationale: Highly agitated and bitter, escalating his verbal assault with mounting intensity. The repeated 'Get thee to a nunnery' and rapid oscillations between marriage advice and rejection reveal someone losing control of his own performance. Beneath the fury is deep pain about corruption and the impossibility of purity.

social_state.rationale: He dominates the exchange completely, delivering pronouncements and commandments while Ophelia can only absorb. His warmth is deeply negative — deliberately cruel, weaponizing intimacy — yet the very specificity of his 'plague for thy dowry' reveals how intimately he imagines their marriage.

known_facts: Ophelia has returned his gifts and denied his access
He suspects or knows Claudius and Polonius are watching
Marriage in this corrupt world will inevitably destroy innocence
He himself is deeply flawed and dangerous to her
hidden_secrets: He may still love Ophelia
He is performing madness partly for hidden observers
He knows about Claudius's murder of his father
false_beliefs: That completely severing this relationship will protect either of them
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet operates on multiple epistemic levels — speaking to Ophelia, to the hidden watchers, and to himself simultaneously. His warnings about calumny and cuckoldry are both genuine philosophical convictions and strategic misdirection.

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement — channeling self-loathing and grief over lost love into misogynistic prophetic pronouncements, converting vulnerability into aggression
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is destroying the person he loves most in order to protect her (or himself, or both) — his cruelty is an expression of care turned inside out
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may have fully succumbed to genuine misogynistic rage rather than performing it strategically — his mother's betrayal may have genuinely poisoned his capacity to see women as anything but corrupt, and this is uncontrolled emotional flooding rather than calculated cruelty.

beat_states.desire_state: To make sense of Hamlet's transformation and to find some way to help or reach him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be a dutiful, loving woman who fulfills her obligations to father, prince, and God — to hold her world together
beat_states.obstacle: The man she loves is unrecognizable, attacking her with escalating venom, and she has no power to stop or redirect him

affect_state.rationale: Devastated and helpless. Her single-line response — a prayer — reveals someone who has no human resources left and turns to the divine. She is emotionally shattered, with no agency, no defense, and no understanding of what is happening to the person she loves.

social_state.rationale: Completely subordinated — she cannot speak back, argue, or resist. Yet her warmth remains positive: her prayer is FOR him, not against him. Even in devastation, she reaches toward compassion rather than self-protection.

known_facts: Hamlet once loved her and gave her tokens of affection
He is now rejecting and cursing her violently
Her father and the King are listening from hiding
hidden_secrets: She was instructed to stage this encounter as a trap
She may feel guilt about her complicity in the surveillance
false_beliefs: That Hamlet's cruelty is purely a symptom of madness rather than a deliberate strategy
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia genuinely believes Hamlet has lost his mind. She cannot perceive the layers of performance, strategy, and displaced grief. Her interpretation — 'restore him' — frames this as illness, not choice, which is both compassionate and tragically incomplete.

beat_states.defense_state: Sublimation — converting unbearable personal anguish into a prayer to heavenly powers, externalizing the crisis as madness rather than cruelty directed at her
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She interprets his cruelty as madness rather than hatred, which protects her from the full devastation but also prevents her from defending herself or seeing reality clearly
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may partially recognize that Hamlet knows about the surveillance and is performing, which would add a layer of shared shame and complicity to her prayer — 'restore him' could mean restore the situation to before all this manipulation began.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I have heard of your paintings too, well enough. God hath given you one face, and you make yourselves another. You jig and amble, and you lisp; you nickname God’s creatures and make your wantonness your ignorance. Go to, I’ll no more on ’t. It hath made me mad. I say we will have no more marriage. Those that are married already, all but one, shall live. The rest shall keep as they are. To a nunnery, go.

beat_states.desire_state: To annihilate Ophelia's pretenses and, through her, condemn all women and the institution of marriage, while delivering a veiled death threat to Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To purge the corruption in Denmark and fulfill the Ghost's command, even at the cost of destroying what he loves
beat_states.obstacle: His own anguish over Ophelia, the awareness of being watched, and the impossibility of trusting anyone in this poisoned court

affect_state.rationale: Escalating fury that borders on losing control. The speech accelerates from accusation to cosmic condemnation to a direct threat ('all but one shall live'). The repetition of 'nunnery' suggests compulsive return to a wound. Arousal is extremely high; control is slipping as genuine pain bleeds through the performance.

social_state.rationale: He assumes absolute moral authority, positioning himself as judge over Ophelia, all women, and marriage itself. Warmth is near its lowest — he is deliberately cruel, weaponizing intimacy. Yet the hidden threat to Claudius ('all but one') reasserts princely power even in apparent madness.

known_facts: He suspects or knows he is being observed by Claudius and Polonius
Ophelia has been positioned as bait to test him
His mother remarried with indecent haste to his father's murderer
Claudius is guilty of regicide
hidden_secrets: The threat 'all but one shall live' is a deliberate signal aimed at Claudius
His 'madness' is at least partly performed for the hidden audience
He still has feelings for Ophelia beneath the cruelty
false_beliefs: That Ophelia is willingly complicit in the plot against him rather than being manipulated by her father
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet operates on multiple epistemic levels simultaneously — performing for eavesdroppers, punishing Ophelia for perceived betrayal, and embedding a death threat to Claudius within what appears to be a madman's rant. His conflation of Ophelia with Gertrude's betrayal may constitute a false belief about Ophelia's agency.

beat_states.defense_state: Projection — displacing his rage at his mother's sexuality and Claudius's corruption onto Ophelia and all women, using misogynistic generalization to avoid confronting his specific grief and love
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He claims women's deception has 'made me mad,' simultaneously performing madness strategically and experiencing genuine emotional disintegration — he cannot fully distinguish his act from his reality
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may have fully surrendered to genuine rage here rather than performing strategically — the threat to Claudius could be an uncontrolled slip rather than a calculated warning, suggesting his madness is becoming less feigned than he intends.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: O, what a noble mind is here o’erthrown! / The courtier’s, soldier’s, scholar’s, eye, tongue, sword, / Th’ expectancy and rose of the fair state, / The glass of fashion and the mold of form, / Th’ observed of all observers, quite, quite down! / And I, of ladies most deject and wretched, / That sucked the honey of his musicked vows, / Now see that noble and most sovereign reason, / Like sweet bells jangled, out of time and harsh; / That unmatched form and stature of blown youth / Blasted with ecstasy. O, woe is me / T’ have seen what I have seen, see what I see!

beat_states.desire_state: to mourn and comprehend the destruction of the man she loved and her own devastation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be loved and valued by Hamlet, to fulfill her role as dutiful daughter and beloved
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has just savagely rejected her, and the idealized prince she loved appears to be irrevocably lost to madness

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is in acute grief and shock. Her certainty is moderate — she is now convinced Hamlet is mad, which gives her a framework, but she has lost all control over her situation. Arousal is high but shaped into lamentation rather than panic. She is maximally vulnerable, stripped of love, hope, and agency simultaneously.

social_state.rationale: She explicitly names herself 'of ladies most deject and wretched' — her self-perceived status has collapsed entirely. Yet her warmth toward Hamlet remains palpable; this is a love-elegy, not a denunciation. She mourns FOR him even as she mourns what she has lost.

known_facts: Hamlet has just violently rejected her and denied ever loving her
Hamlet commanded her to a nunnery and attacked all women
She returned his remembrances as instructed by her father
Hamlet was once the ideal courtier, soldier, and scholar
She once received and believed his vows of love
hidden_secrets: Her father and Claudius are hidden nearby listening to everything
She was deployed as a tool in their scheme to test Hamlet
false_beliefs: Hamlet's behavior is genuine madness ('ecstasy') rather than potentially strategic performance
That her own complicity in the eavesdropping trap played no role in provoking Hamlet's fury
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia interprets everything through the lens of Hamlet's madness — 'blown youth blasted with ecstasy.' She does not consider that Hamlet may have detected the surveillance, nor does she fully reckon with her own role as instrument of her father's plot. Her grief is sincere but built partly on incomplete understanding.

beat_states.defense_state: sublimation — channeling overwhelming grief into eloquent, structured eulogy, converting unbearable personal pain into a public-facing elegy for Denmark's lost hope
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She mourns Hamlet's madness as genuine yet has just witnessed behavior that could be calculated cruelty — she cannot reconcile the noble mind she catalogues with the man who just told her to go to a nunnery
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A minority reading suggests Ophelia is partly performing grief for the hidden audience of Claudius and Polonius, aware they are watching, but the weight of the text and the soliloquy form strongly suggest authentic, unguarded devastation.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Love? His affections do not that way tend; / Nor what he spake, though it lacked form a little, / Was not like madness. There’s something in his soul / O’er which his melancholy sits on brood, / And I do doubt the hatch and the disclose / Will be some danger; which for to prevent, / I have in quick determination / Thus set it down: he shall with speed to England / For the demand of our neglected tribute. / Haply the seas, and countries different, / With variable objects, shall expel / This something-settled matter in his heart, / Whereon his brains still beating puts him thus / From fashion of himself. What think you on ’t?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: It shall do well. But yet do I believe / The origin and commencement of his grief / Sprung from neglected love.— How now, Ophelia? / You need not tell us what Lord Hamlet said; / We heard it all.— My lord, do as you please, / But, if you hold it fit, after the play / Let his queen-mother all alone entreat him / To show his grief. Let her be round with him; / And I’ll be placed, so please you, in the ear / Of all their conference. If she find him not, / To England send him, or confine him where / Your wisdom best shall think.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: It shall be so. / Madness in great ones must not unwatched go.

beat_states.desire_state: To neutralize Hamlet as a threat by sending him to England under a plausible pretext
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his crown and power, keeping the secret of his fratricide buried
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's unpredictable behavior and unclear intentions make him a volatile danger that Claudius cannot fully read or control

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is alarmed — he saw through the nunnery scene and recognized Hamlet is not lovesick but dangerous. He feels urgency but also relief at having a plan. His certainty is high that this is not madness or love, but something threatening. Control is moderate — he can act but cannot fully predict Hamlet.

social_state.rationale: Claudius exercises full royal authority, issuing decisive commands. His warmth is cold — he shows no concern for Ophelia's devastation and treats Hamlet instrumentally as a threat to be managed.

known_facts: Hamlet's behavior in the nunnery scene was not driven by love for Ophelia
Hamlet's speech, though disordered, was not true madness
Something dangerous is brooding in Hamlet's mind
He overheard the entire exchange between Hamlet and Ophelia
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet and fears Hamlet knows or suspects
The England trip may serve as more than therapy — it removes a direct threat to his life and throne
false_beliefs: He may underestimate how much Hamlet already knows about the murder
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has gained critical intelligence: Hamlet is not lovesick, and his 'madness' conceals purposeful, dangerous intent. But Claudius does not yet know Hamlet has specific proof or a plan — he senses the danger without knowing its full shape.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — reframing his fear-driven decision to exile Hamlet as concern for Hamlet's mental health and state affairs
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He dismisses love as the cause with sharp certainty, revealing he perceived real menace in Hamlet's words, yet must publicly frame his response as therapeutic rather than self-protective

beat_states.desire_state: To preserve his theory that neglected love caused Hamlet's condition, and to remain indispensable as the King's chief advisor
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his family's position at court through proximity to royal power and being the one who 'solves' problems for the King
beat_states.obstacle: The King has just flatly rejected his love theory, undermining his advisory credibility; he must find a way to stay relevant without directly contradicting the King

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is somewhat deflated — his grand theory has been overruled — but he recovers quickly by proposing another spying scheme. His certainty has been shaken but not destroyed. He feels diminished control but compensates through busybody initiative.

social_state.rationale: His status is subordinate to Claudius here; he defers but negotiates space for his ideas. His warmth toward Ophelia is perfunctory at best — a brief acknowledgment before pivoting back to scheming with the King.

known_facts: He heard everything Hamlet said to Ophelia
The King rejects the love theory
Hamlet spoke harshly and erratically to Ophelia
Hamlet told Ophelia to go to a nunnery and attacked marriage
hidden_secrets: He used his own daughter as bait and witnessed her emotional devastation without intervening
false_beliefs: He still believes neglected love is the origin of Hamlet's disturbance
He believes another eavesdropping scheme (with Gertrude) will finally prove his theory
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius cannot let go of his diagnosis. Despite direct evidence that Hamlet's behavior was more hostile and political than lovesick, Polonius doubles down, partly from intellectual vanity and partly because abandoning the theory would mean admitting he sacrificed Ophelia's wellbeing for nothing.

beat_states.defense_state: Hedging — he concedes to the King's plan while persistently inserting his own alternative scheme (the Gertrude closet scene) as a fallback, ensuring he remains in the loop
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He still clings to his love hypothesis even after the King dismissed it, revealing intellectual stubbornness masked as deference; he also dismisses Ophelia's pain ('You need not tell us') while ostensibly showing paternal acknowledgment
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius's insistence on the love theory could also be read as genuine fatherly hope — if love is the cause, Ophelia is important and his family is central to the solution, which is less frightening than Hamlet being genuinely dangerous.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Speak the speech, I pray you, as I pronounced it to you, trippingly on the tongue; but if you mouth it, as many of our players do, I had as lief the town-crier spoke my lines. Nor do not saw the air too much with your hand, thus, but use all gently; for in the very torrent, tempest, and, as I may say, whirlwind of your passion, you must acquire and beget a temperance that may give it smoothness. O, it offends me to the soul to hear a robustious, periwig-pated fellow tear a passion to tatters, to very rags, to split the ears of the groundlings, who for the most part are capable of nothing but inexplicable dumb shows and noise. I would have such a fellow whipped for o’erdoing Termagant. It out-Herods Herod. Pray you, avoid it.

utterances.speaker: PLAYER
utterances.text: I warrant your Honor.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Be not too tame neither, but let your own discretion be your tutor. Suit the action to the word, the word to the action, with this special observance, that you o’erstep not the modesty of nature. For anything so o’erdone is from the purpose of playing, whose end, both at the first and now, was and is to hold, as ’twere, the mirror up to nature, to show virtue her own feature, scorn her own image, and the very age and body of the time his form and pressure. Now this overdone or come tardy off, though it makes the unskillful laugh, cannot but make the judicious grieve, the censure of the which one must in your allowance o’erweigh a whole theater of others. O, there be players that I have seen play and heard others praise ( and that highly), not to speak it profanely, that, neither having th’ accent of Christians nor the gait of Christian, pagan, nor man, have so strutted and bellowed that I have thought some of nature’s journeymen had made men, and not made them well, they imitated humanity so abominably.

utterances.speaker: PLAYER
utterances.text: I hope we have reformed that indifferently with us, sir.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O, reform it altogether. And let those that play your clowns speak no more than is set down for them, for there be of them that will themselves laugh, to set on some quantity of barren spectators to laugh too, though in the meantime some necessary question of the play be then to be considered. That’s villainous and shows a most pitiful ambition in the fool that uses it. Go make you ready. / How now, my lord, will the King hear this piece of work?

beat_states.desire_state: To ensure the players perform the inserted speech with naturalistic precision so it will effectively expose Claudius's guilt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To confirm Claudius's guilt and fulfill his duty to avenge his father's murder
beat_states.obstacle: The players may overact or deviate from his instructions, ruining the trap he has set for the King

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and focused, experiencing a rare moment of agency and purpose. His arousal is elevated as the crucial test approaches. He feels relatively in control because he is directing others and executing his plan, but underneath lies vulnerability — everything depends on this performance succeeding.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet asserts clear authority as a prince directing hired players, speaking from a position of unquestioned social dominance. His warmth is low — he is demanding, critical, even threatening ('I would have such a fellow whipped'), though not hostile. He treats the Player as a craftsman receiving orders.

known_facts: The Ghost claimed Claudius murdered King Hamlet
He has inserted a speech into the play designed to mirror Claudius's crime
The play performance is imminent
He plans to observe Claudius's reaction as proof of guilt
hidden_secrets: The true purpose of the inserted speech is to catch Claudius's conscience
He suspects Claudius of murder but has not yet confirmed it
His 'antic disposition' is a deliberate strategy
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with a clear strategic plan. He knows what he needs but conceals from the players why this particular performance matters so much. His obsessive perfectionism about the acting reveals how high the stakes are for him.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — channeling his existential anxiety into an elaborate aesthetic lecture, displacing his terror about the upcoming confrontation into controllable theatrical principles
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He lectures on restraint and temperance while himself being in a state of barely contained emotional turbulence; he demands naturalness from actors while orchestrating an elaborate deception
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's lengthy digression on acting theory could also represent procrastination — another delay tactic, losing himself in intellectual discourse to avoid confronting the terrifying moment of truth

beat_states.desire_state: To satisfy Hamlet's exacting demands and secure the prince's approval and patronage
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his livelihood and professional reputation as a player
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's instructions are extensive, contradictory in places (not too much, not too little), and delivered with intimidating intensity from a social superior

affect_state.rationale: Mildly negative valence — being lectured at length is not pleasant. Moderate arousal from the pressure of an imminent performance with high-stakes scrutiny. Low control as he is entirely subordinate to Hamlet's vision. Vulnerable because his professional competence is being questioned.

social_state.rationale: Clearly subordinate, addressed as a servant-craftsman. His brief, deferential responses ('I warrant your Honor,' 'I hope we have reformed that indifferently with us, sir') confirm his low status position. He maintains warmth through professional courtesy.

known_facts: A prince has commissioned a specific speech to be inserted into their play
The play will be performed before the King and court
Hamlet has unusually specific and passionate requirements for the performance
false_beliefs: This is likely just a prince's theatrical vanity or entertainment preference
epistemic_state.rationale: The Player has no reason to suspect the play is a trap. He likely reads Hamlet's intensity as aristocratic connoisseurship rather than desperate strategic planning.

beat_states.defense_state: Compliance — absorbing criticism without protest to maintain professional standing
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He likely feels professional pride being wounded by Hamlet's patronizing lecture, yet must defer graciously to a prince who is also his employer
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Player may sense something unusual in Hamlet's obsessive specificity and suspect there is more at stake than a simple court entertainment

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: And the Queen too, and that presently.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Bid the players make haste. / Will you two help to hasten them?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Ay, my lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To get Rosencrantz and Guildenstern out of the room so he can prepare for the play-within-the-play with Horatio alone
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To confirm Claudius's guilt and act on the truth of the ghost's revelation
beat_states.obstacle: The constant surveillance by Rosencrantz, Guildenstern, and Polonius; the need to maintain his antic disposition while executing a precise plan

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and focused — the trap is nearly set. He feels a rare sense of agency and control as he orchestrates events, though underneath runs the tension of what the play's outcome may confirm.

social_state.rationale: He exercises princely authority, issuing commands efficiently. His warmth toward Rosencrantz and Guildenstern is cold — he knows they are Claudius's instruments and uses them as errand-runners.

known_facts: The play is about to be performed before the King and Queen
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are spies for Claudius
Polonius is a meddling tool of the King
The play-within-the-play is his device to catch the King's conscience
hidden_secrets: The Murder of Gonzago has been altered to mirror Claudius's crime
He plans to watch Claudius's reaction with Horatio as witness
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with full strategic awareness in this moment, clearing the room of spies before his private conference with Horatio.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing brisk efficiency to mask the intensity of his anticipation and anxiety about the mousetrap
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He commands with princely authority while being fundamentally powerless in the court; he sends away his supposed friends whom he no longer trusts

beat_states.desire_state: To demonstrate his usefulness and insider knowledge by announcing the King and Queen's imminent arrival
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position of influence and proximity to power through service and information-brokering
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's dismissive attitude toward him; his role is essentially that of a messenger here

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is mildly pleased to deliver news and be in the center of activity, comfortable in his role as court facilitator, unaware of any danger.

social_state.rationale: He occupies a subordinate-but-important messenger role, offering information in a servile-yet-self-important manner typical of his character.

known_facts: The King and Queen are coming to watch the play presently
Hamlet has been behaving erratically
He believes Hamlet's madness stems from love for Ophelia
hidden_secrets: He has arranged with Claudius to spy on Hamlet
false_beliefs: Hamlet's antic disposition is genuine madness caused by rejected love
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius has no inkling of the trap Hamlet has set. He thinks this is simply entertainment for the court.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing eager efficiency to justify his presence and importance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He believes he is a shrewd political operator but is functioning as a simple errand-runner, unaware of how Hamlet manipulates everyone around him

beat_states.desire_state: To comply with Hamlet's request and maintain the appearance of loyal friendship
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the King's commission to spy on Hamlet while preserving the relationship with the Prince for personal advancement
beat_states.obstacle: The dual allegiance — serving Claudius while pretending to serve Hamlet — creates a constant tension of performance

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is in a reactive, subordinate position — compliant but somewhat anxious. He has little control over events and must simply follow orders from multiple masters.

social_state.rationale: Clearly subordinate, responding to a command with immediate acquiescence. The warmth is performed rather than felt — a veneer of friendship over duty.

known_facts: Claudius has tasked him with observing Hamlet
Hamlet is behaving strangely
A play is about to be performed
hidden_secrets: He is reporting Hamlet's behavior to Claudius
false_beliefs: He may believe Hamlet still trusts him as a genuine friend
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz has no awareness of the play's true purpose or that Hamlet has already identified him as a spy ('you were sent for').

beat_states.defense_state: Obsequious compliance masks his surveillance role
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs the obedient friend while actually serving as Claudius's agent; his 'Ay, my lord' is both genuine deference and calculated ingratiation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may be genuinely trying to be helpful here with no active duplicity in this specific moment — the errand is innocuous.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What ho, Horatio!

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Here, sweet lord, at your service.

beat_states.desire_state: To summon Horatio quickly so they can finalize the plan to observe Claudius during the play
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To confirm the truth of the Ghost's accusation and avenge his father's murder
beat_states.obstacle: Time pressure — the court is arriving imminently and Hamlet needs his most trusted ally positioned and ready

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is animated and purposeful — the play-within-the-play is his trap and he's moving pieces into position. There's anticipatory energy but also underlying anxiety about what the night will reveal.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet calls out with princely authority ('What ho') but this is directed at Horatio, the one person with whom he drops courtly pretense and allows genuine warmth.

known_facts: The play is about to be performed before the court
Horatio is nearby and loyal
The play has been modified to mirror Claudius's alleged crime
He needs a second pair of eyes on Claudius's reaction
hidden_secrets: He has designed the play as a trap to catch Claudius's conscience
He suspects Claudius murdered his father based on the Ghost's testimony
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with a clear strategic plan but still lacks confirmation of Claudius's guilt — hence the need for Horatio as a corroborating witness.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Outwardly commanding and energized, but inwardly dependent on Horatio's validation to trust his own perception

beat_states.desire_state: To present himself as fully available and loyal to Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve and protect Hamlet as his most faithful friend and moral witness
beat_states.obstacle: He does not yet know exactly what Hamlet needs from him in this moment

affect_state.rationale: Horatio responds with calm, steady warmth. 'Sweet lord' signals genuine affection, not obsequiousness. His arousal is moderate — he senses something is afoot but remains characteristically composed.

social_state.rationale: Horatio places himself below Hamlet in station ('at your service') but the term 'sweet lord' conveys deep personal affection that transcends rank — this is friendship offered through the form of deference.

known_facts: Hamlet has been in a disturbed state since the Ghost's appearance
He witnessed the Ghost on the battlements
A play is about to be performed at court
Hamlet has been behaving erratically
hidden_secrets: He has sworn an oath to Hamlet to keep the Ghost encounter secret
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio knows about the Ghost but may not yet fully understand Hamlet's specific plan for the play — Hamlet is about to reveal that to him.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His humble self-effacement ('at your service') masks someone of sharp intellect and independent judgment whom Hamlet values precisely for those qualities
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may already suspect Hamlet has a specific purpose for the play and is preparing himself to be recruited into it

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Horatio, thou art e’en as just a man / As e’er my conversation coped withal.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: O, my dear lord—

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Nay, do not think I flatter, / For what advancement may I hope from thee / That no revenue hast but thy good spirits / To feed and clothe thee? Why should the poor be flattered? / No, let the candied tongue lick absurd pomp / And crook the pregnant hinges of the knee / Where thrift may follow fawning. Dost thou hear? / Since my dear soul was mistress of her choice / And could of men distinguish, her election / Hath sealed thee for herself. For thou hast been / As one in suffering all that suffers nothing, / A man that Fortune’s buffets and rewards / Hast ta’en with equal thanks; and blessed are those / Whose blood and judgment are so well commeddled / That they are not a pipe for Fortune’s finger / To sound what stop she please. Give me that man / That is not passion’s slave, and I will wear him / In my heart’s core, ay, in my heart of heart, / As I do thee.— Something too much of this.— / There is a play tonight before the King. / One scene of it comes near the circumstance / Which I have told thee of my father’s death. / I prithee, when thou seest that act afoot, / Even with the very comment of thy soul / Observe my uncle. If his occulted guilt / Do not itself unkennel in one speech, / It is a damnèd ghost that we have seen, / And my imaginations are as foul / As Vulcan’s stithy. Give him heedful note, / For I mine eyes will rivet to his face, / And, after, we will both our judgments join / In censure of his seeming.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure Horatio as a trusted ally and co-conspirator for the mousetrap plan, binding him through genuine affection and shared purpose
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To confirm the truth of his father's murder and hold the guilty accountable — he needs a reliable witness whose judgment he can trust absolutely
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet cannot trust anyone at court; he needs Horatio to accept the gravity of the task without Horatio deflecting through modesty; Hamlet also struggles with his own emotional vulnerability in expressing love

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a rare moment of genuine warmth and admiration, which raises his valence, but the stakes of the coming play and the uncertainty about the ghost's honesty keep certainty low. He is more exposed here than almost anywhere else in the play — praising someone sincerely is an act of radical openness for him. His self-interruption reveals he feels the vulnerability acutely.

social_state.rationale: As prince, Hamlet maintains higher social status, but he deliberately lowers the status gap by elevating Horatio's character above wealth and rank. This is perhaps the warmest Hamlet is with anyone in the entire play — he is genuinely loving, not performing.

known_facts: The ghost has accused Claudius of murder
A play tonight contains a scene mirroring the alleged murder
Horatio is trustworthy, stoic, and not a flatterer
Horatio knows about the ghost's revelation
hidden_secrets: His antic disposition is a strategy — he is sane and calculating
He is using the play as a deliberate trap for Claudius's conscience
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating from a position of incomplete knowledge — he still doesn't know for certain if the ghost told the truth. The entire mousetrap plan is designed to close this epistemic gap. He shares the plan openly with Horatio, making Horatio the only person fully in his confidence.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — he cuts off his own emotional exposure ('Something too much of this') and pivots immediately to the practical plan, retreating from vulnerability into strategy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He craves deep emotional connection and openly praises Horatio's stoic equanimity — the very quality Hamlet himself desperately lacks — while simultaneously being unable to sustain the emotional openness, snapping back to tactical mode
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Hamlet's praise of Horatio's stoicism contains self-reproach — that he is indirectly lamenting his own inability to be 'not passion's slave' — making this less a recruitment and more a confession of inadequacy disguised as compliment

beat_states.desire_state: To respond appropriately to Hamlet's overwhelming and unexpected praise without diminishing it or appearing falsely modest
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To faithfully serve and protect Hamlet, his prince and dear friend, through loyal companionship and honest counsel
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's intensity and the asymmetry of their social positions make it nearly impossible for Horatio to respond — Hamlet literally silences his attempt to deflect ('Nay, do not think I flatter')

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is touched and perhaps embarrassed by the depth of Hamlet's praise. His arousal is moderate — he is moved but characteristically contained. He has little control over this interaction; Hamlet dominates the exchange completely. His vulnerability is real but suppressed.

social_state.rationale: Horatio occupies a lower social position (poor scholar, no revenue) and his single interrupted line confirms his deference. Yet the warmth is high — 'my dear lord' is genuine affection, not mere courtly formula.

known_facts: He saw the ghost on the battlements
Hamlet has told him about Claudius's alleged murder of King Hamlet
A play is being performed tonight before the King
Hamlet wants him to observe Claudius during a specific scene
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is fully briefed by Hamlet and serves as the one person at court with near-complete knowledge of Hamlet's plans. He holds no secrets of his own in this beat and has no reason for false beliefs.

beat_states.defense_state: Modesty — his 'O, my dear lord—' is an instinctive attempt to ward off the praise, a habitual self-effacement that Hamlet immediately overrides
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is deeply moved by Hamlet's love but trained by both temperament and social position to minimize himself; the stoic composure Hamlet praises may itself be a form of emotional suppression
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's interrupted 'O, my dear lord—' could be read not as modest deflection but as genuine alarm at Hamlet's emotional state — sensing that Hamlet's effusive praise signals dangerous psychological instability before a critical moment

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Well, my lord. / If he steal aught the whilst this play is playing / And ’scape detecting, I will pay the theft.

beat_states.desire_state: to assure Hamlet of his total commitment to watching Claudius during the play
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve Hamlet faithfully as his most trusted friend and moral anchor
beat_states.obstacle: the difficulty of the task — detecting a fleeting moment of guilt on Claudius's face amid the chaos of a court performance

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is steady and composed — his signature quality. He feels the gravity of Hamlet's request and the honor of being trusted, but channels it into quiet resolve rather than emotional display. High control and certainty reflect his Stoic temperament.

social_state.rationale: Horatio willingly occupies a subordinate position ('Well, my lord'), deferring to Hamlet's authority and plan, but his warmth is evident in the totality of his commitment — he stakes his own reputation ('I will pay the theft') as a gesture of loyal solidarity.

known_facts: Hamlet believes the Ghost's account of murder by Claudius
The play has been modified to mirror the alleged murder
His specific task is to observe Claudius's reaction during the poisoning scene
Hamlet's emotional state is volatile and the stakes are life-and-death
hidden_secrets: He may harbor private doubts about whether the Ghost's account is true, but suppresses them out of loyalty
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio has been fully briefed by Hamlet. He understands the plan clearly. His epistemic position is strong — he knows what to look for and why — though he has not yet had independent confirmation of Claudius's guilt.

beat_states.defense_state: understatement — wrapping his solemn promise in modest, almost wry language to deflect the emotional weight of Hamlet's intense trust
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He accepts a mission whose stakes are enormous (confirming regicide) while adopting a tone of calm, almost casual assurance, as if it were a simple wager
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's brevity and wager-like phrasing could indicate a slight skepticism about the plan's reliability — he may be humoring Hamlet's obsession while reserving private judgment until he sees evidence with his own eyes.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: They are coming to the play. I must be idle. Get you a place.

beat_states.desire_state: To transition into his antic disposition before the court arrives, and to position Horatio as his covert observer
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To confirm Claudius's guilt and fulfill his obligation to avenge his father's murder
beat_states.obstacle: He must conceal his true purpose behind a mask of madness while the entire court watches both the play and him

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is keyed up — the trap is set, the moment is arriving. There's a thrill of anticipation mixed with anxiety. He asserts control by consciously choosing to shift into his 'idle' persona, but the stakes are enormous and the outcome uncertain.

social_state.rationale: Brief, commanding tone with Horatio — the warmth of their previous intimate exchange is now compressed into operational efficiency. He assumes the role of director/strategist giving final instructions before curtain.

known_facts: The court is approaching imminently
The play has been modified to mirror Claudius's alleged crime
Horatio has been briefed to watch Claudius's reactions
He must adopt his antic disposition to deflect suspicion
hidden_secrets: The Mousetrap is designed to catch the conscience of the King
He suspects Claudius murdered his father based on the Ghost's testimony
He and Horatio are conspiring to observe Claudius during the play
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with maximum strategic awareness. He knows exactly what's about to happen and what role he must play. No false beliefs are active — this is his most clear-headed, tactical moment.

beat_states.defense_state: Donning the mask of madness ('be idle') as protective camouflage — preemptive dissimulation before the court enters
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Intense strategic focus and nervous anticipation coexisting with the need to appear carelessly mad and distracted
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: 'I must be idle' could reflect genuine psychic exhaustion — a moment where the performance of madness is also a release valve for unbearable tension, not purely strategic but partly involuntary dissociation.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: How fares our cousin Hamlet?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Excellent, i’ faith, of the chameleon’s dish. I eat the air, promise-crammed. You cannot feed capons so.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: I have nothing with this answer, Hamlet. These words are not mine.

beat_states.desire_state: To needle Claudius with veiled accusations about stolen promises and the crown, while maintaining the mask of madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose and punish Claudius for his father's murder
beat_states.obstacle: He must attack obliquely — he cannot yet openly accuse the King; he must maintain his 'antic disposition'

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and relishing the verbal sparring before the play-within-a-play. He feels in control of the exchange, enjoying the double meanings. Arousal is high because the mousetrap is about to spring. Some uncertainty remains — the play hasn't yet confirmed Claudius's guilt.

social_state.rationale: Formally subordinate to the King but actively subverting that hierarchy through aggressive wordplay. The pun on 'capons' (castrated roosters, implying Hamlet is being fattened with empty promises) is hostile beneath its comic surface.

known_facts: The Ghost accused Claudius of murder
The play-within-a-play is about to test Claudius's guilt
Horatio is watching Claudius for reactions
Claudius promised him the succession ('promise-crammed')
hidden_secrets: He has arranged The Mousetrap to mirror the murder
He suspects Claudius killed his father
His madness is performed, not real
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's pun on 'heir/air' reveals his awareness that Claudius's promises about the succession are empty. He knows more than he reveals.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflects through wordplay and feigned madness — the 'chameleon's dish' pun allows him to say dangerous truths while appearing nonsensical
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is performing madness while executing a precise, calculated verbal assault; wild on the surface, razor-sharp underneath
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may also be testing whether Claudius can decode his wordplay — gauging how nervous or alert the King already is before the play begins

beat_states.desire_state: To gauge Hamlet's mental state and maintain courtly normalcy before the assembled court
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne, marriage, and legitimacy against any threat Hamlet poses
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's answer is deliberately opaque and hostile; Claudius cannot decode or control the conversation

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is unsettled. He initiated the exchange to project kingly ease but Hamlet's cryptic hostility leaves him wrong-footed. He senses danger in Hamlet's words but cannot quite pin it down. His control is slipping — he has to publicly admit he doesn't understand.

social_state.rationale: Opens with performative warmth as king-uncle but quickly drops the pretense. He maintains formal status but the exchange reveals cracks — admitting 'I have nothing with this answer' is a small public concession of powerlessness.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving erratically
Hamlet's madness may or may not be real
A play is about to be performed at Hamlet's request
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He is anxious about what Hamlet may know or suspect
false_beliefs: Hamlet's strange behavior may be caused by love for Ophelia
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius does not yet grasp the full danger of Hamlet's wordplay or the trap being set with the play. He half-senses the hostility in 'promise-crammed' and 'capons' but cannot fully decode it.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflects Hamlet's verbal aggression by declaring the words incomprehensible — refusing to engage with the dangerous subtext
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He opens with warmth ('cousin Hamlet') to perform familial concern, but his closing line ('These words are not mine') betrays irritation and a desire to shut Hamlet down
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may understand more of the subtext than he lets on — his dismissal ('These words are not mine') could be a deliberate refusal to engage rather than genuine incomprehension, a tactic to deny Hamlet the satisfaction of landing a blow

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: No, nor mine now. To Polonius. My lord, you played once i’ th’ university, you say?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: That did I, my lord, and was accounted a good actor.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What did you enact?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: I did enact Julius Caesar. I was killed i’ th’ Capitol. Brutus killed me.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: It was a brute part of him to kill so capital a calf there.— Be the players ready?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Ay, my lord. They stay upon your patience.

beat_states.desire_state: To toy with Polonius while maintaining his antic disposition, and to check that the players are ready for the trap
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose the truth of his father's murder and hold the guilty accountable
beat_states.obstacle: He must wait for the play to begin and maintain his manic facade without revealing his true purpose

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of charged, almost manic excitement before the play-within-the-play. He feels in control of the situation, enjoying his verbal dominance over Polonius, but underneath is the high-stakes tension of what is about to unfold.

social_state.rationale: He is prince and patron of the entertainment; he condescends to Polonius with surface friendliness that barely conceals contempt. The 'capital calf' insult is thinly veiled mockery.

known_facts: The play is about to begin with the Murder of Gonzago scene designed to test Claudius
Horatio is watching Claudius for signs of guilt
Polonius once acted Julius Caesar and was killed by Brutus
Polonius is a tool of the King, spying on him
hidden_secrets: The play has been altered to mirror Claudius's murder of King Hamlet
He and Horatio are using the play as a trap to confirm the Ghost's accusation
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with maximum strategic awareness. His reference to Caesar's assassination contains dark dramatic irony — he will himself later kill Polonius, the man who played Caesar.

beat_states.defense_state: Antic disposition — using wordplay and apparent madness to disguise his lethal intent
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Gleefully joking about assassination (Brutus killing Caesar) while planning to use the play to catch a real murderer; the pun on 'brute/Brutus' and 'capital/Capitol' and 'calf' simultaneously entertains him and foreshadows his awareness that he may need to kill Polonius's master
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Caesar/Brutus exchange could be read as Hamlet unconsciously foreshadowing his own killing of Polonius rather than a deliberate coded threat

beat_states.desire_state: To ingratiate himself with Hamlet and appear cultured and relevant
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position of influence at court by managing information and relationships
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's erratic behavior makes it hard to know if he's being genuinely engaged or mocked

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is pleased to be asked about his university days and to boast of his acting skills. He's relatively relaxed, unaware he's being baited. His vulnerability is moderate because he doesn't realize he's the butt of Hamlet's joke.

social_state.rationale: He is subordinate to the prince but enjoys the attention. He is warm and eager to please, happy to reminisce about his theatrical past.

known_facts: He once acted Julius Caesar in a university production
Hamlet appears to be engaging him in friendly conversation
He has been tasked by the King to observe Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He is helping Claudius spy on Hamlet
He has used Ophelia as bait to test Hamlet's madness
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is caused by love for Ophelia
Hamlet's interest in his acting is genuine and benign
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius is completely blind to the layers of irony in the exchange. He doesn't register 'calf' as an insult directed at him, nor does he sense the ominous foreshadowing in the Caesar/Brutus parallel.

beat_states.defense_state: Obliviousness — Polonius fails to register the insult embedded in 'capital calf,' taking the conversation at face value
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is proud of his acting past yet completely fails to see that he is currently performing the role of unwitting fool in Hamlet's drama
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius might partially sense Hamlet's mockery but chooses to ignore it to avoid confrontation

beat_states.desire_state: To fulfill his function as intermediary and report that the players are ready
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the King's interests by staying close to Hamlet and gathering intelligence while maintaining Hamlet's trust
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's unpredictable behavior and the ambiguity of his own position between King and Prince

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is cautious, delivering a simple factual update. He is neither particularly happy nor distressed, but maintains a wary neutrality. He has low control — he is at Hamlet's mercy for how the interaction goes.

social_state.rationale: Clearly subordinate, performing a messenger's function. His warmth is mildly positive but perfunctory — he uses polite deference ('They stay upon your patience') to maintain a safe distance.

known_facts: The players are ready to perform
Hamlet has arranged for a play to be performed tonight
The King and Queen are in attendance
hidden_secrets: He and Guildenstern have been commissioned by Claudius to spy on Hamlet
false_beliefs: He may still believe Hamlet doesn't fully know about his commission from the King
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is operating with limited understanding of the larger stakes. He doesn't know about the trap within the play and is focused on his logistical role.

beat_states.defense_state: Servility — hiding behind functional courtesy to avoid drawing Hamlet's sharp attention
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presents himself as Hamlet's obedient servant while actually serving Claudius's surveillance agenda
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may be more anxious than he appears, sensing the charged atmosphere and Hamlet's barely contained intensity

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Come hither, my dear Hamlet, sit by me.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: No, good mother. Here’s metal more attractive.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Oh, ho! Do you mark that?

beat_states.desire_state: To position himself near Ophelia so he can observe the King's reaction to the play while also publicly snubbing his mother
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To confirm Claudius's guilt and avenge his father's murder
beat_states.obstacle: His mother's public invitation creates social pressure to comply; he must refuse without fully revealing his strategic purpose

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and purposeful — the mousetrap is about to spring. He feels in command of the situation, using his 'antic disposition' to maneuver freely. The slight negative valence reflects his contempt for his mother's perceived betrayal.

social_state.rationale: He publicly demotes his mother's claim on him while elevating Ophelia as 'more attractive,' a deliberate social provocation that inverts expected filial deference

known_facts: The play-within-the-play will mirror Claudius's crime
He needs to watch Claudius's face closely
Positioning near Ophelia gives him both a vantage point and a cover for his antic disposition
Horatio is also watching the King
hidden_secrets: The entire play is a trap for Claudius
His madness is performed
He knows the Ghost's accusation
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating at peak strategic awareness in this beat — every move is calculated to serve the mousetrap scheme

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through wit — 'metal more attractive' uses wordplay to disguise the cruelty of the rejection and his strategic positioning
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Punishing his mother publicly while needing to maintain the appearance of mere madness rather than purposeful hostility
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A secondary reading suggests Hamlet's choice of Ophelia over Gertrude is an unconscious Oedipal displacement — punishing his mother by giving his attention to the younger woman

beat_states.desire_state: To observe Hamlet's behavior during the play and report useful intelligence to the King, maintaining his role as chief advisor
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To prove his political acumen and maintain his position as the King's chief advisor by solving the mystery of Hamlet's behavior
beat_states.obstacle: He can only observe, not control, Hamlet's behavior; he must interpret ambiguous evidence

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is excited and self-satisfied — 'Oh, ho!' is the sound of a man who believes he's just been vindicated. High certainty because the evidence seems to fit perfectly. Low vulnerability because he feels safely positioned as an observer.

social_state.rationale: He addresses the King conspiratorially, positioning himself as the insightful interpreter of Hamlet's behavior, slightly cold as he treats Hamlet as a specimen to be analyzed

known_facts: Hamlet rejected the Queen's invitation
Hamlet chose to sit near Ophelia instead
He previously intercepted Hamlet's love letters to Ophelia
hidden_secrets: He orchestrated the nunnery encounter to spy on Hamlet
He has been using Ophelia as a pawn
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is caused by unrequited love for Ophelia
Hamlet's choice to sit by Ophelia confirms this diagnosis
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius reads Hamlet's behavior through the single lens of his lovesickness theory, completely missing the strategic calculation behind Hamlet's every move

beat_states.defense_state: Confirmation bias — seizing on any evidence that supports his pre-existing theory
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His delight at being 'proven right' blinds him to the possibility that Hamlet is manipulating everyone, including him

beat_states.desire_state: To draw Hamlet close to her, to reconnect with her son and restore normalcy before the play
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain family harmony and keep Hamlet content within the court
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's erratic behavior and emotional distance from her since her remarriage

affect_state.rationale: She extends an invitation warmly but is uncertain whether Hamlet will accept; the public setting makes rejection particularly exposing

social_state.rationale: As Queen she commands high status, and her invitation is genuinely warm and maternal, a public gesture of affection

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving strangely
The court is watching
A play is about to be performed
hidden_secrets: The speed and nature of her remarriage may trouble her conscience
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is primarily caused by lovesickness for Ophelia
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude operates with incomplete understanding of Hamlet's true grievances and the depth of his resentment toward her

beat_states.defense_state: denial — treating Hamlet as though nothing is wrong, performing maternal warmth publicly
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuinely wants closeness with her son but may also be performing the role of attentive mother for the court's benefit

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Lady, shall I lie in your lap?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: No, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I mean, my head upon your lap?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Ay, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Do you think I meant country matters?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: I think nothing, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: That’s a fair thought to lie between maids’ legs.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: What is, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Nothing.

beat_states.desire_state: To provoke Ophelia with sexual innuendo while performing his antic disposition before the court, and to punish/test her for her complicity with Polonius and Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose corruption and falsehood — Ophelia represents the betrayal of intimacy weaponized against him
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's innocence or feigned innocence deflects his provocations; he cannot express genuine feeling without revealing his strategy

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and manic, enjoying the verbal power play, but underneath there is bitterness and pain about Ophelia's role as a pawn; he controls the exchange but his obsessive sexual wordplay reveals agitation

social_state.rationale: He holds dominant status as prince and as the verbal aggressor; his warmth is actively negative — he is cold, mocking, using Ophelia as an object for his performance of madness

known_facts: Ophelia was used by Polonius and Claudius to spy on him
The play-within-the-play is about to test Claudius's guilt
The court is watching his behavior
His 'antic disposition' gives him license for transgressive speech
hidden_secrets: He is sane and strategically performing madness
The Mousetrap is designed to catch Claudius's conscience
He once genuinely loved Ophelia and may still
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is fully aware of the social dynamics and is manipulating them; his knowledge of Ophelia's betrayal fuels his cruelty

beat_states.defense_state: Obscene wit as a shield — using bawdy humor to simultaneously wound Ophelia, perform madness, and avoid vulnerability
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Still drawn to Ophelia physically and emotionally even as he deliberately humiliates her; his cruelty masks grief over their lost relationship
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's sexual aggression could be read as a desperate, displaced attempt at intimacy — reaching toward Ophelia the only way his wounded pride and strategic mask will allow

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain dignity and composure in the face of Hamlet's humiliating sexual innuendo in a public setting
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be dutiful and good — to her father, to Hamlet, to the court — while navigating impossible demands from all sides
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's escalating obscenity traps her: she cannot confront him without breaking decorum, cannot leave without drawing attention, cannot understand his full meaning without appearing unchaste

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is embarrassed, confused, and exposed; she has no control over the situation and is publicly subjected to degradation by the man she loved; her brief replies betray discomfort and helplessness

social_state.rationale: She is subordinate in every dimension — gender, rank in the exchange, and social position; her warmth is minimal, polite but guarded, a survival strategy

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving strangely since their forced separation
Her father instructed her to return Hamlet's letters and reject him
She was used to spy on Hamlet in the nunnery scene
The court is present and watching
hidden_secrets: She may still love Hamlet
She likely understands more of his sexual wordplay than she admits
false_beliefs: Hamlet may truly be mad rather than performing
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia lacks the crucial knowledge that Hamlet is performing; she is caught between interpretive frameworks — is this madness, cruelty, or some coded message?

beat_states.defense_state: Performing naïveté — retreating into 'I think nothing' to avoid engaging with the sexual content and protect her reputation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She likely understands more of the innuendo than she lets on, but must perform ignorance; she still cares for Hamlet yet is frightened by his transformation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may be more knowing and complicit in the bawdy exchange than her words suggest — her 'I think nothing' could carry its own wit and even a trace of flirtation under duress

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: You are merry, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Who, I?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Ay, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O God, your only jig-maker. What should a man do but be merry? For look you how cheerfully my mother looks, and my father died within ’s two hours.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Nay, ’tis twice two months, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: So long? Nay, then, let the devil wear black, for I’ll have a suit of sables. O heavens, die two months ago, and not forgotten yet? Then there’s hope a great man’s memory may outlive his life half a year. But, by ’r Lady, he must build churches, then, or else shall he suffer not thinking on, with the hobby-horse, whose epitaph is “ For oh, for oh, the hobby-horse is forgot.”

beat_states.desire_state: To expose his mother's shallow grief and test the court's conscience before the play-within-the-play begins
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father by exposing the rottenness in Denmark and confirming Claudius's guilt
beat_states.obstacle: He must maintain the 'antic disposition' mask, which forces him to channel genuine rage and grief through bitter wit, risking being dismissed as merely 'merry'

affect_state.rationale: Beneath the manic wit is deep bitterness about his father being forgotten in two months. His arousal is high as the play-within-the-play approaches. He maintains surface control through his performance but the emotional content keeps breaking through — 'die two months ago, and not forgotten yet?' is barely concealed anguish.

social_state.rationale: He holds court with his wit, maintaining princely status through verbal dominance, but his warmth toward Ophelia is low — she is a tool and audience for his bitter performance rather than a genuine interlocutor. He uses her as a sounding board to broadcast his contempt.

known_facts: His father died approximately four months ago
His mother remarried with suspicious speed
The Ghost has accused Claudius of murder
The play-within-the-play is about to test Claudius's guilt
Ophelia has been used by Polonius and Claudius to spy on him
hidden_secrets: He knows the Ghost's accusation and is using the play as a trap
His 'merriment' is entirely performed as part of his antic disposition
He suspects Ophelia is still being used against him
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is highly epistemically aware in this moment. He knows exactly what he is doing — weaponizing the timeline of his father's death to shame his mother publicly. His correction ('So long?') when Ophelia says four months is ironic performance, not genuine confusion.

beat_states.defense_state: Sardonic deflection — uses dark humor and feigned merriment to shield genuine anguish about his father's death and mother's betrayal
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs exaggerated cheerfulness while seething with grief and disgust; his 'merriment' is the opposite of what he feels, yet he cannot stop performing it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A reading where Hamlet's merriment is partially genuine — a manic high before the crucial test of the play, where nervous energy manifests as genuine giddiness alongside the bitterness

beat_states.desire_state: To make sense of Hamlet's erratic behavior and maintain a normal social interaction with him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To hold onto her connection with Hamlet and navigate between her love for him and her obedience to her father
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's wild oscillation between sexual innuendo, dark humor, and bitter grief makes it impossible for her to find stable ground

affect_state.rationale: She is unsettled by Hamlet's sexual banter from the previous beat and now confused by his pivot to bitter commentary on death and memory. Her uncertainty is high — she cannot tell what is performance and what is madness. She is emotionally exposed, trying to keep up.

social_state.rationale: She occupies a subordinate position — responding to Hamlet's lead, correcting him gently but not challenging. Her warmth is moderate; she still cares but is increasingly wary.

known_facts: Hamlet's father died approximately four months ago
Hamlet has been behaving erratically
She has been positioned to interact with Hamlet under observation by Polonius and Claudius
hidden_secrets: She is aware that her father and the King are observing or have orchestrated her encounters with Hamlet
false_beliefs: Hamlet's merriment may be a sign of genuine recovery or madness rather than calculated performance
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia takes Hamlet's behavior largely at face value — she reads his energy as 'merry' rather than recognizing it as weaponized bitterness. Her factual correction suggests she is trying to anchor herself in what she knows to be true.

beat_states.defense_state: Literal-minded correction — she offers factual precision ('twice two months') as a way to ground the conversation in reality and deflect from the emotional undertow
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is tasked with engaging Hamlet for surveillance purposes while genuinely caring about his mental state; her gentle observations ('You are merry') are both dutiful report-making and authentic concern
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may be more perceptive than she appears — her observation 'You are merry' could be a gentle probe or even a subtle challenge, testing whether Hamlet will acknowledge the inappropriateness of his mood

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: What means this, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Marry, this is miching mallecho. It means mischief.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Belike this show imports the argument of the play.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: We shall know by this fellow. The players cannot keep counsel; they’ll tell all.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Will he tell us what this show meant?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ay, or any show that you will show him. Be not you ashamed to show, he’ll not shame to tell you what it means.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: You are naught, you are naught. I’ll mark the play.

beat_states.desire_state: To use the dumb show as a weapon, broadcasting his knowledge of the murder while maintaining plausible deniability through bawdy wit
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose and avenge his father's murder while testing the truth of the ghost's claims
beat_states.obstacle: He must sustain this performance of antic disposition without revealing his full hand before the play-within-the-play reaches its climax
