affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is riding high on adrenaline — the trap is being sprung. His wit is rapid and aggressive, suggesting high arousal and a strong sense of control. The positive valence comes from the thrill of orchestrating the mousetrap. Low vulnerability because he is the puppet-master here.

social_state.rationale: He dominates the exchange completely, turning Ophelia's every innocent question into sexual material. His warmth is negative — he's wielding intimacy as a weapon, not offering genuine connection. He treats her as an audience for his performance rather than a person.

known_facts: The dumb show depicts the murder of his father as described by the ghost
The play-within-the-play is designed to catch Claudius's conscience
Ophelia is likely being used by Polonius and Claudius to spy on him
'Miching mallecho' — he knows exactly what the show means and is signaling it openly
hidden_secrets: He has arranged the play to mirror Claudius's crime
He knows the specific method of his father's murder
His crude behavior with Ophelia is a deliberate performance, not madness
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet possesses near-complete knowledge of what is unfolding. His comment 'miching mallecho — it means mischief' is a barely veiled announcement of his trap, hiding in plain sight through apparent jest.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflects through sexual innuendo — turning every exchange into a bawdy joke keeps others from seeing his real intent
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is electric with anticipation about the mousetrap yet channels this tension into crude jokes with Ophelia, the woman he may still love but whom he now uses as a prop in his performance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's cruelty to Ophelia could reflect genuine bitterness and sexual disgust rather than purely strategic behavior — his misogynistic streak may be authentic feeling bleeding through the antic disposition

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what is happening in the play and to navigate Hamlet's bewildering sexual aggression with some dignity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain her sense of self and connection to Hamlet despite being caught between his erratic behavior and her father's instructions
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet keeps converting her sincere questions into sexual innuendo, preventing genuine communication and forcing her into an impossible social position in a public setting

affect_state.rationale: She is confused, embarrassed, and socially exposed. Each question she asks in good faith is turned against her. Her 'You are naught' is a flustered attempt to regain composure. High vulnerability — she is publicly subjected to sexual teasing by a prince she once loved.

social_state.rationale: She is subordinate in every dimension — by gender, rank, and conversational power. Her warmth remains slightly positive; she keeps engaging rather than cutting him off entirely, suggesting residual affection or at least deference.

known_facts: Hamlet's behavior has been erratic and cruel toward her
A dumb show has just been performed that seems to preview the play's plot
Hamlet seems to know more about the play's meaning than he's saying
hidden_secrets: She may be aware that Polonius and Claudius are observing her interaction with Hamlet
false_beliefs: She likely still believes Hamlet's transformation is rooted in madness or rejected love rather than a deliberate strategy
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia is genuinely puzzled by the dumb show and asks innocent questions. She has no knowledge of the murder plot the play mirrors. She senses mischief in Hamlet's manner but cannot decode its real target.

beat_states.defense_state: Mild scolding ('You are naught') and withdrawal into the play — she redirects attention away from herself to end the uncomfortable exchange
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is simultaneously drawn to engage with Hamlet (asking him questions, seeking his interpretation) and repelled by his aggressive sexuality — she cannot stop reaching toward him even as he humiliates her
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may be more knowing than she appears — her persistent questioning ('What means this?', 'Will he tell us?') could be a subtle attempt to draw out Hamlet's intentions on behalf of Polonius and Claudius, performing innocence as her own tactic

utterances.speaker: PROLOGUE
utterances.text: For us and for our tragedy, / Here stooping to your clemency, / We beg your hearing patiently.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Is this a prologue or the posy of a ring?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: ’Tis brief, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: As woman’s love.

beat_states.desire_state: To publicly wound Ophelia and test the reactions of Claudius and Gertrude through barbed commentary on women's inconstancy
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose corruption and avenge his father — here channeling rage at his mother's fickleness onto Ophelia and the court
beat_states.obstacle: He cannot directly accuse anyone yet; must work through indirect, performative cruelty

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of controlled aggression, deploying wit like a weapon. The quip 'As woman's love' is rehearsed bitterness — high control, moderate arousal, underlying hurt masked by cynicism.

social_state.rationale: He dominates the exchange, delivering the punchline with princely authority. Warmth is deeply negative — he is deliberately cruel to Ophelia in a semi-public setting.

known_facts: The play-within-the-play is about to begin and mirrors Claudius's crime
Ophelia has been used as a pawn by Polonius and Claudius
His mother remarried with what he considers obscene haste
hidden_secrets: He knows the play is a trap for Claudius
His antic disposition is strategic performance
false_beliefs: He may believe Ophelia is actively complicit in spying on him rather than a coerced participant
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's epistemic position is strong — he is the architect of the evening's events. His cynicism about women's love is an overgeneralization fueled by trauma.

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement — redirecting his rage at Gertrude's betrayal onto Ophelia through cutting wit
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He may still have feelings for Ophelia yet uses her as a vehicle for his misogynistic fury born of his mother's remarriage
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's cruelty to Ophelia could be read as a desperate, self-destructive push to sever remaining emotional bonds so he can focus on revenge

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain composure and engage normally with Hamlet despite his increasingly hostile innuendos
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be a dutiful daughter and obedient woman while preserving whatever remains of her connection to Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet keeps turning every innocent exchange into an attack on her gender and her love

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is being publicly humiliated by the man she loved. Her brief, factual reply suggests she is trying to stay small and safe, but Hamlet's 'As woman's love' lands as a direct wound. High vulnerability, low control.

social_state.rationale: She occupies a subordinate position — responding to the prince's lead, unable to challenge or deflect. She still offers mild warmth through engagement.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving erratically and cruelly toward her
Her father and the King are observing
She returned Hamlet's letters as instructed
hidden_secrets: She may still love Hamlet despite his treatment of her
false_beliefs: She may believe Hamlet's cruelty means he never truly loved her
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia lacks the full picture — she doesn't know about the ghost or Hamlet's strategic antic disposition. She can only interpret his behavior at face value.

beat_states.defense_state: Minimization — she responds with neutral, literal observations ('Tis brief, my lord') to avoid engaging with his hostility
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She still responds to Hamlet with courtesy and attentiveness, even as each reply she gives becomes ammunition for his contempt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia's brief reply could be a subtle attempt to redirect the conversation back to the play, a quiet assertion of agency

beat_states.desire_state: To fulfill his theatrical function by securing the audience's attention and goodwill
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: As a player, to perform well and please the prince who commissioned the performance
beat_states.obstacle: The court audience may be distracted or inattentive; the brevity of the prologue gives little time to establish rapport

affect_state.rationale: The Prologue is performing a conventional theatrical role with standard humility. Moderate arousal from the act of performing before royalty; limited personal emotional investment.

social_state.rationale: The Prologue adopts a deliberately humble, supplicant posture ('stooping to your clemency') — low status by design, with warmth aimed at securing goodwill.

known_facts: The players have been commissioned by Prince Hamlet
The play has been modified with Hamlet's inserted lines
hidden_secrets: The players may or may not fully understand that the play is designed to catch the King's conscience
epistemic_state.rationale: The Prologue is a functional role with limited individual psychology; the actor knows his part but may not grasp the full political stakes.

beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Prologue's extreme brevity may itself be part of Hamlet's design — keeping the framing minimal to let the content of the play hit harder

utterances.speaker: PLAYER KING
utterances.text: Full thirty times hath Phoebus’ cart gone round / Neptune’s salt wash and Tellus’ orbèd ground, / And thirty dozen moons with borrowed sheen / About the world have times twelve thirties been / Since love our hearts and Hymen did our hands / Unite commutual in most sacred bands.

utterances.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
utterances.text: So many journeys may the sun and moon / Make us again count o’er ere love be done! / But woe is me! You are so sick of late, / So far from cheer and from your former state, / That I distrust you. Yet, though I distrust, / Discomfort you, my lord, it nothing must. / For women fear too much, even as they love, / And women’s fear and love hold quantity, / In neither aught, or in extremity. / Now what my love is, proof hath made you know, / And, as my love is sized, my fear is so: / Where love is great, the littlest doubts are fear; / Where little fears grow great, great love grows there.

utterances.speaker: PLAYER KING
utterances.text: Faith, I must leave thee, love, and shortly too. / My operant powers their functions leave to do. / And thou shall live in this fair world behind, / Honored, beloved; and haply one as kind / For husband shalt thou—

utterances.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
utterances.text: O, confound the rest! / Such love must needs be treason in my breast. / In second husband let me be accurst. / None wed the second but who killed the first.

beat_states.desire_state: To prepare his queen for his imminent death and give her permission to remarry
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the play-within-a-play's function of mirroring the murder of King Hamlet and testing Claudius's conscience
beat_states.obstacle: The Player Queen's intense devotion makes it difficult to broach the subject of her future without him

affect_state.rationale: He is resigned to dying, speaking with weary certainty about his fading powers. Low energy, high acceptance, deeply vulnerable in his mortality.

social_state.rationale: He retains the authority of a king and husband but exercises it with tender generosity, releasing his wife from obligation.

known_facts: He has been married thirty years
His body is failing him
He will die soon
His wife will outlive him
hidden_secrets: The manner of his death (poison) is not yet revealed within the play
epistemic_state.rationale: The Player King has full awareness of his mortality and the passage of time, and gently tries to introduce the pragmatic reality of remarriage.

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — framing death as natural inevitability to soften its blow
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He loves her deeply yet must encourage her to accept another husband, undermining the very bond he celebrates
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Could be read as subtly testing the Queen's loyalty by floating the remarriage idea, rather than genuinely encouraging it

beat_states.desire_state: To demonstrate absolute fidelity and reject any possibility of a second marriage
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve as the mirror of Gertrude's behavior — her vehement protests ironically highlight Gertrude's betrayal
beat_states.obstacle: The Player King's gentle insistence on the possibility of remarriage forces her to confront a future she refuses to accept

affect_state.rationale: She oscillates between tender worry about his illness and passionate, almost violent rejection of remarriage. High emotional intensity, grief-driven certainty, but ultimately unable to control the situation of his dying.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate as queen-consort but pouring out devotion and warmth; her love is performatively intense and all-consuming.

known_facts: Her husband is gravely ill
They have been married thirty years
He is suggesting she may remarry
false_beliefs: She believes her own vow of eternal fidelity is unbreakable — the play's structure suggests otherwise
epistemic_state.rationale: She knows his condition is dire but refuses to process its implications. Her certainty about her own constancy is the dramatic irony Hamlet has designed to catch Gertrude and Claudius.

beat_states.defense_state: denial — refusing to entertain the reality of his death or her potential remarriage
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Her extreme protestations of fidelity ('None wed the second but who killed the first') are so absolute they become suspect — the play deliberately sets up the irony that such vows are easily broken
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Could be played as genuinely self-aware — knowing human nature is frail but performing absolute devotion as a desperate act of love rather than naive conviction

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: That’s wormwood!

beat_states.desire_state: To signal to the audience (and himself) that the Player Queen's words about remarriage are hitting their mark against Gertrude
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose the truth of his father's murder and his mother's betrayal, forcing conscience to the surface
beat_states.obstacle: He must wait for Claudius's reaction and cannot yet act directly; the trap is still being set

affect_state.rationale: A flash of dark satisfaction — 'wormwood' is bitter, and Hamlet relishes the bitter truth being dramatized before Gertrude and Claudius. His arousal is high with anticipation that his mousetrap is working. Yet the word 'wormwood' itself betrays his own bitterness — this is personal, not just strategic. He feels in control of the situation as puppet-master of the play-within-the-play.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet occupies a position of secret power — he is the architect of this scene and knows more than anyone else about its true purpose. His warmth is low; this is a cold, pointed aside dripping with schadenfreude directed implicitly at Gertrude.

known_facts: The Player Queen's vow never to remarry mirrors what Gertrude should have felt
Gertrude remarried with indecent haste after King Hamlet's death
The play is designed to test Claudius's guilt
The Ghost's account of murder by poisoning
hidden_secrets: He has engineered the play specifically to catch Claudius's conscience
He suspects Claudius murdered his father
His 'antic disposition' is a deliberate strategy
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with privileged knowledge — he knows the play's true purpose while most of the court does not. His aside reveals his active, analytical monitoring of the play's effect.

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement through the theatrical frame — channeling his pain into sardonic commentary rather than direct confrontation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He savors the bitter truth being spoken aloud (pleasure in exposure) while simultaneously experiencing the deep wound of his mother's actual remarriage (pain in recognition)
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The line could be read as Hamlet speaking not just with satisfaction but with genuine pain — 'wormwood' as an involuntary exclamation of his own bitterness about his mother, rather than a calculated commentary. In this reading, the trap momentarily traps the trapper.

utterances.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
utterances.text: The instances that second marriage move / Are base respects of thrift, but none of love. / A second time I kill my husband dead / When second husband kisses me in bed.

utterances.speaker: PLAYER KING
utterances.text: I do believe you think what now you speak, / But what we do determine oft we break. / Purpose is but the slave to memory, / Of violent birth, but poor validity, / Which now, the fruit unripe, sticks on the tree / But fall unshaken when they mellow be. / Most necessary ’tis that we forget / To pay ourselves what to ourselves is debt. / What to ourselves in passion we propose, / The passion ending, doth the purpose lose. / The violence of either grief or joy / Their own enactures with themselves destroy. / Where joy most revels, grief doth most lament; / Grief joys, joy grieves, on slender accident. / This world is not for aye, nor ’tis not strange / That even our loves should with our fortunes change; / For ’tis a question left us yet to prove / Whether love lead fortune or else fortune love. / The great man down, you mark his favorite flies; / The poor, advanced, makes friends of enemies. / And hitherto doth love on fortune tend, / For who not needs shall never lack a friend, / And who in want a hollow friend doth try / Directly seasons him his enemy. / But, orderly to end where I begun: / Our wills and fates do so contrary run / That our devices still are overthrown; / Our thoughts are ours, their ends none of our own. / So think thou wilt no second husband wed, / But die thy thoughts when thy first lord is dead.

utterances.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
utterances.text: Nor Earth to me give food, nor heaven light, / Sport and repose lock from me day and night, / To desperation turn my trust and hope, / An anchor’s cheer in prison be my scope. / Each opposite that blanks the face of joy / Meet what I would have well and it destroy. / Both here and hence pursue me lasting strife, / If, once a widow, ever I be wife.

beat_states.desire_state: to prepare his queen for the reality that she will remarry after his death, testing the sincerity of her vows
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to face death with philosophical acceptance and ensure his queen is not bound by false promises
beat_states.obstacle: the Player Queen's passionate insistence on eternal fidelity, which he knows contradicts human nature

affect_state.rationale: Melancholic and resigned but intellectually composed; he speaks from a position of worldly wisdom about human frailty, yet this wisdom is tinged with sadness about his own approaching death and the likelihood of his wife's remarriage

social_state.rationale: Maintains the authority of a dying king dispensing wisdom, but tempered with genuine tenderness toward his wife; he is not cruel, but gently corrective

known_facts: He is dying and will soon leave his queen alone
Human resolve made in passion rarely survives the passing of that passion
Fortune governs love more than people admit
His queen sincerely believes her own vows in this moment
hidden_secrets: He suspects or knows she will remarry — this is the mirror Hamlet uses to catch Claudius and Gertrude
epistemic_state.rationale: The Player King possesses deep philosophical insight about human nature and self-deception; his speech is designed (by the playwright-within-the-play, Hamlet) to mirror the real King Hamlet's situation

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — transforms emotional pain of anticipated betrayal into philosophical abstraction
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: loves his wife and wants her fidelity yet argues persuasively that she will inevitably betray her vows
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Could be read as genuinely trying to give his wife permission to remarry out of love, rather than testing or disillusioning her

beat_states.desire_state: to prove the absolute permanence of her love and convince her husband she will never remarry
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be seen as the ideal faithful wife, bound eternally to her first husband
beat_states.obstacle: the Player King's philosophical arguments that undermine the credibility of her vows

affect_state.rationale: Highly emotional, escalating her rhetoric to self-cursing extremes; she feels cornered by her husband's doubt and responds with passionate intensity, calling down cosmic punishments upon herself if she breaks faith

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to her husband's philosophical authority, she compensates with emotional fervor and devotion; her warmth is intense but performatively excessive

known_facts: Her husband is dying
She believes with total conviction she will never remarry
Her husband doubts the permanence of her resolve
hidden_secrets: The dramatic irony: she is a mirror of Gertrude, whose rapid remarriage proves the Player King right
false_beliefs: That the strength of her current feeling guarantees future action
That extreme oaths can bind future behavior
epistemic_state.rationale: She is sincere in the moment but blind to the very human frailty her husband describes; her epistemic state is one of passionate self-certainty that the play's meta-narrative will expose as naive

beat_states.defense_state: reaction formation — the extremity of her oaths masks the very possibility her husband raises
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: the more desperately she protests her eternal fidelity, the more the audience (and Hamlet) suspects she will break these vows — her excess reveals fragility
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Could be read as knowingly performing fidelity she already doubts, making the oaths deliberately hollow — though this reading is less standard

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: If she should break it now!

utterances.speaker: PLAYER KING
utterances.text: ’Tis deeply sworn. Sweet, leave me here awhile. / My spirits grow dull, and fain I would beguile / The tedious day with sleep.

utterances.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
utterances.text: Sleep rock thy brain, / And never come mischance between us twain.

beat_states.desire_state: To publicly mark the Player Queen's vow as a trap that mirrors Gertrude's betrayal, building anticipation for the moment the parallel will become undeniable
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose the truth of his father's murder and his mother's complicity, forcing conscience to surface
beat_states.obstacle: The play hasn't yet reached the poisoning scene; he must sustain tension and wait while his excitement threatens to reveal his purpose prematurely

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is thrilling at the play's effectiveness — the Player Queen's vows are landing — but he's barely containing his anticipation and barely concealing his pointed commentary. His aside is electric with nervous energy.

social_state.rationale: He occupies the privileged position of orchestrator and audience commentator, but his remarks are barbed and cold, directed implicitly at Gertrude

known_facts: The Player Queen has sworn never to remarry — exactly as designed
Gertrude is watching this scene that mirrors her own remarriage
The critical poisoning scene is still to come
hidden_secrets: He wrote the inserted speech specifically to catch Claudius's conscience
His commentary is aimed at Gertrude, not merely at the play
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows exactly what's coming in the play and is monitoring its effect on the royal audience, reading every moment as data

beat_states.defense_state: Deflecting his own anxiety through sardonic commentary, performing the role of casual spectator
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is simultaneously the detached theatrical critic and the agonized son watching his mother's betrayal re-enacted
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: His aside could be a genuine moment of vulnerability — a son desperately wishing his mother had kept her vows, rather than a tactical provocation

beat_states.desire_state: To gracefully conclude his scene with his queen and transition into the sleep that enables the murder
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: As a performer, to execute the play convincingly and please Prince Hamlet, their patron
beat_states.obstacle: His philosophical resignation and emotional fatigue make it difficult to summon genuine tenderness in his farewell

affect_state.rationale: The Player King is weary, gentle, resigned — his spirits are dull, and he invites sleep with an innocence that makes him deeply vulnerable to what comes next

social_state.rationale: He addresses his queen with genuine sweetness and trust, maintaining the warmth of a long marriage even as he acknowledges his own decline

known_facts: He is performing a role written/modified by Hamlet
His character is about to be murdered in his sleep
hidden_secrets: The play mirrors the actual Danish court's situation
epistemic_state.rationale: As a player, he knows the script; as a character within the play, he is unknowing of his impending murder

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs tenderness and trust while the dramatic structure demands the audience know this trust is fatally misplaced

beat_states.desire_state: To perform devoted, absolute fidelity to her husband as he falls asleep
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To deliver a compelling performance that satisfies the prince's specific instructions
beat_states.obstacle: She must convey sincerity in vows that the audience (and Hamlet) know will be broken, creating ironic tension

affect_state.rationale: The Player Queen's blessing is warm and certain — a gentle lullaby — yet structurally she is the vessel of dramatic irony, her certainty making the coming betrayal more devastating

social_state.rationale: She is deferential and loving, performing the ideal of wifely devotion in a blessing that wishes protection upon her husband

known_facts: She is performing a role that Hamlet has specifically shaped
Her character's vows will be broken when she remarries the murderer
hidden_secrets: The role is designed to mirror Queen Gertrude
epistemic_state.rationale: As a player she likely knows the full arc of the story; she may or may not grasp the political implications of the performance

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She performs perfect devotion while the dramatic irony makes every word of comfort a lie in advance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Player Queen may be entirely unaware of the real-court parallels and simply performing her craft

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Madam, how like you this play?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: The lady doth protest too much, methinks.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O, but she’ll keep her word.

beat_states.desire_state: To force Gertrude to recognize herself in the Player Queen and reveal guilt or discomfort about her hasty remarriage
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose the rot in Denmark and hold a mirror up to those who wronged his father
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude deflects with a cool, dismissive observation rather than showing genuine distress or self-recognition

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is enjoying the cat-and-mouse game and feels a surge of predatory energy, but Gertrude's evasive coolness frustrates him and exposes the wound of her betrayal

social_state.rationale: He addresses his mother with surface politeness ('Madam') but the question is loaded with contempt; he is asserting intellectual dominance while withholding filial warmth

known_facts: The Player Queen's vows mirror Gertrude's situation as a widow who remarried quickly
The Mousetrap is designed to test Claudius's guilt and needle Gertrude's conscience
Gertrude's dismissive response suggests she either doesn't see the parallel or refuses to engage with it
hidden_secrets: He has specifically shaped the play to accuse both Claudius and Gertrude
The Ghost has told him Claudius murdered his father
false_beliefs: He may overestimate how clearly Gertrude perceives the personal accusation embedded in the play
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is the puppet-master of this moment, fully aware of the subtext, but he cannot yet read whether Gertrude's coolness is genuine obliviousness or willful denial

beat_states.defense_state: irony — disguising his accusation as casual theatrical commentary
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants his mother to feel the sting of the parallel, yet his quip 'she'll keep her word' is both a jab at Gertrude's inconstancy and a desperate wish that fidelity were possible
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'she'll keep her word' could be read as genuinely testing whether Gertrude will openly defend second marriage, hoping she will incriminate herself further

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain composure and deflect the uncomfortable parallel between herself and the Player Queen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve her place in the court and her relationship with both her son and her husband without confronting painful truths
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is publicly drawing attention to her and the play's obvious parallel to her own remarriage

affect_state.rationale: She maintains a calm, slightly amused exterior, but the fact that Hamlet publicly asks her opinion creates pressure; there is latent unease beneath her poised dismissal

social_state.rationale: She speaks as Queen with confident authority, offering a measured critical judgment; warmth toward Hamlet is neutral to slightly cool, as his question feels like a trap

known_facts: The Player Queen is vowing never to remarry — an obvious parallel to her own situation
Hamlet has been behaving erratically and provocatively all evening
She herself remarried quickly after King Hamlet's death
hidden_secrets: The degree to which she was complicit in or aware of Claudius's crime remains her deepest hidden truth
false_beliefs: She may believe she can contain Hamlet's provocations with surface poise
She may believe her remark successfully neutralizes the parallel
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude's epistemic state is famously ambiguous — Shakespeare leaves unclear how much she knows about the murder; her deflection may be conscious evasion or genuine naïveté about the deeper accusation

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — she reduces the Player Queen to a literary critique ('protests too much') rather than engaging with the personal accusation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Her famous line acknowledges that excessive protestation implies insincerity, which inadvertently admits the possibility that her own constancy was insincere — yet she delivers it as if it applies only to the fictional character
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may fully understand Hamlet's accusation and her line could be a wry, self-aware admission delivered with deliberate courage rather than avoidance

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Have you heard the argument? Is there no offense in ’t?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: No, no, they do but jest, poison in jest. No offense i’ th’ world.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: What do you call the play?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: “ The Mousetrap.” Marry, how? Tropically. This play is the image of a murder done in Vienna. Gonzago is the duke’s name, his wife Baptista. You shall see anon. ’Tis a knavish piece of work, but what of that? Your Majesty and we that have free souls, it touches us not. Let the galled jade wince; our withers are unwrung. / This is one Lucianus, nephew to the king.

beat_states.desire_state: To torment Claudius with barely-veiled accusations while maintaining plausible deniability, pushing him toward a guilty reaction
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To confirm Claudius's guilt and avenge his father's murder
beat_states.obstacle: Must provoke Claudius without being openly treasonous; the court is watching

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is riding a wave of aggressive pleasure. He is practically drunk on the power of the trap he's set. His wordplay ('Mousetrap,' 'tropically,' 'galled jade') shows a mind firing at maximum speed, high arousal and high sense of control. He feels confident the trap is working as the King asks nervous questions.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet seizes the dominant position in this exchange — the King is asking him questions, and Hamlet answers with cruel wit. His warmth toward Claudius is deeply hostile, barely masked by the jester's grin. 'Let the galled jade wince' is a direct taunt disguised as a general observation.

known_facts: The play mirrors the Ghost's account of King Hamlet's murder
Claudius is becoming visibly uncomfortable
The Queen found the Player Queen's protests excessive (from prior beat)
Lucianus is about to enact the poisoning scene
hidden_secrets: He has deliberately altered the play to mirror his father's murder
He is using the play to test the Ghost's claims
He has asked Horatio to watch Claudius's reaction
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is strategically revealing information in layers — naming the play 'The Mousetrap,' calling it 'the image of a murder,' identifying Lucianus — each one a tightening noose. He knows exactly what he's doing but cannot yet be certain of Claudius's guilt until the King fully reacts.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through wordplay and double meanings — 'poison in jest,' 'tropically' — allowing him to deny direct accusation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Exhilarated by his power over the King in this moment, yet this very manic glee risks exposing his own strategy and endangering himself
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's escalating recklessness (naming it 'The Mousetrap,' describing the murder openly) could indicate he is losing control of his strategy, driven more by emotional need to confront than by rational calculation

beat_states.desire_state: To assess how much Hamlet knows and whether the play constitutes a direct threat or accusation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne, his marriage, and the concealment of his fratricide
beat_states.obstacle: The play is becoming an unmistakable mirror of his crime, and Hamlet's answers are menacingly ambiguous — he cannot confront Hamlet without revealing why it disturbs him

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is deeply alarmed. His two rapid questions ('Have you heard the argument? Is there no offense?') betray anxiety beneath regal composure. He is losing control of the situation — Hamlet's answers are evasive and threatening. The word 'offense' reveals his fear that this is a deliberate accusation.

social_state.rationale: Though nominally the King, Claudius is being outmaneuvered in this exchange. Hamlet answers his questions with mocking evasions and then lectures him. Claudius's status is eroding in real time as he is forced into the reactive, questioning position.

known_facts: He murdered his brother by poison
The play depicts a murder that resembles his own crime
Hamlet has been behaving erratically and possibly dangerously
hidden_secrets: He killed King Hamlet by pouring poison in his ear
He married Gertrude to secure the throne
false_beliefs: He may still hope Hamlet's 'antic disposition' means this is coincidence rather than deliberate accusation
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is rapidly losing the ability to maintain his false belief that this is coincidental. Hamlet's naming it 'The Mousetrap,' his emphasis on 'poison in jest,' and his pointed 'let the galled jade wince' are closing off escape routes for Claudius's denial.

beat_states.defense_state: Interrogation disguised as casual interest — asking 'Is there no offense?' to gauge whether Hamlet is acting deliberately or innocently
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Must appear calm and royally composed while internally panicking that his secret murder is being publicly dramatized
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius's questions could be read as genuinely royal concern about propriety rather than guilty panic — a king checking whether a court entertainment is appropriate — though the dramatic context makes this unlikely

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: You are as good as a chorus, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I could interpret between you and your love, if I could see the puppets dallying.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: You are keen, my lord, you are keen.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: It would cost you a groaning to take off mine edge.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Still better and worse.

beat_states.desire_state: To dominate the exchange with Ophelia through sexually aggressive wit, keeping himself armored and deflecting any genuine emotional connection while maintaining his manic energy during the play-within-a-play
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose hidden corruption and fulfill his duty of revenge — here he uses Ophelia as a verbal sparring partner to discharge his agitation while the trap for Claudius unfolds
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's graceful parrying of his barbs refuses to give him the satisfaction of either shocking her completely or drawing genuine engagement

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a heightened, almost manic state of performative aggression. His sexual puns ('groaning,' 'edge') show high arousal and a need for dominance. Underneath, proximity to Ophelia and the stakes of the mousetrap create underlying tension and vulnerability he refuses to acknowledge.

social_state.rationale: He holds conversational dominance, escalating each of Ophelia's responses into cruder territory. His warmth is actively negative — he is weaponizing intimacy, treating her as a puppet-show prop rather than a person.

known_facts: The play-within-a-play is about to reach its crucial moment
Ophelia has been used by Polonius and Claudius to spy on him
His commentary on the play doubles as commentary on real court corruption
hidden_secrets: He is using Ophelia partly to perform madness for the court's benefit
His cruelty masks unresolved feelings for her
The mousetrap is about to spring on Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with high strategic awareness — his bawdy banter is deliberate misdirection, keeping courtiers focused on his 'madness' rather than his true purpose with the play.

beat_states.defense_state: Sexualized aggression as a shield — by reducing all exchange to bawdy innuendo, he prevents any authentic intimacy or vulnerability with Ophelia
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is performing cruelty toward the woman he once loved, using her as an outlet for rage that actually belongs to his mother and Claudius; his wit betrays both brilliance and deep pain
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may genuinely enjoy the verbal sparring with Ophelia and his cruelty contains a perverse attempt to reconnect through the only mode he now permits himself — transgressive wit

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain composure and dignity in the face of Hamlet's escalating sexual aggression while staying engaged in the conversation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To understand and navigate her relationship with Hamlet — she is caught between obedience to her father, her feelings for Hamlet, and self-preservation
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's relentless sexual innuendo pushes her past the boundaries of decorum, and she cannot fully deflect without either submitting or breaking the exchange entirely

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is uncomfortable but not destroyed — she parries with real skill. However, each escalation from Hamlet pushes her further into discomfort. She is publicly exposed to sexual language from a prince she loved, which is both humiliating and confusing. Her 'still better and worse' reveals weary recognition that the wit improves as the decency deteriorates.

social_state.rationale: She is socially subordinate — a young woman enduring a prince's public innuendo with no real power to stop it. Her warmth hovers near neutral; she is neither cold nor encouraging, trying to navigate an impossible social position with minimal damage.

known_facts: Hamlet is behaving erratically and crudely toward her
Her father and the King are watching this interaction
Hamlet's 'chorus' commentary on the play is unusually pointed
hidden_secrets: She may still harbor feelings for Hamlet despite his cruelty
She was deployed as bait in the nunnery scene and knows her role as instrument
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness may be genuine rather than performed
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia perceives Hamlet's 'keenness' — his sharpness — but likely reads it as madness-fueled rather than strategically calculated. She does not know the full purpose of the play-within-a-play.

beat_states.defense_state: Witty understatement and polite redirection — 'Still better and worse' acknowledges his cleverness while signaling moral disapproval, maintaining a thin shield of courtly propriety
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is sharp enough to understand every innuendo and even appreciate the wit ('You are keen'), yet she must perform innocence and restraint; her responses reveal intelligence she is socially required to suppress
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may be more complicit and less uncomfortable than she appears — her witty responses could indicate she is still drawn to Hamlet's intelligence and is semi-willingly engaging in the flirtation despite its cruelty

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: So you mis-take your husbands.— Begin, murderer. Pox, leave thy damnable faces and begin. Come, the croaking raven doth bellow for revenge.

beat_states.desire_state: To accelerate the play toward the murder scene so he can observe Claudius's reaction and spring the trap
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To confirm Claudius's guilt and fulfill his duty to avenge his father's murder
beat_states.obstacle: The actor playing the murderer is taking too long with theatrical grimacing, delaying the crucial moment

affect_state.rationale: Near-feverish excitement as the critical moment approaches. His snapping at the actor ('Pox, leave thy damnable faces') reveals his arousal is overriding his ability to maintain composure. The dark humor of the raven line shows exhilaration mixed with bloodlust. He feels the trap closing but cannot fully control the pace.

social_state.rationale: He is asserting dominance over the actors, the play, and Ophelia simultaneously. His cruel pun about women mis-taking husbands is cold and dismissive. He speaks as both director and prince, commanding the stage with aggressive authority.

known_facts: The murder scene is about to be performed
The Queen has protested too much in the play, which mirrors his mother's behavior
Claudius is watching and has already shown nervousness about the play's content
The Ghost's account of the murder matches what is about to be enacted
hidden_secrets: The play is specifically designed to catch Claudius's conscience
He is watching Claudius more than the play
His 'madness' is strategic performance
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with near-complete knowledge of his plan and its stakes. His impatience with the actor reveals how precisely he understands the timing required — every second of delay is agony because the revelation depends on Claudius seeing the poisoning enacted.

beat_states.defense_state: Manic wit and sexual innuendo serve as cover for his deadly serious purpose — his crude pun on 'mis-take your husbands' deflects from the intensity of his focus on the trap
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is simultaneously the most calculating person in the room and the most emotionally uncontrolled — his impatience threatens to expose his scheme even as he drives toward its climax
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's loss of composure here could indicate genuine psychological destabilization rather than strategic impatience — the proximity to confirming his father's murder may be triggering unprocessed grief and rage that overwhelms his calculated plan.

utterances.speaker: LUCIANUS
utterances.text: Thoughts black, hands apt, drugs fit, and time agreeing, / Confederate season, else no creature seeing, / Thou mixture rank, of midnight weeds collected, / With Hecate’s ban thrice blasted, thrice infected, / Thy natural magic and dire property / On wholesome life usurp immediately.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: He poisons him i’ th’ garden for his estate. His name’s Gonzago. The story is extant and written in very choice Italian. You shall see anon how the murderer gets the love of Gonzago’s wife.

beat_states.desire_state: to ensure Claudius recognizes the murder method and to force a visible reaction that confirms guilt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to confirm Claudius's guilt in his father's murder and fulfill the Ghost's command for justice
beat_states.obstacle: Claudius may not yet have broken — Hamlet must narrate and escalate the pressure to make the parallel unmistakable

affect_state.rationale: Near-manic excitement as the crucial moment arrives; he's almost giddy, breaking dramatic convention to explicitly narrate the poisoning in the garden — high arousal, positive valence from the thrill of the trap, but vulnerability exists in how recklessly transparent he's being

social_state.rationale: Asserting dominance over the entire court by controlling the narrative; coldly aggressive toward Claudius through his 'helpful' commentary, which is really a public accusation barely veiled

known_facts: The Ghost claimed Claudius poisoned King Hamlet in the garden
The play mirrors the method of murder exactly
He is watching Claudius for a reaction
Horatio is also observing the King
hidden_secrets: The play is a deliberate trap to catch the King's conscience
He knows the Ghost's full account of the murder
His 'antic disposition' is strategic
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating at peak epistemic confidence — he's engineered this moment and is now driving it home by spelling out the plot details ('poisons him i' th' garden for his estate') to ensure no one, especially Claudius, can miss the parallel

beat_states.defense_state: performing the role of witty chorus/commentator allows him to attack while maintaining plausible deniability
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: exhilarated by the trap springing yet dangerously overplaying his hand by narrating the plot aloud, risking his own safety
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's overexplicit narration could be read as loss of self-control rather than calculated strategy — his excitement overwhelming his tactical judgment

beat_states.desire_state: to perform the poisoning scene convincingly and complete the player's task
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: as a player, to serve the dramatic narrative Hamlet has commissioned
beat_states.obstacle: the heightened tension in the room and Hamlet's earlier criticism of his acting style

affect_state.rationale: Performing a dark, incantatory speech with high energy; must maintain composure despite the fraught atmosphere and Hamlet's earlier rebuke about 'damnable faces'

social_state.rationale: Low status as a hired player performing before royalty; the character he plays is cold, calculating, and malevolent

known_facts: He has been directed by Hamlet to perform this specific scene
Hamlet inserted lines into the play
He plays a nephew who poisons a king
hidden_secrets: May or may not understand the full political implications of what he's enacting
epistemic_state.rationale: As a player he knows his lines and staging but the full strategic purpose of the Mousetrap may be only partially understood

beat_states.defense_state: ritualistic formality of verse provides a mask for any personal anxiety
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: must play a villain convincingly while being aware the performance is charged with real political danger
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Lucianus may be fully aware he is participating in an accusation against the King and is performing under duress or excitement

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: The King rises.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What, frighted with false fire?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: How fares my lord?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Give o’er the play.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Give me some light. Away!

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Lights, lights, lights!

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Would not this, sir, and a forest of feathers ( if the rest of my fortunes turn Turk with me) with two Provincial roses on my razed shoes, get me a fellowship in a cry of players? / Why, let the strucken deer go weep, The hart ungallèd play. For some must watch, while some must sleep: Thus runs the world away.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Half a share.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: A whole one, I. / For thou dost know, O Damon dear, This realm dismantled was Of Jove himself, and now reigns here A very very— pajock.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: You might have rhymed.

beat_states.desire_state: To celebrate and share his triumph — the play worked, Claudius revealed his guilt — with Horatio, his trusted witness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To confirm Claudius's guilt and find the moral ground for action against him
beat_states.obstacle: The manic energy of vindication threatens to overwhelm coherent thought; he still must translate proof into action

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is near-euphoric — the mousetrap worked. His arousal is extremely high, almost feverish. He now has certainty about Claudius's guilt. But his control is moderate: the giddy, scattered quality of his speech (jumping between acting metaphors, deer metaphors, rhyming verse) suggests he's barely containing himself.

social_state.rationale: He feels ascendant — he's outmaneuvered the king publicly. With Horatio specifically, he shows genuine warmth and camaraderie, calling him 'Damon dear,' treating him as an equal conspirator in this moment of shared knowledge.

known_facts: Claudius reacted with visible guilt to the play-within-the-play
Horatio witnessed the same reaction
The Ghost's account of the murder is confirmed
Claudius killed King Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He has been feigning madness as a strategic cover
He knows Claudius is a murderer and usurper
He is now morally obligated to take revenge
epistemic_state.rationale: This is the moment of maximum epistemic clarity for Hamlet. The play confirmed everything. His coded language ('this realm dismantled was of Jove himself, and now reigns here a very very pajock') communicates to Horatio that Denmark has been stolen from a godlike king by a peacock/ass.

beat_states.defense_state: Manic deflection through wit and theatrical posturing — converting the enormity of confirmed knowledge into playful banter
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Elation at proving Claudius guilty coexists with the terrifying realization that he must now actually act; he performs triumphant joy to avoid confronting what comes next
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The manic quality could indicate that Hamlet is partly performing confidence he doesn't fully feel — that the confirmation of murder has destabilized rather than empowered him, and the wit is a defense against terror.

beat_states.desire_state: To ground Hamlet and share in the confirmation while maintaining his characteristic steadiness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve Hamlet faithfully as witness, counselor, and moral anchor
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's manic energy makes it difficult to have a sober strategic conversation about next steps

affect_state.rationale: Mildly positive — the plan worked — but Horatio is measured where Hamlet is ecstatic. His 'Half a share' and 'You might have rhymed' are deliberately understated, anchoring responses. He is certain of what he saw but remains composed and in control of himself.

social_state.rationale: Horatio maintains his characteristic position slightly below Hamlet in status while offering genuine warmth through his dry, companionable humor. His brevity is itself an act of intimacy — only a close friend would be this laconic at such a moment.

known_facts: Claudius visibly reacted to the poisoning scene
This confirms the Ghost's testimony
Hamlet's coded verse identifies Claudius as a usurper ('pajock')
hidden_secrets: He is Hamlet's co-conspirator in the mousetrap scheme
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio has confirmed with his own eyes what Hamlet asked him to watch for. His epistemic state aligns with Hamlet's — they now share confirmed knowledge of regicide.

beat_states.defense_state: Understatement and dry wit as a calming counterweight to Hamlet's frenzy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He shares Hamlet's conviction about Claudius's guilt but cannot share the euphoria, because he sees the danger ahead more clearly
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's terse responses could indicate worry rather than calm — he may be alarmed at Hamlet's reckless exuberance and trying to signal caution through brevity.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O good Horatio, I’ll take the ghost’s word for a thousand pound. Didst perceive?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Very well, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Upon the talk of the poisoning?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: I did very well note him.

beat_states.desire_state: To confirm with Horatio that Claudius's guilt was visibly exposed during the play
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To verify the truth of his father's murder and find certainty to act against Claudius
beat_states.obstacle: The need for external corroboration — his own perception may be distorted by desire and madness

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is elated — the mousetrap worked. He's high on vindication, almost giddy. But his rapid questioning reveals residual anxiety that he might have misread Claudius's reaction. The ghost's word validated fills him with near-manic relief.

social_state.rationale: With Horatio, Hamlet drops all pretense and antic disposition. He addresses him as 'good Horatio' with genuine warmth and trust — this is the one person he treats as an equal confidant. His status is elevated by the triumph of the play's success.

known_facts: The ghost claimed Claudius murdered King Hamlet by poison in the ear
Claudius reacted visibly during the poisoning scene of the play
The mousetrap has been sprung successfully
Horatio was tasked with observing Claudius's reaction
hidden_secrets: He arranged the play specifically to test Claudius's guilt
He has been in communication with his father's ghost
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet now believes the ghost's account is confirmed by Claudius's reaction. He moves from hypothesis to near-certainty, but wisely seeks Horatio's independent observation as final proof.

beat_states.defense_state: Seeking external validation to overcome his own doubt and potential self-deception
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Euphoric certainty clashing with the compulsive need to double-check with Horatio — if he were truly certain, he wouldn't need to ask
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's giddiness could be read as a man who has been desperate for certainty for so long that the relief overwhelms his judgment — he may be over-interpreting Claudius's reaction.

beat_states.desire_state: To faithfully report what he observed and support Hamlet's investigation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve as Hamlet's loyal, rational anchor — the truthful witness Hamlet needs
beat_states.obstacle: The weight of what confirmation means — affirming Claudius's guilt sets Hamlet on a potentially fatal course of action

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is steady and controlled — his characteristic mode. He is not swept up in Hamlet's euphoria. His responses are measured and factual, suggesting a man who takes seriously the gravity of what he has just witnessed. Mildly positive but sober.

social_state.rationale: Horatio maintains his deferential position ('my lord') while being genuinely warm and loyal. He doesn't challenge or inflate — he simply serves as the reliable witness Hamlet requested. His deference is chosen, not cringing.

known_facts: Hamlet asked him to observe Claudius during the play
Claudius reacted notably during the poisoning scene
The play was designed to mirror the ghost's account of murder
hidden_secrets: He shares knowledge of the ghost's claims — a dangerous secret in Claudius's court
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio has observed carefully and confirms Claudius's reaction. His brief, precise answers suggest he knows exactly what Hamlet is asking and has clear evidence. He lets Hamlet draw conclusions rather than drawing them himself.

beat_states.defense_state: Measured understatement — 'Very well, my lord' and 'I did very well note him' are deliberately restrained, providing confirmation without adding interpretive excess
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His loyalty compels him to confirm what he saw, but his care for Hamlet may make him uneasy about fueling the fire of revenge
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's brevity could indicate reluctance — he may be less certain than his words suggest, or may be withholding fuller commentary because he dreads what comes next.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: For if the King like not the comedy, / Why, then, belike he likes it not, perdy. / Ah ha! Come, some music! Come, the recorders! / Come, some music!

beat_states.desire_state: to publicly celebrate and broadcast his triumph over Claudius, savoring the proof of the King's guilt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father's murder by exposing and punishing Claudius
beat_states.obstacle: the court has dispersed in chaos; he must channel his manic energy somewhere before the next confrontation

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of near-manic euphoria. His rhyming couplet ('comedy/perdy') and repeated demands for music show exhilaration bordering on loss of control. The certainty is high — the mousetrap worked — but his control over himself and the situation is low, as his energy is scattered and performative rather than directed toward action.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet feels supremely dominant in this moment — he has outwitted the King and proven the ghost's word. His warmth is slightly negative; the 'Ah ha!' is directed outward as mockery rather than connection. He commands the room like a victor.

known_facts: Claudius reacted with visible guilt to the play-within-the-play
The ghost's accusation of murder is confirmed
Horatio witnessed and corroborated Claudius's reaction
Claudius killed King Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He specifically designed The Mousetrap to test Claudius
He knows the ghost's word is true and regicide occurred
He is now duty-bound to kill Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has achieved epistemic certainty about the central mystery of the play. The mousetrap has sprung. He now possesses confirmed knowledge of Claudius's guilt, which he must keep strategically concealed from the court at large even as he barely contains his exultation.

beat_states.defense_state: manic elation masks the terrifying reality that he must now actually act on what he knows — the antic disposition becomes a vessel for genuine wild energy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: ecstatic vindication at proving Claudius's guilt coexists with the dread of what must now follow — he calls for music and entertainment as if celebrating, but the celebration delays the killing he is now obligated to perform
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's call for music and revelry could be read not as triumph but as a desperate attempt to sustain the antic disposition and avoid the paralysis that will come when the euphoria fades and he must decide what to do next

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Good my lord, vouchsafe me a word with you.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Sir, a whole history.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: The King, sir—

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ay, sir, what of him?

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Is in his retirement marvelous distempered.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: With drink, sir?

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: No, my lord, with choler.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Your wisdom should show itself more richer to signify this to the doctor, for for me to put him to his purgation would perhaps plunge him into more choler.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Good my lord, put your discourse into some frame and start not so wildly from my affair.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I am tame, sir. Pronounce.

beat_states.desire_state: To deliver the King's message to Hamlet and get a coherent response
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the King and maintain his position at court by managing Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's deliberate evasiveness, wordplay, and refusal to engage seriously

affect_state.rationale: Guildenstern is increasingly frustrated and flustered. He's losing control of the conversation at every turn — Hamlet twists each phrase. His plea to 'put your discourse into some frame' is an admission that he cannot steer this exchange. He feels exposed in his inability to execute his commission.

social_state.rationale: Though nominally addressing a prince with deference, Guildenstern is functionally a servant-messenger being toyed with. His status is continually diminished by Hamlet's wordplay. Warmth is near-absent — this is transactional, not friendly.

known_facts: The King is deeply upset after the play
He has been sent by the King to speak with Hamlet
Hamlet is behaving erratically and evasively
hidden_secrets: He is spying on Hamlet for Claudius
He likely suspects the play was designed to provoke the King
false_beliefs: He may believe he can still manage Hamlet through polite persistence
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern knows his mission but does not fully grasp that Hamlet sees through him entirely. He understands the King's anger but may not fully comprehend the reason — that the play confirmed Claudius's guilt.

beat_states.defense_state: Formal deference used as a shield — maintaining courtly politeness despite frustration
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Must appear respectful and subordinate to Hamlet while actually serving as the King's agent against him

beat_states.desire_state: To humiliate and dominate Guildenstern, exposing him as the King's pawn while reveling in his confirmed knowledge of Claudius's guilt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father by exposing and punishing corruption, and to test who is loyal and who is complicit
beat_states.obstacle: Guildenstern's persistence and the social expectation that Hamlet must eventually engage with the King's message

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is riding high after the Mousetrap's success. He feels intellectually dominant, certain of Claudius's guilt, and in full command of this conversation. Each pun ('drink'/'choler'/'purgation') is a deliberate power move. His vulnerability is low because he's in performance mode, fully armored by wit.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet wields his princely status as a weapon, forcing Guildenstern to keep asking permission while he plays keep-away. His warmth is actively hostile — every response is designed to frustrate and belittle. The final 'I am tame, sir' is savagely ironic condescension.

known_facts: The Ghost's accusation is confirmed — Claudius is guilty of murder
The King reacted with visible distress to the play
Guildenstern is sent by Claudius and is not to be trusted
Guildenstern and Rosencrantz are spying on him
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius killed his father
The play was a deliberate trap to test Claudius
He fully understands Guildenstern's true purpose
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is at peak epistemic advantage. He knows everything Guildenstern doesn't know he knows — the murder, the spying, the purpose of the play. His wordplay is powered by this asymmetry.

beat_states.defense_state: Antic disposition — using wordplay and deliberate misinterpretation as both weapon and shield
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is euphoric from confirming Claudius's guilt yet must maintain the pretense of madness rather than acting directly; his 'tame' declaration is dripping with barely contained aggression
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A minority reading could see Hamlet's manic wordplay as nervous energy masking uncertainty about what to do next — the wit functioning as procrastination rather than pure triumph.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: The Queen your mother, in most great affliction of spirit, hath sent me to you.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: You are welcome.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Nay, good my lord, this courtesy is not of the right breed. If it shall please you to make me a wholesome answer, I will do your mother’s commandment. If not, your pardon and my return shall be the end of my business.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Sir, I cannot.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: What, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Make you a wholesome answer. My wit’s diseased. But, sir, such answer as I can make, you shall command— or, rather, as you say, my mother. Therefore no more but to the matter. My mother, you say—

beat_states.desire_state: To deliver the Queen's message and get a straight answer from Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position at court by serving both the King and appearing loyal to Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's evasiveness and mocking deflections refuse to let him complete his errand

affect_state.rationale: Frustrated and slightly anxious — Hamlet is not cooperating and Guildenstern senses he's being mocked. He tries to assert boundaries ('this courtesy is not of the right breed') but feels his mission slipping away from him.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to Hamlet in rank, and Hamlet is actively reminding him of that subordination through dismissiveness. Guildenstern's warmth is minimal — this is a transactional exchange.

known_facts: The Queen is deeply distressed and wants to speak with Hamlet
Hamlet disrupted the play and the King reacted strongly
Hamlet is behaving erratically
hidden_secrets: He is reporting to Claudius on Hamlet's behavior
He and Rosencrantz are instruments of surveillance
false_beliefs: Hamlet's wit is genuinely diseased rather than strategically deployed
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern has the Queen's explicit message but doesn't fully grasp that Hamlet sees through him as a spy and is deliberately toying with him.

beat_states.defense_state: Asserting formal protocol to shield himself from Hamlet's wit — framing his task as the Queen's commandment rather than his own initiative
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He needs Hamlet's cooperation but must also project authority, caught between deference to a prince and frustration at being toyed with
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern may be genuinely trying to help mediate between mother and son, not purely acting as a spy in this moment

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain his position of power over Guildenstern and Rosencrantz while controlling the flow of information
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father — and now, buoyed by the play's confirmation of Claudius's guilt, he operates from a position of certainty and moral authority
beat_states.obstacle: He must navigate the summons from his mother without revealing his strategy, while dealing with agents he knows are working for Claudius

affect_state.rationale: Riding high from the Mousetrap's success. He feels powerful and certain — Claudius's guilt is confirmed. His engagement with Guildenstern is almost playful cruelty. Low vulnerability because he holds all the cards in this exchange.

social_state.rationale: Asserting dominance through wit and evasion. His 'You are welcome' is deliberately hollow — cold courtesy that signals contempt. He forces Guildenstern to beg for a real answer.

known_facts: Claudius is guilty of his father's murder — the play confirmed it
Guildenstern and Rosencrantz are agents of Claudius
His mother wants to see him
His antic disposition is a deliberate performance
hidden_secrets: He knows the Ghost's accusation is true
He is fully sane and strategically performing madness
He is aware that Guildenstern and Rosencrantz are spying on him
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is in his most epistemically secure position in the play. The Mousetrap worked. He knows who his enemies are and who his friends are.

beat_states.defense_state: Feigning madness — claiming his 'wit's diseased' as both a shield and a weapon, using antic disposition to deflect while demonstrating contempt
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is exhilarated by the confirmation of the Ghost's truth but must suppress that clarity and play the madman; his sharp control contradicts his claimed mental disease

beat_states.desire_state: To support Guildenstern's errand and get Hamlet to engage meaningfully
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the King and Queen's interests while maintaining his standing at court
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's deliberate obfuscation — Rosencrantz's interjection ('What, my lord?') shows he's lost in Hamlet's wordplay

affect_state.rationale: Confused and slightly wrong-footed. His single-line interjection suggests he's struggling to keep up with the exchange. Moderate vulnerability — he's exposed as the slower of the pair.

social_state.rationale: Even lower status than Guildenstern in this exchange — he's the secondary figure, and his interjection is immediately seized upon by Hamlet. Minimal warmth; the relationship has cooled.

known_facts: The Queen wants to see Hamlet
Hamlet is behaving strangely
The play caused a disruption at court
hidden_secrets: He is reporting Hamlet's behavior to Claudius
false_beliefs: Hamlet may actually be mentally unwell
Hamlet still considers them friends
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz appears to take Hamlet's claim of diseased wit more at face value, genuinely confused by the cryptic non-answer.

beat_states.defense_state: Hiding behind the simplicity of a direct question to avoid being drawn into Hamlet's verbal traps
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants to appear as a concerned friend while functioning as a spy; his naïve interjection reveals he may be less shrewd than his role requires
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz's 'What, my lord?' could be a calculated prompt rather than genuine confusion — an attempt to pin Hamlet down

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Then thus she says: your behavior hath struck her into amazement and admiration.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O wonderful son that can so ’stonish a mother! But is there no sequel at the heels of this mother’s admiration? Impart.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: She desires to speak with you in her closet ere you go to bed.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: We shall obey, were she ten times our mother. Have you any further trade with us?

beat_states.desire_state: To dismiss Rosencrantz quickly while maintaining his position of power and demonstrating he cannot be manipulated through maternal guilt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and expose the corruption of the court, which requires maintaining control over all encounters
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern serve as agents of the King and Queen, attempting to manage and surveil him

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is riding high after the success of the play-within-the-play confirming Claudius's guilt. He feels emboldened and in command, toying with Rosencrantz. The slight vulnerability comes from the impending confrontation with his mother.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet asserts princely dominance throughout — 'We shall obey' uses the royal we, and 'Have you any further trade with us?' treats Rosencrantz as a merchant or servant. Coldly dismissive warmth.

known_facts: The play has confirmed Claudius's guilt
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are agents sent by the King and Queen
His mother is distressed by his behavior during the play
Gertrude wants to speak with him privately
hidden_secrets: He knows the play was a trap to catch Claudius's conscience
He is aware Rosencrantz and Guildenstern have been recruited to spy on him
He is preparing himself psychologically for a dangerous confrontation with his mother
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is at peak epistemic clarity in the play — the mousetrap has worked, he sees through his former friends completely, and he knows exactly why his mother summons him.

beat_states.defense_state: Ironic deflection — transforms the Queen's genuine distress into a joke about his own 'wonderful' qualities, maintaining emotional distance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He agrees to obey his mother ('were she ten times our mother') while simultaneously mocking the entire premise of filial obedience, revealing both genuine attachment and resentment toward Gertrude
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'were she ten times our mother' could be read as genuine filial duty beneath the sarcasm — he may be steeling himself for the closet scene rather than simply performing contempt.

beat_states.desire_state: To faithfully deliver the Queen's message and fulfill his role as intermediary
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position at court by serving the King and Queen's interests, which depends on managing Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's aggressive irony and open contempt make it impossible to have a straightforward exchange; Rosencrantz is outmatched

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is uncomfortable — Hamlet's mocking energy after the play is intimidating, and he's been reduced to a message-carrier with no real agency in the conversation. He senses Hamlet's hostility but cannot address it.

social_state.rationale: Rosencrantz has been completely subordinated. He speaks only when spoken to, delivering the Queen's words rather than his own. His warmth is perfunctory — just enough to maintain the pretense of friendship.

known_facts: The Queen is deeply disturbed by Hamlet's behavior
The Queen wants to see Hamlet in her closet
Hamlet's antic disposition during the play upset the court
hidden_secrets: He is reporting back to Claudius on Hamlet's behavior
false_beliefs: He may still believe Hamlet doesn't fully understand that he and Guildenstern are working for the King
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz operates with limited understanding of the deeper game — he doesn't know about the Ghost, the murder, or the true purpose of the play. He is a pawn with partial information.

beat_states.defense_state: Procedural formality — sticks strictly to the message without engaging Hamlet's mockery, using the Queen's words as a shield
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He presents himself as a neutral messenger while actually serving as a surveillance instrument for the crown
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz could be read as more knowingly complicit — deliberately emphasizing the Queen's 'amazement' to guilt Hamlet into compliance, rather than simply relaying a message.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: My lord, you once did love me.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: And do still, by these pickers and stealers.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Good my lord, what is your cause of distemper? You do surely bar the door upon your own liberty if you deny your griefs to your friend.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Sir, I lack advancement.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: How can that be, when you have the voice of the King himself for your succession in Denmark?

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect Rosencrantz's probing without revealing his knowledge of the King's guilt or his own plans
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder while navigating a court of spies and deceivers
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz is pressing for emotional intimacy and honest disclosure, which would be dangerous

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is freshly empowered by the success of the play-within-the-play confirming Claudius's guilt. He feels in control of this exchange, toying with Rosencrantz, but there's a bitter undertone — the invocation of past love stings because he knows it's being weaponized.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet holds the upper hand — he knows Rosencrantz is sent by the King and Queen, while Rosencrantz doesn't know what Hamlet knows. His warmth is performative and laced with irony; 'by these pickers and stealers' is a darkly comic oath that distances even as it affirms.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent by Claudius to spy on him
The play has confirmed Claudius's guilt
Rosencrantz is now trying to extract information on behalf of the King and Queen
His mother is distressed by the play's events
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius murdered his father
He is planning further action against Claudius
He sees through Rosencrantz's appeal to friendship as manipulation
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has near-complete situational awareness. His answer about 'lacking advancement' is a deliberate red herring — a test to see if Rosencrantz will relay this political motive back to Claudius, potentially making Claudius more paranoid.

beat_states.defense_state: Offering a deliberately misleading half-truth ('I lack advancement') that sounds plausible but conceals his real preoccupation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He genuinely once loved Rosencrantz and feels the pull of that bond even as he knows Rosencrantz is now an instrument of Claudius
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's claim to 'lack advancement' could contain a grain of genuine resentment — the throne was stolen from him, and this complaint, while strategic, also voices a real grievance.

beat_states.desire_state: To extract the true cause of Hamlet's disturbing behavior so he can report back to Claudius and Gertrude
