beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain favor with the King and Queen by successfully managing Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is evasive, giving cryptic or ironic answers that resist genuine disclosure

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is anxious and somewhat desperate — his mission is failing. He escalates from the Queen's message to a personal emotional appeal ('you once did love me'), revealing both tactical flexibility and genuine unease. Hamlet's 'lack advancement' answer puzzles him.

social_state.rationale: Rosencrantz deliberately lowers his status by invoking their past friendship as a supplicant, projecting warmth and openness to coax Hamlet into reciprocating. But he is outmatched in this exchange and knows it.

known_facts: The Queen is deeply distressed by Hamlet's behavior
He was sent by the King and Queen to discover Hamlet's state of mind
Hamlet has the King's endorsement for succession
hidden_secrets: His primary loyalty is to Claudius rather than to Hamlet
false_beliefs: Hamlet's 'lack of advancement' may be the genuine cause of his distemper
His appeal to past friendship might succeed in opening Hamlet up
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz takes Hamlet's answer about advancement somewhat at face value, enough to counter it with the fact of Hamlet's promised succession. He doesn't grasp that Hamlet is toying with him or that the real crisis is Hamlet's knowledge of regicide.

beat_states.defense_state: Wrapping his interrogation in the language of friendship and concern to disguise its instrumental purpose
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He may genuinely care about Hamlet on some level while simultaneously serving as Claudius's agent — he likely cannot fully admit to himself the incompatibility of these roles
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may be more self-aware about his compromised position than he lets on — his emotional appeal could contain genuine guilt about betraying a friend.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ay, sir, but “ While the grass grows”— the proverb is something musty. / O, the recorders! Let me see one. / To withdraw with you: why do you go about to recover the wind of me, as if you would drive me into a toil?

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: O, my lord, if my duty be too bold, my love is too unmannerly.

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect Hamlet's accusation and preserve the appearance of loyal friendship rather than covert surveillance
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the King's commission while maintaining enough of Hamlet's trust to remain useful and safe at court
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has directly and precisely named their tactic — the cover is blown, and Guildenstern must improvise a defense

affect_state.rationale: Caught out and exposed, Guildenstern is scrambling. His carefully constructed pretense has been shattered by Hamlet's direct challenge. He is anxious, uncertain how to proceed, and his quick resort to sentimental language betrays desperation.

social_state.rationale: He is in a distinctly subordinate position — both as a courtier to a prince and as a caught-out spy. He performs warmth strategically but from a position of weakness.

known_facts: He and Rosencrantz were commissioned by the King and Queen to observe Hamlet
Hamlet suspects or knows their true purpose
Hamlet has just directly accused them of manipulating him
The play-scene has caused a major disruption at court
hidden_secrets: The full extent of Claudius's instructions to them
His primary loyalty is to the King, not to Hamlet
false_beliefs: He may still believe he can convince Hamlet that his motives are friendly
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern knows the game is up but clings to the hope that rhetorical agility can salvage the situation. He underestimates how thoroughly Hamlet sees through him.

beat_states.defense_state: Reframing espionage as love — claiming that any overstepping of bounds comes from affection rather than duty, inverting the accusation into a compliment
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He invokes love ('my love is too unmannerly') while serving the King's surveillance agenda — the contradiction between professed affection and actual allegiance is acute and he may be partially aware of it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern may genuinely feel some residual affection for Hamlet and his line about love being 'too unmannerly' could contain a grain of authentic feeling amid the performance

beat_states.desire_state: To expose Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's manipulation and reassert dominance over the interrogation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To uncover the truth and hold all deceivers accountable while navigating his mission of revenge
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern persist in their transparent attempts to extract information from him on behalf of the King and Queen

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and on the offensive. The play-within-the-play has confirmed his suspicions about Claudius, and now he sees through his old friends with sharp clarity. The musty proverb carries a tinge of melancholy for lost patience/hope, but he is largely in command.

social_state.rationale: He is wielding his princely authority to corner Guildenstern. The warmth is gone — the hunting metaphor is openly hostile, accusing them of treating him like prey.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent by Claudius to spy on him
They are now attempting to extract information about his state of mind
The play has confirmed Claudius's guilt
His mother has sent for him
These former friends are instruments of the King
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius killed his father
His madness is performed and strategic
He is planning further action against Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's epistemic position is strong — he sees through the manipulation clearly and names it with the hunting metaphor. His knowledge advantage is near-total in this exchange.

beat_states.defense_state: Aggressive counter-interrogation — turning the hunter into the hunted by naming their tactic explicitly (the hunting metaphor of 'recovering the wind' to drive prey into a net)
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He half-acknowledges the old friendship with the proverb about waiting ('while the grass grows') suggesting he once had hope for these relationships, even as he now treats them as enemies

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I do not well understand that. Will you play upon this pipe?

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: My lord, I cannot.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I pray you.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: Believe me, I cannot.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I do beseech you.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: I know no touch of it, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: It is as easy as lying. Govern these ventages with your fingers and thumb, give it breath with your mouth, and it will discourse most eloquent music. Look you, these are the stops.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: But these cannot I command to any utt’rance of harmony. I have not the skill.

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect Hamlet's increasingly insistent request without offending him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his commission from the King while maintaining the appearance of friendship with Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet keeps pressing with escalating politeness, making refusal increasingly awkward; Guildenstern genuinely cannot play the instrument

affect_state.rationale: Growing discomfort as Hamlet persists. Each refusal feels more exposed. He senses something is off but cannot identify it. The insistence feels unusual and he's losing footing in the exchange.

social_state.rationale: Guildenstern is in a subordinate position, repeatedly having to refuse a prince's request while trying to maintain a veneer of respectful friendship. His status drops with each refusal.

known_facts: He cannot play the recorder
Hamlet is behaving erratically tonight
He was sent by the King to discover Hamlet's disposition
The play seemed to disturb the King
hidden_secrets: He is working on behalf of Claudius to extract information from Hamlet
false_beliefs: Hamlet's request to play the recorder is a simple, literal musical request
He is successfully managing the interaction with Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern is completely unaware of the metaphorical dimension of Hamlet's request. He takes the conversation at face value and does not realize he is being set up for a devastating comparison between playing a pipe and playing a man.

beat_states.defense_state: Repeated humble disclaimers of incompetence to avoid compliance without confrontation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Believes he is simply refusing a musical request while being unaware he is walking into a rhetorical trap about his own attempts to 'play' Hamlet
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern may have a slight inkling that something is off given Hamlet's persistence, but his discomfort remains pre-conscious rather than articulated suspicion

beat_states.desire_state: To trap Guildenstern into admitting he is trying to manipulate Hamlet, using the recorder as a metaphor
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose the corruption and deception around him as part of his quest for truth and justice
beat_states.obstacle: Guildenstern refuses to play, not yet seeing the trap Hamlet is laying

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in full intellectual command here, enjoying the setup of his trap. The play-within-the-play has confirmed his suspicions and he is riding high on certainty and control. The comparison 'as easy as lying' reveals controlled anger beneath the wit.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet wields his princely authority through mock-politeness, escalating his requests with increasing formality ('Will you,' 'I pray you,' 'I do beseech you') that masks contempt. The warmth is false — he is setting Guildenstern up for humiliation.

known_facts: Guildenstern and Rosencrantz were sent by the King and Queen to spy on him
The play has confirmed Claudius's guilt
Guildenstern cannot play the recorder
Guildenstern has been trying to extract information from him
hidden_secrets: He is building toward a metaphor that will expose Guildenstern's manipulation
He knows they are instruments of Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with full knowledge of the situation, deliberately constructing an analogy between playing a pipe and playing a person. His epistemic superiority over Guildenstern is total in this moment.

beat_states.defense_state: Strategic misdirection — appearing to make an innocent musical request while constructing an elaborate metaphorical accusation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Performing playful courtesy ('I pray you,' 'I do beseech you') while building toward a devastating confrontation about Guildenstern's attempts to 'play' him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may also be testing whether Guildenstern will comply with any request from a prince, probing the limits of their obedience to authority as a parallel to their obedience to Claudius

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Why, look you now, how unworthy a thing you make of me! You would play upon me, you would seem to know my stops, you would pluck out the heart of my mystery, you would sound me from my lowest note to the top of my compass; and there is much music, excellent voice, in this little organ, yet cannot you make it speak. ’Sblood, do you think I am easier to be played on than a pipe? Call me what instrument you will, though you can fret me, you cannot play upon me. / God bless you, sir.

beat_states.desire_state: To expose Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's manipulation and assert his inviolable autonomy
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To cut through all deception and false performance to reach authentic truth and justice
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern persist in their clumsy attempts to extract information for Claudius, treating him as a simple instrument to be played

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of controlled fury. The rising rhetoric ('you would play upon me, you would seem to know my stops, you would pluck out the heart of my mystery') builds with escalating indignation. He feels certain of their treachery and commands the exchange, but the oath ''Sblood' betrays genuine emotional heat beneath the rhetorical mastery. The vulnerability lies in what he's protecting — 'the heart of my mystery' — acknowledging he has depths that feel threatened.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet fully reasserts his princely dominance, speaking down to Rosencrantz and Guildenstern with contemptuous authority. The dismissive 'God bless you, sir' is a cold, final ejection — warmth has been completely withdrawn. He treats them as beneath him, unworthy even of his sustained attention.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent by the King and Queen to extract information
They are not acting as genuine friends but as instruments of surveillance
They cannot play a simple recorder yet presume to play upon him
The play-within-the-play has confirmed Claudius's guilt
hidden_secrets: His antic disposition is a deliberate performance
He knows Claudius murdered his father
He is planning action against Claudius
The full depth of 'the heart of my mystery' — his grief, rage, and purpose
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has complete epistemic clarity in this moment about his former friends' role as spies. The recorder metaphor is not just clever rhetoric but a precise diagnostic of their relationship — they are tools being used by a player (Claudius) who is himself being watched by Hamlet.

beat_states.defense_state: Counterattack through extended metaphor — transforms the recorder into a weapon that mirrors back their condescension and reveals the crudeness of their espionage
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims he cannot be played upon, yet the very intensity and length of this speech reveals how deeply their manipulation has gotten under his skin — the man who insists he cannot be fretted is visibly fretted
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A smaller reading could emphasize genuine hurt and betrayal rather than triumph — that this speech is less a victorious unmasking and more a pained cry from someone who once loved these friends and now feels the loss of that relationship acutely, with the bravado serving as cover for grief.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: My lord, the Queen would speak with you, and presently.

beat_states.desire_state: To deliver the Queen's summons and extract Hamlet from his current confrontational mood toward Rosencrantz and Guildenstern
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position as chief advisor by managing information flow between the royal family and controlling access to Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has been in a volatile, mocking, and aggressive state — Polonius must interrupt this energy and redirect Hamlet without provoking his ire

affect_state.rationale: Polonius enters a charged atmosphere where Hamlet has just humiliated Rosencrantz and Guildenstern. He carries the Queen's authority which gives him certainty in his message, but he is wary of Hamlet's unpredictable hostility. His sense of control is low because he knows Hamlet may turn on him next.

social_state.rationale: Polonius presents himself as a formal messenger of the Queen, granting him modest borrowed authority. His warmth is minimal — this is a functional, duty-bound interaction. He addresses Hamlet with proper deference ('My lord') but the relationship is strained by Hamlet's repeated mockery of him.

known_facts: The Queen wishes to speak with Hamlet immediately
The play-within-the-play has just caused a major disruption
Claudius left the performance in visible distress
Hamlet has been behaving erratically and confrontationally
hidden_secrets: He has been conspiring with Claudius to spy on Hamlet
He plans to hide behind the arras during Hamlet's meeting with Gertrude
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is primarily caused by rejected love for Ophelia
He believes he can still manage and outwit Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius is operating as an agent of surveillance. He knows the Queen's summons is part of the larger plan to extract information from Hamlet, but he misreads the depth and nature of Hamlet's awareness and intent.

beat_states.defense_state: Retreating behind the authority of the Queen — using Gertrude's status as a shield so the command is not his own
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is a man of considerable self-importance who must repeatedly submit to being Hamlet's object of ridicule while pretending it doesn't affect him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may be genuinely nervous here, entering after witnessing Hamlet's aggressive philosophical dismantling of Guildenstern with the recorder — his brevity and formality could be self-protective minimalism rather than mere duty.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Do you see yonder cloud that’s almost in shape of a camel?

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: By th’ Mass, and ’tis like a camel indeed.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Methinks it is like a weasel.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: It is backed like a weasel.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Or like a whale.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: Very like a whale.

beat_states.desire_state: To demonstrate Polonius's sycophantic servility and expose him as a man who will agree to anything
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To cut through the corruption and false appearances that surround him in the Danish court
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius keeps agreeing, which simultaneously proves Hamlet's point and denies him genuine confrontation

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a position of near-total control, toying with Polonius. There's sardonic pleasure in the game but also underlying disgust. He knows exactly what he's doing and what response he'll get.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet wields his princely status as a weapon, forcing Polonius to perform agreement. His warmth is deeply negative — this is contemptuous play, not friendly banter.

known_facts: Polonius will agree to any statement Hamlet makes
Polonius is Claudius's agent and spy
The play has confirmed Claudius's guilt
The Queen wishes to speak with him
Polonius helped arrange Ophelia as bait
hidden_secrets: He is fully sane and deliberately performing madness
He is cataloguing who can and cannot be trusted
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has just proven Claudius's guilt via the Mousetrap and confronted Rosencrantz and Guildenstern about playing him. He now demonstrates the same principle with Polonius — everyone around him is a tool of power, bending to authority rather than truth.

beat_states.defense_state: Antic disposition — using absurdist games to maintain control while appearing mad
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Contempt for Polonius's pliability mixed with a bitter awareness that this same pliability makes Polonius dangerous as Claudius's instrument
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be genuinely delaying his visit to his mother, using Polonius as a distraction to buy time before a confrontation he dreads

beat_states.desire_state: To manage Hamlet and get him to the Queen as quickly as possible
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position of influence by serving the King and Queen while managing threats to the court's stability
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's erratic behavior traps Polonius into performing absurd agreement, and he cannot redirect the conversation without risking the Prince's displeasure

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is uncomfortable and uncertain, forced into a submissive position. He senses he's being mocked but cannot be sure, and he has no way to regain conversational control without offending the prince.

social_state.rationale: Despite his high office, Polonius is reduced to a performing yes-man. His warmth is superficially positive — he's trying to be agreeable — but it's entirely instrumental.

known_facts: The Queen has summoned Hamlet
Hamlet's behavior is erratic and possibly mad
The King was disturbed by the play
hidden_secrets: He plans to spy on Hamlet's conversation with Gertrude from behind the arras
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is genuine and caused by rejected love for Ophelia
He is successfully humoring a madman rather than being exposed as a sycophant
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius fundamentally misreads the situation. He thinks he is managing a lunatic, when in fact he is the subject of a devastating social experiment proving his intellectual and moral servility.

beat_states.defense_state: Compliance as self-preservation — agreeing with everything to avoid provoking a volatile prince
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Believes himself to be the shrewd political operator reading Hamlet's madness, yet is completely the object of Hamlet's manipulation and contempt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may be more self-aware than he appears — consciously choosing to humor Hamlet as a deliberate diplomatic strategy rather than out of pure sycophancy

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Then I will come to my mother by and by. Aside. They fool me to the top of my bent.— I will come by and by.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: I will say so.

beat_states.desire_state: To dismiss Polonius and gain a moment of private acknowledgment of how far everyone is pushing him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder while navigating a court of spies and deceivers
beat_states.obstacle: Everyone around him treats him as a fool or a madman to be managed, testing the limits of his performed madness

affect_state.rationale: The aside 'They fool me to the top of my bent' reveals seething frustration barely contained. He is close to snapping — his control is slipping as the performance of madness threatens to merge with real rage. High arousal, negative valence, moderate vulnerability because the game is wearing on him.

social_state.rationale: He reasserts princely authority by agreeing to see his mother on his own terms. Zero warmth toward Polonius — cold dismissal after having humiliated him with the cloud game.

known_facts: The play-within-a-play has confirmed Claudius's guilt
Polonius is a tool of the king and queen sent to summon and spy on him
His mother wants to speak with him urgently
The courtiers — Rosencrantz, Guildenstern, Polonius — are all working to manage and surveil him
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius is guilty of regicide
His madness is performed, not real
He is steeling himself for a dangerous confrontation with his mother
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a position of maximum knowledge — the Mousetrap has worked, and he sees through every agent sent to him. The aside confirms his full epistemic awareness of the court's manipulation.

beat_states.defense_state: Bitter ironic detachment — the aside reveals his awareness that his antic disposition is being exploited, yet he must maintain it
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has been fooling others with his madness, yet recognizes with resentment that they are also fooling him — manipulating him through condescension, pushing him toward the very edge of genuine fury
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: 'They fool me to the top of my bent' could also imply self-directed frustration — that by playing the fool so well, he has trapped himself in a role that others now exploit, raising the question of whether his delay is itself a form of being fooled.

beat_states.desire_state: To complete his errand and deliver Hamlet to the Queen as instructed
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position as chief counselor by being indispensable to the king and queen
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's erratic behavior makes every interaction unpredictable and humiliating

affect_state.rationale: Relieved to finally get a straight answer from Hamlet. Low control — he has been at Hamlet's mercy throughout this exchange. Mildly negative valence from the accumulated indignity, but mostly just eager to exit.

social_state.rationale: Completely subordinated — he has been made to agree that a cloud looks like a camel, a weasel, and a whale. His brief 'I will say so' is pure servile compliance. Minimal warmth, just functional courtesy.

known_facts: The Queen has requested Hamlet's presence immediately
Hamlet has agreed to come
Hamlet's behavior is very strange
hidden_secrets: He plans to hide behind the arras in Gertrude's chamber to eavesdrop
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness is genuine and caused by thwarted love for Ophelia
He believes he can still outsmart Hamlet and discover the cause of his behavior
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius remains fundamentally blind to the danger he is in and to Hamlet's true awareness. He doesn't recognize that the cloud game was deliberate mockery of his sycophancy.

beat_states.defense_state: Dutiful servility — retreating into his role as obedient messenger to avoid further humiliation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He considers himself a shrewd reader of men, yet has just been led through an absurd exercise about cloud shapes without recognizing he was being mocked
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may have some dim awareness that he is being toyed with but suppresses it because his role demands compliance — his brief response could indicate weariness rather than pure obliviousness.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: ’Tis now the very witching time of night, / When churchyards yawn and hell itself breathes out / Contagion to this world. Now could I drink hot blood / And do such bitter business as the day / Would quake to look on. Soft, now to my mother. / O heart, lose not thy nature; let not ever / The soul of Nero enter this firm bosom. / Let me be cruel, not unnatural. / I will speak daggers to her, but use none. / My tongue and soul in this be hypocrites: / How in my words somever she be shent, / To give them seals never, my soul, consent. / “ By and by” is easily said. Leave me, friends.

beat_states.desire_state: To steel himself for the confrontation with his mother while restraining himself from physical violence
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and set right the moral corruption of Denmark, but without becoming a monster himself
beat_states.obstacle: His own murderous rage threatens to overwhelm his moral boundaries — the Nero in him could harm his mother

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of dark, ecstatic intensity — the witching-hour imagery reflects genuine bloodthirst and high arousal. He feels dangerous to himself and others. His control is low, which is precisely why he must verbally command himself to hold back. The vulnerability is high because he fears what he might become (Nero, the matricide).

social_state.rationale: Alone and soliloquizing, Hamlet occupies the position of agent and judge — high status as the one who will confront his mother. Warmth is deeply negative; he is preparing for cruelty, though his prohibition against violence reveals a buried thread of filial love.

known_facts: The play-within-the-play has confirmed Claudius's guilt
His mother has summoned him
He must confront Gertrude about her complicity or moral blindness
It is late at night — a liminal, dangerous hour
hidden_secrets: He knows the Ghost's revelation about the murder
He has been performing madness strategically
He fears his own capacity for matricide
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating from confirmed knowledge of Claudius's guilt. His epistemic clarity about the external situation contrasts with deep uncertainty about his own inner capacity for violence.

beat_states.defense_state: Self-exhortation and ritualistic self-instruction to compartmentalize rage from action — splitting cruelty into verbal vs. physical
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is simultaneously summoning bloodlust ('Now could I drink hot blood') and commanding himself to suppress it; he wants to be cruel but not unnatural, to wound with words but not deeds — his tongue and soul must be 'hypocrites'
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: An alternative reading is that Hamlet is performing this self-restraint rhetoric precisely because he has no real intention of harming Gertrude — the speech is less a genuine struggle than a theatrical self-dramatization, feeding his identity as a man of words rather than action.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: I like him not, nor stands it safe with us / To let his madness range. Therefore prepare you. / I your commission will forthwith dispatch, / And he to England shall along with you. / The terms of our estate may not endure / Hazard so near ’s as doth hourly grow / Out of his brows.

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: We will ourselves provide. / Most holy and religious fear it is / To keep those many many bodies safe / That live and feed upon your Majesty.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: The single and peculiar life is bound / With all the strength and armor of the mind / To keep itself from noyance, but much more / That spirit upon whose weal depends and rests / The lives of many. The cess of majesty / Dies not alone, but like a gulf doth draw / What’s near it with it; or it is a massy wheel / Fixed on the summit of the highest mount, / To whose huge spokes ten thousand lesser things / Are mortised and adjoined, which, when it falls, / Each small annexment, petty consequence, / Attends the boist’rous ruin. Never alone / Did the king sigh, but with a general groan.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Arm you, I pray you, to this speedy voyage, / For we will fetters put about this fear, / Which now goes too free-footed.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: We will haste us.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure his position by reinforcing Claudius's trust through displays of zealous loyalty
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To advance his social position by serving the King faithfully and being seen as indispensable
beat_states.obstacle: Having been exposed by Hamlet in the recorder scene, he has no path back to the friendship — his only viable course is to double down on loyalty to Claudius, but the memory of that humiliation fuels anxiety about his own expendability

affect_state.rationale: Eager to please but aware he is in a dangerous political situation. His piety language is performative — there's anxiety underneath the smooth compliance.

social_state.rationale: Deeply subordinate, performing warmth and devotion to the King as a way of cementing his usefulness. The warmth is strategic, not genuine.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving erratically
The King wants Hamlet sent to England
He and Rosencrantz have been tasked with spying on Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He is knowingly betraying a childhood friend for political favor
false_beliefs: That the King's concern is genuinely for the state's welfare rather than personal survival
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern takes Claudius's framing at face value — or pretends to — not grasping the deeper murder secret driving the King's urgency.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — cloaking servile compliance in the language of religious duty and political philosophy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims 'holy and religious fear' motivates him, but is actually motivated by self-interest and ambition
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern may genuinely believe in the divine right of kings and the sanctity of protecting the sovereign, making his flattery sincere rather than calculated

beat_states.desire_state: To remove Hamlet from Denmark immediately by dispatching him to England under guard
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne, his crown, and his life against all threats — especially the threat Hamlet poses as both avenger and rival
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's erratic behavior is escalating and becoming publicly dangerous; Claudius cannot simply execute him without political consequence

affect_state.rationale: The Mousetrap play has just confirmed that Hamlet is dangerous and possibly knows the truth. Claudius is frightened but channeling fear into decisive executive action. His language ('hazard,' 'fetters,' 'fear') reveals agitation beneath the kingly composure.

social_state.rationale: He is issuing royal commands with full authority, treating Rosencrantz and Guildenstern as instruments. No warmth — purely transactional, using the language of sovereign duty.

known_facts: Hamlet's play-within-the-play was a direct accusation of murder
Hamlet is dangerous and possibly knows about the fratricide
Hamlet's 'madness' may be feigned or at least strategically deployed
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are willing servants
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet and this is the real reason he fears Prince Hamlet
The England commission may carry darker instructions than a simple exile
false_beliefs: That removing Hamlet to England will neutralize the threat
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has just been 'caught' by the Mousetrap and is now operating in crisis mode. He knows Hamlet is onto him but cannot admit this publicly.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — framing the removal of Hamlet as a matter of state safety rather than personal fear or guilt
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presents himself as a decisive sovereign acting for the public good, but is driven by private terror that Hamlet knows his secret

beat_states.desire_state: To affirm the King's decision with elaborate intellectual justification and prove his worth as a courtier
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure favor and advancement by making himself essential to the King's designs
beat_states.obstacle: He must rationalize participating in the effective imprisonment of a friend, and must compete with Guildenstern for the King's approval

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is performing confidence and philosophical authority, but he has no real power. His eagerness to elaborate at length suggests he is trying to prove himself. The final 'We will haste us' is brisk compliance — the real note beneath the rhetoric.

social_state.rationale: Deeply subordinate, performing intellectual service. The warmth is directed upward at power — a courtier's calculated deference dressed as philosophical admiration.

known_facts: Hamlet has been acting strangely
The King commands Hamlet be sent to England
He and Guildenstern are commissioned as escorts/guards
hidden_secrets: He is betraying Hamlet's trust for political gain
false_beliefs: That the King's motive is purely the safety of the state
That his elaborate political philosophy demonstrates genuine wisdom rather than sycophancy
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz accepts the official narrative completely. His extended metaphor of the 'massy wheel' shows he has internalized — or performs having internalized — the ideology of absolute monarchy without questioning the King's personal motives.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — constructing an elaborate metaphysical argument about kingship to avoid confronting the moral reality of what he's agreeing to do
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His lengthy, ornate speech about the sacredness of the king's body masks the smallness and sycophancy of his actual position
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz's elaborate speech could be read as nervous overcompensation — he senses something darker at work and is talking himself into compliance

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: My lord, he’s going to his mother’s closet. / Behind the arras I’ll convey myself / To hear the process. I’ll warrant she’ll tax him home; / And, as you said ( and wisely was it said), / ’Tis meet that some more audience than a mother, / Since nature makes them partial, should o’erhear / The speech of vantage. Fare you well, my liege. / I’ll call upon you ere you go to bed / And tell you what I know.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure his role as indispensable intelligence-gatherer by proposing to spy on Hamlet's conversation with Gertrude
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain and increase his position of influence at court by being the King's most trusted advisor
beat_states.obstacle: The King is focused on sending Hamlet to England — Polonius must assert an alternative, complementary scheme to stay relevant

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is energized and confident in his scheme, feeling he has a clear plan and the King's ear. High certainty because he believes his method of spying will yield results. Mild vulnerability because he must sell the plan and maintain favor.

social_state.rationale: Formally subordinate to the King, using deferential language and flattery ('wisely was it said'), but warm in the manner of an eager, loyal servant who genuinely believes he is being helpful. The flattery is strategic but not cold.

known_facts: Hamlet is going to Gertrude's closet
The King is alarmed by Hamlet's behavior and considers him dangerous
A mother's partiality may compromise Gertrude's ability to extract truth
He has successfully spied on Hamlet before (the Ophelia encounter)
hidden_secrets: His deeper motive is self-preservation and political relevance, not just service to the King
false_beliefs: He believes Hamlet's madness stems from rejected love for Ophelia
He believes he can safely eavesdrop without consequence
He believes Gertrude will 'tax him home' and the truth will emerge neatly
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius is operating on his persistent misdiagnosis of Hamlet's condition. He overestimates his own cleverness and safety in spying, fatally underestimating the danger Hamlet poses.

beat_states.defense_state: Preemptive self-importance — he frames his eavesdropping as the King's own wisdom ('as you said, and wisely was it said') to make himself appear indispensable rather than intrusive
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He positions himself as humble servant ('my liege') while simultaneously seizing initiative and dictating the plan without being asked
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Polonius may sense, beneath his bluster, that his earlier spying attempt (with Ophelia) failed to prove his theory, and this second attempt is driven by anxiety that his credibility is eroding — making this less confident self-assertion and more desperate bid to stay relevant.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Thanks, dear my lord. / O, my offense is rank, it smells to heaven; / It hath the primal eldest curse upon ’t, / A brother’s murder. Pray can I not, / Though inclination be as sharp as will. / My stronger guilt defeats my strong intent, / And, like a man to double business bound, / I stand in pause where I shall first begin / And both neglect. What if this cursèd hand / Were thicker than itself with brother’s blood? / Is there not rain enough in the sweet heavens / To wash it white as snow? Whereto serves mercy / But to confront the visage of offense? / And what’s in prayer but this twofold force, / To be forestallèd ere we come to fall, / Or pardoned being down? Then I’ll look up. / My fault is past. But, O, what form of prayer / Can serve my turn? “ Forgive me my foul murder”? / That cannot be, since I am still possessed / Of those effects for which I did the murder: / My crown, mine own ambition, and my queen. / May one be pardoned and retain th’ offense? / In the corrupted currents of this world, / Offense’s gilded hand may shove by justice, / And oft ’tis seen the wicked prize itself / Buys out the law. But ’tis not so above: / There is no shuffling; there the action lies / In his true nature, and we ourselves compelled, / Even to the teeth and forehead of our faults, / To give in evidence. What then? What rests? / Try what repentance can. What can it not? / Yet what can it, when one cannot repent? / O wretched state! O bosom black as death! / O limèd soul, that, struggling to be free, / Art more engaged! Help, angels! Make assay. / Bow, stubborn knees, and heart with strings of steel / Be soft as sinews of the newborn babe. / All may be well.

beat_states.desire_state: to achieve genuine repentance and divine forgiveness for the murder of his brother
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his power and legitimacy while escaping the spiritual consequences of his crime
beat_states.obstacle: he cannot truly repent because he refuses to relinquish the fruits of his sin — the crown, his ambition, and Gertrude

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in profound spiritual anguish. His guilt is overwhelming (low valence), his emotional intensity is high as he wrestles with his soul (high arousal), he is deeply uncertain whether repentance is even possible for him (low certainty), he feels trapped and unable to master either his conscience or his desires (low control), and he is more exposed and vulnerable than at any other point in the play — alone, on his knees, stripped of all political armor.

social_state.rationale: Alone before God, Claudius has abandoned all social performance of kingship. He positions himself as a supplicant, low-status before the divine. There is a flicker of warmth in his appeal to angels and mercy, but it is directed upward, not toward any human being.

known_facts: He murdered his brother King Hamlet
His guilt carries 'the primal eldest curse' — the mark of Cain
He still possesses the crown, his ambition, and Gertrude as fruits of the murder
In heaven there is no 'shuffling' — earthly corruption cannot buy divine pardon
True repentance requires surrendering the gains of the offense
Polonius has gone to spy on Hamlet's meeting with Gertrude
hidden_secrets: He is the murderer of King Hamlet
He knows his prayer may be worthless because he will not relinquish what he gained
false_beliefs: A momentary final hope that kneeling and attempting prayer might still somehow suffice — 'All may be well'
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius possesses devastating self-knowledge in this soliloquy. He sees through his own rationalizations with surgical clarity, understanding that you cannot be pardoned while retaining the offense. His one possible false belief is the faint, self-deluding hope in his closing line that the attempt at prayer might work despite his inability to truly repent.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — he dissects the theology of repentance as a way to avoid the raw emotional surrender it demands
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he desperately wants God's mercy yet simultaneously refuses to give up the very things that make forgiveness impossible; he knows the prayer is hollow even as he forces himself to kneel
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: One could read Claudius as performing a calculated spiritual exercise to inoculate himself against guilt — testing whether prayer 'works' so he can move on — rather than experiencing genuine spiritual crisis. However, the raw imagery (limed soul, bosom black as death, newborn babe) and the structural isolation of the soliloquy strongly favor authentic anguish.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Now might I do it pat, now he is a-praying, / And now I’ll do ’t. / And so he goes to heaven, / And so am I revenged. That would be scanned: / A villain kills my father, and for that, / I, his sole son, do this same villain send / To heaven. / Why, this is hire and salary, not revenge. / He took my father grossly, full of bread, / With all his crimes broad blown, as flush as May; / And how his audit stands who knows save heaven. / But in our circumstance and course of thought / ’Tis heavy with him. And am I then revenged / To take him in the purging of his soul, / When he is fit and seasoned for his passage? / No. / Up sword, and know thou a more horrid hent. / When he is drunk asleep, or in his rage, / Or in th’ incestuous pleasure of his bed, / At game, a-swearing, or about some act / That has no relish of salvation in ’t— / Then trip him, that his heels may kick at heaven, / And that his soul may be as damned and black / As hell, whereto it goes. My mother stays. / This physic but prolongs thy sickly days.

beat_states.desire_state: To kill Claudius in a state of sin so his soul will be damned, not sent to heaven through prayer
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his father's command for revenge and restore moral order, but in a way that satisfies his sense of cosmic justice
beat_states.obstacle: Claudius appears to be in a state of grace through prayer, which would send his soul to heaven — the opposite of damnation Hamlet's father suffers

affect_state.rationale: Initial surge of murderous excitement ('Now might I do it pat') quickly gives way to cold, deliberate reasoning. High arousal from proximity to the act, but he channels it into intellectual control. He convinces himself he is choosing the timing rather than failing to act, lending a sense of mastery. Underneath, the vulnerability of a son who cannot act haunts the speech.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet positions himself as cosmic judge over Claudius's soul, claiming godlike authority over when and how Claudius will die. Zero warmth — he fantasizes about damning a man's soul to hell, imagining him in incestuous pleasure or drunkenness. Coldly sadistic in his projections.

known_facts: Claudius killed his father
Claudius is currently praying and appears vulnerable
His father was killed without chance for repentance ('full of bread, with all his crimes broad blown')
His mother awaits him in her closet
The ghost commanded revenge
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius's guilt with certainty after the play-within-a-play
He is aware of his own pattern of delay but cannot fully confront it
false_beliefs: Claudius is successfully praying and achieving genuine repentance (in fact, Claudius's prayers are failing — 'My words fly up, my thoughts remain below')
Killing Claudius at prayer would send him to heaven
epistemic_state.rationale: The supreme irony of this beat: Hamlet's entire theological argument rests on the false premise that Claudius is in a state of grace. Had he struck, he would have killed a man whose prayers could not rise. His false belief becomes the engine of tragic delay.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — transforms the impulse to act into an elaborate theological argument that justifies delay
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has the perfect physical opportunity to kill Claudius right now but constructs an elaborate moral framework to avoid doing it; his reasoning masks a deeper inability or unwillingness to commit murder
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Hamlet genuinely believes his theological reasoning and is not rationalizing delay but making a sincere strategic choice — that he truly wants maximal damnation for Claudius as parallel justice for his father's unprepared death. Under this reading, the speech reveals not cowardice but terrifying resolve and patience.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: My words fly up, my thoughts remain below; / Words without thoughts never to heaven go.

beat_states.desire_state: to achieve genuine repentance and divine forgiveness for his brother's murder
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his crown and queen while escaping the spiritual consequences of his crime
beat_states.obstacle: his own inability to surrender the fruits of his sin — he cannot truly repent while clinging to what the murder gained him

affect_state.rationale: This is a moment of devastating self-awareness and spiritual defeat. The arousal has dropped from the anguished prayer — he is now exhausted, deflated, certain of his own damnation. He knows his prayer was hollow. The low control reflects his recognition that he cannot will himself into genuine repentance. Maximum vulnerability: he is alone, kneeling, spiritually naked, having just failed at the one act that might save his soul.

social_state.rationale: Alone before God, Claudius is stripped of all royal status — he is a failed supplicant, a sinner who cannot even properly beg. No warmth directed outward; this is cold, bitter self-assessment.

known_facts: He murdered his brother King Hamlet
His prayer has failed — his words did not carry genuine repentance
He cannot relinquish his crown, queen, and ambition
Words without true feeling cannot reach heaven
Hamlet is going to Gertrude's closet
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet by poison in the ear
He knows his prayer is empty performance
false_beliefs: He does not know Hamlet was standing behind him moments ago with a drawn sword, deciding whether to kill him
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has achieved painful epistemological clarity about his own spiritual state — he knows exactly what he is and what he cannot do. But he is completely ignorant that he just narrowly escaped assassination, which creates a profound dramatic irony.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — he diagnoses his own spiritual failure with precise clarity as a way of containing the despair
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he possesses complete self-knowledge about his moral failure yet remains utterly unable to change it; his lucidity is itself a form of damnation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A minor alternative reading: Claudius's final couplet could carry a faint thread of defiance — having tried and failed at repentance, he may be steeling himself to recommit to pragmatic self-preservation, making this less a moment of despair and more a cold pivot back to political ruthlessness.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: He will come straight. Look you lay home to him. / Tell him his pranks have been too broad to bear with / And that your Grace hath screened and stood between / Much heat and him. I’ll silence me even here. / Pray you, be round with him.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Mother, mother, mother!

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: I’ll warrant you. Fear me not. Withdraw, I hear him coming.

beat_states.desire_state: To confront his mother and force her to see the truth of her moral corruption
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and restore moral order, which requires his mother to acknowledge her complicity
beat_states.obstacle: His mother's denial and his own conflicted feelings — he must be cruel but cannot harm her physically (the ghost's command)

affect_state.rationale: The triple repetition of 'Mother' signals extreme emotional intensity — urgency, anger, and underlying need. He has just spared Claudius at prayer and his frustration is redirected. High arousal, low control despite high certainty of purpose.

social_state.rationale: As son to the Queen he is subordinate, but he is about to invert that hierarchy and become her moral judge. Warmth is suppressed by anger but will surface as vulnerability later.

known_facts: Claudius murdered his father
His mother married the murderer
The ghost's command to leave her to heaven
He has just come from sparing Claudius at prayer
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius is guilty of regicide
His 'antic disposition' is performed
false_beliefs: He does not know Polonius is hiding behind the arras
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet enters with full knowledge of the moral situation but is blind to the physical trap — Polonius's concealment — which will lead to the pivotal killing.

beat_states.defense_state: channeling volcanic emotion into purposeful aggression
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is about to attack the person he loves most; his fury at her betrayal coexists with desperate need for her to be redeemable
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Hamlet is more calculated here than emotionally overwhelmed — that the triple 'Mother' is a deliberate intimidation tactic rather than eruption of feeling

beat_states.desire_state: To coach Gertrude into confronting Hamlet firmly and to position himself as hidden witness to the exchange
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his influence at court by managing information and controlling outcomes through surveillance
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's unpredictable behavior and the limited time before Hamlet arrives

affect_state.rationale: Polonius is anxious but feels in command of his scheme. He believes his plan to eavesdrop will yield results. Moderate arousal from urgency as Hamlet approaches.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as advisor to the Queen, asserting authority through instruction, but there is little genuine warmth — this is strategic maneuvering.

known_facts: Hamlet has been acting erratically
The King wants Hamlet monitored
Gertrude has been shielding Hamlet from the King's anger
He is about to hide behind the arras
hidden_secrets: He is spying on behalf of Claudius
His presence behind the arras
false_beliefs: Hamlet's madness stems primarily from rejected love for Ophelia
He believes he can safely eavesdrop without consequence
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius operates on his theory of lovesickness and believes surveillance will confirm his hypothesis. He fatally underestimates Hamlet's capacity for violence.

beat_states.defense_state: concealment — hiding behind the arras to avoid direct confrontation while still controlling the situation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to 'silence' himself but cannot stop talking and directing others

beat_states.desire_state: To reassure Polonius she can handle Hamlet alone and to prepare herself emotionally for a difficult confrontation with her son
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve her relationships and her sense of security — both with Claudius and with Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Her divided loyalty between Claudius's agenda and her maternal bond with Hamlet; uncertainty about what Hamlet will say or do

affect_state.rationale: She projects confidence but is internally uneasy. Hamlet's triple 'Mother' call is urgent and unsettling. She doesn't know what's coming and her certainty is low.

social_state.rationale: As Queen and mother, she holds formal authority, but Polonius's coaching subtly undermines her autonomy. Warmth is muted by the adversarial task she's been assigned.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving strangely
Claudius is angry and concerned
She has been protecting Hamlet from consequences
Polonius will be hidden in the room
hidden_secrets: The speed of her remarriage and possible guilt about it
false_beliefs: She likely does not know Claudius murdered King Hamlet
She may believe Hamlet's disturbance is madness rather than justified grief and moral outrage
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude operates with incomplete knowledge — she doesn't understand the true source of Hamlet's anguish and approaches this as managing a troubled son.

beat_states.defense_state: projection of composure — 'Fear me not' masks genuine anxiety about the encounter
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She agrees to be 'round' with Hamlet but her maternal instinct may soften her; she is caught between duty to her husband-king and love for her son
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Gertrude knows more about the murder than she lets on, which would reframe her anxiety as guilt rather than confusion

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Now, mother, what’s the matter?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Hamlet, thou hast thy father much offended.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Mother, you have my father much offended.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Come, come, you answer with an idle tongue.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Go, go, you question with a wicked tongue.

beat_states.desire_state: To seize moral authority over his mother and force her to confront her guilt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and restore moral order to a corrupted world
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude is deflecting and trying to discipline him as though he is the offender

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is controlled and sharp here, not yet at full emotional eruption. He's intellectually aggressive, using rhetorical mirroring to dominate the exchange. His certainty is high — he knows what he believes about Gertrude's guilt. Some vulnerability lurks beneath the combativeness because this is his mother.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet is claiming higher moral status than his mother, inverting the parent-child hierarchy. His warmth is negative — he is deliberately cold and corrective, refusing filial deference.

known_facts: Claudius killed his father
Gertrude married Claudius with suspicious haste
His mother has been coached to confront him
Claudius is 'my father' only by marriage — 'my father' refers to Old Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He knows the Ghost's revelation about the murder
He suspects he is being observed or tested
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's deliberate wordplay on 'thy father' vs 'my father' reveals his precise epistemic distinction between Claudius and Old Hamlet. He knows more than Gertrude realizes about the true state of affairs.

beat_states.defense_state: Turning her words back on her — using her exact syntax to reverse the accusation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He addresses her with 'mother' — an intimate, familial word — while simultaneously attacking her with verbal precision, revealing both his need for her and his fury at her
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may also be testing how far Gertrude will go in defending Claudius, probing whether she was complicit in the murder

beat_states.desire_state: To reprimand Hamlet and reassert parental authority over his dangerous behavior
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain stability, protect her marriage to Claudius, and keep her son safe from his own recklessness
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet refuses to be chastened and turns every accusation back on her with unsettling precision

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude begins with attempted authority but is already losing ground. Her 'Come, come' betrays frustration and a loss of composure. She is more rattled than she wants to appear — Hamlet's mirroring destabilizes her scripted confrontation.

social_state.rationale: She is nominally the parent and queen, giving her formal status, but Hamlet is rapidly eroding that status. Her warmth is slightly negative — she is in scolding mode, not nurturing.

known_facts: Hamlet has offended Claudius with the play
Polonius told her to be firm with Hamlet
Polonius is hiding behind the arras
hidden_secrets: She may harbor unacknowledged guilt about the speed of her remarriage
false_beliefs: She believes Hamlet's behavior stems from madness or idle rebellion rather than justified moral outrage
She likely believes Old Hamlet died of natural causes
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude operates with limited information. She does not know about the Ghost or the murder. She interprets Hamlet's aggression through the lens of madness and disrespect rather than righteous fury.

beat_states.defense_state: Dismissing Hamlet's words as foolish ('idle tongue') to avoid engaging with their moral content
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is performing the role of stern mother as Polonius instructed, but Hamlet's counter-accusations about 'my father' strike at a guilt she may not fully acknowledge even to herself
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may already sense the moral weight of Hamlet's accusations and be using dismissiveness as a conscious strategy to avoid a confrontation she fears she would lose

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Why, how now, Hamlet?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What’s the matter now?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Have you forgot me?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: No, by the rood, not so. / You are the Queen, your husband’s brother’s wife, / And ( would it were not so) you are my mother.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Nay, then I’ll set those to you that can speak.

beat_states.desire_state: To force his mother to confront her moral transgression — her marriage to Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To purge corruption from Denmark and fulfill the Ghost's command, which includes making Gertrude see her sin
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude's refusal to engage with his accusations; her attempt to reassert maternal authority

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is controlled and precise in his verbal assault, but the parenthetical 'would it were not so' betrays real anguish. He is aroused by moral indignation, certain of her guilt, and maintaining dominance in the exchange — but vulnerable because this is his mother.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet deliberately inverts the parent-child hierarchy, seizing the superior moral position. His warmth is deeply negative — he's weaponizing the relationship labels ('your husband's brother's wife') to shame her, stripping away affection in favor of accusation.

known_facts: Gertrude married Claudius, her husband's brother
The Ghost commanded him to confront Gertrude but not harm her
Polonius is hidden behind the arras
hidden_secrets: He knows (or believes) Claudius murdered his father
The Ghost's visitation and commands
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows precisely what he's doing — each word is calculated. He is aware of the moral architecture of Gertrude's situation and deploys it surgically.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual aggression — using precise, cutting language to maintain emotional distance while delivering devastating truths
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He loves his mother deeply yet is compelled to wound her; his wish that she were 'not so' his mother reveals the pain beneath the attack
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's cruelty could also be read as a desperate plea for his mother to acknowledge the truth — less attack, more anguished provocation born from a child's need for the mother to be better.

beat_states.desire_state: To regain control of the conversation and reassert her authority as his mother
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain family order and her own position while protecting her son from himself
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's refusal to be disciplined; his devastating reframing of her identity that strips her of dignity

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude begins with maternal authority ('Have you forgot me?') but is shaken by Hamlet's precise, lacerating redefinition of who she is. Her certainty drops because the confrontation has gone off-script. Her move to summon others reveals loss of control and rising fear.

social_state.rationale: Gertrude entered with high status as the summoning parent, but Hamlet has rapidly inverted the dynamic. Her warmth cools as maternal concern gives way to alarm and defensive anger.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving erratically
Polonius is hidden behind the arras as a witness
Claudius is concerned about Hamlet's behavior
hidden_secrets: Whatever degree of guilt or awareness she has about the speed/nature of her remarriage
false_beliefs: She likely believes Hamlet's aggression is madness rather than moral clarity
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude has been briefed by Polonius and Claudius to confront Hamlet, but she does not understand the depth of what Hamlet knows or the true source of his rage. Her 'Have you forgot me?' suggests she genuinely doesn't grasp that he's attacking her moral identity, not her person.

beat_states.defense_state: Escalation to external authority — calling for others ('those that can speak') when she cannot match his verbal assault
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She summoned him to scold him, yet within moments she is the one losing ground; her authority as mother is being dismantled by her own son
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's 'Have you forgot me?' could be read not as authority but as genuine hurt — a mother feeling her son has stopped seeing her as his mother, making her more wounded than imperious.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Come, come, and sit you down; you shall not budge. / You go not till I set you up a glass / Where you may see the inmost part of you.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: What wilt thou do? Thou wilt not murder me? / Help, ho!

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: What ho! Help!

beat_states.desire_state: To force Gertrude to confront the truth about herself and her moral corruption
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To purge the rot in Denmark and fulfill the Ghost's command, which includes making Gertrude see her sin
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude's resistance, her refusal to sit and listen, her potential to call for help

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is charged with furious moral purpose. He feels certain of what he must do — hold up a mirror to Gertrude — and physically seizes control of the situation, but underneath his aggression is deep hurt and a son's desperate need for his mother to be better.

social_state.rationale: He has seized dominance in the encounter, physically restraining her. His warmth is deeply negative on the surface — threatening, coercive — though the underlying motivation is love twisted into confrontation.

known_facts: Gertrude married Claudius indecently quickly after King Hamlet's death
The Ghost has commanded him to leave Gertrude to heaven but speak daggers to her
He knows someone may be spying (he has been watched before)
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius murdered his father
He is performing a deliberate moral intervention, not losing control
false_beliefs: He may believe Gertrude was complicit in the murder
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet acts with purpose — 'set you up a glass' is a planned metaphor for moral confrontation. He does not yet know Polonius is behind the arras, though he may suspect a spy is present.

beat_states.defense_state: channeling grief and rage into righteous authority to avoid feeling his own pain
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants to save his mother's soul but his intensity terrorizes her — love expressed as aggression
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be genuinely losing control here rather than acting with calculated purpose — the physical restraint could indicate a breakdown rather than a strategy

beat_states.desire_state: To protect the Queen and fulfill his duty as hidden observer by intervening in a crisis
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be indispensable to the crown and prove his value through intelligence and service
beat_states.obstacle: He is physically concealed behind the arras and cannot directly intervene — his only weapon is his voice

affect_state.rationale: Recovered from malformed string extraction

social_state.rationale: His status as clever schemer collapses into that of a desperate old man behind a curtain. His warmth toward Gertrude is genuine — he acts to protect her at his own peril.

known_facts: He is hidden behind the arras at Claudius's behest
Hamlet has physically restrained Gertrude
Gertrude is crying out that Hamlet will murder her
hidden_secrets: His hiding place behind the arras
false_beliefs: He believes Hamlet may be about to commit violence against Gertrude
He does not realize that crying out will lead to his own death
epistemic_state.rationale: Polonius acts on incomplete and panicked information. He hears what sounds like an assault and responds instinctively. He has no idea that his cry will identify him as a hidden threat to Hamlet.

beat_states.defense_state: Breaking cover out of panic — abandoning his careful hiding strategy in an emergency
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His entire plan depended on remaining hidden and silent, but Gertrude's terror forces him to reveal himself — his instinct to serve overrides his instinct for self-preservation

beat_states.desire_state: To escape Hamlet's physical grip and survive what she perceives as a mortal threat
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain her position and relationships while avoiding confrontation with her own guilt
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's physical dominance and terrifying intensity — she cannot leave

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is in genuine fear for her life. She has completely lost control of the encounter. Her cry 'Thou wilt not murder me?' is raw terror — she does not know what Hamlet is capable of in this state.

social_state.rationale: Her queenly status has collapsed entirely. She is a mother in physical danger from her own son. Whatever warmth or authority she entered with has been obliterated by fear.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving erratically
Polonius is hidden behind the arras and can hear everything
Claudius asked her to confront Hamlet about his behavior
hidden_secrets: She may carry buried guilt about her hasty remarriage
She knows Polonius is listening
false_beliefs: She believes Hamlet may actually intend to kill her
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude's cry for help is partly directed at Polonius — she knows he is there. She genuinely misreads Hamlet's intent as homicidal rather than moral confrontation.

beat_states.defense_state: Crying for help — externalized panic as a survival mechanism, also deflecting from the content of what Hamlet is saying
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She came into this scene intending to scold Hamlet authoritatively, but has been instantly reduced to terror — the reversal of power exposes how fragile her authority always was
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may be exaggerating her fear strategically to summon Polonius's intervention and regain control of the scene

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: How now, a rat? Dead for a ducat, dead.

utterances.speaker: POLONIUS
utterances.text: O, I am slain!

beat_states.desire_state: To destroy the perceived threat lurking behind the arras, hoping it is Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder and purge the corruption in Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: He cannot see who is behind the arras; his rage and adrenaline override rational assessment

affect_state.rationale: Explosive, reactive aggression — a moment of primal action after prolonged intellectual paralysis. He is barely in control. Certainty is low because he doesn't know who he's killing. The thrill of decisive action mixes with dark fury.

social_state.rationale: He seizes total dominance in the room through lethal violence. Warmth is absent — he treats the hidden figure as vermin ('a rat'), dehumanizing the target completely.

known_facts: Someone is hiding behind the arras
His mother just cried for help, suggesting she feels threatened by him
He was confronting his mother about her marriage to Claudius
hidden_secrets: He hopes the hidden figure is Claudius
The Ghost has commanded him to kill Claudius
false_beliefs: The person behind the arras is likely Claudius ('a rat' — the king who killed his father)
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's false belief is the engine of this catastrophic action. He cannot know it is Polonius, and his desperate wish to finally act on the Ghost's command fills the gap in knowledge with hopeful assumption.

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement — channeling accumulated fury at his mother and Claudius onto an unknown target
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has hesitated for months to kill Claudius in deliberate moments, yet here he kills impulsively without even confirming the target's identity
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Hamlet doesn't care who it is — the act is pure reckless violence born from self-loathing at his own inaction, and the target's identity is almost irrelevant to the psychological release.

beat_states.desire_state: To survive — to escape the lethal thrust he has just received
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect his position and family through service to the crown, now rendered instantly meaningless
beat_states.obstacle: He is fatally wounded; there is nothing he can do

affect_state.rationale: Pure shock and agony. He knows he is dying ('I am slain'). Total loss of control — from hidden observer to exposed victim in an instant. Maximum vulnerability.

social_state.rationale: His status collapses absolutely. He who sought to be the invisible power-broker is reduced to a dying body behind a curtain. There is no social relationship left — only the raw fact of his death.

known_facts: Hamlet has stabbed him through the arras
He was hiding to spy on Hamlet's conversation with Gertrude
He is dying
hidden_secrets: He was reporting to Claudius about Hamlet's behavior
He orchestrated this eavesdropping scheme
epistemic_state.rationale: In his final moment, Polonius has sudden, terrible clarity about his situation. The irony is that his entire life of scheming collapses into four words of unadorned truth.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His lifelong strategy of concealment and eavesdropping — the very behavior that defined him — has directly caused his death
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some productions play Polonius's cry as still attempting to identify himself or appeal for mercy, suggesting a final desperate tactic rather than pure declaration of death.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: O me, what hast thou done?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Nay, I know not. Is it the King?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: O, what a rash and bloody deed is this!

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: A bloody deed— almost as bad, good mother, / As kill a king and marry with his brother.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: As kill a king?

beat_states.desire_state: To discover if he has killed Claudius and to force Gertrude to confront her complicity in his father's murder
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder and expose the corruption that has poisoned Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: He doesn't know who he has killed, and Gertrude seems genuinely shocked rather than guilty, which may undermine his accusation

affect_state.rationale: Adrenaline-flooded after stabbing through the arras, hopeful it's Claudius but uncertain. The immediate pivot to accusing Gertrude is a way to seize back moral authority amid the chaos of what he's just done.

social_state.rationale: He assumes aggressive moral superiority over his mother, wielding her guilt as a weapon. Warmth is near zero — this is prosecutorial, not filial.

known_facts: He has killed someone behind the arras
The Ghost told him Claudius murdered King Hamlet
Gertrude married Claudius shortly after King Hamlet's death
hidden_secrets: He has been visited by the Ghost and knows the manner of his father's murder
He has been performing an antic disposition
false_beliefs: He momentarily hopes or believes the person behind the arras is Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: His question 'Is it the King?' reveals genuine uncertainty about who he's killed, while his accusation about killing a king tests whether Gertrude knew about or participated in the murder.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection — redirecting moral horror away from himself and onto Gertrude by equating her marriage with regicide
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has just committed an impulsive, possibly misdirected killing while positioning himself as the moral avenger of his father; the rashness undermines his righteousness
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may already suspect it is NOT Claudius (since Claudius was not in the room when he entered) and the question is partly performative — a way to introduce the regicide accusation regardless of who lies dead.

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what has just happened and to stop the escalating violence and accusations
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain family bonds and personal safety while preserving her status and emotional equilibrium
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has just killed someone in her chamber and is now accusing her of complicity in murder — she is physically and psychologically trapped

affect_state.rationale: She is in acute terror and moral shock. A man has just been killed feet from her. Her son is armed, unstable, and now accusing her of regicide. She has almost no control over the situation.

social_state.rationale: Her queenly authority has completely collapsed; she is reduced to reactive, gasping questions. There is residual maternal connection but it's overwhelmed by fear.

known_facts: Hamlet has just stabbed someone through the arras
Hamlet is in a state of violent agitation
She married Claudius after King Hamlet's death
hidden_secrets: The degree to which she suspected or knew about Claudius's role in King Hamlet's death remains ambiguous
epistemic_state.rationale: Her stunned repetition 'As kill a king?' is the crux of her epistemic ambiguity in the play — Shakespeare deliberately leaves open whether this is innocent surprise or horrified recognition.

beat_states.defense_state: Genuine shock functioning as defense — her horror at the stabbing deflects from and delays engagement with Hamlet's deeper accusation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Her final line 'As kill a king?' could indicate either genuine ignorance of Claudius's crime or a terrified recognition that her buried suspicions are being named aloud
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may fully know about the murder and her shock is partly performative self-protection, though the more commonly supported reading is that she is genuinely ignorant of Claudius's crime and is blindsided by the accusation.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ay, lady, it was my word. / Thou wretched, rash, intruding fool, farewell. / I took thee for thy better. Take thy fortune. / Thou find’st to be too busy is some danger. / To Queen. Leave wringing of your hands. Peace, sit you down, / And let me wring your heart; for so I shall / If it be made of penetrable stuff, / If damnèd custom have not brazed it so / That it be proof and bulwark against sense.
utterances.stage_direction: He pulls Polonius’ body from behind the arras.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: What have I done, that thou dar’st wag thy tongue / In noise so rude against me?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Such an act / That blurs the grace and blush of modesty, / Calls virtue hypocrite, takes off the rose / From the fair forehead of an innocent love / And sets a blister there, makes marriage vows / As false as dicers’ oaths— O, such a deed / As from the body of contraction plucks / The very soul, and sweet religion makes / A rhapsody of words! Heaven’s face does glow / O’er this solidity and compound mass / With heated visage, as against the doom, / Is thought-sick at the act.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Ay me, what act / That roars so loud and thunders in the index?

beat_states.desire_state: To force Gertrude to confront and acknowledge the moral horror of her remarriage to Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To purge corruption from Denmark and fulfill his father's command for justice
