beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude's apparent moral blindness — she genuinely seems not to understand what she has done, and her 'brazed' conscience may be impenetrable

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of high moral fury, speaking with prophetic intensity. He feels certain of his mother's sin and commands the conversation, but underneath there is the unprocessed shock of having just killed someone. His language escalates to cosmic imagery — heaven itself blushing — indicating near-manic rhetorical arousal.

social_state.rationale: He has seized total dominance in the room — commanding Gertrude to sit, dismissing Polonius's corpse, and positioning himself as moral judge. His warmth toward his mother is deeply negative; he is deliberately cruel, treating her as a moral defendant.

known_facts: He has killed the person behind the arras
It was Polonius, not Claudius
Gertrude married Claudius with unseemly haste
His father was murdered by Claudius
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius murdered his father (from the Ghost)
He had hoped the figure behind the arras was Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is fully aware of the situation — he quickly identifies Polonius and dismisses him. His moral certainty about Gertrude's sin is absolute, though he does not yet know how much (or little) she knows about the murder of King Hamlet.

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement — he has just killed a man (possibly the wrong man) and immediately pivots away from that reality to attack his mother, channeling guilt and shock into righteous fury
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has just committed a rash, violent act (killing Polonius) that mirrors the very moral corruption he condemns in others, yet he dismisses it instantly to lecture his mother on morality
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's extreme rhetorical assault on Gertrude may partly be self-directed: displacing horror at his own impulsive killing onto her sins, using moral outrage as a way to avoid confronting what he has just done.

beat_states.desire_state: To understand why Hamlet is attacking her so violently and to defend herself against an accusation she doesn't fully comprehend
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain her world, her relationships, and her sense of self intact
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's overwhelming moral fury and the shock of witnessing him kill Polonius — she is terrified and bewildered

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is in shock and distress. She has just witnessed a killing, her son is raging at her with cosmic imagery, and she cannot locate the specific accusation. She is deeply confused, frightened, and losing control of the situation. Her repeated questions betray both genuine bewilderment and rising fear.

social_state.rationale: Though she is Queen and mother, Hamlet has seized authority in this scene. She has been commanded to sit, told to stop wringing her hands. Her status is subordinated. There is residual maternal warmth but it is overwhelmed by fear and defensive indignation.

known_facts: Hamlet has just killed Polonius
Hamlet is furious with her about some act
She married Claudius after King Hamlet's death
hidden_secrets: The degree to which she may have suspected or known about Claudius's crime remains ambiguous
false_beliefs: She may believe her remarriage was politically reasonable and not morally monstrous
epistemic_state.rationale: The great ambiguity of Gertrude: her 'what act?' can be read as genuine ignorance or willful self-deception. She seems not to connect Hamlet's rage to her marriage specifically, or else she has so normalized it that she cannot see it as the violation Hamlet describes.

beat_states.defense_state: Denial — she genuinely asks 'what act?' suggesting she has compartmentalized or suppressed awareness of the moral implications of her remarriage
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is both terrified of her son (who just murdered a man in front of her) and indignant at being accused — fear and wounded dignity coexist
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may fully understand what Hamlet means but is stalling — her questions could be a tactic to deflect and diminish his accusations rather than genuine confusion. Some readings suggest she knows more about the murder than she lets on.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Look here upon this picture and on this, / The counterfeit presentment of two brothers. / See what a grace was seated on this brow, / Hyperion’s curls, the front of Jove himself, / An eye like Mars’ to threaten and command, / A station like the herald Mercury / New-lighted on a heaven-kissing hill, / A combination and a form indeed / Where every god did seem to set his seal / To give the world assurance of a man. / This was your husband. Look you now what follows. / Here is your husband, like a mildewed ear / Blasting his wholesome brother. Have you eyes? / Could you on this fair mountain leave to feed / And batten on this moor? Ha! Have you eyes? / You cannot call it love, for at your age / The heyday in the blood is tame, it’s humble / And waits upon the judgment; and what judgment / Would step from this to this? Sense sure you have, / Else could you not have motion; but sure that sense / Is apoplexed; for madness would not err, / Nor sense to ecstasy was ne’er so thralled, / But it reserved some quantity of choice / To serve in such a difference. What devil was ’t / That thus hath cozened you at hoodman-blind? / Eyes without feeling, feeling without sight, / Ears without hands or eyes, smelling sans all, / Or but a sickly part of one true sense / Could not so mope. O shame, where is thy blush? / Rebellious hell, / If thou canst mutine in a matron’s bones, / To flaming youth let virtue be as wax / And melt in her own fire. Proclaim no shame / When the compulsive ardor gives the charge, / Since frost itself as actively doth burn, / And reason panders will.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: O Hamlet, speak no more! / Thou turn’st my eyes into my very soul, / And there I see such black and grainèd spots / As will not leave their tinct.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Nay, but to live / In the rank sweat of an enseamèd bed, / Stewed in corruption, honeying and making love / Over the nasty sty!

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: O, speak to me no more! / These words like daggers enter in my ears. / No more, sweet Hamlet!

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: A murderer and a villain, / A slave that is not twentieth part the tithe / Of your precedent lord; a vice of kings, / A cutpurse of the empire and the rule, / That from a shelf the precious diadem stole / And put it in his pocket—

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: No more!

beat_states.desire_state: To force Gertrude to see the full moral horror of her marriage to Claudius and feel genuine shame and repentance
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To purge the corruption in Denmark and fulfill the Ghost's command — making Gertrude confront her complicity is part of setting the moral order right
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude's capacity for denial, her refusal to fully acknowledge her degradation, and Hamlet's own overwhelming disgust which threatens to consume his rhetorical control

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of towering moral fury and disgust. His rhetorical control is extraordinary — the sustained mythological comparisons, the systematic dismantling of Gertrude's defenses — but his escalating imagery (rank sweat, enseamèd bed, nasty sty) reveals he is losing himself in revulsion. He feels certain of his moral position but is only partially in control of the emotional torrent.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet seizes total dominance over his mother, inverting the parent-child hierarchy to become her moral judge and inquisitor. His warmth is deeply negative — he is cruel, relentless, refusing to stop even when she begs. Yet the cruelty comes from a place of wounded love.

known_facts: His father was a noble and godlike king
Claudius murdered his father and married his mother
Gertrude chose Claudius willingly
He has just killed Polonius behind the arras
The Ghost commanded him not to harm Gertrude but to let heaven judge her
hidden_secrets: His own deep sexual disgust and possible Oedipal fixation on his mother's sexuality
He may be exceeding the Ghost's instructions by psychologically torturing Gertrude
false_beliefs: That shaming Gertrude thoroughly enough will produce genuine moral transformation rather than mere traumatized compliance
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet operates from confirmed knowledge of Claudius's crimes and genuine moral clarity about the comparison between the two kings, but his belief that this verbal assault constitutes righteous correction rather than cruelty may be self-deception.

beat_states.defense_state: Projection — channeling his own anguish about his mother's sexuality and his paralysis regarding revenge into an unrelenting moral assault on her
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He claims to act from righteous moral judgment but is driven by visceral sexual disgust at his mother's body and desire, revealing an Oedipal wound he cannot acknowledge
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be deliberately prolonging the assault not to reform Gertrude but to punish her — displacing his rage at his own inability to kill Claudius onto the more available target of his mother.

beat_states.desire_state: To make Hamlet stop — to end the unbearable psychological exposure of her guilt and moral degradation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Gertrude's deepest want is to preserve her world of comfortable relationships and denial; Hamlet is destroying this completely
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's relentless verbal assault and her own emerging recognition that his accusations contain truth she has been suppressing

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is in acute psychological pain. Her three successive pleas escalate in desperation — she is completely powerless to stop Hamlet. The 'daggers enter in my ears' metaphor reveals she experiences his words as physical violence. Her certainty rises as she begins to acknowledge her own guilt, which only deepens her suffering.

social_state.rationale: Gertrude has been reduced from queen and mother to penitent and victim. She has lost all status and authority. Her warmth toward Hamlet persists — she calls him 'sweet Hamlet' even as he eviscerates her — revealing the depth of her maternal love surviving even under assault.

known_facts: She married Claudius shortly after King Hamlet's death
Hamlet is in a state of extreme agitation and moral fury
Hamlet has just killed Polonius
She can now see 'black and grainèd spots' in her own soul
hidden_secrets: The degree to which she may have been complicit in or aware of Claudius's crime remains ambiguous even to herself
false_beliefs: She may still not fully grasp that Claudius is a murderer — Hamlet's accusations may register as moral judgment about her hasty remarriage rather than as literal accusation of regicide
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude's epistemic state is deliberately ambiguous in Shakespeare's text. She acknowledges moral guilt ('spots') but her understanding of what exactly she is guilty of — lust, disloyalty, complicity in murder — remains unclear. Her defenses have cracked enough for partial sight but perhaps not full knowledge.

beat_states.defense_state: Her defenses are collapsing in real time — she moves from partial acknowledgment ('black and grainèd spots') to pure desperate entreaty ('No more!'), as denial gives way under Hamlet's onslaught
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is simultaneously beginning to see her guilt ('I see such black and grainèd spots') and desperately trying to stop the process of seeing — she wants truth and oblivion at the same time
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's pleas could be read as strategic performance of penitence to make Hamlet stop rather than genuine moral awakening — she may be telling him what he wants to hear to survive the encounter.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: A king of shreds and patches— / Save me and hover o’er me with your wings, / You heavenly guards!— What would your gracious figure?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Alas, he’s mad.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Do you not come your tardy son to chide, / That, lapsed in time and passion, lets go by / Th’ important acting of your dread command? / O, say!

utterances.speaker: GHOST
utterances.text: Do not forget. This visitation / Is but to whet thy almost blunted purpose. / But look, amazement on thy mother sits. / O, step between her and her fighting soul. / Conceit in weakest bodies strongest works. / Speak to her, Hamlet.

beat_states.desire_state: To sharpen Hamlet's resolve and redirect him from tormenting Gertrude toward completing the revenge against Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To have his murder avenged by his son and to protect Gertrude from psychological destruction
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has become distracted by moral fury at his mother, losing focus on the primary mission; Gertrude cannot see or hear the Ghost, so he can only work through Hamlet

affect_state.rationale: The Ghost is purposeful and measured — more composed than in Act 1. There is urgency but also tenderness toward Gertrude. His certainty is high: he knows exactly what must happen and that Hamlet is failing.

social_state.rationale: He commands from a position of paternal and supernatural authority, yet his concern for Gertrude ('step between her and her fighting soul') reveals genuine warmth and protectiveness.

known_facts: Hamlet has delayed the revenge
Hamlet has been attacking Gertrude psychologically
Gertrude is in psychological distress
Gertrude cannot perceive him
hidden_secrets: The full nature of his afterlife suffering
Whether his protectiveness of Gertrude stems from love or from his original command to 'leave her to heaven'
epistemic_state.rationale: The Ghost has complete situational awareness — he sees both Hamlet's deviation from the task and Gertrude's fragile state. His instructions are precise and tactical.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He commands revenge against Claudius but simultaneously protects Gertrude — the woman whose remarriage was part of the very betrayal he mourns. His love for her persists alongside the wound of her infidelity.
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Ghost could be read as a projection of Hamlet's own superego — his guilt about delay manifested as a hallucination — which would align with Gertrude's inability to see it. But the play treats the Ghost as ontologically real.

beat_states.desire_state: To receive confirmation and instruction from his father's Ghost, to understand if he has failed in his mission
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the Ghost's command to avenge his father's murder and set the world right
beat_states.obstacle: His own delay and emotional overwhelm; the sudden apparition interrupts his tirade against his mother; his fear that he has been too slow to act

affect_state.rationale: The Ghost's sudden appearance shatters Hamlet's aggressive momentum. He is flooded with awe, guilt, and desperate need for paternal direction. His control collapses as he shifts from dominator to supplicant.

social_state.rationale: He abases himself before the Ghost with reverence and filial love. His dominant position over Gertrude evaporates; he becomes a child before his father.

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared again
He has killed Polonius
He has delayed the revenge
His mother cannot see the Ghost
hidden_secrets: His deepest fear that his delay stems from cowardice or moral paralysis, not circumstance
false_beliefs: He may believe his mother can also see the Ghost
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet immediately assumes the Ghost has come to chide his inaction, revealing his awareness of his own procrastination. He does not yet realize Gertrude cannot perceive the figure.

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement — redirects all his emotional intensity from berating Gertrude to supplicating the Ghost, avoiding the guilt of his own inaction
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has just been the aggressive moral prosecutor of his mother, but the Ghost's appearance instantly transforms him into a terrified, submissive child begging for guidance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's address to the Ghost could be read as partially performative — using the apparition to further unsettle Gertrude — but the text's emotional register suggests genuine shock and submission.

beat_states.desire_state: To make sense of Hamlet's sudden shift — to understand what he is seeing and talking to
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve her world, her son, and her sanity; to avoid confronting the full horror of her moral situation
beat_states.obstacle: She cannot see the Ghost; Hamlet appears to be raving at empty air, which confirms her worst fear that he is truly insane

affect_state.rationale: Fear and distress dominate — her son appears violently insane, speaking to nothing. Yet there is a grim relief in the madness diagnosis, as it invalidates his moral assault. Her 'fighting soul' (as the Ghost describes) is in turmoil.

social_state.rationale: Maternal concern surfaces despite her defensive posture. She has lost her position of being interrogated and is now a helpless observer of apparent madness.

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
Hamlet has been accusing her of moral depravity
Hamlet is now speaking to empty air
hidden_secrets: The degree to which Hamlet's accusations have penetrated her conscience
false_beliefs: Hamlet is mad and hallucinating
There is nothing present in the room for Hamlet to address
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude genuinely cannot perceive the Ghost. Her conclusion of madness is logical from her perspective but factually wrong, and it conveniently shields her from his moral truths.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — categorizing his behavior as madness protects her from having to engage with the devastating moral accusations he was just making
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She was beginning to feel the weight of Hamlet's moral indictment ('O Hamlet, speak no more' in prior beats), but the Ghost's invisible appearance gives her an escape — she can dismiss everything as lunacy rather than confront her guilt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Gertrude can see the Ghost but denies it — a deeper act of repression — but the text and the Ghost's own words ('amazement on thy mother sits') more strongly suggest genuine inability to perceive.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: How is it with you, lady?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Alas, how is ’t with you, / That you do bend your eye on vacancy / And with th’ incorporal air do hold discourse? / Forth at your eyes your spirits wildly peep, / And, as the sleeping soldiers in th’ alarm, / Your bedded hair, like life in excrements, / Start up and stand an end. O gentle son, / Upon the heat and flame of thy distemper / Sprinkle cool patience! Whereon do you look?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: On him, on him! Look you how pale he glares. / His form and cause conjoined, preaching to stones, / Would make them capable. To the Ghost. Do not look upon me, / Lest with this piteous action you convert / My stern effects. Then what I have to do / Will want true color— tears perchance for blood.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: To whom do you speak this?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Do you see nothing there?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Nothing at all; yet all that is I see.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Nor did you nothing hear?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: No, nothing but ourselves.

beat_states.desire_state: To make Gertrude see the Ghost and share his reality, validating his mission and his sanity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the Ghost's command and set right the moral disorder in Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude cannot see the Ghost, which isolates Hamlet in his experience and makes him appear mad

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet begins the beat with concerned tenderness ('How is it with you, lady?') but escalates into desperate urgency as he points to what only he can see. His repeated questions ('Do you see nothing there?' 'Nor did you nothing hear?') reveal mounting desperation and eroding certainty. The Ghost's presence overwhelms him emotionally while Gertrude's inability to perceive it destabilizes his grip.

social_state.rationale: His status drops as he appears increasingly mad to Gertrude. Yet he shows genuine warmth — his opening question is tender, and he desperately wants to share this experience with her rather than attack her.

known_facts: The Ghost is visible and speaking to him right now
The Ghost appeared previously to Horatio and the guards
Gertrude insists she sees and hears nothing
His father was murdered by Claudius
hidden_secrets: The Ghost has just commanded him to comfort Gertrude
He fears his 'stern effects' — his resolve to act — may be undermined by pity
false_beliefs: He believes Gertrude should be able to see the Ghost if she looks
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet possesses privileged knowledge of the Ghost's presence but is confronted with Gertrude's complete inability to share it. His epistemic ground shifts beneath him — the Ghost was corroborated before, but now appears only to him.

beat_states.defense_state: Externalizing — redirecting attention to the Ghost to avoid confronting the possibility that his perception is disordered
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is torn between the overwhelming reality of the Ghost before him and the dawning realization that his mother genuinely cannot see it, which threatens his certainty about his own sanity
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be performing some degree of his distraction strategically — using the Ghost's appearance to deflect from the moral confrontation he was pressing on Gertrude, giving himself breathing room after the intensity of his accusations.

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what is happening to her son and to calm his apparent madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve her family bonds and protect herself from unbearable truths about her choices
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet appears to be hallucinating and conversing with empty air, which terrifies her and makes rational communication impossible

affect_state.rationale: She is deeply frightened by Hamlet's behavior — his wild eyes, standing hair, conversation with vacancy. Her elaborate description of his physical state reveals intense maternal alarm. Yet she speaks with some certainty that nothing is there, which gives her a foothold of sanity against his storm.

social_state.rationale: She regains slight status because Hamlet's apparent madness shifts the dynamic from accused to caretaker. Her warmth is genuine — 'O gentle son' — as maternal instinct overrides the shame of his earlier attacks.

known_facts: Hamlet has just killed Polonius
Hamlet has been savagely criticizing her marriage to Claudius
Hamlet appears to see and hear something she cannot perceive
She sees absolutely nothing in the room besides themselves
hidden_secrets: Whatever degree of guilt she feels about her hasty remarriage
Whether she suspects or knows anything about King Hamlet's murder
false_beliefs: That Hamlet is experiencing a psychotic episode or hallucination
That his madness explains and invalidates his earlier moral accusations
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude is epistemically limited — she genuinely cannot see the Ghost. Her firm 'Nothing at all; yet all that is I see' is both honest and inadvertently tragic. Her inability may reflect spiritual blindness or simply the Ghost's selective visibility.

beat_states.defense_state: Denial — she literally cannot see what Hamlet sees, which may function as psychological repression of the paternal/moral authority the Ghost represents
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is torn between genuine maternal terror for Hamlet's mental state and relief that his wild accusations have been interrupted by what appears to be madness, which lets her dismiss his earlier moral indictments
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may be lying about not seeing the Ghost — choosing not to acknowledge it as a final act of self-protective denial against the moral reckoning it represents. This reading is less supported textually but has been explored in performance.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Why, look you there, look how it steals away! / My father, in his habit as he lived! / Look where he goes even now out at the portal!

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: This is the very coinage of your brain. / This bodiless creation ecstasy / Is very cunning in.

beat_states.desire_state: To make Gertrude see the Ghost and thereby validate his perception and his father's supernatural authority
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the Ghost's command and restore moral order by forcing acknowledgment of truth
beat_states.obstacle: The Ghost is departing and Gertrude cannot see it, undermining Hamlet's credibility at the crucial moment

affect_state.rationale: High arousal from the apparition's departure, losing control of the moment as the Ghost slips away; deeply vulnerable because his mother's disbelief threatens his entire moral framework and sanity

social_state.rationale: His status has plummeted — he looks mad to his mother; but warmth emerges through the tender detail of his father 'in his habit as he lived,' revealing filial love

known_facts: The Ghost has appeared to him again in Gertrude's chamber
The Ghost is now exiting through the portal
He has killed Polonius
Gertrude cannot see the Ghost
hidden_secrets: He is uncertain whether his antic disposition has become real madness
The Ghost's commission to avenge murder
false_beliefs: That if he directs Gertrude's attention precisely enough she will be able to perceive the Ghost
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet operates under genuine perceptual certainty about the Ghost but falsely believes visual evidence can be shared with Gertrude, who the Ghost has chosen not to appear to

beat_states.defense_state: Externalizing — projecting his inner vision outward as shared reality to resist the implication of madness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Desperate certainty that the Ghost is real while sensing from Gertrude's reaction that he may appear insane, creating a fracture between conviction and self-doubt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be partly performing his vision to test whether Gertrude reveals guilt by reacting to the Ghost's presence

beat_states.desire_state: To rationalize what she is witnessing and protect herself from the terrifying possibility that Hamlet is truly mad or that the supernatural is real
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve her psychological stability and maintain her world as she understands it
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's passionate conviction and specific detail make his vision disturbingly persuasive, threatening her rational framework

affect_state.rationale: Frightened and distressed by her son's apparent breakdown; asserts certainty through clinical language but is internally shaken; attempting to regain control through rational explanation

social_state.rationale: Reclaims slight maternal authority by positioning herself as the sane adult diagnosing her son's condition; warmth is suppressed by the defensive need to invalidate him

known_facts: She sees nothing where Hamlet points
Hamlet has killed Polonius
Hamlet has been behaving erratically for weeks
She has remarried quickly after her first husband's death
hidden_secrets: The depth of her guilt about her hasty remarriage
Whether she suspects foul play in King Hamlet's death
false_beliefs: That Hamlet's vision is purely a product of madness ('ecstasy') with no external reality
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude's diagnosis of brain-coinage is genuinely held but may also be motivated reasoning — it is far safer for her to believe Hamlet is mad than to confront the possibility that her dead husband's spirit has appeared

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — reframing Hamlet's vision as a medical symptom ('ecstasy') rather than engaging with its content
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She dismisses Hamlet's vision as madness while harboring guilt that his accusations about her marriage may be valid, creating tension between denial and creeping self-knowledge
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may actually perceive something but deny it to avoid confronting the Ghost's implicit accusation about her complicity

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ecstasy? / My pulse as yours doth temperately keep time / And makes as healthful music. It is not madness / That I have uttered. Bring me to the test, / And I the matter will reword, which madness / Would gambol from. Mother, for love of grace, / Lay not that flattering unction to your soul / That not your trespass but my madness speaks. / It will but skin and film the ulcerous place, / Whiles rank corruption, mining all within, / Infects unseen. Confess yourself to heaven, / Repent what’s past, avoid what is to come, / And do not spread the compost on the weeds / To make them ranker. Forgive me this my virtue, / For, in the fatness of these pursy times, / Virtue itself of vice must pardon beg, / Yea, curb and woo for leave to do him good.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: O Hamlet, thou hast cleft my heart in twain!

beat_states.desire_state: To force Gertrude to acknowledge her sin and repent, rather than dismiss his words as madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To purge corruption and fulfill the Ghost's command — which includes saving his mother's soul
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude's instinct to attribute his revelations to insanity, which would allow her to avoid moral reckoning

affect_state.rationale: Having recovered from the Ghost's visitation, Hamlet is now intensely focused and rhetorically commanding. His certainty is high — he knows he is sane and knows her guilt. The negative valence reflects the ugliness of the corruption he describes, but he feels purposeful and in control of the exchange. Vulnerability emerges in 'Forgive me this my virtue' — a flash of awareness that his moral authority costs him his mother's love.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet occupies the elevated position of confessor/preacher to his mother, inverting the parent-child hierarchy. Warmth is low but present — 'Mother, for love of grace' reveals genuine care beneath the severity. He is harsh precisely because he loves her.

known_facts: Claudius murdered his father
Gertrude married his father's murderer
The Ghost has just appeared to him again
Gertrude cannot see the Ghost
He has killed Polonius
hidden_secrets: He is strategically performing aspects of madness while being fully sane
The Ghost's specific command to avenge the murder
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating from a position of epistemic clarity in this beat — he knows the full truth of the moral situation and is trying to transfer that knowledge to Gertrude. His offer to 'reword' his claims is a demonstration of lucid recall.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — he marshals logical proof of his sanity (pulse, offer to reword) to preempt her dismissal
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He begs forgiveness for his virtue even as he wields it like a weapon; he is simultaneously the loving son and the merciless moral surgeon cutting into his mother
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's insistence on his own sanity could itself be read as protesting too much — the Ghost's selective visibility genuinely unsettles him, and his urgent sermon may partly be an attempt to convince himself that his mission is righteous rather than delusional.

beat_states.desire_state: To end the psychological assault — she wants relief from the guilt Hamlet is forcing her to confront
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain her position and emotional survival while navigating between her son and her husband
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's relentless moral clarity has broken through her defenses; she can no longer dismiss him as mad

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is emotionally shattered. Her single line is a cry of pain — she has been broken open. Arousal is high from the intensity of the encounter. Control is very low; Hamlet has dominated the exchange. Certainty is low because she is now between two realities — her comfortable denial and the ugly truth.

social_state.rationale: She has been reduced from queen and mother to penitent. Her warmth toward Hamlet is present — she calls him by name, appeals to him with 'O Hamlet' — she is reaching out even as she crumbles.

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
Hamlet believes Claudius is a murderer
Hamlet sees something she cannot see
Her remarriage has deeply wounded her son
hidden_secrets: The degree to which she knew or suspected Claudius's crime remains ambiguous even to herself
false_beliefs: She may still partially believe Hamlet is touched by madness despite his persuasive arguments
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude's epistemic state is famously ambiguous. She is beginning to accept the moral reality Hamlet presents but may not fully grasp or believe the murder accusation. Her 'cleft heart' suggests she now knows something is deeply wrong but cannot yet process the full scope.

beat_states.defense_state: Her previous defense (attributing his words to madness) has been stripped away; she is now defenseless
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Her heart is 'cleft in twain' — she is split between loyalty to Claudius/her current life and the moral truth Hamlet has forced upon her. She acknowledges the wound but does not yet commit to action.
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's 'cleft my heart in twain' could be a tactical performance of contrition designed to make Hamlet stop his assault — an appeasement strategy rather than genuine moral breakthrough. Her subsequent actions in the play leave this ambiguous.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O, throw away the worser part of it, / And live the purer with the other half! / Good night. But go not to my uncle’s bed. / Assume a virtue if you have it not. / That monster, custom, who all sense doth eat, / Of habits devil, is angel yet in this, / That to the use of actions fair and good / He likewise gives a frock or livery / That aptly is put on. Refrain tonight, / And that shall lend a kind of easiness / To the next abstinence, the next more easy; / For use almost can change the stamp of nature / And either the devil or throw him out / With wondrous potency. Once more, good night, / And, when you are desirous to be blest, / I’ll blessing beg of you. For this same lord / I do repent; but heaven hath pleased it so / To punish me with this and this with me, / That I must be their scourge and minister. / I will bestow him and will answer well / The death I gave him. So, again, good night. / I must be cruel only to be kind. / This bad begins, and worse remains behind. / One word more, good lady.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: What shall I do?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Not this by no means that I bid you do: / Let the bloat king tempt you again to bed, / Pinch wanton on your cheek, call you his mouse, / And let him, for a pair of reechy kisses / Or paddling in your neck with his damned fingers, / Make you to ravel all this matter out / That I essentially am not in madness, / But mad in craft. ’Twere good you let him know, / For who that’s but a queen, fair, sober, wise, / Would from a paddock, from a bat, a gib, / Such dear concernings hide? Who would do so? / No, in despite of sense and secrecy, / Unpeg the basket on the house’s top, / Let the birds fly, and like the famous ape, / To try conclusions, in the basket creep / And break your own neck down.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Be thou assured, if words be made of breath / And breath of life, I have no life to breathe / What thou hast said to me.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure Gertrude's compliance — her sexual abstinence from Claudius and her silence about his feigned madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the Ghost's command by purging corruption from Denmark while preserving his mother's soul
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude's weakness of will, her entanglement with Claudius, and the risk she will reveal his strategic madness

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet has regained composure after the Ghost's departure. He oscillates between pastoral gentleness ('good night,' 'I'll blessing beg of you') and visceral disgust at Claudius's physicality ('bloat king,' 'reechy kisses,' 'paddling'). His control is high — he is now strategically managing information — but vulnerability leaks through in his expressed need for her blessing and his acknowledgment of repentance over Polonius.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet dominates the interaction completely — he is confessor, moral guide, and strategist simultaneously. Warmth is present but complicated: genuine filial tenderness is weaponized through sarcasm and shaming. The ironic flattery ('fair, sober, wise') carries a cutting edge.

known_facts: Claudius murdered his father
Gertrude shares Claudius's bed
He has killed Polonius and must deal with consequences
His madness is performed, not real
Claudius will try to extract information from Gertrude
hidden_secrets: His antic disposition is strategic craft, not genuine madness
He knows the full scope of Claudius's crime
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is now operating with maximum strategic clarity. He explicitly reveals to Gertrude the crucial intelligence distinction — mad in craft, not in essence — and must trust her to keep it. His epistemic position is strong but depends on her reliability.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — framing his cruelty and Polonius's death as divinely ordained ('heaven hath pleased it so') to manage his guilt
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Simultaneously tender and coercive — begging for her blessing while using grotesque sexual imagery to shame and intimidate her into obedience
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The sarcastic warning ('twere good you let him know') could be read as genuine doubt that she will keep his secret, revealing deeper distrust than the surface instruction suggests.

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what is required of her and to regain her son's love and moral approval
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve her family bonds and survive the moral crisis that has engulfed her world
beat_states.obstacle: She is overwhelmed, caught between Hamlet's demands and her life with Claudius; her own complicity and weakness

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is broken open. Her question 'What shall I do?' is a total abdication of agency — she has surrendered moral authority entirely to Hamlet. Her final vow uses the language of life and death, suggesting she feels existentially cornered. Arousal has decreased from the crisis peak but she remains deeply shaken.

social_state.rationale: A queen reduced to taking orders from her son. Her warmth is genuine — she wants reconciliation and gives him what he demands. The status inversion is complete: she is the penitent, he the authority.

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
Hamlet claims his madness is feigned
Hamlet demands she avoid Claudius's bed
Hamlet believes Claudius is deeply corrupt
hidden_secrets: The degree to which she may have suspected or known about the murder of King Hamlet remains ambiguous
false_beliefs: She may still believe Hamlet is partially mad despite his protestations
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude has received a flood of information and commands. She now knows Hamlet's madness is strategic, but whether she fully believes or comprehends this is uncertain. Her epistemic state is overwhelmed — she processes through obedience rather than analysis.

beat_states.defense_state: Capitulation as self-protection — total verbal surrender to end the assault on her conscience
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Her vow of silence is absolute in language but her capacity to sustain it against Claudius's pressure remains deeply uncertain
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's vow could be read as performative compliance under duress rather than genuine moral transformation — she tells Hamlet what he needs to hear to end the confrontation, without certainty she can or will follow through.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I must to England, you know that.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Alack, / I had forgot! ’Tis so concluded on.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: There’s letters sealed; and my two schoolfellows, / Whom I will trust as I will adders fanged, / They bear the mandate; they must sweep my way / And marshal me to knavery. Let it work, / For ’tis the sport to have the enginer / Hoist with his own petard; and ’t shall go hard / But I will delve one yard below their mines / And blow them at the moon. O, ’tis most sweet / When in one line two crafts directly meet. / This man shall set me packing. / I’ll lug the guts into the neighbor room. / Mother, good night indeed. This counselor / Is now most still, most secret, and most grave, / Who was in life a foolish prating knave.— / Come, sir, to draw toward an end with you.— / Good night, mother.

beat_states.desire_state: To assert mastery over his situation, signal his awareness of the conspiracy against him, and take decisive leave of his mother
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and set right a corrupt world, while maintaining moral and intellectual superiority
beat_states.obstacle: He is being sent to England under a death sentence he suspects; Polonius's body must be dealt with; time is running out

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized, almost giddily so — the crisis has sharpened him into action. He feels a sense of control and strategic clarity about Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's plot. The dark humor over Polonius's body suggests elevated arousal channeled into wit. Vulnerability is present but suppressed beneath bravado.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet commands the scene completely — he is the one with information, plans, and agency. His warmth toward Gertrude is minimal in this beat, reduced to perfunctory 'good night, mother.' He treats Polonius's corpse with contemptuous superiority.

known_facts: He is being sent to England
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern carry sealed letters — likely orders for his death
They are agents of Claudius, not true friends
He has killed Polonius and must deal with the body
His mother now knows of Claudius's guilt
hidden_secrets: His plan to turn the sealed letters against Rosencrantz and Guildenstern
His full awareness that the England trip is a trap
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's intelligence network is sharp here — he knows the conspiracy and has already begun counter-plotting. He reveals his awareness to Gertrude as a way of consolidating her as an ally while demonstrating his competence.

beat_states.defense_state: Manic wit and gallows humor as a defense against the danger closing in — turning fear into sport
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He relishes the intellectual game of outwitting Rosencrantz and Guildenstern while simultaneously being in genuine mortal danger; he makes dark jokes over a corpse he just created, mixing exhilaration with guilt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The manic energy could be read as incipient breakdown rather than strategic mastery — the jokes over Polonius's corpse may indicate psychological fragmentation rather than controlled wit

beat_states.desire_state: To process the overwhelming events of this scene and hold onto her son before he is taken from her
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve her family bonds and survive in a world whose moral ground has collapsed beneath her
beat_states.obstacle: She is powerless — Hamlet is leaving, Polonius is dead, and she is implicated in concealing what she now knows about Claudius

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is emotionally devastated and disoriented. Her brief line reveals forgetting a major political fact — evidence of cognitive overwhelm. She has almost no control over what happens next. Deep vulnerability as she faces losing her son and confronting her complicity.

social_state.rationale: The Queen, normally the second-highest status figure in Denmark, is completely subordinated here. She offers no resistance, no counter-argument. Her warmth is present as maternal anguish — 'Alack' carries genuine distress at his departure.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Hamlet claims Claudius murdered King Hamlet
Hamlet is being sent to England
Hamlet does not trust Rosencrantz and Guildenstern
hidden_secrets: She now harbors knowledge of Claudius's alleged guilt that she must conceal from him
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude has been flooded with new information this scene. She is still metabolizing it. Her forgetting about England suggests her cognitive capacity is overwhelmed by the moral revelations.

beat_states.defense_state: Emotional retreat into passive compliance — 'I had forgot' signals overwhelm and submission
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She has just been shattered by Hamlet's revelations about Claudius, yet she must now face the reality of losing Hamlet to England and navigating Claudius alone with this devastating knowledge
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Her 'I had forgot' could be strategic deflection rather than genuine overwhelm — a way of expressing dismay about the exile without openly opposing Claudius's decision

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: There’s matter in these sighs; these profound heaves / You must translate; ’tis fit we understand them. / Where is your son?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Bestow this place on us a little while. / Ah, mine own lord, what have I seen tonight!

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: What, Gertrude? How does Hamlet?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Mad as the sea and wind when both contend / Which is the mightier. In his lawless fit, / Behind the arras hearing something stir, / Whips out his rapier, cries “ A rat, a rat,” / And in this brainish apprehension kills / The unseen good old man.

beat_states.desire_state: to discover what has happened and assess the threat Hamlet poses to him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to secure his throne, his life, and his marriage against all threats
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude is visibly distressed and may be withholding information or shifting loyalties

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is alarmed — Gertrude's sighs signal something terrible. Upon hearing Hamlet killed Polonius, his mind races to the realization that he himself could have been behind that arras. High arousal from threat, low certainty about what Gertrude knows and what Hamlet knows.

social_state.rationale: He commands as king ('you must translate') but also performs marital warmth with 'What, Gertrude?' — his status is maintained but shaken by the destabilizing news.

known_facts: Hamlet has been behaving erratically
Something terrible has just happened involving Hamlet
Gertrude is deeply shaken
Hamlet killed Polonius behind the arras
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He fears Hamlet may know about the murder
false_beliefs: He may initially hope the 'madness' explanation means Hamlet acted randomly rather than with targeted intent
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius learns in this beat that Hamlet has killed Polonius. He immediately processes this through the lens of his own guilt and vulnerability — the killing could have been him.

beat_states.defense_state: performing concerned husband to mask his fear that Hamlet knows about the murder
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: presents himself as a caring stepfather and husband while his primary concern is whether Hamlet's violence is directed at him specifically
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may already suspect before Gertrude speaks that Hamlet has done something violent, and his questions are less genuine inquiry than strategic positioning to control the narrative.

beat_states.desire_state: to protect Hamlet by framing the killing as madness rather than intentional murder
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to preserve her son and maintain her position while managing the impossible tension between husband and child
beat_states.obstacle: She must relay devastating news to Claudius without exposing Hamlet's lucidity or his accusations against the King

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is genuinely shattered — she witnessed a murder and received shattering accusations from her son. Her sighs and heaves are real grief, but she must channel them strategically. Extremely high vulnerability as she navigates between Hamlet's demands and Claudius's power.

social_state.rationale: She dismisses Rosencrantz and Guildenstern with authority but immediately becomes supplicant to Claudius ('mine own lord'). She is subordinating herself to deliver difficult news carefully.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Hamlet accused Claudius of murdering King Hamlet
Hamlet may or may not be truly mad
The ghost appeared in the closet scene
hidden_secrets: Hamlet's accusations against Claudius
That Hamlet may be more lucid than mad
Her promise to Hamlet to keep his secrets
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude now holds explosive information from the closet scene. She chooses to emphasize 'mad as the sea and wind' — a deliberate editorial choice that conceals Hamlet's purposefulness and his revelations about Claudius.

beat_states.defense_state: constructing a narrative of Hamlet's insanity to shield him from the consequences of the killing and to conceal what he told her in the closet scene
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is traumatized by what Hamlet revealed and did, yet she must perform loyal wife to the man her son accused of murder while simultaneously protecting that son
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may genuinely believe Hamlet is mad and not be strategically protecting him — her emphasis on his madness could reflect her own inability to accept his accusations as truth.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: O heavy deed! / It had been so with us, had we been there. / His liberty is full of threats to all— / To you yourself, to us, to everyone. / Alas, how shall this bloody deed be answered? / It will be laid to us, whose providence / Should have kept short, restrained, and out of haunt / This mad young man. But so much was our love, / We would not understand what was most fit, / But, like the owner of a foul disease, / To keep it from divulging, let it feed / Even on the pith of life. Where is he gone?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: To draw apart the body he hath killed, / O’er whom his very madness, like some ore / Among a mineral of metals base, / Shows itself pure: he weeps for what is done.

beat_states.desire_state: To frame Hamlet's killing of Polonius as a public danger that reflects on Claudius's negligence rather than his own guilt, and to determine Hamlet's location for containment
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne, his life, and his power by neutralizing the threat Hamlet poses
beat_states.obstacle: The murder will be publicly blamed on Claudius's failure to restrain Hamlet; Gertrude's loyalty to Hamlet complicates swift action

affect_state.rationale: Genuinely alarmed that Hamlet nearly killed him; high arousal from the proximity of death. His control is slipping — Polonius's murder proves Hamlet is capable of regicide. The calculating mind is racing but the emotional ground is shaken.

social_state.rationale: Maintains royal authority in his language ('us,' 'our providence') but warmth is performative — his concern for others is strategic framing. He is already building the political narrative.

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
Hamlet was in Gertrude's chamber
This act could have been directed at Claudius himself
Public opinion will hold the King responsible for not restraining Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet and fears Hamlet knows
His primary concern is self-preservation, not grief for Polonius
He is already calculating how to dispose of Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is a supremely accurate political reader. He instantly grasps the PR disaster and the personal danger. No false beliefs — his assessment is ruthlessly clear.

beat_states.defense_state: Projection and displacement — reframes his own existential terror as public-spirited concern; disguises self-preservation as responsible governance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims the killing could have been his own ('had we been there') while simultaneously positioning himself as the victim of his own excessive love/leniency toward Hamlet — a love that never existed
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Could be read as genuinely shaken and momentarily sincere in his self-reproach about not restraining Hamlet, but the speed of his political calculation argues against genuine remorse.

beat_states.desire_state: To protect Hamlet by emphasizing his madness and remorse, softening the implications of the murder
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve her son and maintain the fragile equilibrium of her world between husband and child
beat_states.obstacle: Polonius is dead and the evidence points to Hamlet as a dangerous killer; she cannot deny the act

affect_state.rationale: Deeply shaken, grieving the situation, caught between terror at what Hamlet has done and maternal protectiveness. Low certainty — she is improvising a narrative under extreme pressure. High vulnerability as she is exposed between two dangerous men.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate in this exchange — she is answering the King's questions, providing information. Her warmth is directed toward the absent Hamlet through her defense of him, not toward Claudius.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Hamlet wept after the killing
Hamlet has dragged the body away
Hamlet told her to conceal his antic disposition
hidden_secrets: Hamlet may not be truly mad — she is keeping his secret per his instruction
Hamlet accused Claudius of murdering King Hamlet
The full content of the closet scene conversation
false_beliefs: That emphasizing Hamlet's madness will protect him from severe consequences
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude now holds explosive knowledge from the closet scene but is choosing to conceal it. Her false belief is tactical optimism — she underestimates how far Claudius will go.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — she reframes Hamlet's madness as containing pure nobility ('like some ore among a mineral of metals base'), turning his insanity into a sign of goodness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She has just been devastated by Hamlet's accusations and witnessed his violence, yet she now defends him to Claudius; she is honoring Hamlet's request to conceal his sanity while genuinely unsure whether he is mad
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Gertrude is not fully honoring Hamlet's request and is genuinely convinced of his madness, making her defense sincere rather than strategic concealment.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: O Gertrude, come away! / The sun no sooner shall the mountains touch / But we will ship him hence; and this vile deed / We must with all our majesty and skill / Both countenance and excuse.— Ho, Guildenstern! / Friends both, go join you with some further aid. / Hamlet in madness hath Polonius slain, / And from his mother’s closet hath he dragged him. / Go seek him out, speak fair, and bring the body / Into the chapel. I pray you, haste in this. / Come, Gertrude, we’ll call up our wisest friends / And let them know both what we mean to do / And what’s untimely done. / Whose whisper o’er the world’s diameter, / As level as the cannon to his blank / Transports his poisoned shot, may miss our name / And hit the woundless air. O, come away! / My soul is full of discord and dismay.

beat_states.desire_state: To contain the crisis immediately — remove Hamlet, manage the narrative of Polonius's death, and protect his own position from scandal
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his hold on the throne and keep the secret of his own fratricide buried
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's unpredictable madness, the murdered body of Polonius that must be explained, and the risk that public whisper will turn against the crown

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in crisis mode — genuinely frightened that the killing could have been him, urgent and agitated, issuing rapid commands to reassert control but betraying deep anxiety in his final admission of discord. His certainty is low because he cannot predict how the public or court will respond.

social_state.rationale: He asserts full royal authority — summoning Rosencrantz, Guildenstern, 'wisest friends,' invoking 'our majesty and skill.' Warmth toward Gertrude is perfunctory ('Come, Gertrude') — she is being managed more than comforted. His language toward Hamlet ('vile deed,' 'madness') is cold and distancing.

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
Hamlet dragged the body from Gertrude's closet
Hamlet intended the thrust for someone behind the arras — possibly Claudius himself
Public rumor could destroy the crown's reputation
Hamlet must be removed from Denmark quickly
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet and usurped the throne
He has been considering Hamlet's elimination as a political necessity
His concern for 'our name' is primarily self-preservation, not justice for Polonius
false_beliefs: That framing this as Hamlet's madness will be sufficient to contain the political fallout
That he can 'countenance and excuse' the deed with mere spin
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius rapidly calculates political consequences. He understands the danger to his reputation but may underestimate how Laertes and the public will react. His 'false beliefs' are strategic miscalculations — his PR instinct is strong but the situation will spiral beyond his control.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — reframing Hamlet's act as madness rather than political threat, and casting himself as the responsible sovereign managing a crisis rather than a guilty man eliminating a rival
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He frames Hamlet's deed as 'vile' while knowing his own fratricide is far worse; he performs decisive authority while his final couplet confesses inner 'discord and dismay'
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A reading where Claudius's 'discord and dismay' is less genuine vulnerability and more performed distress for Gertrude's benefit — keeping her allied to him by showing he shares her anguish, while internally he is already coldly calculating Hamlet's death in England.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Safely stowed.

utterances.speaker: GENTLEMEN
utterances.text: Hamlet! Lord Hamlet!

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: But soft, what noise? Who calls on Hamlet? O, here they come.

beat_states.desire_state: To locate Hamlet and bring him before the King as instructed
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the crown and maintain order in a court destabilized by Hamlet's actions
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's unpredictability and his antic disposition make him difficult to manage or confront directly

affect_state.rationale: They are anxious and urgently searching. They lack certainty about what Hamlet has done or how he will respond. Their control is low — they are agents of others, not autonomous actors.

social_state.rationale: They are subordinate to Hamlet in rank yet tasked with a quasi-authoritative mission. Their warmth is neutral — dutiful rather than personal.

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
The King has ordered them to find Hamlet and the body
Hamlet has been behaving erratically
false_beliefs: They may believe Hamlet can be reasoned with through conventional courtly appeal
epistemic_state.rationale: They know the basic facts of the crisis but not where the body is hidden or what Hamlet's mental state truly is.

beat_states.defense_state: Maintaining formal address ('Lord Hamlet') to assert institutional authority and keep the encounter controlled
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: They must confront a prince — someone who outranks them socially — while acting on orders that imply he is dangerous or guilty
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Gentlemen may be more fearful than dutiful, genuinely uncertain whether Hamlet is dangerous to them personally

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain composure and control over the situation after hiding Polonius's body
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder while navigating the corrupt court without being destroyed
beat_states.obstacle: The courtiers are already pursuing him, threatening to expose or apprehend him before he can manage the narrative

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is riding an adrenaline high after hiding the body, maintaining a dark humor that masks real anxiety. He knows pursuit is coming but hasn't fully reckoned with consequences. His control is moderate — he's improvising.

social_state.rationale: His status is compromised — he's a prince who has just killed a councillor and is being hunted by servants. His warmth is low; he treats the approaching gentlemen with detached irony rather than openness.

known_facts: He has killed Polonius
He has hidden the body
The court will be searching for him and the body
Claudius will use this against him
hidden_secrets: The location of Polonius's body
His knowledge of Claudius's guilt in King Hamlet's murder
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is highly aware of his situation and the political danger. He knows he must conceal the body's location to maintain whatever leverage he can.

beat_states.defense_state: Adopting a sardonic, performative casualness to mask the gravity of what he has just done
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has just committed a violent act (killing Polonius) yet speaks with eerie lightness ('Safely stowed'), oscillating between genuine danger-awareness and antic disposition
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be genuinely rattled and the lightness is not strategic but a dissociative response to the trauma of killing Polonius

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: What have you done, my lord, with the dead body?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Compounded it with dust, whereto ’tis kin.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Tell us where ’tis, that we may take it thence / And bear it to the chapel.

beat_states.desire_state: To refuse to reveal the location of Polonius's body while toying with Rosencrantz
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father while exposing the corruption and duplicity of Claudius's court
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz is pressing him directly for information he doesn't want to give

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and in command of this exchange. He relishes the wordplay and the power of withholding information. Post-killing adrenaline gives him a dark, manic edge. He feels relatively secure in his ability to manipulate this encounter.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet asserts princely dominance over Rosencrantz, treating him with contemptuous dismissal disguised as wit. There is no warmth — he regards Rosencrantz as a tool of Claudius, undeserving of honest engagement.

known_facts: He has killed Polonius and hidden the body
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are agents of Claudius
Revealing the body's location gives Claudius leverage over him
His 'antic disposition' gives him cover to speak obliquely
hidden_secrets: The exact location of Polonius's body
His awareness that Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are being used against him
His strategic calculation behind every evasion
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has full awareness of the dynamics at play. He knows exactly what Rosencrantz is after and why, and he deliberately withholds while performing philosophical obscurity.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — transforming a grisly reality into a philosophical riddle about mortality and dust
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs antic madness and wit while operating with precise strategic awareness of his situation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may genuinely be in a dissociative or manic state post-killing, and the wit is less controlled strategy than psychic overflow from the trauma of having murdered Polonius

beat_states.desire_state: To extract the location of Polonius's body and fulfill his assignment from Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the King faithfully and maintain his position at court
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is being deliberately evasive and cryptic, refusing to give a straight answer

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is anxious and unsettled — he is in the presence of someone who just killed a man and is now speaking in riddles. He feels pressure from Claudius to produce results but has no leverage over Hamlet. His uncertainty is high.

social_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is subordinate to Hamlet in rank and in this exchange's power dynamic. He offers minimal surface warmth through formal politeness but there is no genuine connection — he is performing a function.

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
The body needs to be recovered and brought to the chapel
Claudius has sent him to find out where the body is
Hamlet is behaving erratically
hidden_secrets: His primary loyalty is to Claudius, not to Hamlet
false_beliefs: That persistent direct questioning will eventually get Hamlet to cooperate
That Hamlet's evasions may be genuine madness rather than calculated strategy
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz understands the surface situation but fundamentally misreads Hamlet's capacity for strategic deception. He likely interprets Hamlet's cryptic answer as madness rather than deliberate withholding.

beat_states.defense_state: Maintaining a veneer of respectful formality ('my lord') to manage the danger of confronting a prince who has just committed murder
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must act as both friend and interrogator — addressing Hamlet with courtesy while functioning as Claudius's agent extracting information
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may have more genuine concern for Hamlet than is apparent — his urgency about the chapel could reflect some residual friendship mixed with duty

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Do not believe it.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Believe what?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: That I can keep your counsel and not mine own. Besides, to be demanded of a sponge, what replication should be made by the son of a king?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Take you me for a sponge, my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Ay, sir, that soaks up the King’s countenance, his rewards, his authorities. But such officers do the King best service in the end. He keeps them like an ape an apple in the corner of his jaw, first mouthed, to be last swallowed. When he needs what you have gleaned, it is but squeezing you, and, sponge, you shall be dry again.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: I understand you not, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I am glad of it. A knavish speech sleeps in a foolish ear.

beat_states.desire_state: To expose Rosencrantz as a tool of the King and assert dominance over the interrogation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To resist the corrupt machinery of Claudius's court and maintain his own agency against those who would manipulate him
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are sent to extract the body's location and bring him to Claudius

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and in command of the exchange, taking clear pleasure in the sponge metaphor and its elaboration. He feels intellectually superior and relatively safe in this moment, though the murder of Polonius has placed him in genuine danger.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet wields his royal status explicitly ('the son of a king') while treating Rosencrantz with contempt, calling him a sponge and dismissing his comprehension as a 'foolish ear.' There is zero warmth — this is a former friend now treated as an enemy agent.

known_facts: He has killed Polonius and hidden the body
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern serve Claudius and are sent to extract information
Claudius will discard them when they are no longer useful
He is a prince being interrogated by those beneath his station
hidden_secrets: The location of Polonius's body
The depth of his strategic calculation behind his 'antic disposition'
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet sees through the situation with penetrating clarity — he knows exactly what Rosencrantz is doing and why, and his sponge metaphor reveals a sophisticated understanding of court politics and Claudius's instrumentalization of people.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through riddling language and metaphor — refusing to answer directly while turning the interrogation back on the interrogator
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Enjoys the intellectual sport of humiliating former friends even as he is increasingly isolated and endangered by his actions
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's aggression toward Rosencrantz could be read as displaced guilt and anxiety about killing Polonius, channeled into intellectual cruelty rather than genuine strategic dominance.

beat_states.desire_state: To extract the location of Polonius's body and bring Hamlet to the King as instructed
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the King faithfully and maintain his own position at court
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's riddling, contemptuous speech makes it impossible to get a straight answer or maintain control of the conversation

affect_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is off-balance throughout. He asks clarifying questions that only invite further humiliation. His final claim of incomprehension reads as a defensive retreat. He is failing at his task and being stripped of dignity.

social_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is subordinate both by rank and by the dynamics of this exchange. He attempts a neutral, professional tone but is continuously demoted by Hamlet's attacks. Whatever residual warmth of friendship once existed has been destroyed.

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
The King has sent him to find the body and bring Hamlet
Hamlet is behaving erratically and speaking in riddles
hidden_secrets: The extent to which he knowingly serves as Claudius's spy against his former friend
false_beliefs: That his service to Claudius is straightforwardly honorable and will be rewarded
That Hamlet's riddling speech is mere madness rather than pointed truth
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz operates with limited self-awareness about his own instrumentalization. Hamlet's sponge metaphor is prophetically accurate — Rosencrantz will indeed be discarded — but Rosencrantz either cannot or will not grasp this.

beat_states.defense_state: Feigning incomprehension ('I understand you not, my lord') to avoid engaging with Hamlet's humiliating truths about his servility
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims not to understand Hamlet's meaning, but the sponge metaphor is devastatingly clear — his 'not understanding' may be willful denial of an uncomfortable truth about his own instrumentalization
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may genuinely not understand the metaphor, being less intelligent than Hamlet assumes, rather than willfully denying the truth — his confusion could be authentic rather than defensive.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: My lord, you must tell us where the body is and go with us to the King.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: The body is with the King, but the King is not with the body. The King is a thing—

utterances.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
utterances.text: A “ thing,” my lord?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Of nothing. Bring me to him. Hide fox, and all after!

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what Hamlet means and assess the danger of the situation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve Claudius's interests while navigating a volatile situation with a potentially dangerous prince
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's language is cryptic and possibly threatening; after the recorder humiliation and the King's mounting alarm, Guildenstern now approaches Hamlet with real fear rather than mere confusion — he cannot parse whether Hamlet is mad, mocking, or genuinely dangerous

affect_state.rationale: Unsettled and confused. His brief interjection shows someone trying to get bearings in a conversation that has become genuinely disorienting. Less overtly frustrated than Rosencrantz but more intellectually disturbed.

social_state.rationale: Slightly more deferential than Rosencrantz — his question is genuinely curious rather than commanding. Still addressed as an inferior by Hamlet's contemptuous wordplay.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
The body is hidden
They must bring Hamlet to the King
false_beliefs: That pressing Hamlet for clarification will yield useful information
epistemic_state.rationale: Guildenstern senses there is meaning in Hamlet's riddle about the King being 'a thing' — his question shows he caught the potentially treasonous implication — but he cannot fully decode it.

beat_states.defense_state: Cautious interrogation — asking for clarification rather than making demands, letting Rosencrantz take the more exposed position
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is drawn to understand Hamlet's meaning ('A thing, my lord?') but fears what the answer might reveal
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Guildenstern's 'A thing, my lord?' could be an attempt to get Hamlet to say something explicitly treasonous that could be reported to Claudius, making it less innocent curiosity and more a trap

beat_states.desire_state: To evade giving a direct answer about Polonius's body while taunting Rosencrantz and Guildenstern with philosophical riddles that expose the hollowness of Claudius's kingship
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and expose the corruption of Claudius's reign, maintaining his freedom to act
beat_states.obstacle: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are pressing him with increasing urgency and authority to reveal the body's location and submit to the King

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a manic, high-energy state, enjoying the game of wits. His riddling shows intellectual pleasure but also nervous energy — he knows the stakes are rising. He controls the conversation but not his ultimate fate.

social_state.rationale: He asserts dominance by treating Rosencrantz and Guildenstern as inferiors unworthy of straight answers. His contempt for them as Claudius's pawns is barely veiled. The 'Hide fox' line turns the whole situation into a child's game, further diminishing them.

known_facts: He has killed Polonius and hidden the body
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are agents of Claudius
Claudius is a usurper — 'the King is a thing of nothing'
He will eventually be brought before Claudius
hidden_secrets: The location of Polonius's body
His knowledge that Claudius murdered his father
The full depth of his contempt for Claudius's legitimacy encoded in 'The body is with the King, but the King is not with the body'
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's riddling statement contains layered meaning — 'the body is with the King' likely refers to his father's dead body being with the dead true king, while 'the King is not with the body' suggests Claudius lacks the true body/substance of kingship. He knows more than anyone present and uses ambiguity strategically.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through riddling paradox and antic disposition — using wordplay to maintain control and avoid compliance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is simultaneously exhilarated by his verbal dominance and aware that he is being cornered; his playful 'Hide fox, and all after!' suggests both glee and the desperate energy of a hunted creature
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'Hide fox, and all after' and his willingness to be brought to Claudius could indicate he is already planning his next confrontation and wants to face Claudius, using apparent submission as a tactical choice rather than pure evasion

beat_states.desire_state: To compel Hamlet to reveal the body's location and bring him obediently to Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the King's commission and maintain his position at court through loyal service
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet refuses to answer directly, speaks in incomprehensible riddles, and treats the situation as a game

affect_state.rationale: Frustrated and increasingly anxious. His direct, firm statement ('you must tell us') betrays desperation — previous approaches have failed. He's losing control of the situation and knows Claudius will hold him accountable.

social_state.rationale: Attempting to claim borrowed authority from Claudius but clearly subordinate to Hamlet in wit and rank. His warmth has evaporated — this is now purely transactional.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
The body has been hidden somewhere
The King wants the body found and Hamlet brought to him
false_beliefs: That Hamlet's strange talk may be genuine madness rather than deliberate evasion
That asserting the King's authority will compel Hamlet's compliance
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz lacks the interpretive framework to decode Hamlet's riddles about kingship and legitimacy. He takes Hamlet's words at face value as nonsense.

beat_states.defense_state: Invoking royal authority — 'go with us to the King' — and calling for backup guards to compensate for his personal inability to control Hamlet
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must assert authority over a prince who outranks him socially, creating an impossible tension between duty and deference
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may partially understand he is being mocked and feel genuine fear about what Hamlet might do, given that Hamlet has just killed a man

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: I have sent to seek him and to find the body. / How dangerous is it that this man goes loose! / Yet must not we put the strong law on him. / He’s loved of the distracted multitude, / Who like not in their judgment, but their eyes; / And, where ’tis so, th’ offender’s scourge is weighed, / But never the offense. To bear all smooth and even, / This sudden sending him away must seem / Deliberate pause. Diseases desperate grown / By desperate appliance are relieved / Or not at all. / How now, what hath befallen?
