beat_states.desire_state: To manage the crisis of Polonius's murder by finding the body, controlling the narrative, and expediting Hamlet's removal from Denmark
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure and maintain his grip on the throne and his own survival against all threats
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's popularity with the common people makes direct punishment politically dangerous; the body has not yet been found; Hamlet is loose and unpredictable

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in a state of high anxiety — Hamlet has killed Polonius and is running loose. His language of 'dangerous' and 'desperate' diseases reveals genuine fear beneath the political calculus. He's trying to project control but the situation is slipping — he doesn't have the body, doesn't have Hamlet, and can't use the law against him.

social_state.rationale: He speaks as king to attendants, maintaining royal authority and commanding searches, but there is no warmth — this is cold political strategizing. His status is high positionally but under genuine threat from Hamlet's popularity with the 'distracted multitude.'

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
The body of Polonius has not been found
Hamlet is at large somewhere in the castle
Hamlet is loved by the common people
Direct legal punishment of Hamlet would cause public backlash
He has already decided to send Hamlet away
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet and usurped the throne
His plan to send Hamlet to England likely involves having him killed
His concern for 'justice' is entirely self-serving
false_beliefs: That his political maneuvering can fully contain the threat Hamlet poses
That framing Hamlet's exile as 'deliberate pause' will fool the public
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has accurate intelligence about the immediate crisis but is potentially underestimating Hamlet's cunning. He conceals his deepest guilt and his lethal intentions for Hamlet behind the mask of a concerned sovereign managing a crisis.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — converting his fear and guilt into calculated political strategy and medical metaphor
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He frames himself as a measured ruler acting for the common good, but is actually a murderer desperate to eliminate the one person who threatens to expose him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A slightly more sympathetic reading: Claudius genuinely feels the weight of kingship and the impossible bind of managing a dangerous stepson he cannot legally touch — making this less pure villainy and more a cornered political animal acting from survival instinct.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Where the dead body is bestowed, my lord, / We cannot get from him.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: But where is he?

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Without, my lord; guarded, to know your pleasure.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Bring him before us.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Ho! Bring in the lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To get Hamlet before him immediately so he can interrogate him about Polonius's body and execute his plan to send him to England
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne and eliminate Hamlet as a threat to his power and safety
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's defiance and unpredictability — he refuses to reveal where the body is, and Claudius must manage this crisis while appearing kingly rather than panicked

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is alarmed that Hamlet won't reveal the body's location — this complicates his public management of the crisis. His terse, clipped commands ('But where is he?' / 'Bring him before us') reveal controlled urgency. He maintains surface authority but knows he's dealing with someone who could expose him.

social_state.rationale: Fully in sovereign mode, issuing commands without pleasantries. No warmth — this is crisis management. He treats Rosencrantz as a pure instrument.

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
Hamlet has hidden the body and refuses to reveal its location
Hamlet is under guard outside
Hamlet is dangerous and popular with the common people
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He is already planning to send Hamlet to England to be killed
His concern about Hamlet is as much about self-preservation as justice
false_beliefs: He may believe he can still fully control this situation through political maneuvering
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is operating with substantial information but cannot know Hamlet's full state of mind or what Hamlet may reveal publicly. His secret guilt drives every calculation.

beat_states.defense_state: Projecting calm authority to mask his urgency and fear
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He needs to appear in measured control as king while internally desperate to neutralize a man who knows his deepest secret and has just committed murder

beat_states.desire_state: To report accurately and serve the King's will efficiently, maintaining his standing as a useful courtier
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To advance himself at court by being indispensable to power
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's refusal to cooperate has made his mission only partially successful — he has the prince but not the information about the body

affect_state.rationale: Slightly anxious about delivering incomplete news to the King. He compensates by quickly pivoting to the positive — Hamlet is secured and waiting. His eagerness to call 'Ho! Bring in the lord' shows desire to please and demonstrate competence.

social_state.rationale: Fully subordinate, deferential. 'My lord' repeated twice. He is a functionary performing his role with minimal personal affect, seeking approval through efficiency.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Hamlet refuses to reveal where the body is
Hamlet is under guard outside the room
The King wants Hamlet brought before him
hidden_secrets: He may suspect Hamlet's madness is performed but cannot be sure
false_beliefs: He likely believes his service to the King is straightforward political loyalty rather than complicity in a deeper scheme
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz operates as an information conduit with limited understanding of the larger power dynamics. He doesn't know about Claudius's murder or the England plot.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflecting any blame for incomplete results by emphasizing what he has accomplished (securing Hamlet under guard)
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He serves the King against his former friend Hamlet, and must suppress any residual loyalty or discomfort about this betrayal
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may feel genuine unease about his role in betraying Hamlet but suppresses it entirely in this moment of duty

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Now, Hamlet, where’s Polonius?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: At supper.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: At supper where?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Not where he eats, but where he is eaten. A certain convocation of politic worms are e’en at him. Your worm is your only emperor for diet. We fat all creatures else to fat us, and we fat ourselves for maggots. Your fat king and your lean beggar is but variable service— two dishes but to one table. That’s the end.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Alas, alas!

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: A man may fish with the worm that hath eat of a king and eat of the fish that hath fed of that worm.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: What dost thou mean by this?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Nothing but to show you how a king may go a progress through the guts of a beggar.

beat_states.desire_state: To taunt Claudius with the truth of death's equalizing power and implicitly threaten him as a king who will also be consumed
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose and punish the corruption embodied by Claudius, avenging his father
beat_states.obstacle: Claudius holds political power and Hamlet is in a vulnerable position having just killed Polonius

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and takes dark pleasure in his wordplay, wielding death imagery as a weapon. He feels intellectually in command of the exchange even while politically cornered. The manic wit suggests high arousal and a perverse positive valence from the act of unmasking.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet is nominally subordinate (a subject and murder suspect before the king) but seizes rhetorical dominance through his riddles. His warmth is deeply hostile — every line is a veiled death threat dressed as philosophy.

known_facts: He killed Polonius and knows where the body is
Claudius murdered his father
Claudius is trying to locate Polonius's body
Death reduces kings and beggars to the same matter
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius is a murderer and his 'king through the guts of a beggar' is a direct threat
He knows exactly where Polonius's body is hidden
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet possesses more knowledge than anyone else in the scene — he knows the location of the body and the truth about Claudius. His riddles encode this asymmetric knowledge.

beat_states.defense_state: Uses antic disposition and dark wit as a shield — his macabre riddles simultaneously conceal and reveal his intent
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is in genuine danger yet chooses to escalate and antagonize the very person who controls his fate, suggesting a near-suicidal recklessness coexisting with sharp tactical intelligence
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's morbid wordplay could reflect genuine existential despair and philosophical preoccupation with death rather than calculated provocation — the 'antic disposition' may have partially merged with actual instability after killing Polonius

beat_states.desire_state: To locate Polonius's body and regain control of a dangerously unstable situation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne, power, and the appearance of legitimate rule while neutralizing threats
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet refuses to give a straight answer and instead uses the exchange to psychologically assault him with barely veiled threats about the mortality of kings

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is alarmed. Hamlet's talk of kings going through beggars' guts is a barely coded threat that hits his deepest fear — exposure and death. His 'What dost thou mean by this?' reveals genuine unease beneath the performance. He is losing control of the conversation.

social_state.rationale: Claudius holds formal authority but Hamlet is dominating the rhetorical exchange. His warmth is performed (the 'alas') but hollow — underneath is cold calculation and growing hostility toward Hamlet.

known_facts: Hamlet has killed Polonius
Hamlet is behaving erratically and possibly dangerously
The body must be found to manage the political fallout
Hamlet's 'madness' may be more calculated than real
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet and fears exposure
He is already planning to send Hamlet to England to be killed
false_beliefs: He may still partially believe Hamlet's behavior stems from madness rather than fully recognizing it as targeted aggression
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is beginning to understand the depth of Hamlet's hostility but may not yet fully grasp that Hamlet knows the truth about his father's murder. The 'king through the guts of a beggar' line should be deeply unsettling to him.

beat_states.defense_state: Feigns bewilderment and sorrow ('Alas, alas!') to maintain the mask of the concerned stepfather rather than reveal he feels personally threatened
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must perform grief for Polonius and paternal concern for Hamlet while internally calculating how to eliminate Hamlet — and Hamlet's words about kings being consumed strike uncomfortably close to his guilty conscience
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius's 'Alas, alas!' and 'What dost thou mean?' could be more performative than genuinely confused — he may already fully understand Hamlet's threat and be using these responses to buy time while solidifying his plan to have Hamlet killed in England

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Where is Polonius?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: In heaven. Send thither to see. If your messenger find him not there, seek him i’ th’ other place yourself. But if, indeed, you find him not within this month, you shall nose him as you go up the stairs into the lobby.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Go, seek him there.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: He will stay till you come.

beat_states.desire_state: To torment Claudius with the reality of death and damnation while revealing Polonius's location through grotesque mockery
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To expose and punish Claudius's corruption while fulfilling his duty of revenge
beat_states.obstacle: He is physically in Claudius's power and cannot yet act directly against him

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized and darkly gleeful, riding a manic high after the killing. He relishes the power of knowing something Claudius doesn't, and takes pleasure in the implicit threat ('seek him i' th' other place yourself'). His arousal is high from the dangerous game he's playing.

social_state.rationale: Despite being a subject under interrogation, Hamlet seizes rhetorical dominance through wit. His warmth is deeply negative — he is openly contemptuous, sending Claudius to hell in a joke that barely conceals genuine hatred.

known_facts: He killed Polonius
Polonius's body is rotting near the stairs to the lobby
Claudius is a murderer and usurper
Claudius needs to find the body to manage the political situation
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius killed his father
He is using the antic disposition strategically
The suggestion to seek 'the other place yourself' is a veiled death threat
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet holds all the informational cards here — he knows where the body is and chooses to reveal it through riddles. His epistemic advantage gives him the upper hand in this exchange.

beat_states.defense_state: Using macabre wit and antic disposition as a shield against consequences of murder
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He boldly tells Claudius to seek hell 'yourself' — nearly dropping the mask of madness to openly accuse — while maintaining the verbal game that keeps him safe
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's manic behavior could reflect genuine psychological destabilization after killing Polonius rather than calculated performance — the wit may be compulsive rather than strategic

beat_states.desire_state: To locate Polonius's body immediately to contain the political crisis and maintain order
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne, eliminate threats to his power, and maintain the appearance of legitimate rule
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's defiant mockery and refusal to answer directly; Hamlet's veiled threats expose Claudius's vulnerability

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is deeply unsettled. Hamlet's 'seek him i' th' other place yourself' lands as a direct threat — does Hamlet know about the murder? His terse 'Go, seek him there' reveals a man trying to regain control of a situation slipping away from him. High arousal from fear and anger, low control despite his rank.

social_state.rationale: Though nominally king, Claudius is losing the status battle in this exchange. Hamlet's wit dominates the room. Claudius's warmth is cold — he sees Hamlet as an existential threat that must be eliminated.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Hamlet is behaving erratically and dangerously
The body must be found to manage public perception
He has already formulated a plan to send Hamlet to England
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He plans to have Hamlet killed in England
His guilt makes Hamlet's references to hell and damnation psychologically devastating
false_beliefs: He may believe he can still fully control this situation through political maneuvering
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is acutely aware that Hamlet is dangerous but uncertain how much Hamlet actually knows about the original murder. Every barb from Hamlet could be madness or could be pointed accusation.

beat_states.defense_state: Suppressing emotional reaction to Hamlet's provocations and maintaining kingly composure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must appear as a grieving, concerned king seeking justice for Polonius while internally calculating how to use this crisis to dispose of Hamlet — the man who just essentially told him to go to hell
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius's brevity ('Go, seek him there') could be read as cold, calculated efficiency rather than rattled loss of control — he may already be focused entirely on the England plan and simply enduring Hamlet's provocations

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Hamlet, this deed, for thine especial safety / ( Which we do tender, as we dearly grieve / For that which thou hast done) must send thee hence / With fiery quickness. Therefore prepare thyself. / The bark is ready, and the wind at help, / Th’ associates tend, and everything is bent / For England.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: For England?

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Ay, Hamlet.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: So is it, if thou knew’st our purposes.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I see a cherub that sees them. But come, for England. / Farewell, dear mother.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Thy loving father, Hamlet.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: My mother. Father and mother is man and wife, / Man and wife is one flesh, and so, my mother.— / Come, for England.

beat_states.desire_state: To signal to Claudius that he sees through the plot while maintaining the mask of compliance
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder and expose the corruption of the Danish court
beat_states.obstacle: He is powerless to refuse exile and suspects the trip to England is a death sentence

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet senses mortal danger but channels it into sardonic defiance. 'I see a cherub that sees them' reveals he intuits Claudius's murderous intent. The 'dear mother' insult is aggressive but also born of someone cornered.

social_state.rationale: Formally subordinate and being banished, but seizes micro-dominance through wit. The 'mother' jab is a deliberate, contemptuous degradation of Claudius — refusing to acknowledge his paternal authority and implying sexual disgust.

known_facts: He is being sent to England immediately
Claudius is using Polonius's death as pretext
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are his escorts
Claudius has hidden purposes regarding this trip
hidden_secrets: He suspects or intuits that the English trip conceals a death warrant
He continues to feign madness strategically
epistemic_state.rationale: The 'cherub' line shows Hamlet has strong intuition about Claudius's lethal intentions without necessarily knowing the specific mechanism (the sealed letter). His knowledge is spiritual/intuitive rather than factual.

beat_states.defense_state: Ironic wit and antic disposition used to both reveal and conceal his knowledge of danger
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He complies with the order to leave while simultaneously signaling he knows it's a trap — submitting and resisting at once
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'Good' could be genuine relief at escaping Denmark rather than pure irony — he may partly welcome departure as escape from his paralysis

beat_states.desire_state: To dispatch Hamlet to England swiftly where he will be killed, while maintaining the appearance of paternal concern
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne, his life, and his marriage by eliminating the threat Hamlet poses
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's piercing wit keeps destabilizing his composure and authority; Hamlet seems to see through the pretense

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is anxious and urgent ('fiery quickness') — the speed betrays fear. He momentarily loses composure with the ominous aside about purposes. Hamlet's 'mother' insult rattles him but he cannot afford to react.

social_state.rationale: He wields royal authority to banish Hamlet and maintains the formal posture of a loving father-king, but Hamlet's refusal to call him 'father' publicly humiliates him and strips away the warmth pretense.

known_facts: He has arranged for England to execute Hamlet upon arrival
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern carry sealed orders
Hamlet killed Polonius
Hamlet is dangerous and possibly mad
hidden_secrets: The sealed letter commanding Hamlet's death
His murder of King Hamlet
His true motive for the English trip is assassination, not safety
false_beliefs: He believes Hamlet does not know the specific contents of his plan
He believes Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are reliable instruments
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius holds the cards operationally but Hamlet's 'cherub' line unsettles him — he cannot be sure how much Hamlet knows or intuits. His slip about 'our purposes' suggests he's both boasting and testing.

beat_states.defense_state: Performative parental concern masks murderous intent — rationalization that this is for Hamlet's 'safety'
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He frames exile as tender care while orchestrating assassination; the line 'So is it, if thou knew'st our purposes' is a barely contained threat that almost breaks his own cover
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The 'So is it, if thou knew'st our purposes' line could be read as genuine double meaning — Claudius savoring his power privately rather than a slip of composure

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Follow him at foot; tempt him with speed aboard. / Delay it not. I’ll have him hence tonight. / Away, for everything is sealed and done / That else leans on th’ affair. Pray you, make haste. / And England, if my love thou hold’st at aught / ( As my great power thereof may give thee sense, / Since yet thy cicatrice looks raw and red / After the Danish sword, and thy free awe / Pays homage to us), thou mayst not coldly set / Our sovereign process, which imports at full, / By letters congruing to that effect, / The present death of Hamlet. Do it, England, / For like the hectic in my blood he rages, / And thou must cure me. Till I know ’tis done, / Howe’er my haps, my joys will ne’er begin.

beat_states.desire_state: To ensure Hamlet is killed in England swiftly and irrevocably
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne, his life, and his stolen crown against all threats
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is still alive and dangerous; Claudius cannot kill him openly due to Gertrude's love and the public's favor for Hamlet; he must rely on distant agents to carry out the deed

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in a state of urgent, feverish anxiety. The rapid-fire commands ('Delay it not,' 'make haste') reveal high arousal. His final couplet — 'Till I know 'tis done, / Howe'er my haps, my joys will ne'er begin' — confesses deep unhappiness and vulnerability. He projects certainty in his power over England (citing the 'cicatrice' of Danish conquest) but the conditional framing and the need to invoke threats reveal underlying doubt about whether his plan will succeed.

social_state.rationale: Claudius performs maximum sovereign authority — commanding attendants, invoking tributary obligations, wielding the threat of Danish military power over England. There is zero warmth; even the word 'love' to England is purely coercive. He treats every person as an instrument of his will.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius and is dangerous and unpredictable
The sealed letters command England to execute Hamlet
England is a tributary state that owes obedience to Denmark
He cannot kill Hamlet publicly without political consequences
Hamlet has been sent aboard a ship for England
hidden_secrets: The letters contain orders for Hamlet's immediate execution
His true motive is self-preservation and the security of his illegitimately obtained throne
He murdered King Hamlet to gain the crown
false_beliefs: That Hamlet does not know or suspect the true contents of the letters
That England will comply without complication
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius believes his plan is watertight ('everything is sealed and done'), but he cannot confirm England's compliance. He assumes Hamlet is ignorant of the letters' contents — a critical miscalculation that will later be exposed. His knowledge of his own guilt colors everything with paranoid urgency.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — framing murder as a necessary medical cure ('like the hectic in my blood he rages, and thou must cure me'), transforming assassination into statecraft and self-preservation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wields enormous sovereign power yet feels desperately helpless — his happiness is entirely contingent on a distant king's compliance; the controller is out of control
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A less anxious reading: Claudius is performing calm strategic mastery in a soliloquy of triumph, savoring the neatness of his scheme. But the disease imagery, the conditional 'if,' and the final admission that his joys 'will ne'er begin' until confirmation arrives strongly favor a reading of desperate, barely-contained panic beneath the sovereign posture.

utterances.speaker: FORTINBRAS
utterances.text: Go, Captain, from me greet the Danish king. / Tell him that by his license Fortinbras / Craves the conveyance of a promised march / Over his kingdom. You know the rendezvous. / If that his Majesty would aught with us, / We shall express our duty in his eye; / And let him know so.

utterances.speaker: CAPTAIN
utterances.text: I will do ’t, my lord.

utterances.speaker: FORTINBRAS
utterances.text: Go softly on.

beat_states.desire_state: To receive his orders clearly and execute them without error
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve Fortinbras loyally and advance through dutiful obedience
beat_states.obstacle: No significant obstacle — the task is clear and straightforward

affect_state.rationale: The Captain is in a neutral, dutiful state. Low arousal — this is routine military duty. He has little control over the situation (he is a subordinate receiving orders) but high certainty about what is expected of him.

social_state.rationale: Clearly subordinate to Fortinbras. His single line is pure compliance — 'I will do't, my lord.' Minimal warmth, purely professional deference.

known_facts: He knows the rendezvous point
He knows the diplomatic message to deliver
He understands the terms of passage through Denmark
epistemic_state.rationale: The Captain has been briefed and understands his mission. Fortinbras's 'You know the rendezvous' confirms the Captain is already informed of operational details.

beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Captain may privately question the worth of this military campaign — a perspective he will later reveal to Hamlet — but in this beat he shows nothing but obedience

beat_states.desire_state: To secure diplomatic passage through Denmark smoothly and efficiently while projecting authority
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To reclaim lost territory and assert Norwegian power through disciplined military action
beat_states.obstacle: He must navigate diplomatic protocol with Denmark rather than simply marching through — he needs permission

affect_state.rationale: Fortinbras is calm, controlled, and assured. He is a man of action executing a plan already in motion. Minimal emotional turbulence — this is routine command for him.

social_state.rationale: He is the supreme commander issuing orders to a subordinate. His warmth is minimal — this is brisk, efficient military communication. The courtesy toward the Danish king is strategic, not warm.

known_facts: He has permission from Denmark for military passage
He knows the rendezvous point for his army
He is marching ostensibly toward Poland
He has a diplomatic agreement to honor
hidden_secrets: His deeper ambition may extend beyond the 'little patch of ground' in Poland — his eye is ultimately on greater conquest and legacy
epistemic_state.rationale: Fortinbras operates with clear strategic intelligence. He knows exactly what he's doing and where he's going. His information is accurate and his plan is well-formed.

beat_states.defense_state: formality — uses measured, diplomatic language to mask the raw ambition of his military campaign
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Projects deference to the Danish king ('Craves the conveyance') while commanding an army that fundamentally challenges Danish sovereignty
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Fortinbras's offer to 'express our duty in his eye' could be read as a veiled threat — presenting his army directly to the king as a display of force disguised as courtesy

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Good sir, whose powers are these?

utterances.speaker: CAPTAIN
utterances.text: They are of Norway, sir.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: How purposed, sir, I pray you?

utterances.speaker: CAPTAIN
utterances.text: Against some part of Poland.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Who commands them, sir?

utterances.speaker: CAPTAIN
utterances.text: The nephew to old Norway, Fortinbras.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Goes it against the main of Poland, sir, / Or for some frontier?

beat_states.desire_state: to answer the traveler's questions efficiently and continue on his mission to the Danish king
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve Fortinbras faithfully and complete his diplomatic errand
beat_states.obstacle: minimal — the questions are straightforward, but he must be diplomatically careful about revealing military details to a stranger on Danish soil

affect_state.rationale: The Captain is emotionally neutral, operating in a functional military mode. He is certain of his facts, feels no particular threat, and responds with professional calm.

social_state.rationale: He recognizes Hamlet as a gentleman of some standing (the 'sir' exchange is mutual) and responds with dutiful courtesy, but maintains the lower-status posture of a soldier answering a nobleman.

known_facts: The army belongs to Norway under Fortinbras
Their target is a part of Poland
He has been sent to greet the Danish king and secure passage
hidden_secrets: He likely knows the specific objective is a worthless patch of ground — information he hasn't yet volunteered
epistemic_state.rationale: The Captain has full military knowledge of the campaign's purpose but is answering only what is asked, not yet revealing the triviality of the prize.

beat_states.defense_state: professional restraint — giving factual, clipped answers without volunteering unnecessary detail
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Captain may be slightly guarded, uncertain whether this questioner has authority to receive military intelligence.

beat_states.desire_state: to gather intelligence about this army and its purpose — seeking to understand the world of action around him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to find the resolve and moral clarity to act against Claudius; encountering men of action sharpens his self-reproach
beat_states.obstacle: he is a stranger to this captain, exiled and powerless, moving toward England under guard — he must extract information without revealing his turmoil

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is restless and unsettled; each answer the Captain gives deepens a quiet agitation. He controls his tone but feels his own powerlessness acutely as he learns of Fortinbras's decisive military campaign.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet addresses the Captain with polite deference ('Good sir', 'I pray you') but his repeated 'sir' and probing questions mark him as someone of higher rank who is deliberately leveling himself to extract honest information.

known_facts: An army is passing through Danish territory
He is being sent to England by Claudius
Fortinbras had previously threatened Denmark
hidden_secrets: He suspects Claudius is sending him to England to be killed
He has been charged by the Ghost to avenge his father's murder
He has killed Polonius
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is actively building his knowledge base about Fortinbras's campaign with each question. He does not yet know the trivial nature of the objective, which will catalyze his great soliloquy.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — channeling his emotional paralysis into calm, methodical questioning
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: outwardly composed and courteous, inwardly desperate for a mirror against which to measure his own inaction
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may also be stalling — delaying his departure to England by any means, and the questioning is partly a tactic of avoidance.

utterances.speaker: CAPTAIN
utterances.text: Truly to speak, and with no addition, / We go to gain a little patch of ground / That hath in it no profit but the name. / To pay five ducats, five, I would not farm it; / Nor will it yield to Norway or the Pole / A ranker rate, should it be sold in fee.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Why, then, the Polack never will defend it.

utterances.speaker: CAPTAIN
utterances.text: Yes, it is already garrisoned.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Two thousand souls and twenty thousand ducats / Will not debate the question of this straw. / This is th’ impostume of much wealth and peace, / That inward breaks and shows no cause without / Why the man dies.— I humbly thank you, sir.

beat_states.desire_state: to honestly answer Hamlet's questions about the campaign without overstating its importance
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty as a soldier under Fortinbras's command
beat_states.obstacle: the absurdity of the mission he must describe — fighting for worthless land

affect_state.rationale: Matter-of-fact and slightly cynical about the campaign's futility; he speaks plainly without investment, a professional soldier stating facts with dry honesty

social_state.rationale: Deferential to a prince but candid; his willingness to speak honestly ('truly to speak, and with no addition') shows a functional warmth and directness

known_facts: The land they fight for is nearly worthless
He would not farm it for five ducats
The Polack has already garrisoned it
He is under Fortinbras's command marching through Denmark
epistemic_state.rationale: The Captain has clear operational knowledge and no reason to conceal or distort; he is a straightforward informant

beat_states.defense_state: minimizing — he distances himself from the campaign's logic by emphasizing the land's worthlessness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he serves loyally in a campaign he implicitly acknowledges is pointless

beat_states.desire_state: to understand the meaning and implications of Fortinbras's campaign — and through it, to confront his own inaction
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to find the moral and psychological will to avenge his father's murder
beat_states.obstacle: his own paralysis — the contrast between Fortinbras risking thousands over nothing and his failure to act on a genuine cause torments him

affect_state.rationale: Agitated self-disgust masked as philosophical observation; the image of 'th' impostume' — an internal abscess — is a projection of his own diseased inaction. Rising arousal and negative valence as he processes the comparison to himself

social_state.rationale: Maintains princely status but is barely attending to the social interaction; his polite dismissal ('I humbly thank you, sir') is gracious but signals withdrawal into inner turmoil

known_facts: Fortinbras's army fights for a worthless patch of ground
Two thousand lives and twenty thousand ducats will be spent on it
The Polack will defend it despite its worthlessness
He himself has a far greater cause and has not acted
hidden_secrets: His father was murdered by Claudius
He has been commanded by a ghost to take revenge
He recognizes this scene as a mirror of his own failure
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet grasps the military facts instantly and immediately translates them into moral self-examination; the 'impostume' metaphor reveals he sees the rot in the world and in himself

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — he transforms his self-reproach into philosophical generalization about 'th' impostume of much wealth and peace'
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he sees clearly that fighting for nothing is absurd, yet simultaneously envies the capacity for decisive action it represents; he condemns the enterprise while being shamed by it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be genuinely philosophizing about the absurdity of war without yet connecting it to himself — the full self-reproach may not crystallize until the soliloquy that follows

utterances.speaker: CAPTAIN
utterances.text: God be wi’ you, sir.

utterances.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
utterances.text: Will ’t please you go, my lord?

beat_states.desire_state: to end the conversation politely and return to his military duties
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve Fortinbras faithfully and fulfill his mission
beat_states.obstacle: no significant obstacle — the conversation has naturally concluded

affect_state.rationale: Neutral, professional demeanor. The Captain has disclosed uncomfortable truths about the worthlessness of the campaign and may sense he has said too much; his brief farewell signals a desire to close the exchange cleanly.

social_state.rationale: Deferential to a prince but maintaining soldierly courtesy. The farewell is warm enough but perfunctory — he owes Hamlet respect but is not invested in the relationship.

known_facts: The army marches for a worthless patch of Polish ground
Fortinbras has sent him to greet the Danish king
He has been speaking with a Danish prince
hidden_secrets: He may privately doubt the value of the campaign he serves
epistemic_state.rationale: The Captain has been remarkably honest about the futility of the expedition. His knowledge is straightforward and military.

beat_states.defense_state: formality — uses conventional farewell to avoid further engagement
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Captain's brevity could indicate discomfort at having been too candid about Fortinbras's campaign to a foreign prince.

beat_states.desire_state: to move Hamlet along toward England as instructed by Claudius
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill Claudius's commission and maintain royal favor by escorting Hamlet to England
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's absorption in the encounter with the Captain and his tendency to delay and reflect

affect_state.rationale: Slightly anxious — aware that Hamlet is unpredictable and that Claudius expects results. His polite phrasing masks underlying tension about whether Hamlet will comply.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to Hamlet in rank but tasked with managing him. The surface warmth is performative — the courtesy formula 'Will't please you' maintains the fiction that Hamlet has choice.

known_facts: He is escorting Hamlet to England on Claudius's orders
Hamlet has been lingering to speak with the Norwegian Captain
Hamlet killed Polonius
hidden_secrets: He carries sealed letters from Claudius regarding Hamlet's fate in England
He is spying on Hamlet for Claudius
false_beliefs: He likely does not know the letters call for Hamlet's execution
epistemic_state.rationale: Rosencrantz is an instrument of Claudius but may not grasp the full extent of the plot. He knows his job is to deliver Hamlet but may not know the deadly purpose.

beat_states.defense_state: deference — couches his directive as a polite question to mask the fact that he is Hamlet's handler
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He frames compulsion as invitation — he must get Hamlet moving but cannot command a prince
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Rosencrantz may be genuinely solicitous — simply asking if Hamlet is ready — rather than strategically pressuring him.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I’ll be with you straight. Go a little before. / How all occasions do inform against me / And spur my dull revenge. What is a man / If his chief good and market of his time / Be but to sleep and feed? A beast, no more. / Sure He that made us with such large discourse, / Looking before and after, gave us not / That capability and godlike reason / To fust in us unused. Now whether it be / Bestial oblivion or some craven scruple / Of thinking too precisely on th’ event / ( A thought which, quartered, hath but one part wisdom / And ever three parts coward), I do not know / Why yet I live to say “ This thing’s to do,” / Sith I have cause, and will, and strength, and means / To do ’t. Examples gross as Earth exhort me: / Witness this army of such mass and charge, / Led by a delicate and tender prince, / Whose spirit with divine ambition puffed / Makes mouths at the invisible event, / Exposing what is mortal and unsure / To all that fortune, death, and danger dare, / Even for an eggshell. Rightly to be great / Is not to stir without great argument, / But greatly to find quarrel in a straw / When honor’s at the stake. How stand I, then, / That have a father killed, a mother stained, / Excitements of my reason and my blood, / And let all sleep, while to my shame I see / The imminent death of twenty thousand men / That for a fantasy and trick of fame / Go to their graves like beds, fight for a plot / Whereon the numbers cannot try the cause, / Which is not tomb enough and continent / To hide the slain? O, from this time forth / My thoughts be bloody or be nothing worth!

beat_states.desire_state: To finally galvanize himself into decisive, bloody action against Claudius by shaming himself through comparison with Fortinbras
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the Ghost's command and avenge his father's murder, achieving moral and existential wholeness
beat_states.obstacle: His own paralyzing tendency toward over-thinking, his 'craven scruple' and intellectual hesitation that has kept him from acting despite having cause, will, strength, and means

affect_state.rationale: Deep self-disgust and shame fuel rising emotional intensity. He begins in analytical mode but builds to a passionate crescendo. The final couplet is a vow born of high arousal, but his certainty is only moderate — he has made similar vows before ('Now could I drink hot blood'). He feels out of control of his own nature, exposed in his inadequacy when 20,000 men march to die for an eggshell while he delays over his father's murder.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as inferior to Fortinbras, a 'delicate and tender prince' who acts with 'divine ambition' while Hamlet 'lets all sleep.' He dismisses his companions curtly ('Go a little before') to be alone with his shame. There is cold self-contempt rather than warmth.

known_facts: Fortinbras's army marches to fight and die for a worthless patch of Polish ground
His father was murdered by Claudius
His mother has been 'stained' by marrying the murderer
He has cause, will, strength, and means to act but has not
Twenty thousand men face death for a 'fantasy and trick of fame'
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius killed his father (from the Ghost and the play-within-the-play)
He is being sent to England under Claudius's orders, likely into danger
false_beliefs: That this vow of bloody thoughts will be the turning point that finally resolves his delay
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has full knowledge of his situation and his enemy's guilt. His epistemic problem is not lack of information but an inability to translate knowledge into action. His belief that this particular moment of resolve will be different from previous ones may prove false — though events in England will indeed transform him.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — he philosophically dissects his own inaction as a way of both confronting and distancing himself from his failure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He simultaneously condemns over-thinking as cowardice ('three parts coward') while engaging in an elaborate, precisely reasoned soliloquy — the very act of philosophizing about the problem IS the problem he diagnoses
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: An alternative reading suggests Hamlet is not genuinely self-lacerating but is performing a ritual of self-examination he has grown accustomed to — that the soliloquy is itself a sophisticated form of procrastination dressed as self-motivation, and he unconsciously knows it.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: I will not speak with her.

utterances.speaker: GENTLEMAN
utterances.text: She is importunate, / Indeed distract; her mood will needs be pitied.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: What would she have?

utterances.speaker: GENTLEMAN
utterances.text: She speaks much of her father, says she hears / There’s tricks i’ th’ world, and hems, and beats her heart, / Spurns enviously at straws, speaks things in doubt / That carry but half sense. Her speech is nothing, / Yet the unshapèd use of it doth move / The hearers to collection. They aim at it / And botch the words up fit to their own thoughts; / Which, as her winks and nods and gestures yield them, / Indeed would make one think there might be thought, / Though nothing sure, yet much unhappily.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: ’Twere good she were spoken with, for she may strew / Dangerous conjectures in ill-breeding minds.

beat_states.desire_state: to persuade the Queen to see Ophelia before the situation worsens
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty as a courtier by alerting his sovereign to a brewing crisis
beat_states.obstacle: The Queen's firm refusal to engage

affect_state.rationale: He is unsettled by Ophelia's state and urgently trying to convey its gravity. His long, detailed description shows high arousal and effort to make the Queen understand without overstepping

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to the Queen, speaking up respectfully but with genuine compassion for Ophelia — 'her mood will needs be pitied'

known_facts: Ophelia is mentally distracted
She speaks obsessively of her father
Her words are being interpreted by courtiers in dangerous ways
People at court are drawing their own conclusions
epistemic_state.rationale: He is a reliable witness reporting what he has observed; his description is careful and precise, noting the political danger of interpretation

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must convey how dangerous Ophelia's ravings are while also being careful not to directly accuse the crown of causing her madness

beat_states.desire_state: to convince the Queen to see Ophelia in order to contain political danger
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to protect the truth and those he cares about — serving as Hamlet's moral proxy in Hamlet's absence
beat_states.obstacle: The Queen's resistance and his own limited authority at court

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is measured but concerned. He speaks with characteristic analytical calm, but the urgency of 'dangerous conjectures' reveals real worry about the political fallout

social_state.rationale: A scholar-outsider at court, he has limited standing but speaks with moral authority; his warmth is restrained, channeled through practical advice

known_facts: Ophelia's behavior is attracting dangerous attention
The court is politically unstable
Polonius was killed
Hamlet has been sent away
hidden_secrets: He knows more about Hamlet's state of mind and mission than anyone at court
He may know about the ghost and Claudius's guilt
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio sees the political landscape clearly and understands that uncontrolled narrative around Ophelia's madness could fuel rebellion or suspicion against the crown

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He cares about Ophelia's wellbeing but frames his argument in purely strategic terms, suppressing emotional appeal in favor of political logic
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's intervention could also be read as protective of Ophelia herself — wanting her to receive care — rather than purely strategic

beat_states.desire_state: to avoid confronting Ophelia and the guilt she represents
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain her position and psychic equilibrium while surrounded by the consequences of her complicity
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's madness threatens to expose the moral rot at court and stir Gertrude's own guilt over Polonius's death

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is anxious and guilt-ridden. Her opening refusal betrays fear rather than authority. She asks 'What would she have?' revealing she's wavering, losing control of the situation.

social_state.rationale: She retains formal queenly authority but is cold/withdrawn, using her rank as a shield to avoid emotional engagement

known_facts: Polonius is dead
Hamlet killed Polonius
Hamlet has been sent to England
Ophelia is distressed
hidden_secrets: She knows the circumstances of Polonius's death more intimately than she reveals
Her own moral uncertainty about her marriage to Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude understands the political danger but tries to suppress its implications; her question 'What would she have?' suggests she half-knows what Ophelia wants but doesn't want to face it

beat_states.defense_state: avoidance — refusing contact to prevent emotional contamination and guilt exposure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She knows she should see Ophelia out of duty and compassion, but her guilt and fear of what Ophelia's ravings might reveal make her resist
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Her refusal could be read as pure political calculation — not wanting to give audience to a madwoman — rather than guilt-driven avoidance

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Let her come in. / Aside. To my sick soul ( as sin’s true nature is), / Each toy seems prologue to some great amiss. / So full of artless jealousy is guilt, / It spills itself in fearing to be spilt.

beat_states.desire_state: to steel herself to face Ophelia while managing her overwhelming guilt and dread
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to preserve her position and safety while suppressing the moral consequences of her complicity
beat_states.obstacle: her own guilty conscience which transforms every encounter into a potential exposure of her sins

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is in a state of anxious dread. Her aside reveals a 'sick soul' consumed by guilt. She feels low control — guilt 'spills itself' involuntarily. The paranoid awareness that every small thing ('toy') portends disaster shows high arousal and negative valence with deep uncertainty about what's coming.

social_state.rationale: She maintains her queenly authority by granting audience ('Let her come in') but her warmth is muted — she's not reaching toward Ophelia with genuine care but rather bracing for an ordeal. Her status is diminished from her usual regal position by her private confession of guilt.

known_facts: Ophelia is in distress and seeking audience
Hamlet has killed Polonius, Ophelia's father
Hamlet has been sent away to England
Her own remarriage is morally compromised
Something is deeply wrong in the state of Denmark
hidden_secrets: She suspects or knows her marriage to Claudius is built on murder
Hamlet has confronted her about her moral corruption in the closet scene
false_beliefs: She may believe she can contain the consequences of these events through regal composure
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude's aside reveals she understands that guilt makes one paranoid and self-betraying. She has absorbed Hamlet's accusations from the closet scene and now sees threats everywhere. She knows enough to feel deeply implicated but may not know the full extent of Claudius's crimes.

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — she names her guilt abstractly to gain intellectual distance from it
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she reverses her refusal to see Ophelia, simultaneously wanting to avoid confrontation and recognizing that avoidance itself reveals guilt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's guilt may be more specifically about Polonius's death and her role in Hamlet's exile rather than broader complicity in Claudius's crimes — her 'great amiss' may refer to feared political consequences rather than moral self-knowledge.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Where is the beauteous Majesty of Denmark?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: How now, Ophelia?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: How should I your true love know From another one? By his cockle hat and staff And his sandal shoon.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Alas, sweet lady, what imports this song?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Say you? Nay, pray you, mark. / Oh, ho! / He is dead and gone, lady, He is dead and gone; At his head a grass-green turf, At his heels a stone.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Nay, but Ophelia—

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Pray you, mark. / White his shroud as the mountain snow—

beat_states.desire_state: to be heard and acknowledged in her grief for her father's death and Hamlet's abandonment
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to find coherence and love in a world that has destroyed every attachment she had
beat_states.obstacle: her rational mind has shattered; she cannot communicate directly and is trapped in fragmentary song

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is in a state of profound grief and psychic disintegration. Her songs about death and lost love reveal deep negative affect, but her arousal fluctuates — she shifts between eerie calm and sudden exclamatory bursts ('Oh, ho!'). She has almost no control over her own expression, and her certainty about reality is fractured.

social_state.rationale: She addresses the Queen with a mix of former courtly habit ('beauteous Majesty') and total disregard for social protocol. Her warmth is near-neutral — she is not hostile but is essentially unreachable, performing grief rather than connecting.

known_facts: Her father Polonius is dead
Hamlet was her love and has been sent away or turned against her
The world she knew has collapsed
hidden_secrets: The specific nature of her relationship with Hamlet and what transpired between them privately
false_beliefs: She may conflate her father's death with Hamlet's abandonment, blurring the identities of the dead man in her songs
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia knows her father is dead but her madness collapses multiple griefs — lost love, lost father, lost protector — into overlapping symbolic expressions. The songs serve as coded truths she cannot state plainly.

beat_states.defense_state: dissociation — channeling unbearable pain through folk songs and oblique symbolism rather than confronting reality directly
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she asks for the Queen's attention ('pray you, mark') with lucid insistence while simultaneously being unable to engage in rational discourse
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Ophelia's madness is partially performative — a deliberate if unconscious strategy to speak truths (about Hamlet, about the court's corruption) that would be dangerous to voice sanely.

beat_states.desire_state: to contain the situation and stop Ophelia from exposing painful truths about death and guilt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to preserve the stability of the court and protect herself from the consequences of the moral rot surrounding her
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia cannot be reasoned with or redirected; her songs keep piercing through Gertrude's defenses

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is distressed and increasingly helpless. She entered this scene already primed with guilt ('my sick soul'), and Ophelia's songs about death and lost love strike directly at her conscience. She tries to maintain queenly composure but keeps being interrupted and overwhelmed.

social_state.rationale: She retains nominal authority as Queen but it is ineffective — Ophelia ignores her attempts to redirect. Her warmth is genuine; she uses 'sweet lady' and shows real maternal concern, though it is mixed with self-protective anxiety.

known_facts: Polonius is dead, killed by Hamlet
Hamlet has been sent to England
Ophelia has lost her mind
The people are restless and Laertes may return
hidden_secrets: Her complicity or awareness regarding Claudius's crimes
The depth of her own guilt about the state of affairs
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude understands the political and personal danger of this moment. She knows the cause of Ophelia's madness but cannot openly acknowledge the court's role in it.

beat_states.defense_state: minimization — she tries to reduce Ophelia's utterances to a manageable question ('what imports this song?') rather than absorbing their devastating content
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she is genuinely moved with compassion for Ophelia ('Alas, sweet lady') while simultaneously wanting to shut down the encounter that triggers her own guilt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings play Gertrude as more genuinely bewildered than guilty — focusing on maternal concern without the guilt subtext, though her earlier aside ('to my sick soul') strongly supports the guilt reading.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Alas, look here, my lord.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Larded all with sweet flowers; Which bewept to the ground did not go With true-love showers.

beat_states.desire_state: to complete her funeral song and mourn the dead — to give ritual expression to unbearable grief
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to process and express the love and loss that rational speech can no longer contain
beat_states.obstacle: her fractured mind cannot organize grief into coherent communication; her audience cannot truly hear her

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is deep in madness, singing of a body 'larded with sweet flowers' going to the grave with 'true-love showers.' The imagery fuses death and love into a single unbearable knot. Her arousal is modulated by the dreamlike, song-like quality, but the grief underneath is total.

social_state.rationale: She has lost all social standing — a nobleman's daughter now performing madness before the court. There is a residual sweetness in her singing, a ghost of warmth, but it is not directed at anyone present.

known_facts: her father is dead
flowers and burial rites matter
hidden_secrets: the specific trauma of Hamlet's rejection and cruelty in the nunnery scene lives unspoken beneath her songs
false_beliefs: the songs may conflate her father's death with a lover's abandonment, suggesting she cannot distinguish between the two losses
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia's knowledge is fragmented and expressed symbolically. She knows death has occurred but processes it through folk-song imagery that blurs specific facts into archetypal patterns of loss.

beat_states.defense_state: dissociation — channeling overwhelming grief into folk song fragments that distance her from direct emotional confrontation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she is performing mourning rituals for a father whose death she half-knows and half-denies, while also grieving Hamlet's betrayal of love
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Ophelia's madness contains a subversive, accusatory clarity — that her songs deliberately expose the court's sins rather than simply expressing personal grief.

beat_states.desire_state: to get Claudius to witness and share the burden of Ophelia's madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain her position and stability while managing the mounting guilt and chaos around her
beat_states.obstacle: she cannot fix Ophelia's broken mind, and witnessing it deepens her own guilt

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is distressed and overwhelmed by Ophelia's deterioration. Her brief line — 'Alas, look here, my lord' — is a plea for help, revealing she feels inadequate to handle this alone and emotionally exposed.

social_state.rationale: She retains nominal authority as queen but is diminished by helplessness; her warmth is genuine pity for Ophelia, though mixed with self-concern.

known_facts: Ophelia has lost her sanity
Polonius is dead
Hamlet killed Polonius
Ophelia's songs reference death and lost love
hidden_secrets: her own guilt about her hasty marriage and its role in the chain of events
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude understands the surface causes of Ophelia's madness but may not fully grasp the depth of Hamlet's cruelty toward Ophelia or the full political danger unfolding.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection — redirecting attention to Claudius so she doesn't have to bear witness alone
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she feels compassion for Ophelia yet knows her own complicity in the events that caused this madness
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may also be strategically alerting Claudius to manage a political crisis rather than purely expressing compassion.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: How do you, pretty lady?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Well, God dild you. They say the owl was a baker’s daughter. Lord, we know what we are but know not what we may be. God be at your table.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Conceit upon her father.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Pray let’s have no words of this, but when they ask you what it means, say you this: / Tomorrow is Saint Valentine’s day, All in the morning betime, And I a maid at your window, To be your Valentine. / Then up he rose and donned his clothes And dupped the chamber door, Let in the maid, that out a maid Never departed more.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Pretty Ophelia—

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Indeed, without an oath, I’ll make an end on ’t: / He answers: / By Gis and by Saint Charity, Alack and fie for shame, Young men will do ’t, if they come to ’t; By Cock, they are to blame. / Quoth she “ Before you tumbled me, You promised me to wed.” / “ So would I ’a done, by yonder sun, An thou hadst not come to my bed.”

beat_states.desire_state: To manage and contain Ophelia's madness before she reveals dangerous truths about Polonius's death or Claudius's guilt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain power, legitimacy, and control over the court and its narratives
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's madness is unpredictable and her utterances edge toward exposing uncomfortable truths about betrayal, sexuality, and death that could destabilize his authority

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is unsettled. Ophelia's fragmented speech is increasingly sexual and pointed — the St. Valentine's song about seduction and broken promises could be read as allegory for his own sins. He tries to maintain composure but keeps being interrupted and overridden by her, losing control of the exchange.

social_state.rationale: He attempts to hold kingly authority with diminutive terms ('pretty lady,' 'Pretty Ophelia') that assert status through condescension disguised as warmth, but Ophelia's disregard for social hierarchy undercuts his position

known_facts: Polonius is dead, killed by Hamlet
Ophelia has gone mad from grief
Hamlet has been sent to England
Laertes may return seeking vengeance
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He has ordered Hamlet's execution in England
false_beliefs: He believes he can contain the fallout of Polonius's death
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius correctly identifies grief for her father as a cause ('Conceit upon her father') but may be willfully ignoring the sexual betrayal content of Ophelia's songs, which could point to Hamlet's treatment of her or to broader themes of male betrayal that implicate Claudius himself

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through clinical diagnosis — 'Conceit upon her father' — reframing her words as mere grief to neutralize their dangerous content
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs gentle concern ('pretty lady,' 'Pretty Ophelia') while internally calculating how to manage a political threat; his tenderness is instrumentalized
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may genuinely feel a flicker of guilt and pity for Ophelia as collateral damage of his crimes, making his 'Pretty Ophelia' not purely tactical but tinged with real remorse

beat_states.desire_state: To speak the unspeakable truths — about death, sexual betrayal, and broken promises — that her rational mind could never articulate
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be seen, acknowledged, and validated in a world where every man she trusted has abandoned or destroyed her
beat_states.obstacle: Her sanity has shattered; she cannot communicate directly and is patronized and dismissed by those around her

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia is in a state of fractured grief and dissociation. Her affect swings wildly — blessing, folk wisdom, bawdy song — with high emotional intensity but no stable center. She is profoundly unprotected, speaking without social filters, the most vulnerable figure on stage.

social_state.rationale: She has lost all social standing through madness — she is pitied and patronized. Yet her songs carry a strange authority that overrides Claudius's attempts to control the conversation, creating an inverted power dynamic where the mad woman silences the king

known_facts: Her father is dead
Hamlet rejected and humiliated her
Men make promises they break
hidden_secrets: The full nature of her relationship with Hamlet, possibly including sexual intimacy implied by the Valentine's song
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia's madness gives her paradoxical clarity — 'we know what we are but know not what we may be' is one of the play's most lucid philosophical statements. Her songs encode real knowledge about betrayal, sexuality, and death that she processes through folk narrative rather than rational discourse.

beat_states.defense_state: Dissociation — madness itself functions as both defense and liberation, allowing her to speak truths through folk songs and riddles that would be forbidden to a sane noblewoman
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She oscillates between pious blessing ('God be at your table') and explicit sexual content; between childlike fragmentation and devastating insight ('we know what we are but know not what we may be')
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The St. Valentine's song may not reflect Ophelia's personal sexual experience but rather her processing of her father's warnings about Hamlet, her fears made vivid through madness — the 'tumbled maid' as anticipated rather than experienced betrayal

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: How long hath she been thus?

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: I hope all will be well. We must be patient, but I cannot choose but weep to think they would lay him i’ th’ cold ground. My brother shall know of it. And so I thank you for your good counsel. Come, my coach! Good night, ladies, good night, sweet ladies, good night, good night.

beat_states.desire_state: to assess the extent and danger of Ophelia's madness and understand what she might reveal
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his throne, power, and the concealment of his crimes
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's madness is unpredictable and her references to her father's death threaten to expose uncomfortable truths and incite Laertes

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is anxious and calculating; his brief question masks deep concern about political fallout, especially her mention of her brother knowing

social_state.rationale: Maintains royal authority by directing the inquiry, but his warmth is performative — he is managing a crisis, not genuinely caring

known_facts: Polonius is dead
Hamlet killed Polonius
Ophelia has descended into madness
Laertes is returning from France
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He sent Hamlet to England with orders for his execution
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is acutely aware of the chain of events and is gathering intelligence on how far Ophelia's madness has progressed and what danger Laertes poses

beat_states.defense_state: clinical detachment — framing her condition as medical rather than politically dangerous
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: needs to appear compassionate toward Ophelia while being terrified of what her madness might expose about Polonius's death
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may also be experiencing genuine pity mixed with his political calculations, making his question partially compassionate

beat_states.desire_state: to process and express the unbearable grief of her father's death and abandonment
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be loved, protected, and anchored in a world that has stripped away every person she depended on
beat_states.obstacle: her mind has fractured — she cannot organize her grief into coherent communication or action

affect_state.rationale: Ophelia oscillates between resignation, grief, and veiled threat; she has almost no control over her emotional state, drifting between courtly formality and raw anguish

social_state.rationale: She has lost all social standing through her madness, yet retains habitual courtesy ('good night, sweet ladies') — warmth is reflexive, not connected to present reality

known_facts: Her father is dead
He was laid in the cold ground
She has a brother who should know what happened
false_beliefs: She may believe someone is offering her counsel
She appears to believe a coach is waiting for her
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia's grasp on reality is fractured; she mixes true knowledge of her father's death with hallucinated social situations, imagined departures, and displaced memories

beat_states.defense_state: dissociation — retreating into fragmented speech, polite formulas, and imagined departures to avoid the full weight of her anguish
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she says 'I hope all will be well' and 'we must be patient' while simultaneously revealing she cannot stop weeping and threatens that her brother will know — hope and fury coexist in shattered fragments
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some of Ophelia's speech may be more calculated than it appears — 'my brother shall know of it' could be a lucid and deliberate threat embedded within her madness

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Follow her close; give her good watch, I pray you. / O, this is the poison of deep grief. It springs / All from her father’s death, and now behold! / O Gertrude, Gertrude, / When sorrows come, they come not single spies, / But in battalions: first, her father slain; / Next, your son gone, and he most violent author / Of his own just remove; the people muddied, / Thick, and unwholesome in their thoughts and whispers / For good Polonius’ death, and we have done but greenly / In hugger-mugger to inter him; poor Ophelia / Divided from herself and her fair judgment, / Without the which we are pictures or mere beasts; / Last, and as much containing as all these, / Her brother is in secret come from France, / Feeds on his wonder, keeps himself in clouds, / And wants not buzzers to infect his ear / With pestilent speeches of his father’s death, / Wherein necessity, of matter beggared, / Will nothing stick our person to arraign / In ear and ear. O, my dear Gertrude, this, / Like to a murd’ring piece, in many places / Gives me superfluous death.

beat_states.desire_state: to frame the cascading crises as externally caused misfortunes and enlist Gertrude's sympathy and alliance against the threats closing in on him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his grip on the throne and conceal his guilt for the murder of King Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: multiple converging threats — Ophelia's madness, the people's suspicion, Laertes' secret return — are all pointing toward him as the source of catastrophe

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is genuinely frightened. The speech builds in escalating panic — 'battalions' of sorrows, 'murd'ring piece' giving 'superfluous death.' His control over events is slipping. The arousal is high but channeled into rhetorical performance. The vulnerability is real but strategically deployed to Gertrude.

social_state.rationale: Claudius is diminished here — he is not commanding but confiding, almost pleading with Gertrude. He addresses her with intimate repetition ('O Gertrude, Gertrude') and 'my dear Gertrude,' seeking the warmth of partnership in crisis rather than asserting royal authority.

known_facts: Ophelia has gone mad from grief over Polonius' death
Hamlet has been sent away (and Claudius has secretly ordered his death)
Polonius was buried hastily and secretly ('in hugger-mugger')
The people are whispering suspiciously about Polonius' death
Laertes has returned secretly from France
Laertes is being fed rumors that implicate Claudius in Polonius' death
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He has sent orders for Hamlet to be killed in England
He knows his own guilt is the ultimate root cause of every crisis he enumerates
false_beliefs: He may believe he can still manage and contain these converging threats through political maneuvering
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has excellent intelligence — he knows about Laertes' secret return and the 'buzzers' feeding him information. But his self-presentation to Gertrude systematically omits his own culpability in creating these conditions.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection — he catalogues the crises as if they are fate's doing rather than consequences of his own actions, positioning himself as fellow victim rather than cause
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he speaks of Polonius' death and Hamlet's banishment as misfortunes that have befallen him, while knowing he ordered Hamlet's execution and that his own original murder of King Hamlet set the entire chain in motion
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A more sympathetic reading: Claudius here is genuinely overwhelmed and not merely performing — his fear is authentic and his appeal to Gertrude reflects a man who truly loves her and needs her support, momentarily stripped of his Machiavellian calculation.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Alack, what noise is this?

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Attend! / Where is my Switzers? Let them guard the door. / What is the matter?

utterances.speaker: MESSENGER
utterances.text: Save yourself, my lord. / The ocean, overpeering of his list, / Eats not the flats with more impiteous haste / Than young Laertes, in a riotous head, / O’erbears your officers. The rabble call him “ lord,” / And, as the world were now but to begin, / Antiquity forgot, custom not known, / The ratifiers and props of every word, / They cry “ Choose we, Laertes shall be king!” / Caps, hands, and tongues applaud it to the clouds, / “ Laertes shall be king! Laertes king!”

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: How cheerfully on the false trail they cry. / O, this is counter, you false Danish dogs!

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: The doors are broke.

beat_states.desire_state: to maintain control of the situation and protect his throne from Laertes' uprising
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to preserve his crown and the power he seized through fratricide
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes has overrun his officers and the mob is calling for Laertes to be king — physical and political threat converging simultaneously

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in acute crisis — his guards have been overwhelmed, the doors are broken, and a popular revolt threatens his life and crown. His terse commands ('Attend!', 'The doors are broke') reveal high arousal with diminishing control.

social_state.rationale: His status is actively being stripped by the mob. He issues commands but they are reactive, not proactive. No warmth — purely in survival and authority-assertion mode.

known_facts: Laertes has returned and overwhelmed the royal officers
The mob supports Laertes as king
The doors have been breached
Ophelia's madness and Polonius's death are the catalysts
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He is responsible for the chain of events that killed Polonius indirectly
false_beliefs: He may believe his Swiss guards can still restore order
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius quickly grasps the political dimensions of the threat but may underestimate how personally targeted Laertes' rage will be.

beat_states.defense_state: asserting authority through rapid orders to compensate for loss of actual control
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: projects kingly authority while internally recognizing how fragile his hold on power truly is — the very consequences he feared from Polonius's death are materializing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may already be calculating how to redirect Laertes' anger toward Hamlet, meaning his apparent panic is partly performative to gain sympathy from Gertrude

beat_states.desire_state: to warn the king in time and fulfill his duty of reporting the crisis
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: survival and loyalty to the crown — delivering urgent intelligence
beat_states.obstacle: the situation is nearly beyond salvaging; he must convey the enormity of the threat quickly enough for action

affect_state.rationale: Extremely high arousal — breathless, urgent delivery. He has witnessed the mob firsthand and knows the physical danger. His opening 'Save yourself, my lord' reveals genuine fear for the king's life and his own.

social_state.rationale: Low status as a servant/messenger, but his report carries enormous weight in this moment. Slight warmth in his genuine concern for the king's safety.

known_facts: Laertes has overpowered the royal officers
The mob is calling for Laertes to be crowned king
The uprising has overwhelmed normal order — 'antiquity forgot, custom not known'
The physical perimeter of the castle is being breached
epistemic_state.rationale: The messenger is a direct eyewitness delivering raw intelligence. His knowledge is immediate and reliable.

beat_states.defense_state: rhetorical distancing through elaborate metaphor (ocean imagery) to manage his own terror
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: duty compels him toward the danger he is urging the king to flee from

beat_states.desire_state: to understand and contain the threat, and to defend the legitimacy of the crown
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to preserve stability and her family/position, even as the world she inhabits collapses around her
beat_states.obstacle: the mob has turned against the throne and she is powerless to stop it

affect_state.rationale: Initial alarm ('Alack, what noise is this?') gives way to fierce indignation. Her hunting metaphor ('counter') shows she's processing the threat through contempt rather than terror, but the vulnerability is real.

social_state.rationale: She asserts queenly status by condemning the mob with aristocratic disdain, but her actual power to affect events is minimal. No warmth — only contempt for the populace.
