known_facts: A violent mob is approaching
They support Laertes for king
Ophelia has gone mad
Polonius is dead
hidden_secrets: Hamlet killed Polonius (she was told to keep this from becoming public knowledge in the way Claudius framed it)
Whatever she suspects or knows about Claudius's fratricide
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude understands the political danger clearly — her hunting metaphor shows sophisticated reading of the mob as being on a 'false trail' of legitimacy.

beat_states.defense_state: displacement of fear into contemptuous anger at the mob ('false Danish dogs')
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she calls the mob 'false' for following Laertes, yet she herself lives within a false succession built on murder she may suspect
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Her fierce defense of the crown could mask guilt — she knows the succession is tainted and her outrage at the mob may be partly self-directed projection

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Where is this king?— Sirs, stand you all without.

utterances.speaker: ALL
utterances.text: No, let’s come in!

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: I pray you, give me leave.

utterances.speaker: ALL
utterances.text: We will, we will.

beat_states.desire_state: To storm in alongside Laertes and witness or participate in the overthrow of the King
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To seize power and install Laertes as their chosen leader — expressing populist discontent with Claudius's reign
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes himself, who commands them to stay outside

affect_state.rationale: Extremely high arousal — mob energy, adrenaline, the thrill of insurrection. Low control because they are a collective without real agency, deferring to Laertes. Low certainty — they push back once then yield instantly.

social_state.rationale: They have momentary power as a mob but their status is subordinate to Laertes. Warmth toward Laertes is positive — they are his loyal followers — but hostile toward the court.

known_facts: Polonius is dead
Something is rotten in the state under Claudius
Laertes is their chosen leader
false_beliefs: Laertes has a clear plan and rightful claim to the throne
epistemic_state.rationale: The mob operates on rumor, grievance, and charismatic attachment to Laertes rather than specific knowledge of court intrigue.

beat_states.defense_state: Mob mentality — individual moral responsibility dissolved into collective action
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: They want Laertes as king but immediately obey his command to withdraw, revealing they are followers not true revolutionaries
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Their quick compliance ('We will, we will') could indicate they never truly intended to defy Laertes — the initial protest was performative loyalty rather than genuine insistence.

beat_states.desire_state: To confront the King directly and alone, asserting total command of the situation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father Polonius's death and restore his family's honor
beat_states.obstacle: His own followers who want to surge in with him, potentially diluting his authority and muddying the confrontation

affect_state.rationale: Furious, adrenalized, operating on righteous rage. High control because he has successfully broken through the doors and now commands his followers. Certainty is high — he knows what he came to do. Vulnerability is present but suppressed beneath the rage.

social_state.rationale: He has seized power — the mob follows him, he has literally broken into the palace. Warmth is cold and hostile; he is here to accuse, not negotiate. He treats even his allies with imperious authority.

known_facts: His father Polonius is dead
His father was buried secretly without proper ceremony
His sister Ophelia has gone mad
The King is responsible or at minimum complicit in these events
false_beliefs: The King is directly responsible for Polonius's death
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes has returned in a fury based on incomplete information. He blames Claudius for his father's death and family's ruin, not knowing Hamlet was the actual killer. His rage is misdirected.

beat_states.defense_state: Channeling grief and rage into decisive military-style action to avoid feeling the helplessness underneath
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He storms in as a revolutionary with a mob but then immediately dismisses the mob — he wants to be both populist avenger and noble aristocrat handling his own affairs
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The dismissal of the mob could also be read as Laertes wanting privacy because he fears breaking down emotionally in front of them, rather than pure aristocratic authority.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: I thank you. Keep the door. Followers exit. O, thou vile king, / Give me my father!

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Calmly, good Laertes.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: That drop of blood that’s calm proclaims me bastard, / Cries “ cuckold” to my father, brands the harlot / Even here between the chaste unsmirchèd brow / Of my true mother.

beat_states.desire_state: To confront the King directly and demand accountability for his father's death
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father Polonius's murder and restore his family's honor
beat_states.obstacle: The Queen is attempting to restrain him; he must also manage his own followers to maintain control of the confrontation

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is at peak emotional intensity — grief-fueled rage channeled into direct confrontation. His certainty is high that vengeance is owed but not fully certain Claudius is guilty (he addresses him as 'vile king'). His control is fractured: he dismisses followers with composure but immediately loses restraint. The elaborate metaphor about bastardy and cuckoldry reveals deep vulnerability about family honor.

social_state.rationale: Laertes has seized power through armed rebellion, giving him temporary high status — he commands followers and directly insults the King. But his warmth is deeply negative: he is hostile to the King and dismissive of the Queen's attempt to calm him.

known_facts: His father Polonius is dead
Ophelia has gone mad
He believes the King bears responsibility for his father's death
He has successfully stormed the castle with armed supporters
hidden_secrets: He may harbor some uncertainty about who exactly killed his father
false_beliefs: He believes Claudius directly ordered or caused Polonius's death
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes has returned in haste and has incomplete information. He knows his father is dead and Ophelia is mad, and popular rumor has pointed him toward the King, but he does not yet know the full circumstances — that Hamlet killed Polonius behind the arras.

beat_states.defense_state: Transforms grief into righteous fury, using honor-code language to justify his rage and preempt any call for restraint
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He dismisses his followers to appear in control and reasonable, yet immediately erupts with volcanic accusation — a tension between strategic calculation and overwhelming rage
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may be performing rage partly for the Queen's benefit, using the confrontation to force information and political concessions rather than purely expressing uncontrollable fury

beat_states.desire_state: To de-escalate the volatile confrontation and protect Claudius from Laertes's wrath
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain stability, protect her position, and shield those she cares about from destruction
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes is beyond reason, consumed by grief and rage; her single line of intervention is immediately overwhelmed by his rhetorical force

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is frightened and feels powerless. Her single interjection ('Calmly, good Laertes') is an attempt to exert maternal/royal authority but reveals her desperation. She knows the truth of Polonius's death, making her deeply uncertain about how this will unfold. She is highly vulnerable — physically present in a room with an armed, enraged man.

social_state.rationale: Though she is Queen, her authority has been effectively overridden by Laertes's armed insurrection. Her warmth is genuine but strategic — she addresses him with 'good Laertes,' attempting maternal gentleness to soothe.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Laertes has broken into the castle with armed followers
Laertes blames the King for his father's death
Ophelia has gone mad
hidden_secrets: She knows Hamlet is the true killer of Polonius
She knows Claudius sent Hamlet to England
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude holds critical information that could redirect Laertes's fury but is bound by loyalty to Hamlet and complicity with Claudius to conceal it. She is in a uniquely agonizing epistemic position.

beat_states.defense_state: Minimizing the danger through appeal to civility and composure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She knows the actual killer is her own son Hamlet, yet she cannot reveal this — she must protect both Claudius and Hamlet while facing a man whose rage is justified
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's 'Calmly' could be read as genuinely protective of Laertes himself — a maternal impulse toward a young man she has known since childhood, not just strategic de-escalation

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: What is the cause, Laertes, / That thy rebellion looks so giant-like?— / Let him go, Gertrude. Do not fear our person. / There’s such divinity doth hedge a king / That treason can but peep to what it would, / Acts little of his will.— Tell me, Laertes, / Why thou art thus incensed.— Let him go, Gertrude.— / Speak, man.

beat_states.desire_state: To regain control of the situation and defuse Laertes' violent rebellion through an assertion of royal authority and calm reason
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne, power, and the concealment of his crimes at all costs
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes has stormed in with armed followers demanding vengeance; Gertrude is physically restraining Laertes, which actually undermines Claudius's performance of fearless sovereignty

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in genuine physical danger and knows it, producing high arousal and real vulnerability, but he is a masterful political performer who channels fear into an authoritative display. His certainty is moderate — he believes he can manipulate Laertes but the situation is volatile. The repeated commands to Gertrude reveal controlled panic beneath the regal façade.

social_state.rationale: Claudius reasserts maximum royal status through divine right rhetoric, but his actual status is contested — a mob just broke down his doors. He offers a sliver of warmth/reasonableness by inviting Laertes to speak and explain himself, positioning himself as the patient, rational sovereign willing to listen.

known_facts: Polonius is dead and Claudius knows the circumstances
Laertes believes the King is responsible for his father's death
Laertes has gathered armed followers and forced entry into the castle
Gertrude is physically trying to restrain Laertes
Hamlet actually killed Polonius
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He knows Hamlet killed Polonius and has been trying to use that fact strategically
He has already sent Hamlet to England with orders for his execution
false_beliefs: That invoking divine right will reliably protect him — he has no legitimate divine mandate as a usurper
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius possesses crucial information Laertes lacks — that Hamlet, not Claudius, killed Polonius. He plans to redirect Laertes' rage toward Hamlet. His invocation of divine right is a performed belief he cannot truly hold given his own regicide.

beat_states.defense_state: Grandiose invocation of divine right as a psychological shield — wrapping himself in the mythology of kingship to mask genuine physical danger
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Projects absolute fearlessness and divinely-protected sovereignty while knowing he is a regicide and usurper with no legitimate divine protection; his repeated 'Let him go, Gertrude' betrays urgency beneath the calm
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A less Machiavellian reading: Claudius genuinely believes in divine right and is drawing on authentic courage here rather than performing it — his calm is real, not strategic. However, the triple repetition of commands to Gertrude and the calculated rhetorical structure suggest a deeply strategic mind at work.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Where is my father?

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Dead.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: But not by him.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Let him demand his fill.

beat_states.desire_state: to control Laertes's rage by appearing transparent and honest, redirecting blame toward Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his throne and power by neutralizing all threats, including turning Laertes into an instrument against Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes's volatile fury could turn lethal at any moment; the Queen might say too much

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is calculating rapidly. His one-word answer 'Dead' and his instruction 'Let him demand his fill' are masterstrokes of performed calm. He is anxious but highly controlled, sensing an opportunity to weaponize Laertes.

social_state.rationale: He is reasserting kingly composure and authority through transparency. The slight warmth is strategic — he's beginning to position himself as Laertes's ally rather than adversary.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Hamlet has been sent to England with orders for execution
Laertes's rebellion threatens his throne
He can redirect Laertes's anger toward Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He ordered Hamlet's execution in England
He is responsible for the original murder of King Hamlet
He intends to use Laertes as a tool against Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius possesses virtually complete information and is wielding it strategically, revealing facts piecemeal to control the narrative.

beat_states.defense_state: performing radical honesty as a manipulation — answering bluntly to build trust
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is the architect of the conditions that led to Polonius's death (by harboring Hamlet, by spying) but presents himself as an open, trustworthy authority who has nothing to hide

beat_states.desire_state: to learn the truth of his father's death and identify who is responsible
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father's murder and restore family honor
beat_states.obstacle: he does not yet know who killed Polonius; the King controls the flow of information

affect_state.rationale: The question is stripped bare — no rhetoric, no threats. The blunt directness reveals a man oscillating between murderous rage and desperate grief. He doesn't yet have an object for his vengeance.

social_state.rationale: He stormed the palace and holds physical power, but in this moment he is an information-seeker dependent on the King's answers, which lowers his effective status. No warmth toward anyone present.

known_facts: His father is dead
His father was buried hastily and without ceremony
He suspects the King may be responsible
false_beliefs: He likely believes the King may be directly responsible for Polonius's death
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes has returned to Denmark on rumors and rage. He does not yet know Hamlet killed Polonius. The Queen's interjection ('But not by him') is the first clue redirecting blame away from Claudius.

beat_states.defense_state: channeling grief into aggression to avoid collapse
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: his rage is enormous but he is momentarily reduced to a simple, almost childlike question — 'Where is my father?' — revealing the vulnerability beneath the fury
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may already suspect the King is not the killer and is testing him, but the directness of the question suggests genuine ignorance.

beat_states.desire_state: to protect Claudius from Laertes's wrath by clarifying he is not the killer
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to preserve her family and the stability of her world, even at the cost of truth
beat_states.obstacle: she risks exposing Hamlet as the killer, which threatens her son

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude speaks reactively, fearfully. Her interjection is panicked and protective — she sees Laertes as a physical threat to Claudius. The emotional cost of potentially implicating Hamlet hasn't fully registered yet.

social_state.rationale: She is diminished in this scene — physically endangered, unable to command the situation. Her warmth is directed at Claudius in protective instinct, though she has some maternal concern lurking.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Claudius did not kill Polonius
Laertes believes someone at court is responsible
hidden_secrets: She witnessed Hamlet kill Polonius
She knows Hamlet's state of mind during the killing
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude is one of the few who knows exactly what happened in her chamber. Her interjection reveals she is thinking faster than she should — blurting truth that may have consequences.

beat_states.defense_state: impulsive interjection — acting on instinct before thinking through consequences
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: by protecting Claudius she implicitly points toward Hamlet; she is caught between defending her husband and protecting her son
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's interjection may also be a calculated attempt to redirect Laertes toward the truth without naming Hamlet, hoping to de-escalate the situation before violence erupts.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: How came he dead? I’ll not be juggled with. / To hell, allegiance! Vows, to the blackest devil! / Conscience and grace, to the profoundest pit! / I dare damnation. To this point I stand, / That both the worlds I give to negligence, / Let come what comes, only I’ll be revenged / Most throughly for my father.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Who shall stay you?

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: My will, not all the world. / And for my means, I’ll husband them so well / They shall go far with little.

beat_states.desire_state: To channel Laertes' fury away from himself and toward Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne, his life, and his power by neutralizing all threats
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes is in a volatile, undirected rage that could turn lethal at any moment; Claudius must redirect without appearing manipulative

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is calculating rapidly. 'Who shall stay you?' is a masterfully minimal provocation — only two beats of speech that give Laertes all the rope he needs. Moderate arousal held tightly in check; high control despite real danger.

social_state.rationale: His status is temporarily diminished by Laertes' armed incursion, but he's regaining ground through calm authority; the slight warmth of 'Who shall stay you?' is seductive — implying he won't be the one to stop Laertes

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Laertes does not yet know who killed his father
Laertes can be weaponized against Hamlet
Laertes has popular support and armed followers
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He has already sent Hamlet to England with orders for his execution
He sees Laertes as a tool, not an ally
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius holds nearly all the information cards; his two-word question is a calculated move to let Laertes commit himself fully before Claudius reveals just enough truth to redirect the rage toward Hamlet

beat_states.defense_state: Strategic composure — appearing calm and even inviting in the face of Laertes' fury to establish dominance and trustworthiness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is the one who benefited from the chain of events that killed Polonius, yet he positions himself as Laertes' potential ally; he must feel both fear and predatory opportunism simultaneously

beat_states.desire_state: To commit fully and irrevocably to avenging his father's death, rejecting all moral and spiritual constraints
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To restore his family's honor and punish whoever murdered his father
beat_states.obstacle: He still doesn't know who killed his father; Claudius is deflecting and managing him

affect_state.rationale: Fury and grief dominate; he has whipped himself into a state of absolute resolve, but beneath the bravado is a young man terrified of inadequacy and loss. High arousal, high certainty about his purpose if not his target.

social_state.rationale: He is asserting dominance through sheer recklessness and willingness to damn himself; coldly hostile toward anyone who might impede him, including the king

known_facts: His father Polonius is dead
The king has confirmed Polonius is dead
He does not yet know who killed his father or the circumstances
false_beliefs: He may suspect Claudius is responsible for his father's death
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes demands 'How came he dead?' — he still lacks the crucial information about the killer's identity. His rage is partially undirected, which makes it dangerous and exploitable.

beat_states.defense_state: Manic escalation — renouncing all moral frameworks preemptively to avoid the paralysis of conscience
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: The very extremity of his declarations ('I dare damnation') suggests he still feels the pull of conscience and grace he's renouncing; a truly amoral person wouldn't need to announce it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes' extreme rhetoric could be partially performative — establishing his credentials as a dangerous man to force Claudius into full disclosure

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Good Laertes, / If you desire to know the certainty / Of your dear father, is ’t writ in your revenge / That, swoopstake, you will draw both friend and foe, / Winner and loser?

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: None but his enemies.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Will you know them, then?

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: To his good friends thus wide I’ll ope my arms / And, like the kind life-rend’ring pelican, / Repast them with my blood.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Why, now you speak / Like a good child and a true gentleman. / That I am guiltless of your father’s death / And am most sensibly in grief for it, / It shall as level to your judgment ’pear / As day does to your eye.

beat_states.desire_state: To channel Laertes' rage away from himself and toward Hamlet by appearing innocent, rational, and sympathetic
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne and conceal his crimes at all costs
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes' blind fury could turn on him at any moment if he suspects Claudius's involvement in Polonius's death

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is gaining control of the situation. His questioning strategy is working — Laertes is becoming more rational and discriminating. The danger is receding but not gone; he maintains heightened alertness beneath his calm exterior.

social_state.rationale: He strategically positions himself as a benevolent father figure ('good child and a true gentleman'), restoring his authority by praising Laertes' reasonableness. The warmth is instrumental — every compliment is a leash.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
He himself is complicit in the chain of events leading to Polonius's death
Laertes can be manipulated through his grief and sense of honor
Laertes has just agreed to distinguish friends from enemies
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He sent Hamlet to England with orders for his execution
His spying scheme with Polonius contributed directly to Polonius's death
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has full knowledge of the situation and is masterfully controlling the flow of information. He knows exactly how to frame the narrative to redirect Laertes' vengeance toward Hamlet.

beat_states.defense_state: Projecting paternal warmth and innocence to deflect suspicion; using logical Socratic questioning to guide Laertes toward a controlled conclusion
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is the architect of the circumstances that led to Polonius's death, yet he performs grief and guiltlessness with practiced conviction
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may also be genuinely relieved and experiencing real emotion — the threat of being overthrown or killed by Laertes was very real moments ago

beat_states.desire_state: To identify and destroy whoever is responsible for his father's death while honoring his father's true allies
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To restore his family's honor and avenge his father at any cost
beat_states.obstacle: He doesn't yet know who specifically killed his father; his rage is powerful but unfocused, making him susceptible to manipulation

affect_state.rationale: Still deeply grieving and enraged but beginning to organize his emotion under Claudius's guidance. His grand pelican metaphor reveals the intensity of his devotion and the rawness of his pain. He is calming but still volatile.

social_state.rationale: He has implicitly ceded authority back to Claudius by engaging in dialogue rather than violence. His warmth toward his father's 'good friends' is passionate and genuine, but he is unwittingly subordinating himself to Claudius's manipulation.

known_facts: His father Polonius is dead
His sister Ophelia has gone mad
He returned from France to find his family destroyed
false_beliefs: Claudius may be innocent and a genuine ally in his quest for revenge
The King's sympathy and grief are sincere
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is operating with a critical information deficit. He does not know Hamlet is the killer, nor does he understand Claudius's role. His emotional state makes him highly susceptible to Claudius's framing of events.

beat_states.defense_state: Channeling overwhelming grief into a code of honor — distinguishing friends from foes gives him structure amid chaos
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has just declared 'to hell, allegiance' and renounced all vows, yet here he is being drawn back into a framework of loyalty and rational discrimination by the very king he stormed in to confront
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may retain some suspicion of Claudius even while appearing to be won over — his compliance could be partly strategic, waiting to gather more information before committing fully

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: A noise within: “ Let her come in!”

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: How now, what noise is that? / O heat, dry up my brains! Tears seven times salt / Burn out the sense and virtue of mine eye! / By heaven, thy madness shall be paid with weight / Till our scale turn the beam! O rose of May, / Dear maid, kind sister, sweet Ophelia! / O heavens, is ’t possible a young maid’s wits / Should be as mortal as an old man’s life? / Nature is fine in love, and, where ’tis fine, / It sends some precious instance of itself / After the thing it loves.

beat_states.desire_state: to comprehend and respond to the devastating sight of Ophelia's madness, to hold onto his grief and channel it toward revenge
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father's death and restore his family's honor
beat_states.obstacle: the overwhelming emotional shock of seeing his sister's madness fractures his rage and replaces it with unbearable grief

affect_state.rationale: The sight of Ophelia's madness overwhelms his carefully constructed rage. He is emotionally shattered — asking his own brains to dry up, acknowledging tears burning his eyes. He has lost all composure and control.

social_state.rationale: His status as threatening rebel leader evaporates as he is reduced to a grieving brother. His warmth toward Ophelia is immense — 'rose of May,' 'dear maid, kind sister, sweet Ophelia' — the tenderest language in his vocabulary.

known_facts: His father Polonius is dead
Ophelia has gone mad
Someone is responsible for both catastrophes
The King has offered to let neutral friends judge his innocence
hidden_secrets: He does not yet know who killed his father
false_beliefs: He may still implicitly suspect the King is responsible for his father's death
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes now sees the full scope of his family's destruction — father murdered, sister mad — but still lacks the specific knowledge of Hamlet's culpability. His understanding of the situation is emotionally overwhelming but informationally incomplete.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — he tries to philosophize about love and nature to manage the raw pain of witnessing Ophelia's madness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He came storming in as an avenger demanding blood, but Ophelia's madness collapses him into tender, helpless grief — his warrior posture dissolves into weeping vulnerability
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: One could read Laertes as performing grief strategically to bolster his case for revenge in front of the court, but the raw language and the way it breaks his rhetorical pattern strongly suggests genuine emotional collapse.

beat_states.desire_state: to allow Ophelia entry to the scene, to advocate for her presence
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: a functional voice facilitating the dramatic confrontation between Laertes and Ophelia
beat_states.obstacle: whatever barrier (door, guards) that prevents Ophelia's entrance

affect_state.rationale: The offstage voice expresses urgency and sympathy — likely one of Laertes' followers or an attendant moved by Ophelia's pitiful state.

social_state.rationale: Low-status voice (servant, follower, or crowd member) expressing compassion by demanding Ophelia be admitted.

known_facts: Ophelia is outside and wishes to enter
She is visibly distressed or mad
epistemic_state.rationale: Minimal epistemic content — this is a functional voice whose knowledge is limited to the immediate situation.

beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Could be Horatio, a gentleman, or one of Laertes' rebel followers — the text is deliberately ambiguous about who speaks.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: They bore him barefaced on the bier, / Hey non nonny, nonny, hey nonny, / And in his grave rained many a tear. / Fare you well, my dove.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Hadst thou thy wits and didst persuade revenge, / It could not move thus.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: You must sing “ A-down a-down”— and you “ Call him a-down-a.”— O, how the wheel becomes it! It is the false steward that stole his master’s daughter.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: This nothing’s more than matter.

beat_states.desire_state: to endure witnessing his sister's madness while channeling the horror into fuel for revenge
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father's death and restore his family's honor — Ophelia's madness makes this imperative unbearable and absolute
beat_states.obstacle: Ophelia's madness is more devastating than any rational argument; it disarms his rage and replaces it with anguish he cannot act upon

affect_state.rationale: Devastated witnessing his sister's condition; high arousal from grief and rage combined. He is trying to maintain control but openly failing — his aside-like observations reveal a man barely holding himself together.

social_state.rationale: As elder brother he retains nominal authority but is rendered powerless by her condition. His warmth and tenderness toward Ophelia are fully exposed — he cannot hide his love.

known_facts: His father is dead
Ophelia has gone mad
Her madness is connected to their father's death
Her nonsense contains meaning ('This nothing's more than matter')
hidden_secrets: He does not yet know Claudius killed Polonius — he still likely suspects the King
false_beliefs: He may still believe the King is directly responsible for Polonius's death
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is piecing together the situation. He recognizes Ophelia's mad speech carries coded truths but cannot yet decode them fully. The King has not yet redirected his rage toward Hamlet.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — he tries to frame her madness analytically ('This nothing's more than matter') to maintain composure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He acknowledges her madness is more persuasive than sanity ('It could not move thus') — the very thing that should weaken his resolve is what strengthens it most, yet it also immobilizes him with grief
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: 'This nothing's more than matter' could be performed not just as grief observation but as a direct political aside to the audience/King — weaponizing Ophelia's condition as evidence demanding retribution.

beat_states.desire_state: to express and process her father's death through the only language left to her — fragmented song and parable
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be heard and loved in a world that has betrayed and abandoned her
beat_states.obstacle: her shattered mind cannot articulate grief directly; coherence is lost

affect_state.rationale: Deep grief expressed through eerie calm of madness; the singing creates a dissociated, drifting quality (moderate arousal) masking devastating pain. She has no control over her own mind or situation.

social_state.rationale: Stripped of all social standing by madness, yet her songs carry a tender, almost maternal warmth ('Fare you well, my dove') — she still reaches toward connection even in dissolution.

known_facts: Her father is dead and buried
Someone bore him on a bier
There was mourning at his grave
hidden_secrets: The buried knowledge of who killed her father and how may be encoded in her 'false steward' parable
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia knows the essential fact of her father's death and burial, and her mad speech may encode truths she cannot consciously articulate — the 'false steward' could be an oblique accusation.

beat_states.defense_state: dissociation — retreating into song and oblique narrative to survive unbearable grief
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she is simultaneously present (the content of her songs directly addresses her father's funeral) and absent (she cannot engage rationally with anyone in the room)
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The 'false steward that stole his master's daughter' could refer to Hamlet's seduction and abandonment of her rather than to Claudius, making this a conflation of her two griefs.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: There’s rosemary, that’s for remembrance. Pray you, love, remember. And there is pansies, that’s for thoughts.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: A document in madness: thoughts and remembrance fitted.

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: Sings. For bonny sweet Robin is all my joy. / There’s fennel for you, and columbines. There’s rue for you, and here’s some for me; we may call it herb of grace o’ Sundays. You must wear your rue with a difference. There’s a daisy. I would give you some violets, but they withered all when my father died. They say he made a good end.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Thought and afflictions, passion, hell itself / She turns to favor and to prettiness.

beat_states.desire_state: to endure witnessing his sister's madness without breaking down, while processing the full horror of what has been done to his family
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father's death and restore his family's honor — Ophelia's madness intensifies his need for vengeance by showing the full scope of destruction
beat_states.obstacle: he is paralyzed by grief and helplessness; he cannot fix Ophelia's mind, and her beauty-in-madness disarms his rage, leaving him raw

affect_state.rationale: Deeply pained, oscillating between analytical distance and overwhelming sorrow; his aside 'She turns to favor and to prettiness' reveals he is barely holding together, moved almost to the breaking point

social_state.rationale: He retains some status as the grieving eldest son and recent rebel leader, but here he is reduced to a helpless brother; his warmth toward Ophelia is profound and unguarded

known_facts: His father Polonius is dead
Ophelia has gone mad from grief
The King claims innocence in Polonius's death
Ophelia's mad speech contains real meaning — 'thoughts and remembrance fitted'
hidden_secrets: He has already raised a rebellion and stormed the castle — his political leverage is active
false_beliefs: He may believe the King is entirely innocent of his father's death, having been partially persuaded by Claudius's earlier rhetoric
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes recognizes the coherence within Ophelia's madness but does not yet know who killed his father; the King is actively manipulating his understanding

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — he frames her madness as a 'document,' analyzing it to create distance from the emotional devastation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he wants to channel everything into rage and action, but Ophelia's fragile beauty keeps pulling him into tender grief that weakens his resolve for revenge
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes's intellectualizing asides could be read as performed stoicism for the court audience rather than genuine coping mechanism, but the intimacy of the moment suggests authentic emotional processing

beat_states.desire_state: to communicate truths about betrayal, grief, and moral corruption that she cannot speak directly
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be witnessed and understood in a world that has destroyed every source of love and meaning she had
beat_states.obstacle: her fractured mind cannot organize direct speech; the trauma of her father's death and Hamlet's rejection have shattered her rational faculties

affect_state.rationale: Deep grief pervades but expressed with an eerie gentleness; she drifts between lucidity and fragmentation, utterly exposed, with no social armor left

social_state.rationale: She has lost all courtly status — she is now the mad girl to be pitied — but her gestures of giving flowers retain a tender, generous warmth even as many carry stinging symbolic meanings

known_facts: Her father is dead
She has been abandoned by Hamlet
The court is full of deception and moral corruption
Each flower carries specific symbolic meaning — fennel/columbines for flattery and infidelity, rue for regret, rosemary for remembrance
hidden_secrets: Her flower distributions are targeted accusations against specific people (fennel and columbines likely aimed at the King, rue at the Queen or herself)
She may understand more about the circumstances of her father's death than anyone realizes
epistemic_state.rationale: Ophelia's madness is a veil over acute perception; she knows the moral landscape of the court and encodes it in botanical symbolism, though she may not fully organize this knowledge consciously

beat_states.defense_state: displacement — channeling unbearable knowledge into the symbolic language of flowers, allowing madness to speak what sanity cannot
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she appears helpless and scattered yet each flower is a precisely aimed accusation or lament — her madness contains more truth than anyone else's sanity
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Ophelia may be purely dissociated with no intentional targeting — the flowers random rather than aimed — but the textual tradition and specificity of the symbolism strongly suggest coded communication

utterances.speaker: OPHELIA
utterances.text: And of all Christians’ souls, I pray God. God be wi’ you. / And will he not come again? And will he not come again? No, no, he is dead. Go to thy deathbed. He never will come again. / His beard was as white as snow, All flaxen was his poll. He is gone, he is gone, And we cast away moan. God ’a mercy on his soul.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Do you see this, O God?

beat_states.desire_state: to demand that God and the world witness the atrocity of his sister's destruction
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father's death and hold those responsible accountable — seeing Ophelia's madness intensifies this drive to a sacred imperative
beat_states.obstacle: he is powerless to heal Ophelia or undo what has been done; his rage has no immediate target in the room

affect_state.rationale: His single line is a raw cry — barely a sentence, addressed to God. The brevity indicates he is overwhelmed and nearly unable to speak. High arousal from rage and grief combined. He is certain of the injustice but has no control over the situation. Deeply vulnerable despite his warrior posture.

social_state.rationale: Despite having arrived as a would-be avenger with a mob behind him, he is reduced to a grieving brother. His warmth toward Ophelia is acute — her suffering is more devastating to him than his father's death.

known_facts: Ophelia has gone mad
his father is dead
Claudius claims innocence in Polonius's death
false_beliefs: he may still partly believe Claudius bears direct responsibility for Polonius's death, or is being manipulated toward believing Hamlet alone is to blame
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is in an epistemic fog — he knows the effects (dead father, mad sister) but the full causal chain remains unclear. This makes him manipulable, which Claudius will soon exploit.

beat_states.defense_state: converting grief into righteous fury by appealing to divine witness — channeling unbearable sorrow into accusation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he wants to act violently but is paralyzed by tenderness; the warrior in him is unmanned by his sister's gentle madness
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: His appeal to God could be read less as indictment and more as a man whose faith is cracking — questioning whether God sees or cares at all.

beat_states.desire_state: to complete the ritual mourning for her father and release him to God's mercy
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to process the unbearable losses of father and lover through the only language left to her — fragmented song and ritual
beat_states.obstacle: her shattered mind cannot hold coherent grief; the finality of death keeps breaking through her madness

affect_state.rationale: This is Ophelia's most lucid moment of grief — the song moves from denial to acceptance to benediction. The arousal is lower than earlier beats because she has arrived at a strange, exhausted peace. The certainty is higher because she finally confronts the finality: 'he never will come again.' She is completely undefended.

social_state.rationale: She has no social standing left — she is the mad daughter praying for the dead. Yet her warmth is genuine; she prays for 'all Christians' souls' and blesses those present with 'God be wi' you,' extending grace even in her brokenness.

known_facts: her father is dead
he will never return
he was old (white beard, flaxen hair)
hidden_secrets: the circumstances of Polonius's death at Hamlet's hands may flicker in her fractured awareness
epistemic_state.rationale: In this beat Ophelia achieves painful clarity about the one central fact — her father's death is permanent. The song's details (white beard, flaxen hair) show she remembers him vividly.

beat_states.defense_state: dissociation through song and prayer — transforming raw grief into liturgical formula
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she oscillates between desperate denial ('will he not come again?') and absolute acceptance ('no, no, he is dead') within the same breath
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The 'he' in the song could conflate Polonius with Hamlet (who has also 'gone' from her life), making this a double elegy for father and lover simultaneously.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Laertes, I must commune with your grief, / Or you deny me right. Go but apart, / Make choice of whom your wisest friends you will, / And they shall hear and judge ’twixt you and me. / If by direct or by collateral hand / They find us touched, we will our kingdom give, / Our crown, our life, and all that we call ours, / To you in satisfaction; but if not, / Be you content to lend your patience to us, / And we shall jointly labor with your soul / To give it due content.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Let this be so. / His means of death, his obscure funeral / ( No trophy, sword, nor hatchment o’er his bones, / No noble rite nor formal ostentation) / Cry to be heard, as ’twere from heaven to earth, / That I must call ’t in question.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: So you shall, / And where th’ offense is, let the great ax fall. / I pray you, go with me.

beat_states.desire_state: To redirect Laertes' rage away from himself and toward Hamlet by appearing transparent and just
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne, his crown, and his life by concealing his guilt and eliminating threats
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes' volatile fury and suspicion could expose Claudius or destabilize the kingdom

affect_state.rationale: Claudius has regained composure after Ophelia's devastating scene. He senses opportunity in Laertes' grief—a weapon to aim at Hamlet. His confidence is high because he knows the 'collateral hand' investigation will implicate Hamlet, not himself. Moderate arousal from the danger still present but increasingly managed.

social_state.rationale: Reasserts kingly authority through magnanimous offer, but performs warmth and solidarity ('commune with your grief,' 'jointly labor with your soul') to bind Laertes to him as ally rather than enemy

known_facts: He killed Polonius's father (Old Hamlet), not Polonius directly
Hamlet killed Polonius
Hamlet has been sent to England with orders for his execution
Ophelia's madness has inflamed Laertes further
Laertes' grief can be weaponized against Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He murdered Old Hamlet
He has sent Hamlet to England to be killed
He is guilty of the broader corruption Laertes senses
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius operates with near-complete information and is several steps ahead of Laertes. His offer of a fair tribunal is a calculated performance because he already knows who killed Polonius and is prepared to let the 'great ax' fall on Hamlet.

beat_states.defense_state: Projection of total innocence through an extravagant wager of crown and life, knowing the evidence will point elsewhere
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Offers to submit to judgment while already planning to manipulate the outcome; performs vulnerability from a position of calculated control
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may feel genuine relief at having survived the mob and Laertes' direct confrontation, making his offer partly sincere rather than entirely calculated

beat_states.desire_state: To obtain justice and public acknowledgment for his father's dishonorable, concealed death
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder and restore the honor of the Polonius family
beat_states.obstacle: He does not yet know who killed his father or why the funeral was so shamefully handled; the King's apparent reasonableness checks his impulse to violence

affect_state.rationale: Still deeply grieving and enraged after witnessing Ophelia's madness. His arousal has lowered from the mob-storming peak but remains high. Low certainty—he doesn't know who to blame. He's partially surrendering control to the King's proposed process, which makes him vulnerable to manipulation.

social_state.rationale: Has been maneuvered from insurrectionist back to supplicant. His status has dropped as he accepts the King's framework. Warmth is low—he remains suspicious and grieving, not yet allied.

known_facts: His father is dead
The funeral was conducted without proper honors or ceremony
Ophelia has gone mad
Something is deeply wrong at the Danish court
false_beliefs: The King may be directly responsible for Polonius's death
A fair investigation will yield true justice
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is operating in an information vacuum, which is precisely what makes him susceptible to Claudius's manipulation. He does not yet know Hamlet is the killer. His false belief that the process will be fair makes him a pawn.

beat_states.defense_state: Channeling overwhelming grief into righteous procedural indignation about the funeral rites, maintaining emotional control through specificity of complaint
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Accepts the King's judicial framework while still burning with rage that may not be satisfied by any fair process; his earlier vow to damn conscience is now being tamed into legalistic patience
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may be accepting the King's offer strategically—appearing to agree while privately reserving the right to act outside the law if the process fails him

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: What are they that would speak with me?

utterances.speaker: GENTLEMAN
utterances.text: Seafaring men, sir. They say they have letters for you.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Let them come in. Gentleman exits. I do not know from what part of the world I should be greeted, if not from Lord Hamlet.

beat_states.desire_state: to fulfill his duty by announcing the visitors and relaying their request
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve the court and its members faithfully as a reliable messenger
beat_states.obstacle: no significant obstacle — a straightforward errand

affect_state.rationale: The Gentleman is emotionally neutral, performing a routine duty. He is certain of the information he carries and experiences no significant emotional stakes in this exchange.

social_state.rationale: He is subordinate to Horatio in this interaction, acting as a go-between. He is politely deferential, addressing Horatio as 'sir.'

known_facts: Seafaring men have arrived
They claim to have letters for Horatio
epistemic_state.rationale: The Gentleman knows only what the sailors told him. He has no deeper knowledge of the political intrigues or of Hamlet's situation.

beat_states.desire_state: to identify who is seeking him and whether they bring news from Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to faithfully serve and protect Hamlet's interests and uncover the truth
beat_states.obstacle: uncertainty about the source of the message and anxiety about Hamlet's fate at sea

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is in a state of vigilant uncertainty. He is hopeful the letters are from Hamlet but anxious given the dangers Hamlet faces. His quick deduction that the letters must be from Hamlet reveals both his sharp mind and his preoccupation with his friend's welfare.

social_state.rationale: Horatio occupies a modest social position — a scholar, not a nobleman — but carries authority as Hamlet's trusted confidant. He is civil but restrained with the Gentleman, focused on the task at hand.

known_facts: Hamlet was sent to England by Claudius
Claudius is dangerous and may intend harm to Hamlet
Hamlet entrusted Horatio as his closest ally
Seafaring men have arrived with letters for him
hidden_secrets: Knowledge of Claudius's guilt in King Hamlet's murder
Awareness of Hamlet's suspicions and plans
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio possesses critical knowledge about the political conspiracy at Elsinore. He immediately deduces the letters are from Hamlet, showing his awareness of who would contact him and from where. He has no false beliefs — he is one of the most epistemically clear characters in the play.

beat_states.defense_state: cautious guardedness — screening visitors before admitting them
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: maintains outward composure while internally anxious about Hamlet's safety and the dangerous political situation in Denmark
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's aside could also reflect a moment of loneliness — he has no one else in the world who would send him letters, underscoring his isolation as a foreigner at the Danish court.

utterances.speaker: SAILOR
utterances.text: God bless you, sir.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Let Him bless thee too.

utterances.speaker: SAILOR
utterances.text: He shall, sir, an ’t please Him. There’s a letter for you, sir. It came from th’ ambassador that was bound for England— if your name be Horatio, as I am let to know it is.

beat_states.desire_state: to learn what message these strangers carry and from whom
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve Hamlet faithfully and uncover the truth of the corruption in Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: uncertainty about who sent the letter and whether this is trustworthy

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is in a state of suspended anxiety — Hamlet has been sent to England under suspicious circumstances, and a letter from 'the ambassador' sparks cautious hope mixed with dread

social_state.rationale: Horatio is a gentleman and scholar addressing a common sailor; he is gracious and reciprocates the blessing, maintaining warmth without condescension

known_facts: Hamlet was sent to England with Rosencrantz and Guildenstern
Claudius arranged the voyage under suspicious pretexts
Something is deeply wrong in Denmark
hidden_secrets: He knows Claudius killed King Hamlet (from the Ghost revelation)
He suspects the England voyage may be a death trap
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio does not yet know the contents of the letter or that Hamlet has escaped; the mention of 'the ambassador bound for England' immediately signals this concerns Hamlet

beat_states.defense_state: measured caution — responds politely but guardedly, not revealing eagerness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: maintaining calm courtesy while internally anxious about Hamlet's fate at sea
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may already suspect Hamlet has found a way to communicate and feels relief rather than surprise

beat_states.desire_state: to deliver the letter to the correct person and fulfill his duty
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to complete the task entrusted to him by the sender (Hamlet) reliably
beat_states.obstacle: needs to confirm the identity of the recipient before handing over the letter

affect_state.rationale: The sailor is simply performing a task; he is calm, practical, and mildly cautious about getting the right man, with the mild pleasure of completing a duty

social_state.rationale: A common sailor addressing a gentleman — he is deferential ('sir' repeated multiple times) but friendly and open in manner, with the easy warmth of a man without agenda

known_facts: He carries a letter from someone described as an ambassador bound for England
The letter is addressed to someone named Horatio
He was given instructions to find this person
false_beliefs: He may believe the sender is simply an ambassador, not knowing the full political circumstances
epistemic_state.rationale: The sailor is a messenger with limited knowledge of the court intrigue; he knows only what he was told in order to make the delivery

beat_states.defense_state: deferential formality — uses blessing and politeness as social lubrication with a stranger of higher station
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The sailor may know more than he lets on — Hamlet may have briefed him — but the text suggests straightforward delivery

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Come, I will give you way for these your letters / And do ’t the speedier that you may direct me / To him from whom you brought them. / Horatio, when thou shalt have overlooked this, give these fellows some means to the King. They have letters for him. Ere we were two days old at sea, a pirate of very warlike appointment gave us chase. Finding ourselves too slow of sail, we put on a compelled valor, and in the grapple I boarded them. On the instant, they got clear of our ship; so I alone became their prisoner. They have dealt with me like thieves of mercy, but they knew what they did: I am to do a good turn for them. Let the King have the letters I have sent, and repair thou to me with as much speed as thou wouldst fly death. I have words to speak in thine ear will make thee dumb; yet are they much too light for the bore of the matter. These good fellows will bring thee where I am. Rosencrantz and Guildenstern hold their course for England; of them I have much to tell thee. Farewell. He that thou knowest thine, Hamlet.

beat_states.desire_state: To act immediately on Hamlet's instructions — get the sailors to the King and reach Hamlet as fast as possible
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To faithfully serve and protect Hamlet, the person he loves and trusts above all
beat_states.obstacle: The urgency and incompleteness of the information — Hamlet hints at enormous revelations but withholds them, creating anxiety; Horatio must coordinate multiple logistics quickly

affect_state.rationale: The letter brings the enormous positive news that Hamlet lives, driving valence up, but Hamlet's references to pirates, being taken prisoner, the cryptic warning about unspeakable news, and the mention of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern create high arousal and significant uncertainty. Horatio takes charge of the logistics (some control), but he is deeply dependent on Hamlet's fate (vulnerability).

social_state.rationale: Horatio steps into a position of authority — directing the sailors, executing Hamlet's plan — elevating his functional status. His warmth is high: he reads the letter aloud with evident care, promises the sailors passage, and his entire orientation is one of devoted loyalty to Hamlet.

known_facts: Hamlet is alive and in Denmark
Hamlet was captured by pirates during a sea battle
The pirates treated Hamlet mercifully in exchange for a future favor
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are continuing to England without Hamlet
Hamlet has letters for the King that must be delivered
Hamlet has devastating news he will only share in person
Hamlet needs Horatio to come to him with extreme urgency
hidden_secrets: Horatio likely suspects Claudius sent Hamlet to England with murderous intent, though the letter doesn't confirm it explicitly
Horatio knows of Hamlet's distrust of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern
epistemic_state.rationale: The letter gives Horatio a burst of new information but deliberately withholds the most critical details ('words to speak in thine ear will make thee dumb'). Horatio can infer much — that the England voyage was a trap, that something happened regarding R&G — but he doesn't yet know specifics about the altered commission or Claudius's plot to have Hamlet killed.

beat_states.defense_state: compartmentalize — suppresses his alarm and questions to focus on efficient action
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Tremendous relief that Hamlet is alive wars with dread about what Hamlet's cryptic, ominous hints ('words to speak in thine ear will make thee dumb') portend
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: One could read Horatio as more fearful than galvanized — that the cryptic urgency of Hamlet's letter paralyzes him with dread rather than mobilizes him. But the text's 'Come, I will give you way for these your letters / And do 't the speedier' strongly suggests decisive, purposeful action.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Now must your conscience my acquittance seal, / And you must put me in your heart for friend, / Sith you have heard, and with a knowing ear, / That he which hath your noble father slain / Pursued my life.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: It well appears. But tell me / Why you proceeded not against these feats, / So criminal and so capital in nature, / As by your safety, greatness, wisdom, all things else, / You mainly were stirred up.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: O, for two special reasons, / Which may to you perhaps seem much unsinewed, / But yet to me they’re strong. The Queen his mother / Lives almost by his looks, and for myself / ( My virtue or my plague, be it either which), / She is so conjunctive to my life and soul / That, as the star moves not but in his sphere, / I could not but by her. The other motive / Why to a public count I might not go / Is the great love the general gender bear him, / Who, dipping all his faults in their affection, / Work like the spring that turneth wood to stone, / Convert his gyves to graces, so that my arrows, / Too slightly timbered for so loud a wind, / Would have reverted to my bow again, / But not where I have aimed them.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: And so have I a noble father lost, / A sister driven into desp’rate terms, / Whose worth, if praises may go back again, / Stood challenger on mount of all the age / For her perfections. But my revenge will come.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Break not your sleeps for that. You must not think / That we are made of stuff so flat and dull / That we can let our beard be shook with danger / And think it pastime. You shortly shall hear more. / I loved your father, and we love ourself, / And that, I hope, will teach you to imagine— / How now? What news?

beat_states.desire_state: To bind Laertes to him as an ally against Hamlet by framing himself as a fellow victim and reasonable man
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne, his queen, and his life by eliminating all threats — especially Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes's skepticism about why Claudius hasn't already punished Hamlet, and the risk that Laertes could turn against him if he sees through the manipulation

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is under pressure but performing composure and strategic patience. He's threading a needle — admitting weakness (dependence on Gertrude, Hamlet's popularity) to appear honest, while maintaining dominance over the conversation's direction. Elevated arousal from the danger of the situation but high control in his rhetorical mastery.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as king and protector but deliberately lowers his status slightly to create intimacy and trust with Laertes, performing warmth and shared grief to recruit him.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Hamlet has been sent to England with orders for his execution
Gertrude is deeply attached to Hamlet
The public loves Hamlet
Laertes is enraged and seeking revenge
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He has already sent orders for Hamlet's execution in England
His concern is not justice for Polonius but eliminating a threat to his own power
false_beliefs: That his plan to have Hamlet killed in England is proceeding smoothly
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius knows far more than he reveals. He conceals his own guilt entirely, presenting himself as an innocent party threatened by Hamlet. He is unaware that his English plot may already have failed.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — presenting his inaction as reasoned restraint rather than guilt-driven paralysis
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to have loved Polonius and to be threatened by Hamlet, while he is actually the original murderer whose crimes set everything in motion
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may genuinely feel some affection for Gertrude that constrains his actions, making his stated reasons partially sincere rather than purely manipulative

beat_states.desire_state: To understand why his father's killer has gone unpunished, and to secure assurance that revenge is forthcoming
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder and his sister's madness — to restore family honor at any cost
beat_states.obstacle: Claudius's explanations partially satisfy him but he remains impatient and suspicious; he has no independent means to reach Hamlet

affect_state.rationale: Deep grief and rage over Polonius's death and Ophelia's madness. His certainty is low — he's being persuaded but not fully convinced. He lacks control over the situation and depends on Claudius for action. High vulnerability masked by aggressive posturing.

social_state.rationale: Laertes is a nobleman but positioned as supplicant to the king, dependent on Claudius's plan. His warmth is guarded — he's being drawn in but hasn't fully surrendered to alliance.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Ophelia has gone mad
Claudius claims Hamlet also pursued his life
The public loves Hamlet
Gertrude protects Hamlet
false_beliefs: That Claudius is an innocent victim of Hamlet's violence
That Claudius genuinely loved Polonius
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is operating on incomplete and distorted information fed to him by Claudius. He doesn't know about Old Hamlet's murder or Claudius's deeper machinations. He takes Claudius's narrative at face value.

beat_states.defense_state: Suppression of deeper grief beneath aggressive questioning and declarations of resolve
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He's allowing himself to be led by the very king whose negligence (at minimum) allowed his father to die unprotected, trusting the authority figure he should most distrust
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may harbor more suspicion of Claudius than he shows, using apparent compliance as a strategy to gain access to the king's resources for his own revenge

utterances.speaker: MESSENGER
utterances.text: Letters, my lord, from Hamlet. / These to your Majesty, this to the Queen.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: From Hamlet? Who brought them?

utterances.speaker: MESSENGER
utterances.text: Sailors, my lord, they say. I saw them not. / They were given me by Claudio. He received them / Of him that brought them.

beat_states.desire_state: to understand how Hamlet could possibly be sending letters when he should be en route to his death in England
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to secure his throne and eliminate all threats to his power, especially Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: the shocking, inexplicable return of letters from Hamlet shatters his carefully laid plan to have Hamlet killed in England

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is blindsided. His plan depended on Hamlet being delivered to execution. The letters signal catastrophic failure. His repeated question 'From Hamlet?' betrays shock he cannot fully conceal. His sense of control collapses.

social_state.rationale: He maintains royal authority in addressing the messenger but his warmth is absent — he is purely transactional, seeking information urgently. His status is still high but internally destabilized.

known_facts: He sent Hamlet to England with secret orders for his execution
Letters have arrived bearing Hamlet's name
Sailors delivered the letters through an intermediary named Claudio
hidden_secrets: He orchestrated Hamlet's murder via the English commission
He has been actively recruiting Laertes to kill Hamlet as a backup
false_beliefs: He believed Hamlet was still at sea heading to his death
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius's entire strategic framework has just been disrupted. He doesn't yet know the content of the letters or how Hamlet escaped, creating a dangerous epistemic gap.

beat_states.defense_state: masking panic with controlled questioning to gather intelligence before revealing his alarm
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he has just been confidently manipulating Laertes into believing Hamlet will never return, and now evidence of Hamlet's survival arrives mid-scheme
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius might briefly entertain the possibility that the letters are a forgery or trick, which would explain his probing questions about provenance

beat_states.desire_state: to deliver the letters and discharge his duty accurately
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve the court faithfully and avoid trouble
beat_states.obstacle: the King's intense questioning about provenance puts pressure on him to account for details he doesn't fully possess

affect_state.rationale: The messenger is relatively neutral but slightly anxious — he senses the King's shock and wants to be thorough. He admits he didn't see the sailors directly, showing honest caution rather than invention.

social_state.rationale: He is a subordinate performing a function, deferential and formal. Minimal warmth — this is a professional interaction with his sovereign.

known_facts: Letters arrived purportedly from Hamlet
One letter is for the King, one for the Queen
Sailors reportedly brought them
Claudio was the intermediary who passed them along
epistemic_state.rationale: The messenger has limited knowledge — he is a conduit, not a player. He transparently acknowledges the limits of what he knows.

beat_states.defense_state: preemptively clarifying the chain of custody to protect himself from any implication of negligence or complicity

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Laertes, you shall hear them.— / Leave us. / What should this mean? Are all the rest come back? / Or is it some abuse and no such thing?

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Know you the hand?

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: ’Tis Hamlet’s character. “ Naked”— / And in a postscript here, he says “ alone.” / Can you advise me?

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: I am lost in it, my lord. But let him come. / It warms the very sickness in my heart / That I shall live and tell him to his teeth / “ Thus didst thou.”

beat_states.desire_state: To understand Hamlet's unexpected return and quickly recalibrate his murder plot
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne and eliminate all threats to his power and security
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's return is completely unexpected, disrupting the plan to have him killed in England; he lacks information about what happened

affect_state.rationale: Deeply alarmed that his assassination plot has failed. The words 'Naked' and 'alone' puzzle him. His control over events has slipped dramatically, and he's exposed — if Hamlet knows the truth, Claudius is in mortal danger.

social_state.rationale: Still performing kingly authority (dismissing the messenger, reading aloud) but drawing Laertes in as a co-conspirator with 'Can you advise me?' — a strategic show of warmth and false equality to keep Laertes bound to him

known_facts: Hamlet has written a letter announcing his return
Hamlet claims to be 'naked' (without resources) and 'alone'
The letter is in Hamlet's handwriting
The plot to have Hamlet killed in England appears to have failed
hidden_secrets: He commissioned Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to carry letters ordering Hamlet's execution in England
He murdered King Hamlet
false_beliefs: He may believe Hamlet does not yet know about the English execution order
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius is in epistemic crisis — he doesn't know how Hamlet escaped, what happened to Rosencrantz and Guildenstern, or what Hamlet now knows. He's fishing for understanding.

beat_states.defense_state: Masking panic behind analytical composure — framing the letter as a puzzle to solve collaboratively rather than revealing his alarm
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He engineered Hamlet's death in England but must now feign bewilderment at the letter while internally scrambling to devise a new murder plan
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may also be testing whether the letter is a forgery or trap, genuinely uncertain if Hamlet is truly back

beat_states.desire_state: To confront and kill Hamlet in revenge for his father's death and sister's madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To restore family honor by avenging Polonius's murder and Ophelia's destruction
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's absence had previously prevented direct revenge; now his return resolves that obstacle but the 'how' remains unclear

affect_state.rationale: The 'sickness in my heart' reveals deep emotional pain, but the prospect of face-to-face confrontation transforms that grief into fierce, almost feverish anticipation. Arousal is high with vengeful excitement despite the confusion.

social_state.rationale: Deferential to the King ('my lord') but speaking with increasing personal authority and passion about his own vengeance. The alliance with Claudius is intact but Laertes is driven by his own fire, not Claudius's agenda.

known_facts: Hamlet killed his father Polonius
Hamlet has written announcing his return alone and without resources
The handwriting is confirmed as Hamlet's
false_beliefs: He believes Hamlet is solely and maliciously responsible for all his family's suffering
He likely does not suspect that Claudius had plotted to have Hamlet killed in England
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is being manipulated by Claudius but doesn't know it. He has no knowledge of the English plot. His understanding of events is filtered entirely through grief and rage.

beat_states.defense_state: Channeling grief and confusion into aggressive anticipation — transforming uncertainty into the pleasure of imminent confrontation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is 'lost' in confusion about Hamlet's return but simultaneously grateful for it — his intellect is baffled while his rage is satisfied
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes's eagerness to welcome Hamlet's return could also mask anxiety about whether he can actually go through with killing him face to face

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: If it be so, Laertes / ( As how should it be so? how otherwise?), / Will you be ruled by me?

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Ay, my lord, / So you will not o’errule me to a peace.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: To thine own peace. If he be now returned, / As checking at his voyage, and that he means / No more to undertake it, I will work him / To an exploit, now ripe in my device, / Under the which he shall not choose but fall; / And for his death no wind of blame shall breathe, / But even his mother shall uncharge the practice / And call it accident.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: My lord, I will be ruled, / The rather if you could devise it so / That I might be the organ.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure Laertes as his willing instrument in a plot to kill Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne, his queen, and his life by eliminating all threats
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes's independence and potential reluctance; Hamlet's unexpected return disrupts previous plans

affect_state.rationale: He is energized by the opportunity to co-opt Laertes but unsettled by Hamlet's return. He performs mastery over the situation while privately uncertain about how Hamlet escaped. His promise that 'even his mother shall uncharge the practice' reveals how carefully he must manage multiple threats.

social_state.rationale: Maintains dominant position as king and strategist, offering Laertes a role in the plot. Warmth is instrumental — pseudo-paternal guidance masking ruthless manipulation.

known_facts: Hamlet has returned to Denmark unexpectedly
Hamlet claims to be alone
Laertes is consumed with desire for revenge against Hamlet
The previous plot (sending Hamlet to England to be killed) has failed
hidden_secrets: He ordered Hamlet's execution in England via letters to the English king
His primary motive is self-preservation, not justice for Polonius
false_beliefs: That the plot can be designed so perfectly that Gertrude will call it an accident
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius knows his assassination-by-proxy failed but doesn't know how. He pivots immediately to a new scheme, revealing both his cunning and his dangerous overconfidence in controlling outcomes.

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement — framing Hamlet's murder as justice for Laertes rather than self-preservation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Projects confidence in his 'ripe device' while internally destabilized by Hamlet's inexplicable return; the parenthetical '(As how should it be so? how otherwise?)' betrays genuine bewilderment he must conceal
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The parenthetical could be read as performative confusion designed to seem transparent with Laertes rather than genuine bewilderment

beat_states.desire_state: To be the direct instrument of Hamlet's death — to kill with his own hands
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder and restore family honor at any cost
beat_states.obstacle: He needs the King's strategic mind and political cover to act without consequences

affect_state.rationale: Grief and rage still dominate but now channeled into focused intent. He feels more certain now that there is a plan taking shape, but he is dependent on Claudius and aware of it. His eagerness to be 'the organ' reveals how desperately he needs the physical act of revenge to restore his sense of agency.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to the King and accepting that position, but conditionally — his single caveat ('So you will not o'errule me to a peace') asserts a hard boundary. No genuine warmth, only transactional alliance.

known_facts: Hamlet killed his father Polonius
Hamlet has returned to Denmark
The King shares his desire to see Hamlet dead
The King has a plan already in mind
hidden_secrets: He does not know the full extent of the King's self-interested motives
false_beliefs: That the King's primary motivation is sympathy for Laertes's loss rather than self-preservation
That this alliance is based on shared justice rather than mutual exploitation
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is being manipulated but doesn't perceive it. His grief makes him a willing tool. He accepts Claudius's framing without questioning why the King also wants Hamlet dead so urgently.

beat_states.defense_state: Splitting — Hamlet is pure enemy, the King is pure ally; no moral complexity admitted
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Surrenders his autonomy ('I will be ruled') while simultaneously insisting on personal agency ('That I might be the organ') — he wants to be both obedient and sovereign in the act of killing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may be more politically savvy than he appears — he could understand Claudius's self-interest but simply not care, as long as he gets to kill Hamlet himself

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: It falls right. / You have been talked of since your travel much, / And that in Hamlet’s hearing, for a quality / Wherein they say you shine. Your sum of parts / Did not together pluck such envy from him / As did that one, and that, in my regard, / Of the unworthiest siege.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: What part is that, my lord?

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: A very ribbon in the cap of youth— / Yet needful too, for youth no less becomes / The light and careless livery that it wears / Than settled age his sables and his weeds, / Importing health and graveness. Two months since / Here was a gentleman of Normandy. / I have seen myself, and served against, the French, / And they can well on horseback, but this gallant / Had witchcraft in ’t. He grew unto his seat, / And to such wondrous doing brought his horse / As had he been encorpsed and demi-natured / With the brave beast. So far he topped my thought / That I in forgery of shapes and tricks / Come short of what he did.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: A Norman was ’t?

utterances.speaker: KING
