hidden_secrets: His own proximity to death through the revenge plot and Claudius's machinations
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is building a chain of reasoning from Yorick to Alexander, testing whether Horatio will resist the leveling logic. He knows the answer before he asks.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — converting grief and horror into a philosophical game about Alexander
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs detached philosophical inquiry while physically recoiling from the smell, revealing that his body knows what his mind tries to master
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be deliberately performing nihilism to test whether Horatio will push back or simply agree — disappointed that Horatio only confirms rather than challenges

beat_states.desire_state: To support Hamlet through this dark meditation without fueling it further or shutting it down
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Horatio's deepest want is to be a faithful, steadying companion to Hamlet — to bear witness and protect through presence
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's questions are rhetorically loaded and leave almost no room for genuine dialogue — Horatio can only confirm the obvious

affect_state.rationale: Low arousal, steady — Horatio is the calm center. Mildly negative because the topic is grim. He maintains composure but the addition of 'my lord' in the second response may signal growing concern about Hamlet's state.

social_state.rationale: Deferential ('my lord') but genuinely warm — he stays close rather than retreating from the grotesque scene. His lower status is chosen, not imposed.

known_facts: Alexander decomposed like all men
Hamlet is spiraling through morbid philosophical territory
Hamlet has been emotionally volatile since the ghost encounter
hidden_secrets: His own worry about Hamlet's mental state
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is a scholar and knows the philosophical point is valid. But he's also reading Hamlet's psychological state — the 'Pah!' would register as a concerning involuntary break.

beat_states.defense_state: emotional restraint — containing his own discomfort behind minimal, measured responses
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His brevity could signal either deep agreement or quiet discomfort with where Hamlet is heading — the repeated 'E'en so' is both loyal and evasive
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's minimal responses could indicate he is genuinely philosophically engaged and simply agrees, rather than managing Hamlet — he may be a fellow stoic contemplating mortality calmly

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: To what base uses we may return, Horatio! Why may not imagination trace the noble dust of Alexander till he find it stopping a bunghole?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: ’Twere to consider too curiously to consider so.

beat_states.desire_state: To draw Horatio into his philosophical revelation about the futility of earthly greatness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To find meaning and resolve in a world revealed as corrupt and mortal
beat_states.obstacle: The absurdity of the insight threatens to dissolve into nihilism rather than productive understanding

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is intellectually energized by this dark philosophical exercise — there's a grim pleasure in the logic, but underneath it lies genuine disturbance at mortality's reduction of all greatness to nothing

social_state.rationale: Hamlet positions himself as the intellectual leader guiding Horatio through a thought experiment, but does so with genuine collegial warmth — he wants Horatio to engage, not just listen

known_facts: Alexander the Great died and decomposed like everyone else
All flesh returns to dust
Yorick's skull has shown him the physical reality of death
Dust can be repurposed for the most base uses
hidden_secrets: He is contemplating his own potential death and legacy
He has killed Polonius and sent Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to their deaths
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's philosophical chain — Alexander to dust to bunghole — is logically sound and he knows it. The question is rhetorical, designed to test whether Horatio will follow him into this dark territory.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — converting existential dread into a philosophical game of tracing dust
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Finds morbid delight in demolishing human pretension while simultaneously being a prince whose own nobility is at stake
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be testing his own sanity by checking whether Horatio finds this line of thinking excessive or mad

beat_states.desire_state: To gently rein Hamlet back from an obsessive and potentially destabilizing line of thought
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve and protect Hamlet by being his rational, loyal anchor
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's rhetorical energy and intellectual authority make direct contradiction feel disrespectful

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is mildly uncomfortable — he senses Hamlet spiraling into morbid territory. His response is measured but carries worry beneath its philosophical phrasing

social_state.rationale: Horatio occupies the subordinate position of friend-advisor, but his warmth and care for Hamlet are evident in his careful, non-judgmental pushback

known_facts: Hamlet has been in a graveyard handling skulls and growing increasingly philosophical
Hamlet's reasoning is logically sound but excessive
Hamlet's mental state has been unstable
hidden_secrets: He may fear Hamlet is losing grip on practical reality
He knows about the ghost and Hamlet's mission
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio understands the logic but identifies the thinking as 'too curious' — too fine, too obsessive — revealing his awareness that Hamlet's mind is reaching into dangerous territory

beat_states.defense_state: diplomatic deflection — framing the objection as a matter of method rather than content to avoid confrontation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Recognizes the logical validity of Hamlet's argument while fearing where it leads emotionally
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may genuinely find the argument philosophically overdrawn rather than being primarily concerned about Hamlet's mental state

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Imperious Caesar, dead and turned to clay, / Might stop a hole to keep the wind away. / O, that that earth which kept the world in awe / Should patch a wall t’ expel the winter’s flaw! / But soft, but soft awhile! Here comes the King, / The Queen, the courtiers. Who is this they follow? / And with such maimèd rites? This doth betoken / The corse they follow did with desp’rate hand / Fordo its own life. ’Twas of some estate. / Couch we awhile and mark. / No, faith, not a jot; but to follow him thither, with modesty enough and likelihood to lead it, as thus: Alexander died, Alexander was buried, Alexander returneth to dust; the dust is earth; of earth we make loam; and why of that loam whereto he was converted might they not stop a beer barrel?
utterances.stage_direction: Enter King, Queen, Laertes, Lords attendant, and the corpse of Ophelia, with a Doctor of Divinity.

beat_states.desire_state: To complete his philosophical meditation on mortality's leveling power, then to observe and understand the approaching funeral procession
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: His obsession with the gap between appearance and reality — what things seem versus what they are — drives both his meditation on death and his need to spy on this mysterious funeral
beat_states.obstacle: The arrival of the court interrupts his philosophical reverie and forces him into hiding; his ignorance of whose funeral this is creates urgent curiosity

affect_state.rationale: The philosophical verse about Caesar carries a darkly playful energy, but the sudden shift to observing the funeral cortege spikes arousal and uncertainty. He doesn't know whose body it is. The 'desperate hand' / suicide inference shows his mind racing. Control is moderate — he can still choose to hide and watch, maintaining agency.

social_state.rationale: He is hiding from the King and court — literally lowering his status to that of a covert observer. His warmth toward Horatio remains as he shares these observations, but he is alienated from the approaching social world of the court.

known_facts: All humans — even Caesar and Alexander — return to dust
A funeral procession approaches with the King, Queen, and courtiers
The rites are 'maimed' — abbreviated or irregular
This suggests the corpse died by suicide
The person was 'of some estate' — highborn
hidden_secrets: He has returned to Denmark secretly
He is observing the court without their knowledge
He has killed Polonius
false_beliefs: He does not yet know this is Ophelia's funeral
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is piecing together clues rapidly — maimed rites, the presence of the full court, signs of suicide — but crucially lacks the identity of the deceased. This dramatic irony is about to shatter his philosophical composure.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — converting raw grief and existential terror into witty philosophical syllogisms about Alexander and Caesar; then retreating into concealment to observe rather than act
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs elaborate intellectual detachment about death's universality while simultaneously being drawn with intense emotional urgency toward this specific death — the 'maimed rites' and 'desperate hand' language betrays anxiety beneath the philosophical composure
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: An alternative reading: Hamlet's philosophical speeches about Caesar and Alexander are not mere intellectualization but a deliberate performance for Horatio — testing whether his friend will challenge his nihilism or validate it, using wit as a social bonding mechanism before the emotional devastation ahead.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: What ceremony else?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: That is Laertes, a very noble youth. Mark.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: What ceremony else?

utterances.speaker: DOCTOR
utterances.text: Her obsequies have been as far enlarged / As we have warranty. Her death was doubtful, / And, but that great command o’ersways the order, / She should in ground unsanctified been lodged / Till the last trumpet. For charitable prayers / Shards, flints, and pebbles should be thrown on her. / Yet here she is allowed her virgin crants, / Her maiden strewments, and the bringing home / Of bell and burial.

beat_states.desire_state: to defend the church's position and justify the limited rites given to Ophelia
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to uphold ecclesiastical authority and proper religious order
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes' aggressive demands and the royal command that forced expanded rites against church protocol

affect_state.rationale: The Doctor is defensive but certain of his theological ground. He is irritated by Laertes' challenge but feels fortified by doctrine. His speech is pointed—'shards, flints, and pebbles should be thrown on her'—a deliberately harsh reminder of what could have been.

social_state.rationale: He wields institutional authority but shows little pastoral warmth. His tone is cold and legalistic, almost punitive toward the dead woman and dismissive of the brother's grief.

known_facts: Ophelia's death was doubtful/suspected suicide
royal command forced expanded rites
church law would have denied her consecrated burial
epistemic_state.rationale: The Doctor has full knowledge of the ecclesiastical situation and the political pressure that shaped the compromise.

beat_states.defense_state: appeal to institutional authority as shield against personal confrontation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he has already compromised church law under royal pressure, yet still insists on the propriety of limiting the rites further
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Doctor may feel some private sympathy for Ophelia but suppresses it to maintain doctrinal authority

beat_states.desire_state: to observe and understand what is happening at this funeral, to identify the mourners
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to find truth and meaning in a world of corruption and mortality
beat_states.obstacle: he is hidden and does not yet know whose funeral this is; he is an outsider to this grief

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet shifts from philosophical detachment to heightened attention. Recognizing Laertes produces a note of genuine respect ('a very noble youth') but also unease—he senses something significant is unfolding but doesn't yet know the full truth.

social_state.rationale: Hidden and observing, Hamlet's status is temporarily low (he's concealed, watching). His warmth toward Laertes is genuine—he calls him noble—suggesting residual admiration and perhaps guilt.

known_facts: Laertes is present at a funeral
this is a significant ceremony
he has been away and returned to Denmark
hidden_secrets: he killed Polonius, Laertes' father
he does not yet know Ophelia is dead
false_beliefs: he may not yet suspect this funeral is for someone he loved
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet recognizes Laertes but has not yet pieced together that this is Ophelia's funeral. His ignorance is about to be shattered.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization—shifting into observational mode to manage rising emotional stakes
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he has just been philosophizing coolly about death, but recognizing Laertes signals an approaching emotional collision he is not prepared for
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may already suspect whose funeral this is and is bracing himself emotionally

beat_states.desire_state: to demand full funeral rites for Ophelia, to challenge the inadequacy of the ceremony
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to honor and avenge his family against those who destroyed them
beat_states.obstacle: the church has curtailed the funeral rites due to the suspicious nature of Ophelia's death

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is in acute grief, barely containing his anguish. His repeated demand 'What ceremony else?' betrays both fury and desperation—he channels raw sorrow into aggressive insistence on what is owed to his sister.

social_state.rationale: As a nobleman and grieving brother, he commands social authority, but his warmth toward the Doctor/Priest is hostile—he treats the clergyman as an obstacle to proper honor.

known_facts: Ophelia is dead
the funeral rites are being truncated
her death was ambiguous—possibly suicide
hidden_secrets: he has conspired with Claudius to kill Hamlet with a poisoned sword
false_beliefs: Hamlet is solely responsible for all his family's suffering
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes knows the church suspects suicide but refuses to accept any diminishment of his sister's honor.

beat_states.defense_state: displacement of grief into righteous indignation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he demands ceremony while knowing the circumstances of her death may not warrant it

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Must there no more be done?

utterances.speaker: DOCTOR
utterances.text: No more be done. / We should profane the service of the dead / To sing a requiem and such rest to her / As to peace-parted souls.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Lay her i’ th’ earth, / And from her fair and unpolluted flesh / May violets spring! I tell thee, churlish priest, / A minist’ring angel shall my sister be / When thou liest howling.

beat_states.desire_state: to maintain ecclesiastical law and close the matter firmly
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to uphold the authority and doctrine of the church regardless of social pressure
beat_states.obstacle: a volatile, grief-stricken nobleman demanding more than the church can sanctify

affect_state.rationale: The Doctor (priest) is firm and doctrinally certain but somewhat tense under Laertes' aggression. He maintains composure through institutional authority but is not entirely comfortable delivering this harsh verdict at a graveside.

social_state.rationale: He wields religious authority that even a nobleman must nominally respect, granting him status. But his coldness in this moment — denying a requiem — reads as lacking warmth and pastoral care.

known_facts: Ophelia's death was 'doubtful' — possibly suicide
church law forbids full rites for suicides
royal command has already stretched what rites are permitted
hidden_secrets: he likely knows the full rites were curtailed specifically because of suicide, a fact the court may be trying to obscure
epistemic_state.rationale: The priest is well-informed about the ecclesiastical and political circumstances. He knows the king's intervention already secured more than the church would normally allow.

beat_states.defense_state: institutional authority — hiding behind doctrine to deflect personal responsibility for the judgment
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he may feel human sympathy for the bereaved family while being bound to enforce a harsh theological ruling
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The priest might be taking a degree of personal satisfaction in his moral authority, using doctrine as a passive-aggressive weapon against a family he considers tainted by suicide.

beat_states.desire_state: to demand full funeral rites and sacred honor for Ophelia
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to restore and defend his family's honor against all who have wronged them
beat_states.obstacle: the church refuses full rites due to the suspicious nature of Ophelia's death

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is in acute grief that erupts as fury. He feels powerless against the church's authority but compensates with moral certainty about his sister's goodness. His volcanic anger barely conceals devastating vulnerability.

social_state.rationale: As a nobleman he has some status, but the priest's refusal diminishes him publicly. His warmth toward the priest is hostile and contemptuous, while his tenderness for Ophelia bleeds through in the violet imagery.

known_facts: Ophelia is dead
the church is limiting her funeral rites
her death was likely suicide or at least questionable
the priest considers full rites inappropriate
hidden_secrets: he has conspired with Claudius to kill Hamlet
false_beliefs: he may believe Ophelia's death is entirely Hamlet's fault rather than a complex convergence of causes
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes knows the church's judgment implies suicide but refuses to accept this framing. His secret plot with Claudius sits uneasily alongside his genuine grief.

beat_states.defense_state: idealization — elevating Ophelia to angelic purity to counter the church's implicit judgment of suicide
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: his rage at the priest exposes his grief's helplessness; he asserts Ophelia's sanctity while knowing the circumstances of her death undermine his claim
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes could be performing grief publicly to build sympathy and further justify his revenge against Hamlet, though the raw emotion reads as genuine.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What, the fair Ophelia?

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Sweets to the sweet, farewell! / I hoped thou shouldst have been my Hamlet’s wife; / I thought thy bride-bed to have decked, sweet maid, / And not have strewed thy grave.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: O, treble woe / Fall ten times treble on that cursèd head / Whose wicked deed thy most ingenious sense / Deprived thee of!— Hold off the earth awhile, / Till I have caught her once more in mine arms. / Now pile your dust upon the quick and dead, / Till of this flat a mountain you have made / T’ o’ertop old Pelion or the skyish head / Of blue Olympus.

beat_states.desire_state: to comprehend the shocking revelation that the dead person being buried is Ophelia
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: his quest for justice and meaning is now colliding with personal grief and the consequences of his actions
beat_states.obstacle: the sudden, overwhelming realization that Ophelia is dead shatters his philosophical detachment

affect_state.rationale: The terse, stunned half-line 'What, the fair Ophelia?' reveals a man whose defenses have been stripped away in an instant. He is blindsided, vulnerable, and his prior composure is shattered.

social_state.rationale: The word 'fair' reveals lingering tenderness and admiration. He is no longer performing or controlling the scene — he is a shocked bystander, diminished in status.

known_facts: Ophelia is dead
This is her funeral
Laertes is grieving her
He himself treated Ophelia cruelly
hidden_secrets: He loved Ophelia deeply despite his rejection of her
He is disguised among the mourners
epistemic_state.rationale: The question format suggests he is just now processing the identity of the corpse — the knowledge is arriving in real time.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization collapses — his brief question is naked shock
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: moments ago he was philosophizing abstractly about death; now death has become unbearably personal and specific
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Hamlet already suspected but the confirmation still shocks; the line could also contain an element of guilt-driven denial.

beat_states.desire_state: to physically embrace Ophelia one last time and curse the person responsible for her death
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Laertes seeks total vengeance for his family's destruction — his grief fuels his resolve to destroy Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: the finality of burial — the earth is about to separate him from his sister forever

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is at peak emotional intensity — cursing, weeping, physically leaping into the grave. His arousal is extreme, his valence deeply negative, and though he is certain in his rage, he is losing behavioral control.

social_state.rationale: His warmth toward Ophelia is overwhelming — he literally embraces her corpse. His status is complicated: he commands attention through the spectacle of his grief but is also debasing himself publicly by leaping into a grave.

known_facts: Ophelia is dead
Someone's 'wicked deed' drove her mad
She was denied full funeral rites
hidden_secrets: He has already conspired with Claudius to kill Hamlet with a poisoned sword
false_beliefs: He likely believes Hamlet is solely and directly responsible for Ophelia's madness and death
epistemic_state.rationale: The 'cursèd head' he references is almost certainly Hamlet. His conspiracy with Claudius sharpens his grief into weaponized resolve, though here it manifests as raw sorrow.

beat_states.defense_state: grandiose rhetorical excess as a container for unbearable grief
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: his extravagant language ('Pelion,' 'Olympus') inflates grief to mythic proportions, which simultaneously expresses and distances him from raw pain

beat_states.desire_state: to honor Ophelia with maternal tenderness and mourn the future that was lost
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Gertrude wants familial harmony and happiness — she genuinely wished Ophelia to be Hamlet's wife, a union that would have restored order
beat_states.obstacle: death has made all her hopes for reconciliation and normalcy impossible

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude's speech is deeply sorrowful but measured — she channels grief through the ritual gesture and through articulate, tender language. Her vulnerability is high as she acknowledges shattered maternal hopes.

social_state.rationale: She speaks with queenly grace but exceptional warmth, addressing Ophelia as 'sweet maid' and expressing genuine maternal affection. She elevates Ophelia's worth by naming her as the intended bride.

known_facts: Ophelia is dead
Ophelia went mad
She had hoped for a marriage between Hamlet and Ophelia
hidden_secrets: She may suspect or know more about how Ophelia died than she reveals publicly
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude's earlier detailed account of Ophelia's drowning suggests intimate knowledge; here she focuses on what might have been rather than what happened.

beat_states.defense_state: ritualizing grief through the act of strewing flowers to maintain composure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she mourns the bride-bed she imagined while standing beside the son whose madness contributed to Ophelia's destruction
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Gertrude's tenderness masks guilt — she may feel complicit in the chain of events that destroyed Ophelia.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What is he whose grief / Bears such an emphasis, whose phrase of sorrow / Conjures the wand’ring stars and makes them stand / Like wonder-wounded hearers? This is I, / Hamlet the Dane.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: The devil take thy soul!

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Thou pray’st not well. / I prithee take thy fingers from my throat, / For though I am not splenitive and rash, / Yet have I in me something dangerous, / Which let thy wisdom fear. Hold off thy hand.

beat_states.desire_state: To assert his identity and rightful presence, to claim ownership of grief for Ophelia against Laertes' theatrical display
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To confront the corruption around him and fulfill his role as rightful prince — here, to reclaim his place in the moral and emotional world after witnessing Ophelia's burial
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes' physical attack and overwhelming public grief that seems to outperform Hamlet's own; his own guilt and shock at Ophelia's death

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is surging with grief, shock, and competitive rage at Laertes' display. He asserts control verbally ('something dangerous') but has just revealed himself impulsively, suggesting low actual control. The discovery of Ophelia's death has shattered his defenses.

social_state.rationale: He reclaims royal authority by announcing himself as 'Hamlet the Dane' — a title claim. But his warmth is negative; he is confrontational, mocking Laertes' grief before warning him off with veiled threat.

known_facts: Ophelia is dead and being buried
Laertes is publicly performing extreme grief
He has been away and has just returned to Denmark
Laertes is physically attacking him
hidden_secrets: His role in Polonius's death which contributed to Ophelia's madness
His feelings of love and guilt toward Ophelia
false_beliefs: He may believe his grief for Ophelia is equal to or greater than Laertes', not fully grasping a brother's loss
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has just learned of Ophelia's death moments ago. He knows the political landscape but is acting on impulse and emotion rather than strategic knowledge.

beat_states.defense_state: Grandiosity — inflating himself to 'Hamlet the Dane' to mask the raw vulnerability of discovering Ophelia's death; then controlled warning to reframe Laertes' attack as reckless
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is devastated by Ophelia's death and likely wracked with guilt, yet he enters with competitive bravado challenging Laertes' right to grieve; he claims composure while the situation is anything but composed
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's outburst may be less about grief-competition and more about existential self-assertion — after the graveyard meditation on mortality, he needs to declare himself alive and present, 'This is I,' as an act of radical self-definition rather than rivalry.

beat_states.desire_state: To destroy Hamlet — the man who killed his father and drove his sister to madness and death
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and sister by any means necessary; Hamlet is the singular target of all his rage and grief
beat_states.obstacle: Physical restraint likely from others, Hamlet's warning of danger, the public/sacred setting of a funeral

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is at maximum arousal — pure fury and grief combined. His certainty about Hamlet's guilt is absolute. He has lost control, going for Hamlet's throat. Despite the aggression, he is deeply vulnerable: this is a man burying his sister who has already lost his father.

social_state.rationale: Laertes holds some social sympathy as the grieving brother, but by physically attacking a prince at a funeral he risks his standing. His warmth toward Hamlet is absolute zero — pure lethal hostility.

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Ophelia went mad and drowned
Claudius has confirmed Hamlet as the cause of his family's destruction
Hamlet has just revealed himself publicly
hidden_secrets: His conspiracy with Claudius to kill Hamlet with a poisoned sword
false_beliefs: He likely believes Hamlet intentionally and maliciously destroyed his family
He trusts Claudius as an ally rather than a manipulator
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes operates with Claudius-shaped information. He knows the facts of his losses but has been manipulated about Hamlet's motivations. His secret plot with Claudius is hidden from all present.

beat_states.defense_state: Externalization — channeling unbearable grief into immediate physical violence against the clear enemy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is at his sister's funeral, a place of mourning and sacred ritual, yet he instantly abandons grief for murderous rage the moment Hamlet appears — suggesting the grief and rage are fused inseparably
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes' immediate physical attack may partly be performance for the crowd — demonstrating his commitment to revenge in front of witnesses — though the overwhelming evidence suggests pure uncontrolled rage.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Pluck them asunder.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Hamlet! Hamlet!

utterances.speaker: ALL
utterances.text: Gentlemen!

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Good my lord, be quiet.

beat_states.desire_state: To restore order and prevent bloodshed at a funeral
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Social cohesion and the propriety of a sacred occasion
beat_states.obstacle: Two noblemen are physically fighting in a grave

affect_state.rationale: Collective alarm and shock at the desecration of the funeral; a crowd energy of anxious intervention

social_state.rationale: Invoking the word 'Gentlemen' appeals to rank and civility — a collective social corrective

known_facts: Hamlet and Laertes are fighting
This is Ophelia's funeral
epistemic_state.rationale: The crowd sees only the surface chaos

beat_states.defense_state: Appeal to shared social codes ('Gentlemen') to de-escalate
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The collective cry may be more performative panic than genuine intervention

beat_states.desire_state: To calm Hamlet and prevent him from destroying himself politically and physically
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect and serve Hamlet as his truest friend and moral compass
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is consumed by grief-rage and is not in a rational state

affect_state.rationale: Deeply concerned but more controlled than others; his arousal is tempered by his philosophical discipline and his focus on practical help

social_state.rationale: 'Good my lord' maintains deference while 'be quiet' is intimate, urgent counsel — warmth expressed through protective honesty

known_facts: Hamlet killed Polonius
Claudius murdered King Hamlet
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are sent to their deaths
Hamlet's grief for Ophelia is genuine
This fight endangers Hamlet
hidden_secrets: The full extent of what Hamlet has confided about Claudius's guilt
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is the most informed and rational observer present; he knows the stakes of Hamlet's exposure

beat_states.defense_state: Maintains his characteristic composure even in crisis
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He understands Hamlet's grief but knows this outburst serves no strategic purpose and could be fatal

beat_states.desire_state: To physically separate Hamlet and Laertes before violence escalates and his plans unravel
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne and neutralize Hamlet as a threat — on his own terms, not in a public brawl
beat_states.obstacle: The physical struggle between Hamlet and Laertes is chaotic and beyond immediate verbal control

affect_state.rationale: High alarm beneath a commanding exterior; the situation is slipping dangerously out of his orchestrated control

social_state.rationale: Exercising kingly authority with a terse command, no warmth — pure crisis management

known_facts: Laertes and Hamlet are physically grappling
He has a secret plot with Laertes to kill Hamlet via poisoned sword
This public confrontation could ruin that plan
hidden_secrets: The poisoning plot with Laertes
His murder of King Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius sees with total clarity how dangerous this moment is to his carefully laid plans

beat_states.defense_state: Projecting authoritative calm to mask his deeper panic that this confrontation could expose his machinations
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He needs Laertes alive and angry at Hamlet for his poisoning plot, but cannot let this fight happen publicly where Laertes might kill or be killed prematurely

beat_states.desire_state: To reach her son emotionally and physically, to stop him from harming himself or being harmed
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect Hamlet, whom she loves above all despite his disturbing behavior
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is in a wild, grief-fueled rage and cannot hear her

affect_state.rationale: Deeply frightened, desperate; the double repetition of 'Hamlet' reveals raw maternal terror and helplessness

social_state.rationale: Her queenly status is irrelevant in this moment; she is purely a mother reaching for her child

known_facts: Hamlet is grappling with Laertes in Ophelia's grave
Hamlet's behavior has been erratic
Ophelia drowned
hidden_secrets: Her lingering guilt about her hasty remarriage
false_beliefs: She may believe Hamlet's madness is genuine rather than performed
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude operates from emotional knowledge, not strategic awareness; she doesn't know about the Claudius-Laertes plot

beat_states.defense_state: Maternal urgency overriding all social propriety
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She mourns Ophelia and understands Laertes' grief, yet her instinct is entirely to protect her son who is causing the disruption

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Why, I will fight with him upon this theme / Until my eyelids will no longer wag!

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: O my son, what theme?

beat_states.desire_state: To assert his love for Ophelia as supreme and unchallengeable against Laertes' display of grief
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To act decisively and authentically in a world of corruption and false seeming
beat_states.obstacle: He is being physically restrained; Laertes' grief challenges and rivals his own; the court witnesses his apparent madness

affect_state.rationale: Extremely high arousal — near-frenzy of grief and rage. Negative valence from the pain of Ophelia's death and confrontation with Laertes. He speaks with conviction but is losing behavioral control. Underneath the bravado lies deep vulnerability about his role in Ophelia's destruction.

social_state.rationale: He claims princely authority and superior love but his wild behavior lowers his social standing in the eyes of the court. Warmth is negative — he is combative, directing aggression outward.

known_facts: Ophelia is dead and being buried
Laertes is her brother and is consumed with grief
He himself loved Ophelia
Laertes leapt into the grave in an extravagant display of mourning
hidden_secrets: His role in Polonius's death which catalyzed Ophelia's madness
His feigned and possibly real madness
His knowledge of Claudius's guilt
false_beliefs: That fighting Laertes on this 'theme' will prove or validate his love
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows the facts of Ophelia's death but may be deceiving himself about what his combative display actually accomplishes. His guilt is sublimated into competitive grief.

beat_states.defense_state: Manic escalation — transforming grief into combative bravado to avoid collapsing into raw sorrow
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is driven by genuine love and guilt over Ophelia yet expresses it through aggression and competitive posturing, undermining the sincerity he claims
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be deliberately performing wildness to maintain his 'antic disposition' even here, using the confrontation strategically rather than being genuinely out of control

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what is driving her son's violent outburst and to de-escalate the situation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect her son and preserve familial bonds despite the disintegrating world around her
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's frenzy is incomprehensible to her; she cannot reach him emotionally; she may not fully understand the depth of his connection to Ophelia

affect_state.rationale: Deeply distressed and confused. Her question 'what theme?' reveals genuine bewilderment. She feels powerless to intervene. High vulnerability — her son is publicly unraveling at a funeral.

social_state.rationale: She retains queenly status but her warmth is high — she reaches toward Hamlet with maternal concern, using 'O my son' as an intimate, tender address in a public setting.

known_facts: Hamlet is behaving wildly at Ophelia's funeral
Hamlet and Laertes are in physical conflict
Ophelia has drowned
hidden_secrets: Her own guilt about her hasty marriage to Claudius
The impact of the closet scene on her awareness of Claudius's nature
false_beliefs: She may believe Hamlet's behavior is purely madness rather than grief mixed with guilt
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude genuinely does not seem to grasp the 'theme' — whether Hamlet fights for love of Ophelia or rivalry with Laertes. Her question is sincere, not rhetorical.

beat_states.defense_state: Bewildered maternal appeal — retreating into the role of confused mother to avoid confronting the full horror of the situation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She wants to help her son but her complicity in the marriage to Claudius has contributed to the very madness she now witnesses
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may understand more than she lets on — her question could be a tactic to slow Hamlet down by making him articulate his feelings rather than act on them

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I loved Ophelia. Forty thousand brothers / Could not with all their quantity of love / Make up my sum. What wilt thou do for her?

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: O, he is mad, Laertes!

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: For love of God, forbear him.

beat_states.desire_state: To prove the depth and authenticity of his love for Ophelia, surpassing all others — especially Laertes
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: His need to assert authentic feeling connects to his struggle against a world of seeming and corruption — proving he is not merely performing grief
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes' public display of grief threatens to claim superior love for Ophelia; the court dismisses Hamlet as mad

affect_state.rationale: This is one of Hamlet's most emotionally naked moments — the declarative simplicity of 'I loved Ophelia' breaks through his habitual irony and intellectualization. He is overwhelmed by grief, guilt, and competitive rage simultaneously. High arousal, deeply negative valence, with enormous vulnerability despite his aggressive posture.

social_state.rationale: He reclaims status as prince and lover against Laertes' challenge, but his warmth is directed only toward the memory of Ophelia — toward Laertes he is combative and dismissive

known_facts: Ophelia is dead
Laertes is her brother and claims superior grief
He did love Ophelia despite his cruel treatment of her
The court is watching this confrontation
hidden_secrets: His rejection of Ophelia was partly performed for surveillance
He killed Ophelia's father Polonius
His guilt over his role in her madness and death
false_beliefs: That the magnitude of his love can be proven through competitive declaration
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows the full tragic chain of events but cannot publicly acknowledge his culpability; he channels guilt into a competitive assertion of love

beat_states.defense_state: grandiosity — inflating his love to cosmic proportions to ward off the devastating guilt and grief beneath
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He genuinely loved Ophelia yet his actions contributed to her destruction; his passionate declaration coexists with the knowledge he rejected and humiliated her
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Hamlet's outburst is primarily performative — a deliberate provocation meant to test the court's reaction or to adopt an antic disposition, rather than genuine emotional overflow

beat_states.desire_state: To neutralize Hamlet's outburst by framing it as madness, protecting Laertes from reconciliation with Hamlet and preserving the plot against Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Claudius needs to maintain power and eliminate Hamlet; keeping Laertes angry at Hamlet is essential to the poisoning plot
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's passionate sincerity threatens to humanize him in Laertes' eyes and undermine the revenge alliance

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is alarmed — Hamlet's raw declaration of love could sway Laertes or the court's sympathy. He intervenes quickly with the madness excuse, but the situation is slipping from his control.

social_state.rationale: Maintains royal authority to define reality ('he is mad') but his warmth toward Laertes is instrumental, not genuine — he is manipulating, not comforting

known_facts: He has conspired with Laertes to kill Hamlet via poisoned sword and drink
Hamlet's madness may be feigned
Ophelia's death resulted from the chain of events Claudius helped set in motion
hidden_secrets: The murder of King Hamlet
The plot to kill Hamlet through the fencing match
His awareness that Hamlet may not truly be mad
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius knows more than anyone about the web of deception; his public framing of Hamlet as mad is strategic, not sincere

beat_states.defense_state: deflection — redirecting attention from Hamlet's emotional truth to the label of madness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He needs Laertes enraged enough to kill Hamlet, yet must appear to be a calming, reasonable monarch
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius might genuinely believe Hamlet is unhinged at this moment, making his dismissal partly sincere rather than purely tactical

beat_states.desire_state: To protect Hamlet from the consequences of his outburst and prevent further violence
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Gertrude's deepest want is to preserve her son and maintain familial harmony, even as the world collapses around her
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's reckless emotional display and Laertes' rage make physical harm imminent; she has limited power to intervene

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is frightened and desperate. She sees her son in danger, hears his genuine love for Ophelia (which may stir her own guilt), and feels powerless to contain the escalating conflict. Her plea 'For love of God' reveals maternal terror.

social_state.rationale: She uses her royal status to intervene but her primary register is maternal warmth and protective concern for Hamlet; she appeals to Laertes' compassion

known_facts: Hamlet loved Ophelia
Ophelia is dead and was being buried
Hamlet and Laertes fought physically in the grave
Hamlet's behavior has been erratic
hidden_secrets: What Hamlet revealed to her in the closet scene about Claudius
false_beliefs: That appealing for forbearance can resolve the deeper conflict between Hamlet and Laertes
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude knows more than she lets on (the closet scene revelations) but publicly maintains ignorance; she may not know about the King's plot with Laertes

beat_states.defense_state: maternal appeal — invoking God and patience to defuse the situation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She recognizes Hamlet's genuine grief for Ophelia yet must publicly support the madness narrative to protect him; she may also feel guilt about her own role in the circumstances that led to Ophelia's death
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude's 'forbear him' could be directed at Hamlet rather than Laertes — asking Hamlet to stop rather than asking Laertes to be patient with him

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: ’Swounds, show me what thou ’t do. / Woo’t weep, woo’t fight, woo’t fast, woo’t tear thyself, / Woo’t drink up eisel, eat a crocodile? / I’ll do ’t. Dost thou come here to whine? / To outface me with leaping in her grave? / Be buried quick with her, and so will I. / And if thou prate of mountains, let them throw / Millions of acres on us, till our ground, / Singeing his pate against the burning zone, / Make Ossa like a wart. Nay, an thou ’lt mouth, / I’ll rant as well as thou.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: This is mere madness; / And thus awhile the fit will work on him. / Anon, as patient as the female dove / When that her golden couplets are disclosed, / His silence will sit drooping.

beat_states.desire_state: To overwhelm Laertes utterly, proving that no one can match the intensity of his grief for Ophelia
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To act authentically in a world of pretense — here, to assert the reality of his love against what he perceives as Laertes' performative grief
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes' public display of grief threatens to delegitimize Hamlet's own love for Ophelia; Hamlet's own guilt and grief are spiraling beyond his control

affect_state.rationale: Extreme emotional flooding — grief, rage, competitive fury all at once. The hyperbolic rhetoric ('millions of acres,' 'eat a crocodile') signals someone losing control. The self-aware 'I'll rant as well as thou' reveals a flicker of recognition that he's unraveling, but he cannot stop.

social_state.rationale: He is asserting dominance over Laertes through sheer rhetorical force, but his status is undermined by the appearance of madness before the court. Warmth is hostile — he is attacking Laertes even as the underlying emotion is love for Ophelia.

known_facts: Ophelia is dead and being buried
Laertes leapt into the grave in a display of grief
He loved Ophelia deeply
He bears responsibility for Polonius's death which contributed to Ophelia's madness
hidden_secrets: His guilt over his role in Ophelia's decline and death
His antic disposition is partly performance but here is collapsing into genuine distress
false_beliefs: That Laertes' grief is mere performance meant to 'outface' him
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet projects his own habit of performance onto Laertes, reading Laertes' genuine grief as theatrical posturing. This allows him to avoid confronting his own culpability.

beat_states.defense_state: Manic escalation — transforming unbearable grief into competitive bravado and rhetorical one-upmanship to avoid the raw pain underneath
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He genuinely loved Ophelia and is devastated, yet he is turning her funeral into a contest of ego; his grief is real but his expression of it is self-consciously theatrical, and he seems half-aware of this ('I'll rant as well as thou')
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be fully conscious that his ranting is performative — a deliberate continuation of the antic disposition to provoke and test the court's reaction, rather than genuine loss of control.

beat_states.desire_state: To protect Hamlet from the consequences of his outburst by reframing his behavior as temporary madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve her son and maintain whatever fragile family bond remains amid the corruption of Elsinore
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's behavior is publicly alarming and potentially dangerous; she cannot physically or emotionally reach him in this state

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is distressed but deliberately composed — she is performing calm maternal authority for the court. The dove image is wistful and tender, revealing her emotional investment, but her certainty is moderate: she hopes rather than knows that the fit will pass.

social_state.rationale: She asserts queenly authority to manage the situation, speaking as an interpreter of Hamlet's behavior to the court. Her warmth toward Hamlet is high — the dove metaphor is deeply maternal and protective.

known_facts: Hamlet has shown erratic behavior for some time
He loved Ophelia
Hamlet killed Polonius
The closet scene revealed Hamlet's deep anger at her
hidden_secrets: Her own guilt and complicity in the hasty remarriage that torments Hamlet
false_beliefs: That Hamlet's current state is purely madness rather than a complex mix of genuine grief, guilt, and rage
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude has partial insight — she recognizes the emotional pattern (escalation followed by quiet) but may not fully grasp the depths of Hamlet's guilt or the rational elements within his frenzy.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — she frames Hamlet's terrifying outburst as a passing 'fit' that will naturally subside, containing her own fear and helplessness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She uses the language of madness ('mere madness') publicly, but the tenderness of the dove simile reveals a mother who deeply understands and empathizes with her son's pain even as she distances herself from it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gertrude may be more strategically aware than she appears — deliberately labeling Hamlet 'mad' to protect him from Claudius's suspicion and Laertes's wrath, rather than genuinely believing the diagnosis.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Hear you, sir, / What is the reason that you use me thus? / I loved you ever. But it is no matter. / Let Hercules himself do what he may, / The cat will mew, and dog will have his day.

beat_states.desire_state: to reclaim dignity and issue a veiled warning to Laertes while de-escalating from his own frenzy
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to act authentically in a world of deception and fulfill his moral obligation
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes' hostility and the public spectacle he has just made of himself

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is coming down from the manic peak of his graveside confrontation. The arousal is dropping but still present. He's regaining composure but the hurt and bewilderment at Laertes' hatred ('What is the reason that you use me thus?') reveals genuine vulnerability. The proverbial ending ('the cat will mew, and dog will have his day') restores a measure of philosophical control — a coded promise that his time will come.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet reasserts status through the veiled threat of the proverb, positioning himself as the 'dog' who will have his day. The warmth flickers briefly in 'I loved you ever' — a genuine if complicated appeal to their former friendship — before hardening into cryptic warning.

known_facts: Laertes attacked him at Ophelia's grave
He genuinely loved Ophelia
He once considered Laertes a friend
The court is watching this confrontation
hidden_secrets: He killed Laertes' father Polonius
He suspects or knows Claudius orchestrates hostility against him
false_beliefs: He may not fully grasp the depth of Laertes' desire for revenge over Polonius's death
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's question 'What is the reason that you use me thus?' may be partly genuine — he understands Laertes grieves Ophelia, but may not fully comprehend that Laertes' rage is also fueled by Polonius's murder and Claudius's manipulation. The 'dog will have his day' suggests he senses a larger game being played.

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — reframing his outburst as righteous and inevitable, using proverbial wisdom to dignify his retreat
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: simultaneously claiming love for Laertes ('I loved you ever') while threatening him ('dog will have his day'), oscillating between reconciliation and menace
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's entire speech could be read as purely strategic — the 'I loved you ever' is tactical flattery designed to appear reasonable to the court audience while 'dog will have his day' is a deliberate provocation masked as resignation, with Hamlet fully aware of exactly why Laertes hates him.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: I pray thee, good Horatio, wait upon him. / To Laertes. Strengthen your patience in our last night’s speech. / We’ll put the matter to the present push.— / Good Gertrude, set some watch over your son.— / This grave shall have a living monument. / An hour of quiet thereby shall we see. / Till then in patience our proceeding be.

beat_states.desire_state: To regain control of the situation, separate the volatile parties, and advance his plot against Hamlet under the cover of calm authority
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne and eliminate Hamlet as a threat to his power and secret
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's public outburst has destabilized the court and drawn sympathy; Laertes's rage must be channeled privately, not publicly

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is inwardly agitated by Hamlet's unpredictable behavior but feels he has regained the upper hand by dispatching orders to multiple parties. His certainty is high because the plan with Laertes is already in motion. Low vulnerability because he occupies the position of sovereign command.

social_state.rationale: Reasserts full royal authority by issuing directives to Horatio, Laertes, and Gertrude in rapid succession. Surface warmth — 'good Horatio,' 'Good Gertrude' — is strategic politeness, not genuine care.

known_facts: He and Laertes have already plotted Hamlet's death via the poisoned/unbated sword
Hamlet's graveyard outburst reveals emotional instability that could be exploited
Laertes's grief and rage make him a willing instrument
Gertrude is unaware of the plot against her son
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
The 'living monument' is code confirming to Laertes that the murder plot proceeds
He has arranged a poisoned cup as backup
false_beliefs: He believes he can fully control Laertes and the outcome of events
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius operates with more information than anyone else present. His language is deliberately coded — 'last night's speech' and 'living monument' carry private meaning for Laertes alone. He believes his control over the situation is nearly total.

beat_states.defense_state: Masking his murderous scheming behind a performance of measured, paternal concern for all parties
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Projects compassion and patience while actively manipulating everyone present toward Hamlet's death — 'living monument' carries a sinister double meaning only he and Laertes understand
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A softer reading: Claudius genuinely wants peace and order, and the 'living monument' is simply a promise to honor Ophelia — but the dramatic context of the existing murder plot makes this unlikely.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: So much for this, sir. Now shall you see the other. / You do remember all the circumstance?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Remember it, my lord!

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Sir, in my heart there was a kind of fighting / That would not let me sleep. Methought I lay / Worse than the mutines in the bilboes. Rashly— / And praised be rashness for it: let us know, / Our indiscretion sometime serves us well / When our deep plots do pall; and that should learn us / There’s a divinity that shapes our ends, / Rough-hew them how we will—

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: That is most certain.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Up from my cabin, / My sea-gown scarfed about me, in the dark / Groped I to find out them; had my desire, / Fingered their packet, and in fine withdrew / To mine own room again, making so bold / ( My fears forgetting manners) to unfold / Their grand commission; where I found, Horatio, / A royal knavery— an exact command, / Larded with many several sorts of reasons / Importing Denmark’s health and England’s too, / With— ho!— such bugs and goblins in my life, / That on the supervise, no leisure bated, / No, not to stay the grinding of the ax, / My head should be struck off.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Is ’t possible?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Here’s the commission. Read it at more leisure. / But wilt thou hear now how I did proceed?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: I beseech you.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Being thus benetted round with villainies, / Or I could make a prologue to my brains, / They had begun the play. I sat me down, / Devised a new commission, wrote it fair— / I once did hold it, as our statists do, / A baseness to write fair, and labored much / How to forget that learning; but, sir, now / It did me yeoman’s service. Wilt thou know / Th’ effect of what I wrote?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Ay, good my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: An earnest conjuration from the King, / As England was his faithful tributary, / As love between them like the palm might flourish, / As peace should still her wheaten garland wear / And stand a comma ’tween their amities, / And many suchlike ases of great charge, / That, on the view and knowing of these contents, / Without debatement further, more or less, / He should those bearers put to sudden death, / Not shriving time allowed.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: How was this sealed?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Why, even in that was heaven ordinant. / I had my father’s signet in my purse, / Which was the model of that Danish seal; / Folded the writ up in the form of th’ other, / Subscribed it, gave ’t th’ impression, placed it safely, / The changeling never known. Now, the next day / Was our sea-fight; and what to this was sequent / Thou knowest already.

beat_states.desire_state: To narrate his triumphant escape and justify his actions to Horatio, securing his friend's validation and moral approval
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty of revenge while reconciling his moral conscience with the corrupt world around him
beat_states.obstacle: The potential moral weight of having sent Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to their deaths, and the lingering question of whether his actions align with divine will or mere self-preservation

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is animated and energized by recounting his clever escape. There's genuine pleasure in the telling — he relishes the narrative of outwitting Claudius. Yet beneath the bravado is an awareness of how close he came to death, and a philosophical need to frame it all as fate rather than luck.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet holds the floor entirely, performing for Horatio as both storyteller and prince. He is warm and confiding — calling him 'sir,' sharing intimate details of his fear and ingenuity — treating Horatio as his one trusted confidant.

known_facts: Claudius ordered his execution via sealed commission to England
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern carried the commission unknowingly or knowingly
He forged a replacement commission ordering their deaths
He sealed it with his father's signet ring
The sea-fight with pirates intervened and returned him to Denmark
Providence or fate seems to have guided events
hidden_secrets: The full weight of his responsibility for Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's deaths, which he has not yet fully addressed
His deeper uncertainty about whether he is an instrument of God or a murderer
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a rare position of epistemic clarity — he has seen the evidence of Claudius's treachery with his own eyes and acted on it. His knowledge is concrete and verified by the physical commission he offers Horatio to read.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — framing his impulsive, desperate actions as evidence of divine providence ('there's a divinity that shapes our ends') to absolve himself of guilt
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to embrace rashness and divine guidance, yet meticulously narrates every calculated detail of his forgery, revealing a mind that is both impulsive and deeply strategic
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's energetic narration could be read as manic deflection — talking at length about the sea voyage to avoid confronting what he must now do about Claudius upon his return to Denmark.

beat_states.desire_state: To fully understand what happened to Hamlet and to serve as faithful witness and moral sounding board
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To protect and support Hamlet through loyal companionship and honest counsel
beat_states.obstacle: The shocking scale of Claudius's treachery and the moral implications of Hamlet's counter-actions leave him struggling to process

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is shaken by the revelation of the execution order — 'Is't possible?' is genuine disbelief. He is emotionally engaged but largely passive, absorbing rather than acting. His affect is mixed: relief that Hamlet survived, horror at what was planned.

social_state.rationale: Horatio occupies the listener/subordinate role willingly, using deferential language ('I beseech you,' 'good my lord'). His warmth is deep and genuine — he is the one person Hamlet can trust completely, and his attentiveness demonstrates that bond.

known_facts: Hamlet was sent to England with Rosencrantz and Guildenstern
A sea-fight occurred and Hamlet returned
Claudius's commission ordered Hamlet's immediate execution
Hamlet forged a replacement ordering the bearers' deaths
The forgery was sealed with King Hamlet's signet ring
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is learning all of this in real time from Hamlet's narration. He knew the broad outlines of the voyage but not the details of the treachery or Hamlet's counter-plot. He is rapidly updating his understanding.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His brief, emphatic responses ('That is most certain,' 'Is't possible?') suggest a man torn between philosophical agreement with Hamlet's providence framework and genuine shock at the murderous realities being described
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's terse responses could indicate suppressed moral discomfort about Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's fate, rather than pure supportive affirmation.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: So Guildenstern and Rosencrantz go to ’t.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Why, man, they did make love to this employment. / They are not near my conscience. Their defeat / Does by their own insinuation grow. / ’Tis dangerous when the baser nature comes / Between the pass and fell incensèd points / Of mighty opposites.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Why, what a king is this!

beat_states.desire_state: To justify the deaths of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern and absolve himself of guilt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty of revenge while maintaining his moral integrity
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio's implicit moral questioning — the weight of having sent two men to their deaths

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet speaks with rhetorical force and philosophical distance, elevating himself as one of the 'mighty opposites' — but the need to justify reveals underlying unease. His certainty is performed rather than fully felt.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet positions himself as a 'mighty opposite' — a prince locked in combat with a king — asserting enormous status. Warmth toward Horatio is present but secondary to the self-aggrandizing rhetoric.

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern carried sealed orders for his execution
He substituted the letter to send them to their deaths
They willingly served Claudius against him
He is locked in mortal conflict with Claudius
hidden_secrets: The degree to which their deaths may actually trouble his conscience
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has full knowledge of the facts but may be deceiving himself about the moral weight of his actions. His claim that they 'made love to this employment' is partially true but also a convenient simplification.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — reframes their deaths as self-inflicted consequences of their own scheming
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims they are 'not near my conscience' while the very act of elaborate justification suggests they are
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may genuinely feel no guilt — his philosophical framework of 'mighty opposites' may represent a genuine fatalistic acceptance rather than defensive rationalization

beat_states.desire_state: To process the moral enormity of what Hamlet has done and what Claudius is
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To bear faithful witness to Hamlet's story and serve as his moral anchor
beat_states.obstacle: His loyalty to Hamlet conflicts with his horror at the escalating body count and Claudius's depravity

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is disturbed. His terse opening line carries the weight of moral reckoning. His exclamation about Claudius reveals genuine alarm and disgust, but also uncertainty about where all this leads.

social_state.rationale: Horatio maintains his subordinate-friend position. His questions are careful — the first is a quiet moral probe, the second redirects to validate Hamlet's framing by condemning Claudius. Warmth and loyalty remain even as he is shaken.

known_facts: Hamlet forged letters sending Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to death
Claudius originally ordered Hamlet's execution
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were complicit in carrying out the king's orders
hidden_secrets: His private moral assessment of Hamlet's actions
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio knows all the facts Hamlet has shared but keeps his own moral judgment largely private. His exclamation about Claudius suggests he accepts Hamlet's framing — or chooses to.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His first line subtly questions Hamlet's culpability ('So Guildenstern and Rosencrantz go to 't') while his second line ('Why, what a king is this!') redirects moral outrage toward Claudius — serving both as genuine horror and as deflection from judging Hamlet
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: 'Why, what a king is this!' could be read as pure moral outrage at Claudius with no implicit judgment of Hamlet, rather than a strategic redirection of moral scrutiny

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Does it not, think thee, stand me now upon— / He that hath killed my king and whored my mother, / Popped in between th’ election and my hopes, / Thrown out his angle for my proper life, / And with such cozenage— is ’t not perfect conscience / To quit him with this arm? And is ’t not to be damned / To let this canker of our nature come / In further evil?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: It must be shortly known to him from England / What is the issue of the business there.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: It will be short. The interim’s mine, / And a man’s life’s no more than to say “ one.” / But I am very sorry, good Horatio, / That to Laertes I forgot myself, / For by the image of my cause I see / The portraiture of his. I’ll court his favors. / But, sure, the bravery of his grief did put me / Into a tow’ring passion.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure moral validation from Horatio that killing Claudius is not only justified but obligatory
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty of revenge against Claudius while maintaining his moral integrity
beat_states.obstacle: Lingering moral uncertainty about whether revenge is righteous or damnable, and awareness that time is running out; also guilt about his treatment of Laertes

affect_state.rationale: High arousal as he catalogs Claudius's crimes with escalating intensity; the rhetorical questions betray underlying uncertainty despite surface conviction. The pivot to Laertes reveals a sudden drop in control and rise in vulnerability — genuine remorse softens the martial resolve.

social_state.rationale: Asserts princely authority by listing his grievances and claiming his right to act, but shows genuine warmth and equality with Horatio ('good Horatio') and empathic warmth toward Laertes through identification with his grief

known_facts: Claudius killed his father
Claudius corrupted Gertrude
Claudius intercepted the Danish succession
Claudius sent orders to have Hamlet killed in England
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern carry the forged letter to their deaths
News from England will soon reach Claudius
Laertes has a legitimate parallel grievance over Polonius's death
hidden_secrets: He forged the commission that sends Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to death
He possesses Claudius's original death warrant as evidence
false_beliefs: He may overestimate the amount of time remaining before Claudius learns the truth
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has full knowledge of Claudius's crimes and his own counter-actions; he recognizes the clock is ticking but believes the interim is sufficient. His insight about Laertes shows genuine self-knowledge.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — building an exhaustive legal-moral case to overwhelm any remaining doubt about the justice of killing Claudius
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He argues killing Claudius is 'perfect conscience' yet simultaneously reveals deep anxiety that it might not be, needing external confirmation; also shifts abruptly from steely resolve about murder to tender remorse about offending Laertes
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The entire moral argument could be read as Hamlet still procrastinating — building justifications rather than acting, with the pivot to Laertes serving as yet another delay mechanism

beat_states.desire_state: To protect Hamlet by alerting him to the urgent practical danger that Claudius will soon learn what happened
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve and preserve Hamlet through loyal counsel and truthful witness
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is absorbed in moral self-justification and emotional reflection rather than attending to the immediate tactical threat

affect_state.rationale: Negative valence from fear for Hamlet's safety; moderate arousal as he urgently presses the time constraint; high certainty about the factual situation but low control — he cannot make Hamlet act

social_state.rationale: Remains in the subordinate friend/counselor position but speaks with the directness that only deep loyalty permits; warmth is high but expressed through pragmatic concern rather than sentiment

known_facts: The forged commission sends Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to death
English ambassadors will report the outcome to Claudius
Very little time remains before Claudius discovers the truth
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio's knowledge mirrors Hamlet's but he focuses on the most actionable inference — the imminent intelligence breach

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection — sidesteps the moral question entirely to redirect to practical urgency
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He neither endorses nor challenges Hamlet's moral case for killing Claudius, suggesting either diplomatic evasion or genuine uncertainty about the ethics of regicide
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's redirection could be read not just as practical warning but as deliberate avoidance of endorsing murder, maintaining moral neutrality as a scholar and Stoic

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Peace, who comes here?

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: Your Lordship is right welcome back to Denmark.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I humbly thank you, sir. / Dost know this waterfly?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: No, my good lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Thy state is the more gracious, for ’tis a vice to know him. He hath much land, and fertile. Let a beast be lord of beasts and his crib shall stand at the king’s mess. ’Tis a chough, but, as I say, spacious in the possession of dirt.

beat_states.desire_state: To establish superiority over Osric and share contemptuous amusement with Horatio
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Asserting his intellectual and moral authority in a corrupt court before confronting Claudius
beat_states.obstacle: The interruption of Osric breaks the momentum of his serious conversation with Horatio about Claudius

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is amused and in command of the social dynamic. His wit is sharp and he enjoys performing for Horatio. The low vulnerability reflects his comfortable superiority over Osric, though underneath the levity lies unresolved tension about what he must do.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet asserts aristocratic and intellectual superiority over Osric, treating him with open contempt. His warmth is reserved exclusively for Horatio through the aside.

known_facts: Osric is a wealthy courtier with much land
Osric is a sycophant of the court
The court is full of such hollow figures serving Claudius
hidden_secrets: He has killed Rosencrantz and Guildenstern
He is resolved to kill Claudius
He has replaced the commission letters
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows exactly what Osric represents — the shallow, land-rich courtier class that thrives under Claudius. He does not yet know why Osric has come.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflecting from the weight of his unfinished revenge through wit and social derision
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He was just speaking of profound moral duty and killing a king, and now pivots instantly to trivial social mockery — suggesting an avoidance of the gravity he just articulated
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's mockery could also be a test to see if Horatio shares his contempt, reinforcing their bond before the dangerous events ahead

beat_states.desire_state: To stay attentive and supportive of Hamlet while assessing the new arrival
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be Hamlet's loyal witness and moral anchor through the unfolding crisis
beat_states.obstacle: He does not know Osric and cannot fully participate in Hamlet's mockery without Hamlet's guidance

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is alert but neutral — he was just processing the moral weight of Hamlet's revelations about Rosencrantz and Guildenstern. The interruption shifts his attention without resolving his concern.

social_state.rationale: Horatio occupies his usual position as trusted companion but socially subordinate. His warmth is directed at Hamlet through loyal attentiveness.

known_facts: Hamlet has returned from the sea voyage
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are going to their deaths
Hamlet is resolved against Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio genuinely does not know Osric and says so honestly. He trusts Hamlet's judgment of the courtier.

beat_states.desire_state: To ingratiate himself with Hamlet and deliver his message from the King
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his status at court by serving as a reliable instrument of the King's will
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's immediate contempt and mockery undercut his attempt at courtly flattery

affect_state.rationale: Osric is eager and performing courtly enthusiasm. His arousal is moderate — he has a task to perform. His control is low because the social dynamic is being shaped entirely by Hamlet.

social_state.rationale: Osric performs high warmth and deference as a courtier greeting a prince. His actual status is low — he is a messenger and a figure of ridicule, wealthy but not respected.

known_facts: Hamlet has returned to Denmark
The King has arranged a wager involving a fencing match between Hamlet and Laertes
He has been sent to deliver this message
hidden_secrets: The match may be part of a plot against Hamlet (Osric may or may not know this)
false_beliefs: His courtly manners are winning Hamlet's favor
epistemic_state.rationale: Osric knows his errand but likely does not grasp the full depth of the conspiracy. He almost certainly does not realize how thoroughly Hamlet despises him.

beat_states.defense_state: Obliviousness — Osric appears not to register or not to understand the contempt being directed at him
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is performing deference to a prince who is openly mocking him, possibly unaware of the mockery or choosing to ignore it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Osric may be more aware of Hamlet's contempt than he lets on, performing obliviousness as a survival strategy in a dangerous court

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: Sweet lord, if your Lordship were at leisure, I should impart a thing to you from his Majesty.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I will receive it, sir, with all diligence of spirit. Put your bonnet to his right use: ’tis for the head.

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: I thank your Lordship; it is very hot.

beat_states.desire_state: to assess and toy with this courtier before hearing his message
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: navigating the corrupt court while preparing to fulfill his duty of revenge
beat_states.obstacle: the interruption of a fawning courtier he finds ridiculous, delaying more serious matters

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a relatively settled, even playful mood after his philosophical acceptance of providence. He senses an opportunity for amusement and feels in command of this social encounter, though he doesn't yet know what Claudius wants.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet asserts his higher rank immediately by correcting Osric's hat behavior — a seemingly polite gesture that is actually a power move, establishing dominance over the interaction from the first moment.

known_facts: Osric is a courtier sent by Claudius with a message
Claudius has been plotting against him
He has just returned from England having escaped a death plot
hidden_secrets: He altered the commission to send Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to their deaths
He suspects Claudius may be planning something further
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is wary of anything coming from Claudius but doesn't yet know this is about the fencing match. His guard is up beneath the playful exterior.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectual superiority — uses wit and social command to maintain distance and control
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: maintains gracious princely courtesy while already beginning to mock Osric's affectations
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be genuinely at ease and simply being courteous rather than already beginning to mock — his instruction about the bonnet could be sincere princely concern rather than a setup for the extended hat game.

beat_states.desire_state: to deliver the King's message about the wager with Laertes while impressing Hamlet with his courtly manners
