beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to advance his standing at court by successfully executing commissions from the King
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's superior status and intellectual sharpness make Osric anxious to perform correctly; his own affectations work against him

affect_state.rationale: Osric is eager to please but unsure of himself in Hamlet's presence. He's already yielding to Hamlet's direction about the hat, showing how easily manipulated he is. His claim about the heat is a flustered attempt to justify his hat-off posture.

social_state.rationale: Osric positions himself as deeply subordinate, using 'Sweet lord' and 'your Lordship' — his warmth is performative court fawning rather than genuine connection. He immediately defers to Hamlet's correction.

known_facts: The King has arranged a wager on a fencing match between Hamlet and Laertes
He has been tasked to deliver this message
Hamlet is a prince of the realm
false_beliefs: His elaborate courtly manner is impressive and appropriate
This is a straightforward sporting wager with no ulterior motive
epistemic_state.rationale: Osric is a willing but unwitting instrument of Claudius — he likely has no knowledge of the poisoned sword or drink plot. He genuinely believes he is delivering a benign invitation.

beat_states.defense_state: excessive formality — hides behind elaborate courtly language and deference to avoid exposure of his shallowness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: wants to appear sophisticated and at ease but is transparently nervous and sycophantic, making him an easy target
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Osric could be more self-aware than he appears — his excessive courtesy might be a deliberate strategy of survival in a dangerous court rather than pure vanity

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: No, believe me, ’tis very cold; the wind is northerly.

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: It is indifferent cold, my lord, indeed.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: But yet methinks it is very sultry and hot for my complexion.

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: Exceedingly, my lord; it is very sultry, as ’twere— I cannot tell how. My lord, his Majesty bade me signify to you that he has laid a great wager on your head. Sir, this is the matter—

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I beseech you, remember.

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: Nay, good my lord, for my ease, in good faith. Sir, here is newly come to court Laertes— believe me, an absolute gentleman, full of most excellent differences, of very soft society and great showing. Indeed, to speak feelingly of him, he is the card or calendar of gentry, for you shall find in him the continent of what part a gentleman would see.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Sir, his definement suffers no perdition in you, though I know to divide him inventorially would dozy th’ arithmetic of memory, and yet but yaw neither, in respect of his quick sail. But, in the verity of extolment, I take him to be a soul of great article, and his infusion of such dearth and rareness as, to make true diction of him, his semblable is his mirror, and who else would trace him, his umbrage, nothing more.

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: Your Lordship speaks most infallibly of him.

beat_states.desire_state: To expose Osric as a sycophantic fool by toying with him, while maintaining control of the interaction before learning the King's purpose
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Hamlet's need to test and unmask falseness in others connects to his broader mission of exposing corruption and deceit at Elsinore
beat_states.obstacle: Osric is too obsequious to resist or even notice the manipulation, making the game almost too easy; Hamlet must also eventually attend to the King's actual message

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is genuinely amused and in full command of the exchange, enjoying the sport of manipulating Osric's agreement on the weather. His wit is sharp and effortless, giving him high control and moderate positive affect. Low vulnerability because he is performing, not revealing.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet occupies princely status and wields it here with deliberate condescension. His warmth is negative — he treats Osric as an object of ridicule, and his parody of ornate language when describing Laertes is designed to humiliate Osric's verbal pretensions.

known_facts: Osric is a courtier sent by the King with a message
The King has laid a wager involving Hamlet
Laertes has returned to court
Osric will agree with anything Hamlet says regardless of truth
Claudius killed King Hamlet and seduced Gertrude
hidden_secrets: Hamlet has resolved to kill Claudius
Hamlet suspects the King's purposes may be treacherous
Hamlet has altered the commission to send Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to their deaths
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is perceptive about Osric's nature and suspects the King's motives but does not yet know the specifics of the wager or the poisoned sword plot. His weather-game demonstrates his epistemic dominance over Osric.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual superiority as armor — by turning Osric into entertainment, Hamlet deflects from the gravity of his situation and the approaching danger
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Hamlet is deeply aware of mortal stakes (just resolved to kill Claudius) yet indulges in elaborate wordplay with a fool, suggesting both contempt for the court world and an avoidance of the weight bearing down on him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's elaborate mockery could also be read as nervous displacement — a man who senses a trap but cannot yet identify it, using wit to buy time and probe for information

beat_states.desire_state: To deliver the King's message about the wager with Laertes and win Hamlet's favorable regard as a courtier
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Osric seeks advancement and approval at court by being the perfect messenger and pleasing those in power
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is deliberately contradicting himself about the weather and using inflated language that Osric cannot match or even fully comprehend, making it impossible for him to maintain coherent agreement

affect_state.rationale: Osric is slightly flustered — he senses something is off in the weather exchange but cannot identify the game. His arousal is elevated from the effort of pleasing a prince. Low certainty because Hamlet keeps shifting positions. He has lost control of the conversation and is being led.

social_state.rationale: Osric is in a deeply subordinate position, performing exaggerated deference. His warmth is high in the sense of eager friendliness and desire to be liked, though it is shallow and performative rather than genuine.

known_facts: The King has laid a wager on Hamlet's head
Laertes has recently come to court
He has been sent by the King to deliver this message
Hamlet is a prince to be flattered
hidden_secrets: Whether Osric knows the wager is a pretext for a plot against Hamlet is ambiguous — likely he does not
false_beliefs: That his elaborate language is impressive and appropriate
That Hamlet is genuinely engaging with him as an equal
epistemic_state.rationale: Osric appears to be a genuine dupe — he knows only the surface message and is unaware of deeper machinations. He fails to perceive that Hamlet is mocking him.

beat_states.defense_state: Reflexive agreement and verbal ornamentation — Osric protects himself from any conflict by instantly adopting whatever position the prince takes
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Osric clearly knows the weather is one thing, yet agrees it is cold, then hot, revealing that his need to please has entirely overridden his capacity for independent perception
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A minority reading suggests Osric is not entirely oblivious but rather performing deliberate servility as a survival strategy at a dangerous court, making his sycophancy more calculated than foolish

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: The concernancy, sir? Why do we wrap the gentleman in our more rawer breath?

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: Sir?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Is ’t not possible to understand in another tongue? You will to ’t, sir, really.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: What imports the nomination of this gentleman?

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: Of Laertes?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: His purse is empty already; all ’s golden words are spent.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Of him, sir.

beat_states.desire_state: To force Osric to get to the point while simultaneously exposing his verbal pretentiousness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Asserting intellectual dominance and control in a world of corruption and false appearances
beat_states.obstacle: Osric's inability to speak plainly and his layers of courtly affectation

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a playful, dominant mode, relishing his intellectual superiority over Osric. He feels in control of this exchange and takes sardonic pleasure in stripping away the courtier's ornamental language.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet wields his princely rank and superior wit to humiliate Osric. There is no warmth toward Osric—only the cold amusement of a cat with a mouse.

known_facts: Osric has come with a message from the king
Osric is praising Laertes extravagantly
Osric is a foppish courtier
hidden_secrets: His awareness that something dangerous may be afoot
His fatalistic readiness for whatever comes
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet perceives Osric's mission involves Laertes but hasn't yet learned the specific nature of the wager. He suspects courtly manipulation but doesn't yet know the details.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual superiority as deflection from the gravity of what message Osric may carry
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Enjoys the game of tormenting Osric while simultaneously growing impatient with the delay
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may already suspect a trap and is deliberately prolonging the exchange to study Osric for signs of deception

beat_states.desire_state: To support Hamlet's game and share in the amusement of deflating Osric's pretensions
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To stand faithfully beside Hamlet as his loyal intellectual companion and moral witness
beat_states.obstacle: Osric's obtuseness means the game could go on indefinitely without reaching the message

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is amused and relaxed, playing the role of witty commentator. His aside about Osric's purse being empty shows dry satisfaction. He's enjoying the spectacle from a position of safety.

social_state.rationale: Horatio positions himself as Hamlet's ally and intellectual equal in mocking Osric. His warmth toward Osric is low—he's dismissive—but his solidarity with Hamlet is implicit.

known_facts: Osric is a courtier sent with a message
Osric's rhetoric is hollow and exhausted
Hamlet is toying with Osric deliberately
hidden_secrets: His deep concern about Hamlet's safety and fate
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio reads the social dynamics clearly and provides astute commentary. He understands Hamlet's game perfectly and sees through Osric's verbal emptiness.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Enjoys the comedy while potentially sensing that whatever message Osric carries could be consequential
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's mockery may also serve as a subtle warning to Hamlet not to trust anything this courtier brings from the king

beat_states.desire_state: to faithfully deliver the Queen's message and ensure Hamlet complies with her wish for courtesy toward Laertes
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve the royal household dutifully and maintain courtly order
beat_states.obstacle: uncertainty about whether Hamlet will receive the request graciously given the tensions surrounding the match

affect_state.rationale: The Lord is a messenger with a simple task; low emotional investment but mild concern about delivering the message properly to the volatile prince

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to both the Queen and Hamlet; the request itself carries a gentle, maternal warmth which the Lord channels

known_facts: The Queen wants Hamlet to be courteous to Laertes before the fencing match
The match is imminent
The King has already communicated through Osric
false_beliefs: This is likely a straightforward sporting event
epistemic_state.rationale: The Lord has no knowledge of the poisoned sword or cup plot; he is an innocent intermediary

beat_states.defense_state: formality — hiding behind the authority of the Queen's request

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: You will lose, my lord.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I do not think so. Since he went into France, I have been in continual practice. I shall win at the odds; but thou wouldst not think how ill all’s here about my heart. But it is no matter.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Nay, good my lord—

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: It is but foolery, but it is such a kind of gaingiving as would perhaps trouble a woman.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: If your mind dislike anything, obey it. I will forestall their repair hither and say you are not fit.

beat_states.desire_state: To dismiss his own premonition of death and commit to the fencing match despite deep unease
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his destiny and enact divine providence, accepting whatever comes
beat_states.obstacle: A powerful somatic intuition that something is deeply wrong — a 'gaingiving' around his heart that he cannot rationally explain or fully suppress

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is experiencing a somatic foreboding ('how ill all's here about my heart') that he cannot rationalize away. His confidence in winning the match is surface-level bravado contradicted by deep bodily unease. This is one of his most emotionally exposed moments with Horatio.

social_state.rationale: With Horatio alone, Hamlet drops princely posture and reveals intimate vulnerability. This is the warmest, most trusting relationship in the play — Hamlet confides what he would tell no one else.

known_facts: Laertes has been practicing fencing in France
The King has wagered on Hamlet's behalf with favorable odds
He has been in continual fencing practice himself
He has a powerful premonition of disaster he cannot explain
hidden_secrets: He suspects Claudius may have arranged something treacherous but cannot articulate it
He has already accepted death philosophically since the graveyard
false_beliefs: That the match is merely a sporting wager with no lethal design
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's body knows what his mind hasn't confirmed: the match is a trap. He lacks specific knowledge of the poisoned sword and cup but his intuition is firing. His false belief about the match's innocence is actively being eroded by his 'gaingiving.'

beat_states.defense_state: Minimization — labeling his genuine death-premonition as 'foolery' and gendering it as feminine weakness to distance himself from it
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He confesses mortal dread to Horatio while simultaneously insisting it doesn't matter; he trusts providence yet his body is screaming danger
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may be deliberately testing whether Horatio will give him permission to withdraw — seeking an external voice to validate his fear so he doesn't have to own it himself

beat_states.desire_state: To protect Hamlet from danger by persuading him to cancel the match
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve and preserve the person he loves and respects above all others
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's determination to proceed and his habit of dismissing counsel; Horatio's subordinate position limits how forcefully he can insist

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is alarmed. His opening line 'You will lose' is unusually blunt for him, suggesting real anxiety. When Hamlet confesses his foreboding, Horatio's alarm escalates — he immediately offers to cancel the event. He feels helpless against Hamlet's fatalism.

social_state.rationale: Horatio occupies a lower social station but their intimacy here is at its peak. He steps beyond his usual deference to actively urge Hamlet to withdraw — an act of deep loyalty that briefly overrides status norms.

known_facts: Laertes is a skilled fencer who trained in France
The odds favor Hamlet but the match is risky
Hamlet is experiencing a powerful physical premonition
Claudius has proven himself capable of murder
hidden_secrets: He may suspect Claudius's involvement in arranging this match but has no proof
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is the most clear-sighted character in the play. He takes Hamlet's premonition seriously where others might not. He doesn't know about the poisoned sword or cup but his rational suspicion of Claudius and respect for intuition make him the most accurate reader of danger.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He respects Hamlet's autonomy and royal authority yet feels compelled to override it out of love and fear for his safety
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's 'You will lose' could be a deliberate provocation to goad Hamlet's competitive pride into taking the match more seriously, though this reading is less supported by the intimacy of the scene

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Not a whit. We defy augury. There is a special providence in the fall of a sparrow. If it be now, ’tis not to come; if it be not to come, it will be now; if it be not now, yet it will come. The readiness is all. Since no man of aught he leaves knows, what is ’t to leave betimes? Let be.

beat_states.desire_state: to silence Horatio's warning and accept whatever fate awaits him in the duel
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty of revenge while reconciling himself to the moral and mortal cost
beat_states.obstacle: Horatio's concern amplifies the premonition of death Hamlet already feels but refuses to heed

affect_state.rationale: A profound calm overlays deep vulnerability. The low arousal and moderate positive valence reflect genuine spiritual surrender rather than panic, but the high vulnerability reveals that beneath the stoic surface he senses his own death approaching. His certainty is high — not about outcomes, but about the rightness of acceptance. Control is moderate: he has relinquished control to providence, which is itself a form of mastery.

social_state.rationale: He speaks with the quiet authority of a prince who has made peace with himself, gently overriding Horatio's worry. The warmth is genuine — 'Let be' is tender, addressed to a dear friend, asking him to stop worrying on his behalf.

known_facts: The King has arranged a fencing match with Laertes
Horatio suspects Hamlet will lose or that something is amiss
The Queen has asked him to be gracious to Laertes
He has a foreboding about what is to come
Claudius killed his father
Laertes has reason to want revenge against him
hidden_secrets: He senses this may be a trap but chooses not to investigate further
He carries unresolved guilt over Polonius's death and Ophelia's fate
false_beliefs: That submitting to providence will yield a just outcome
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's epistemic state is uniquely mature here — he has moved beyond needing to know. He acknowledges ignorance ('no man of aught he leaves knows') and converts it into permission to act without full knowledge. He likely suspects treachery but has decided that suspicion itself is no longer a reason to delay.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — transforms dread of death into philosophical acceptance, converting fear into stoic resignation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He claims total readiness and defiance of augury, yet the very need to articulate this elaborate philosophy of providence suggests he is actively wrestling with a deep foreboding he cannot fully suppress
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: An alternative reading is that Hamlet is not genuinely at peace but is performing stoic acceptance as a final mask — that this is actually a form of suicidal resignation dressed up as philosophy, and the speech is more self-anesthetizing than spiritually transcendent.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Come, Hamlet, come and take this hand from me.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Give me your pardon, sir. I have done you wrong; / But pardon ’t as you are a gentleman. This presence knows, / And you must needs have heard, how I am punished / With a sore distraction. What I have done / That might your nature, honor, and exception / Roughly awake, I here proclaim was madness. / Was ’t Hamlet wronged Laertes? Never Hamlet. / If Hamlet from himself be ta’en away, / And when he’s not himself does wrong Laertes, / Then Hamlet does it not; Hamlet denies it. / Who does it, then? His madness. If ’t be so, / Hamlet is of the faction that is wronged; / His madness is poor Hamlet’s enemy. / Sir, in this audience / Let my disclaiming from a purposed evil / Free me so far in your most generous thoughts / That I have shot my arrow o’er the house / And hurt my brother.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: I am satisfied in nature, / Whose motive in this case should stir me most / To my revenge; but in my terms of honor / I stand aloof and will no reconcilement / Till by some elder masters of known honor / I have a voice and precedent of peace / To keep my name ungored. But till that time / I do receive your offered love like love / And will not wrong it.

beat_states.desire_state: To publicly apologize to Laertes and absolve himself of culpability for Polonius's death by attributing it to madness
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To act with integrity in a corrupt world while fulfilling his duty — here he seeks to clear his conscience before what may be his final contest
beat_states.obstacle: The genuine wrong he has done (killing Polonius, contributing to Ophelia's death) cannot truly be undone by words, and his 'madness defense' is partly a rhetorical performance

affect_state.rationale: He is emotionally open, performing humility before the court, but with measured rhetorical control. His 'readiness is all' philosophy gives him calm, yet genuine regret about Laertes creates real vulnerability.

social_state.rationale: He deliberately lowers his status by asking pardon publicly, calling Laertes 'brother,' showing warmth and generosity — but this is the prince publicly humbling himself, which paradoxically elevates his nobility

known_facts: He killed Polonius
His actions contributed to Ophelia's madness and death
The court is watching this reconciliation
He has accepted whatever fate the duel brings
The King arranged this match
hidden_secrets: His madness was largely performed, not genuine insanity
He suspects the King may be plotting against him
false_beliefs: He does not know the swords are switched or that one is poisoned
He does not know about the poisoned cup
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is sincere in his regret but strategically dishonest about the nature of his 'madness.' He is unaware of the deadly trap being set.

beat_states.defense_state: Dissociation — splitting himself into 'Hamlet' and 'his madness' to externalize responsibility for violence
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is genuinely remorseful toward Laertes yet deploying a sophisticated rhetorical strategy that deflects true accountability; he knows his 'madness' was at least partly performed
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's apology could be read as entirely sincere with no strategic calculation — a man who has genuinely surrendered to providence and wants to make amends before possible death

beat_states.desire_state: To facilitate the appearance of reconciliation so the rigged duel can proceed without suspicion
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his throne and eliminate Hamlet, the threat to his power and the witness to his crime
beat_states.obstacle: He must appear benevolent and kingly while orchestrating murder; any delay or disruption to the duel threatens his plot

affect_state.rationale: He feels controlled satisfaction that his plan is proceeding. High certainty because the trap is set. Moderate arousal from the tension of the moment. Low vulnerability because he believes he holds all the cards.

social_state.rationale: He commands the scene, physically handing Laertes's hand to Hamlet — the sovereign presiding over reconciliation. His warmth is entirely performed.

known_facts: The rapier is poisoned and unbated
The wine is poisoned as backup
Laertes is his co-conspirator
Hamlet is unsuspecting
hidden_secrets: He murdered King Hamlet
He has arranged for Hamlet's death through the rigged duel
The poisoned cup is prepared
false_beliefs: He believes his plan is foolproof and will succeed cleanly
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has maximum information advantage in this scene — he knows both murder mechanisms while Hamlet knows neither

beat_states.defense_state: Performative magnanimity — playing the role of gracious peacemaker to mask lethal intent
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He performs the role of loving stepfather brokering peace while actively engineering Hamlet's death

beat_states.desire_state: To appear honorable in accepting Hamlet's apology while preserving his commitment to the revenge plot
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father Polonius and sister Ophelia — to restore his family's honor through Hamlet's death
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's generous and moving apology stirs genuine feeling that conflicts with his murderous intent; he must navigate sincerity and treachery simultaneously

affect_state.rationale: Deeply conflicted — Hamlet's apology hits home emotionally, creating guilt about the poisoned sword. His certainty wavers; he's losing internal control. High vulnerability because his conscience is being activated at the worst possible moment.

social_state.rationale: He receives the apology from a prince, which elevates him, but he's in a subordinate position accepting grace. His warmth is guarded — genuine feeling leaks through but is contained by his murderous purpose.

known_facts: His rapier is poisoned and unbated
The King has also poisoned the wine
Hamlet killed his father
Hamlet's actions led to Ophelia's death
He is about to commit premeditated murder disguised as sport
hidden_secrets: He intends to kill Hamlet with the poisoned sword
He is co-conspirator with the King
false_beliefs: He may believe he can go through with the murder without moral consequence
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes knows the full scope of the plot but Hamlet's apology is creating cognitive dissonance. His later dying confession ('I am justly killed with mine own treachery') is seeded here in this moment of moral wavering.

beat_states.defense_state: Legalistic distancing — hiding behind 'terms of honor' and 'elder masters' to avoid either true forgiveness or exposure of his plot
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is 'satisfied in nature' (genuinely moved) but committed to killing Hamlet with a poisoned blade; his speech about honor is a mask for dishonorable treachery
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes's response could be read as entirely strategic with no genuine conflict — a cold assassin buying time with polite words — but the text's specificity about 'nature' being 'satisfied' suggests real emotional impact

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I embrace it freely / And will this brothers’ wager frankly play.— / Give us the foils. Come on.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Come, one for me.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I’ll be your foil, Laertes; in mine ignorance / Your skill shall, like a star i’ th’ darkest night, / Stick fiery off indeed.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: You mock me, sir.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: No, by this hand.

beat_states.desire_state: To establish gracious goodwill with Laertes before the match, performing magnanimity publicly
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To align himself with providence and act rightly in a corrupt world
beat_states.obstacle: The genuine wrong he has done Laertes (killing Polonius) and the lingering unease about what this match truly is

affect_state.rationale: A calm, almost serene energy after accepting fate, but with an undercurrent of tension. He is performing ease. The pun on 'foil' reveals a mind still sharp and playful even on the edge of danger.

social_state.rationale: As prince he holds inherent high status but deliberately lowers himself through compliment to Laertes' skill, projecting warmth and generosity before the court

known_facts: He killed Laertes' father Polonius
Laertes has cause to be angry with him
He has apologized publicly in the previous beat
He senses something foreboding but has chosen to accept it
Laertes is reputed a superior fencer
hidden_secrets: He orchestrated Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's deaths
He suspects the King may be plotting against him
false_beliefs: This is a fair sporting match with blunted foils
His apology has been genuinely accepted by Laertes
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is completely unaware of the poisoned sword and poisoned cup. His false belief in the fairness of the match is the dramatic irony that makes this beat devastating.

beat_states.defense_state: Wit and self-deprecation as armor — by lowering himself he controls the social dynamic and deflects from deeper tension
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has surrendered to providence ('the readiness is all') yet still performs social grace as though the outcome matters; his humility about fencing skill may be genuine or strategic misdirection
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's excessive praise of Laertes' skill could be read as subtle mockery or competitive gamesmanship rather than genuine humility — a way of putting Laertes off-balance by seeming not to take the match seriously

beat_states.desire_state: To get through the social pleasantries and begin the match so he can execute the murder plot
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and sister by killing Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's genuine warmth and graciousness are pricking his conscience; he must maintain his murderous resolve while performing civility

affect_state.rationale: High internal tension masked by brevity. His conscience is actively troubled — 'You mock me, sir' reveals paranoia that Hamlet may see through him, and also projects his own self-contempt outward. He is losing control of his emotional state.

social_state.rationale: Socially subordinate to the prince; his brief, clipped responses suggest withdrawal rather than engagement. The warmth he might naturally feel toward a gracious opponent is being suppressed.

known_facts: His rapier is unbated and poisoned
The King has poisoned the wine as backup
Hamlet killed his father
Hamlet's madness may have driven Ophelia to death
Hamlet has just publicly apologized
hidden_secrets: He intends to kill Hamlet with the poisoned sword
He is conspiring with the King
false_beliefs: He can go through with this without moral consequence
His honor demands this treacherous method
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes knows everything about the plot but is beginning to feel the weight of that knowledge against Hamlet's open-hearted behavior. His conscience is the ticking clock.

beat_states.defense_state: Terse responses to minimize emotional engagement and avoid being won over by Hamlet's charm
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is about to commit dishonorable murder by poisoned blade while standing in the posture of an honorable sportsman; Hamlet's kindness is making the contradiction unbearable
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes' 'You mock me, sir' could be read as genuine social discomfort from a young man who simply doesn't know how to receive princely compliments, rather than guilt-driven paranoia

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Give them the foils, young Osric. Cousin Hamlet, / You know the wager?

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Very well, my lord. / Your Grace has laid the odds o’ th’ weaker side.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: I do not fear it; I have seen you both. / But, since he is better, we have therefore odds.

beat_states.desire_state: To engage the match with honest sportsmanship while subtly asserting his awareness that things may not be as they seem
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his duty of justice against Claudius while accepting whatever fate Providence holds
beat_states.obstacle: He has surrendered himself to providence and cannot act preemptively; he must play the game without knowing the full danger

affect_state.rationale: Having accepted providence and defied augury, Hamlet is in a state of calm fatalism. Low certainty because he senses something is off but cannot name it. High vulnerability because he has consciously surrendered control to fate.

social_state.rationale: Addresses Claudius with formal courtesy ('my lord,' 'Your Grace') but the remark about odds on the weaker side carries a dry, slightly pointed edge — not hostile but not warm either.

known_facts: Claudius has wagered on him against Laertes
Laertes is considered the better fencer
He has a foreboding feeling about the match
He has chosen to submit to providence regardless
hidden_secrets: He suspects Claudius of general treachery but does not know the specific plot
false_beliefs: He believes this is a legitimate fencing match with fair weapons
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet knows Claudius is his enemy but has no specific intelligence about the poisoned foil or wine. His comment about odds is genuine sporting observation, not evidence of suspicion about rigging.

beat_states.defense_state: Ironic self-deprecation ('laid the odds o' th' weaker side') as both genuine modesty and a probe of Claudius's intentions
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Has accepted readiness for death yet still engages competitively, as though life still matters
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's remark about 'the weaker side' could be a more deliberate provocation — testing whether Claudius reveals anything about why he's so confident, suggesting Hamlet suspects more than he lets on.

beat_states.desire_state: To move the match forward quickly while maintaining the facade of a genial, fair-minded host
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To eliminate Hamlet and secure his throne and life
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's sharp wit and veiled insinuations could expose the plot; must appear natural and unsuspicious

affect_state.rationale: Outwardly calm and jovial but internally highly aroused with anticipation and anxiety. He has prepared the poisoned foil and cup, giving him a sense of control, but the unpredictability of the match creates real vulnerability.

social_state.rationale: Commands the proceedings as king, directing Osric and framing the wager. Projects avuncular warmth toward Hamlet ('Cousin Hamlet') but it is entirely performed.

known_facts: The foils include one with an unbated, poisoned tip
A poisoned cup of wine is prepared as a backup
Laertes is his co-conspirator
Hamlet has accepted the match freely
hidden_secrets: The entire match is a murder plot
He has poisoned the wine
false_beliefs: He believes the plan is foolproof and that Hamlet suspects nothing
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius holds all the hidden information about the trap. His comment about the odds ('since he is better, we have therefore odds') cleverly masks the real reason he feels confident — Hamlet cannot win because the game is rigged.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing casual confidence and sporting fairness to mask the lethal trap he has set
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Must project warmth and familial ease while orchestrating a nephew's murder
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius's line 'I do not fear it' could betray a flicker of genuine nervousness he is actively suppressing, suggesting less control than he projects.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: This is too heavy. Let me see another.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: This likes me well. These foils have all a length?

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: Ay, my good lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To confirm fair conditions for the match and proceed with confident sportsmanship
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live authentically and meet fate with readiness — 'the readiness is all'
beat_states.obstacle: He is unaware of the conspiracy and therefore cannot protect himself from it

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a state of calm readiness, relatively positive and composed. His vulnerability is very high objectively — he is walking into a trap — but subjectively he feels at peace.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet is princely, gracious, and at ease — his earlier apology and current sportsmanship elevate his social standing. He treats the moment as genuinely collegial.

known_facts: The wager terms involve hits in a fencing bout
He has chosen a foil that suits him
Foils should be of equal length for fairness
hidden_secrets: His earlier commission of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's deaths
false_beliefs: This is a fair and friendly sporting contest
Laertes is switching foils for comfort, not treachery
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is fatally ignorant of the plot. His question about foil length shows basic due diligence but no suspicion of poisoned tips.

beat_states.defense_state: Projecting ease and control — his philosophical acceptance of fate functions as a shield against anxiety
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has accepted that Providence governs all outcomes, yet still instinctively checks for fairness in the match — residual vigilance beneath surrender
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Hamlet has a premonitory unease (the 'ill about my heart' earlier) that subtly colors even this moment of apparent ease

beat_states.desire_state: To switch to the foil with the unbated, poisoned tip without arousing suspicion
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and sister by killing Hamlet through any means necessary
beat_states.obstacle: He must appear to be making a casual, innocent choice while selecting the lethal weapon

affect_state.rationale: High internal tension masked by outward calm. He is nervous about selecting the right foil without detection. His conscience is troubled after Hamlet's apology but rage and commitment to the plot keep him on course.

social_state.rationale: Maintaining the social appearance of a courteous opponent while internally cold and calculating. His status is subordinate to the King's orchestration.

known_facts: One foil is unbated and poisoned
The King has also poisoned the wine as backup
He must select the correct lethal foil
hidden_secrets: The entire duel is a murder plot
His foil is tipped with poison
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is fully aware of the conspiracy. His request to switch foils is the critical moment of selecting the murder weapon.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing nonchalance — disguising murderous intent as a fencer's ordinary preference for weight and balance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is about to commit premeditated murder under the guise of honorable sport, even after Hamlet's sincere apology moved him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A small possibility that 'too heavy' is genuinely about comfort and he's briefly delaying, testing his own resolve

beat_states.desire_state: To facilitate the match smoothly and serve the court with visible competence
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position and favor at court through obsequious service
beat_states.obstacle: He must appear neutral and efficient while the foil exchange happens — he may or may not know which foil is poisoned

affect_state.rationale: Osric is performing his role with nervous eagerness. His quick affirmative answer suggests a desire to keep things moving smoothly. Moderate arousal from the public ceremonial pressure.

social_state.rationale: Osric is clearly subordinate — a functionary serving princes. His warmth is performative deference, eager to please.

known_facts: The foils are of equal length
He is responsible for distributing them
The King arranged this match
false_beliefs: This is likely a straightforward sporting event
epistemic_state.rationale: Osric's level of knowledge about the conspiracy is textually ambiguous. Most readings treat him as an innocent dupe, a courtier doing his job without understanding the lethal stakes.

beat_states.defense_state: Officious compliance — hiding behind procedural correctness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: If he knows about the plot, he is complicit in murder while performing courtly propriety; if he doesn't, he is an unwitting instrument
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Osric may be a knowing accomplice who deliberately ensures Laertes gets the poisoned foil — some productions play him as complicit

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Set me the stoups of wine upon that table.— / If Hamlet give the first or second hit / Or quit in answer of the third exchange, / Let all the battlements their ordnance fire. / The King shall drink to Hamlet’s better breath, / And in the cup an union shall he throw, / Richer than that which four successive kings / In Denmark’s crown have worn. Give me the cups, / And let the kettle to the trumpet speak, / The trumpet to the cannoneer without, / The cannons to the heavens, the heaven to earth, / “ Now the King drinks to Hamlet.” Come, begin. / And you, the judges, bear a wary eye.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Come on, sir.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Come, my lord.

beat_states.desire_state: To engage in the fencing match with genuine sportsmanship and prove his skill
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To find authentic action in a world of seeming — this moment of honest combat connects to his desire for truth, though he is unknowingly walking into deception
beat_states.obstacle: His fatalistic acceptance ('the readiness is all') has lowered his guard against specific danger

affect_state.rationale: His 'Come on, sir' is clipped, focused, sportsman-like. He has surrendered to providence, which gives him calm but also makes him deeply exposed. Moderate arousal — ready but not anxious.

social_state.rationale: Princely but not performing — his brevity contrasts with the King's grandiosity. He is respectful toward Laertes but economical. His status is inherent rather than displayed.

known_facts: The King has wagered on his ability
He has apologized to Laertes
He sensed foreboding but dismissed it
He believes this is a legitimate sporting contest
hidden_secrets: He suspects Claudius killed his father (and knows it)
He has killed Polonius
false_beliefs: He believes the match is an honest contest
He believes the wine is a legitimate celebratory gesture
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is catastrophically uninformed about the specific dangers. His broader knowledge of Claudius's villainy has not translated into suspicion about this particular trap.

beat_states.defense_state: Spiritual resignation functioning as both courage and dangerous complacency
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has accepted death philosophically but is paradoxically more alive and present in this moment than he has been — eager, brief, ready
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may have a subliminal awareness that this is a trap but has chosen to walk into it as part of his submission to providence

beat_states.desire_state: To execute his poisoning plan under the cover of elaborate ceremonial pageantry
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To eliminate Hamlet and secure his throne, his crown, and his queen
beat_states.obstacle: He must maintain the appearance of a benevolent, celebratory host while hiding murder in plain sight

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in his element — orchestrating, commanding, performing kingship. High arousal from the danger of the moment, but he feels in control of the plan with both the poisoned blade and the poisoned cup as backup. Some vulnerability because everything is at stake.

social_state.rationale: At peak performative authority — commanding servants, judges, cannoneers, the entire apparatus of court. The warmth is manufactured, a show of avuncular generosity toward Hamlet designed to make the scene appear innocent.

known_facts: The cup contains poison ('union')
Laertes' blade is unbated and envenomed
This is a dual-mechanism assassination attempt
The court believes this is a friendly wager
hidden_secrets: The pearl/union is actually poison
He has conspired with Laertes to murder Hamlet
He murdered Hamlet's father
false_beliefs: He believes his plan is airtight and under his complete control
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius holds maximum information asymmetry — he knows both killing mechanisms while the court and Hamlet know neither. His reference to the 'union' is a public alibi for dropping poison into the cup.

beat_states.defense_state: Concealment through spectacle — the more ornate the ceremony, the less visible the poison
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Performing lavish paternal pride in Hamlet while actively engineering his death

beat_states.desire_state: To begin the match and find his moment to strike Hamlet with the poisoned blade
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father Polonius and his sister Ophelia through Hamlet's death
beat_states.obstacle: His conscience — Hamlet's recent apology and generosity trouble him, and he must perform normalcy while planning murder

affect_state.rationale: His brief 'Come, my lord' mirrors Hamlet's economy but for opposite reasons — Hamlet is at ease, Laertes is containing turmoil. High arousal from the danger and moral conflict. Low certainty because his resolve is wavering. Low control because the plan is Claudius's.

social_state.rationale: Formally respectful — 'my lord' acknowledges Hamlet's rank. But the warmth is suppressed and conflicted. He is subordinate both to the King's scheme and to his own grief-driven oath.

known_facts: His blade is unbated and poisoned
The cup contains poison as a backup
Hamlet genuinely apologized to him
This match is designed to kill Hamlet
hidden_secrets: He has conspired with Claudius to murder Hamlet through treachery
His sword is envenomed
false_beliefs: He may still believe that pure vengeance will bring him peace
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes knows almost everything Claudius knows about the plot, but unlike Claudius, this knowledge torments rather than empowers him. He is an informed but reluctant conspirator.

beat_states.defense_state: Compartmentalization — performing the role of honorable opponent while suppressing moral doubt
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has just heard Hamlet's sincere apology and is moved by it, yet he is about to use a weapon that violates every code of honor he was raised with — 'it is almost against my conscience'
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may already be internally resolved to abandon the plot, and his brief reply reflects emotional withdrawal rather than readiness

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: One.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Judgment!

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: A hit, a very palpable hit.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Well, again.

beat_states.desire_state: To win the first hit and establish dominance in the bout
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To act with decisive purpose and prove his worth, even as fate closes in
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes denies the hit, challenging Hamlet's claim

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is energized, confident, and enjoying the moment of athletic triumph. His single-word declarations ('One.' and 'Judgment!') show crisp certainty and command. He feels in control of the bout.

social_state.rationale: Scoring the first hit elevates his status before the court. His appeal to judgment is authoritative but not warm — it's competitive assertion.

known_facts: He has scored a legitimate hit on Laertes
The wager requires him to overcome Laertes' advantage
The court is watching
false_beliefs: This is a fair sporting contest
Laertes fights with an unbated foil like his own
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is entirely unaware of the poisoned sword and poisoned wine. He takes the match at face value, which is precisely the trap.

beat_states.defense_state: Channeling existential anxiety into competitive focus
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He fights with genuine sporting energy and confidence, yet has already surrendered to providence ('the readiness is all'), creating a strange lightness in a deadly situation

beat_states.desire_state: To deny or minimize Hamlet's hit and reset to continue the bout toward his lethal goal
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and sister by killing Hamlet with the poisoned blade
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has scored first, putting Laertes on the defensive both athletically and in terms of his murder plot — he needs to wound Hamlet, not be outfenced

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is rattled. Losing the first exchange means Hamlet is sharper than expected. His denial of the hit ('No') reveals frustration, and 'Well, again' is a tense, clipped recovery masking anxiety about whether his plan will succeed.

social_state.rationale: Losing the first point lowers his public status. His warmth toward Hamlet is negative — he is performing civility while intending murder.

known_facts: His sword is poisoned and unbated
The King has poisoned the wine as backup
He has just been outfenced in the first exchange
He must wound Hamlet to kill him
hidden_secrets: The poisoned tip of his rapier
His conspiracy with Claudius
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes possesses full knowledge of the conspiracy but is discovering in real time that Hamlet's fencing skill may be a serious obstacle to executing the plan.

beat_states.defense_state: Compartmentalizing guilt by staying in competitive mode; his terse 'Well, again' masks desperation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must simultaneously fence well enough to wound Hamlet with the poisoned tip while appearing to compete honestly — and Hamlet's superior skill threatens the entire plan
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes' 'No' could be genuine competitive disagreement rather than strategic deflection, and 'Well, again' could reflect genuine sportsmanship rather than masked desperation

beat_states.desire_state: To fulfill his role as judge fairly and impressively before the court
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain favor and status at court through diligent service
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes disputes the hit, putting Osric in the position of having to assert authority

affect_state.rationale: Osric is excited to perform his role and speaks with emphatic certainty ('a very palpable hit') — the repetition and emphasis suggest he relishes the moment of authority, though he remains a subordinate figure.

social_state.rationale: Momentarily elevated by his judging role, but fundamentally a courtier serving his betters. His enthusiasm reads as eager-to-please warmth.

known_facts: Hamlet landed a clear hit
He is the appointed judge of the bout
The King has wagered on Hamlet
false_beliefs: This is a legitimate sporting contest
epistemic_state.rationale: Osric is entirely ignorant of the conspiracy. He judges the bout in good faith, unaware of the poisoned weapons and wine.

beat_states.defense_state: Hiding behind the formality of his official role to avoid personal exposure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants to please everyone but must render a judgment that necessarily displeases one party
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Osric's emphatic phrasing could be sycophantic over-confirmation aimed at pleasing Hamlet and/or the King rather than pure judicial certainty

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Stay, give me drink.— Hamlet, this pearl is thine. / Here’s to thy health. / Give him the cup.
utterances.stage_direction: He drinks and then drops the pearl in the cup.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I’ll play this bout first. Set it by awhile. / Come. They play. Another hit. What say you?

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: A touch, a touch. I do confess ’t.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Our son shall win.

beat_states.desire_state: To continue winning the fencing match and prove his skill
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To act with authentic purpose and honor, even as fate closes in
beat_states.obstacle: The King's insistence on interrupting the match with ceremony and drink

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is in a rare state of flow — physically engaged, winning, confident. He brushes aside the cup with ease, not from suspicion but from competitive momentum. This is Hamlet at his most present and least overthinking.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet holds courtly status as prince and is winning the match, elevating him. His warmth is low — he's focused on competition, not social connection. His dismissal of the King's toast is casually disrespectful.

known_facts: He has scored two hits
The King has wagered on the match
He is fencing Laertes
hidden_secrets: His own suspicions about Claudius's nature
His killing of Polonius
false_beliefs: This is a fair sporting match
The cup is ordinary celebratory wine
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is completely unaware of the poison in the cup and on Laertes' blade. His ignorance is what makes this beat so dramatically ironic and devastating.

beat_states.defense_state: Focused absorption in the contest deflects any awareness of danger
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Has accepted readiness for death ('the readiness is all') yet is fully engaged in competitive vitality and the pleasure of winning
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's refusal of the cup could carry a subtle, instinctive distrust of anything Claudius offers, even if not conscious suspicion of poison

beat_states.desire_state: To get Hamlet to drink the poisoned cup before the bout continues
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To eliminate Hamlet and secure his throne and life against exposure
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet refuses the cup, choosing to continue playing instead of drinking

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is increasingly panicked. His primary plan (the poisoned cup) has just been deflected by Hamlet's casual refusal. He must mask rising anxiety with congratulatory warmth. His 'Our son shall win' is forced cheerfulness concealing growing desperation as his assassination plot unravels.

social_state.rationale: Performs the role of generous, proud stepfather-king before the court, toasting Hamlet's health and offering the pearl. The warmth is entirely performed — a lethal facade of paternal affection.

known_facts: The cup contains poison
The pearl is the pretext for poisoning the cup
Laertes' sword is also poisoned
Hamlet has won two hits
hidden_secrets: He has poisoned the wine
He conspired with Laertes to murder Hamlet
He murdered King Hamlet
false_beliefs: He still believes he can control the outcome of this scheme
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius knows both layers of the plot but cannot control whether Hamlet drinks. His confidence in the scheme is eroding as Hamlet ignores the cup.

beat_states.defense_state: Masking murderous intent behind celebratory generosity and parental pride
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Must appear to celebrate Hamlet's success while desperately needing Hamlet to drink the poison that will kill him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: 'Our son shall win' could be read as Claudius genuinely momentarily swept up in the spectacle, a flicker of authentic pride before remembering his purpose

beat_states.desire_state: To find an opening to wound Hamlet with the poisoned blade
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father Polonius and sister Ophelia's deaths
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet is outfencing him, and his conscience is stirring as he witnesses Hamlet's fair play and skill

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is losing the match and losing his moral footing. Confessing 'A touch, a touch' honestly while holding a poisoned sword creates profound internal dissonance. He is shaken by Hamlet's competence and his own complicity.

social_state.rationale: Laertes is in the diminished position of the losing fencer. His acknowledgment of the hit shows minimal warmth — gracious but strained. The court watches him fall behind.

known_facts: His sword is poisoned and unbated
The cup contains poison
Hamlet has now scored two hits
Claudius is his co-conspirator
hidden_secrets: The sword is envenomed
He conspired with Claudius to kill Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes knows the full scope of the plot. His 'almost against my conscience' (coming soon) is already germinating here. Each honest acknowledgment of Hamlet's hits deepens his moral crisis.

beat_states.defense_state: Compartmentalizing guilt by performing sportsmanship while concealing treachery
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Honorably acknowledging Hamlet's hits while planning to murder him with a poisoned, unbated sword — his sense of honor wars with his commitment to vengeance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes' confession of the touch could be read as purely mechanical sportsmanship with no moral weight yet — the conscience crisis fully emerging only later

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: He’s fat and scant of breath.— / Here, Hamlet, take my napkin; rub thy brows. / The Queen carouses to thy fortune, Hamlet.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Good madam.

beat_states.desire_state: to accept his mother's affection graciously while maintaining focus on the match
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his duty of justice against Claudius while navigating the complex web of relationships that entangle him
beat_states.obstacle: His deep ambivalence toward his mother — he craves her love but cannot fully trust or forgive her

affect_state.rationale: A brief moment of genuine warmth from his mother creates a flicker of positive feeling, but Hamlet's deeper turmoil and suspicion keep his emotional engagement guarded. His terse 'Good madam' reveals controlled affect — not cold, but contained.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet allows himself to receive his mother's care without pushing her away, but his warmth is muted. In the public context of the match, he is respectful but emotionally distant.

The King offered him a poisoned-looking drink which he declined

Claudius murdered his father

His readiness to let providence guide whatever happens
His suspicion about the King's intentions with the drink
false_beliefs: He may not yet realize the full extent of the trap laid for him in this match

beat_states.defense_state: emotional restraint — keeping his response minimal and polite to avoid deeper engagement
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He receives maternal warmth he has desperately wanted, yet he cannot fully embrace it given his knowledge of her complicity in his father's displacement and his unresolved mission of revenge
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's brevity could indicate distraction or physical exhaustion rather than emotional guardedness — he may simply be catching his breath.

beat_states.desire_state: to nurture and celebrate her son's success in the fencing match
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain closeness with Hamlet and see him restored to happiness and health
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's physical exertion and her inability to truly reach him emotionally after their fractured relationship

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is experiencing genuine maternal pride and warmth — Hamlet is winning, and she can publicly demonstrate affection. Her arousal is moderate from the excitement of the match. She feels relatively in control of this social moment.

social_state.rationale: As Queen, she holds high status and uses this public moment to display maternal warmth openly, toasting her son before the court — an act of both love and royal endorsement.

known_facts: Hamlet has scored the first hit
Hamlet appears physically winded and sweating
The King has offered Hamlet a drink with a pearl
hidden_secrets: Her conflicted feelings about her hasty remarriage
Her awareness of Hamlet's deep hostility toward Claudius
false_beliefs: The cup she is about to drink from is safe
This fencing match is a genuine reconciliation between Hamlet and Laertes
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude is entirely ignorant of the poisoning plot. She sees only a sporting event and a chance for familial healing. Her false belief about the cup's safety makes this moment tragically ironic.

beat_states.defense_state: displacement — channeling her deep anxieties about Hamlet into maternal fussing over his physical state
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is celebrating a moment of normalcy and pride in her son while unknowingly about to drink from a poisoned cup — her joy is built on a foundation of lethal deception she cannot perceive
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Gertrude suspects something is wrong with the cup and drinks deliberately to protect Hamlet — a sacrificial reading — but the text gives no clear signal of this yet.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Gertrude, do not drink.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: I will, my lord; I pray you pardon me.

utterances.speaker: KING

beat_states.desire_state: To stop Gertrude from drinking the poisoned cup intended for Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure his throne and eliminate threats — but now his own trap is destroying what he sought to protect
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude's willful independence and his inability to explain WHY she must not drink without exposing his murder plot

affect_state.rationale: Claudius knows exactly what is in the cup and watches helplessly as Gertrude drinks. His aside reveals total horror and the recognition that his scheme has catastrophically backfired. He is in a state of acute panic masked by forced calm.

social_state.rationale: His command to Gertrude is technically a royal order, but she overrides him publicly — his status collapses in this moment. The warmth is genuine but impotent; he does care about Gertrude but cannot act on it.

known_facts: The cup contains poison he placed there for Hamlet
Gertrude is about to drink — and then has drunk — the poison
He cannot reveal the truth without exposing the entire plot
Gertrude will die from this
hidden_secrets: He poisoned the cup
He conspired with Laertes to kill Hamlet
Laertes' sword is envenomed
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has perfect knowledge of the situation and that is precisely what makes it unbearable — he knows everything and can do nothing.

beat_states.defense_state: suppression — he cannot reveal the poison so he minimizes his warning to a casual command
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must save his wife but cannot do so without exposing himself as a murderer; his love for Gertrude collides with his self-preservation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Claudius's warning is more perfunctory than desperate — that he has already calculated Gertrude as expendable collateral and his aside is more about the unraveling plan than grief.

beat_states.desire_state: To toast her son's success and share in his moment of triumph
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain familial bonds and love her son, navigating between husband and child
beat_states.obstacle: Claudius's unexplained command not to drink, which she dismisses as overprotectiveness or irrelevance

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is in a state of maternal pride and mild festive joy. She feels confident and autonomous in this moment. She has no awareness of danger, so her vulnerability is low subjectively even though it is catastrophically high objectively.

social_state.rationale: She asserts queenly prerogative by politely refusing her husband's command — 'I pray you pardon me' is courteous defiance. Her warmth is directed at Hamlet; this toast is an act of maternal love.

known_facts: Hamlet has scored the first hit in the fencing match
She wants to honor him with a drink
Claudius told her not to drink but gave no reason
false_beliefs: The cup is an ordinary celebratory drink
Claudius's objection is trivial or about decorum
The fencing match is a genuine sporting contest
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude is entirely ignorant of the conspiracy. Her false beliefs are total — she inhabits a completely different reality than Claudius in this moment.

beat_states.defense_state: denial — she brushes aside Claudius's warning as trivial, asserting her autonomy with polite firmness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is performing a loving maternal act that will kill her; her defiance of Claudius is both an assertion of independence and an unwitting self-destruction
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some productions suggest Gertrude suspects the cup is poisoned and drinks deliberately — either to save Hamlet or as a form of suicide upon realizing Claudius's treachery. Her 'I pray you pardon me' could be a knowing farewell.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: I dare not drink yet, madam— by and by.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: Come, let me wipe thy face.

beat_states.desire_state: To avoid drinking from the poisoned cup while maintaining composure during the match
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill the Ghost's command and expose/punish Claudius's corruption
beat_states.obstacle: His mother is innocently offering him the poisoned drink; he cannot explain why he refuses without revealing his suspicions

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is alert and uneasy — he may not know the cup is poisoned, but Claudius's earlier gesture with the pearl was suspicious. He declines politely but with an undercurrent of vigilance. His focus is on the match, not refreshment, but something holds him back.

social_state.rationale: He addresses his mother with filial courtesy ('madam') but keeps emotional distance; he is performing the role of dutiful son in a public setting while remaining guarded.

known_facts: Claudius dropped a pearl into the cup
Claudius urged him to drink
He is in a fencing match with Laertes
He has already scored hits
hidden_secrets: He suspects Claudius of his father's murder
He has been anticipating some treachery
false_beliefs: He may not fully realize the cup is poisoned
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet's refusal to drink may be instinctive wariness rather than certain knowledge of poison. He knows Claudius is capable of treachery but doesn't have specific proof about the cup.

beat_states.defense_state: polite deferral — uses courtesy to mask wariness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He senses danger in the cup but cannot articulate it; he must appear casual while his instincts scream warning
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may simply be too focused on the bout to drink, with his refusal being purely practical rather than suspicious — 'by and by' suggesting he'll drink later.

beat_states.desire_state: To nurture and care for her son in a moment of maternal pride and concern
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain her family bonds and see Hamlet restored to health and happiness
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet keeps her at emotional distance; she has just drunk from a cup Claudius told her not to drink from, though she doesn't understand why

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is in a state of proud, anxious maternal warmth. She is energized by the match, happy Hamlet is winning, but concerned about his physical state. She has already defied Claudius by drinking, an act of unconscious rebellion that will prove fatal.

social_state.rationale: She is fully in her maternal role, wiping her adult son's face — an intimate, tender gesture that temporarily dissolves court hierarchy. Her warmth is at its peak in the play.

known_facts: Hamlet is winning the bout
He is sweating and out of breath
Claudius told her not to drink but she drank anyway
false_beliefs: The cup she drank from was a harmless celebratory toast
Claudius's warning was mere concern about propriety
This is a friendly sporting match
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude is tragically ignorant. She has already consumed the poison. She believes the match is genuine sport and that her drink was a loving toast to her son's success.

beat_states.defense_state: displacement — channels anxiety about Hamlet's condition into physical caretaking
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is joyful watching her son succeed yet physically tending to signs of his exhaustion; her maternal instinct overrides all court protocol and her husband's strange warning

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: My lord, I’ll hit him now.

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: I do not think ’t.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: And yet it is almost against my conscience.

beat_states.desire_state: to keep Laertes committed to the poisoned sword plot and ensure Hamlet dies in this bout
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his throne, his life, and his power by eliminating the threat Hamlet poses
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes is wavering — showing signs of conscience — and the Queen has drunk the poisoned wine, creating cascading complications

affect_state.rationale: The situation is spiraling. The Queen has drunk the poison. Laertes is losing nerve. Claudius's careful plan is unraveling and he's experiencing high anxiety masked as composure. His control is slipping on multiple fronts simultaneously.

social_state.rationale: Claudius maintains his royal authority but is coldly manipulative — his 'I do not think't' is a calculated goad disguised as casual skepticism, using status to pressure Laertes into action through shame.

known_facts: The sword is poisoned
The cup is poisoned
The Queen has drunk from the poisoned cup
Laertes has not yet landed a hit with the poisoned blade
Hamlet is winning the match
hidden_secrets: He orchestrated the entire assassination plot
He knows the Queen is dying or will die from the cup
false_beliefs: He may believe he can still control the outcome of this situation
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has full knowledge of all the treacherous mechanisms in play and is watching them go wrong one by one. His false belief in his continued control is his tragic flaw in this moment.

beat_states.defense_state: denial — suppressing panic about the Queen's poisoning to maintain focus on the assassination
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he needs Laertes to strike fatally but his dismissive 'I do not think't' simultaneously undermines Laertes' confidence and goads him through wounded pride — a manipulation that risks pushing Laertes further into doubt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius's 'I do not think't' could be genuine doubt about Laertes' ability rather than a deliberate provocation — simple pessimism rather than tactical goading.

beat_states.desire_state: to follow through on the poisoned sword plot and kill Hamlet, while wrestling with mounting guilt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father and sister's deaths and restore family honor
beat_states.obstacle: his conscience is awakening — Hamlet's nobility, the Queen's unwitting poisoning, and the dishonorable nature of the treachery are eroding his resolve

affect_state.rationale: Deep internal conflict — guilt and shame are surfacing. He is losing control of his own moral bearings. The aside about conscience reveals he is close to breaking from the conspiracy. High arousal from the pressure of the moment and the weight of what he's about to do.

social_state.rationale: Laertes is subordinate to the King in this conspiracy, reporting his intentions like a soldier to a commander. His warmth toward Hamlet is paradoxically rising (the conscience line), while his alliance with the King feels increasingly degrading.

known_facts: His sword is poisoned and unbated
The King has also poisoned the cup
The Queen has just drunk from the poisoned cup
Hamlet has been fighting honorably and generously
hidden_secrets: He is on the verge of moral collapse and may not follow through with full commitment
The sword is poisoned — unknown to Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is fully aware of the conspiracy and is now witnessing its collateral damage (the Queen). This full knowledge is precisely what triggers his conscience.

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — trying to override conscience by recommitting verbally to the King
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he announces his intent to strike with the poisoned blade while simultaneously confessing it violates his conscience — he is split between duty to vengeance and moral awareness
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The conscience line could be a calculated aside meant to establish plausible deniability or moral distance rather than genuine remorse — a preemptive self-excuse before committing the act.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Come, for the third, Laertes. You do but dally. / I pray you pass with your best violence. / I am afeard you make a wanton of me.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES

utterances.speaker: OSRIC

beat_states.desire_state: to provoke Laertes into fighting with full intensity so he can prove his superiority
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to act decisively and meet his fate with honor, having already surrendered to providence
beat_states.obstacle: Laertes appears to be holding back, not fully engaging

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is exhilarated by his success in the match, confident and dominant. He feels in control of the bout and is energized by competition. His vulnerability is low because he is utterly unaware of the poisoned sword.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet is publicly dominant—he's winning the match and teasing Laertes about treating him like a child ('make a wanton of me'). His warmth is minimal; the goading is competitive, bordering on contemptuous.

Laertes' restraint is mere timidity or courtesy rather than strategic calculation with a poisoned blade
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is completely blind to the conspiracy. He reads Laertes' hesitation as lack of commitment rather than the moral hesitation of a man about to commit murder.

beat_states.defense_state: projecting supreme confidence to mask any awareness of danger
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He taunts Laertes for going easy on him while being unaware that Laertes is actually trying to find the right moment to deliver a fatal poisoned strike

beat_states.desire_state: to land the poisoned strike on Hamlet and avenge his father and sister
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to restore family honor by killing Hamlet, fulfilling his oath to Claudius
beat_states.obstacle: His conscience is actively warring with his intention—he told the King 'it is almost against my conscience' moments ago; Hamlet's sportsmanship and openness makes the treachery harder

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is in deep inner turmoil. His 'Say you so? Come on' is clipped, tense—a man forcing himself past moral resistance. High arousal from the urgency of the moment, low control because his conscience is undermining his resolve.

social_state.rationale: Publicly diminished by losing two bouts and being taunted. His warmth is negative—he must treat Hamlet as an enemy to go through with the plan, yet the pretense of sportsmanship creates a cold falseness.

known_facts: The sword is poisoned and unbated
The King has also poisoned the wine
He has already expressed doubt about the plan
Hamlet is fighting openly and fairly
hidden_secrets: The entire assassination plot
His growing moral revulsion at his own treachery
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes has complete knowledge of the conspiracy and is the one character in the bout who sees the full picture, which is precisely what makes his position so agonizing.

beat_states.defense_state: suppressing conscience by channeling Hamlet's taunts as fuel for action
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He needs to kill Hamlet but is increasingly disgusted by the dishonorable means; Hamlet's provocation both shames him and gives him the anger he needs to act
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes' 'Say you so? Come on' could be read as genuine competitive fire finally igniting, temporarily overriding his moral qualms

beat_states.desire_state: to fulfill his role as judge and scorekeeper impartially
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his position at court by performing his duties with precision and grace
beat_states.obstacle: The bout is intensifying and the stakes seem higher than a normal match; he senses tension he cannot fully explain

affect_state.rationale: Osric is functionally neutral—his call of 'Nothing neither way' is a clean judgment. Slightly elevated arousal from the intensity of the bout but professionally composed.

social_state.rationale: Osric is a functionary here, subordinate to all principals. His warmth is neutral—he is performing a role, not relating personally.

known_facts: Hamlet leads 2-0
This third pass resulted in no clear hit
The King wagered on Hamlet
false_beliefs: This is a legitimate sporting contest
epistemic_state.rationale: Osric is an innocent bystander with no knowledge of the poison plot. He reads events at face value.

beat_states.defense_state: retreating into procedural formality to avoid engaging with the escalating emotional charge
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Osric may be subtly aware that something is wrong given the King's unusual behavior and Gertrude drinking the wine, but suppresses any suspicion

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Have at you now!

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: Part them. They are incensed.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Nay, come again.

beat_states.desire_state: to continue fighting — to meet the challenge with full force and refuse to be denied
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to act decisively and meet his fate with courage, fulfilling his sense of readiness ('the readiness is all')
beat_states.obstacle: The King is trying to stop the bout; something irregular has happened but Hamlet doesn't fully understand what

affect_state.rationale: Adrenaline-fueled determination. Not quite positive — there's a dark edge — but he is energized and engaged. Low certainty about what just happened, moderate control as he demands the fight continue.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet asserts dominance by refusing to stop, commanding the resumption. Warmth toward Laertes has cooled — something dishonorable just occurred.

known_facts: Laertes just struck him outside the formal rules of the bout
The King wants them separated
He has been performing well in the match
Providence governs outcomes ('the readiness is all')
