hidden_secrets: He has already arranged for Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's deaths
false_beliefs: He likely does not yet know the sword is poisoned
He may not yet realize the swords have been exchanged
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is operating with incomplete information — he senses treachery instinctively but does not yet have the full picture. His demand to continue suggests suspicion but not full knowledge.

beat_states.defense_state: channeling suspicion into physical action rather than analysis
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He senses something is wrong — the foul play, the irregularity — but his fatalistic acceptance of providence overrides caution; he rushes toward danger rather than away
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet may already suspect poisoned weapons given the irregularity, and 'come again' may be a deliberate choice to force the truth into the open

beat_states.desire_state: to stop the scuffle before the poisoned blade's treachery is exposed or before Hamlet wounds Laertes with the same weapon
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his throne and eliminate Hamlet while preserving the appearance of innocence
beat_states.obstacle: The situation has spiraled beyond his control — the swords may be exchanged, the queen may be poisoned, and the court is watching

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in near-panic. The uncontrolled scuffle threatens to expose the poisoned sword, and the Queen may already be dying. His control over events is collapsing.

social_state.rationale: He still commands the room as king, but his authority is performative; his warmth is entirely feigned — he cares only about concealment.

known_facts: The sword is poisoned and unbated
The wine is poisoned
Gertrude drank from the poisoned cup
Laertes just struck outside the rules, risking exposure
hidden_secrets: He orchestrated the entire assassination plot
He poisoned the wine as a backup
He murdered King Hamlet
false_beliefs: He may believe he can still contain the situation
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius possesses full knowledge of the conspiracy but is losing his ability to manage its unfolding. He sees the mechanism breaking down in real time.

beat_states.defense_state: performing the role of concerned sovereign to mask panic
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He calls for them to be parted as though protecting them, but he engineered the entire lethal setup; his intervention is self-preservation disguised as care
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Claudius may also fear the swords have been exchanged and that Laertes is now in danger from his own plot

beat_states.desire_state: to wound Hamlet with the poisoned, unbated sword before the moment passes
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to avenge his father and sister's deaths by killing Hamlet
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet's skill has prevented a clean hit; time and conscience are running out

affect_state.rationale: Desperation and shame converge — he lunges outside the formal bout, knowing this is treachery. High arousal from the physical act and moral crisis.

social_state.rationale: He has just violated the rules of the match in front of the entire court, degrading his status as a gentleman fencer. Warmth toward Hamlet collapses into aggression.

known_facts: His sword is poisoned and unbated
Claudius has also poisoned the wine
He has failed to score a legitimate hit
The Queen may have drunk from the poisoned cup
hidden_secrets: The entire match is a murder plot orchestrated with Claudius
His blade carries lethal poison
false_beliefs: He may still believe this act will restore his family's honor
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes is fully aware of the conspiracy but increasingly doubts its moral legitimacy; his knowledge is complete but his conviction is crumbling.

beat_states.defense_state: abandoning all pretense of fair play — rage overrides shame
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must commit a dishonorable act (striking outside the rules) to satisfy what he frames as honor; his conscience is actively rebelling against Claudius's plot
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes may be acting partly out of self-loathing, wanting the crisis to break open so the truth is forced out

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: Look to the Queen there, ho!

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: They bleed on both sides.— How is it, my lord?

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: How is ’t, Laertes?

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: Why as a woodcock to mine own springe, Osric. / I am justly killed with mine own treachery.

beat_states.desire_state: To understand what has happened and protect Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To faithfully serve and safeguard Hamlet through all dangers
beat_states.obstacle: The chaos of the moment — both combatants are wounded and he doesn't yet understand the full treachery

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is in acute alarm — his dearest friend is bleeding, the Queen appears to be in distress, and the situation is spinning out of control. He is desperate to understand and help but feels powerless.

social_state.rationale: Low status as a bystander unable to intervene in the royal catastrophe, but his warmth and devotion to Hamlet are at their peak as he rushes to his side.

known_facts: Both Hamlet and Laertes are bleeding
The Queen is in distress
Something has gone terribly wrong in the match
false_beliefs: This may be an accidental wound from a fair bout
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio sees the physical evidence of disaster but does not yet know about the poisoned sword or the poisoned cup. He is rapidly trying to piece together what is happening.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Trained intellectual composure clashing with rising panic at seeing Hamlet bleed
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may already suspect foul play given his earlier warnings about the match

beat_states.desire_state: To confess his treachery and acknowledge his own moral destruction
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and sister — but now that mission has turned into his own undoing, and his deepest want shifts toward moral reckoning
beat_states.obstacle: The poison is killing him; he cannot undo what he has done

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is in the grip of mortal certainty — he knows he is dying by his own device. The self-loathing and tragic recognition produce extreme negative valence. High certainty because the truth is now undeniable. No control remains; his body is failing and his scheme has consumed him.

social_state.rationale: His status has collapsed from noble avenger to self-acknowledged traitor. The warmth is a flicker — the woodcock metaphor directed at Osric carries a bitter intimacy, a willingness to be honest in his dying moments.

known_facts: The rapiers were exchanged in the scuffle
He is cut with his own poisoned blade
He will die from the poison
Hamlet is also poisoned
The plot was engineered with Claudius
hidden_secrets: The full details of Claudius's involvement — though he is about to reveal them
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes now has complete knowledge of the situation. The dramatic irony has collapsed into tragic recognition — he is the woodcock caught in his own snare.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He set out to kill Hamlet for justice but now recognizes himself as the true villain — the avenger become the self-condemned

beat_states.desire_state: To manage the crisis and fulfill his courtly duty of attending to the combatants and the Queen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To serve the court and maintain his position through attentive, proper conduct
beat_states.obstacle: Multiple emergencies are happening simultaneously — the Queen, both fencers wounded — and he is overwhelmed

affect_state.rationale: Osric is panicking but channeling it into action — calling attention to the Queen, checking on Laertes. His affect is highly negative and aroused but he clings to his functional role.

social_state.rationale: His courtly status is meaningless in this catastrophe. His warmth is minimal — his concern is genuine but shallow, more protocol than depth.

known_facts: The Queen is in distress
Both Hamlet and Laertes are bleeding
Something has gone wrong with the fencing match
false_beliefs: This may be an accidental injury from the bout
epistemic_state.rationale: Osric is entirely ignorant of the poisoning plot. He is a superficial courtier witnessing events whose true meaning he cannot fathom.

beat_states.defense_state: Retreating into procedural courtly behavior as a way to cope with the catastrophe
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His shallow courtier's training is wholly inadequate for the mortal horror unfolding before him

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: How does the Queen?

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: She swoons to see them bleed.

beat_states.desire_state: to understand what is happening to his mother — sensing something is deeply wrong
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to seek and expose truth, to fulfill his duty to those he loves
beat_states.obstacle: he has just been wounded with a poisoned blade and chaos is erupting; information is being withheld from him

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is wounded, alarmed, and desperate. The fencing match has dissolved into violence. His question is sharp and urgent — he senses something catastrophic but doesn't yet know the full truth. His body is failing him even as his instinct to protect his mother surges.

social_state.rationale: Despite being Prince and victorious in the bout, his status is eroding as he realizes he's been ensnared. His warmth toward Gertrude is fully activated — the son's love overrides everything else in this moment.

known_facts: The Queen has collapsed
He and Laertes have both been wounded and are bleeding
The swords were somehow exchanged during the scuffle
Something is very wrong with this entire situation
hidden_secrets: He arranged the deaths of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern
He suspects but does not yet fully know Claudius has orchestrated this treachery
false_beliefs: He may still believe the Queen's collapse could be a simple faint
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet is piecing together the conspiracy but hasn't yet received Laertes' confession. His question is both genuine inquiry and the beginning of his final pursuit of truth.

beat_states.defense_state: hypervigilance — cutting through chaos to focus on immediate danger to his mother
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he is physically wounded and dying but his concern turns entirely outward toward Gertrude

beat_states.desire_state: to deflect attention from the poisoned cup and prevent anyone from discovering his plot
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to preserve his throne, his life, and his power at all costs
beat_states.obstacle: Gertrude has drunk from the poisoned cup in full view of the court; his entire conspiracy is unraveling before his eyes

affect_state.rationale: Claudius is in maximum crisis. His plan has gone catastrophically wrong — the wrong person drank the poison, Laertes and Hamlet are both wounded with the envenomed sword. He is terrified but must project composure. His lie about the swoon is desperate, reflexive, and transparent.

social_state.rationale: His royal authority is crumbling. The warmth he projects is entirely false — he is protecting himself, not Gertrude. His lie about her swooning reveals cold self-preservation disguised as concern.

known_facts: Gertrude drank from the poisoned cup meant for Hamlet
She is dying, not merely swooning
The envenomed sword has wounded both Hamlet and Laertes
His conspiracy is collapsing
hidden_secrets: He poisoned the cup
He conspired with Laertes to use an envenomed and unbated sword
He murdered King Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has full knowledge of the conspiracy and knows exactly why Gertrude is dying. His lie is not born of ignorance but of desperate concealment. He knows far more than anyone else in the room about what is truly happening.

beat_states.defense_state: minimization — reducing Gertrude's collapse to a mere swoon caused by the sight of blood, a plausible lie to buy time
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he is watching his wife die from poison he prepared for his nephew, and must perform calm reassurance while experiencing inner panic and horror at the unraveling of his plan
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A slight possibility that Claudius is also in partial denial — that some fraction of him clings to the hope she merely swooned, a psychological self-deception to manage his own guilt.

utterances.speaker: QUEEN
utterances.text: No, no, the drink, the drink! O, my dear Hamlet! / The drink, the drink! I am poisoned.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O villainy! Ho! Let the door be locked. / Treachery! Seek it out.

beat_states.desire_state: to identify and trap the source of the treachery that has killed his mother
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to expose and punish the corruption at the heart of Denmark — this is the final eruption of that mission
beat_states.obstacle: he does not yet know who is responsible; he is also wounded and poisoned himself

affect_state.rationale: Rage and horror fuse into white-hot action. He is certain treachery exists but not yet certain of its exact author. He seizes control of the space by ordering the doors locked — an act of sovereign authority. Underneath the command is devastating grief and vulnerability.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet assumes total command of the room, speaking as a prince seizing authority in crisis. His warmth toward the court is negative — everyone is now suspect. His warmth toward Gertrude is implicit but suppressed beneath action.

known_facts: his mother has been poisoned
the drink was the instrument
there is a conspiracy
Laertes wounded him with a suspicious blade
hidden_secrets: he may already suspect Claudius given the Ghost's earlier revelations
epistemic_state.rationale: Gertrude's dying words confirm what the whole scene has been building toward. Hamlet connects the dots rapidly — the poisoned drink, the earlier fencing irregularity. He does not yet have Laertes's confession but is moments away from it.

beat_states.defense_state: channeling grief into immediate decisive action
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: the man who delayed revenge for the entire play now acts with explosive immediacy; the shock of losing his mother catalyzes what philosophical doubt never could
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Hamlet's 'seek it out' could be read as still genuinely uncertain rather than rhetorically commanding — he may not yet have connected all the threads to Claudius

beat_states.desire_state: to warn Hamlet that the drink is poisoned before she dies
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to protect and love her son, even at the cost of truth about those closest to her
beat_states.obstacle: the poison is killing her rapidly; she has limited time and breath

affect_state.rationale: Gertrude is dying and knows it. Her certainty is absolute — she has tasted the poison. She has no control over her fate but channels her final energy into desperate maternal warning. The tenderness of 'my dear Hamlet' reveals total emotional openness.

social_state.rationale: She is stripped of all queenly status, reduced to a dying mother. Her warmth toward Hamlet is at its peak — the address 'my dear Hamlet' is one of the most intimate things she says in the entire play.

known_facts: the drink was poisoned
she is dying
there is treachery afoot
epistemic_state.rationale: In this moment she knows the truth with bodily certainty. Whether she suspects Claudius specifically is ambiguous in the text, but she knows the drink was the instrument of murder.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she has spent the entire play avoiding confrontation with Claudius's crimes, yet in death she finally names the treachery
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some readings suggest Gertrude drank knowingly to save Hamlet, which would reframe this as a confession of sacrificial choice rather than a warning about discovered treachery

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: It is here, Hamlet. Hamlet, thou art slain. / No med’cine in the world can do thee good. / In thee there is not half an hour’s life. / The treacherous instrument is in thy hand, / Unbated and envenomed. The foul practice / Hath turned itself on me. Lo, here I lie, / Never to rise again. Thy mother’s poisoned. / I can no more. The King, the King’s to blame.

beat_states.desire_state: To confess the full truth of the conspiracy before dying, to expose Claudius, and to achieve moral reconciliation with Hamlet
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To restore his family's honor — now redirected from revenge against Hamlet to exposing the true villain who corrupted him
beat_states.obstacle: He is dying from the poison on his own sword; time is running out; the weight of his own complicity in the treachery

affect_state.rationale: Laertes is in extremis — dying, stripped of all pretense. His certainty is absolute because he knows every detail of the plot. His control is nearly gone as death approaches. The negative valence reflects agony, guilt, and grief, but there is a fierce urgency driving his arousal as he races to get the truth out before he dies.

social_state.rationale: Laertes has fallen from courtly champion to dying confessor, his status collapsed. But his warmth toward Hamlet surges — he addresses him by name twice, warns him of his death with genuine care, and redirects blame away from Hamlet toward the King. This is an act of moral generosity from a man who minutes ago was trying to murder him.

known_facts: Hamlet has been cut with the envenomed, unbated sword and will die within half an hour
The sword has switched hands — Hamlet now holds the poisoned weapon
Laertes himself has been cut with the same poisoned sword and is dying
The Queen has been poisoned by the drink Claudius prepared
Claudius is the architect of the entire plot — the poisoned sword and the poisoned cup
No medicine can save either of them
epistemic_state.rationale: Laertes has total epistemic clarity in this moment. He is unburdening every secret. He conceals nothing — this is a full deathbed confession. There are no false beliefs; he sees the situation with devastating accuracy.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He sought to kill Hamlet to avenge his father and sister, but now recognizes Hamlet as a fellow victim of Claudius's manipulation and must confess his own role as instrument of that evil
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A small possibility that Laertes's confession is partly motivated by a desire to be forgiven by Hamlet before death — that the exposure of Claudius serves not only justice but also Laertes's need for absolution and to die with a clean conscience.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: The point envenomed too! Then, venom, to thy work.

utterances.speaker: ALL
utterances.text: Treason, treason!

utterances.speaker: KING
utterances.text: O, yet defend me, friends! I am but hurt.

beat_states.desire_state: To respond to the shocking violence unfolding before them — to name the chaos and possibly intervene
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: As courtiers, their deepest want is survival and alignment with legitimate power
beat_states.obstacle: Confusion about who is the legitimate authority — the dying prince attacking the king — paralyzes them between loyalty and horror

affect_state.rationale: Pure panic and chaos. The court witnesses the Queen's death, Laertes's confession, and now Hamlet stabbing the King. They have no control over events and are terrified.

social_state.rationale: The courtiers are powerless witnesses; their status is collapsed by the disintegration of the social order they depend upon.

known_facts: Hamlet has just stabbed the King
The Queen has collapsed or died
Laertes has confessed to treachery
false_beliefs: Some may still believe Hamlet is committing unprovoked treason rather than justified revenge
epistemic_state.rationale: They have heard Laertes's confession but in the chaos may not have fully processed its implications. Their cry of 'treason' suggests at least some instinctively side with the King's authority.

beat_states.defense_state: Collective shock response — crying 'treason' is a reflexive appeal to order without knowing whom to defend
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: They cry 'treason' but it is ambiguous — is Hamlet committing treason against the King, or has the King committed treason against them all? They don't yet know which way to turn.
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Some in the crowd may already understand Hamlet is justified and cry 'treason' against Claudius rather than Hamlet

beat_states.desire_state: To execute immediate justice on Claudius for all his crimes — the murder of his father, the poisoning of Gertrude, and the treacherous plot that is now killing him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father's murder and purge Denmark of Claudius's corruption — this is the moment of fulfillment
beat_states.obstacle: He is dying from the envenomed wound; time itself is running out

affect_state.rationale: Extreme fury and righteous wrath propel him — he finally has complete certainty of Claudius's guilt confirmed publicly by Laertes. He seizes total control of the moment even as his body is failing him. The negative valence comes from grief over his mother and his own imminent death.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet seizes absolute authority in this moment — he is prince, avenger, executioner. His warmth is entirely negative, channeled into lethal violence against the King.

known_facts: The sword point is envenomed — Laertes just confessed it
The King is the source of all the treachery
The Queen has been poisoned by the drink Claudius prepared
He himself is fatally poisoned
Laertes confirmed the King's guilt publicly before the entire court
epistemic_state.rationale: Complete epistemic clarity — all deceptions have been stripped away. Laertes's confession has made everything public. Hamlet acts with full knowledge for the first time in the play.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: The act he has delayed for the entire play now comes in a burst of spontaneous rage rather than premeditated resolve — he acts not from philosophical certainty but from visceral fury

beat_states.desire_state: To survive — to rally the court to his defense and minimize the wound as non-fatal
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain the throne and power he seized through fratricide — self-preservation is the ultimate expression of this
beat_states.obstacle: He has been struck with the envenomed blade that he himself arranged — his own weapon of murder is turned against him

affect_state.rationale: Absolute terror. Claudius knows better than anyone what the envenomed blade means. His control has completely collapsed — the elaborate scheme has backfired catastrophically. He is maximally vulnerable, exposed, and desperate.

social_state.rationale: He pleads for help, attempting warmth and connection ('friends') to rally defenders, but his status is crumbling as Laertes's public confession has exposed his crimes to the court.

known_facts: The blade is envenomed — he arranged it
He is almost certainly fatally wounded
Laertes has publicly confessed the entire plot
The Queen is dead from the poisoned cup he prepared
His crimes are now publicly known
false_beliefs: He may cling to a desperate hope that the court hasn't fully absorbed Laertes's confession and might still defend him
epistemic_state.rationale: Claudius has perfect knowledge of how lethal his situation is — he designed the very weapons being used against him. His secrets are no longer secret. His lie ('I am but hurt') is a last-gasp political maneuver, not genuine self-deception.

beat_states.defense_state: Denial and deflection — 'I am but hurt' desperately downplays the mortal wound to prevent the court from abandoning him
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He knows the blade is envenomed and therefore lethal — he arranged it himself — yet he publicly claims to be merely hurt. He is lying even in his death throes, performing innocence to the end.
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A small possibility that Claudius genuinely doesn't realize the wound is from the envenomed blade, though this is unlikely given he orchestrated the plot

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Here, thou incestuous, murd’rous, damnèd Dane, / Drink off this potion. Is thy union here? / Follow my mother.
utterances.stage_direction: Forcing him to drink the poison.

beat_states.desire_state: To execute final justice on Claudius — to kill the king who murdered his father, poisoned his mother, and orchestrated his own death
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To avenge his father and purge the corruption from Denmark — this is the ultimate fulfillment of the Ghost's command
beat_states.obstacle: His own dying body — the envenomed wound is already killing him; time itself is running out

affect_state.rationale: Maximum arousal — this is the climactic act of the play. Certainty is absolute: he now knows everything (the poison, the treachery, his mother's death). He seizes total control in this moment even as his body fails. Valence is deeply negative — fury, grief for his mother, the horror of the situation. Vulnerability is extreme because he is mortally wounded and losing everything.

social_state.rationale: Hamlet assumes absolute sovereign authority — he is judge, jury, and executioner. He forces the king to drink poison, the ultimate assertion of dominance. Warmth is at its most negative: 'incestuous, murd'rous, damnèd Dane' is pure contempt and righteous hatred. The only warmth is the echo of 'Follow my mother,' which acknowledges his love for Gertrude even as he sentences Claudius.

known_facts: Claudius murdered his father
Claudius plotted his death through the envenomed sword
The drink was poisoned by Claudius
His mother is dead from the poisoned cup
He himself is dying from the envenomed blade
Laertes confirmed the entire conspiracy
epistemic_state.rationale: Complete epistemic clarity — all deceptions have been stripped away. Laertes' confession in the previous beat gave Hamlet total knowledge of Claudius's treachery. There are no more secrets, no more doubts. The 'union' pun bitterly connects the poisoned pearl (the 'union' Claudius dropped in the cup) with the incestuous marriage.

beat_states.defense_state: Channeling all grief and rage into decisive action, converting vulnerability into lethal force
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: The act he has agonized over for the entire play now comes in a moment of pure reactive fury rather than deliberation — the delay is over precisely when he himself is already dead
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: One could read 'Follow my mother' as containing a flicker of dark irony or even mercy — sending Claudius after Gertrude in death — but the dominant reading is wrathful execution and bitter contempt.

utterances.speaker: LAERTES
utterances.text: He is justly served. / It is a poison tempered by himself. / Exchange forgiveness with me, noble Hamlet. / Mine and my father’s death come not upon thee, / Nor thine on me.

beat_states.desire_state: to achieve mutual forgiveness with Hamlet before death and die with a clear conscience
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to restore his family's honor — now transformed in death into a need for spiritual peace and moral redemption
beat_states.obstacle: he is dying and has limited time; he must overcome the enormity of his own treachery and guilt

affect_state.rationale: Dying, Laertes has lost all physical control but has achieved moral clarity. The negative valence reflects his suffering and guilt, but there is a measure of peace in the act of confession and forgiveness. Certainty is high — he knows what happened and what must be done. Vulnerability is absolute as he faces death openly.

social_state.rationale: Laertes has lost all status — he is a dying man who was a pawn in Claudius's scheme. But his warmth toward Hamlet is at its highest, addressing him as 'noble Hamlet' and offering unconditional forgiveness, reaching across the enmity that defined their relationship.

known_facts: The poison on the sword and in the cup was Claudius's design
Both he and Hamlet are fatally poisoned
Claudius is now dead — 'justly served'
His own complicity in the plot has caused his death and Hamlet's
The Queen is dead from the poisoned cup
epistemic_state.rationale: In death, Laertes has nothing left to hide. He has already exposed the full truth of the plot in his previous confession. His epistemic state is now fully transparent — all secrets have been revealed, and he sees the situation with complete moral clarity.

beat_states.defense_state: projection onto Claudius — 'He is justly served' deflects his own culpability by redirecting blame
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he was the instrument of the poisoned plot yet now seeks to position himself as a moral agent capable of granting and receiving forgiveness
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Laertes's forgiveness could be read as a final strategic act — ensuring his reputation is preserved in the narrative that survivors will tell, rather than being remembered purely as a treacherous poisoner. But the weight of the text strongly favors genuine contrition.

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: Heaven make thee free of it. I follow thee.— / I am dead, Horatio.— Wretched queen, adieu.— / You that look pale and tremble at this chance, / That are but mutes or audience to this act, / Had I but time ( as this fell sergeant, Death, / Is strict in his arrest), O, I could tell you— / But let it be.— Horatio, I am dead. / Thou livest; report me and my cause aright / To the unsatisfied.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Never believe it. / I am more an antique Roman than a Dane. / Here’s yet some liquor left.

beat_states.desire_state: To ensure his story and cause are truthfully told after his death, entrusting Horatio with this sacred task
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Having fulfilled his duty to avenge his father and set right the rottenness of Denmark, he now seeks to secure his legacy and meaning
beat_states.obstacle: Death is closing in rapidly, leaving him insufficient time to explain himself to the court witnesses; he must compress everything into a final charge to Horatio

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is dying and knows it with absolute certainty. His arousal is modulated by a strange calm that comes with finality, but urgency about his legacy keeps it elevated. He has little control over his body or fate, and his vulnerability is extreme — he is fully exposed, dying publicly, dependent on Horatio's love and loyalty.

social_state.rationale: Even in death, Hamlet commands the room — addressing the court, his mother's corpse, and Horatio with princely authority. His warmth toward Horatio is profound, intimate, trusting — this is the deepest bond left to him.

known_facts: He is dying from the envenomed sword
Claudius is dead
Laertes has forgiven him and confirmed Claudius's treachery
The queen is dead
The court witnesses do not understand the full story
Horatio is the only one who can tell his story truthfully
epistemic_state.rationale: At this moment Hamlet possesses complete knowledge of the conspiracy and its aftermath. All secrets have been exposed. His only concern is epistemological — that the world beyond this room will not know the truth without Horatio's testimony.

beat_states.defense_state: Acceptance and composure in the face of death — transforming terror into dignified command
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has achieved his revenge and found peace with Laertes, yet he is anguished that he cannot speak his full truth and must rely on another to vindicate him

beat_states.desire_state: To die alongside Hamlet rather than survive in a world without him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Horatio's deepest loyalty and love is to Hamlet — his entire sense of purpose is bound to serving and being with his prince
beat_states.obstacle: Hamlet has just explicitly asked him to live and tell the story, directly opposing his desire to die

affect_state.rationale: Horatio is in acute grief, watching his dearest friend die. His arousal is very high — this is an impulsive, passionate act of reaching for the poisoned cup. He has some certainty about what he wants (death), but almost no control over the situation. His vulnerability is near-total; he is emotionally shattered.

social_state.rationale: Horatio has always occupied a lower social status relative to Hamlet, but in this moment his defiance briefly asserts a kind of equality — he claims the right to choose his own death. His warmth is at its peak: this is the ultimate expression of devotion.

known_facts: Hamlet is dying
The poisoned cup still has liquor remaining
He knows the full truth of Claudius's crimes and the conspiracy
Hamlet wants him to live and report the truth
false_beliefs: That dying with Hamlet is the most honorable course — he hasn't yet fully reckoned with Hamlet's charge that living is the harder, nobler duty
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio knows everything Hamlet knows. His only 'false belief' is a value judgment — that Roman-style suicide is more fitting than the survival Hamlet demands of him.

beat_states.defense_state: Identifying with Roman Stoic honor to reframe suicide as noble duty rather than grief-driven despair
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His deepest love for Hamlet demands both obedience (living to tell the story) and defiance (dying alongside him); the two pulls are irreconcilable in this instant
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's reach for the cup could also be read as a test — forcing Hamlet to articulate why he must live, thereby extracting the final, most urgent expression of Hamlet's will

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: As thou ’rt a man, / Give me the cup. Let go! By heaven, I’ll ha ’t. / O God, Horatio, what a wounded name, / Things standing thus unknown, shall I leave behind me! / If thou didst ever hold me in thy heart, / Absent thee from felicity awhile / And in this harsh world draw thy breath in pain / To tell my story. / What warlike noise is this?
utterances.stage_direction: A march afar off and shot within.

utterances.speaker: OSRIC
utterances.text: Young Fortinbras, with conquest come from Poland, / To th’ ambassadors of England gives / This warlike volley.

beat_states.desire_state: To prevent Horatio from committing suicide and to compel him to survive and tell Hamlet's story to the world
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Having fulfilled his mission of avenging his father, his deepest need shifts to legacy — ensuring the truth is known and his name is cleared
beat_states.obstacle: He is dying and losing physical strength; Horatio is trying to drink the poison; time is running out and the world does not yet know the truth

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is dying and knows it. His arousal surges as he physically wrestles the cup from Horatio. Deeply negative valence from pain, grief, and the knowledge of his 'wounded name.' High vulnerability as he pleads with his dearest friend. Some certainty about what must be done but diminishing control over his own body and the world.

social_state.rationale: Despite dying, Hamlet commands with princely authority — physically seizing the cup, invoking heaven. Yet his warmth toward Horatio is at its peak: 'If thou didst ever hold me in thy heart' is the most intimate, loving plea in the play. He is simultaneously sovereign and supplicant.

known_facts: He is dying from the poisoned sword
Claudius is dead
Laertes has died and forgiven him
Gertrude is dead
The full truth of Claudius's crimes will die with him unless someone tells it
Horatio was about to drink the remaining poison
hidden_secrets: The ghost's revelation and full scope of Claudius's treachery remain unknown to the court and world
epistemic_state.rationale: Hamlet has near-complete knowledge of the situation. His urgent concern is epistemic: the world does NOT know what he knows, and his 'wounded name' will persist unless Horatio survives to narrate the truth.

beat_states.defense_state: Sublimation — transforms the terror of death into an urgent mission for Horatio, channeling anguish into purposeful entreaty
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He simultaneously longs for the release of death ('felicity') yet fights desperately to control what happens after he's gone — the man who deliberated endlessly about action now acts with absolute urgency in his final moments
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: One could read Hamlet's demand as partially selfish — not just truth-telling but a desperate bid for posthumous vindication and reputation, revealing that even at death's door, his ego and self-image drive him

beat_states.desire_state: To answer Hamlet's question and provide useful information about the warlike noise
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: Osric's fundamental drive is to serve, perform his courtly function, and remain relevant to those in power
beat_states.obstacle: The catastrophic scene around him — the entire court lies dead or dying — reduces his role to near-irrelevance; he can only report external facts

affect_state.rationale: Osric is shaken but falls back on his trained courtly function. Moderate arousal from the chaos around him. Negative valence from witnessing the carnage. He has certainty about the external fact he reports but no control over the unfolding catastrophe.

social_state.rationale: Osric is a minor functionary in a room of dead royalty. His status is negligible. Minimal warmth — he delivers information dutifully but without deep personal connection to the dying prince.

known_facts: Fortinbras has returned victorious from Poland
The warlike noise is a volley fired for the English ambassadors
The King, Queen, Laertes, and Hamlet are dead or dying
epistemic_state.rationale: Osric knows the external political facts. He has witnessed the duel and its aftermath but likely does not understand the full web of poisoning and treachery.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — retreats into the factual, procedural role of courtly announcer to cope with the surrounding horror
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He delivers a routine diplomatic report with courtly composure while standing amid a massacre of the entire Danish royal family
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Osric's calm report could be read as genuine shock — a kind of dissociative numbness where he can only process the mundane because the enormity of what has happened hasn't registered

utterances.speaker: HAMLET
utterances.text: O, I die, Horatio! / The potent poison quite o’ercrows my spirit. / I cannot live to hear the news from England. / But I do prophesy th’ election lights / On Fortinbras; he has my dying voice. / So tell him, with th’ occurrents, more and less, / Which have solicited— the rest is silence. / O, O, O, O!

beat_states.desire_state: To ensure political order after his death by naming Fortinbras as successor and entrusting Horatio to tell his story
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To set right a world thrown out of joint — now transferred to Fortinbras and Horatio as his final act of purpose
beat_states.obstacle: The poison is overwhelming his body and consciousness; he is literally running out of time and breath

affect_state.rationale: Hamlet is dying and knows it — he has zero physical control. Yet his mind achieves a strange clarity and peace. The valence is negative from suffering but tempered by acceptance and the satisfaction of having completed his mission. Arousal is moderate — urgency fighting against the body shutting down. His certainty about Fortinbras and what must be told is remarkably high. Total vulnerability as life leaves him.

social_state.rationale: In death Hamlet achieves his highest status — he speaks as the dying king exercising his sovereign right to name a successor. His warmth toward Horatio is palpable in his trust, and toward Denmark in his care for its future. The repeated 'O, O, O, O' strips away all social performance into raw human vulnerability.

known_facts: He is dying from the poisoned sword
Claudius is dead — his revenge is accomplished
Laertes has forgiven him and is dead
Gertrude is dead from the poisoned cup
Fortinbras is returning from Poland
Ambassadors are arriving from England with news of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's deaths
Horatio has agreed to live and tell his story
epistemic_state.rationale: At the moment of death, Hamlet has achieved full epistemic clarity — the poisonings are revealed, the treacheries exposed, his father avenged. He has no more secrets to keep; everything is laid bare. His prophesy about Fortinbras shows political foresight. The interrupted sentence 'Which have solicited—' enacts the final erasure of knowledge by death itself.

beat_states.defense_state: Sublimation — transforming the terror of death into purposeful political action and prophetic speech
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: The man who delayed action his entire life now acts with urgent clarity in his final seconds; the prince who questioned everything now speaks with absolute prophetic certainty
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The 'O, O, O, O' could be read not as peaceful surrender but as a final protest against death — a last eruption of the will to live that Hamlet cannot suppress, making his ending more agonized than transcendent

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Now cracks a noble heart. Good night, sweet prince, / And flights of angels sing thee to thy rest. / Why does the drum come hither?

beat_states.desire_state: to honor Hamlet's death with the dignity and love it deserves, to consecrate his passing
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve as faithful witness and keeper of Hamlet's truth and legacy
beat_states.obstacle: the overwhelming finality of Hamlet's death and the impossibility of adequate expression for this loss; the intrusion of the external world (Fortinbras's drum) already pressing in

affect_state.rationale: This is the most devastating moment of Horatio's life — the person he loved most has just died in his arms. The low arousal relative to the magnitude of loss reflects a stunned, reverent grief rather than hysteria. He has no control over what has happened but possesses terrible certainty. The language is gentle, hushed, almost lullaby-like ('Good night, sweet prince'), suggesting grief that has passed through shock into tender devastation.

social_state.rationale: Horatio elevates Hamlet even in death ('noble heart,' 'sweet prince'), positioning himself as the humble mourner beneath the departed. His warmth is absolute — this is pure, unguarded love expressed over a body still warm. The shift to noticing the drum signals his awareness that he must now assume a public role as witness.

known_facts: Hamlet is dead
Hamlet was poisoned by Laertes' blade and the King's plot
Claudius, Gertrude, and Laertes are all dead
Hamlet has named Fortinbras as successor
Hamlet charged him to tell the full story
Fortinbras's army is approaching — the drum signals imminent arrival
hidden_secrets: The full narrative of Hamlet's experience — the ghost, the madness, the moral struggle — which only he can now relay
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is now the sole living repository of the complete truth. He knows everything and carries the burden of being the only witness who can make sense of the carnage for the world that is about to walk through the door.

beat_states.defense_state: ritualization — channeling unbearable grief into formal benediction to maintain composure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he must honor his promise to live and tell Hamlet's story, but every fiber of him wanted to die alongside his friend moments ago
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A minor reading could emphasize Horatio's anxiety about the approaching drum — that even in this sacred moment of farewell, political reality is already encroaching, and his grief is tinged with dread about the performative task ahead of explaining this catastrophe to Fortinbras.

utterances.speaker: FORTINBRAS
utterances.text: Where is this sight?

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: What is it you would see? / If aught of woe or wonder, cease your search.

utterances.speaker: FORTINBRAS
utterances.text: This quarry cries on havoc. O proud Death, / What feast is toward in thine eternal cell / That thou so many princes at a shot / So bloodily hast struck?

utterances.speaker: AMBASSADOR
utterances.text: The sight is dismal, / And our affairs from England come too late. / The ears are senseless that should give us hearing / To tell him his commandment is fulfilled, / That Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead. / Where should we have our thanks?

beat_states.desire_state: to deliver his report on Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's execution and receive acknowledgment from the Danish king
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to fulfill his diplomatic duty to England and secure proper diplomatic relations with Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: the person who should receive the report (the King) is dead; the entire political structure he expected to address has been annihilated

affect_state.rationale: Dismayed by the carnage, disoriented by the collapse of his mission's purpose; his question 'Where should we have our thanks?' carries both literal confusion and dark irony

social_state.rationale: An envoy of some standing but addressing an uncertain power vacuum; purely formal, no personal connection to anyone present

known_facts: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern have been executed per the King's command
The Danish court lies in ruin before him
false_beliefs: He believes it was Claudius's genuine commandment that ordered the execution, unaware Hamlet forged the commission
epistemic_state.rationale: The Ambassador knows only his narrow diplomatic brief and is ignorant of the entire web of intrigue that led to these deaths

beat_states.defense_state: bureaucratic detachment — retreating into the formality of his mission to cope with the grotesque scene
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: dutiful professionalism clashing with the absurdity of delivering a message to a court of corpses
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: The Ambassador's 'Where should we have our thanks?' could be read as darkly sardonic rather than genuinely bewildered — a grim recognition of the absurdity

beat_states.desire_state: to understand what catastrophe has occurred and to assert his authority over the scene
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to claim power and territory — Denmark is now ripe for his taking
beat_states.obstacle: he has arrived too late to interact with any of the principals; he must piece together what happened from survivors

affect_state.rationale: Shocked by the spectacle of so many royal dead, but his military bearing and ambition quickly reassert a sense of command; uncertainty about events but confidence in his own position

social_state.rationale: Enters as a conquering prince with full authority; his address is formal and commanding, showing little personal warmth but a ceremonial gravity

known_facts: He has arrived in Denmark with his army
Multiple royal figures lie dead before him
He has some claim to the Danish throne
false_beliefs: He may assume this is the aftermath of a battle or coup rather than a poisoning plot
epistemic_state.rationale: Fortinbras knows nothing of the specifics — the poisoned rapier, the cup, Claudius's treachery — and must rely on witnesses like Horatio to explain

beat_states.defense_state: rhetorical grandeur — aestheticizing the carnage as Death's 'feast' to manage the shock
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: genuine horror at the bloodshed coexists with a dawning recognition of political opportunity
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Fortinbras could be read as purely calculating here, the shock entirely performative, his real focus already on seizing the throne

beat_states.desire_state: to fulfill Hamlet's dying wish by telling the true story and to protect Hamlet's name and legacy
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to serve Hamlet's truth and honor — the one sacred duty that kept him from following Hamlet in death
beat_states.obstacle: overwhelming grief and exhaustion; he must now narrate unspeakable events to strangers who have no context

affect_state.rationale: Profound grief barely held in check; his arousal has dropped from the frenzy of Hamlet's death to a kind of hollowed-out resolve; he knows the truth but feels powerless in his loss

social_state.rationale: Socially diminished — a scholar among soldiers and royalty, yet elevated by being the sole surviving witness; his manner is weary and guarded rather than warm

known_facts: Hamlet is dead
Claudius poisoned the cup and the rapier
Laertes confessed the plot
Hamlet killed Claudius
Hamlet gave him dying instructions to tell the story
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were sent to their deaths by Hamlet's counter-plot
hidden_secrets: The full scope of Hamlet's inner torment and the Ghost's revelation, which he must now choose how to frame
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is the most epistemically complete character alive — he knows virtually everything and must now decide how to present it

beat_states.defense_state: intellectual composure — channeling devastation into the role of witness and narrator
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he wishes he were dead alongside Hamlet, yet he must remain alive and coherent to tell the story
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio's 'cease your search' could be read as an attempt to shield the dead from political exploitation, a subtle resistance to Fortinbras's authority

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Not from his mouth, / Had it th’ ability of life to thank you. / He never gave commandment for their death. / But since, so jump upon this bloody question, / You from the Polack wars, and you from England, / Are here arrived, give order that these bodies / High on a stage be placed to the view, / And let me speak to th’ yet unknowing world / How these things came about. So shall you hear / Of carnal, bloody, and unnatural acts, / Of accidental judgments, casual slaughters, / Of deaths put on by cunning and forced cause, / And, in this upshot, purposes mistook / Fall’n on th’ inventors’ heads. All this can I / Truly deliver.

beat_states.desire_state: To seize narrative authority and ensure the true story of Hamlet's life and death is told accurately before anyone else can distort it
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To honor and protect Hamlet — in life through loyalty, in death through truth
beat_states.obstacle: Fortinbras and the English ambassadors have arrived with their own frameworks of understanding; the bodies are mute; grief threatens to overwhelm his composure; time is short before political narratives take hold

affect_state.rationale: Deep grief underlies everything, but a fierce sense of mission elevates his arousal and certainty. He knows the full truth — perhaps the only living person who does — and this gives him unusual control and purpose even as he is profoundly wounded. The vulnerability is high because he is stepping far outside his nature to claim a public voice.

social_state.rationale: Horatio elevates himself from observer/friend to essential witness and narrator, asserting surprising authority before a prince (Fortinbras) and ambassadors. Warmth is muted — his tone is grave, forensic, almost judicial — though love for Hamlet animates every word. He corrects Fortinbras's assumption about the bodies with quiet firmness.

known_facts: The full chain of events: Claudius's murder of King Hamlet, the play-within-a-play, Polonius's death, Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's commission and Hamlet's reversal of it, the poisoned sword and cup plot, Laertes's confession, Gertrude's accidental poisoning, Claudius's death, Hamlet's dying wish for Fortinbras
Hamlet never ordered the deaths of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern in the way the ambassadors might assume
Fortinbras has returned from Poland and the English ambassadors have arrived simultaneously
He is the sole surviving witness who can connect all the threads
hidden_secrets: The ghost's visitation and its specific charges — knowledge shared only with the dead
The depth of his own near-suicide attempt moments ago, thwarted by Hamlet's dying plea
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio possesses the most complete epistemic picture of anyone alive. His unique position as Hamlet's confidant through every stage of the tragedy makes him the indispensable narrator. He has no false beliefs — his role has always been the rational, truthful observer.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — channeling devastating grief into the structured, almost legalistic task of cataloguing and narrating events
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is shattered by loss yet must project authority and clarity he has never before claimed; the most self-effacing character in the play suddenly demands the world's attention
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may also be subtly positioning himself politically — ensuring Fortinbras inherits legitimately by framing the narrative correctly, thus fulfilling Hamlet's dying political wish alongside the personal one of clearing his name.

utterances.speaker: FORTINBRAS
utterances.text: Let us haste to hear it / And call the noblest to the audience. / For me, with sorrow I embrace my fortune. / I have some rights of memory in this kingdom, / Which now to claim my vantage doth invite me.

utterances.speaker: HORATIO
utterances.text: Of that I shall have also cause to speak, / And from his mouth whose voice will draw on more. / But let this same be presently performed / Even while men’s minds are wild, lest more mischance / On plots and errors happen.

beat_states.desire_state: To legitimize and consolidate his claim to the Danish throne immediately amid the chaos
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To reclaim lost territory and establish his sovereign power — the ambition that drove his march through Denmark
beat_states.obstacle: He lacks full understanding of what happened here and must navigate the political optics of seizing power over a court full of corpses

affect_state.rationale: Fortinbras is in a position of immense strategic advantage and knows it. His arousal is moderated by the gravity of the scene, but he feels confident and in control. The sorrow is performed, not deeply felt — his valence tips positive because this is the fulfillment of his campaign.

social_state.rationale: He is the highest-ranking living authority in the room and assumes command naturally. His warmth is minimal — he is a military conqueror, not a mourner, though he performs a thin veneer of diplomatic respect.

known_facts: The Danish royal family is dead
He has hereditary claims to the Danish throne
He has a military force at his disposal
Hamlet's dying voice nominated him (from Horatio's account)
hidden_secrets: The speed of his claim suggests premeditated ambition beyond his stated mission against Poland
epistemic_state.rationale: Fortinbras understands the political situation with clarity but lacks the full narrative of what transpired. He knows enough to act decisively.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing sorrow as a diplomatic mask over what is fundamentally an opportunistic power grab
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to embrace his fortune 'with sorrow' while the speed and decisiveness of his claim reveals barely concealed ambition and satisfaction
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Fortinbras may genuinely feel some sorrow — as a soldier who respects fellow princes, the carnage may disturb him even as he benefits from it

beat_states.desire_state: To ensure Hamlet's dying wishes are honored and the true story is told before political chaos distorts everything
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be faithful to Hamlet — to preserve his friend's truth and legacy in a world of corruption and misrepresentation
beat_states.obstacle: Time is against him — men's minds are 'wild,' and if he doesn't act quickly, further violence or political manipulation could overtake the truth

affect_state.rationale: Deep grief persists but is being overridden by urgent purpose. His arousal is high because he senses the danger of delay. He has moderate certainty about what must happen but limited control — he depends on Fortinbras's cooperation.

social_state.rationale: Horatio is a scholar with no political rank, yet he is the sole possessor of the narrative — this gives him temporary moral authority. His warmth is restrained; he is task-focused, operating as Hamlet's posthumous advocate.

known_facts: The full story of Claudius's murder of King Hamlet
Hamlet's dying wish that Fortinbras succeed him
The sequence of events leading to all the deaths
Hamlet's dying voice supports Fortinbras's election
hidden_secrets: The depth of his own desire to have died alongside Hamlet
epistemic_state.rationale: Horatio is the most epistemically privileged character alive — he knows virtually the entire truth and is desperate to transmit it before it is lost or corrupted.

beat_states.defense_state: Channeling overwhelming grief into urgent pragmatic action — intellectualizing the crisis to avoid emotional collapse
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is the man who moments ago wanted to die with Hamlet, now forced to be the most politically astute person in the room to protect Hamlet's name
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Horatio may be subtly managing Fortinbras — ensuring the new king feels indebted to him and to Hamlet's memory, a quiet political move disguised as scholarly duty

utterances.speaker: FORTINBRAS
utterances.text: Let four captains / Bear Hamlet like a soldier to the stage, / For he was likely, had he been put on, / To have proved most royal; and for his passage, / The soldier’s music and the rite of war / Speak loudly for him. / Take up the bodies. Such a sight as this / Becomes the field but here shows much amiss. / Go, bid the soldiers shoot.

beat_states.desire_state: To establish immediate command by honoring Hamlet with full military rites and restoring order to the chaos of the Danish court
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To claim and consolidate power over Denmark as its rightful sovereign
beat_states.obstacle: The grotesque spectacle of multiple royal deaths threatens to undermine the legitimacy and stability of his new reign; he must transform carnage into ceremony

affect_state.rationale: Fortinbras is in full command mode. There is solemnity but also the quiet exhilaration of a man who has just inherited a kingdom. His certainty and control are very high — he issues a rapid sequence of precise commands. Valence is slightly positive: this is his moment of ascension, tempered by the gravity of the scene.

social_state.rationale: He is now the highest-ranking figure in Denmark, issuing sovereign commands. His warmth is moderate — he shows genuine respect for Hamlet as a fellow soldier-prince, but his primary register is authoritative command, not grief.

known_facts: Hamlet, Claudius, Gertrude, and Laertes are all dead
He has dying rights to the Danish throne, supported by Hamlet's own voice
Horatio has promised a full account of the events
The English ambassadors have reported Rosencrantz and Guildenstern's deaths
false_beliefs: That Hamlet, had he been 'put on,' would have proved 'most royal' — Fortinbras projects a soldier's idealized image onto a far more complicated figure he never truly knew
epistemic_state.rationale: Fortinbras knows the political facts but not the psychological depths. He reads Hamlet through a military lens — as a fellow prince of action — which is precisely what Hamlet was not. His ignorance of the full story is what makes his eulogy both generous and ironic.

beat_states.defense_state: Ritualization — converting the horror of mass death into orderly military protocol, keeping emotion at a distance through ceremony
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He eulogizes Hamlet as one who would have 'proved most royal,' yet he is the direct beneficiary of Hamlet's failure to ever reach the throne — his praise simultaneously honors and displaces
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Fortinbras's praise of Hamlet could be read as entirely strategic — elevating the dead prince legitimizes his own succession by appearing to honor rather than usurp. The military honors may be less genuine tribute than political theater designed for the watching Danish court and soldiers.

character_bibles.superobjective: To be believed — to have his frightening, destabilizing experience of the supernatural validated by credible authority, so that what he has witnessed means something beyond his own private terror
character_bibles.wounds_fears_needs: Barnardo carries the wound of the unwitnessed: he has seen something monstrous and no one of rank or education was present to confirm it. This breeds a deep fear of being dismissed as credulous, superstitious, or unfit for his post. His unmet need is epistemic legitimacy — he needs a scholar, a superior, someone whose word carries weight, to stand beside him and say 'yes, this is real.' Beneath the soldier's practical exterior lives a man shaken to his foundations by two nights of inexplicable terror, desperately seeking an authority outside himself to re-anchor his sense of reality.
recurring_tactics: Challenging preemptively to seize control of uncertain encounters before fear can paralyze him
Narrating with precise, verifiable detail (astronomical, temporal) to fortify his account against accusations of fantasy
Welcoming and rallying allies as a way of redistributing his private anxiety across a group
Probing others for confirmation before committing to his own position
Reporting in neutral, observational language to perform composure he does not fully feel
Corroborating others' interpretations to attach himself to more authoritative voices
Alerting the group to the Ghost's movements, retreating into the functional sentinel role
preferred_defense_mechanisms: Preemptive aggression — acting first, challenging first, speaking first to override fear with action
Deflection through practicality — keeping exchanges brisk and task-oriented to avoid sitting with dread
Anchoring to concrete, measurable detail (time, stars, the bell) to counteract the irrational
Role-retreat — hiding behind the identity of 'soldier on watch' when the supernatural exceeds his capacity to process
Alignment with authority — subordinating his own interpretation to Horatio's scholarly explanation to reduce personal exposure
Understatement — minimizing his own emotional investment ('I have seen nothing') to appear credible rather than desperate
psychological_contradictions: He is the one arriving to relieve Francisco yet challenges as though he is the one already on post — fear overrides protocol
He projects calm, caretaking authority toward Francisco while privately being terrified enough to have summoned outside witnesses
He desperately wants Horatio to believe him but responds with cautious understatement rather than passionate advocacy, fearing that urgency will make him seem unhinged
He relives a genuinely terrifying experience while needing to appear measured and authoritative in the retelling
He is triumphant when the Ghost reappears — vindicated — and simultaneously more afraid than before, because being right means the nightmare is real
He endorses a rational political explanation for the Ghost while simultaneously calling it 'portentous,' betraying a suppressed sense that it exceeds political logic
He has been exposed to the Ghost twice yet remains a passive reporter and witness rather than an active agent — repeated exposure has not produced courage, only a practiced performance of steadiness
character_bibles.speech_style: Barnardo speaks in the plain, functional idiom of a professional soldier — short declaratives, direct address, efficient sentences. When recounting the Ghost, however, his language briefly elevates into precise, almost hypnotic astronomical and temporal imagery ('yond same star that's westward from the pole'), suggesting a man who has rehearsed his testimony obsessively, polishing it into something irrefutable. He tends toward clipped, confirmatory phrases ('In the same figure,' 'I think it be no other but e'en so') and rarely indulges in extended rhetoric except when building the ghost narrative as a set piece. His rhythm is watchful — short bursts, pauses implied, the cadence of a man listening even as he speaks.
rhetorical_patterns: Preemptive challenge as opener — seizing the first word to control the encounter ('Who's there?')
Confirmatory echo — repeating or paraphrasing another's statement to align himself with it ('In the same figure like the King that's dead')
Invitation to witness — drawing others into shared perception rather than insisting on his own ('Sit down awhile, and let us once again assail your ears')
Rhetorical question as pressure — using questions to force acknowledgment from skeptics ('Is not this something more than fantasy? What think you on 't?')
Precise temporal and astronomical anchoring — grounding uncanny claims in measurable, verifiable coordinates
Clipped informational update — delivering new data in minimal, declarative form ('It was about to speak when the cock crew')
Welcoming as social bonding — using greeting to reestablish solidarity and reduce his own isolation ('Welcome, Horatio. Welcome, good Marcellus.')
few_shot_lines: ’Tis now struck twelve. Get thee to bed, Francisco.
It would be spoke to.
Sit down awhile, / And let us once again assail your ears, / That are so fortified against our story, / What we have two nights seen.
Have you had quiet guard?
How now, Horatio, you tremble and look pale. / Is not this something more than fantasy? / What think you on ’t?
Last night of all, / When yond same star that’s westward from the pole / Had made his course t’ illume that part of heaven / Where now it burns, Marcellus and myself, / The bell then beating one—
Well, good night. / If you do meet Horatio and Marcellus, / The rivals of my watch, bid them make haste.
I think it be no other but e’en so. / Well may it sort that this portentous figure / Comes armèd through our watch so like the king / That was and is the question of these wars.
In the same figure like the King that’s dead.
Say, what, is Horatio there?
known_facts: Barnardo is a soldier stationed as a night watchman at Elsinore castle
He has witnessed the Ghost of King Hamlet on at least two consecutive nights before the play opens
He took the initiative to arrange for Horatio and Marcellus to join his watch as witnesses
He is the one who relieves Francisco at midnight at the play's opening
He appears only in Act 1, Scene 1, and his role is entirely confined to the ghost-sighting and its immediate aftermath
secrets: He is more profoundly frightened than his soldierly bearing allows him to admit — his brisk practicality is largely performance
His summoning of Horatio is a tacit confession that he does not fully trust his own sanity or his own testimony, even though he argues for it outwardly
His eagerness to have Horatio — a scholar, a social superior — validate the experience suggests a deep-seated anxiety about his own standing and credibility

The triumphant edge in 'Is not this something more than fantasy?' betrays that part of him has been silently doubting his own perception, and the Ghost's reappearance is as much a relief from self-doubt as it is a terror

arc_by_scene.1_1_opening: Barnardo arrives at his post already destabilized — his preemptive challenge reveals that two nights of ghost-sightings have eroded his professional composure. He masks anxiety behind brisk military routine but has already made the decisive move of summoning Horatio as a credible witness, indicating he does not trust his own testimony alone.
arc_by_scene.1_1_horatio_arrival: With Horatio present, Barnardo shifts from isolated witness to advocate. He moderates his tone carefully, offering understatement rather than urgency so as not to seem credulous. He takes on the role of narrator-in-chief, constructing the ghost account with deliberate, astronomical precision — this is a performance of rationality in service of an irrational claim.
arc_by_scene.1_1_ghost_appears: Barnardo's arc reaches its emotional climax: the Ghost appears, and he is simultaneously vindicated and terrified. His triumphant 'Is not this something more than fantasy?' is the release of accumulated anxiety — but the triumph is shadowed by the recognition that being right is itself frightening. He pivots to the functional role of identifier and reporter, naming what he sees ('Looks he not like the King?') to keep himself useful and grounded.
arc_by_scene.1_1_ghost_departs: Deflated by the Ghost's silence and frustrated by the lost opportunity for communication, Barnardo retreats fully into the sentinel's observational register. His final contribution — 'It was about to speak when the cock crew' — is a small, precise, almost elegiac detail that captures his character: the man who is always almost getting the answer he needs, always one interruption away from certainty.

character_bibles.superobjective: To restore order and safety — to transform the terrifying, destabilizing supernatural encounter into something knowable, manageable, and properly handled by those with the authority and learning to act on it
character_bibles.wounds_fears_needs: Marcellus is a practical soldier whose entire identity rests on the reliability of military protocol, chain of command, and the material world. The Ghost has cracked that foundation irrevocably. His deepest wound is the helplessness of witnessing something that his training and weapons cannot address — a violence-trained man confronting the untouchable. He fears being dismissed as a fantasist or coward, fears the chaos the Ghost portends, and fears he has already been complicit in something far beyond his rank to contain. His unmet need is for a higher authority — Horatio's scholarship, Hamlet's princeship — to take this burden from his hands and make it mean something.
recurring_tactics: Recruiting or deferring to figures of higher authority (Horatio, Hamlet) to legitimize his experience and offload interpretive responsibility
Converting fear into purposeful action — volunteering, urging, physically restraining — so that doing something prevents feeling everything
Grounding uncanny experience in concrete, verifiable facts (the clock, martial stalk, twice before) to re-anchor himself in the material world
Appeasing those above him in rank to smooth over friction and restore functional order
Warning others of danger as a way of exercising the only protective power available to him
preferred_defense_mechanisms: Delegation — assigning interpretive and verbal authority to scholars and princes so he need not confront the supernatural alone
Action orientation — channeling dread into physical readiness (offering to strike the Ghost, moving to restrain Hamlet, urging pursuit)
Rationalization through religious faith — invoking sacred time and Christian cosmology to domesticate supernatural terror within a safe framework
Deflection into political and material concerns — pivoting from the Ghost to geopolitics, from the uncanny to the explainable
Military formality — using protocol, rank, and courtesy as emotional armor against the extraordinary
psychological_contradictions: He performs measured, strategic calm while burning with genuine terror — soldier's composure worn over a shaking interior
He recruits Horatio to speak to the Ghost precisely because he is too afraid to do so himself, yet frames this as rational planning rather than avoidance
He offers to strike a ghost with a partisan — an act of absurd physical bravado that simultaneously reveals his courage and his utter inadequacy before something violence cannot touch
He acknowledges the Ghost's 'majestical' and even 'courteous' nature while insisting it is dangerous — caught between reverence and dread for the same figure
He is relieved each time the Ghost disappears yet urgently desires it to be resolved — wanting both the encounter to end and to be made meaningful
He pivots from supernatural horror to geopolitical inquiry (the military buildup) as if rational explanation could contain what he has seen
He disobeys Hamlet's direct order to stay yet frames this disobedience as the truest form of military duty and loyalty
He is eager to act and follow up on plans, yet his eagerness masks residual fear he refuses to name as fear
character_bibles.speech_style: Marcellus speaks in the plain, direct register of a competent NCO — concrete nouns, active verbs, clear declarative sentences. He is not eloquent but he is precise. When fear or wonder overtake him, he briefly ascends into richer, more poetic language (the cock-crowing passage on hallowed time), suggesting a man of genuine feeling and folk piety beneath the soldier's plainness. He defers syntactically to superiors — often confirming, corroborating, or completing others' statements rather than initiating long speeches. His rhetorical habit is the appeal to shared observable fact: 'twice before,' 'jump at this dead hour,' 'the clock hath stricken.' He uses repetition when under stress ('We have sworn, my lord, already') — a soldier's instinct to hold a position under pressure.
rhetorical_patterns: Appeal to empirical, repeatable evidence: establishing facts through enumeration ('twice before,' 'jump at this dead hour')
Elevation of authority figures to lend weight to his own testimony ('Thou art a scholar — speak to it')
Terse, declarative confirmations that signal reliability without overreach ('Tis gone and will not answer,' 'And liegemen to the Dane')
Sudden lyrical expansion when religious or cosmic feeling breaks through his soldier's plainness (the Nativity/cock passage)
Blunt, diagnostic aphorism under maximum stress ('Something is rotten in the state of Denmark') — the one moment he speaks politically and prophetically
Repetition as a form of holding ground rather than escalating ('We have sworn, my lord, already' — said three times)
few_shot_lines: And liegemen to the Dane.

Horatio says ’tis but our fantasy / And will not let belief take hold of him / Touching this dreaded sight twice seen of us. / Therefore I have entreated him along / With us to watch the minutes of this night, / That, if again this apparition come, / He may approve our eyes and speak to it.
Thou art a scholar. Speak to it, Horatio.
’Tis gone and will not answer.
Thus twice before, and jump at this dead hour, / With martial stalk hath he gone by our watch.
Shall I strike it with my partisan?

It faded on the crowing of the cock. / Some say that ever ’gainst that season comes / Wherein our Savior’s birth is celebrated, / This bird of dawning singeth all night long; / And then, they say, no spirit dare stir abroad, / The nights are wholesome; then no planets strike, / No fairy takes, nor witch hath power to charm, / So hallowed and so gracious is that time.
And liegemen to the Dane.

Horatio says ’tis but our fantasy / And will not let belief take hold of him / Touching this dreaded sight twice seen of us. / Therefore I have entreated him along / With us to watch the minutes of this night, / That, if again this apparition come, / He may approve our eyes and speak to it.
Thou art a scholar. Speak to it, Horatio.
known_facts: He is a soldier stationed at Elsinore, a liegeman to the Danish crown, and holds sufficient rank to command a watch and bring invited guests to the platform
He has personally witnessed the Ghost of King Hamlet at least twice before the play's opening night, making him the character with the longest direct exposure to the supernatural event
He is the one who takes the practical initiative to bring Horatio as a witness and interpreter, suggesting competence, social intelligence, and genuine problem-solving instinct
He physically attempts to restrain Prince Hamlet from following the Ghost, directly defying a royal command — an act of considerable personal courage and risk
secrets: His cool soldier's manner is a performance — beneath it he is more profoundly frightened than he allows anyone, including himself, to fully acknowledge; his repeated urgency and need for others to take charge is the tell

He suspects — perhaps more clearly than he articulates — that what the Ghost portends is catastrophic for Denmark, not merely strange; 'Something is rotten in the state of Denmark' is not a casual observation but a half-suppressed conviction he has been carrying since the first sighting
His eagerness to pass authority to Horatio, then to Hamlet, contains a private relief at being released from knowledge he never wanted and does not know how to bear alone

arc_by_scene.1_1: Marcellus begins as the primary architect of the night's watch — the man who has seen the Ghost twice and taken the initiative to bring Horatio. He is the most active organizer in the scene, but his activity is driven by a need to offload the weight of the experience onto someone more qualified. By the scene's end, the Ghost has appeared and departed twice, he has attempted physical action (offering to strike it), and he has retreated into religious reassurance. He ends the scene eager to act on Horatio's suggestion — urgency masking the terror that has settled into him.
arc_by_scene.1_2: Reduced to a supporting role in Horatio's report to Hamlet, Marcellus defers throughout — performing social courtesy, corroborating details, staying present without overstepping. The contrast between the shattering events of the night before and the formal politeness of this court scene encapsulates his central tension: extraordinary knowledge contained within ordinary rank.
arc_by_scene.1_4: His most physically active and emotionally exposed scene. He tries to prevent Hamlet from following the Ghost, first through verbal warning and then through physical restraint — directly disobeying a royal command out of loyalty and protective instinct. When Hamlet breaks free, Marcellus is left behind in helpless horror. His famous line ('Something is rotten in the state of Denmark') is the moment his fear explodes outward into political prophecy — displacement at its most eloquent.
arc_by_scene.1_5: Marcellus is now peripheral to the central action — searching for Hamlet, relieved to find him alive, trying to read the situation. He swears the oath Hamlet demands, initially with slight resistance (the soldier's pragmatism chafing at what feels like ceremonial excess), but ultimately compliant. With this oath, he exits the play's active story — his role complete, his burden handed off. He ends not transformed but faithfully discharged.

character_bibles.superobjective: To fulfill his duties competently and be released — to execute his assignments efficiently, earn his master's trust, and escape the discomfort of being complicit in morally questionable schemes
